content:
[1] Adam. Seth. Enosh. [2] Kenan. Mahalalel. Jared. [3] Enoch. Methuselah. Lamech. [4] Noah — and his sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

[5] Japheth's line: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras. [6] Gomer's sons: Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah. [7] Javan's sons: Elishah, Tarshish, the Kittim, the Rodanim.

[8] Ham's line: Cush, Egypt, Put, Canaan. [9] Cush's sons: Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raamah, Sabteca. Raamah's sons: Sheba and Dedan. [10] Cush also fathered Nimrod — the first man on earth to wield real power.

[11] Egypt fathered the Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim, [12] Pathrusim, Casluhim (from whom the Philistines came), and the Caphtorim.

[13] Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, then Heth, [14] then the Jebusite, the Amorite, the Girgashite, [15] the Hivite, the Arkite, the Sinite, [16] the Arvadite, the Zemarite, the Hamathite.

[17] Shem's line: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, Aram — and Aram's sons: Uz, Hul, Gether, Meshech. [18] Arpachshad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber. [19] Eber had two sons: one was named Peleg — "Division" — because the earth was split in his days. His brother was Joktan. [20] Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah, [21] Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah, [22] Ebal, Abimael, Sheba, [23] Ophir, Havilah, Jobab. All of them — Joktan's sons.

[24] Shem. Arpachshad. Shelah. [25] Eber. Peleg. Reu. [26] Serug. Nahor. Terah. [27] Abram — that is, Abraham.

[28] Abraham's sons: Isaac and Ishmael.

[29] Their descendants: Ishmael's firstborn Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam, [30] Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema, [31] Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah. The twelve sons of Ishmael.

[32] Abraham's concubine Keturah bore Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, Shuah. Jokshan's sons: Sheba and Dedan. [33] Midian's sons: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. All from Keturah.

[34] Abraham fathered Isaac. Isaac's sons: Esau and Israel.

[35] Esau's sons: Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. [36] Eliphaz's sons: Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, Amalek. [37] Reuel's sons: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah.

[38] Seir's sons: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. [39] Lotan's sons: Hori and Homam. Lotan's sister: Timna. [40] Shobal's sons: Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, Onam. Zibeon's sons: Aiah and Anah. [41] Anah's son: Dishon. Dishon's sons: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran. [42] Ezer's sons: Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan. Dishan's sons: Uz and Aran.

[43] These were the kings who ruled Edom before any king ruled in Israel: Bela son of Beor — his city, Dinhabah. [44] Bela died; Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah took the throne. [45] Jobab died; Husham from the land of the Temanites took the throne. [46] Husham died; Hadad son of Bedad — who crushed Midian in the fields of Moab — took the throne. His city: Avith. [47] Hadad died; Samlah of Masrekah took the throne. [48] Samlah died; Shaul of Rehoboth by the River took the throne. [49] Shaul died; Baal-hanan son of Achbor took the throne. [50] Baal-hanan died; Hadad took the throne. His city: Pai. His wife: Mehetabel daughter of Matred, daughter of Mezahab.

[51] Then Hadad died. The chiefs of Edom that followed: Timna, Aliah, Jetheth, [52] Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon, [53] Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar, [54] Magdiel, Iram. The chiefs of Edom.

[1] Israel's sons: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, [2] Dan, Joseph, Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, Asher.

[3] Judah's sons: Er, Onan, and Shelah — three born to him by the Canaanite woman, Bath-shua. Er, the firstborn, was evil in the LORD's eyes, and the LORD struck him dead. [4] Tamar, Judah's daughter-in-law, bore him Perez and Zerah. Judah had five sons in all.

[5] Perez's sons: Hezron and Hamul. [6] Zerah's sons: Zimri, Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara — five of them. [7] Carmi's son: Achar, the troubler of Israel, who broke faith over what had been devoted to destruction. [8] Ethan's son: Azariah.

[9] Hezron's sons, born to him: Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai.

[10] Ram fathered Amminadab, and Amminadab fathered Nahshon, chief of the people of Judah. [11] Nahshon fathered Salma, Salma fathered Boaz, [12] Boaz fathered Obed, Obed fathered Jesse. [13] Jesse fathered his firstborn Eliab, then Abinadab the second, Shimea the third, [14] Nethanel the fourth, Raddai the fifth, [15] Ozem the sixth, David the seventh. [16] Their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. Zeruiah's sons: Abishai, Joab, and Asahel — three of them. [17] Abigail bore Amasa, and Amasa's father was Jether the Ishmaelite.

[18] Caleb son of Hezron had children by his wife Azubah and by Jerioth. Her sons: Jesher, Shobab, and Ardon. [19] When Azubah died, Caleb married Ephrath, who bore him Hur. [20] Hur fathered Uri, and Uri fathered Bezalel.

[21] Later, Hezron — at sixty years old — went in to the daughter of Machir, father of Gilead, and married her. She bore him Segub. [22] Segub fathered Jair, who held twenty-three towns in the land of Gilead. [23] But Geshur and Aram seized the villages of Jair, including Kenath and its dependent towns — sixty in all. All these were descendants of Machir, father of Gilead.

[24] After Hezron died in Caleb-ephrathah, Hezron's wife Abijah bore him Ashhur, father of Tekoa.

[25] The sons of Jerahmeel, Hezron's firstborn: Ram the firstborn, Bunah, Oren, Ozem, and Ahijah. [26] Jerahmeel had another wife named Atarah, who was the mother of Onam. [27] The sons of Ram, Jerahmeel's firstborn: Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. [28] Onam's sons: Shammai and Jada. Shammai's sons: Nadab and Abishur. [29] Abishur's wife was named Abihail; she bore him Ahban and Molid. [30] Nadab's sons: Seled and Appaim. Seled died childless. [31] Appaim's son: Ishi. Ishi's son: Sheshan. Sheshan's child: Ahlai. [32] The sons of Jada, Shammai's brother: Jether and Jonathan. Jether died childless. [33] Jonathan's sons: Peleth and Zaza. These were the descendants of Jerahmeel.

[34] Sheshan had no sons — only daughters. He owned an Egyptian slave named Jarha. [35] Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha as wife, and she bore him Attai. [36] Attai fathered Nathan, Nathan fathered Zabad, [37] Zabad fathered Ephlal, Ephlal fathered Obed, [38] Obed fathered Jehu, Jehu fathered Azariah, [39] Azariah fathered Helez, Helez fathered Eleasah, [40] Eleasah fathered Sismai, Sismai fathered Shallum, [41] Shallum fathered Jekamiah, Jekamiah fathered Elishama.

[42] The sons of Caleb, Jerahmeel's brother: Mesha his firstborn, father of Ziph, and the sons of Mareshah, father of Hebron. [43] Hebron's sons: Korah, Tappuah, Rekem, and Shema. [44] Shema fathered Raham, father of Jorkeam, and Rekem fathered Shammai. [45] Shammai's son: Maon, the father of Beth-zur.

[46] Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez. Haran fathered Gazez. [47] Jahdai's sons: Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph. [48] Maacah, Caleb's concubine, bore Sheber and Tirhanah. [49] She also bore Shaaph the father of Madmannah, and Sheva the father of Machbenah and of Gibea. Caleb's daughter was Achsah.

[50] These were Caleb's descendants. The sons of Hur, Ephrathah's firstborn: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim, [51] Salma the father of Bethlehem, and Hareph the father of Beth-gader.

[52] Shobal, father of Kiriath-jearim, had descendants: Haroeh and half the Manahathites, [53] and the clans of Kiriath-jearim — Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, and Mishraites. From these came the Zorathites and Eshtaolites.

[54] Salma's descendants: Bethlehem, the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, half the Manahathites, the Zorites, [55] and the clans of scribes living at Jabez — Tirathites, Shimeathites, and Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, ancestor of the house of Rechab.

[1] David's sons, born in Hebron: Amnon came first, son of Ahinoam of Jezreel; second was Daniel, son of Abigail of Carmel; [2] third, Absalom, whose mother was Maacah, daughter of King Talmai of Geshur; fourth, Adonijah, son of Haggith; [3] fifth, Shephatiah, son of Abital; sixth, Ithream, son of David's wife Eglah. [4] Six sons in all, born to him in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months. Then he ruled from Jerusalem for thirty-three more.

[5] In Jerusalem these were born to him: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon — four by Bathsheba, daughter of Ammiel. [6] Then Ibhar, Elishama, Eliphelet, [7] Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia, [8] Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet — nine more. [9] All these were David's sons, not counting the sons of his concubines. Tamar was their sister.

[10] Solomon's line ran like this: his son Rehoboam, then Abijah, then Asa, then Jehoshaphat, [11] then Joram, then Ahaziah, then Joash, [12] then Amaziah, then Azariah, then Jotham, [13] then Ahaz, then Hezekiah, then Manasseh, [14] then Amon, then Josiah.

[15] Josiah's sons: Johanan the firstborn, Jehoiakim second, Zedekiah third, Shallum fourth. [16] Jehoiakim's sons: Jeconiah and Zedekiah.

[17] The sons of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel his son, [18] Malchiram, Pedaiah, Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah. [19] Pedaiah's sons: Zerubbabel and Shimei. Zerubbabel's children: Meshullam and Hananiah, with Shelomith their sister, [20] and five more — Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab-hesed.

[21] Hananiah's sons: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah; then the line of Rephaiah, the line of Arnan, the line of Obadiah, the line of Shecaniah. [22] Shecaniah's son: Shemaiah. Shemaiah's sons: Hattush, Igal, Bariah, Neariah, and Shaphat — six in all. [23] Neariah's sons: Elioenai, Hizkiah, and Azrikam — three. [24] Elioenai's sons: Hodaviah, Eliashib, Pelaiah, Akkub, Johanan, Delaiah, and Anani — seven.

[1] Judah's sons: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal.

[2] Reaiah son of Shobal fathered Jahath, and Jahath fathered Ahumai and Lahad. These were the clans of the Zorathites.

[3] These belonged to Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash. Their sister was named Hazzelelponi.

[4] Penuel fathered Gedor, and Ezer fathered Hushah. These descended from Hur, Ephrathah's firstborn, founder of Bethlehem.

[5] Ashhur, founder of Tekoa, had two wives: Helah and Naarah.

[6] Naarah bore him Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari — her line.

[7] Helah's sons: Zereth, Zohar, Ethnan,

[8] and Koz, who fathered Anub and Zobebah, and the clans of Aharhel son of Harum.

[9] Jabez outweighed his brothers in honor. His mother named him Jabez — "Pain" — saying, "I bore him in agony."

[10] Jabez cried out to the God of Israel: "Bless me — really bless me. Stretch my borders wide. Put your hand on me. Keep me from harm so I am not crushed by pain." And God gave him what he asked.

[11] Chelub, brother of Shuhah, fathered Mehir, who fathered Eshton.

[12] Eshton fathered Beth-rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah, founder of Ir-nahash. These were the men of Recah.

[13] Kenaz's sons: Othniel and Seraiah. Othniel's sons: Hathath and Meonothai.

[14] Meonothai fathered Ophrah. Seraiah fathered Joab, founder of Ge-harashim — the Valley of Craftsmen — so called because craftsmen lived there.

[15] Caleb son of Jephunneh had sons: Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah's son: Kenaz.

[16] Jehallelel's sons: Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.

[17] Ezrah's sons: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. Mered's wife Bithiah bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah, founder of Eshtemoa.

[18] His Judahite wife bore Jered, founder of Gedor; Heber, founder of Soco; and Jekuthiel, founder of Zanoah. These were the sons of Bithiah, Pharaoh's daughter, whom Mered married.

[19] The sons of Hodiah's wife, Naham's sister: the father of Keilah the Garmite and Eshtemoa the Maacathite.

[20] Shimon's sons: Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-hanan, and Tilon. Ishi's sons: Zoheth and Ben-zoheth.

[21] Sons of Shelah son of Judah: Er, founder of Lecah; Laadah, founder of Mareshah; the linen-working clans of Beth-ashbea;

[22] Jokim; the men of Cozeba; and Joash and Saraph, who ruled in Moab before returning to Lehem — these are ancient records.

[23] They were the potters who lived at Netaim and Gederah, working in the king's service.

[24] Simeon's sons: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul.

[25] Shaul's line: Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son.

[26] Mishma's line: Hammuel his son, Zaccur his son, Shimei his son.

[27] Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters, but his brothers had few children — their whole clan never matched Judah's numbers.

[28] They settled in Beersheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual,

[29] Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad,

[30] Bethuel, Hormah, Ziklag,

[31] Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim — these were their towns until David took the throne.

[32] Their five villages: Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Tochen, and Ashan —

[33] and every settlement ringing them out to Baal. This was where they lived, and they kept a genealogy.

[34] Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah son of Amaziah,

[35] Joel, Jehu son of Joshibiah son of Seraiah son of Asiel,

[36] Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah,

[37] and Ziza son of Shiphi son of Allon son of Jedaiah son of Shimri son of Shemaiah —

[38] these named men were chiefs of their clans, and their families exploded in size.

[39] They pushed out to the edge of Gedor, east of the valley, hunting pasture for their flocks.

[40] They found it — lush, wide-open country, quiet and secure. The previous tenants were Hamites.

[41] In the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, these men came, smashed the tents and the Meunites they found there, and wiped them out — to this day — then settled in their place because the grazing was good.

[42] Five hundred Simeonites pushed on into the hill country of Seir, led by Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel — sons of Ishi.

[43] They cut down the last of the Amalekite survivors and have lived there ever since.

[1] Reuben — Israel's firstborn. He should have held the birthright, but he climbed into his father's bed and defiled it, so the birthright passed to the sons of Joseph son of Israel. That's why the genealogies don't reckon him as firstborn. [2] Judah grew dominant among his brothers, and the ruler came from his line — but the birthright itself went to Joseph.

[3] Reuben's sons — the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, Carmi.

[4] Joel's line: his son Shemaiah, then Gog, then Shimei, [5] then Micah, then Reaiah, then Baal, [6] then Beerah — whom Tiglath-Pileser king of Assyria carried into exile. Beerah was a chief among the Reubenites.

[7] His kinsmen, by clan, as the records were taken: the chief Jeiel, then Zechariah, [8] then Bela son of Azaz son of Shema son of Joel. They settled in Aroer and spread to Nebo and Baal-meon, [9] and eastward all the way to the edge of the wilderness that runs from the Euphrates — because their herds had multiplied across Gilead.

[10] In Saul's days they made war on the Hagrites, broke them, and took over their tents across the whole eastern stretch of Gilead.

[11] Gad's sons lived across from them in Bashan, reaching to Salecah: [12] Joel the chief, Shapham second, then Janai and Shaphat in Bashan. [13] Their kinsmen, by their fathers' houses: Michael, Meshullam, Sheba, Jorai, Jacan, Zia, Eber — seven of them. [14] These were sons of Abihail son of Huri son of Jaroah son of Gilead son of Michael son of Jeshishai son of Jahdo son of Buz. [15] Ahi son of Abdiel son of Guni was the head of their clan. [16] They lived in Gilead, in Bashan and its towns, and across all the pastureland of Sharon to its limits.

[17] All of them were registered in the days of Jotham king of Judah and Jeroboam king of Israel.

[18] The Reubenites, the Gadites, and half the tribe of Manasseh fielded fierce men — shield and sword carriers, archers, trained for war: forty-four thousand seven hundred and sixty soldiers ready to march. [19] They went to war against the Hagrites — against Jetur, Naphish, and Nodab. [20] God came through for them. The Hagrites and everyone with them were handed over, because in the heat of battle these men cried out to God and trusted him — and he answered. [21] They captured the enemy's livestock: fifty thousand camels, two hundred fifty thousand sheep, two thousand donkeys — plus a hundred thousand people. [22] Many fell dead, because the war was God's. And they lived in that land in place of the conquered, until the exile.

[23] The half-tribe of Manasseh spread out and filled the land from Bashan to Baal-hermon and Senir and Mount Hermon — and they grew vast in number. [24] These were the heads of their clans: Epher, Ishi, Eliel, Azriel, Jeremiah, Hodaviah, Jahdiel — fighters, men of reputation, leaders of their houses.

[25] But they broke faith with the God of their fathers. They prostituted themselves to the gods of the peoples of the land — the very peoples God had wiped out before them. [26] So the God of Israel stirred up the spirit of Pul king of Assyria — that is, Tiglath-Pileser — and he carried them off: the Reubenites, the Gadites, the half-tribe of Manasseh. He hauled them to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river of Gozan — where they remain to this day.

[1] Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, Merari.

[2] Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel.

[3] Amram's children: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam. Aaron's sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar.

[4] Eleazar fathered Phinehas. Phinehas fathered Abishua.

[5] Abishua fathered Bukki. Bukki fathered Uzzi.

[6] Uzzi fathered Zerahiah. Zerahiah fathered Meraioth.

[7] Meraioth fathered Amariah. Amariah fathered Ahitub.

[8] Ahitub fathered Zadok. Zadok fathered Ahimaaz.

[9] Ahimaaz fathered Azariah. Azariah fathered Johanan.

[10] Johanan fathered Azariah — the one who served as priest in the temple Solomon built in Jerusalem.

[11] Azariah fathered Amariah. Amariah fathered Ahitub.

[12] Ahitub fathered Zadok. Zadok fathered Shallum.

[13] Shallum fathered Hilkiah. Hilkiah fathered Azariah.

[14] Azariah fathered Seraiah. Seraiah fathered Jehozadak —

[15] and Jehozadak was carried off the day the LORD sent Judah and Jerusalem into exile by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.

[16] Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, Merari.

[17] Gershon's sons by name: Libni and Shimei.

[18] Kohath's sons: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel.

[19] Merari's sons: Mahli and Mushi. These are the Levite clans, traced through their fathers.

[20] From Gershon: Libni his son, Jahath his son, Zimmah his son,

[21] Joah his son, Iddo his son, Zerah his son, Jeatherai his son.

[22] From Kohath: Amminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son,

[23] Elkanah his son, Ebiasaph his son, Assir his son,

[24] Tahath his son, Uriel his son, Uzziah his son, Shaul his son.

[25] Elkanah's sons: Amasai and Ahimoth.

[26] Elkanah — his line ran: Elkanah, Zophai his son, Nahath his son,

[27] Eliab his son, Jeroham his son, Elkanah his son.

[28] Samuel's sons: the firstborn Joel, and Abijah the second.

[29] From Merari: Mahli, Libni his son, Shimei his son, Uzzah his son,

[30] Shimea his son, Haggiah his son, Asaiah his son.

[31] These are the men David put in charge of music in the LORD's house once the ark came to rest there.

[32] They led worship with song at the tent of meeting — the dwelling — until Solomon built the LORD's temple in Jerusalem. They stood at their posts and did their work by the book.

[33] Here are the men who served, and their sons:

From the Kohathites — Heman, the singer, son of Joel, son of Samuel,

[34] son of Elkanah, son of Jeroham, son of Eliel, son of Toah,

[35] son of Zuph, son of Elkanah, son of Mahath, son of Amasai,

[36] son of Elkanah, son of Joel, son of Azariah, son of Zephaniah,

[37] son of Tahath, son of Assir, son of Ebiasaph, son of Korah,

[38] son of Izhar, son of Kohath, son of Levi, son of Israel.

[39] At Heman's right hand stood his kinsman Asaph: Asaph son of Berekiah, son of Shimea,

[40] son of Michael, son of Baaseiah, son of Malkijah,

[41] son of Ethni, son of Zerah, son of Adaiah,

[42] son of Ethan, son of Zimmah, son of Shimei,

[43] son of Jahath, son of Gershon, son of Levi.

[44] At his left hand, their kinsmen the sons of Merari: Ethan son of Kishi, son of Abdi, son of Malluch,

[45] son of Hashabiah, son of Amaziah, son of Hilkiah,

[46] son of Amzi, son of Bani, son of Shemer,

[47] son of Mahli, son of Mushi, son of Merari, son of Levi.

[48] The rest of their Levite brothers were assigned every other task in the dwelling — the house of God.

[49] But Aaron and his sons — they were the ones who sent smoke up from the altar of burnt offering and the altar of incense, who handled every rite of the Most Holy Place, who made atonement for Israel, exactly as Moses, God's servant, had commanded.

[50] Aaron's line: Eleazar his son, Phinehas his son, Abishua his son,

[51] Bukki his son, Uzzi his son, Zerahiah his son,

[52] Meraioth his son, Amariah his son, Ahitub his son,

[53] Zadok his son, Ahimaaz his son.

[54] Now their settlements — their tent camps within their borders. To Aaron's descendants, the Kohathite clan, since the first lot fell to them:

[55] they got Hebron in Judah, with its surrounding pasturelands.

[56] But the farmland of the city and its villages went to Caleb son of Jephunneh.

[57] So Aaron's sons received the cities of refuge: Hebron, Libnah with its pastures, Jattir, Eshtemoa with its pastures,

[58] Hilen with its pastures, Debir with its pastures,

[59] Ashan with its pastures, and Beth Shemesh with its pastures.

[60] From the tribe of Benjamin: Geba with its pastures, Alemeth with its pastures, Anathoth with its pastures. Thirteen cities in all, distributed among their clans.

[61] The remaining Kohathites drew their ten cities by lot from half the tribe of Manasseh.

[62] The Gershonites, clan by clan, drew thirteen cities from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan.

[63] The Merarites, clan by clan, drew twelve cities by lot from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.

[64] So the Israelites gave the Levites these cities and their pasturelands.

[65] They allotted by lot — from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin — these cities listed by name.

[66] As for some of the Kohathite clans, the cities of their territory came from the tribe of Ephraim:

[67] they received the cities of refuge — Shechem with its pastures in the hill country of Ephraim, Gezer with its pastures,

[68] Jokmeam with its pastures, Beth Horon with its pastures,

[69] Aijalon with its pastures, Gath Rimmon with its pastures.

[70] From the half-tribe of Manasseh: Aner with its pastures and Bileam with its pastures — for the rest of the Kohathite clan.

[71] To the Gershonites: from the clan of the half-tribe of Manasseh, Golan in Bashan with its pastures, and Ashtaroth with its pastures.

[72] From Issachar: Kedesh with its pastures, Daberath with its pastures,

[73] Ramoth with its pastures, and Anem with its pastures.

[74] From Asher: Mashal with its pastures, Abdon with its pastures,

[75] Hukok with its pastures, and Rehob with its pastures.

[76] From Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee with its pastures, Hammon with its pastures, and Kiriathaim with its pastures.

[77] To the rest of the Merarites: from Zebulun, Rimmono with its pastures, and Tabor with its pastures.

[78] Across the Jordan east of Jericho, on the far side of the Jordan: from the tribe of Reuben, Bezer in the wilderness with its pastures, Jahzah with its pastures,

[79] Kedemoth with its pastures, and Mephaath with its pastures.

[80] From Gad: Ramoth in Gilead with its pastures, Mahanaim with its pastures,

[81] Heshbon with its pastures, and Jazer with its pastures.

[1] Issachar had four sons: Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron.

[2] Tola's sons—Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Shemuel—were chiefs of their father's house, fighting men of the first rank, each one a head of his line. In David's day their muster rolled out at twenty-two thousand six hundred.

[3] Uzzi's son was Izrahiah. Izrahiah's sons—Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah—five chiefs, all of them.

[4] Their lineage carried with it war-bands totaling thirty-six thousand troops, because they kept producing sons and wives in abundance.

[5] Across all the clans of Issachar, the warriors on the rolls numbered eighty-seven thousand—every one a fighter of standing.

[6] Benjamin had three sons: Bela, Becher, and Jediael.

[7] Bela's sons—Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri—five chiefs of their father's house, hardened fighters. Their genealogy mustered twenty-two thousand thirty-four.

[8] Becher's sons: Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, and Alemeth—all of them Becher's.

[9] Their lineage by household and chief, all fighting men, came to twenty thousand two hundred.

[10] Jediael's son was Bilhan. Bilhan's sons: Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Chenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, and Ahishahar—

[11] all sons of Jediael, heads of their fathers' houses, seventeen thousand two hundred warriors fit to march out for war.

[12] Shuppim and Huppim were sons of Ir; Hushim was a son of Aher.

[13] Naphtali's sons: Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, and Shallum—Bilhah's descendants.

[14] Manasseh's sons: Asriel, born to him by his Aramean concubine, who also bore Machir, father of Gilead.

[15] Machir took a wife from among Huppim and Shuppim; his sister was Maacah. The second son was named Zelophehad—and Zelophehad had only daughters.

[16] Maacah, Machir's wife, bore a son and named him Peresh. His brother was Sheresh, whose sons were Ulam and Rakem.

[17] Ulam's son was Bedan. These were the descendants of Gilead son of Machir son of Manasseh.

[18] His sister Hammolecheth bore Ishhod, Abiezer, and Mahlah.

[19] Shemida's sons: Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam.

[20] Ephraim's sons: Shuthelah, his son Bered, his son Tahath, his son Eleadah, his son Tahath,

[21] his son Zabad, his son Shuthelah—and then Ezer and Elead, killed by the men of Gath, native-born, when they came down to raid their cattle.

[22] Ephraim their father mourned them for days on end, and his kin came to console him.

[23] He went in to his wife; she conceived and bore a son, and he called him Beriah—*disaster*—because tragedy had walked through his house.

[24] His daughter was Sheerah. She built Lower Beth-horon, Upper Beth-horon, and Uzzen-sheerah.

[25] His son was Rephah, then Resheph, his son Telah, his son Tahan,

[26] his son Ladan, his son Ammihud, his son Elishama,

[27] his son Nun, his son Joshua.

[28] Their holdings and settlements ran through Bethel and its surrounding towns, eastward to Naaran, westward to Gezer and its towns, then Shechem and its towns, all the way to Ayyah and its towns.

[29] Along the border with Manasseh sat Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, Dor—each with its towns. In these lived the sons of Joseph son of Israel.

[30] Asher's sons: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah—and Serah their sister.

[31] Beriah's sons: Heber and Malchiel, who fathered Birzaith.

[32] Heber fathered Japhlet, Shomer, Hotham—and Shua their sister.

[33] Japhlet's sons: Pasach, Bimhal, and Ashvath. These were Japhlet's.

[34] Shomer's sons: Ahi, Rohgah, Hubbah, and Aram.

[35] His brother Helem's sons: Zophah, Imna, Shelesh, and Amal.

[36] Zophah's sons: Suah, Harnepher, Shual, Beri, Imrah,

[37] Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera.

[38] Jether's sons: Jephunneh, Pispa, and Ara.

[39] Ulla's sons: Arah, Hanniel, and Rizia.

[40] All these were sons of Asher—heads of their fathers' houses, picked men, warriors of the first order, chiefs among chiefs. Enrolled for war, their fighting roster came to twenty-six thousand men.

[1] Benjamin fathered Bela his firstborn, Ashbel second, Aharah third, [2] Nohah fourth, Rapha fifth.

[3] Bela's sons: Addar, Gera, Abihud, [4] Abishua, Naaman, Ahoah, [5] Gera, Shephuphan, Huram.

[6] These are the sons of Ehud — heads of clans living in Geba, later deported to Manahath: [7] Naaman, Ahijah, and Gera — Gera was the one who deported them; he fathered Uzza and Ahihud.

[8] Shaharaim fathered children in the territory of Moab after he sent away his wives Hushim and Baara. [9] By his wife Hodesh he fathered Jobab, Zibia, Mesha, Malcam, [10] Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah. These were his sons — clan chiefs. [11] By Hushim he had fathered Abitub and Elpaal.

[12] Elpaal's sons: Eber, Misham, Shemed (who built Ono and Lod with its surrounding villages), [13] and Beriah and Shema. These two were clan heads in Aijalon — they're the ones who drove out the inhabitants of Gath.

[14] Ahio, Shashak, Jeremoth, [15] Zebadiah, Arad, Eder, [16] Michael, Ishpah, and Joha — sons of Beriah.

[17] Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hizki, Heber, [18] Ishmerai, Izliah, and Jobab — sons of Elpaal.

[19] Jakim, Zichri, Zabdi, [20] Elienai, Zillethai, Eliel, [21] Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath — sons of Shimei.

[22] Ishpan, Eber, Eliel, [23] Abdon, Zichri, Hanan, [24] Hananiah, Elam, Anthothijah, [25] Iphdeiah, and Penuel — sons of Shashak.

[26] Shamsherai, Shehariah, Athaliah, [27] Jaareshiah, Elijah, and Zichri — sons of Jeroham.

[28] These were heads of clans by their generations — chiefs. They lived in Jerusalem.

[29] In Gibeon lived Jeiel, the father of Gibeon — his wife's name was Maacah — [30] and his firstborn son Abdon, then Zur, Kish, Baal, Nadab, [31] Gedor, Ahio, Zecher, [32] and Mikloth, who fathered Shimeah. These too lived near their kin in Jerusalem, alongside them.

[33] Ner fathered Kish, Kish fathered Saul, and Saul fathered Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Eshbaal.

[34] Jonathan's son: Merib-baal. Merib-baal fathered Micah. [35] Micah's sons: Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz.

[36] Ahaz fathered Jehoaddah. Jehoaddah fathered Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri. Zimri fathered Moza. [37] Moza fathered Binea. His son was Raphah, his son Eleasah, his son Azel.

[38] Azel had six sons, and these are their names: Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. All of them — sons of Azel.

[39] His brother Eshek's sons: Ulam the firstborn, Jeush second, Eliphelet third. [40] Ulam's sons were soldiers — formidable men, archers — and they had many sons and grandsons, one hundred fifty in all.

All of these were the sons of Benjamin.

[1] Every Israelite was registered, name by name, in the royal record of the kings of Israel. Then Judah was hauled off to Babylon — exiled for their faithlessness.

[2] The first to return and resettle their own land, their own towns, were ordinary Israelites along with priests, Levites, and the temple servants.

[3] In Jerusalem itself, families from Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh took up residence:

[4] From Judah: Uthai son of Ammihud, son of Omri, son of Imri, son of Bani — descendants of Perez son of Judah.

[5] From the Shilonites: Asaiah the firstborn, and his sons.

[6] From the line of Zerah: Jeuel — and their relatives, six hundred and ninety in all.

[7] From Benjamin: Sallu son of Meshullam, son of Hodaviah, son of Hassenuah;

[8] Ibneiah son of Jeroham; Elah son of Uzzi, son of Michri; Meshullam son of Shephatiah, son of Reuel, son of Ibnijah —

[9] their relatives by lineage, nine hundred and fifty-six. Every man named was the head of his ancestral house.

[10] From the priests: Jedaiah, Jehoiarib, Jachin,

[11] and Azariah son of Hilkiah, son of Meshullam, son of Zadok, son of Meraioth, son of Ahitub — the chief officer of God's house.

[12] Also Adaiah son of Jeroham, son of Pashhur, son of Malchijah; and Maasai son of Adiel, son of Jahzerah, son of Meshullam, son of Meshillemith, son of Immer —

[13] and their kin, heads of their ancestral houses, one thousand seven hundred and sixty — men of substance, capable hands for the work of God's house.

[14] From the Levites: Shemaiah son of Hasshub, son of Azrikam, son of Hashabiah, of the line of Merari;

[15] Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, and Mattaniah son of Mica, son of Zichri, son of Asaph;

[16] Obadiah son of Shemaiah, son of Galal, son of Jeduthun; and Berechiah son of Asa, son of Elkanah, who lived in the villages of the Netophathites.

[17] The gatekeepers: Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman, and their kin — Shallum being the chief.

[18] To this day they station themselves at the King's Gate on the east. These were the gatekeepers of the camp of the Levites.

[19] Shallum son of Kore, son of Ebiasaph, son of Korah — together with his clansmen of the house of Korah — handled the work of the service: guarding the thresholds of the Tent, just as their fathers had guarded the entrance to the camp of the LORD.

[20] Phinehas son of Eleazar had been their leader in earlier days, and the LORD was with him.

[21] Zechariah son of Meshelemiah was gatekeeper at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting.

[22] All of those chosen as gatekeepers at the thresholds came to two hundred and twelve. They were enrolled by lineage in their own villages. David and Samuel the seer had personally appointed them to their trusted posts.

[23] So they and their descendants stood guard over the gates of the LORD's house — the house that had been the Tent — assigned watch by watch.

[24] The gatekeepers were posted on all four sides: east, west, north, and south.

[25] Their relatives, who lived in their villages, would come in for seven-day rotations to serve alongside them,

[26] while the four chief gatekeepers — Levites in permanent positions of trust — were responsible for the chambers and the treasuries of God's house.

[27] They spent the nights stationed around God's house, because the watch was their charge — and they held the key for the morning opening.

[28] Some were in charge of the worship vessels: every piece counted in and counted out.

[29] Others were assigned to the furniture and the sacred utensils — the fine flour, the wine, the oil, the frankincense, and the spices.

[30] But the actual blending of the spice ointments was done by some of the sons of the priests.

[31] Mattithiah, a Levite — the firstborn of Shallum the Korahite — was entrusted with what was baked in pans.

[32] Some of their Kohathite kin oversaw the bread of the Presence, preparing it Sabbath after Sabbath.

[33] The singers — heads of Levitical families — lived in the chambers and were exempt from other duties, because they were on call day and night.

[34] These were the heads of the Levitical families by lineage, the leading men. They lived in Jerusalem.

[35] In Gibeon lived Jeiel, father of Gibeon, whose wife was Maacah.

[36] His firstborn son was Abdon, followed by Zur, Kish, Baal, Ner, Nadab,

[37] Gedor, Ahio, Zechariah, and Mikloth.

[38] Mikloth fathered Shimeam. They too lived alongside their kin in Jerusalem.

[39] Ner fathered Kish, Kish fathered Saul, and Saul fathered Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Esh-baal.

[40] Jonathan's son was Merib-baal, and Merib-baal fathered Micah.

[41] Micah's sons: Pithon, Melech, Tahrea, and Ahaz.

[42] Ahaz fathered Jarah; Jarah fathered Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri; Zimri fathered Moza;

[43] Moza fathered Binea; his son Rephaiah, his son Eleasah, his son Azel.

[44] Azel had six sons, and their names were Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. These were Azel's sons.

[1] The Philistines came at Israel hard. Israel broke and ran, and the bodies piled up on Mount Gilboa.

[2] The Philistines locked onto Saul and his sons. They cut down Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua — Saul's own.

[3] The fighting closed in around Saul. The archers found him, and the arrows tore into him. He was in agony.

[4] Saul turned to his armor-bearer: "Pull your sword. Run me through. I will not let those uncircumcised animals take me alive and make sport of me." But the armor-bearer froze — he was terrified. So Saul took his own sword and fell on it.

[5] When the armor-bearer saw Saul dead, he fell on his sword too, and died beside him.

[6] So Saul died. His three sons died. His whole house went down together on that one day.

[7] When the Israelites in the valley saw that the army had collapsed and that Saul and his sons were dead, they abandoned their towns and fled. The Philistines moved in and took over.

[8] The next day the Philistines came to strip the dead, and they found Saul and his sons sprawled on Mount Gilboa.

[9] They stripped him, took his head and his armor, and sent runners across Philistine territory to broadcast the good news to their idols and their people.

[10] They mounted his armor in the temple of their gods. His skull they nailed up in the house of Dagon.

[11] But when the people of Jabesh-gilead heard what the Philistines had done to Saul —

[12] every fighting man among them rose up, traveled through the night, took down the bodies of Saul and his sons from the wall of Beth-shan, carried them home to Jabesh, and buried their bones under the oak there. Then they fasted seven days.

[13] Saul died because he was unfaithful to the LORD. He did not keep the LORD's word. He even sought out a medium for guidance —

[14] he did not seek the LORD. So the LORD killed him and handed the kingdom over to David, son of Jesse.

[1] Then all Israel converged on David at Hebron and said, "Look — we are your bone and your flesh. [2] Even back when Saul was king, you were the one leading Israel out and bringing them home. And the LORD your God told you: 'You will shepherd my people Israel. You will be their ruler.'"

[3] So every elder of Israel came to the king at Hebron, and David cut a covenant with them there before the LORD. They anointed him king over Israel — exactly as the LORD had said through Samuel.

[4] David and all Israel marched on Jerusalem — Jebus, as it was then called — where the Jebusites held the land. [5] The locals taunted David: "You will never get in here." But David took the stronghold of Zion anyway. It became the City of David.

[6] David had declared: "Whoever strikes down a Jebusite first will be chief and commander." Joab son of Zeruiah went up first — and he got the post.

[7] David made the stronghold his home, which is why they named it the City of David. [8] He built up the city in every direction, from the Millo outward, and Joab restored the rest.

[9] David grew greater and greater, because the LORD of Hosts was with him.

[10] These are the chiefs of David's warriors — the men who threw their full strength behind him in his kingdom, who joined with all Israel to make him king, just as the LORD had promised concerning Israel.

[11] Here is the roster of David's mighty men:

Jashobeam son of a Hachmonite, chief of the Thirty. He lifted his spear against three hundred men and killed them in a single engagement.

[12] After him, Eleazar son of Dodo the Ahohite — one of the three mightiest. [13] He stood with David at Pas-dammim when the Philistines massed there for battle. The ground was a field of barley. The army broke and ran from the Philistines, [14] but he and David planted themselves in the middle of that field, defended it, and cut the Philistines down. The LORD delivered them — a stunning rescue.

[15] Three of the thirty chiefs went down to David at the cave of Adullam while the Philistine army camped in the Valley of Rephaim. [16] David was in the stronghold then, and a Philistine garrison held Bethlehem. [17] David said, aching with thirst: "If only someone would bring me a drink from the well by the gate of Bethlehem."

[18] So the Three broke through the Philistine camp, drew water from the well at Bethlehem's gate, and carried it back to David. But David refused to drink it. He poured it out to the LORD [19] and said, "God forbid I should do this. Shall I drink the blood of these men who risked their lives? They brought it at the cost of their lives." So he would not drink it.

These were the deeds of the Three.

[20] Abishai, Joab's brother, was chief of the Thirty. He lifted his spear against three hundred and killed them. He made a name alongside the Three. [21] He was the most honored of the Thirty — their commander — but he did not reach the Three.

[22] Benaiah son of Jehoiada, from Kabzeel, was a man of action — a doer of mighty deeds. He struck down the two sons of Ariel of Moab. He climbed down into a pit on a snowy day and killed a lion. [23] He struck down an Egyptian — a giant of a man, seven and a half feet tall, with a spear like a weaver's beam in his hand. Benaiah went at him with only a club, ripped the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and killed him with his own weapon. [24] These were the deeds of Benaiah son of Jehoiada. He made a name alongside the three mighty men. [25] He was honored above the Thirty, but he did not reach the Three. David put him in charge of his bodyguard.

[26] The mighty warriors:

Asahel brother of Joab, Elhanan son of Dodo of Bethlehem, [27] Shammoth the Harorite, Helez the Pelonite, [28] Ira son of Ikkesh of Tekoa, Abiezer of Anathoth, [29] Sibbecai the Hushathite, Ilai the Ahohite, [30] Maharai of Netophah, Heled son of Baanah of Netophah, [31] Ithai son of Ribai from Gibeah of the Benjaminites, Benaiah the Pirathonite, [32] Hurai from the wadis of Gaash, Abiel the Arbathite, [33] Azmaveth of Baharum, Eliahba the Shaalbonite, [34] the sons of Hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan son of Shagee the Hararite, [35] Ahiam son of Sacar the Hararite, Eliphal son of Ur, [36] Hepher the Mecherathite, Ahijah the Pelonite, [37] Hezro of Carmel, Naarai son of Ezbai, [38] Joel brother of Nathan, Mibhar son of Hagri, [39] Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai of Beeroth (armor-bearer to Joab son of Zeruiah), [40] Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite, [41] Uriah the Hittite, Zabad son of Ahlai, [42] Adina son of Shiza the Reubenite — a chief of the Reubenites — with thirty under him, [43] Hanan son of Maacah, Joshaphat the Mithnite, [44] Uzzia the Ashterathite, Shama and Jeiel the sons of Hotham the Aroerite, [45] Jediael son of Shimri and his brother Joha the Tizite, [46] Eliel the Mahavite, Jeribai and Joshaviah the sons of Elnaam, Ithmah the Moabite, [47] Eliel and Obed and Jaasiel the Mezobaite.

[1] These are the men who joined David at Ziklag while he was still cut off from Saul son of Kish — warriors who stood with him in battle, [2] armed with bows, able to sling stones and shoot arrows with either hand. They were Saul's own kin from Benjamin.

[3] Ahiezer was their chief, then Joash — both sons of Shemaah from Gibeah; Jeziel and Pelet, sons of Azmaveth; Beracah; Jehu from Anathoth; [4] Ishmaiah from Gibeon, a warrior among the Thirty and a leader over them; Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, and Jozabad from Gederah; [5] Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah from Haruph; [6] Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam — Korahites; [7] and Joelah and Zebadiah, sons of Jeroham from Gedor.

[8] From the Gadites, men defected to David at his stronghold in the wilderness — battle-hardened soldiers trained for war, skilled with shield and spear. Their faces were lion-faces; they ran the ridges like gazelles.

[9] Ezer led them, Obadiah second, Eliab third, [10] Mishmannah fourth, Jeremiah fifth, [11] Attai sixth, Eliel seventh, [12] Johanan eighth, Elzabad ninth, [13] Jeremiah tenth, Machbannai eleventh. [14] These Gadites were officers — the least of them worth a hundred men, the greatest worth a thousand. [15] They were the ones who crossed the Jordan in the first month when it was overflowing its banks, and they scattered everyone in the valleys, east and west.

[16] Then men from Benjamin and Judah also came to David at the stronghold. [17] David went out to meet them and said plainly: "If you've come in peace, to help me, then my heart is bound to yours. But if you've come to betray me to my enemies — though there's no violence in my hands — let the God of our fathers see it and judge."

[18] Then the Spirit clothed Amasai, chief of the Thirty, and he said:

"We are yours, David —
with you, son of Jesse.
Peace, peace to you,
and peace to whoever helps you,
because your God is your help."

So David received them and made them officers in his raiding band.

[19] Some from Manasseh also crossed over to David when he marched out with the Philistines against Saul — though in the end he did not fight for them. The Philistine rulers consulted and sent him back, saying, "He'll defect to his master Saul at the cost of our heads." [20] As he was traveling back to Ziklag, these men from Manasseh joined him: Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, Jozabad, Elihu, and Zillethai — commanders of thousands in Manasseh. [21] They fought alongside David against the raiding bands, every one of them a fierce warrior, every one a commander in the army. [22] Day after day men kept coming to David to help him, until the camp swelled into a vast army — like the army of God.

[23] These are the numbers of the armed troops who came to David at Hebron to turn Saul's kingdom over to him, as the LORD had said:

[24] From Judah, carrying shield and spear — 6,800, ready for war.

[25] From Simeon, fighting men ready for battle — 7,100.

[26] From Levi — 4,600, [27] with Jehoiada, leader of the house of Aaron, and 3,700 with him, [28] and Zadok, a young warrior, and twenty-two officers from his father's house.

[29] From Benjamin, Saul's own tribe — 3,000, though most of them had stayed loyal to the house of Saul until now.

[30] From Ephraim — 20,800, fighting men, famous in their clans.

[31] From the half-tribe of Manasseh — 18,000, named by name to come and make David king.

[32] From Issachar — 200 chiefs who knew how to read the times and what Israel should do, with all their kin under their command.

[33] From Zebulun — 50,000 seasoned soldiers, equipped with every weapon of war, marching in formation with undivided hearts.

[34] From Naphtali — 1,000 commanders, and with them 37,000 carrying shield and spear.

[35] From Dan, ready for battle — 28,600.

[36] From Asher, seasoned soldiers ready for battle — 40,000.

[37] From across the Jordan — Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh — 120,000 armed with every weapon of war.

[38] All these warriors, drawn up in battle order, came to Hebron with one heart, one purpose: to make David king over all Israel. And the rest of Israel was of one mind on the matter too. [39] They stayed there with David three days, eating and drinking, because their kin had laid in provisions for them. [40] And the people nearby — even from as far as Issachar, Zebulun, and Naphtali — kept bringing in food on donkeys, camels, mules, and oxen: flour, fig cakes, raisin cakes, wine, oil, cattle, sheep — abundance heaped on abundance. Joy ran through Israel like a river.

[1] David huddled with his commanders — the officers over thousands and hundreds, every leader he had.

[2] Then he stood before the whole assembly of Israel and said: "If this seems right to you, and if it's from the LORD our God, let's send word — far and wide — to the rest of our brothers across every territory of Israel, and to the priests and Levites in their towns and pasturelands. Tell them to come. Gather to us.

[3] Let's bring back the ark of our God. We didn't bother with it during Saul's reign."

[4] The whole assembly agreed. The plan was right in every eye.

[5] So David rallied all Israel — from the Shihor in Egypt all the way to Lebo-hamath — to bring the ark of God home from Kiriath-jearim.

[6] David and all Israel marched up to Baalah — that is, Kiriath-jearim in Judah — to retrieve the ark of God, the LORD enthroned above the cherubim, the ark that bears his name.

[7] They loaded the ark of God onto a new cart and rolled it out of Abinadab's house. Uzzah and Ahio steered the cart.

[8] David and all Israel danced before God with everything they had — songs, lyres, harps, tambourines, cymbals, trumpets — full-throated celebration.

[9] But when they reached the threshing floor of Chidon, the oxen stumbled. Uzzah threw out his hand to steady the ark.

[10] And the LORD's anger flared against Uzzah. He struck him down — right there, for laying hands on the ark. He died before God.

[11] David burned — angry that the LORD had ripped through Uzzah like that. The place still bears the name: Perez-uzzah. The Breaking-of-Uzzah.

[12] Fear of God seized David that day. He said, "How can I ever bring the ark of God home to me?"

[13] So David did not take the ark with him into the City of David. He rerouted it to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite.

[14] The ark of God stayed there — three months — with Obed-edom, in his house. And the LORD blessed Obed-edom's household and everything he owned.

[1] Hiram, king of Tyre, sent envoys to David — along with cedar logs, masons, and carpenters — to build him a palace.

[2] That's when David knew, with full certainty: the Lord had locked him in as king over Israel. His throne had been lifted high for the sake of His people.

[3] In Jerusalem, David took more wives, and they bore him more sons and daughters.

[4] Here are the names of the children born to him there: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,

[5] Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet,

[6] Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia,

[7] Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet.

[8] When the Philistines caught wind that David had been anointed king over all Israel, every last one of them mobilized to hunt him down. David heard they were coming — and he marched out to meet them.

[9] The Philistines had already arrived, sprawling across the Valley of Rephaim, raiding.

[10] David asked God: "Should I go up against the Philistines? Will you hand them over to me?" The Lord answered: "Go. I will deliver them into your hand."

[11] So David went up to Baal-perazim, and there he cut them down. Then he said, "God has burst through my enemies by my hand like floodwaters bursting a dam." That's how the place got its name — Baal-perazim, the Lord of the Breakthrough.

[12] The Philistines abandoned their gods there in the field, and David ordered them burned.

[13] But the Philistines came back. Again they spread out through the valley, raiding.

[14] David asked God again — and this time God said: "Don't go straight at them. Circle around behind. Hit them from the direction of the balsam trees.

[15] When you hear marching in the tops of the balsams — that's your signal. Move. Because that's God going out ahead of you to strike the Philistine army."

[16] David did exactly what God told him. And they cut down the Philistine army all the way from Gibeon to Gezer.

[17] David's name spread through every land, and the Lord made every nation tremble at the sound of him.

[1] David built houses for himself in the City of David, then prepared a place for the Ark of God and pitched a tent for it.

[2] David declared: "No one carries the Ark of God except the Levites. The LORD chose them to carry it and to serve him forever."

[3] David gathered all Israel to Jerusalem to bring the Ark of the LORD up to the place he had prepared for it.

[4] He assembled the descendants of Aaron and the Levites:

[5] From the Kohathites — Uriel the chief and a hundred and twenty of his kin;

[6] from the Merarites — Asaiah the chief and two hundred and twenty of his kin;

[7] from the Gershomites — Joel the chief and a hundred and thirty of his kin;

[8] from the Elizaphanites — Shemaiah the chief and two hundred of his kin;

[9] from the Hebronites — Eliel the chief and eighty of his kin;

[10] from the Uzzielites — Amminadab the chief and a hundred and twelve of his kin.

[11] David summoned Zadok and Abiathar the priests, along with the Levites Uriel, Asaiah, Joel, Shemaiah, Eliel, and Amminadab.

[12] He told them: "You are the heads of the Levitical families. Consecrate yourselves — you and your kin — and bring the Ark of the LORD, the God of Israel, up to the place I have prepared for it.

[13] The first time, we didn't do this right — and the LORD our God broke out against us, because we didn't seek him about the proper way."

[14] So the priests and Levites consecrated themselves to bring up the Ark of the LORD, the God of Israel.

[15] And the Levites carried the Ark of God on their shoulders, with the poles on them, just as Moses had commanded by the word of the LORD.

[16] David ordered the chiefs of the Levites to appoint their kin as singers — to sound out with instruments, harps and lyres and cymbals, lifting their voices in joy.

[17] So the Levites appointed Heman son of Joel; from his kin Asaph son of Berechiah; and from the Merarites their kin Ethan son of Kushaiah.

[18] With them were their kin of the second rank: Zechariah, Jaaziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, and the gatekeepers Obed-edom and Jeiel.

[19] Heman, Asaph, and Ethan — the singers — sounded the bronze cymbals.

[20] Zechariah, Aziel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Unni, Eliab, Maaseiah, and Benaiah played harps tuned to Alamoth.

[21] Mattithiah, Eliphelehu, Mikneiah, Obed-edom, Jeiel, and Azaziah led with lyres tuned to Sheminith.

[22] Chenaniah, chief of the Levites in music, directed the singing — he was a master of it.

[23] Berechiah and Elkanah were gatekeepers for the Ark.

[24] The priests Shebaniah, Joshaphat, Nethanel, Amasai, Zechariah, Benaiah, and Eliezer blew trumpets before the Ark of God. Obed-edom and Jehiah also served as gatekeepers for the Ark.

[25] So David, the elders of Israel, and the commanders of thousands went to bring up the Ark of the covenant of the LORD from the house of Obed-edom — and they went with joy.

[26] Because God was helping the Levites who carried the Ark, they sacrificed seven bulls and seven rams.

[27] David was robed in fine linen, as were all the Levites carrying the Ark, the singers, and Chenaniah the music director. David also wore a linen ephod.

[28] So all Israel brought the Ark of the covenant of the LORD up with shouting, with the blast of the ram's horn and trumpets, with cymbals, harps, and lyres — sound thundering everywhere.

[29] But as the Ark of the covenant of the LORD entered the City of David, Michal daughter of Saul looked down from her window — and there was David, leaping and dancing. And in her heart she despised him.

[1] They brought the Ark of God in and set it inside the tent David had pitched for it. Then they offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before God. [2] When David finished offering the burnt offerings and peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of the LORD. [3] He distributed to every Israelite — man and woman alike — a loaf of bread, a date cake, and a raisin cake.

[4] He appointed Levites to minister before the Ark of the LORD — to invoke, to thank, and to praise the LORD, the God of Israel. [5] Asaph led, with Zechariah second, then Jeiel, Shemiramoth, Jehiel, Mattithiah, Eliab, Benaiah, Obed-edom, and Jeiel — with harps and lyres, while Asaph crashed the cymbals. [6] The priests Benaiah and Jahaziel sounded trumpets continually before the Ark of the Covenant of God.

[7] That day — that very day — David first handed Asaph and his brothers this song of thanks to the LORD:

[8] Thank the LORD! Call out his name!
Tell the nations what he has done.
[9] Sing to him, make music for him,
turn his wonders into stories on every tongue.
[10] Glory in his holy name —
let every heart that seeks the LORD light up.
[11] Hunt for the LORD and his strength,
seek his face — never stop.
[12] Remember the wonders he worked,
his signs, the verdicts from his mouth,
[13] you children of Israel his servant,
sons of Jacob, his chosen ones.

[14] He is the LORD our God.
His judgments cover the earth.
[15] He remembers his covenant forever —
the word he commanded to a thousand generations —
[16] the covenant he cut with Abraham,
his oath to Isaac,
[17] confirmed to Jacob as decree,
to Israel as covenant unbreakable:
[18] "I will give you the land of Canaan —
the portion that belongs to you."

[19] When you were few — barely a handful —
strangers passing through the land,
[20] wandering from nation to nation,
from one kingdom to another people,
[21] he let no one crush you.
For your sake he rebuked kings:
[22] "Hands off my anointed ones!
Do my prophets no harm!"

[23] Sing to the LORD, all the earth!
Day after day announce his salvation.
[24] Declare his glory among the nations,
his marvels among every people.
[25] The LORD is great — supremely worthy of praise,
to be feared above every god.
[26] The gods of the nations are nothing but idols,
but the LORD made the heavens.
[27] Splendor and majesty stand before him;
strength and joy fill his sanctuary.

[28] Give to the LORD, you families of nations,
give to the LORD glory and strength.
[29] Give to the LORD the glory his name deserves.
Bring an offering, come into his presence.
Bow before the LORD in the splendor of holiness.
[30] Tremble before him, all the earth —
the world stands firm, it will not be shaken.
[31] Let the heavens be glad, let the earth rejoice;
let them say among the nations: "The LORD reigns!"
[32] Let the sea roar — and everything in it —
let the fields exult, and all that grows there.
[33] Then the trees of the forest will sing for joy
before the LORD — for he is coming to judge the earth.

[34] Thank the LORD, because he is good,
because his loyal love never quits.
[35] Cry out: "Save us, God of our salvation!
Gather us, rescue us from the nations,
that we may give thanks to your holy name
and glory in your praise."
[36] Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel,
from forever to forever.

And all the people said, "Amen!" — and they praised the LORD.

[37] David left Asaph and his brothers there before the Ark of the Covenant of the LORD, to minister continually before the Ark — each day's work on its day — [38] along with Obed-edom and his sixty-eight brothers. Obed-edom son of Jeduthun and Hosah served as gatekeepers.

[39] As for Zadok the priest and his fellow priests, David stationed them before the tabernacle of the LORD at the high place in Gibeon, [40] to offer burnt offerings to the LORD on the altar of burnt offering, morning and evening — everything written in the Law of the LORD that he had commanded Israel. [41] With them were Heman and Jeduthun and the rest chosen by name, designated to thank the LORD — "because his loyal love never quits." [42] Heman and Jeduthun had the trumpets and cymbals, and the instruments for sacred song. The sons of Jeduthun were stationed at the gate.

[43] Then all the people went home, and David turned back to bless his own household.

[1] Once David was settled into his palace, he said to Nathan the prophet, "Look at this — I'm living in a house of cedar, while the ark of the LORD's covenant sits under tent curtains."

[2] Nathan answered, "Whatever's on your heart, do it. God is with you."

[3] But that night the word of God came to Nathan:

[4] "Go tell my servant David: This is what the LORD says. You are not the one who will build me a house to live in.

[5] I have not lived in a house from the day I brought Israel up out of Egypt until now. I have moved from tent to tent, from one dwelling to another.

[6] In all my travels with Israel, did I ever once say to any of their leaders — the ones I commanded to shepherd my people — 'Why haven't you built me a house of cedar?'

[7] So tell my servant David: This is what the LORD of Armies says. I took you from the pasture, from following sheep, and made you ruler over my people Israel.

[8] I have been with you everywhere you've gone. I have cut down every enemy in front of you. And I will make your name great — as great as the greatest names on earth.

[9] I will plant my people Israel in their own place. They will put down roots. They will not be uprooted again. Violent men will not grind them down the way they used to,

[10] back in the days when I appointed judges over my people. I will subdue every one of your enemies. And here is what I am telling you: the LORD himself will build you a house.

[11] When your days are finished and you go to lie with your ancestors, I will raise up your offspring after you — one of your own sons — and I will establish his kingdom.

[12] He is the one who will build me a house. And I will establish his throne forever.

[13] I will be his father, and he will be my son. I will never strip my loyal love from him the way I stripped it from the one before you.

[14] I will set him over my house and my kingdom forever. His throne will stand, secured, for all time."

[15] Nathan reported every word of this vision back to David.

[16] Then King David went in, sat before the LORD, and said: "Who am I, LORD God, and what is my house, that you have brought me this far?

[17] And as if that weren't enough in your eyes, God, you have spoken about your servant's house far into the future. You have looked on me as if I were someone of high standing, LORD God.

[18] What more can David say to you for the honor you've given your servant? You know me through and through.

[19] LORD — for the sake of your servant, and because it is what your heart wanted, you have done this enormous thing and made known every great promise.

[20] There is no one like you, LORD. There is no God but you — everything we've ever heard confirms it.

[21] And who is like your people Israel? One nation on earth that God went out to redeem for himself — to make a name for yourself by doing great and terrifying wonders, driving out nations before your people, the ones you rescued from Egypt.

[22] You claimed Israel as your own people forever. And you, LORD, became their God.

[23] So now, LORD — let the word you spoke about your servant and his house stand firm forever. Do what you said you would do.

[24] Let it stand. Let your name be magnified forever, so people will say: 'The LORD of Armies, God of Israel — he is God to Israel.' And let the house of your servant David be established before you.

[25] You, my God, have whispered to your servant that you will build him a house. That's how I found the courage to pray this prayer.

[26] And now, LORD — you are God. You have made this good promise to your servant.

[27] So please — bless the house of your servant. Let it continue before you forever. What you bless, LORD, is blessed forever."

[1] After this, David struck the Philistines and broke them. He tore Gath and its surrounding villages from their grip.

[2] He struck Moab too, and the Moabites became his servants, hauling tribute to his door.

[3] Then David went after Hadadezer king of Zobah, all the way to Hamath, as Hadadezer was reaching to plant his power along the Euphrates.

[4] David seized a thousand chariots, seven thousand horsemen, and twenty thousand foot soldiers. He hamstrung every chariot horse but kept a hundred for himself.

[5] When the Arameans of Damascus came running to rescue Hadadezer, David cut down twenty-two thousand of them.

[6] He planted garrisons throughout Aram of Damascus, and the Arameans became his servants, bringing tribute. Wherever David marched, the LORD made him win.

[7] David stripped the gold shields off Hadadezer's officers and carried them to Jerusalem.

[8] From Tibhath and Cun, Hadadezer's cities, he hauled away enormous quantities of bronze — the same bronze Solomon would later forge into the Sea, the pillars, and the temple vessels.

[9] When Tou king of Hamath heard David had shattered the entire army of Hadadezer king of Zobah —

[10] he sent his son Hadoram to King David to greet him and congratulate him for fighting Hadadezer and breaking him, because Hadadezer had been Tou's enemy. Hadoram brought every kind of gift: gold, silver, bronze.

[11] David dedicated all of it to the LORD, along with the silver and gold he had taken from every nation — Edom, Moab, the Ammonites, the Philistines, Amalek.

[12] Abishai son of Zeruiah cut down eighteen thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.

[13] He set garrisons in Edom, and all the Edomites became David's servants. Wherever David went, the LORD gave him the victory.

[14] So David ruled over all Israel, dealing out justice and righteousness to his people.

[15] Joab son of Zeruiah commanded the army. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud kept the records.

[16] Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests. Shavsha was the scribe.

[17] Benaiah son of Jehoiada commanded the Kerethites and the Pelethites. And David's sons stood at the king's side, his chief officials.

[1] Time passed, and Nahash king of Ammon died, and his son took the throne. [2] David said, "I'll show kindness to Hanun son of Nahash, because his father showed kindness to me." So David sent envoys to comfort him over his father's death. David's men arrived in Ammonite territory to bring comfort to Hanun, [3] but the Ammonite officials cornered Hanun: "You think David's honoring your father by sending these so-called comforters? No — his men came to scout the land, to spy it out, to overthrow it." [4] So Hanun seized David's envoys, shaved them, sliced their robes off at the hips, and sent them packing.

[5] When David heard what had been done to his men, he sent word to meet them — they were humiliated beyond words. The king said, "Stay in Jericho until your beards grow back. Then come home."

[6] When the Ammonites realized they had made themselves stink in David's nostrils, Hanun and his people sent a thousand talents of silver to hire chariots and horsemen from Aram Naharaim, Aram Maacah, and Zobah. [7] They bought themselves thirty-two thousand chariots, along with the king of Maacah and his army, who came and camped at Medeba. The Ammonites mustered out of their cities and marched to war.

[8] David heard. He sent Joab and the entire army — every fighting man he had. [9] The Ammonites came out and lined up for battle at the city gate, while the kings who'd come for hire took position by themselves in the open field.

[10] Joab saw the trap — enemy lines in front of him, enemy lines behind him. He picked the best of Israel's troops and arrayed them against the Arameans. [11] The rest he put under his brother Abishai's command, set against the Ammonites. [12] He said, "If the Arameans overpower me, you come rescue me. If the Ammonites overpower you, I'll come rescue you. [13] Be strong. Let's fight like men for our people and for the cities of our God. And the LORD — let him do what seems good to him."

[14] Joab and his troops moved forward to engage the Arameans, and the Arameans broke and ran. [15] When the Ammonites saw the Arameans in flight, they too ran from Abishai and bolted into the city. Joab returned to Jerusalem.

[16] The Arameans saw they'd been routed by Israel. They sent messengers and brought up the Arameans from beyond the Euphrates, with Shophach, commander of Hadadezer's army, leading them.

[17] When David got word, he gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and came at them and formed his lines. David drew up against the Arameans, and they engaged. [18] But the Arameans broke before Israel, and David cut down seven thousand of their charioteers and forty thousand foot soldiers. He killed Shophach the army commander too.

[19] When Hadadezer's vassals saw they were beaten by Israel, they made peace with David and became his subjects. After that, Aram wanted nothing more to do with helping the Ammonites.

[1] Spring came — the season when kings march out to war. Joab led the army out, ravaged the land of the Ammonites, and laid siege to Rabbah. David stayed back in Jerusalem. Joab hammered Rabbah until it fell, then leveled it.

[2] David lifted the crown from the head of their king — gold, seventy-five pounds of it, set with a precious stone — and it was placed on David's own head. He hauled out massive plunder from the city.

[3] He brought out the people inside it and put them to forced labor with saws, iron picks, and axes. David did this to every Ammonite city. Then he and the whole army returned to Jerusalem.

[4] Later, war erupted again at Gezer against the Philistines. That was when Sibbecai the Hushathite cut down Sippai, one of the descendants of the giants. The Philistines were broken.

[5] War with the Philistines flared up once more, and Elhanan son of Jair struck down Lahmi — the brother of Goliath of Gath — whose spear shaft was thick as a weaver's beam.

[6] Another battle at Gath: there stood a man of enormous size, six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot — twenty-four in all — also descended from the giants.

[7] He hurled defiance at Israel, and Jonathan son of David's brother Shimea cut him down.

[8] These were giants of Gath, and they fell at the hands of David and his men.

[1] The Adversary rose against Israel and incited David to take a census of the nation.

[2] David ordered Joab and the commanders: "Go. Count Israel from Beersheba to Dan. Bring me the number. I want to know."

[3] Joab pushed back: "May the LORD multiply his people a hundred times over. But, my lord the king — aren't they all yours already? Why does my lord want this? Why bring guilt on Israel?"

[4] The king's word overrode Joab. So Joab went out, traveled through all Israel, and returned to Jerusalem.

[5] He handed David the count: Israel — one million one hundred thousand men who could draw a sword. Judah — four hundred seventy thousand.

[6] Joab never counted Levi or Benjamin in the total — the king's command sickened him.

[7] And it sickened God too. He struck Israel.

[8] David said to God: "I've sinned. What I did was monstrous. Please — take away your servant's guilt. I acted like a fool."

[9] The LORD spoke to Gad, David's seer:

[10] "Go tell David: This is what the LORD says — I'm offering you three options. Choose one. That's what I'll do to you."

[11] Gad came to David: "Here is what the LORD says — pick your sentence:

[12] three years of famine, or three months running for your life with your enemies' swords at your back, or three days of the LORD's own sword — plague across the land, the angel of the LORD cutting down every corner of Israel. Decide. What answer do I bring back to the one who sent me?"

[13] David said to Gad: "This crushes me. Let me fall into the hands of the LORD — his mercy runs deep. Don't let me fall into human hands."

[14] So the LORD sent plague on Israel. Seventy thousand men of Israel fell.

[15] God dispatched an angel to destroy Jerusalem. But as the angel was about to strike, the LORD looked, and grief seized him over the catastrophe. He said to the destroying angel, "Enough. Drop your hand." The angel of the LORD was standing at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.

[16] David lifted his eyes and saw the angel of the LORD suspended between earth and sky, sword drawn, hand stretched out over Jerusalem. David and the elders, dressed in sackcloth, fell facedown.

[17] David said to God: "Wasn't it me who ordered the count? I'm the one who sinned. I'm the one who did wrong. These — this flock — what did they do? LORD my God, let your hand fall on me and my father's house. But not on your people. Don't let the plague touch them."

[18] The angel of the LORD told Gad to tell David: go up and build an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite.

[19] David went up, exactly as Gad told him to in the name of the LORD.

[20] Ornan was threshing wheat. He turned and saw the angel. His four sons with him hid themselves.

[21] David approached. Ornan looked up, saw David, left the threshing floor, and bowed to the ground before him.

[22] David said to Ornan: "Sell me this threshing floor. I need to build an altar to the LORD on it — give it to me at full price — so the plague stops attacking the people."

[23] Ornan said to David: "Take it. Let my lord the king do whatever he sees fit. Look — I'll throw in oxen for burnt offerings, the threshing sledges for wood, the wheat for the grain offering. All of it. Yours."

[24] But King David said to Ornan: "No. I will pay the full price. I will not take what is yours and hand it to the LORD. I will not offer a burnt offering that cost me nothing."

[25] So David paid Ornan six hundred shekels of gold by weight for the site.

[26] David built an altar to the LORD there and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. He called on the LORD — and the LORD answered him with fire from heaven on the altar of burnt offering.

[27] The LORD spoke to the angel, and the angel sheathed his sword.

[28] When David saw that the LORD had answered him at the threshing floor of Ornan the Jebusite, he offered sacrifices there.

[29] The LORD's tabernacle that Moses had made in the wilderness — and the altar of burnt offering — were at that time at the high place in Gibeon.

[30] But David could not go before it to seek God. He was terrified of the angel's sword.

[1] Then David declared: "This is it. This is the house of the LORD God. This is the altar of burnt offering for Israel."

[2] David ordered every foreigner living in Israel rounded up. From their ranks he conscripted stonecutters to dress the blocks for building God's house.

[3] He stockpiled iron — mountains of it — for nails for the doors of the gates and for the joinings. Bronze, too, in such quantities no one could weigh it.

[4] Cedar logs without count, because the men of Sidon and Tyre kept hauling them in to David by the boatload.

[5] David's thinking was this: "Solomon, my son — he's young. He's untested. And the house being built for the LORD has to be staggering, famous, glorious in every land. I'll do the groundwork myself." So David poured himself into the preparation before he died.

[6] Then he summoned Solomon his son and laid the charge on him: build the house for the LORD, the God of Israel.

[7] David said to him: "My son, it was in my heart — I wanted to build a house for the name of the LORD my God.

[8] But the word of the LORD came to me: 'You've spilled too much blood. You've fought enormous wars. You will not build a house for my name — not with all that blood soaking the ground in my presence.

[9] But — a son will be born to you. A man of rest. I will give him rest from every enemy on every side. Solomon will be his name, and peace and quiet will mark his days in Israel.

[10] He is the one who will build a house for my name. He will be a son to me, and I will be a father to him. I will plant his royal throne over Israel and it will stand forever.'

[11] So now, my son — may the LORD be with you. May you succeed. Build the house of the LORD your God, just as he promised about you.

[12] Only — may the LORD give you wisdom and understanding. May he set you over Israel so you guard the law of the LORD your God.

[13] Then you'll thrive — if you're careful to live out the statutes and the rulings the LORD handed Moses for Israel. Be strong. Take courage. Don't flinch. Don't fold.

[14] Look — even in my hard-pressed days I've stockpiled for the house of the LORD: a hundred thousand talents of gold, a million talents of silver, bronze and iron beyond weighing — there's just too much. Timber and stone too. And you can add to it.

[15] You've got workers in abundance: stonecutters, masons, carpenters, craftsmen skilled in every trade —

[16] gold, silver, bronze, iron, no end of them. Get up. Get to work. And may the LORD be with you."

[17] Then David ordered all the leaders of Israel to throw their weight behind his son Solomon:

[18] "Isn't the LORD your God with you? Hasn't he given you rest on every side? He has handed the inhabitants of this land into my grip. The land has been subdued before the LORD and before his people.

[19] So now — set your heart, set your soul, to seek the LORD your God. Get up and build the sanctuary of the LORD God. Bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD and the holy vessels of God into the house that will be built for the name of the LORD."

[1] David was old, full of years, when he made Solomon his son king over Israel.

[2] He gathered all the leaders of Israel together with the priests and the Levites.

[3] The Levites were counted from thirty years old and up. Head by head, man by man — thirty-eight thousand of them.

[4] Twenty-four thousand of these would oversee the work of the house of the LORD. Six thousand would serve as officials and judges.

[5] Four thousand would be gatekeepers, and four thousand would praise the LORD with the instruments David had made for that purpose.

[6] David divided them into divisions according to the sons of Levi — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

[7] The Gershonites: Ladan and Shimei.

[8] The sons of Ladan — Jehiel first, then Zetham and Joel. Three.

[9] The sons of Shimei: Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran. Three. These were the heads of the fathers' houses of Ladan.

[10] The sons of Shimei: Jahath, Zina, Jeush, and Beriah. Four sons in all.

[11] Jahath was first, Zizah second. Jeush and Beriah had few sons, so they were counted as one father's house.

[12] The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel. Four.

[13] The sons of Amram: Aaron and Moses. Aaron was set apart — he and his sons forever — to consecrate the most holy things, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister to him, and to bless in his name forever.

[14] As for Moses, the man of God, his sons were named among the tribe of Levi.

[15] The sons of Moses: Gershom and Eliezer.

[16] The son of Gershom: Shebuel, the chief.

[17] The sons of Eliezer: Rehabiah, the chief. Eliezer had no other sons, but the sons of Rehabiah were many.

[18] The sons of Izhar: Shelomith, the chief.

[19] The sons of Hebron: Jeriah first, Amariah second, Jahaziel third, Jekameam fourth.

[20] The sons of Uzziel: Micah first, Isshiah second.

[21] The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. The sons of Mahli: Eleazar and Kish.

[22] Eleazar died with no sons, only daughters. Their cousins, the sons of Kish, married them.

[23] The sons of Mushi: Mahli, Eder, and Jeremoth. Three.

[24] These were the sons of Levi — by their fathers' houses, the heads of the houses as they were registered, counted by name, head by head, all who would do the work of the service of the house of the LORD, from twenty years old and up.

[25] For David said: "The LORD, the God of Israel, has given rest to his people, and he dwells in Jerusalem forever.

[26] The Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle or any of the vessels for its service."

[27] So by David's final orders, the Levites were counted from twenty years old and up.

[28] Their assignment: to assist the sons of Aaron in the service of the house of the LORD — caring for the courts and the chambers, the purification of every holy thing, the work of the service of the house of God.

[29] They were responsible for the bread of the Presence, the fine flour for the grain offering, the wafers of unleavened bread, what was baked on the griddle, what was mixed with oil — all measures of quantity and size.

[30] They were to stand every morning to thank and praise the LORD, and likewise every evening.

[31] And whenever burnt offerings were offered to the LORD — on Sabbaths, new moons, and the appointed feasts — they were to serve before the LORD continually, in the prescribed number, according to the rule given them.

[32] So they kept the charge of the tent of meeting, the charge of the sanctuary, and the charge of their brothers, the sons of Aaron — for the service of the house of the LORD.

[1] Aaron's descendants, organized by division: his sons were Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

[2] Nadab and Abihu died before their father, leaving no sons, so Eleazar and Ithamar served as priests.

[3] David, working alongside Zadok from Eleazar's line and Ahimelech from Ithamar's line, sorted them into divisions according to their assigned duties.

[4] Eleazar's descendants turned out to have more leading men than Ithamar's, so the breakdown went like this: sixteen heads of families from Eleazar's line, eight from Ithamar's.

[5] They cast lots to assign positions — no favoritism, no rank-pulling — because both lines produced officials of the sanctuary and officials before God.
