[6] He looked, and there at his head was bread baked over hot stones and a jar of water. He ate. He drank. He lay back down.

[7] The angel of the Lord came a second time, touched him, and said, "Get up. Eat. The road ahead is more than you can bear."

[8] So he rose. He ate and drank. And on the strength of that meal he walked forty days and forty nights, all the way to Horeb, the mountain of God.

[9] He came to a cave there and spent the night. And the word of the Lord came to him: "What are you doing here, Elijah?"

[10] He said, "I have been on fire for the Lord, the God of Armies. Israel has torn down your altars, broken your covenant, killed your prophets with the sword. I am the only one left. And now they're hunting me too."

[11] He said, "Come out. Stand on the mountain in the presence of the Lord. The Lord is about to pass by." Then came a wind — vast, splitting mountains, shattering rocks before the Lord. The Lord was not in the wind. After the wind, an earthquake. The Lord was not in the earthquake.

[12] After the earthquake, fire. The Lord was not in the fire. And after the fire — a whisper. A thin, hushed sound.

[13] When Elijah heard it, he pulled his cloak across his face, stepped out, and stood at the mouth of the cave. And a voice came to him: "What are you doing here, Elijah?"

[14] He said, "I have been on fire for the Lord, the God of Armies. Israel has torn down your altars, broken your covenant, killed your prophets with the sword. I am the only one left. And now they're hunting me too."

[15] The Lord said, "Go back the way you came. Head for the wilderness of Damascus. When you arrive, anoint Hazael as king over Aram.

[16] Anoint Jehu son of Nimshi as king over Israel. And anoint Elisha son of Shaphat from Abel-meholah as prophet in your place.

[17] Whoever escapes Hazael's sword, Jehu will kill. Whoever escapes Jehu's sword, Elisha will kill.

[18] And I will leave seven thousand in Israel — every knee that has not bent to Baal, every mouth that has not kissed him."

[19] Elijah left and found Elisha son of Shaphat plowing — twelve yoke of oxen ahead of him, he himself driving the twelfth. Elijah walked past and threw his cloak across him.

[20] Elisha left the oxen and ran after Elijah. "Let me kiss my father and mother goodbye," he said. "Then I'll follow you." Elijah said, "Go on. Go back. What have I done to you?"

[21] So Elisha turned back. He took the pair of oxen and slaughtered them. He used the plowing gear to cook the meat, gave it to his people, and they ate. Then he rose, followed Elijah, and became his servant.

[1] Ben-Hadad, king of Aram, mustered his entire army. Thirty-two kings rode with him, with horses and chariots. He marched on Samaria, laid siege, and attacked.

[2] He sent messengers into the city to Ahab, king of Israel,

[3] with this word: "Ben-Hadad says: Your silver and your gold belong to me. Your wives and your finest sons belong to me."

[4] The king of Israel answered, "As you say, my lord the king. I am yours, and everything I have."

[5] The messengers came back: "Ben-Hadad says: I sent word that you would hand over your silver and gold, your wives and sons.

[6] But tomorrow, about this time, I'm sending my officers to ransack your palace and the houses of your servants. Whatever your eyes treasure, they'll seize and carry off."

[7] The king of Israel summoned every elder in the land and said, "See it — this man is hunting for trouble. He demanded my wives, my sons, my silver, my gold, and I didn't refuse him."

[8] The elders and all the people answered, "Don't listen. Don't agree."

[9] So Ahab told Ben-Hadad's messengers, "Tell my lord the king: Everything you demanded the first time, I'll do. But this — this I cannot do." The messengers left and carried the answer back.

[10] Ben-Hadad sent another message: "May the gods do worse than their worst to me if there's enough dust left in Samaria to fill the hands of the men following me."

[11] The king of Israel sent back: "Tell him this — the man strapping on his armor shouldn't brag like the man taking it off."

[12] Ben-Hadad heard this while drinking with the kings in their tents. He ordered, "Take positions." And they took positions against the city.

[13] Then a prophet approached Ahab king of Israel and said, "This is what the LORD says: Do you see this enormous horde? Today I'm handing it over to you, and you'll know that I am the LORD."

[14] Ahab asked, "By whom?" The prophet answered, "The LORD says: By the young officers under the provincial governors." "And who starts the battle?" Ahab asked. The prophet said, "You do."

[15] So Ahab mustered the young officers under the provincial governors — two hundred and thirty-two of them. After them he mustered the rest of the army of Israel — seven thousand strong.

[16] They marched out at noon. Ben-Hadad was in his tent getting drunk with the thirty-two kings allied with him.

[17] The young officers under the provincial governors went out first. Ben-Hadad's scouts reported: "Men are coming out of Samaria."

[18] He said, "Whether they come for peace or for war — take them alive."

[19] So they marched out of the city — the young officers in front, the army behind —

[20] and each man cut down the one in front of him. The Arameans broke and ran. Israel chased them. Ben-Hadad king of Aram escaped on horseback with his cavalry.

[21] The king of Israel rode out and struck down the horses and chariots. He shattered the Arameans in a great slaughter.

[22] Then the prophet came to the king of Israel and said, "Go. Strengthen your position. Know what you're doing. Next spring, the king of Aram will come at you again."

[23] Meanwhile, the officials of the king of Aram told him, "Their gods are gods of the hills — that's why they beat us. Fight them on level ground, and we'll be stronger than they are.

[24] Do this: remove the kings from command, replace them with field commanders,

[25] and raise an army to replace the one you lost — horse for horse, chariot for chariot. We'll fight them on the plains, and we'll be the stronger." He listened, and he did it.

[26] In the spring, Ben-Hadad mustered the Arameans and marched on Aphek to fight Israel.

[27] The Israelites were mustered and provisioned, and they went out to meet them. The Israelites camped opposite the enemy like two small flocks of goats. The Arameans filled the countryside.

[28] Then the man of God approached the king of Israel and said, "This is what the LORD says: Because Aram has said, 'The LORD is a god of the hills, not a god of the valleys,' I'll hand over this entire vast horde to you. And you'll know that I am the LORD."

[29] They camped facing each other for seven days. On the seventh day, the battle was joined. The Israelites cut down a hundred thousand Aramean foot soldiers in a single day.

[30] The rest fled to Aphek, into the city — and the wall came down on twenty-seven thousand of them. Ben-Hadad fled into the city and hid in an inner room.

[31] His officials said to him, "Listen — we've heard that the kings of Israel are merciful kings. Let's put sackcloth around our waists and ropes on our heads and go out to the king of Israel. Maybe he'll spare your life."

[32] So they wrapped themselves in sackcloth and put ropes on their heads, and they came to the king of Israel and said, "Your servant Ben-Hadad begs: please let me live." Ahab said, "He's still alive? He is my brother."

[33] The men took this as a sign and seized on his words. "Yes — your brother Ben-Hadad." Ahab said, "Bring him out." Ben-Hadad came out, and Ahab brought him up into his chariot.

[34] Ben-Hadad said, "The cities my father took from your father — I'll give them back. You can set up your own markets in Damascus, as my father did in Samaria." Ahab said, "On those terms, I'll release you." So he made a treaty with him and let him go.

[35] One of the sons of the prophets said to his companion, by the word of the LORD, "Strike me." The man refused.

[36] So the prophet said to him, "Because you didn't obey the voice of the LORD — the moment you leave me, a lion will kill you." And the moment he left, a lion found him and killed him.

[37] Then the prophet found another man and said, "Strike me." This one struck him and wounded him.

[38] The prophet went and stood by the road, waiting for the king, with a bandage over his eyes to disguise himself.

[39] When the king passed by, the prophet called out: "Your servant went into the thick of the battle, and a soldier came over and brought me a captive and said, 'Guard this man. If he goes missing, your life for his, or you pay a talent of silver.'

[40] But while your servant was busy here and there, the man was gone." The king of Israel said to him, "That's your sentence. You pronounced it yourself."

[41] Then the prophet pulled the bandage from his eyes, and the king of Israel saw — this was one of the prophets.

[42] He said to the king, "This is what the LORD says: Because you let go from your hand the man I had marked for destruction, your life will go for his life, and your people for his people."

[43] The king of Israel went home to Samaria — sullen and seething.

[1] Some time after this, here's what went down. Naboth of Jezreel owned a vineyard right next to the palace of Ahab, king of Samaria.

[2] Ahab made him an offer: "Hand over your vineyard. I want it for a vegetable garden — it's right next to my house. I'll give you a better vineyard in exchange. Or if you'd rather have cash, I'll pay you what it's worth."

[3] Naboth shot back: "May the Lord forbid it. This is the inheritance my fathers left me. I'm not giving it to you."

[4] Ahab went home sullen and seething over what Naboth of Jezreel had told him — *the inheritance my fathers left me, I'm not giving it to you*. He threw himself on his bed, turned his face to the wall, and refused to eat.

[5] His wife Jezebel came in. "Why are you so sour? Why won't you eat?"

[6] He told her: "Because I talked to Naboth of Jezreel. I offered to buy his vineyard, or trade him another one for it. And he said, 'I won't give you my vineyard.'"

[7] Jezebel snapped back: "Is this how a king of Israel acts? Get up. Eat something. Cheer up. I'll get you Naboth's vineyard myself."

[8] So she wrote letters in Ahab's name, sealed them with his seal, and sent them to the elders and nobles who lived in Naboth's city.

[9] The letters said: *Proclaim a fast. Seat Naboth at the head of the assembly.*

[10] *Plant two scoundrels across from him. Have them testify against him: "You cursed God and the king." Then drag him out and stone him to death.*

[11] The men of the city — the elders and the nobles — did exactly what Jezebel had ordered. Exactly what the letters said.

[12] They proclaimed a fast and put Naboth at the head of the assembly.

[13] The two scoundrels came in, sat across from him, and accused him in front of everyone: "Naboth cursed God and the king." So they hauled him outside the city and stoned him to death.

[14] Then they sent word back to Jezebel: *Naboth has been stoned. He's dead.*

[15] The moment Jezebel heard he was dead, she said to Ahab: "Go on. Take the vineyard of Naboth of Jezreel — the one he refused to sell you. Naboth isn't alive anymore. He's dead."

[16] When Ahab heard Naboth was dead, he got up and went down to claim the vineyard for himself.

[17] Then the word of the Lord came to Elijah the Tishbite:

[18] "Go down and confront Ahab king of Israel, the one in Samaria. Right now he's in Naboth's vineyard, taking possession of it.

[19] Tell him: *This is what the Lord says — You murdered a man and now you're stealing his land?* Then tell him: *This is what the Lord says — In the very place where dogs licked up Naboth's blood, dogs will lick up your blood. Yours.*"

[20] Ahab said to Elijah, "So — you found me, my enemy?" Elijah answered: "I found you. Because you've sold yourself to do what is evil in the eyes of the Lord.

[21] Listen — I am bringing disaster on you. I will sweep you away. I will cut off from Ahab every male in Israel, slave or free.

[22] I'll make your house like the house of Jeroboam son of Nebat and like the house of Baasha son of Ahijah — because you have provoked me to fury and dragged Israel into sin.

[23] And about Jezebel — the Lord says this too: *Dogs will devour Jezebel by the wall of Jezreel.*

[24] Anyone of Ahab's line who dies in the city, dogs will eat. Anyone who dies in the open country, the birds of the sky will eat."

[25] There was never anyone like Ahab — a man who sold himself to do evil in the eyes of the Lord, with his wife Jezebel pushing him into it.

[26] He sank to the lowest depths, chasing idols just like the Amorites — the ones the Lord drove out before Israel.

[27] But when Ahab heard those words, he tore his clothes, put on sackcloth against his skin, fasted, slept in sackcloth, and walked around in mourning.

[28] Then the word of the Lord came to Elijah the Tishbite:

[29] "Did you see how Ahab humbled himself before me? Because he has humbled himself, I won't bring the disaster during his lifetime. I'll bring it on his house in the days of his son."

[1] For three years there was no war between Aram and Israel. [2] But in the third year, Jehoshaphat of Judah came down to visit the king of Israel.

[3] The king of Israel said to his officers, "You know Ramoth-gilead belongs to us — and we're just sitting here, doing nothing to take it back from the king of Aram." [4] He turned to Jehoshaphat: "Will you come with me to fight at Ramoth-gilead?"

Jehoshaphat answered, "I'm with you. My people are your people. My horses are your horses." [5] Then he added, "But first — let's ask the LORD what he says."

[6] So the king of Israel rounded up his prophets, about four hundred of them, and asked, "Should I march on Ramoth-gilead, or hold back?"

"Go," they said. "The Lord will hand it to you."

[7] Jehoshaphat wasn't satisfied. "Isn't there a prophet of the LORD here we could ask?"

[8] The king of Israel said, "There's one more — Micaiah son of Imlah. But I hate him. He never prophesies anything good about me. Only disaster."

"Don't say that," Jehoshaphat replied.

[9] So the king summoned an officer and said, "Bring Micaiah son of Imlah. Now."

[10] The king of Israel and Jehoshaphat king of Judah were sitting on their thrones, decked out in royal robes, on a threshing floor at the entrance to the gate of Samaria. Every prophet in the country was raving in front of them. [11] Zedekiah son of Chenaanah had made himself iron horns. "This is what the LORD says," he announced. "With these you'll gore the Arameans into oblivion."

[12] The rest of the prophets sang the same song: "March on Ramoth-gilead. Win. The LORD will hand it to the king."

[13] Meanwhile, the messenger sent to fetch Micaiah leaned in: "Look — every prophet in there is telling the king he'll win. Make your word match theirs. Say something favorable."

[14] Micaiah said, "As the LORD lives, I will say whatever the LORD tells me to say."

[15] When he arrived, the king asked, "Micaiah — should we march on Ramoth-gilead, or hold back?"

"Go up. Win," he said. "The LORD will hand it to the king."

[16] The king snapped: "How many times do I have to make you swear to tell me nothing but the truth in the name of the LORD?"

[17] So Micaiah said:

"I saw all Israel scattered on the mountains —
sheep with no shepherd.
And the LORD said,
'These have no master.
Let each man go home in peace.'"

[18] The king of Israel turned to Jehoshaphat. "Didn't I tell you? He never has anything good to say about me. Only disaster."

[19] But Micaiah pressed on: "Then hear the word of the LORD. I saw the LORD sitting on his throne, with all the armies of heaven standing beside him, on his right and on his left. [20] And the LORD said, 'Who will lure Ahab into marching on Ramoth-gilead, so he falls there?'

"One spirit said this, another said that. [21] Then a spirit stepped forward, stood before the LORD, and said, 'I'll lure him.'

[22] "'How?' the LORD asked.

"'I'll go out and become a lying spirit in the mouths of all his prophets.'

"'You'll succeed,' the LORD said. 'Go. Do it.'

[23] "So there it is. The LORD has put a lying spirit in the mouths of all these prophets of yours. The LORD has decreed disaster for you."

[24] Zedekiah son of Chenaanah marched up and slapped Micaiah across the face. "Which way did the Spirit of the LORD pass from me to speak to you?"

[25] Micaiah answered, "You'll find out — the day you run from room to room trying to hide."

[26] The king of Israel ordered, "Take Micaiah. Hand him over to Amon the governor of the city and to Joash the king's son. [27] Tell them: the king says, lock this man up and feed him bread and water rations until I come home in peace."

[28] Micaiah said, "If you come back in peace, then the LORD has not spoken through me." And he added, "Hear that — all of you."

[29] So the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat king of Judah marched up to Ramoth-gilead. [30] The king of Israel said to Jehoshaphat, "I'll disguise myself before I go into battle. You wear your royal robes." So the king of Israel disguised himself and went into battle.

[31] Now the king of Aram had given his thirty-two chariot commanders this order: "Don't bother with anyone — small or great. Kill the king of Israel. Only him." [32] When the chariot commanders saw Jehoshaphat, they said, "That has to be the king of Israel." They wheeled toward him to attack. Jehoshaphat cried out — [33] and when the commanders realized he wasn't the king of Israel, they turned away.

[34] But a man drew his bow at random — and the arrow struck the king of Israel between the joints of his armor. He told his chariot driver, "Turn around. Get me out of here. I'm wounded."

[35] The battle raged on through the day. The king was propped up in his chariot, facing the Arameans. Blood from the wound ran down into the floor of the chariot. By evening, he was dead. [36] As the sun went down, a cry swept through the army: "Every man to his city. Every man to his land."

[37] So the king died, and they brought him to Samaria and buried him there. [38] They washed the chariot at the pool of Samaria — and the dogs licked up his blood, and the prostitutes bathed in it — just as the word of the LORD had said.

[39] The rest of what Ahab did — the ivory palace he built, the cities he fortified — it's all written in the annals of the kings of Israel. [40] Ahab joined his ancestors, and his son Ahaziah became king after him.

[41] Jehoshaphat son of Asa became king of Judah in the fourth year of Ahab king of Israel. [42] He was thirty-five when he took the throne and reigned twenty-five years in Jerusalem. His mother was Azubah daughter of Shilhi. [43] He walked in every way of his father Asa — never veering — doing what was right in the LORD's eyes. But the high places were not torn down. The people kept sacrificing and burning incense there. [44] And Jehoshaphat made peace with the king of Israel.

[45] The rest of what Jehoshaphat did — his might, his wars — it's all written in the annals of the kings of Judah. [46] He swept the last of the temple prostitutes from the land — the ones his father Asa had missed.

[47] There was no king in Edom — only a deputy. [48] Jehoshaphat built a fleet of trading ships to sail to Ophir for gold, but they never made it. They were wrecked at Ezion-geber. [49] Then Ahaziah son of Ahab said to Jehoshaphat, "Let my men sail with yours." But Jehoshaphat refused.

[50] Jehoshaphat joined his ancestors and was buried with them in the city of David his father. His son Jehoram became king after him.

[51] Ahaziah son of Ahab became king of Israel in Samaria in the seventeenth year of Jehoshaphat king of Judah, and he reigned over Israel two years. [52] He did what was evil in the LORD's eyes — walking the path of his father, his mother, and Jeroboam son of Nebat, who dragged Israel into sin. [53] He served Baal. He worshiped him. He provoked the LORD, the God of Israel, to fury — exactly as his father had done.

[1] Peter, sent one of Jesus the Anointed — to the scattered ones, refugees living as foreigners across Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, [2] chosen before time by the Father who knew, set apart by the Spirit, destined to obey Jesus the Anointed and be sprinkled with his blood: may grace and peace flood over you.

[3] Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus the Anointed. Out of the deep well of his mercy he gave us new birth — birth into a living hope through Jesus rising from the dead, [4] birth into an inheritance that cannot rot, cannot stain, cannot fade, held in safekeeping in the heavens for you — [5] you, who through faith are being guarded by God's own power until the rescue is unveiled at the final hour.

[6] Hold onto that, and you can rejoice — even now, even when for a little while you have had to suffer through every kind of trial. [7] These are the fires that prove your faith genuine — faith more precious than gold, and gold itself only survives the furnace. When Jesus the Anointed is unveiled, your tested faith will come out as praise, glory, honor. [8] You have never laid eyes on him, yet you love him. You cannot see him now, yet you trust him — and the joy that rises in you is past words, drenched in glory, [9] because you are already receiving the goal of your faith: the rescue of your souls.

[10] This rescue — the prophets searched it out, dug into it, strained to see it. They prophesied about the grace coming to you, [11] probing to learn what time, what circumstance the Spirit of the Anointed inside them was pointing toward when he kept testifying in advance about the sufferings of the Messiah and the glories that would follow. [12] It was revealed to them that they were not serving themselves — they were serving you. And now these very things have been announced to you by those who brought you the good news through the Holy Spirit sent down from heaven. Even angels lean in, longing to look into this.

[13] So pull your minds together for action. Stay clear-headed. Set your hope — set it completely — on the grace coming to you when Jesus the Anointed is unveiled. [14] Like children who actually obey, do not slide back into the shape your old cravings used to press you into when you knew no better. [15] No — the One who called you is holy. So become holy yourselves, in every move you make, [16] because it stands written: Be holy, because I am holy.

[17] You call him Father — the One who judges each person's work without favoritism. Then live out your time as foreigners here with reverence. [18] You know what it cost to ransom you out of the empty inherited life — and it was not silver or gold, things that corrode. [19] It was the priceless blood of the Anointed, a lamb without flaw, without blemish. [20] He was chosen for this before the world's foundations were laid, and revealed in these final days for you — [21] for you who through him trust God, the God who raised him from the dead and gave him glory, so that your faith and hope rest in God himself.

[22] You have purified yourselves by obeying the truth, so that you can love your brothers and sisters without a shred of pretense. So love each other — deeply, from a clean heart. [23] You have been born again, and not from seed that perishes but from seed that does not — through the living, enduring word of God. [24] As the prophet said:

All flesh is grass,
all its glory like the wildflower —
the grass withers,
the flower drops,
[25] but the word of the Lord stands forever.

And that word is the good news that was announced to you.

[1] So strip it all off — every shred of malice, every lie on your lips, every mask, every flicker of envy, every word designed to tear someone down.

[2] Like newborns crying for milk, crave the pure, unadulterated word — the kind that makes you grow into salvation.

[3] You've tasted that the Lord is good. You know.

[4] Come to him — the living Stone. Rejected by people. Chosen by God. Precious beyond measure.

[5] And you, like living stones, are being built into a spiritual house, becoming a holy priesthood, offering up the kind of sacrifices that God actually wants — the kind made possible through Jesus the Anointed.

[6] Scripture says it plain: *Look — I'm laying a stone in Zion. A cornerstone. Hand-picked. Honored. Whoever stakes their life on him will never be put to shame.*

[7] To you who trust him, he is everything. But to those who refuse — *the very stone the builders threw aside has become the cornerstone* —

[8] and *a stone that trips them, a rock that throws them down.* They stumble because they refuse the word. That's how it was always going to go for them.

[9] But you — you are a chosen people. A royal priesthood. A holy nation. God's own treasure. And the reason? So you can broadcast the excellencies of the One who called you out of darkness into his staggering light.

[10] Once you weren't a people. Now you are God's people. Once mercy was foreign to you. Now mercy is your address.

[11] Dear friends — I'm pleading with you, as exiles passing through — refuse the cravings of the flesh. They're waging war against your soul.

[12] Live so beautifully among the outsiders that even when they slander you as criminals, they'll end up watching your good deeds and giving glory to God on the day he comes.

[13] Submit to every human institution for the Lord's sake — whether to the emperor as the highest authority,

[14] or to governors he sends to punish wrongdoers and recognize those who do right.

[15] Because this is God's will: that by doing good, you silence the ignorance of fools.

[16] Live free. But don't use your freedom as cover for evil. Live as servants of God.

[17] Honor everyone. Love the family. Fear God. Honor the emperor.

[18] Servants — submit to your masters with deep respect. Not just to the kind ones, but to the harsh ones too.

[19] Because here's what counts as grace: bearing up under unjust suffering because you're conscious of God.

[20] What credit is it if you get beaten for messing up and take it? But if you suffer for doing right and you endure it — that catches God's attention. That's grace.

[21] This is exactly what you were called to. Christ suffered for you, leaving a trail you can follow step by step:

[22] *He committed no sin. No deceit was ever found in his mouth.*

[23] When they hurled insults at him, he didn't fire back. When he suffered, he didn't threaten. He just handed himself over to the One who judges justly.

[24] He carried our sins in his own body up the tree, so that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. *By his wounds, you've been healed.*

[25] You were sheep wandering off in every direction. But now you've come home — to the Shepherd, the Guardian of your souls.

[1] In the same way, wives — fall in step with your own husbands. Then even the ones who refuse to believe the word can be won over without a single word being spoken — won by the way their wives live, [2] when they see the purity and the reverence of that life up close.

[3] Don't let your beauty be the surface kind — the elaborate hair, the gold jewelry, the designer wardrobe. [4] Let it be the hidden self of the heart, the unfading beauty of a calm and steady spirit. That is what God treasures.

[5] This is how the holy women of old made themselves beautiful — the ones who set their hope in God. They fell in step with their husbands. [6] Sarah did this. She listened to Abraham and called him her lord. You become her daughters when you do what is right and refuse to be ruled by fear.

[7] And you, husbands — live with your wives in understanding. Honor her as the more delicate vessel, as a co-heir of the grace of life. Do this so nothing chokes off your prayers.

[8] Finally — all of you — be of one mind. Feel what each other feels. Love like family. Be tender. Stay humble. [9] Don't pay back insult for insult, or harm for harm. Do the opposite — bless. Because that is what you were called to, and a blessing is what you'll inherit.

[10] As the Scripture says:

Whoever wants to love life
and see good days —
keep your tongue from evil,
your lips from twisted speech.
[11] Turn from evil and do good.
Hunt down peace and chase it.
[12] The eyes of the Lord are on the righteous,
his ears tuned to their cry —
but his face is set against those who do evil.

[13] And really — who is going to harm you if you become consumed with doing good? [14] But even if you do suffer for the sake of what is right, you are blessed. Don't be afraid of their threats. Don't be shaken. [15] In your hearts, set apart the Messiah as Lord. Always be ready to give an answer — a reasoned defense — to anyone who asks you to explain the hope inside you. But do it with gentleness and reverence. [16] Keep your conscience clean, so that when they slander you, the ones smearing your good life in Christ will end up ashamed of themselves. [17] If God wills suffering for you, far better that it be for doing good than for doing evil.

[18] Because Christ also suffered — once for all — for sins. The righteous one for the unrighteous ones, to bring you home to God. Put to death in the flesh, made alive in the Spirit. [19] In that Spirit he went and made the announcement to the spirits in prison — [20] the ones who refused to listen long ago, back when God waited patiently in the days of Noah while the ark was being built. In that ark, a few — eight people — were brought safely through the water. [21] That water is a picture of the baptism that now saves you — not the scrubbing of dirt from the body, but the pledge of a clean conscience toward God — through the resurrection of Jesus Christ. [22] He has gone into heaven and is now at God's right hand, with angels and authorities and powers in submission under him.

[1] Christ suffered in the flesh — so arm yourselves with the same resolve. Whoever has suffered in the body is finished with sin. [2] From here on out, you don't live the rest of your earthly life chasing human appetites. You live it chasing what God wants. [3] You've already wasted enough time doing what the pagans love to do — wallowing in lust, drunkenness, wild parties, binges, and the obscene worship of idols. [4] They think it's strange now that you won't dive back into the same flood of debauchery with them — so they slander you. [5] They'll answer for it. There's a Judge ready to render his verdict on the living and the dead. [6] This is why the gospel was preached even to those now dead: so that though judged in the flesh like all humans, they might live in the spirit the way God lives.

[7] The end of everything is close. So keep your head clear and your prayers sharp. [8] Above all else, love each other with everything you've got — because love covers a multitude of sins. [9] Open your homes to one another without grumbling about it. [10] Whatever gift you've been handed, use it to serve the others — you're stewards of God's grace in all its variety. [11] If you speak, speak as one delivering the very words of God. If you serve, serve with the strength God supplies — so that in everything, God gets the glory through Jesus Christ. To him belong the glory and the power forever and ever. Amen.

[12] Friends, don't be shocked when the fire comes to test you, as though something bizarre were happening. [13] Instead, celebrate — because you're sharing in what Christ went through. And when his glory is unveiled, your joy will explode. [14] If they insult you for the name of Christ, count yourself blessed — because the Spirit of glory, the Spirit of God, is resting on you. [15] But make sure none of you suffers for being a murderer, a thief, a criminal, or a busybody in other people's lives. [16] If you suffer as a Christian, don't be ashamed — wear that name and give God the glory. [17] The time has come for judgment to begin with God's own household. And if it starts with us, what's the end going to look like for those who refuse the gospel of God? [18] If the righteous are barely making it through, what hope is there for the godless and the sinner? [19] So those who suffer according to God's will — entrust your souls to a faithful Creator and keep doing what is right.

[1] Now a word to the elders among you, from one who stands with you — a fellow elder, an eyewitness to what Christ suffered, and a partner in the glory about to break open:

[2] Shepherd the flock of God that's in your care. Watch over them — not because you have to, but because you want to, the way God wants you to. Not chasing money. Eager to serve.

[3] Don't lord it over the people entrusted to you. Be the kind of example worth following.

[4] And when the Chief Shepherd appears, you'll receive a crown of glory that will never tarnish.

[5] Younger ones, defer to your elders. And all of you — wrap yourselves in humility toward each other, because God stands against the arrogant but pours grace on the humble.

[6] So bow low under God's strong hand, and at the right time he'll lift you up.

[7] Take everything weighing on you and hand it to him — he's already carrying you.

[8] Stay sharp. Stay awake. Your enemy the devil is prowling like a hungry lion, hunting for someone to devour.

[9] Stand your ground. Resist him. Hold the line in your faith, knowing your brothers and sisters across the world are walking through the same fire.

[10] And the God of all grace — the one who called you into his eternal glory in Christ — after you've suffered a little while, he himself will restore you, steady you, strengthen you, plant you on solid rock.

[11] To him belongs the power, forever and ever. Amen.

[12] I'm writing this briefly through Silvanus, a brother I trust completely — encouraging you and bearing witness that this is the real grace of God. Plant your feet in it.

[13] The church in Babylon, chosen alongside you, sends her greetings. So does Mark, my son.

[14] Greet each other with a kiss of love. Peace to all of you who belong to Christ.

[1] There was a man from Ramathaim-zophim in the hill country of Ephraim. His name was Elkanah, son of Jeroham, son of Elihu, son of Tohu, son of Zuph — an Ephraimite.

[2] He had two wives. One was Hannah. The other was Peninnah. Peninnah had children. Hannah had none.

[3] Year after year this man traveled up from his town to worship and to sacrifice to the LORD of Armies at Shiloh, where Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, served as priests of the LORD.

[4] On sacrifice days, Elkanah would hand out portions of meat — pieces for Peninnah his wife, pieces for each of her sons and daughters.

[5] But to Hannah he gave a double portion, because Hannah was the one he loved. The LORD had sealed her womb.

[6] And her rival tormented her. Sharpened the knife year after year — provoking her, picking at the wound, all because the LORD had closed her womb.

[7] Year after year, the same thing. Every time Hannah went up to the house of the LORD, Peninnah would needle her until she broke. She wouldn't eat. She would only weep.

[8] Elkanah, her husband, would ask, "Hannah, why are you crying? Why won't you eat? Why does your heart sit so heavy? Am I not more to you than ten sons?"

[9] After they ate and drank at Shiloh, Hannah got up. Eli the priest was sitting on his chair by the doorpost of the LORD's temple.

[10] Her soul was bitter. She prayed to the LORD and wept — wept hard.

[11] And she made a vow: "LORD of Armies, if you will only look — really look — at the misery of your servant, and remember me, and not forget your servant, and give your servant a son, then I will give him back to you. All the days of his life. No razor will ever touch his head."

[12] She kept on praying before the LORD, and Eli watched her mouth.

[13] Hannah was speaking from inside herself — only her lips moved, no sound came out. So Eli took her for a drunk.

[14] He said to her, "How long are you going to be drunk? Get rid of your wine."

[15] Hannah answered, "No, my lord. I am a woman with a crushed spirit. I haven't touched wine or strong drink. I have been pouring out my soul before the LORD.

[16] Do not write off your servant as some worthless woman. I've been speaking out of the depth of my anguish and grief."

[17] Eli answered, "Go in peace. May the God of Israel give you what you have asked of him."

[18] She said, "May your servant find favor in your eyes." Then she went on her way, and she ate, and her face was no longer the same.

[19] Early the next morning they bowed in worship before the LORD and went home — back to Ramah. Elkanah knew Hannah his wife, and the LORD remembered her.

[20] In time, Hannah conceived and gave birth to a son. She named him Samuel, "because," she said, "I asked the LORD for him."

[21] When the time came for the yearly sacrifice, Elkanah went up with his whole household to offer it to the LORD and to fulfill his vow.

[22] But Hannah did not go. She told her husband, "Once the boy is weaned, I'll bring him. He will appear before the LORD and stay there — for good."

[23] Elkanah her husband said, "Do what seems right to you. Stay here until you've weaned him. Only — may the LORD make good on his word." So the woman stayed and nursed her son until she had weaned him.

[24] When he was weaned, she took him up with her — small as he was — along with a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine. She brought him to the house of the LORD at Shiloh.

[25] They slaughtered the bull and brought the boy to Eli.

[26] And she said, "Please, my lord. As surely as you live, my lord — I am the woman who stood right here beside you, praying to the LORD.

[27] This boy is what I prayed for. The LORD gave me exactly what I asked him for.

[28] So now I'm giving him back. As long as he lives, he is on loan to the LORD." And there, they worshiped the LORD.

[1] Then Hannah prayed:

My heart leaps in the LORD.
My strength rises high because of him.
My mouth opens wide against my enemies —
I am drunk on his rescue.

[2] No one is holy like the LORD.
There is no one but him.
No rock stands like our God.

[3] Enough of your proud talk.
Shut the arrogance pouring from your lips.
The LORD is a God who knows.
He weighs every deed.

[4] The bows of warriors snap.
The stumbling are belted with strength.

[5] The full sell themselves for bread.
The starving go hungry no more.
The barren bears seven.
The mother of many wastes away.

[6] The LORD kills and makes alive.
He drags down to the grave and pulls back up.

[7] The LORD makes poor and makes rich.
He brings low and lifts high.

[8] He lifts the destitute from the dust,
hauls the beggar off the ash heap,
seats them with princes,
hands them a throne of honor.

The pillars of the earth belong to the LORD.
On them he set the world.

[9] He guards the steps of those who love him.
The wicked vanish in darkness —
no one wins by sheer force.

[10] Anyone who fights the LORD will be shattered.
From the heavens he thunders against them.
The LORD will judge the ends of the earth.
He will give strength to his king,
lift up the horn of his anointed.

[11] Elkanah went home to Ramah. The boy stayed and served the LORD under Eli the priest.

[12] Eli's sons were worthless. They had no regard for the LORD,

[13] and no respect for what a priest owed the people. Whenever someone offered a sacrifice, while the meat was still boiling the priest's servant would come over with a three-pronged fork

[14] and jab it into the pot or pan or kettle or cauldron. Whatever the fork brought up, the priest claimed. That was how they treated every Israelite who came to Shiloh.

[15] Worse — before the fat was even burned, the servant would show up and tell the man sacrificing, "Hand over meat for the priest to roast. He won't take it boiled. He wants it raw."

[16] If the man protested, "Let the fat be burned first, then take whatever you want," the servant would shoot back, "No. Hand it over now. Or I'll take it by force."

[17] The sin of those young men was enormous in the LORD's sight. They were treating the LORD's offering with contempt.

[18] But Samuel served before the LORD — a boy wrapped in a linen ephod.

[19] Each year his mother sewed him a little robe and brought it to him when she came up with her husband for the yearly sacrifice.

[20] Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife: "May the LORD give you children through this woman in place of the one she dedicated to him." Then they would go home.

[21] And the LORD remembered Hannah. She conceived and bore three more sons and two daughters. Meanwhile the boy Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD.

[22] Eli was very old. He kept hearing what his sons were doing to all Israel — and how they were sleeping with the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

[23] He confronted them: "Why are you doing these things? Everyone is telling me about your evil.

[24] Stop, my sons. The report I'm hearing — the LORD's people are passing it around. It isn't good.

[25] If a man sins against another man, God can mediate. But if a man sins against the LORD — who steps in for him?"

They wouldn't listen to their father. The LORD had already decided to put them to death.

[26] But the boy Samuel kept growing — in stature, in favor with the LORD, in favor with people.

[27] A man of God came to Eli and said: "This is what the LORD says: Did I not reveal myself plainly to your ancestor's house when they were slaves in Egypt under Pharaoh?

[28] Out of every tribe in Israel, I chose your line to be my priests — to climb my altar, to burn incense, to wear the ephod in my presence. I gave your ancestor's house every fire offering the Israelites brought.

[29] So why do you trample on my sacrifice and my offering — the ones I commanded in my house? Why do you honor your sons more than me? Why do you fatten yourselves on the best cuts of every offering my people Israel bring?

[30] Therefore — this is the word of the LORD, the God of Israel — I did say your house and your father's house would walk before me forever. But now the LORD declares: Far from it. I honor those who honor me. Those who despise me will be despised.

[31] The days are coming when I will cut off your strength and the strength of your father's house. No old man will be left in your line.

[32] You'll watch hardship fall on my dwelling, even as Israel prospers — and never again will there be an old man in your house.

[33] One I won't cut off from my altar — only so your eyes can weep and your soul can ache. But every descendant of your house will die in the prime of life.

[34] And here is the sign — what happens to your two sons, Hophni and Phinehas: on the same day, both of them will die.

[35] Then I will raise up for myself a faithful priest who will act according to what is in my heart and mind. I will build him a lasting house. He will walk before my anointed always.

[36] Anyone left in your house will come crawling to him for a scrap of silver or a piece of bread, begging, 'Give me some priestly job, please — just so I can have a crust to eat.'"

[1] The boy Samuel served the LORD under Eli's watch. In those days the word of the LORD was rare; visions did not break through.

[2] One night Eli was lying down in his usual place. His eyes had grown so dim he could barely see.

[3] The lamp of God had not yet gone out, and Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD, where the ark of God rested.

[4] Then the LORD called: "Samuel!"

He answered, "Here I am."

[5] He ran to Eli and said, "Here I am — you called me."

"I didn't call you," Eli said. "Go back and lie down." So he did.

[6] Again the LORD called: "Samuel!" Samuel got up, went to Eli, and said, "Here I am — you called me."

"I didn't call you, son," he said. "Go back and lie down."

[7] Samuel did not yet know the LORD. The word of the LORD had not yet been revealed to him.

[8] A third time the LORD called Samuel. He got up, went to Eli, and said, "Here I am — you called me."

Then it hit Eli: the LORD was calling the boy.

[9] He said to Samuel, "Go lie down. If he calls you again, say, 'Speak, LORD — your servant is listening.'" So Samuel went back and lay down in his place.

[10] Then the LORD came and stood there and called as before: "Samuel! Samuel!"

And Samuel said, "Speak. Your servant is listening."

[11] The LORD said to Samuel, "Listen — I am about to do something in Israel that will leave every ear ringing.

[12] On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I spoke about his house — from start to finish.

[13] I told him I would judge his house forever for the guilt he knew about: his sons were cursing God, and he did nothing to stop them.

[14] So I have sworn to the house of Eli: no sacrifice, no offering, nothing will ever atone for the guilt of this house."

[15] Samuel lay there until morning, then opened the doors of the house of the LORD. He was afraid to tell Eli what he had seen.

[16] But Eli called him: "Samuel, my son."

"Here I am," he answered.

[17] "What did he say to you?" Eli asked. "Don't hold anything back. May God deal with you, and worse, if you hide a single word."

[18] So Samuel told him everything — kept nothing back.

And Eli said, "He is the LORD. Let him do what he sees fit."

[19] Samuel grew up, and the LORD was with him. He let none of his words fall to the ground.

[20] All Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, knew that Samuel was confirmed as a prophet of the LORD.

[21] And the LORD kept appearing at Shiloh. There he revealed himself to Samuel through his word.

[1] Samuel's word went out to all Israel.

Israel marched out to fight the Philistines. They camped at Ebenezer; the Philistines camped at Aphek. [2] The Philistines drew up their lines against Israel, the battle spread, and Israel was shattered before them. About four thousand men fell on the field that day.

[3] When the survivors stumbled back to camp, Israel's elders asked, "Why did the LORD break us today before the Philistines? Let's bring the ark of the LORD's covenant from Shiloh. Let it come into our midst and save us from the hand of our enemies."

[4] So they sent men to Shiloh, and they carried back the ark of the covenant of the LORD of hosts — the One enthroned between the cherubim. Hophni and Phinehas, Eli's two sons, came with the ark of God's covenant.

[5] When the ark entered the camp, all Israel let loose a shout so loud the ground shook.

[6] The Philistines heard the roar and said, "What's this thundering shout in the Hebrew camp?" Then they learned: the ark of the LORD had arrived. [7] Fear gripped them. "A god has come into their camp!" they cried. "We're finished. Nothing like this has ever happened. [8] We're done for! Who can rescue us from the hand of these mighty gods? These are the gods who struck Egypt with every kind of plague in the wilderness. [9] Stand your ground, Philistines! Be men, or you'll end up slaves to the Hebrews the way they were slaves to you. Be men. Fight."

[10] So the Philistines fought. Israel was crushed — every man fled to his tent. The slaughter was massive: thirty thousand foot soldiers of Israel fell. [11] The ark of God was captured. Hophni and Phinehas, Eli's two sons, died.

[12] A man from the tribe of Benjamin ran from the battle line and reached Shiloh that same day, his clothes ripped, dirt smeared on his head. [13] When he arrived, Eli was sitting on his chair beside the road, watching — his heart trembling for the ark of God. The man came into the town with the news, and the whole town erupted in wailing.

[14] Eli heard the sound of the cry and said, "What is that uproar?" The man hurried over and told Eli — [15] Eli was ninety-eight years old, his eyes fixed and unseeing.

[16] The man said to Eli, "I'm the one who came from the battle. I ran from the lines today."

Eli said, "What happened, my son?"

[17] The messenger answered: "Israel ran from the Philistines. The army was butchered. Your two sons are dead — Hophni and Phinehas. And the ark of God has been captured."

[18] The moment he mentioned the ark of God, Eli toppled backward off his chair beside the gate. His neck snapped, and he died — he was old and heavy. He had judged Israel forty years.

[19] His daughter-in-law, Phinehas's wife, was pregnant, near her time. When she heard the news — the ark captured, her father-in-law and husband dead — her labor crashed down on her, and she crumpled and gave birth. [20] As she was dying, the women attending her said, "Don't be afraid — you've had a son." But she didn't answer, didn't take it in.

[21] She named the boy Ichabod, saying, "The glory has fled from Israel" — because the ark of God had been captured, and because of her father-in-law and her husband. [22] She said, "The glory has fled from Israel — the ark of God is gone."

[1] The Philistines seized the Ark of God and hauled it from Ebenezer to Ashdod.

[2] They carried it into Dagon's temple and set it beside their god.

[3] When the people of Ashdod got up the next morning — there was Dagon, face-down on the ground before the Ark of the Lord. They picked him up and put him back in his place.

[4] The next morning, same scene — only worse. Dagon was sprawled face-down before the Ark again, but this time his head was snapped off and both his hands lay severed on the threshold. Only the stump of him remained.

[5] That is why, to this day, the priests of Dagon and everyone who enters his temple in Ashdod refuse to step on the threshold.

[6] Then the hand of the Lord came down hard on Ashdod. He devastated them. He struck the city and its whole territory with tumors.

[7] When the men of Ashdod saw what was happening, they said, "The Ark of Israel's God cannot stay here. His hand is crushing us — and crushing Dagon our god."

[8] So they summoned every Philistine ruler and asked, "What do we do with the Ark of Israel's God?" The verdict: "Move it to Gath." So they moved it.

[9] But the moment the Ark arrived, the hand of the Lord turned on that city too — sheer panic. He struck down the men of Gath, young and old, and tumors broke out across them.

[10] So they shipped the Ark off to Ekron. But as it entered the city, the Ekronites screamed, "They've brought us the Ark of Israel's God to kill us — all of us!"

[11] They called every Philistine ruler together and begged, "Send it away. Send it back where it came from before it kills us all." Death-terror gripped the whole city — God's hand bore down on them, heavy.

[12] Those who survived broke out in tumors, and the cry of the city went up to the heavens.

[1] The ark of the LORD sat in Philistine territory for seven months.

[2] Finally the Philistines summoned their priests and diviners. "What do we do with the ark of the LORD? Tell us how to send it back where it belongs."

[3] They answered, "If you're sending the ark of Israel's God away, don't send it empty. You must return it with a guilt offering. Then you'll be healed — and you'll know why his hand never lifted from you."

[4] "What kind of guilt offering?" they asked.

The priests said, "Five gold tumors and five gold rats — one for each Philistine ruler. The same plague struck all of you, rulers and people alike.

[5] Make replicas of the tumors and the rats that have been ravaging the land, and give glory to Israel's God. Maybe then he'll lift his hand from you, your gods, and your soil.

[6] Why harden your hearts the way Egypt and Pharaoh hardened theirs? Remember — when he was finished toying with them, didn't they finally let Israel go?

[7] Now do this: build a new cart. Take two milk cows that have never been yoked. Hitch them to the cart, but pen up their calves at home, away from them.

[8] Put the ark of the LORD on the cart. Beside it, in a box, place the gold objects — your guilt offering. Then send it off.

[9] Watch where it goes. If it heads up the road toward Beth-shemesh, into his own territory, then he is the one who unleashed this catastrophe on us. If not — then we'll know his hand never touched us. It was just bad luck."

[10] So they did it. They took two milk cows, harnessed them to the cart, and shut the calves up at home.

[11] They set the ark of the LORD on the cart, along with the box containing the gold rats and the replicas of the tumors.

[12] The cows went straight up the road toward Beth-shemesh — one road, no swerving, lowing the whole way, never turning right or left. The Philistine rulers followed behind, all the way to the border of Beth-shemesh.

[13] The people of Beth-shemesh were down in the valley harvesting wheat. They looked up — and there was the ark. They were beside themselves with joy.

[14] The cart rolled into the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stopped beside a large stone. The people split the cart up for firewood and offered the cows as a burnt offering to the LORD.

[15] The Levites lifted down the ark of the LORD and the box of gold beside it, and set them on the great stone. That day the men of Beth-shemesh offered burnt offerings and sacrifices to the LORD.

[16] The five Philistine rulers watched it all, then turned and went back to Ekron that same day.

[17] These are the gold tumors the Philistines sent as a guilt offering to the LORD — one each for Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, and Ekron.

[18] And the gold rats matched the number of all the Philistine towns under the five rulers — fortified cities and open villages alike. The great stone where they set down the ark of the LORD stands to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh.

[19] But God struck down the men of Beth-shemesh for looking into the ark of the LORD. He killed seventy of them. The people mourned because the LORD had struck them with such a heavy blow.

[20] The men of Beth-shemesh said, "Who can stand before the LORD, this holy God? Where do we send him next — away from us?"

[21] So they sent messengers to the people of Kiriath-jearim: "The Philistines have returned the ark of the LORD. Come down and take it up to your place."

[1] The men of Kiriath-jearim came down and took the Ark of the LORD. They brought it up to Abinadab's house on the hill and consecrated his son Eleazar to guard it.

[2] The Ark stayed at Kiriath-jearim a long time — twenty years passed — and all Israel mourned, aching for the LORD.

[3] Then Samuel spoke to the whole house of Israel: "If you're returning to the LORD with everything in you, get rid of the foreign gods and the Ashtoreth idols. Set your heart on the LORD. Serve him alone. Then he'll pull you free from the hand of the Philistines."

[4] So Israel threw out the Baals and the Ashtoreths and served the LORD — only him.

[5] Samuel said, "Gather all Israel to Mizpah. I'll pray to the LORD for you."

[6] They gathered at Mizpah. They drew water and poured it out before the LORD. They fasted that day and confessed: "We have sinned against the LORD." And Samuel judged the people of Israel at Mizpah.

[7] When the Philistines heard Israel had massed at Mizpah, their warlords moved up against them. Israel heard it and went cold with fear.

[8] They begged Samuel, "Don't stop crying out to the LORD our God for us — save us from the Philistines."

[9] Samuel took a nursing lamb and offered it whole as a burnt offering to the LORD. He cried out to the LORD for Israel, and the LORD answered him.

[10] As Samuel was lifting up that offering, the Philistines closed in for battle. But the LORD thundered against them that day — a massive, shattering roar — and threw them into chaos. Israel cut them down.

[11] The men of Israel poured out of Mizpah, chased the Philistines, and struck them all the way to below Beth-car.

[12] Samuel took a stone and set it up between Mizpah and Shen. He named it Ebenezer — "Stone of Help" — and said, "This far the LORD has carried us."

[13] So the Philistines were broken. They didn't come into Israel's territory again. The hand of the LORD pressed against them all the days of Samuel.

[14] The towns the Philistines had taken from Israel — from Ekron to Gath — were returned. Israel reclaimed the surrounding land from Philistine control. And there was peace between Israel and the Amorites too.

[15] Samuel judged Israel his whole life.

[16] Year after year he traveled a circuit — Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah — judging Israel in each place.

[17] Then he'd return home to Ramah. That's where he lived, where he judged Israel, and where he built an altar to the LORD.

[1] When Samuel grew old, he made his sons judges over Israel.

[2] His firstborn was Joel, his second Abijah. They held court in Beersheba.

[3] But the sons did not walk their father's road. They chased money, took bribes, twisted justice.

[4] So every elder in Israel gathered and came to Samuel at Ramah.

[5] They told him straight: "You're old. Your sons don't follow your ways. Appoint us a king to rule us — like every other nation has."

[6] The word *king* hit Samuel like a blow. He went to the LORD and prayed.

[7] And the LORD answered: "Listen to them. Give them what they ask. It isn't you they're rejecting — it's me. They don't want me as their king anymore.

[8] From the day I pulled them out of Egypt until this moment, this is what they've done — walked away from me, chased other gods. Now they're doing it to you.

[9] So listen to them. But warn them first. Spell out, in full, the kind of rule a king will lay on them."

[10] Samuel relayed every word of the LORD to the people clamoring for a king.

[11] He said: "Here is the kind of rule your king will impose. He will take your sons and put them in his chariots and on his horses, and they will run before his wheels.

[12] He will make them his commanders — of thousands, of fifties. He will set them to plowing his fields and reaping his harvest, forging his weapons and the gear of his chariots.

[13] He will take your daughters to mix his perfumes, cook his meals, bake his bread.

[14] He will take the best of your fields, your vineyards, your olive groves — and hand them to his officials.

[15] He will take a tenth of your grain and your wine and give it to his court and his servants.

[16] He will take your servants, your finest young men, your donkeys — and put them to his work.

[17] He will take a tenth of your flocks. And you yourselves — you will be his slaves.

[18] When that day comes, you will cry out because of the king you chose. But the LORD will not answer you on that day."

[19] The people refused to listen. "No," they said. "We want a king over us.

[20] Then we'll be like every other nation. A king to judge us. A king to march out before us and fight our battles."

[21] Samuel heard them out, then carried every word back to the LORD.

[22] And the LORD said, "Listen to them. Give them a king." Then Samuel told the men of Israel, "Go home, every one of you."

[1] There was a man from Benjamin — Kish was his name. His line ran back through Abiel, Zeror, Becorath, and Aphiah. He was a Benjaminite of standing, a man with weight behind him.

[2] He had a son named Saul. Young. Striking. No one in Israel matched him — head and shoulders above every other man in the country.

[3] One day Kish's donkeys wandered off. He turned to Saul: "Take one of the servants. Go find them."

[4] So Saul crossed the hill country of Ephraim, swept through the land of Shalishah — nothing. They tried Shaalim — nothing. They cut through Benjamin — still nothing.

[5] When they reached the region of Zuph, Saul finally said to his servant, "Let's turn back. My father's stopped worrying about the donkeys by now — he's worrying about us."

[6] But the servant said, "Wait. There's a man of God in this town. People respect him. Every word he speaks comes true. Let's go to him. Maybe he can tell us which way to go."

[7] Saul answered, "And if we go — what do we bring him? The bread in our packs is gone. We have no gift for the man of God. What have we got?"

[8] The servant tried again: "Look — I have a quarter shekel of silver right here. I'll give it to the man of God so he'll point us the way."

[9] (Back then in Israel, when a man went to inquire of God, he'd say, "Let's go to the seer." The one we now call prophet, they called seer.)

[10] "Good thinking," Saul said. "Let's go." So they headed for the town where the man of God lived.

[11] As they climbed the hill into the town, they came across young women going out to draw water. "Is the seer here?" they asked.

[12] "Yes," the women said. "He's just ahead of you. Hurry — he came to town today because there's a sacrifice up at the high place.

[13] Catch him before he heads up to eat. The people won't begin until he arrives — he blesses the sacrifice, then the guests eat. Go now. You'll find him."

[14] They went up to the town, and just as they entered, there was Samuel — coming straight toward them on his way to the high place.

[15] The day before Saul arrived, the LORD had spoken privately to Samuel:

[16] "Tomorrow, about this time, I'm sending you a man from the territory of Benjamin. Anoint him as ruler over my people Israel. He will rescue them from the Philistines. I have seen my people. Their cry has reached me."

[17] The moment Samuel laid eyes on Saul, the LORD said to him: "That's the man. The one I told you about. He'll govern my people."

[18] Saul walked up to Samuel at the gate. "Excuse me — could you tell me where the seer's house is?"

[19] "I'm the seer," Samuel said. "Go up ahead of me to the high place. You'll eat with me today. In the morning I'll send you off — and I'll tell you everything on your mind.

[20] As for the donkeys you lost three days ago — stop worrying. They've been found. And anyway — who is it that all Israel longs for? You. You and your father's house."

[21] Saul said, "But I'm a Benjaminite — from the smallest tribe in Israel. And my clan is the least of all the clans of Benjamin. Why are you saying this to me?"

[22] Samuel brought Saul and his servant into the dining hall and seated them at the head of the invited guests — about thirty people.

[23] Then Samuel turned to the cook: "Bring out the portion I gave you — the one I told you to set aside."

[24] The cook lifted up the thigh and what was on it and placed it before Saul. Samuel said, "This was kept for you. Eat. I set it aside for this moment — for when I called the guests." So Saul ate with Samuel that day.

[25] After they came down from the high place into the town, Samuel talked with Saul up on the roof.

[26] They rose at dawn. As the first light came, Samuel called up to Saul on the roof: "Get up. I'll send you on your way." Saul got up, and the two of them — he and Samuel — walked out together into the street.

[27] As they neared the edge of town, Samuel said to Saul, "Tell your servant to go on ahead." The servant went on. "But you — stop here a moment. I have a word from God to give you."

[1] Then Samuel took the flask of oil, poured it over Saul's head, kissed him, and said: "The LORD has anointed you ruler over his inheritance. This is no human appointment.

[2] When you leave me today, you'll meet two men near Rachel's tomb, at Zelzah on the border of Benjamin. They'll tell you: 'The donkeys you went looking for have been found. Your father has stopped worrying about them and started worrying about you — asking, What do I do about my son?'

[3] Then keep going. When you reach the oak of Tabor, three men will meet you on their way up to worship God at Bethel. One will be carrying three young goats, another three loaves of bread, another a skin of wine.

[4] They'll greet you and offer you two loaves. Take them.

[5] After that, head to Gibeah of God — the Philistine garrison is there. As you enter the town, you'll run straight into a band of prophets coming down from the high place with harps, tambourines, flutes, and lyres going in front of them, and they'll be prophesying.

[6] Then the Spirit of the LORD will rush over you. You'll prophesy with them. You will be turned into another man.

[7] When these signs hit, do whatever your hand finds to do. God is with you.

[8] Go down ahead of me to Gilgal. I'll come down to you to offer burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. Wait seven days until I arrive and show you what to do."

[9] The moment Saul turned to leave Samuel, God gave him a different heart. Every one of those signs landed that same day.

[10] When they came to Gibeah, there were the prophets — coming right at him. The Spirit of God rushed over him, and he prophesied right along with them.

[11] Everyone who had known him before saw him prophesying with the prophets and started asking each other, "What in the world has happened to the son of Kish? Saul too? Among the prophets?"

[12] A local answered, "And who exactly is their father?" That's how the saying got started: "Is Saul also among the prophets?"

[13] When he finished prophesying, he went up to the high place.

[14] Saul's uncle asked him and his servant, "Where have you two been?" Saul said, "Looking for the donkeys. When we couldn't find them, we went to Samuel."

[15] His uncle said, "Tell me — what did Samuel say to you?"

[16] Saul answered, "He told us straight out that the donkeys had been found." But about the kingship — what Samuel had said — Saul kept his mouth shut.

[17] Samuel called the people together to the LORD at Mizpah

[18] and said to them: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I brought Israel up out of Egypt. I rescued you from Egypt's grip and from every kingdom that had its boot on your neck.

[19] But today you have rejected your God — the One who saves you from every disaster and distress. You've said to him, 'No — give us a king.' So now, take your places before the LORD by tribes and clans."

[20] Samuel had all the tribes of Israel step forward, and the tribe of Benjamin was singled out.

[21] He brought Benjamin forward clan by clan, and the Matrite clan was singled out. Then Saul son of Kish was singled out. But when they went looking for him, he was nowhere to be found.

[22] So they asked the LORD again: "Has the man even arrived?" The LORD said, "He's hiding among the baggage."

[23] They ran and dragged him out. When he stood among the people, he was a head taller than anyone there.

[24] Samuel said to them all, "Look at the one the LORD has chosen. There is no one like him in all the people." And the people roared, "Long live the king!"

[25] Then Samuel laid out for the people the rights and duties of the kingship, wrote them in a scroll, and placed it before the LORD. Then he sent everyone home.

[26] Saul also went home to Gibeah, and with him went a company of warriors whose hearts God had touched.

[27] But certain worthless men sneered, "How is this guy supposed to save us?" They despised him and brought him no gift. Saul let it pass in silence.

[1] Then Nahash the Ammonite marched up and laid siege to Jabesh-gilead. The men of Jabesh said to him, "Cut a deal with us and we'll serve you."

[2] Nahash the Ammonite shot back, "Here's my deal: I gouge out every right eye in this town. I'll make Israel a national disgrace."

[3] The elders of Jabesh said, "Give us seven days. Let us send messengers across Israel. If no one comes to save us, we'll walk out and surrender."

[4] The messengers reached Gibeah — Saul's town — and laid the story out before the people. The whole town broke down weeping.

[5] Right then Saul came in from the field, walking behind his oxen. He said, "Why is everyone crying?" They told him what the men of Jabesh had said.

[6] The Spirit of God rushed onto Saul the moment he heard it. His fury blazed.

[7] He grabbed a pair of oxen, hacked them into pieces, and sent the chunks throughout Israel by the hand of messengers: "Anyone who doesn't fall in behind Saul and Samuel — this is what happens to his oxen." The dread of the LORD fell on the people, and they came out as one man.

[8] He mustered them at Bezek: three hundred thousand from Israel, thirty thousand from Judah.

[9] They told the messengers who had come, "Tell the men of Jabesh-gilead this: 'By the time tomorrow's sun is hot, rescue is yours.'" The messengers carried the word back, and Jabesh rejoiced.

[10] The men of Jabesh sent word to Nahash: "Tomorrow we'll come out to you. Do with us whatever seems right to you."

[11] By morning Saul had split the army into three companies. They broke into the Ammonite camp during the last watch of the night and cut them down until the day grew hot. Whoever survived scattered — no two of them left together.

[12] Then the people turned to Samuel: "Who said 'Saul should not reign over us'? Hand them over — we'll kill them."

[13] But Saul said, "No one dies today. Today the LORD has delivered Israel."

[14] Samuel said to the people, "Come — let's go to Gilgal and renew the kingship there."

[15] So the whole nation went to Gilgal, and there in the presence of the LORD they made Saul king. They offered peace offerings before the LORD, and Saul and every man of Israel celebrated — and it was a wild, joyful celebration.

[1] Samuel turned to all Israel and said: Listen — I've done everything you asked of me. I gave you a king. [2] And there he is, walking out in front of you. Meanwhile I've grown old and gray, and my own sons are standing here among you. I've led you from the time I was young until this very day.

[3] So here I am. Testify against me, right here, before the LORD and before his anointed one. Whose ox did I take? Whose donkey? Who did I cheat? Who did I crush? From whose hand did I accept a bribe to look the other way? Name it, and I'll pay it back.

[4] They answered, You haven't cheated us. You haven't crushed us. You haven't taken a thing from anyone.

[5] He said, The LORD is witness against you today — and his anointed is witness — that you've found nothing in my hand. They said, He is witness.

[6] Then Samuel said to the people: The LORD himself is witness — the one who appointed Moses and Aaron and brought your ancestors up out of Egypt. [7] Now stand still. Let me put it to you straight, before the LORD, every saving act he performed for you and for your ancestors.

[8] When Jacob went down into Egypt and your ancestors cried out, the LORD sent Moses and Aaron, who led them out of that land and settled them in this place. [9] But they forgot the LORD their God. So he sold them — into the hand of Sisera, commander of Hazor's army; into the hand of the Philistines; into the hand of the king of Moab. And these enemies made war on them. [10] Then they cried out to the LORD and said, We've sinned. We abandoned the LORD and served the Baals and the Ashtoreths. Rescue us now from the hand of our enemies, and we'll serve you. [11] So the LORD sent Jerubbaal and Barak and Jephthah and Samuel. He pulled you out of the grip of the enemies surrounding you, and you lived in safety.

[12] But then you saw Nahash, king of the Ammonites, marching against you, and you said to me, No — give us a king to rule over us. As if the LORD your God weren't already your king.

[13] So here he is — the king you chose, the one you asked for. The LORD has given him to you. [14] If you will fear the LORD and serve him, listen to his voice and not rebel against his command — if both you and the king who reigns over you will follow the LORD your God — then good. [15] But if you refuse to listen, if you rebel against the LORD's command, then the hand of the LORD will turn against you, just as it turned against your ancestors.

[16] Now stand still and watch this great thing the LORD is about to do right before your eyes. [17] Isn't it wheat harvest today? I'm going to call on the LORD, and he'll send thunder and rain. Then you'll know — really know — what an evil thing you did in the sight of the LORD when you demanded a king.

[18] So Samuel called out to the LORD, and that very day the LORD sent thunder and rain. And the people stood there terrified — terrified of the LORD and of Samuel.

[19] They begged Samuel, Pray for your servants to the LORD your God. Don't let us die. We've piled this evil on top of all our other sins — asking for a king.

[20] Samuel answered: Don't be afraid. Yes, you've done all this evil. But don't turn away from following the LORD now. Serve him with everything you've got. [21] Don't go chasing after empty things — things that can't help you, can't rescue you, because they're nothing at all. [22] The LORD will not abandon his people. His great name is on the line. It pleased him to make you his own. [23] As for me — I will never sin against the LORD by stopping my prayers for you. I'll keep teaching you the good and right way. [24] Just fear the LORD. Serve him faithfully. With all your heart. Remember the great things he's done for you. [25] But if you keep on doing evil, you and your king will both be swept away.

[1] Saul was thirty when he became king, and he reigned over Israel for forty-two years.

[2] He picked three thousand men from Israel — two thousand stationed with him at Michmash and the hills of Bethel, a thousand with Jonathan at Gibeah of Benjamin. The rest he sent home.

[3] Jonathan struck down the Philistine garrison at Geba, and the Philistines heard about it. Saul blew the trumpet across the whole land: "Let the Hebrews hear!"

[4] And all Israel got the news — Saul has hit the Philistine garrison, and now Israel stinks in Philistine nostrils. The people rallied to Saul at Gilgal.

[5] The Philistines mustered to fight Israel — thirty thousand chariots, six thousand horsemen, and foot soldiers like sand on the seashore. They came up and camped at Michmash, east of Beth-aven.

[6] When the men of Israel saw the trap closing — the pressure crushing in — they hid. In caves. In thickets. Among rocks. In pits. In cisterns.

[7] Some Hebrews even slipped across the Jordan into the territory of Gad and Gilead. Saul was still at Gilgal, and every man with him was shaking.

[8] He waited seven days, the time Samuel had set. But Samuel did not come to Gilgal, and the army was bleeding away from Saul.

[9] So Saul said, "Bring me the burnt offering and the peace offerings." And he offered the burnt offering himself.

[10] The moment he finished — there was Samuel, arriving. Saul went out to meet him, to greet him.

[11] Samuel said, "What have you done?" Saul answered, "I saw the people slipping away from me, and you didn't come when you said, and the Philistines were gathering at Michmash —

[12] so I thought: the Philistines are about to come down on me at Gilgal, and I have not begged the LORD's favor. I forced myself. I offered the burnt offering."

[13] Samuel said to Saul, "You have played the fool. You did not keep the command the LORD your God gave you. He would have established your kingdom over Israel forever.

[14] But now your kingdom will not stand. The LORD has sought out a man after his own heart and appointed him ruler over his people — because you did not keep what the LORD commanded you."

[15] Then Samuel rose and went up from Gilgal to Gibeah of Benjamin. Saul counted what was left of his men — about six hundred.

[16] Saul and his son Jonathan and the men with them stayed at Geba of Benjamin, while the Philistines were camped at Michmash.

[17] Raiders went out from the Philistine camp in three bands — one turned toward Ophrah in the land of Shual,

[18] one toward Beth-horon, and one toward the ridge overlooking the Valley of Zeboim, out toward the wilderness.

[19] Not a single blacksmith could be found in all Israel. The Philistines had made sure of it — "or the Hebrews will make themselves swords and spears."

[20] Every Israelite had to go down to the Philistines to sharpen his plow, his mattock, his axe, his sickle.

[21] The fee was two-thirds of a shekel for plows and mattocks, a third of a shekel for the forks and axes, and for setting the goads.

[22] So on the day of battle, not a sword or spear could be found in the hands of any soldier with Saul and Jonathan. Only Saul and Jonathan had them.

[23] And a detachment of the Philistines pushed out to the pass of Michmash.

[1] One day Jonathan, Saul's son, turned to the young man carrying his armor: "Come on—let's slip across to the Philistine outpost on the other side." But he didn't tell his father.

[2] Saul was sitting under the pomegranate tree at the edge of Gibeah, out by Migron, with about six hundred men around him.

[3] Ahijah was wearing the ephod—son of Ahitub, brother of Ichabod, son of Phinehas, son of Eli, the priest of the LORD at Shiloh. Nobody knew Jonathan was gone.

[4] The pass Jonathan was trying to cross to reach the Philistines ran between two jagged crags—one called Bozez, the other Seneh.

[5] One cliff faced north toward Michmash, the other south toward Geba.

[6] Jonathan said to his armor-bearer: "Come on. Let's cross over to the camp of those uncircumcised men. Maybe the LORD will move for us. Nothing can stop the LORD from delivering—whether we're many or just two."

[7] His armor-bearer answered, "Do whatever's in your heart. Go ahead—I'm with you, heart and soul."

[8] Jonathan said, "Here's how we'll do it. We'll cross over and let them see us.

[9] If they say, 'Stay there till we come down to you,' we hold our ground. We don't go up.

[10] But if they say, 'Come up to us,' then we climb—because that's the sign. The LORD has handed them to us."

[11] So the two of them stepped into the open and the Philistine garrison spotted them. "Look," the Philistines sneered, "the Hebrews are crawling out of their holes."

[12] The men at the outpost called down to Jonathan and his armor-bearer: "Come on up—we've got something to show you." Jonathan turned to his armor-bearer: "Climb. Follow me up. The LORD has given them into Israel's hand."

[13] Jonathan scrambled up on hands and feet, his armor-bearer right behind him. Philistines fell before Jonathan, and his armor-bearer finished them off behind him.

[14] That first strike—Jonathan and his armor-bearer—dropped about twenty men in a space half the size of a furrowed acre.

[15] Then panic ripped through the camp, across the field, through every Philistine—garrison, raiders, all of them. The ground itself shook. Terror from God.

[16] Saul's lookouts at Gibeah of Benjamin saw it: the Philistine horde dissolving, melting in every direction.

[17] Saul ordered his men, "Count the troops. Find out who's missing." They counted. Jonathan and his armor-bearer were gone.

[18] Saul said to Ahijah, "Bring the ark of God." (The ark was with Israel that day.)

[19] But while Saul was still talking to the priest, the chaos in the Philistine camp kept building. So Saul cut him off: "Forget it. Pull your hand back."

[20] Saul and every man with him roared into the fight. They found the Philistines turning their swords on each other—pure mayhem.

[21] The Hebrews who'd been with the Philistines, who had gone up to their camp earlier—they switched sides and joined the Israelites with Saul and Jonathan.

[22] And the Israelites who'd been hiding in the hills of Ephraim—when they heard the Philistines were running, they came pouring out of cover and joined the chase.

[23] So the LORD rescued Israel that day. The fighting swept on past Beth-aven.

[24] But the Israelite men were running on empty, because Saul had bound the army with an oath: "Cursed is the man who eats anything before evening, before I've taken revenge on my enemies." So nobody tasted food.

[25] The whole army came into a wooded area, and there was honey on the ground.

[26] As they walked into the forest—honey running everywhere—nobody lifted a hand to their mouth. They were terrified of the oath.
