[27] But Jonathan hadn't heard his father swear the army to it. He reached out the staff in his hand, dipped the tip into the honeycomb, brought it to his mouth—and his eyes brightened.

[28] One of the soldiers spoke up: "Your father put the army under a strict oath: cursed is the man who eats anything today. That's why they're so faint."

[29] Jonathan said, "My father has wrecked the land. Look at me—just a taste of this honey and my eyes came alive.

[30] Imagine if the men had eaten freely today from the plunder they took off their enemies. Wouldn't the slaughter of Philistines have been even greater?"

[31] That day they cut the Philistines down from Michmash to Aijalon. And the men were collapsing from hunger.

[32] So the army tore into the plunder—grabbing sheep, oxen, calves—butchering them on the ground and eating the meat with the blood still in it.

[33] Word came to Saul: "Look—the men are sinning against the LORD, eating with the blood." He said, "You've betrayed faith. Roll a big stone over here, now."

[34] Then Saul ordered, "Spread out through the men. Tell them: every man bring his ox, his sheep—slaughter it here, eat it. Don't sin against the LORD by eating with the blood." So that night every man brought what he had and slaughtered it there.

[35] And Saul built an altar to the LORD. It was the first altar he ever built for the LORD.

[36] Saul said, "Let's go down after the Philistines tonight. Plunder them till morning. Leave not one man standing." They answered, "Whatever seems right to you, do it." But the priest said, "Let's draw near to God first."

[37] So Saul asked God: "Should I go down after the Philistines? Will you give them into Israel's hand?" But God didn't answer him that day.

[38] Saul said, "Get over here, all you leaders of the people. Find out—figure out—what sin happened today.

[39] As surely as the LORD lives, the one who rescues Israel—even if it's my own son Jonathan, he will die." Not one man in the army spoke.

[40] Then Saul said to all Israel, "You stand on one side. I and my son Jonathan will stand on the other." The men said, "Whatever seems right to you, do it."

[41] So Saul prayed to the LORD, God of Israel: "Give a perfect answer." Jonathan and Saul were taken; the army was cleared.

[42] Saul said, "Cast the lot between me and Jonathan my son." Jonathan was taken.

[43] Saul said to Jonathan, "Tell me what you did." Jonathan told him: "I tasted a little honey on the end of my staff. Here I am. I'll die."

[44] Saul said, "May God do this and more to me, Jonathan—you will surely die."

[45] But the men said to Saul, "Jonathan dies? The one who pulled off this massive rescue in Israel? Never. As surely as the LORD lives, not one hair of his head will hit the ground. He worked with God today." So the army ransomed Jonathan, and he didn't die.

[46] Saul broke off the pursuit of the Philistines, and the Philistines went back to their own territory.

[47] When Saul had secured the kingship over Israel, he fought enemies on every side—Moab, Ammon, Edom, the kings of Zobah, the Philistines. Wherever he turned, he punished them.

[48] He fought hard, struck the Amalekites, and rescued Israel from the hands of those who'd been raiding them.

[49] Saul's sons: Jonathan, Ishvi, Malchishua. His two daughters: the older, Merab; the younger, Michal.

[50] Saul's wife was Ahinoam, daughter of Ahimaaz. The commander of his army was Abner, son of Ner, Saul's uncle.

[51] Kish, Saul's father, and Ner, Abner's father, were sons of Abiel.

[52] The war with the Philistines was relentless all the days of Saul. Whenever Saul saw any strong man, any warrior, he pulled him in.

[1] Samuel said to Saul, "The LORD sent me to anoint you king over his people Israel. So now—listen. Hear the words of the LORD.

[2] This is what the LORD of armies says: I am calling Amalek to account for what they did to Israel—ambushing them on the road when they came up from Egypt.

[3] Go now. Strike Amalek. Devote everything they have to destruction. Spare nothing. Kill man and woman, child and infant, ox and sheep, camel and donkey."

[4] Saul mustered the troops at Telaim—two hundred thousand foot soldiers, plus ten thousand men from Judah.

[5] He advanced to the city of Amalek and set an ambush in the valley.

[6] Saul sent word to the Kenites: "Get out. Move away from the Amalekites or I'll sweep you up with them. You showed kindness to Israel when we came up from Egypt." So the Kenites pulled back from Amalek.

[7] Then Saul cut down the Amalekites from Havilah all the way to Shur, on the edge of Egypt.

[8] He captured Agag king of Amalek alive—but every last one of his people he put to the sword.

[9] Then Saul and the army spared Agag. They spared the best of the sheep and cattle, the fattened calves, the lambs—everything worth keeping. Whatever was worthless and weak, that they destroyed.

[10] The word of the LORD came to Samuel:

[11] "I regret making Saul king. He has turned away from me. He has not carried out my orders." Samuel was shaken. He cried out to the LORD all night.

[12] Early the next morning Samuel set out to find Saul. Word reached him: "Saul went to Carmel. He set up a monument to himself there. Then he turned and went down to Gilgal."

[13] When Samuel caught up to him, Saul said, "Blessed are you by the LORD! I've carried out the LORD's command."

[14] Samuel said, "Then what is this bleating of sheep in my ears? What is this lowing of cattle that I hear?"

[15] Saul said, "They brought them from the Amalekites. The troops spared the best of the sheep and cattle to sacrifice to the LORD your God. The rest we destroyed."

[16] "Stop," Samuel said. "Let me tell you what the LORD said to me last night." "Speak," Saul said.

[17] Samuel said, "When you were small in your own eyes, weren't you made head of the tribes of Israel? The LORD anointed you king over Israel.

[18] The LORD sent you on a mission. He said: Go. Destroy those sinners, the Amalekites. Fight them until they're gone.

[19] Why did you not obey the LORD? Why did you lunge at the plunder and do evil in the LORD's eyes?"

[20] Saul said, "But I did obey the LORD! I went on the mission he gave me. I brought back Agag king of Amalek. I destroyed the Amalekites.

[21] The troops took plunder from what was devoted—sheep and cattle, the best of what should have been destroyed—to sacrifice to the LORD your God at Gilgal."

[22] Samuel said,
"Does the LORD delight in offerings and sacrifices
as much as in a voice that obeys him?
Listen—obedience is better than sacrifice.
Attention is better than the fat of rams.

[23] Rebellion is the sin of sorcery.
Defiance is the evil of idols.
Because you rejected the word of the LORD,
he has rejected you as king."

[24] Saul said to Samuel, "I have sinned. I broke the LORD's command and your words. I was afraid of the people. I listened to their voice.

[25] Now, please—forgive my sin. Come back with me, so I can worship the LORD."

[26] Samuel said, "I will not come back with you. You rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD has rejected you as king over Israel."

[27] Samuel turned to leave. Saul grabbed the hem of his robe—and it tore.

[28] Samuel said, "The LORD has torn the kingdom of Israel from you today and given it to someone better than you—your neighbor.

[29] The Glory of Israel does not lie. He does not change his mind. He is not a man, that he should change his mind."

[30] Saul said, "I have sinned. But please—honor me now in front of the elders of my people, in front of Israel. Come back with me. Let me worship the LORD your God."

[31] So Samuel turned and went back with Saul, and Saul worshiped the LORD.

[32] Then Samuel said, "Bring me Agag, king of Amalek." Agag came to him in chains, confident. "Surely," he said, "the bitterness of death is past."

[33] Samuel said,
"As your sword made women childless,
so your mother will be childless among women."
And Samuel hacked Agag to pieces before the LORD at Gilgal.

[34] Samuel left for Ramah. Saul went up to his house at Gibeah of Saul.

[35] Samuel never saw Saul again as long as he lived. He grieved for Saul. And the LORD regretted making Saul king over Israel.

[1] Yahweh said to Samuel, "How long will you grieve over Saul? I've rejected him from being king over Israel. Fill your horn with oil and go. I'm sending you to Jesse the Bethlehemite — I've spotted my king among his sons."

[2] Samuel said, "How can I go? If Saul hears, he'll kill me."

Yahweh said, "Take a heifer with you and say, 'I've come to sacrifice to Yahweh.'

[3] Invite Jesse to the sacrifice. I'll show you what to do then. You'll anoint the one I point to."

[4] Samuel did what Yahweh said and went to Bethlehem. The elders of the town came out trembling to meet him. "Do you come in peace?" they asked.

[5] "In peace," he said. "I've come to sacrifice to Yahweh. Consecrate yourselves and come with me." Then he consecrated Jesse and his sons and called them to the sacrifice.

[6] When they arrived, Samuel caught sight of Eliab and thought, "Surely Yahweh's anointed stands here before him."

[7] But Yahweh said to Samuel, "Don't look at his face or his height. I've passed him over. Yahweh doesn't see the way humans see. Humans see the surface; Yahweh sees the heart."

[8] Then Jesse called Abinadab and walked him past Samuel. "Not this one either," Samuel said. "Yahweh hasn't chosen him."

[9] Jesse brought Shammah past. "Not this one either."

[10] Seven sons of Jesse passed before Samuel. Samuel told Jesse, "Yahweh hasn't chosen any of these."

[11] Then he asked, "Is this all the boys?"

"There's still the youngest," Jesse said. "He's out with the sheep."

"Send for him," Samuel said. "We won't sit down to eat until he gets here."

[12] So they sent and brought him in — ruddy, with beautiful eyes, and handsome. And Yahweh said, "Up. Anoint him. This is the one."

[13] Samuel took the horn of oil and anointed him there in front of his brothers. And from that day forward, the Spirit of Yahweh rushed upon David. Samuel got up and went home to Ramah.

[14] Meanwhile, the Spirit of Yahweh had left Saul, and a tormenting spirit from Yahweh began to terrorize him.

[15] Saul's servants said, "Look — a tormenting spirit from God is shaking you.

[16] Let our lord command us — your servants here — to find a man skilled with the lyre. When the dark spirit from God falls on you, he'll play, and you'll feel better."

[17] Saul said, "Find me someone who plays well. Bring him to me."

[18] One of the young men spoke up: "I've seen a son of Jesse the Bethlehemite who can play. He's a warrior, a man of valor, sharp with words, good-looking — and Yahweh is with him."

[19] So Saul sent messengers to Jesse: "Send me your son David, the one with the sheep."

[20] Jesse loaded a donkey with bread, a skin of wine, and a young goat, and sent them with David his son to Saul.

[21] David came to Saul and entered his service. Saul loved him deeply and made him his armor-bearer.

[22] Saul sent word to Jesse: "Let David stay in my service. He has found favor in my eyes."

[23] And whenever the dark spirit from God came over Saul, David would take up his lyre and play. Saul would breathe again. The shadow would lift. The tormenting spirit would let him go.

[1] The Philistines pulled their forces together for war. They massed at Socoh in Judah and pitched camp between Socoh and Azekah, at Ephes-dammim. [2] Saul and the men of Israel rallied and camped in the Valley of Elah, lining up for battle against the Philistines. [3] The Philistines held one ridge, Israel held the other, with the valley gaping between them.

[4] Out of the Philistine camp stepped a champion. His name was Goliath of Gath. He stood nearly ten feet tall. [5] A bronze helmet sat on his head, and he wore a coat of scaled bronze armor weighing one hundred twenty-five pounds. [6] Bronze guards on his legs, a bronze javelin slung across his back. [7] His spear shaft was thick as a weaver's beam, its iron head alone weighing fifteen pounds. A shield-bearer walked ahead of him.

[8] He planted himself there and bellowed at the Israelite ranks: "Why have you all come out to line up for war? I am a Philistine. You are Saul's slaves. Pick a man. Send him down to me. [9] If he can fight me and kill me, we will be your servants. But if I take him down — if I kill him — you become ours. You will serve us." [10] The Philistine roared on: "I spit on the armies of Israel today! Give me a man — let's fight!" [11] Saul heard it. All Israel heard it. And they were shattered, terrified to the bone.

[12] Now David was the son of an Ephrathite named Jesse from Bethlehem in Judah. Jesse had eight sons. By Saul's day, he was old, well along in years. [13] The three eldest had followed Saul to war — Eliab the firstborn, Abinadab next, then Shammah. [14] David was the youngest. The three oldest were with Saul, [15] but David shuttled back and forth from Saul's camp to Bethlehem, tending his father's sheep.

[16] Morning and evening, for forty days straight, the Philistine strode out and took his stand.

[17] One day Jesse said to David, "Take this sack of roasted grain and these ten loaves of bread to your brothers in camp. Hurry. [18] And these ten wheels of cheese — give them to the commander of their unit. Check on your brothers. See how they are. Bring back some token from them. [19] Saul and your brothers and all the men of Israel are in the Valley of Elah, fighting the Philistines."

[20] David got up early, left the flock with a keeper, loaded up, and went, just as Jesse told him. He reached the camp circle just as the army was moving out to the battle line, shouting the war cry. [21] Israel and the Philistines lined up facing each other, rank to rank. [22] David dropped his supplies with the supply officer, ran to the front, and went looking for his brothers to see if they were all right. [23] He was still talking with them when the champion stepped out — Goliath of Gath — and shouted his usual taunts. David heard every word.

[24] The Israelite soldiers, the moment they saw him, broke and ran. They were terrified. [25] They kept saying, "Have you seen this man who keeps coming out? He's here to humiliate Israel. The king has put a fortune on his head — and his daughter — and tax exemption for the man's whole family in Israel, for whoever kills him." [26] David turned to the men standing near him: "What does a man get for killing this Philistine and wiping the disgrace off Israel? Who does this uncircumcised Philistine think he is, mocking the armies of the living God?" [27] They told him the same thing again: "That's what the man who kills him gets."

[28] Eliab, David's oldest brother, overheard him talking to the men, and his temper flared. "What are you even doing down here? Who is watching those few sheep in the wilderness? I know your arrogance, your reckless heart — you only came down to watch the battle." [29] David said, "What did I do now? It was just a question." [30] He turned away from him to someone else and asked the same thing. The men gave him the same answer.

[31] Word of what David had said got around. It reached Saul, who sent for him.

[32] David said to Saul, "Don't let anyone lose heart on account of this man. Your servant will go and fight him." [33] Saul said, "You cannot go up against this Philistine. You're a boy. He has been a warrior since his youth." [34] David answered, "Your servant tends his father's sheep. When a lion came — or a bear — and carried off a lamb from the flock, [35] I went after it, struck it down, and tore the lamb from its mouth. When it turned on me, I grabbed it by the beard, struck it, and killed it. [36] Lion and bear — your servant has killed them both. And this uncircumcised Philistine will be like one of them, because he has mocked the armies of the living God. [37] The Lord who saved me from the paw of the lion and the paw of the bear — He will save me from the hand of this Philistine." Saul said to David, "Go. And may the Lord be with you."

[38] Saul dressed David in his own gear — a bronze helmet on his head, a coat of armor on his body. [39] David strapped Saul's sword over the armor and tried to walk. He couldn't. He had never worn this. "I can't move in these," he said to Saul. "I'm not used to them." He stripped them off.

[40] Then he took his staff in his hand, picked five smooth stones from the streambed, and dropped them into the shepherd's pouch he carried. With his sling in his hand, he walked out to meet the Philistine.

[41] The Philistine came on, closing the distance, his shield-bearer in front of him. [42] He looked David over. And he despised him — just a boy, ruddy and handsome. [43] The Philistine said to David, "Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks?" And he cursed David by his gods. [44] "Come here," the Philistine said. "I'll feed your flesh to the birds of the air and the beasts of the field."

[45] David said to the Philistine: "You come at me with sword and spear and javelin. I come at you in the name of the Lord of Hosts, the God of the armies of Israel — the one you have mocked. [46] Today the Lord will hand you over to me. I will strike you down. I will take your head off your body. And today I will give the corpses of the Philistine army to the birds of the air and the wild beasts of the earth — so that all the earth will know there is a God in Israel. [47] And everyone gathered here will know that the Lord does not save by sword or spear. The battle belongs to the Lord. He will hand all of you over to us."

[48] The Philistine started forward, moving in to meet him. David ran — sprinted toward the battle line, straight at the Philistine. [49] He reached into his pouch, pulled out a stone, slung it — and it struck the Philistine in the forehead. The stone sank in. He fell on his face to the ground.

[50] So David beat the Philistine with a sling and a stone. He struck him down. He killed him. No sword in his hand. [51] David ran and stood over him, drew the Philistine's own sword from its sheath, and finished him — cut off his head with it.

When the Philistines saw their champion was dead, they broke and ran. [52] The men of Israel and Judah surged up, shouting, and chased the Philistines all the way to the entrance of the valley and the gates of Ekron. Philistine dead littered the road to Shaaraim, all the way to Gath and Ekron. [53] The Israelites came back from chasing the Philistines and looted their camp. [54] David took the Philistine's head and brought it to Jerusalem. The weapons he kept in his own tent.

[55] When Saul had watched David go out against the Philistine, he had said to Abner, his commander, "Whose son is that boy, Abner?" Abner said, "On your life, O king — I have no idea." [56] The king said, "Find out whose son the young man is." [57] So when David came back from killing the Philistine, Abner brought him to Saul, the head of the Philistine still in his hand. [58] Saul said, "Whose son are you, young man?" David said, "The son of your servant Jesse, the Bethlehemite."

[1] The moment David finished speaking with Saul, something locked between him and Jonathan — soul fused to soul. Jonathan loved him as he loved his own life.

[2] From that day on, Saul kept David close and would not let him return to his father's house.

[3] Jonathan cut a covenant with David, because he loved him as his own soul.

[4] He stripped off the robe he was wearing and gave it to David — his tunic too, even his sword, his bow, his belt.

[5] Wherever Saul sent David, he went, and he succeeded. Saul put him in command of the fighting men, and the whole army approved — even Saul's own officers.

[6] But as they were coming home, when David returned from cutting down the Philistine, the women streamed out of every town in Israel to meet King Saul — singing, dancing, tambourines bright, lutes ringing, joy everywhere.

[7] And the women answered each other in their song: *Saul has killed his thousands — and David his tens of thousands.*

[8] Saul burned. The line landed like a blade. *They've given David tens of thousands,* he muttered, *and me only thousands. What's left for him but the throne itself?*

[9] From that day forward, Saul watched David out of the corner of his eye.

[10] The next day an evil spirit from God rushed on Saul, and he raved inside the house while David played the lyre for him, as he did day after day. Saul had a spear in his hand —

[11] and he hurled it, thinking, *I'll pin David to the wall.* Twice. Both times David twisted away.

[12] Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with David — and had left Saul.

[13] So Saul sent him away, made him a commander of a thousand, and David led the troops out and back.

[14] Everything he touched succeeded, because the LORD was with him.

[15] When Saul saw how he kept winning, he was terrified of him.

[16] But all Israel and Judah loved David — he was the one leading them out and bringing them home.

[17] Then Saul said to David, *Here is my older daughter, Merab. I'll give her to you as your wife — just be a fighting man for me and wage the LORD's battles.* Saul was thinking: *Better the Philistines kill him than me.*

[18] David answered, *Who am I? What is my life, what is my father's clan in Israel, that I should be the king's son-in-law?*

[19] But when the time came for Merab to be given to David, she was handed to Adriel of Meholah instead.

[20] Meanwhile Saul's daughter Michal had fallen in love with David. They told Saul, and it pleased him.

[21] *I'll give her to him,* Saul thought, *and she'll be the bait — and the hand of the Philistines will be on him.* So Saul said to David, *Today you have a second chance to become my son-in-law.*

[22] He instructed his servants: *Speak to David privately. Say — Look, the king is pleased with you, all his servants love you. Become his son-in-law.*

[23] Saul's men whispered the words in David's ear. David said, *Do you think it's a small thing to marry into the king's family? I'm a nobody. I have nothing.*

[24] The servants reported back: *This is what David said.*

[25] Saul said, *Tell David this — the king wants no bride-price. Just a hundred Philistine foreskins, payback against the king's enemies.* Saul was plotting to drop David into Philistine hands.

[26] When the servants relayed the offer, David was pleased. Becoming the king's son-in-law — yes. Before the deadline ran out,

[27] David got up and went, he and his men, and struck down two hundred Philistines. He brought back the foreskins and counted them out in full before the king, to become his son-in-law. So Saul gave him Michal his daughter as his wife.

[28] Saul saw it clearly now — the LORD was with David, and Michal loved him.

[29] And Saul's fear of David only deepened. Saul was David's enemy from then on, every day of his life.

[30] The Philistine commanders kept marching out to fight, and every time they did, David outperformed every officer Saul had. His name became a fire that spread.

[1] Saul told his son Jonathan and all his servants to kill David. But Jonathan loved David fiercely.

[2] So he warned him: "My father Saul is hunting your life. Be careful tomorrow morning. Hide. Stay out of sight.

[3] I'll go stand beside my father in the field where you're hiding. I'll bring up your name and see what he says. Whatever I learn, I'll tell you."

[4] Jonathan spoke up for David to his father. "Don't let the king sin against his servant David. He hasn't sinned against you — his work has been nothing but good for you.

[5] He took his life in his hands when he struck down the Philistine. The LORD won a great victory for all Israel through him. You saw it. You celebrated. Why would you sin against innocent blood by killing David for no reason?"

[6] Saul listened to Jonathan. He swore: "As the LORD lives, he will not be killed."

[7] Jonathan called David back and told him everything. He brought David to Saul, and David served him as before.

[8] War broke out again. David marched out and fought the Philistines, struck them so hard they fled before him.

[9] Then an evil spirit from the LORD came over Saul as he sat in his house, spear in hand, while David played the lyre.

[10] Saul tried to pin David to the wall with the spear. David dodged. The spear sank into the wall, and David ran into the night.

[11] Saul sent messengers to David's house to stake it out and kill him at dawn. But Michal, David's wife, warned him: "If you don't escape tonight, tomorrow you're a dead man."

[12] She lowered him through a window. He ran. He escaped.

[13] Michal took the household idol and laid it on the bed, put a goat-hair pillow at its head, and covered it with a blanket.

[14] When Saul's messengers came for David, she told them he was sick.

[15] Saul sent them back to see for themselves: "Bring him to me — bed and all — so I can kill him."

[16] The messengers walked in and found the idol on the bed with the goat-hair pillow at its head.

[17] Saul confronted Michal: "Why did you deceive me like this? Why did you let my enemy go?" Michal answered, "He told me, 'Let me go — or I'll kill you.'"

[18] David ran. He escaped. He came to Samuel at Ramah and told him everything Saul had done. The two of them went and stayed at Naioth.

[19] Word reached Saul: David is at Naioth in Ramah.

[20] Saul sent men to seize him. They saw the company of prophets prophesying, with Samuel standing over them as their leader — and the Spirit of God came down on Saul's men, and they started prophesying too.

[21] When Saul heard, he sent more men. They prophesied too. He sent a third group. Same thing.

[22] Finally Saul went himself. He came to the great cistern at Secu and asked, "Where are Samuel and David?" Someone said, "At Naioth in Ramah."

[23] So he set out for Naioth in Ramah. And the Spirit of God came on him too — he walked the whole way prophesying until he arrived at Naioth.

[24] He stripped off his clothes. He prophesied before Samuel. He collapsed and lay there naked all that day and all that night. That's why people still say, "Is Saul also among the prophets?"

[1] David fled from Naioth in Ramah and came to Jonathan. "What have I done?" he asked. "What's my crime? How have I wronged your father that he wants me dead?"

[2] "Never," Jonathan said. "You won't die. Listen — my father does nothing, big or small, without telling me. Why would he hide this from me? It isn't true."

[3] But David swore back, "Your father knows exactly how much you care for me. He's thinking, 'Jonathan can't know this — it would break him.' But I swear, as surely as the Lord lives and you live, I am one step from death."

[4] Jonathan said, "Whatever you want, I'll do it."

[5] David said, "Tomorrow is the new moon, and I'm supposed to sit at the king's table. Let me go. I'll hide in the field until the evening of the third day.

[6] If your father notices I'm gone, tell him, 'David begged me to let him run home to Bethlehem — there's an annual sacrifice for the whole clan.'

[7] If he says, 'Fine,' I'm safe. If he flies into a rage, you'll know he's decided to destroy me.

[8] Stand by me — you swore a covenant with me before the Lord. If there's guilt on me, kill me yourself. Just don't hand me over to your father."

[9] "Never," Jonathan said. "If I had any idea my father was set on killing you, don't you think I'd tell you?"

[10] David asked, "Who'll tell me if your father answers you with venom?"

[11] "Come out to the field," Jonathan said. And they walked out together.

[12] Jonathan said to David, "By the Lord, the God of Israel — I'll sound my father out by this time tomorrow, or the day after. If he's well-disposed toward you, I'll send word. I swear it.

[13] But if my father intends harm and I fail to warn you and get you safely away — may the Lord strike me down. May the Lord be with you the way he was with my father.

[14] And if I'm still alive, show me the Lord's faithful love. And if I die,

[15] never cut off your loyalty from my house — not even when the Lord has wiped every one of David's enemies off the face of the earth."

[16] So Jonathan bound himself by covenant to the house of David, saying, "May the Lord call David's enemies to account."

[17] Then he made David swear it again, by the love he had for him — because Jonathan loved him as his own soul.

[18] Jonathan said, "Tomorrow is the new moon. You'll be missed — your seat will be empty.

[19] On the third day, go down to the place where you hid the last time this happened, and stay by the stone Ezel.

[20] I'll shoot three arrows toward it, like I'm aiming at a target.

[21] Then I'll send my boy: 'Go find the arrows.' If I call, 'They're on this side of you — bring them here,' come out. You're safe. As the Lord lives, nothing's wrong.

[22] But if I call to the boy, 'The arrows are past you, farther on' — go. The Lord is sending you away.

[23] And the promise we just made — the Lord stands between us forever as witness."

[24] So David hid in the field. When the new moon came, the king sat down to the feast.

[25] He took his usual seat by the wall. Jonathan stood, Abner sat at Saul's side, and David's place was empty.

[26] Saul said nothing that day. He figured, "Something must have happened — he's unclean, that's all."

[27] But the next day, the second day of the moon, David's place was still empty. Saul said to Jonathan, "Why hasn't the son of Jesse come to the table — yesterday or today?"

[28] Jonathan answered, "David begged me to let him go to Bethlehem.

[29] He said, 'Please, let me go. Our family has a sacrifice in town and my brother summoned me. If I matter to you, let me slip away to see my brothers.' That's why he's not at the king's table."

[30] Saul's fury exploded. "You son of a perverse, rebellious woman! You think I don't know? You've chosen the son of Jesse — to your own shame and the shame of the mother who bore you!

[31] As long as that boy is alive on this earth, you have no throne, no kingdom. Send for him. Bring him to me. He's a dead man."

[32] Jonathan answered his father, "Why kill him? What has he done?"

[33] Saul hurled his spear at him — meaning to kill him. Then Jonathan knew: his father had made up his mind to murder David.

[34] Jonathan rose from the table in a burning rage. He ate nothing on the second day of the moon — grieving for David, sickened by what his father had done to him.

[35] In the morning Jonathan went out to the field for his meeting with David. A small boy was with him.

[36] He told the boy, "Run. Find the arrows I shoot." The boy ran, and Jonathan shot an arrow beyond him.

[37] When the boy reached the spot where the arrow had landed, Jonathan called after him, "The arrow is past you, farther on!"

[38] Then: "Hurry! Don't stop! Run!" The boy gathered the arrows and brought them back to his master.

[39] He had no idea what was happening. Only Jonathan and David knew.

[40] Jonathan handed his weapons to the boy and told him, "Go. Take these to town."

[41] When the boy was gone, David rose from behind the stone, fell to the ground, and bowed three times. Then they kissed each other and wept together — David most of all.

[42] Jonathan said, "Go in peace. We've sworn it together in the Lord's name: the Lord stands between you and me, between your descendants and mine, forever." Then David rose and left, and Jonathan went back into the city.

[1] David made his way to Nob, to Ahimelech the priest. Ahimelech came out to meet him, trembling. "Why are you alone?" he asked. "Why is no one with you?"

[2] David told him, "The king sent me on a mission. He said, 'Tell no one anything about what I'm sending you to do or what I've ordered.' I've arranged to meet my men at a certain place.

[3] Now then — what have you got on hand? Give me five loaves of bread, or whatever you can find."

[4] The priest answered, "I don't have any ordinary bread here — only the consecrated bread. That is, if your men have kept themselves from women."

[5] David said, "Of course women have been kept from us — same as always when I go out. The men's gear is holy even on an ordinary mission, and how much more so today."

[6] So the priest gave him the consecrated bread, since there was nothing else there but the bread of the Presence, which had been removed from before the LORD and replaced that same day with fresh, hot loaves.

[7] One of Saul's servants happened to be there that day, detained before the LORD — Doeg the Edomite, the chief of Saul's herdsmen.

[8] David said to Ahimelech, "Is there a spear or a sword here you could give me? I didn't even bring my own weapon — the king's business was urgent."

[9] The priest said, "The sword of Goliath the Philistine — the one you killed in the Valley of Elah — it's here, wrapped in cloth behind the ephod. If you want it, take it. There's nothing else." David said, "There's none like it. Give it to me."

[10] That day David ran. He fled from Saul and went straight to Achish, king of Gath.

[11] But Achish's servants murmured to him, "Isn't this David, the king of his land? Isn't he the one they sing about in their dances — *Saul has killed his thousands, and David his tens of thousands*?"

[12] David turned these words over in his heart and grew very afraid of Achish, king of Gath.

[13] So he changed his behavior right there in front of them. He played the madman in their hands — scratching at the doors of the gate, letting spit run down into his beard.

[14] Achish said to his men, "Look at him — the man is insane. Why bring him to me?

[15] Am I short on lunatics, that you bring this one here to rave in my presence? Is this the kind of man who comes into my house?"

[1] David slipped out of Gath and hid in the cave at Adullam. When his brothers and his father's whole household heard, they came down to him there. [2] And others gathered to him too — anyone in trouble, anyone drowning in debt, anyone with a grudge against life. He became their captain. About four hundred men.

[3] From there David went to Mizpeh in Moab and said to the king of Moab, "Let my father and mother stay with you until I know what God intends to do with me." [4] He brought them before the king of Moab, and they stayed there the whole time David was holed up in the stronghold.

[5] Then the prophet Gad came to David and said, "Don't stay in the stronghold. Go. Get back to the land of Judah." So David left and made his way to the forest of Hereth.

[6] Word reached Saul that David had been spotted, and the men with him too. Saul was sitting in Gibeah under the tamarisk on the high ground, his spear in his hand, his officers ringed around him. [7] He said to them, "Listen to me, you Benjaminites. Is the son of Jesse going to hand out fields and vineyards to every one of you? Is he going to make you all commanders of thousands and hundreds? [8] Is that why you've all conspired against me? Not one of you tipped me off when my own son cut a deal with the son of Jesse. Not one of you cared enough to tell me that my son has set my own servant against me — waiting to ambush me, like he's doing today."

[9] Then Doeg the Edomite spoke up — he was standing there with Saul's officers. "I saw the son of Jesse," he said. "He came to Nob, to Ahimelech son of Ahitub. [10] Ahimelech consulted the LORD for him, gave him provisions, and handed him the sword of Goliath the Philistine."

[11] The king summoned Ahimelech son of Ahitub, the priest, and his entire family — the priests of Nob. They all came before the king. [12] Saul said, "Listen to me, son of Ahitub." "Here I am, my lord," he answered. [13] Saul said, "Why did you and the son of Jesse conspire against me? You gave him bread and a sword and you consulted God for him — so he could rise up against me and lie in wait, like he's doing today?"

[14] Ahimelech answered the king: "Who among all your servants is as faithful as David? The king's own son-in-law, captain of your bodyguard, honored in your house? [15] Was this the first time I'd consulted God for him? Of course not. Let the king not lay this charge on me or any of my father's house. Your servant knew nothing — nothing — about any of this."

[16] The king said, "You will die, Ahimelech. You and your whole father's house."

[17] And he gave the order to the guards standing near him: "Turn. Kill the priests of the LORD. They've thrown in with David. They knew he was running and never told me." But the king's servants would not lift a hand against the priests of the LORD.

[18] So the king turned to Doeg. "You. Strike them down." And Doeg the Edomite turned and struck the priests himself. That day he killed eighty-five men who wore the linen ephod. [19] And Nob, the city of priests — he put it to the sword. Men, women, children, nursing infants, cattle, donkeys, sheep — all of it, by the edge of the sword.

[20] One son of Ahimelech son of Ahitub escaped. His name was Abiathar. He fled to David. [21] Abiathar told David that Saul had butchered the priests of the LORD. [22] David said to Abiathar, "I knew that day — when Doeg the Edomite was there — I knew he would tell Saul. I am the reason every soul in your father's house is dead. [23] Stay with me. Don't be afraid. The man who wants my life wants yours. With me you are safe."

[1] Word reached David: "The Philistines are attacking Keilah. They're looting the threshing floors."

[2] David asked the LORD, "Should I go strike down these Philistines?"

The LORD answered, "Go. Strike them down. Rescue Keilah."

[3] But David's men told him, "We're already scared stiff here in Judah. How much worse if we march into Keilah against Philistine battle lines?"

[4] So David asked the LORD a second time. The LORD answered him: "Get up. Go down to Keilah. I'm handing the Philistines over to you."

[5] David and his men went to Keilah, fought the Philistines, drove off their livestock, and crushed them in a slaughter. David saved the people of Keilah.

[6] Now when Abiathar son of Ahimelech had fled to David at Keilah, he had come with an ephod in his hand.

[7] Word reached Saul that David had walked into Keilah. Saul said, "God has handed him to me. He's locked himself inside a town with gates and bars. He's trapped."

[8] Saul mustered the whole army for war — to march on Keilah and surround David and his men.

[9] David knew Saul was scheming against him. He told Abiathar the priest, "Bring the ephod."

[10] Then David prayed: "LORD, God of Israel, your servant has heard for certain that Saul is coming to Keilah to destroy the town because of me.

[11] Will the leaders of Keilah hand me over to him? Will Saul really come down, like I've heard? LORD, God of Israel, please — tell your servant."

The LORD said, "He'll come down."

[12] David asked, "Will the leaders of Keilah hand me and my men over to Saul?"

The LORD said, "They'll hand you over."

[13] So David and his men — about six hundred of them — got up and left Keilah, moving from place to place. When Saul heard David had slipped away from Keilah, he called off the campaign.

[14] David stayed in the wilderness strongholds, in the hill country of the Ziph wilderness. Saul hunted him day after day, but God never let him fall into Saul's hand.

[15] David realized Saul had come out to take his life. He was in the Ziph wilderness, at Horesh.

[16] Then Jonathan, Saul's son, got up and went to David at Horesh and strengthened his hand in God.

[17] He told him, "Don't be afraid. My father Saul's hand will never find you. You'll be king over Israel, and I'll be second to you. Even my father Saul knows this."

[18] The two of them cut a covenant before the LORD. David stayed at Horesh. Jonathan went home.

[19] The men of Ziph came up to Saul at Gibeah. "Isn't David hiding right under our noses — in the strongholds at Horesh, on the hill of Hachilah, south of Jeshimon?

[20] Now come down, your majesty — whenever you want. Our job will be to hand him over to you."

[21] Saul said, "Blessed are you by the LORD, for caring about me.

[22] Now go. Make absolutely sure. Find out exactly where he goes and who's seen him there. They tell me he's cunning.

[23] Scout every hiding place where he could possibly disappear. Come back to me with solid information, and I'll go with you. If he's in the land, I'll hunt him down through every clan in Judah."

[24] They got up and went ahead of Saul to Ziph. David and his men were in the Maon wilderness, in the Arabah, south of Jeshimon.

[25] Saul and his men set out to search. They told David, and he went down to the rock and stayed in the Maon wilderness. Saul heard and pursued David into the Maon wilderness.

[26] Saul was on one side of the mountain; David and his men were on the other side. David was scrambling to get away from Saul, and Saul and his men were closing in to capture them —

[27] when a messenger came to Saul: "Hurry. Come quick. The Philistines are raiding the land."

[28] So Saul broke off the chase and turned to meet the Philistines. That's why they call that place the Rock of Escape.

[29] David went up from there and lived in the strongholds of En-gedi.

[1] When Saul came back from chasing the Philistines, word reached him: "David is in the wilderness of En-gedi."

[2] So Saul took three thousand handpicked men from all Israel and went hunting for David and his men out on the Crags of the Wild Goats.

[3] He came to the sheep pens along the road, where there was a cave, and Saul went in to relieve himself. David and his men were sitting in the deep recesses of that very cave.

[4] His men whispered, "This is it. This is the day the LORD told you about — 'I'll hand your enemy over to you, do with him what you want.'" David crept forward and quietly sliced off the corner of Saul's robe.

[5] But the moment he did it, his conscience struck him hard.

[6] He said to his men, "God forbid I do this to my lord — the LORD's anointed — to raise my hand against him. He's the one the LORD chose."

[7] With those words David held his men back. He wouldn't let them touch Saul. And Saul stood up, walked out of the cave, and went on his way.

[8] Then David got up, went out after him, and called out, "My lord the king!" Saul turned around. David bowed low, face to the ground.

[9] And David said to Saul: "Why do you keep listening to people who tell you, 'David wants you dead'?

[10] Look — today your own eyes have seen it. The LORD handed you over to me in that cave. They told me to kill you. But I spared you. I said, 'I will not lift my hand against my lord. He is the LORD's anointed.'

[11] Look, my father — look at this in my hand. The corner of your robe. I cut this off and I did not kill you. See it for what it is: there is no treason in me, no rebellion. I haven't wronged you. But you're hunting me down to take my life.

[12] Let the LORD judge between us. Let the LORD avenge me. But my hand — my hand will never touch you.

[13] As the old proverb says: 'From the wicked comes wickedness.' My hand will never touch you.

[14] Who is the king of Israel chasing? Who are you running down? A dead dog. A single flea.

[15] Let the LORD be the judge. Let him decide between us. Let him see this, plead my case, and rescue me from your hand."

[16] When David finished speaking, Saul said, "Is that your voice, my son David?" And Saul broke down and wept.

[17] He said, "You're more righteous than I am. You repaid me with good — I repaid you with evil.

[18] You've shown me today what kind of man you are. The LORD handed me over to you, and you didn't kill me.

[19] When a man finds his enemy, does he send him off in peace? May the LORD reward you well for what you've done for me today.

[20] And now I know it — you will be king. The kingdom of Israel will be established in your hand.

[21] Swear to me by the LORD that you won't wipe out my descendants after me. That you won't erase my name from my father's house."

[22] So David swore it to Saul. Then Saul went home, and David and his men climbed back up to their stronghold.

[1] Samuel died. All Israel gathered to mourn him, and they buried him at his home in Ramah. Then David got up and went down into the wilderness of Paran.

[2] In Maon there was a man whose holdings were in Carmel — a man of serious wealth: three thousand sheep, a thousand goats. It was sheep-shearing time in Carmel.

[3] The man's name was Nabal. His wife's name was Abigail. She was sharp and beautiful. He was harsh and mean in his dealings — a Calebite.

[4] David, out in the wilderness, heard that Nabal was shearing his sheep.

[5] So he sent ten of his young men. "Go up to Carmel," he told them. "Find Nabal. Greet him in my name.

[6] Say this to him: 'Long life to you. Peace to you, peace to your household, peace to everything that's yours.

[7] I hear it's shearing time. Listen — your shepherds were with us out here. We never mistreated them. Nothing of theirs went missing the whole time they were in Carmel.

[8] Ask your own men. They'll tell you. So show my men some kindness — we've come on a good day. Give whatever you can spare to your servants and to your son David.'"

[9] David's men arrived, delivered the message word for word in David's name, and then they waited.

[10] Nabal answered them: "Who is David? Who is this son of Jesse? Servants are running off from their masters all over the place these days.

[11] You think I'm going to take my bread, my water, the meat I slaughtered for my shearers, and hand it over to men I don't know — men from God-knows-where?"

[12] David's men turned around, went back, and reported every word.

[13] David said to his men, "Strap on your swords." They strapped them on. David strapped on his. About four hundred men marched out behind David. Two hundred stayed with the supplies.

[14] Meanwhile, one of Nabal's servants told Abigail: "David sent messengers from the wilderness to greet our master, and he flew at them.

[15] But those men treated us well. We weren't harmed. Nothing went missing the whole time we were out in the fields with them.

[16] They were a wall around us, night and day, the entire time we were with them shepherding.

[17] Now think. Figure something out. Disaster is coming for our master and the whole household. And he's such a worthless brute nobody can talk to him."

[18] Abigail moved fast. Two hundred loaves. Two skins of wine. Five sheep already dressed. Five seahs of roasted grain. A hundred clusters of raisins. Two hundred cakes of pressed figs. She loaded it all on donkeys.

[19] She told her servants, "Go on ahead. I'm right behind you." She said nothing to her husband Nabal.

[20] She was riding her donkey, coming down under cover of the mountain — and there was David coming down from the other side, straight toward her. They met.

[21] David had been muttering to himself: "What a waste — guarding everything that belonged to that man in the wilderness so that nothing of his went missing. And this is how he pays me back: evil for good.

[22] God do worse than the worst to me if by morning a single male of his is still breathing."

[23] When Abigail saw David, she scrambled off her donkey and threw herself face-down before him, bowing to the ground.

[24] She fell at his feet. "On me, my lord — on me alone let the blame fall. Let your servant speak. Hear me out.

[25] Please — pay no attention to that worthless man Nabal. His name says it all: Nabal means fool, and folly is what he is. I didn't see your men when they came.

[26] And now, my lord — as surely as the LORD lives and you live — the LORD has held you back from spilling blood, from taking matters into your own hand. Let your enemies, anyone wishing you harm, end up like Nabal.

[27] This gift your servant has brought — let it be given to the young men who follow you.

[28] Forgive your servant's offense. The LORD will build my lord a sure house, because my lord fights the LORD's battles. No wrong will be found in you all your days.

[29] If anyone rises to chase you, to come for your life — your life will be bound in the bundle of the living with the LORD your God. But the lives of your enemies — he'll sling them out as from the pocket of a sling.

[30] And when the LORD has done for my lord everything good he has spoken — when he makes you ruler over Israel —

[31] then this won't haunt you. No stumbling block. No regret in your heart over needless bloodshed, over my lord taking vengeance into his own hands. And when the LORD makes good on his promise to my lord, remember your servant."

[32] David said to Abigail, "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who sent you to meet me today.

[33] Blessed be your judgment. Blessed be you — you who kept me today from spilling blood, from taking my own vengeance.

[34] As surely as the LORD, the God of Israel, lives — the One who held me back from harming you — if you hadn't come, hadn't come fast, not a single male of Nabal's would have been left breathing by morning."

[35] David accepted what she had brought him. "Go home in peace," he said. "I've heard you. I grant your request."

[36] Abigail went back to Nabal. He was in the house, throwing a feast fit for a king. His heart was high — he was thoroughly drunk. So she told him nothing, not a word, until morning light.

[37] In the morning, when the wine had left him, his wife told him everything. His heart died inside him. He went stiff as stone.

[38] Ten days later, the LORD struck Nabal, and he died.

[39] When David heard that Nabal was dead, he said: "Blessed be the LORD, who has taken up my case against Nabal's insult — who held back his servant from doing evil. The LORD turned Nabal's evil back on his own head." Then David sent word to Abigail, asking her to become his wife.

[40] His servants came to Abigail at Carmel. "David has sent us to you," they said, "to take you as his wife."

[41] She stood up, bowed her face to the ground, and said: "Your servant is a slave — ready to wash the feet of my lord's servants."

[42] She didn't hesitate. She got up, mounted her donkey — five of her young women going with her — followed David's messengers, and became his wife.

[43] David had also married Ahinoam of Jezreel. The two of them were now both his wives.

[44] As for Michal, Saul's daughter, David's wife — Saul had given her to Palti son of Laish, from Gallim.

[1] The Ziphites came to Saul at Gibeah with the tip-off: "David's hiding on the hill of Hakilah, right across from Jeshimon."

[2] Saul moved out. He took three thousand handpicked men down into the Ziph wilderness to hunt David.

[3] He camped on the hill of Hakilah, beside the road across from Jeshimon. David was deep in the wilderness when he learned Saul had come after him.

[4] He sent scouts. They confirmed it: Saul was there.

[5] David slipped out and found the place where Saul had bedded down — Saul lying in the middle of the camp, Abner son of Ner, his commander, beside him, the troops circled around them, all of them asleep.

[6] David spoke to Ahimelech the Hittite and to Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab's brother: "Who's coming down into that camp with me — to Saul?" Abishai said, "I am."

[7] So David and Abishai crept into the camp by night. There was Saul, sound asleep inside the perimeter, his spear stabbed into the ground by his head, Abner and the men ringed around him in sleep.

[8] Abishai whispered, "God has handed your enemy to you tonight. Let me pin him to the ground with one stroke of his own spear. One thrust. I won't need a second."

[9] David said, "Don't touch him. Who can lift a hand against the LORD's anointed and walk away clean?"

[10] Then David said, "As the LORD lives — the LORD himself will strike him down. His day will come and he'll die, or he'll go to battle and be swept away.

[11] But God forbid I lift my hand against the LORD's anointed. Take the spear by his head and the water jug. Let's go."

[12] So David took the spear and the water jug from beside Saul's head, and they walked out. Nobody saw. Nobody knew. Nobody woke. The LORD had dropped a deep sleep on the whole camp.

[13] David crossed to the other side and stood on top of a hill far off — a wide gap between them.

[14] He shouted across to the army and to Abner son of Ner, "Answer me, Abner!" Abner called back, "Who are you, shouting at the king?"

[15] David said, "Aren't you the great man? Who in Israel is like you? Then why didn't you guard your master the king? Someone came in to kill him.

[16] What you did is shameful. As the LORD lives, you and your men deserve to die — you failed to guard your master, the LORD's anointed. Look around. Where's the king's spear? Where's the water jug that was by his head?"

[17] Saul recognized David's voice. He said, "Is that your voice, David, my son?" David answered, "It is, my lord and king."

[18] He said, "Why is my lord chasing his servant? What have I done? What evil is on my hands?

[19] Now let my lord the king hear his servant out. If the LORD has stirred you up against me, let him accept an offering. But if it's men — let them be cursed before the LORD, because they've driven me out today from any share in the LORD's inheritance, telling me, 'Go serve other gods.'

[20] Don't let my blood spill onto the ground far from the LORD's presence. The king of Israel has marched out to hunt a single flea — like a man going after a partridge in the hills."

[21] Saul said, "I've sinned. Come back, David my son. I won't hurt you again — my life was precious in your eyes today. I've been a fool. I've been terribly wrong."

[22] David answered, "Here's the king's spear. Send one of your young men to come get it.

[23] The LORD pays every man for his righteousness and his faithfulness. The LORD handed you over to me today, and I wouldn't lift my hand against the LORD's anointed.

[24] As your life mattered to me today, may my life matter to the LORD. May he deliver me from every trouble."

[25] Saul said, "Blessed are you, David my son. You'll do great things and you'll prevail." Then David went his way, and Saul went home.

[1] David said to himself, "One of these days Saul is going to finish me. The smartest move I have left is to slip across the border into Philistine territory. Saul will give up hunting me through every corner of Israel, and I'll finally be out of his reach."

[2] So David got up and crossed over — he and the six hundred men with him — to Achish son of Maoch, king of Gath.

[3] David settled in Gath under Achish's protection, every man with his household, David with his two wives: Ahinoam of Jezreel, and Abigail of Carmel, Nabal's widow.

[4] Word reached Saul that David had fled to Gath, and he called off the hunt.

[5] David said to Achish, "If I've found favor with you, give me a place in one of the outlying towns. Why should your servant live in the royal city alongside you?"

[6] So Achish gave him Ziklag that day — which is why Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah ever since.

[7] David's time in Philistine country came to a year and four months.

[8] David and his men went up and raided the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites — peoples who had lived in that land from way back, stretching toward Shur and on to Egypt.

[9] When David struck a region he left no one alive, man or woman, and took the sheep, the cattle, the donkeys, the camels, the clothing — then came back to Achish.

[10] Achish would ask, "Where did you raid today?" and David would answer, "The Negev of Judah," or "the Negev of the Jerahmeelites," or "the Negev of the Kenites."

[11] He left no man or woman breathing to be hauled back to Gath, because he thought: They might talk. They might say, "This is what David did." That was his standard practice the whole time he lived in Philistine country.

[12] And Achish trusted David completely. He told himself, "David has made himself stink to his own people in Israel. He'll be my servant for life."

[1] In those days, the Philistines mustered their armies for war against Israel. Achish said to David, "You understand, of course, that you and your men will march out with me into battle."

[2] David answered, "Then you'll see for yourself what your servant can do." And Achish said, "Good. I'm making you my personal bodyguard for life."

[3] Samuel was dead by now. All Israel had mourned him and buried him in Ramah, his hometown. And Saul had purged the land of mediums and necromancers.

[4] The Philistines gathered and made camp at Shunem. Saul gathered all Israel and made camp at Gilboa.

[5] When Saul saw the Philistine army, fear gripped him — his heart hammered in his chest.

[6] He asked the LORD for guidance, but the LORD gave him nothing — not through dreams, not through Urim, not through prophets.

[7] So Saul told his servants, "Find me a woman who summons the dead. I'll go to her and ask." They told him, "There's one in Endor."

[8] Saul disguised himself — different clothes, no royal trappings — and went to her by night with two men. "Conjure a spirit for me," he said. "Bring up the one I name."

[9] The woman said, "You know what Saul has done — he's wiped the mediums and necromancers out of the land. Are you setting a trap to get me killed?"

[10] Saul swore to her by the LORD: "As the LORD lives, no harm will come to you for this."

[11] "Who shall I bring up for you?" she asked. "Bring me Samuel," he said.

[12] When the woman saw Samuel, she shrieked. She turned on Saul: "Why did you deceive me? You're Saul!"

[13] The king said, "Don't be afraid. What do you see?" The woman answered, "I see a divine being rising out of the earth."

[14] "What does he look like?" Saul asked. "An old man, wrapped in a robe." Then Saul knew. It was Samuel. He fell facedown to the ground and bowed.

[15] Samuel said to Saul, "Why have you disturbed me, dragging me up?" Saul answered, "I'm in agony. The Philistines are at war with me, and God has turned away — he won't answer me anymore, not through prophets, not through dreams. So I called for you. Tell me what to do."

[16] Samuel said, "Why ask me, when the LORD has turned from you and become your enemy?

[17] The LORD has done exactly what he said he would do through me. He has ripped the kingdom out of your hands and given it to your neighbor — to David.

[18] Because you would not obey the LORD, because you did not unleash his burning anger on Amalek — this is why the LORD is doing this to you today.

[19] And more: the LORD will hand Israel over with you to the Philistines. Tomorrow — you and your sons will be with me. The LORD will hand the army of Israel over to the Philistines."

[20] Saul collapsed full-length on the ground, paralyzed with terror at Samuel's words. He had no strength left — he had not eaten all day and all night.

[21] The woman came to Saul and saw how shattered he was. She said, "Listen — your servant obeyed you. I risked my life to do what you asked.

[22] Now please, listen to your servant. Let me set some food before you. Eat. You need strength for the road back."

[23] He refused. "I won't eat." But his servants joined the woman in pressing him, and finally he gave in. He got up off the ground and sat on the bed.

[24] The woman had a fattened calf in the house. She slaughtered it quickly, took flour, kneaded it, and baked unleavened bread.

[25] She set it before Saul and his servants. They ate. Then they got up and walked out into the night.

[1] The Philistines gathered all their forces at Aphek. Israel camped by the spring in Jezreel.

[2] The Philistine commanders advanced by hundreds and by thousands, and David and his men were marching at the rear with Achish.

[3] The Philistine officers said, "What are these Hebrews doing here?" Achish answered, "This is David — Saul's man, king of Israel — who's been with me for over a year now. From the day he deserted to me until today, I've found nothing against him."

[4] The officers were furious. "Send him back," they snapped. "Send him to whatever place you assigned him. He's not going into battle with us — he'll turn on us mid-fight. What better way to get back in his master's good graces than with the heads of our men?

[5] Isn't this the David they danced about, singing, *Saul struck down thousands, but David — tens of thousands*?"

[6] Achish called David in. "As surely as the LORD lives, you've been straight with me. Your conduct in the camp has been faultless — from the day you came to me until now, I've found nothing wrong with you. But the warlords don't trust you.

[7] So go home. Go in peace. Don't do anything to upset them."

[8] David protested, "What have I done? What have you found against your servant from the day I came to you until now, that I can't go fight the enemies of my lord the king?"

[9] Achish answered, "I know you. You're as good as an angel of God in my eyes. But the Philistine commanders have spoken: he doesn't go into battle with us.

[10] So get up early in the morning — you and your master's servants who came with you. Get up at first light and move out."

[11] So David and his men got up early to head back to Philistine territory. And the Philistines marched up to Jezreel.

[1] Three days later, David and his men reached Ziklag — and found the Amalekites had hit the Negev and Ziklag itself, sacked the town and burned it to the ground.

[2] They had taken the women and everyone in the place, young and old, captive. No one was killed — they were marched off as plunder.

[3] When David and his men arrived at what was left of the city — smoke still rising, walls blackened — and saw that their wives, sons, and daughters were gone,

[4] they broke. They wept until there was nothing left in them to weep with.

[5] David's two wives were among the missing — Ahinoam from Jezreel, and Abigail, who had been Nabal's wife from Carmel.

[6] David was in agony. The men were talking about stoning him — every one of them grieving for his own sons and daughters, and turning that grief into rage at David. But David steadied himself in the LORD his God.

[7] He said to Abiathar the priest, son of Ahimelech, "Bring me the ephod." Abiathar brought it.

[8] David asked the LORD: "Should I chase this raiding party? Will I catch them?" The answer came: "Chase them. You'll catch them. You'll recover everything."

[9] So David set out — he and the six hundred men with him. They reached the Besor ravine, where some of them stayed behind.

[10] David pressed on with four hundred. Two hundred were too exhausted to cross the Besor and stopped there.

[11] Out in the open country they found an Egyptian and brought him to David. They gave him bread, and he ate. They gave him water,

[12] and a slab of pressed figs and two clusters of raisins. He ate, and life came back into him — he hadn't tasted food or water for three days and nights.

[13] David asked him, "Whose man are you? Where are you from?" He said, "I'm an Egyptian, slave to an Amalekite. My master left me here three days ago because I got sick.

[14] We raided the southern Cherethites, the territory of Judah, and the southern lands of Caleb. And we burned Ziklag."

[15] David said, "Can you take me to this raiding party?" He answered, "Swear to me by God you won't kill me or hand me back to my master, and I'll take you to them."

[16] He led them down — and there they were, spread across the whole valley, eating and drinking and reveling in the mountain of loot they'd carried off from Philistia and Judah.

[17] David hit them from twilight until the evening of the next day. Not one escaped — except four hundred young men who jumped on camels and fled.

[18] David got back everything the Amalekites had taken. He got back his two wives.

[19] Nothing was missing — not the smallest thing, not a son, not a daughter, not a piece of plunder, nothing they had carried off. David recovered all of it.

[20] He also took all the flocks and herds. His men drove them ahead of the other livestock, saying, "This is David's share."

[21] David came to the two hundred men too worn out to follow him, the ones left at the Besor ravine. They came out to meet David and the troops with him, and David greeted them warmly.

[22] But every troublemaker and scoundrel among David's men spoke up: "Since these men didn't come with us, they get nothing from what we recovered — except their own wives and children. Let them take those and clear out."

[23] David said, "No, brothers. Not with what the LORD has given us. He protected us. He handed that raiding party over to us.

[24] Who's going to listen to you on this? The share of the man who goes into battle and the share of the man who stays with the supplies are the same. They share alike."

[25] From that day on, David made it a binding rule for Israel — and it stands to this day.

[26] When David got back to Ziklag, he sent portions of the plunder to the elders of Judah, to his friends, with this message: "A gift for you from the spoils of the LORD's enemies."

[27] He sent to those in Bethel, in Ramoth of the Negev, in Jattir,

[28] in Aroer, Siphmoth, and Eshtemoa,

[29] in Racal, in the towns of the Jerahmeelites and the Kenites,

[30] in Hormah, Bor-ashan, and Athach,

[31] in Hebron — and to every place where David and his men had wandered.

[1] The Philistines pressed the attack against Israel, and Israel's men broke and ran. They fell, slaughtered, on the slopes of Mount Gilboa.

[2] The Philistines closed in on Saul and his sons. They cut down Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchishua — Saul's sons, all of them.

[3] The fighting tightened around Saul. The archers spotted him, and their arrows found him. He was bleeding, in agony.

[4] Saul turned to his armor-bearer: "Draw your sword. Run me through. Don't let these uncircumcised animals get to me and make a sport of me." But the armor-bearer wouldn't do it — he was terrified. So Saul took his own sword and threw himself on it.

[5] When the armor-bearer saw Saul was dead, he fell on his own sword and died with him.

[6] So Saul died — and his three sons, and his armor-bearer, and every man with him — all of them, that same day, together.

[7] When the Israelites across the valley and beyond the Jordan saw their army shattered and Saul and his sons dead, they abandoned their towns and fled. The Philistines moved in and occupied them.

[8] The next day, when the Philistines came to strip the dead, they found Saul and his three sons sprawled on Mount Gilboa.

[9] They cut off his head and stripped his armor. Then they sent runners through Philistine territory to broadcast the news in their temples and to their people.

[10] They put his armor on display in the temple of Ashtoreth and nailed his body to the wall of Beth Shan.

[11] But when word reached Jabesh-Gilead — when they heard what the Philistines had done to Saul —

[12] every fighting man among them rose up. They marched all night, took Saul's body and the bodies of his sons down from the wall of Beth Shan, carried them home to Jabesh, and burned them there.

[13] They buried the bones beneath the tamarisk tree in Jabesh and fasted for seven days.

[1] From Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy — to the church of the Thessalonians, rooted in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. Grace to you. Peace.

[2] We thank God for all of you, constantly, naming you in our prayers,

[3] remembering — without pause, in the presence of our God and Father — the work your faith produces, the labor your love demands, the endurance your hope in our Lord Jesus Christ sustains.

[4] Brothers and sisters loved by God, we know he chose you.

[5] Because the gospel didn't arrive among you as mere words. It came with power. With the Holy Spirit. With deep conviction. You know the kind of people we were while we were with you — and we were that way for your sake.

[6] And you imitated us. You imitated the Lord. You took the message in the middle of brutal pressure, and the Holy Spirit filled you with joy anyway.

[7] So you became the template — every believer across Macedonia and Achaia looks to you.

[8] The word of the Lord didn't just echo out from you into those two regions — your faith in God has rung out everywhere. We don't even need to speak of it anymore.

[9] People themselves are reporting it back to us: what kind of welcome you gave us, how you turned from idols to serve a living and true God,

[10] and how you're now waiting for his Son from heaven — the one he raised from the dead — Jesus, who rescues us from the wrath that is coming.

[1] You know yourselves, brothers — our visit to you was not a wasted effort. [2] We had just been beaten and humiliated in Philippi, as you're aware, but God gave us the nerve to deliver his gospel to you anyway, in the teeth of fierce opposition. [3] Our appeal didn't spring from delusion or impure motives or any attempt to manipulate you. [4] No — God vetted us and entrusted us with the gospel, so that's how we speak: not angling for human approval, but for the approval of the God who reads the heart. [5] We never resorted to flattery — you know that — and we never disguised greed under a layer of spiritual talk. God is our witness. [6] We weren't fishing for praise, not from you, not from anyone, [7] though as apostles of Christ we could have leaned on our authority. Instead we were gentle in your midst, like a nursing mother cradling her own children. [8] We were that fond of you. We didn't just hand you the gospel — we handed you our very lives, because that's how dear you became to us.

[9] Remember, brothers, the sweat and the strain. Working night and day so we wouldn't be a burden on anyone, we preached God's gospel to you. [10] You witnessed it, and God did too — how devout, how upright, how blameless we were among you believers. [11] You know we treated each one of you the way a father treats his own children — [12] encouraging you, urging you, pleading with you to walk in a manner worthy of the God who is calling you into his own kingdom and glory.

[13] And this is why we never stop thanking God: when you received the word of God from us, you didn't take it as the word of men. You received it for what it actually is — the word of God, alive and at work in you who believe. [14] You became imitators, brothers, of God's churches in Judea, the ones in Christ Jesus. You suffered the same things at the hands of your own countrymen that they suffered at the hands of the Jews — [15] the ones who killed the Lord Jesus and the prophets, who drove us out, who displease God and oppose every human being, [16] trying to silence us when we speak to the Gentiles about salvation. They keep filling up the measure of their sins. But wrath has caught up with them at last.

[17] Brothers, when we were ripped away from you for a little while — out of sight, never out of heart — we ached all the more to see you face to face. [18] We tried to come, I, Paul, again and again. Satan blocked the way. [19] Because what is our hope, our joy, the crown we'll wear with pride before our Lord Jesus when he comes? Isn't it you? [20] Yes — you are our glory and our joy.

[1] Finally, when we couldn't stand it any longer, we decided it was better to stay behind in Athens alone [2] and send Timothy — our brother, God's coworker in spreading the good news about the Messiah — to steady you and strengthen your faith, [3] so that none of you would be shaken by these afflictions. You know perfectly well this is what we're built for. [4] Even when we were with you, we kept warning you: suffering is coming. And it came, just as we said. [5] That's why, when I couldn't take the silence anymore, I sent to find out about your faith — terrified that the tempter had gotten to you and our work among you had collapsed into nothing.

[6] But Timothy has just returned from you, and the news is good — your faith, your love, the warm memory you carry of us always, your longing to see us as much as we long to see you. [7] In all our distress and pressure, brothers and sisters, this is what carried us: your faith. [8] We come alive again knowing you're standing firm in the Lord. [9] How can we possibly thank God enough for you — for all the joy you give us in his presence? [10] Night and day we beg him, beyond words, to let us see your faces again and finish what's still unfinished in your faith.

[11] May our God and Father himself, and our Lord Jesus, clear the road and bring us to you. [12] And may the Lord flood you with love — for each other, for everyone — overflowing the way ours overflows for you. [13] May he anchor your hearts: blameless, holy, ready to stand before our God and Father on the day our Lord Jesus arrives with all who belong to him.

[1] One last thing, brothers and sisters — and we're asking you, urging you in the Lord Jesus: you learned from us how to walk in a way that pleases God, and you're already doing it. Now do it more. Go further.

[2] You remember the instructions we gave you, delivered with the authority of the Lord Jesus.

[3] Here is what God wants for you: that you be made holy. That means cutting sexual immorality out of your life completely.

[4] Each of you needs to learn how to handle your own body with holiness and honor —

[5] not driven by raw cravings like people who have no knowledge of God.

[6] No one should violate or exploit a brother or sister in these matters. The Lord will settle every account on this — we warned you before, and we're warning you again.

[7] God did not call us to filth. He called us to holiness.

[8] So if anyone shrugs this off, they're not shrugging off a man — they're shrugging off the God who gives you his Holy Spirit.

[9] You don't need anyone to write you a lesson on loving one another. God himself has taught you that.

[10] And you're already living it out toward every believer across Macedonia. We're just asking you — keep pressing in. Love more.

[11] Make it your ambition to live quietly, to mind your own business, to work with your hands, just as we instructed —

[12] so that outsiders see your life and respect it, and you depend on no one.

[13] Now, brothers and sisters, we don't want you in the dark about those who have fallen asleep — we don't want you grieving the way people grieve who have no hope.

[14] If we believe Jesus died and rose — and we do — then we also believe this: God will bring with Jesus those who have fallen asleep in him.

[15] Here is what we tell you on the Lord's own word: those of us still alive when the Lord comes will not get there ahead of those who have died.

[16] Because the Lord himself will descend from heaven — a commanding shout, the voice of the archangel, the trumpet blast of God — and the dead in Christ will rise first.

[17] Then we who are alive, who are still here, will be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air. And from that moment on, we will always be with the Lord.

[18] So speak these words to one another. Let them carry you.

[1] About the timing — the dates, the schedule — you don't need anyone to write you about that. [2] You already know exactly: the Day of the Lord arrives like a thief in the dark. [3] The moment people are saying "peace and security" — that's when ruin crashes down on them, sudden as labor pains hitting a pregnant woman. No escape.

[4] But you — you're not in the dark. That Day won't ambush you like a thief. [5] You belong to the light. You belong to the day. We're not creatures of night, not creatures of shadow. [6] So let's not sleep through this like everyone else. Stay awake. Stay sharp. [7] Sleepers sleep at night. Drunks get drunk at night. [8] But we live in the daylight — so let's stay clear-headed, strapped in: faith and love as the breastplate, the hope of salvation as the helmet. [9] God didn't appoint us for wrath. He appointed us for salvation through our Lord Jesus the Anointed, [10] who died for us so that whether we're awake or asleep when he comes, we live together with him. [11] So encourage each other. Build each other up. Which is exactly what you're already doing.

[12] One request, friends: recognize the people working hard among you — the ones leading you in the Lord, the ones who tell you the truth even when it stings. [13] Hold them in the highest regard, with love, because of what they do. Live at peace with one another.

[14] And we're urging you, brothers and sisters: confront the lazy, steady the anxious, stay close to the weak, be patient with everyone. [15] Make sure no one returns evil for evil. Chase after good — for each other, for everyone.

[16] Rejoice always.
[17] Pray nonstop.
[18] Give thanks in every circumstance — this is what God wants from you in the Anointed Jesus.

[19] Don't smother the Spirit's fire.
[20] Don't sneer at prophecy.
[21] Test everything. Hold onto what's good.
[22] Walk away from every shade of evil.

[23] May the God of peace himself make you completely his own — every part of you, spirit and soul and body, kept whole and blameless for the arrival of our Lord Jesus the Anointed. [24] The one who calls you is faithful. He'll do it.

[25] Pray for us too, family.

[26] Greet everyone there with a holy kiss.

[27] I'm putting you under oath before the Lord: read this letter to all of them.

[28] The grace of our Lord Jesus the Anointed be with you.

[1] From Paul, sent as an envoy of Christ Jesus by direct command of God our Savior and Christ Jesus our hope—

[2] To Timothy, my true son in the faith: grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.

[3] When I left for Macedonia, I urged you to stay put in Ephesus for one reason: certain people there need to be told, in no uncertain terms, to stop peddling a different doctrine.

[4] They need to drop the obsession with myths and endless genealogies. That stuff breeds speculation, not the work of God that comes by faith.

[5] The whole point of what we're charging them with is love—love flowing from a clean heart, a clear conscience, and faith that isn't faking it.

[6] Some have wandered off from all of that and ended up in empty talk.

[7] They want to be teachers of the Law, but they have no idea what they're saying or what they're so confidently asserting.

[8] Now, the Law is good—provided you use it the way it was meant to be used.

[9] Here's the thing: the Law isn't aimed at the righteous. It's aimed at the lawless and rebellious, the godless and sinful, the unholy and profane, those who would kill their own father or mother, murderers,

[10] the sexually immoral, men who use other men, slave traders, liars, perjurers—anything else that stands against the healthy teaching

[11] of the glorious gospel of the blessed God, which has been entrusted to me.

[12] I'm grateful to Christ Jesus our Lord, who gave me strength, because he counted me trustworthy and put me into his service—

[13] me, of all people. Once a blasphemer, a persecutor, a violent man. But I was shown mercy because I acted in ignorance, in unbelief.

[14] The grace of our Lord crashed over me like a flood, bringing with it the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus.

[15] Here's a statement you can stake your life on: Christ Jesus came into the world to rescue sinners. And I'm exhibit A.

[16] But that's exactly why I was shown mercy—so that in me, the worst-case scenario, Christ Jesus could put his unlimited patience on full display, as a template for everyone who would later believe in him and find eternal life.

[17] To the King of the ages—immortal, invisible, the only God—honor and glory forever and ever. Amen.

[18] Timothy, my son, this charge I'm giving you ties back to the prophecies once spoken over you. Use them. Fight the good fight.
