[21] He walked every road his father walked, served the same idols, bowed to them —

[22] abandoning the LORD, the God of his ancestors, refusing to walk the LORD's way.

[23] Amon's own officials conspired against him and assassinated him in his palace.

[24] But the people of the land struck down everyone who had conspired against King Amon and made his son Josiah king in his place.

[25] The rest of Amon's story is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[26] He was buried in his tomb in the garden of Uzza. His son Josiah became king.

[1] Josiah was eight years old when he took the throne, and he reigned in Jerusalem for thirty-one years. His mother was Jedidah, daughter of Adaiah, from Bozkath.

[2] He did what was right in the LORD's eyes. He walked the road his ancestor David walked — never veering right, never veering left.

[3] In the eighteenth year of his reign, King Josiah sent Shaphan — son of Azaliah, son of Meshullam — the royal secretary, to the temple of the LORD with these orders:

[4] "Go to Hilkiah the high priest. Have him count out the silver brought into the temple — the money the gatekeepers have collected from the people.

[5] Put it in the hands of the foremen overseeing the work on the LORD's house. Let them pay the workers doing repairs —

[6] the carpenters, the builders, the masons — and let them buy timber and quarried stone to restore the temple.

[7] No need to make them account for the money they receive. They're trustworthy men."

[8] Then Hilkiah the high priest said to Shaphan the secretary, "I've found it — the Book of the Law. Here, in the temple of the LORD." He handed the scroll to Shaphan, and Shaphan read it.

[9] Shaphan went back to the king and gave his report: "Your servants have emptied out the silver from the temple and turned it over to the foremen supervising the work on the LORD's house."

[10] Then he added, "Hilkiah the priest gave me a scroll." And Shaphan began to read it aloud in the king's presence.

[11] When the king heard the words of the Book of the Law, he tore his clothes.

[12] He gave orders to Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Akbor son of Micaiah, Shaphan the secretary, and Asaiah the king's attendant:

[13] "Go. Inquire of the LORD for me — for me, for the people, for all of Judah — about what's written in this scroll we've found. The LORD's anger must be blazing against us, because our ancestors did not listen to the words of this book. They did not do what it tells us to do."

[14] So Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam, Akbor, Shaphan, and Asaiah went to Huldah the prophetess. She was the wife of Shallum son of Tikvah, son of Harhas, keeper of the wardrobe. She lived in Jerusalem, in the Second District. They laid the matter before her.

[15] She answered: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — tell the man who sent you to me:

[16] The LORD says this: I am bringing disaster on this place and on its people. Everything written in that scroll the king of Judah has read — all of it is coming.

[17] They abandoned me. They burned incense to other gods. They provoked me with everything their hands made. My wrath is kindled against this place, and it will not be extinguished.

[18] But to the king of Judah, who sent you to seek the LORD — tell him this. The LORD, the God of Israel, says about the words you heard:

[19] Because your heart was tender, because you humbled yourself before the LORD when you heard what I declared against this place and its people — that it would become a wasteland and a curse — because you tore your clothes and wept before me, I have heard you. This is the LORD's word.

[20] So I will gather you to your ancestors. You will be laid in your grave in peace. Your eyes will not have to see all the disaster I am about to bring on this place."

And they brought her answer back to the king.

[1] The king sent word, and they gathered to him every elder of Judah and Jerusalem. [2] He went up to the house of the LORD — every man of Judah, every resident of Jerusalem, the priests and the prophets, the whole people from least to greatest — and he read in their hearing every word of the covenant scroll discovered in the house of the LORD.

[3] The king took his stand by the pillar and cut covenant before the LORD: to walk after the LORD, to keep his commands and his decrees and his statutes with all his heart and all his soul, to put into effect the words of this covenant written in the scroll. And the whole people stepped into the covenant.

[4] Then the king ordered Hilkiah the high priest, the priests of the second order, and the gatekeepers to haul out of the temple of the LORD every object made for Baal, for Asherah, and for the host of heaven. He burned them outside Jerusalem in the terraces of the Kidron and carried the ashes off to Bethel.

[5] He liquidated the priestlings the kings of Judah had installed to burn incense at the high places throughout the towns of Judah and the outskirts of Jerusalem — those who burned to Baal, to the sun and moon, the constellations, the whole array of heaven.

[6] He hauled the Asherah pole out of the house of the LORD, outside Jerusalem to the Kidron Valley. He burned it there, ground it to dust, and threw the dust on the graves of the common dead.

[7] He demolished the quarters of the cult prostitutes inside the house of the LORD — the rooms where women wove hangings for Asherah.

[8] He brought in all the priests from the towns of Judah and defiled the high places where those priests had been burning incense, from Geba to Beersheba. He tore down the high places at the gates — the one at the entrance to the Gate of Joshua the city governor, on a man's left as he came through the city gate.

[9] The priests of the high places, though, did not go up to the altar of the LORD in Jerusalem. They only ate unleavened bread among their own kin.

[10] He defiled Topheth in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom so no one could put a son or daughter through the fire to Molech.

[11] He removed the horses the kings of Judah had dedicated to the sun — at the entrance to the house of the LORD, near the chamber of Nathan-melech the eunuch in the colonnade — and he torched the sun-chariots.

[12] The altars on the roof of Ahaz's upper room, built by the kings of Judah, and the altars Manasseh had built in the two courts of the LORD's house — the king pulled them down, smashed them on the spot, and flung the rubble into the Kidron.

[13] The high places east of Jerusalem, south of the Mount of Corruption — the ones Solomon king of Israel had built for Ashtoreth the abomination of the Sidonians, for Chemosh the abomination of Moab, and for Milcom the disgusting thing of the Ammonites — the king defiled them all.

[14] He shattered the sacred pillars, chopped down the Asherah poles, and filled the spots with human bones.

[15] Even the altar at Bethel — the high place built by Jeroboam son of Nebat, who dragged Israel into sin — even that altar and high place he tore down. He burned the high place, ground it to dust, and torched the Asherah.

[16] Josiah turned and saw the tombs there on the hillside. He sent men to pull the bones out of the tombs, and he burned them on the altar, defiling it — exactly as the LORD had announced through the man of God who had foretold all this.

[17] Then he asked, "What is that monument I see?" The men of the city told him, "The grave of the man of God who came from Judah and announced these very things you have just done to the altar of Bethel."

[18] "Leave him be," Josiah said. "No one is to disturb his bones." So they let his bones rest, along with the bones of the prophet who had come from Samaria.

[19] Every shrine on every high place in the towns of Samaria — built by the kings of Israel to provoke the LORD — Josiah removed them. He did to them exactly what he had done at Bethel.

[20] He slaughtered all the priests of those high places on their own altars and burned human bones on top of them. Then he went home to Jerusalem.

[21] The king commanded the whole people: "Keep the Passover to the LORD your God, as written in this covenant scroll."

[22] No Passover like it had been kept since the days of the judges who governed Israel — not through all the reigns of the kings of Israel and Judah. [23] Only in the eighteenth year of King Josiah was this Passover kept to the LORD in Jerusalem.

[24] The mediums, the spiritists, the household gods, the idols, every detestable thing seen in the land of Judah and in Jerusalem — Josiah swept it all away, putting into force the words of the law written in the scroll Hilkiah the priest had found in the house of the LORD.

[25] No king before him ever turned to the LORD as he did — with all his heart, all his soul, all his strength, the whole of the law of Moses. And no king after him rose to match him.

[26] Even so, the LORD did not turn from the white heat of his anger that burned against Judah — kindled by everything Manasseh had done to provoke him. [27] The LORD said, "I will sweep Judah from my sight too, the way I swept Israel away. I will reject this city I chose — Jerusalem — and this house I said my name would inhabit."

[28] The rest of Josiah's record, everything he did — it is written in the chronicles of the kings of Judah.

[29] In Josiah's days, Pharaoh Neco king of Egypt marched up toward the king of Assyria at the Euphrates River. King Josiah rode out to meet him, and Neco killed him at Megiddo the moment they faced each other.

[30] His servants drove his body in a chariot from Megiddo back to Jerusalem and buried him in his own tomb. The people of the land took Jehoahaz son of Josiah, anointed him, and crowned him in his father's place.

[31] Jehoahaz was twenty-three when he became king. He reigned three months in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah, from Libnah. [32] He did evil in the LORD's eyes — exactly as his fathers had done.

[33] Pharaoh Neco clapped him in irons at Riblah in the land of Hamath, ending his reign in Jerusalem, and levied on the land a tribute of a hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold.

[34] Pharaoh Neco installed Eliakim son of Josiah as king in place of his father Josiah and changed his name to Jehoiakim. He took Jehoahaz away — he went to Egypt and died there.

[35] Jehoiakim paid the silver and gold to Pharaoh, but he had to tax the land to come up with the money Pharaoh demanded. From each man of the land, according to his assessment, he extracted the silver and gold to give to Pharaoh Neco.

[36] Jehoiakim was twenty-five when he became king. He reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. His mother's name was Zebidah daughter of Pedaiah, from Rumah. [37] He did evil in the LORD's eyes — exactly as his fathers had done.

[1] During Jehoiakim's reign, Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon marched in, and Jehoiakim became his vassal for three years. Then he flipped — rebellion.

[2] So the LORD unleashed raiding parties against him: Chaldeans, Arameans, Moabites, Ammonites. He turned them loose on Judah to grind it down — exactly what He had said through His servants the prophets.

[3] Make no mistake: this came down on Judah at the LORD's command, to sweep them out of His sight. The blood was on Manasseh — everything he had done,

[4] including the innocent blood he spilled until Jerusalem was drenched with it. That, the LORD would not forgive.

[5] The rest of Jehoiakim's story — all of it — is written in the chronicles of Judah's kings.

[6] Jehoiakim joined his ancestors, and his son Jehoiachin took the throne.

[7] Egypt's king never marched out of his own land again, because Babylon's king had swallowed everything from the Wadi of Egypt to the Euphrates — every inch that had been Egypt's.

[8] Jehoiachin was eighteen when he became king, and he reigned three months in Jerusalem. His mother was Nehushta, daughter of Elnathan, from Jerusalem.

[9] He did what the LORD called evil — same script as his father.

[10] That was when Nebuchadnezzar's officers came up against Jerusalem and laid siege to it.

[11] Then Nebuchadnezzar himself arrived at the city while his troops had it surrounded.

[12] Jehoiachin of Judah walked out and surrendered — king, mother, servants, officers, eunuchs, all of them. Babylon's king took him in the eighth year of his own reign.

[13] He hauled out every treasure of the LORD's house and the royal palace. He stripped the gold off everything Solomon king of Israel had made for the temple of the LORD — just as the LORD had warned.

[14] He deported all of Jerusalem: every officer, every fighting man — ten thousand exiles — every craftsman, every smith. Only the poorest of the land were left behind.

[15] He carried Jehoiachin off to Babylon. The king's mother, his wives, his eunuchs, the leading men of the land — he marched them all from Jerusalem into exile in Babylon.

[16] Seven thousand soldiers, a thousand craftsmen and smiths — all of them fit and battle-ready — Babylon's king brought them as captives to Babylon.

[17] Then he installed Mattaniah, Jehoiachin's uncle, as king in his place — and renamed him Zedekiah.

[18] Zedekiah was twenty-one when he became king, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. His mother was Hamutal, daughter of Jeremiah, from Libnah.

[19] He did what the LORD called evil — Jehoiakim all over again.

[20] All of this happened because the LORD's anger had reached Jerusalem and Judah, and He was done with them — until He hurled them from His presence. Then Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.

[1] In the ninth year of Zedekiah's reign, on the tenth day of the tenth month, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came down on Jerusalem with his entire army. He camped against the city and threw up siege works around it on every side.

[2] The city stayed locked in siege until Zedekiah's eleventh year.

[3] By the ninth day of the fourth month, the famine inside the walls had become unbearable. There was no bread left for anyone.

[4] Then the wall was breached. That night every soldier slipped out through the gate between the two walls beside the king's garden — Chaldeans ringing the city, but the king and his men ran for the Arabah.

[5] The Babylonian army gave chase and ran Zedekiah down on the plains of Jericho. His entire force scattered, every man for himself.

[6] They seized the king and dragged him north to the king of Babylon at Riblah, where sentence was passed on him.

[7] They butchered Zedekiah's sons in front of him. Then they put out his eyes — that image, his sons dying, the last thing he would ever see — bound him in bronze chains, and hauled him to Babylon.

[8] In the fifth month, on the seventh day, in Nebuchadnezzar's nineteenth year as king of Babylon, Nebuzaradan — commander of the imperial guard, the king's own man — marched into Jerusalem.

[9] He torched the temple of the LORD. He torched the palace. He torched every house in Jerusalem — every building of any size went up in flames.

[10] The Babylonian forces under the commander tore down the walls of Jerusalem on every side.

[11] Nebuzaradan deported the survivors still in the city, the defectors who had gone over to the king of Babylon, and what remained of the population.

[12] But the commander left some of the poorest of the land behind to tend vineyards and work the fields.

[13] The Chaldeans broke up the bronze pillars of the temple, the movable stands, and the great bronze Sea — and carried the bronze off to Babylon.

[14] They took the pots, the shovels, the wick trimmers, the dishes — every bronze vessel used in the temple service.

[15] The captain of the guard hauled away the firepans and the sprinkling bowls — whatever was pure gold as gold, whatever was pure silver as silver.

[16] The two pillars, the one Sea, the stands Solomon had crafted for the house of the LORD — the bronze in it all was beyond weighing.

[17] Each pillar stood eighteen cubits high, topped with a bronze capital three cubits tall, wrapped in bronze latticework and pomegranates. The second pillar matched the first, lattice and all.

[18] The commander took Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the second priest, and the three guardians of the threshold.

[19] From the city he took a court officer in charge of the soldiers, five men from the king's inner circle who were still in the city, the secretary of the army commander who conscripted men from the land, and sixty more civilians caught inside the walls.

[20] Nebuzaradan the commander rounded them all up and marched them to the king of Babylon at Riblah.

[21] And there at Riblah in the land of Hamath, the king of Babylon struck them down. He killed them. So Judah went into exile, far from its own soil.

[22] As for the people Nebuchadnezzar left behind in Judah, he appointed Gedaliah son of Ahikam, grandson of Shaphan, over them.

[23] When the army officers and their men heard the king of Babylon had made Gedaliah governor, they came to him at Mizpah — Ishmael son of Nethaniah, Johanan son of Kareah, Seraiah son of Tanhumeth the Netophathite, and Jaazaniah son of the Maacathite, along with their men.

[24] Gedaliah swore to them and their men: "Do not be afraid of the Chaldean officials. Settle here. Serve the king of Babylon. It will go well with you."

[25] But in the seventh month, Ishmael son of Nethaniah son of Elishama — royal blood — came with ten men and cut Gedaliah down. They killed him and the Jews and Chaldeans with him at Mizpah.

[26] Then everyone, from the least to the greatest, every last officer, panicked and fled to Egypt. They were terrified of what Babylon would do.

[27] In the thirty-seventh year of Jehoiachin king of Judah's exile, in the twelfth month, on the twenty-seventh day, Evil-merodach king of Babylon — in the year he came to the throne — lifted Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison.

[28] He spoke kindly to him and gave him a seat above the seats of the other kings exiled with him in Babylon.

[29] Jehoiachin set aside his prison clothes. For the rest of his life, he ate at the king's table.

[30] And his allowance — a steady allowance from the king, day by day — was given to him for as long as he lived.

[1] Simon Peter — slave and envoy of Jesus the Anointed — to those who have been handed a faith as priceless as ours, granted through the justice of our God and Savior, Jesus the Anointed:

[2] May grace and peace flood over you as you come to know God and Jesus our Lord more deeply.

[3] His divine power has already handed us everything we need for life and godliness, through knowing the One who called us by his own glory and excellence.

[4] Through that glory and excellence he has gifted us promises — staggering ones, enormous ones — so that through them you might escape the rot that desire has unleashed on the world, and actually share in the divine nature itself.

[5] So for this very reason — bring everything you have. Lace your faith with excellence; lace excellence with knowledge;

[6] knowledge with self-mastery; self-mastery with endurance; endurance with godliness;

[7] godliness with deep affection for the family; and that affection with love itself.

[8] When these qualities take root in you and keep growing, they will not let you become useless or barren in knowing our Lord Jesus the Anointed.

[9] Anyone who lacks them is blind — nearsighted at best — and has forgotten he was scrubbed clean of his old sins.

[10] So, family — work all the harder to nail down your calling and election. Do this and you will never stumble.

[11] Do this and the door swings wide open — a grand entrance into the everlasting kingdom of our Lord and Savior, Jesus the Anointed.

[12] This is why I will keep reminding you of these things, even though you already know them and stand firmly planted in the truth that has reached you.

[13] As long as I am still in this tent of a body, I think it right to keep stirring you up, keep waking you up.

[14] I know my tent is coming down soon — our Lord Jesus the Anointed made that plain to me.

[15] But I will do everything I can to make sure that after I am gone, you can call these things to mind anytime.

[16] When we told you about the power and the coming of our Lord Jesus the Anointed, we were not peddling clever myths. We saw his majesty with our own eyes.

[17] He received honor and glory from God the Father when that voice rolled out from the Magnificent Glory: *This is my Son, the Beloved. In him I delight.*

[18] We heard that voice ourselves — it came out of heaven while we stood with him on the holy mountain.

[19] And so the prophetic word stands confirmed, more solid than ever. You would do well to fix your attention on it — like a lamp burning in a dark place — until the day breaks and the morning star rises in your hearts.

[20] Above all, understand this: no prophecy in Scripture is a matter of someone's private interpretation.

[21] Prophecy never came from a human impulse. Men spoke from God, carried along by the Holy Spirit like a sail catching wind.

[1] But there were also false prophets among the people—just as there will be false teachers among you. They'll smuggle in destructive lies, even denying the Master who bought them, dragging swift ruin down on their own heads. [2] Plenty will follow them into the gutter, and because of them the way of truth will be smeared. [3] Greed will drive them. They'll exploit you with stories they've invented. But their sentence, pronounced long ago, isn't idling—and their destruction isn't sleeping.

[4] God didn't spare angels when they sinned. He hurled them into the pit, locked them in chains of darkness, holding them for judgment. [5] He didn't spare the ancient world either—but he preserved Noah, that herald of righteousness, and seven others, when he unleashed the flood on a world of the ungodly. [6] He reduced Sodom and Gomorrah to ash, condemning them as a preview of what's coming for everyone who lives that way. [7] But he rescued Lot, a righteous man worn down by the obscene conduct of the lawless— [8] day after day, that righteous soul, living among them, tortured himself watching and hearing their lawless deeds.

[9] So the Lord knows how to rescue the godly from trial—and how to hold the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment, [10] especially those who chase the corrupt cravings of the flesh and despise authority.

Brazen. Arrogant. They don't even tremble when they slander glorious beings— [11] though angels, stronger and more powerful, won't bring a slanderous charge against them before the Lord. [12] These people are like animals—creatures of instinct, born to be caught and killed. They mock what they don't understand, and like animals they'll be destroyed. [13] They'll be paid back with the harm they've caused.

Their idea of pleasure is debauchery in broad daylight. They're stains and blemishes, reveling in their deceptions while they eat at your table. [14] Their eyes are full of adultery, never satisfied with sin. They bait unsteady souls. Their hearts are trained for greed. Cursed children. [15] They've abandoned the straight road and wandered off, chasing the path of Balaam son of Bosor, who loved the wages of wrongdoing— [16] and was rebuked for his crime by a mute donkey, speaking in a human voice, holding back the prophet's madness.

[17] These men are dry springs. Mists driven by a storm. The blackest darkness has been reserved for them. [18] Spouting empty, inflated nonsense, they bait—through the cravings of the flesh, through sensuality—people who are just barely escaping from those who live in error. [19] They promise them freedom while they themselves are slaves to corruption. Because whatever masters you, that's what you're a slave to.

[20] If people have escaped the filth of the world by knowing our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, only to get tangled up in it again and overcome, they're worse off at the end than they were at the start. [21] Better never to have known the way of righteousness than to know it and then turn their backs on the sacred command handed down to them. [22] The proverbs are true of them: A dog returns to its vomit. A pig, freshly washed, goes back to wallow in the mud.

[1] This is now my second letter to you, beloved—and in both I've been stirring up your sincere mind by way of reminder, [2] urging you to remember what the holy prophets said long ago, and what your apostles delivered to you from the Lord and Savior himself.

[3] First, know this: in the last days mockers will come, mocking, marching to the cadence of their own appetites, [4] saying, "Where is this 'coming' he promised? Our fathers fell asleep and everything goes on exactly as it has since the world began." [5] They want to forget this: that long ago, by the word of God, the heavens existed and the earth was formed out of water and through water— [6] and by that same water the world of that day was drowned and destroyed. [7] By the same word, the present heavens and earth are stockpiled for fire, kept for the day of judgment and the ruin of the godless.

[8] But do not overlook this one thing, beloved: with the Lord, a single day is a thousand years, and a thousand years a single day. [9] The Lord is not dragging his feet on his promise, as some count slowness. He is being patient with you—unwilling that any should perish, wanting everyone to turn.

[10] But the Day of the Lord will come like a thief. The heavens will vanish with a roar, the elements will dissolve in scorching heat, and the earth and everything done on it will be laid bare.

[11] Since everything is going to come undone like this—what kind of people ought you to be? Lives of holiness. Lives bent toward God. [12] Waiting for the Day of God. Hurrying it along. The day when the heavens, set ablaze, will dissolve, and the elements melt in the fire. [13] But we are watching for new heavens and a new earth, where righteousness is at home—just as he promised.

[14] So, beloved, while you wait for this, throw yourselves into being found by him at peace—spotless, blameless. [15] Count the patience of our Lord as salvation, just as our beloved brother Paul also wrote to you, with the wisdom given him. [16] He says the same in all his letters when he speaks of these matters. There are things in them that are hard to grasp, which the untaught and unstable twist—as they do the rest of the Scriptures—to their own destruction.

[17] So, beloved, since you've been warned, guard yourselves. Don't let the deception of the lawless drag you off and topple you from your own steady ground. [18] Instead, grow—in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be the glory, now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

[1] After Saul was dead and David had come back from cutting down the Amalekites, he stayed two days at Ziklag.

[2] On the third day, a man showed up from Saul's camp — clothes ripped, dirt on his head. He came to David, dropped to the ground, and bowed low.

[3] David asked, "Where have you come from?" He answered, "I escaped from the camp of Israel."

[4] "What happened?" David pressed. "Tell me." He said, "The army broke and ran. Thousands fell. Saul and his son Jonathan are dead."

[5] David turned on the young man who brought the news: "How do you know Saul and Jonathan are dead?"

[6] The man answered, "I just happened to be on Mount Gilboa, and there was Saul, leaning on his spear, with chariots and horsemen closing in on him.

[7] He turned, saw me, called me over. I said, 'Here I am.'

[8] He asked who I was. I told him — an Amalekite.

[9] He said, 'Stand over me and finish it. I'm reeling, but I'm still alive.'

[10] So I stood over him and killed him, because I knew he would not survive the fall. I took the crown from his head and the band from his arm, and I've brought them here to my lord."

[11] David grabbed his own clothes and tore them. Every man with him did the same.

[12] They mourned, they wept, they fasted until evening — for Saul, for Jonathan his son, for the army of the Lord, for the house of Israel, because they had fallen by the sword.

[13] David said to the young man who brought the report, "Where are you from?" He answered, "I'm the son of a foreigner — an Amalekite."

[14] David said, "How were you not afraid to lift your hand and destroy the Lord's anointed?"

[15] David called one of his men: "Step forward. Cut him down." The man struck him, and he died.

[16] David said to him, "Your blood is on your own head. Your own mouth testified against you when you said, 'I killed the Lord's anointed.'"

[17] Then David sang this lament over Saul and his son Jonathan,

[18] and he ordered that the people of Judah be taught it — the Song of the Bow. It is written in the Book of Jashar:

[19] *Your glory, O Israel, lies slain on your heights.*
*How the mighty have fallen.*

[20] *Do not say it in Gath.*
*Do not breathe it in the streets of Ashkelon —*
*or the daughters of the Philistines will dance,*
*the daughters of the uncircumcised will celebrate.*

[21] *Mountains of Gilboa —*
*no dew on you, no rain,*
*no fields yielding offerings.*
*For there the shield of the mighty was defiled,*
*the shield of Saul — no longer rubbed with oil.*

[22] *From the blood of the slain, from the flesh of the mighty,*
*Jonathan's bow never turned back,*
*Saul's sword never came home empty.*

[23] *Saul and Jonathan —*
*beloved, beautiful in life,*
*and in death not divided.*
*Faster than eagles. Stronger than lions.*

[24] *Daughters of Israel, weep for Saul,*
*who clothed you in scarlet and luxury,*
*who set gold on your robes.*

[25] *How the mighty have fallen in the thick of battle.*
*Jonathan — slain on your heights.*

[26] *I ache for you, my brother Jonathan.*
*You were everything to me.*
*Your love to me was wonderful —*
*deeper than the love of women.*

[27] *How the mighty have fallen,*
*and the weapons of war perished.*

[1] After all this, David put the question to the LORD: "Should I go up to one of the towns of Judah?" The LORD said, "Go." David asked, "Where?" The answer came back: "Hebron."

[2] So David went up — both his wives with him, Ahinoam from Jezreel and Abigail, Nabal's widow from Carmel.

[3] He brought his men too, every one of them with his household, and they settled in the villages around Hebron.

[4] Then the men of Judah came and there, in Hebron, they anointed David king over the house of Judah. Word reached David that it was the men of Jabesh-gilead who had buried Saul.

[5] So David sent messengers to them: "The LORD bless you for the loyalty you showed your master Saul — for burying him.

[6] May the LORD show you that same loyalty and faithfulness now. And I will not forget what you have done — I will repay it.

[7] So pull yourselves together. Be strong. Your master Saul is dead, but the house of Judah has anointed me king over them."

[8] Meanwhile, Abner son of Ner — Saul's army commander — had taken Saul's son Ish-bosheth and brought him across to Mahanaim.

[9] He made him king over Gilead, over the Ashurites, over Jezreel, over Ephraim, over Benjamin — over all Israel.

[10] Ish-bosheth was forty when he became king over Israel, and he reigned two years. The house of Judah, though — they were with David.

[11] David's reign in Hebron over the house of Judah lasted seven years and six months.

[12] Then Abner son of Ner marched out from Mahanaim to Gibeon, leading the men of Ish-bosheth, Saul's son.

[13] Joab son of Zeruiah marched out too, leading David's men. They met at the pool of Gibeon — one army on one side of the pool, the other on the other side.

[14] Abner called across to Joab: "Let the young men get up and put on a show for us." Joab said, "Let them."

[15] So they stood up and crossed over by count — twelve for Benjamin and Ish-bosheth, twelve from David's men.

[16] Each one grabbed his opponent by the head and drove his sword into his side, and they all fell together. That place near Gibeon got a name: the Field of Daggers.

[17] The fighting that day was savage. Abner and the men of Israel were beaten by David's men.

[18] The three sons of Zeruiah were there — Joab, Abishai, and Asahel. Asahel ran like a gazelle on open ground.

[19] He locked onto Abner. Refused to swerve right or left — just chased him down.

[20] Abner looked back. "Is that you, Asahel?" "It's me," he answered.

[21] Abner said, "Peel off — go right or left. Grab one of the young men and take his gear for yourself." But Asahel would not break off.

[22] Again Abner called back: "Get off me. Why should I cut you down? How could I ever look your brother Joab in the face again?"

[23] But he refused to turn aside. So Abner drove the butt of his spear backward, into Asahel's belly. It came out his back. He fell there and died on the spot. And every man who came to the place where Asahel lay stopped cold.

[24] But Joab and Abishai went after Abner. The sun was setting when they reached the hill of Ammah, near Giah, on the road to the Gibeon wilderness.

[25] The Benjaminites regrouped behind Abner, formed a single line, and took their stand on top of a hill.

[26] Abner shouted down to Joab: "Is the sword going to devour forever? Don't you know how this ends — in bitterness? When are you going to tell your men to stop chasing their own brothers?"

[27] Joab answered, "As God lives — if you had not spoken, the men would have kept after their brothers till morning."

[28] Joab blew the trumpet. The whole army halted. No more pursuit. No more fighting.

[29] Abner and his men marched all night through the Arabah, crossed the Jordan, pushed through the whole Bithron, and came to Mahanaim.

[30] Joab broke off the pursuit and gathered his men. Nineteen of David's men were missing — and Asahel.

[31] But David's men had cut down three hundred and sixty Benjaminites who had followed Abner. Dead.

[32] They carried Asahel home and buried him in his father's tomb in Bethlehem. Joab and his men marched all night and reached Hebron at first light.

[1] The war between Saul's house and David's house dragged on. David grew stronger and stronger; Saul's house withered.

[2] Sons were born to David at Hebron. The firstborn: Amnon, by Ahinoam of Jezreel. [3] The second: Chileab, by Abigail, widow of Nabal of Carmel. The third: Absalom, son of Maacah, daughter of King Talmai of Geshur. [4] The fourth: Adonijah, son of Haggith. The fifth: Shephatiah, son of Abital. [5] The sixth: Ithream, by David's wife Eglah. All born to David at Hebron.

[6] While the war ground on between the two houses, Abner was tightening his grip on Saul's faction. [7] Saul had a concubine named Rizpah, daughter of Aiah. Ish-bosheth confronted Abner: "Why did you sleep with my father's concubine?"

[8] Abner exploded at the accusation. "Am I some dog's head from Judah? After everything I've done — keeping faith with your father's house, his brothers, his friends, refusing to hand you over to David — you turn on me today over a woman? [9] God do worse to me if I don't deliver to David exactly what the LORD swore to him — [10] tearing the kingdom from Saul's house and planting David's throne over Israel and Judah, from Dan to Beersheba."

[11] Ish-bosheth couldn't manage a single word back. He was terrified of Abner.

[12] Abner sent messengers straight to David: "Whose land is this? Make a covenant with me, and my hand will be with you — I'll bring all Israel over to your side."

[13] David answered: "Good. I'll make a covenant with you. But one condition: don't show your face here unless you bring Michal, Saul's daughter, with you."

[14] Then David sent word to Ish-bosheth himself: "Give me my wife Michal. I paid for her with a hundred Philistine foreskins."

[15] Ish-bosheth had her taken from her husband, Paltiel son of Laish. [16] Her husband followed behind her, weeping the whole way to Bahurim. Abner told him, "Go home." So he went.

[17] Abner had been working on the elders of Israel: "For some time now you've wanted David as your king. [18] Make it happen. The LORD said of David: 'Through my servant David I will rescue my people Israel from the hand of the Philistines and all their enemies.'"

[19] Abner spoke directly to Benjamin, too. Then he went to Hebron to lay before David everything Israel and the whole house of Benjamin were ready to do.

[20] Abner came to David at Hebron with twenty men. David threw a feast for them. [21] Abner said to David, "Let me go. I'll gather all Israel to my lord the king. They'll cut a covenant with you, and you'll rule everything your heart desires." David sent Abner off in peace.

[22] Just then Joab and David's men returned from a raid, loaded with plunder. Abner was no longer with David at Hebron — he'd been sent off in peace. [23] When Joab and his whole force arrived, they told him: "Abner son of Ner came to the king, and the king sent him away in peace."

[24] Joab stormed in to the king. "What have you done? Abner came to you — and you just let him walk away? [25] You know Abner son of Ner. He came to deceive you, to track your movements, to know everything you do."

[26] Joab left David and sent messengers after Abner. They brought him back from the well of Sirah. David knew nothing about it. [27] When Abner reached Hebron, Joab pulled him aside inside the gate, as if for a private word — and drove a blade into his belly. Abner died. Blood for blood. Asahel, Joab's brother.

[28] When David heard, he said: "Before the LORD, I and my kingdom are forever innocent of the blood of Abner son of Ner. [29] Let it fall on Joab's head and his whole father's house. May Joab's line never lack someone with an open sore, or leprosy, or a crutch, or a sword's death, or an empty belly."

[30] So Joab and his brother Abishai killed Abner — for killing their brother Asahel in the battle at Gibeon.

[31] David ordered Joab and everyone with him: "Tear your clothes. Put on sackcloth. Mourn in front of Abner's body." King David himself walked behind the bier.

[32] They buried Abner at Hebron. The king wept aloud at the grave. All the people wept. [33] The king sang a lament for Abner:

Should Abner have died a fool's death?
[34] Your hands were not bound.
Your feet were not shackled.
You fell as one falls before criminals.

And all the people wept over him again.

[35] They came urging David to eat something while it was still day, but he swore an oath: "God do worse to me if I taste bread or anything else before the sun sets."

[36] The people noticed. They approved. Everything the king did, they approved. [37] That day every last one of them — all Israel — knew the king had no hand in the murder of Abner son of Ner.

[38] The king said to his servants: "Don't you understand? A prince. A great man. Has fallen in Israel today. [39] And me — anointed king, yes, but worn thin today. These men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too hard for me. May the LORD repay the evildoer according to his evil."

[1] When Saul's son heard that Abner had died in Hebron, his hands went slack and all Israel was shaken.

[2] Ish-bosheth had two men who captained raiding bands — Baanah and Rechab, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, from the tribe of Benjamin. (Beeroth counts as Benjaminite territory,

[3] though the original Beerothites fled to Gittaim long ago and have lived as outsiders there ever since.)

[4] One more thing: Jonathan, Saul's son, had a son with crippled feet. He was five years old when the news from Jezreel arrived — that Saul and Jonathan were dead. His nurse snatched him up and ran. In her panic to escape, she dropped him. That is how he became lame. His name was Mephibosheth.

[5] So Rechab and Baanah, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, set out and arrived at Ish-bosheth's house in the heat of the day, while he was taking his midday rest.

[6] They slipped inside as if to fetch a sack of wheat, drove a blade into his gut, and slipped back out — Rechab and his brother Baanah, vanishing.

[7] They had walked into the house while he slept on his bed in the inner room, struck him down, killed him, beheaded him, and taken the head with them. They traveled all night through the Arabah,

[8] and they brought Ish-bosheth's head to David at Hebron. "Look," they said to the king. "The head of Ish-bosheth, son of Saul — your enemy, the man who hunted your life. Today the LORD has paid back my lord the king on Saul and his offspring."

[9] David answered Rechab and his brother Baanah, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite: "As surely as the LORD lives — the one who has rescued me out of every trouble —

[10] when the man came to Ziklag telling me Saul was dead, thinking he was the bearer of good news, I had him seized and killed. That was the reward I gave him for his news.

[11] How much more, then, when wicked men murder an innocent man in his own house, on his own bed? Should I not demand his blood from your hands and wipe you off the earth?"

[12] David gave the order. His men killed them, cut off their hands and feet, and hung the bodies up beside the pool at Hebron. The head of Ish-bosheth they took and buried in Abner's tomb at Hebron.

[1] Then every tribe of Israel came to David at Hebron and said: "Look — we are your own bone, your own flesh. [2] Even back when Saul was king, you were the one leading Israel out and bringing them home. And the LORD told you: 'You will shepherd my people Israel. You will be their ruler.'"

[3] So all the elders of Israel came to the king at Hebron, and David cut a covenant with them there before the LORD. And they anointed him king over Israel.

[4] David was thirty when he took the throne. He reigned forty years. [5] Seven years and six months in Hebron over Judah, and thirty-three years in Jerusalem over all Israel and Judah together.

[6] The king and his men marched on Jerusalem against the Jebusites who held the land. The Jebusites told David: "You'll never get in here. Even the blind and the lame could turn you back." — meaning: David doesn't stand a chance.

[7] But David took the fortress of Zion anyway — the City of David.

[8] That day David said, "Anyone who wants to strike the Jebusites — go up through the water shaft to get at those 'lame and blind' that David's soul hates." That is where the saying comes from: "The blind and the lame shall not enter the house."

[9] David moved into the fortress and called it the City of David. He built it up — from the Millo and inward. [10] And he kept growing stronger, because the LORD God of Armies was with him.

[11] Hiram king of Tyre sent envoys to David, along with cedar timber, carpenters, and stonemasons. They built David a palace. [12] And David understood it then: the LORD had established him as king over Israel and was lifting up his kingdom for the sake of his people Israel.

[13] After he moved from Hebron to Jerusalem, David took more concubines and wives, and more sons and daughters were born to him. [14] These are the names of the children born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon, [15] Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia, [16] Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet.

[17] When the Philistines heard David had been anointed king over Israel, every last one of them came up looking for him. David heard about it and went down to the stronghold. [18] The Philistines came and spread themselves across the Valley of Rephaim.

[19] David asked the LORD: "Should I go up against the Philistines? Will you give them into my hand?" The LORD said to David: "Go. I am handing them over."

[20] So David came to Baal-perazim and struck them down there. And he said: "The LORD has burst through my enemies in front of me — like water bursting a dam." That is why he named the place Baal-perazim — Master of Breakthroughs. [21] The Philistines abandoned their idols there, and David and his men carried them off.

[22] The Philistines came up again and spread across the Valley of Rephaim. [23] David asked the LORD, and this time the LORD said: "Don't go straight up. Circle around behind them and come at them opposite the balsam trees. [24] When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the trees — move. Move fast. Because that's the LORD himself going out ahead of you to cut down the Philistine army."

[25] David did exactly what the LORD commanded. He cut the Philistines down all the way from Geba to Gezer.

[1] David gathered Israel's finest again—thirty thousand strong. [2] He marched them out from Baale-judah to bring back the Ark of God, the Ark that bears the Name—the Name of the LORD of Armies, who sits enthroned above the cherubim.

[3] They loaded the Ark of God onto a new cart and hauled it down from Abinadab's house on the hill. Uzzah and Ahio, Abinadab's sons, drove the cart, [4] guiding it with the Ark riding behind. Ahio walked in front. [5] David and the whole house of Israel celebrated before the LORD with everything they had—songs, harps, lyres, tambourines, rattles, cymbals—a riot of music.

[6] But when they reached Nacon's threshing floor, the oxen stumbled. Uzzah's hand shot out to steady the Ark. [7] The LORD's anger blazed against him. God struck him down on the spot for his irreverence, and Uzzah died there beside the Ark of God.

[8] David burned—furious that the LORD had broken out against Uzzah. That place has been called Perez-uzzah ever since. [9] And David was afraid of the LORD that day. He said, "How can the Ark of the LORD ever come to me?" [10] So he refused to bring it to the City of David. He diverted it instead to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite.

[11] The Ark stayed there three months. And the LORD blessed Obed-edom and everything he had.

[12] Word reached David: "The LORD has blessed Obed-edom's household and everything he owns, all because of the Ark of God." So David went down and brought the Ark up from Obed-edom's house to the City of David—this time with joy. [13] Every six steps the bearers took, he sacrificed a bull and a fattened calf. [14] And David danced before the LORD with everything in him, wearing only a linen ephod. [15] David and all Israel brought the Ark up with shouting and the blast of the ram's horn.

[16] As the Ark entered the City of David, Michal—Saul's daughter—looked down from her window. She saw King David leaping and spinning before the LORD, and contempt curdled in her heart.

[17] They carried the Ark in and set it in its place inside the tent David had pitched for it. Then David offered burnt offerings and peace offerings before the LORD. [18] When he finished, he blessed the people in the name of the LORD of Armies, [19] and he handed out food to everyone in the crowd—every Israelite, men and women alike—a loaf of bread, a date-cake, and a raisin-cake apiece. Then the whole nation went home.

[20] David went home too, to bless his own household. But Michal came out to meet him with venom on her tongue: "How dignified the king of Israel looked today—stripping down in front of his servants' slave girls like some shameless fool!"

[21] David shot back: "It was before the LORD. The LORD, who chose me over your father and his whole house, who appointed me ruler over the LORD's people Israel—I will dance before the LORD. [22] And I'll go lower than this. I'll humiliate myself in my own eyes. But those slave girls you mentioned? They will hold me in honor."

[23] And Michal, daughter of Saul, never bore a child to the day she died.

[1] The king settled into his house. The LORD had given him rest from every enemy ringed around him.

[2] David turned to Nathan the prophet: "Look at this. I'm living in a house of cedar while the ark of God sits under tent cloth."

[3] Nathan answered, "Whatever you have in mind, go do it. The LORD is with you."

[4] But that night the word of the LORD came to Nathan:

[5] "Go tell my servant David — this is what the LORD says: You? You're going to build me a house to live in?

[6] I haven't lived in a house from the day I brought Israel up out of Egypt to this very day. I've been moving — a tent for my dwelling, a tabernacle for my home.

[7] Everywhere I've traveled with the people of Israel, did I ever once say a word to any of the tribal leaders I appointed to shepherd my people — did I ever ask, 'Why haven't you built me a house of cedar?'

[8] So now — tell my servant David: This is what the LORD of armies says. I took you from the pasture, from trailing the flock, and made you ruler over my people Israel.

[9] I've been with you wherever you went. I've cut down every enemy in your path. And I'm going to make your name great — a name to rank with the greatest names on earth.

[10] I'll plant my people Israel in a place of their own. They'll put down roots and live undisturbed. No more torment. No more violent men grinding them down the way they did at the beginning,

[11] from the days I set judges over my people Israel. I'm giving you rest from all your enemies. And hear this: the LORD himself will build you a house.

[12] When your days are done and you lie down with your fathers, I'll raise up your offspring after you — your own flesh and blood — and I'll establish his kingdom.

[13] He's the one who will build a house for my name. And I'll set the throne of his kingdom in place — forever.

[14] I'll be his father. He'll be my son. When he goes wrong, I'll discipline him — with a rod the way any man does, with the strokes anyone deserves.

[15] But my steadfast love? I will never tear it away from him the way I tore it from Saul, whom I removed before you.

[16] Your house and your kingdom will stand secure before me forever. Your throne will be established forever."

[17] Nathan delivered the whole vision, every word of it, to David.

[18] Then King David went in and sat before the LORD. He said: "Who am I, Lord GOD, and what is my house, that you have brought me this far?

[19] And as if this were not enough in your eyes, Lord GOD, you've spoken about the future of your servant's house — far into the distance. Is this how you deal with a man, Lord GOD?

[20] What more can David say to you? You know your servant, Lord GOD.

[21] For the sake of your word, and because your heart willed it, you've done this enormous thing and let your servant in on it.

[22] So you are great, Lord GOD. There is none like you. There is no God but you — everything we've heard with our own ears confirms it.

[23] And who is like your people Israel — one nation on earth that God himself went out to redeem as his own, to make a name for himself, to do for them great and awe-inspiring things by driving out nations and their gods before your people, whom you redeemed for yourself out of Egypt?

[24] You established Israel as your people forever. And you, LORD, became their God.

[25] So now, LORD God, take the word you've spoken about your servant and his house and make it stand forever. Do exactly what you said.

[26] Your name will be magnified forever — they'll say, 'The LORD of armies is God over Israel.' And the house of your servant David will stand secure before you.

[27] Because you, LORD of armies, God of Israel, have opened your servant's ear and said, 'I will build you a house' — that's why your servant has found the courage to pray this prayer to you.

[28] And now, Lord GOD, you are God. Your words are true. And you've promised this good thing to your servant.

[29] So now — be pleased to bless the house of your servant, that it may go on before you forever. You yourself, Lord GOD, have spoken it. And by your blessing the house of your servant will be blessed forever."

[1] After this, David struck the Philistines hard. He broke them. He wrenched the reins of power right out of their hands.

[2] Then he turned on Moab. He made them lie down on the ground and measured them off with a rope—two lengths to die, one length to live. Moab became his, paying tribute.

[3] He hit Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah, when Hadadezer marched out to reclaim his grip on the Euphrates.

[4] David seized seventeen hundred horsemen and twenty thousand foot soldiers. He hamstrung all the chariot horses except for a hundred he kept for himself.

[5] When the Arameans of Damascus came to bail Hadadezer out, David cut down twenty-two thousand of them.

[6] He planted garrisons throughout Aram of Damascus, and the Arameans became his, paying tribute. Wherever David went, the LORD made him win.

[7] David stripped the gold shields off Hadadezer's officers and carried them back to Jerusalem.

[8] From Betah and Berothai, Hadadezer's cities, he hauled away staggering loads of bronze.

[9] When Toi, king of Hamath, heard that David had crushed Hadadezer's entire army—

[10] he sent his son Joram to David with greetings and congratulations, because Hadadezer had been Toi's enemy too, and Toi had been at war with him. Joram brought articles of silver, gold, and bronze,

[11] which David dedicated to the LORD, along with the silver and gold he had set apart from every nation he had subdued—

[12] Edom, Moab, the Ammonites, the Philistines, Amalek, and the plunder from Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.

[13] David's name thundered after he came back from striking down eighteen thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.

[14] He stationed garrisons throughout Edom, all of it, and every Edomite became David's subject. Wherever David went, the LORD made him win.

[15] So David reigned over all Israel. He governed his people with justice and made things right.

[16] Joab son of Zeruiah commanded the army. Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud kept the records.

[17] Zadok son of Ahitub and Ahimelech son of Abiathar were priests. Seraiah was the scribe.

[18] Benaiah son of Jehoiada led the Kerethites and the Pelethites. And David's sons served as priests.

[1] One day David asked, "Is anyone from Saul's house still alive? I want to show kindness for Jonathan's sake."

[2] There was a servant of Saul's household named Ziba. They summoned him to David, and the king said, "Are you Ziba?" "At your service," he answered.

[3] The king pressed him: "Is there anyone left from Saul's family I can show God's own kindness to?" Ziba said, "There's still a son of Jonathan — crippled in both feet."

[4] "Where is he?" David asked. Ziba answered, "At the house of Machir son of Ammiel, in Lo Debar."

[5] So King David sent for him and brought him out of Machir's house in Lo Debar.

[6] When Mephibosheth, son of Jonathan, grandson of Saul, came before David, he fell on his face in homage. David said, "Mephibosheth." "Your servant," he answered.

[7] "Don't be afraid," David told him. "I'm going to show you kindness for your father Jonathan's sake. I'll restore to you every acre of land that belonged to your grandfather Saul, and you'll eat at my table — always."

[8] Mephibosheth bowed low and said, "What am I, that you'd even look at a dead dog like me?"

[9] Then the king called Ziba, Saul's servant, and told him: "Everything that belonged to Saul and his household — I'm giving it all to your master's grandson.

[10] You and your sons and your servants will work the land for him and bring in the harvest, so that your master's grandson has food on his table. But Mephibosheth himself will always eat at my table." Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.

[11] Ziba said to the king, "Whatever my lord the king commands, your servant will do." So Mephibosheth ate at David's table like one of the king's own sons.

[12] Mephibosheth had a young son named Mica, and every member of Ziba's household became Mephibosheth's servants.

[13] And Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem, eating at the king's table every day — crippled in both feet.

[1] Some time later, the king of Ammon died, and his son Hanun took the throne. [2] David said, "I'll show kindness to Hanun son of Nahash, the way his father showed kindness to me." So David sent a delegation to console him over his father.

But when David's men arrived in Ammonite territory, [3] the Ammonite nobles whispered to Hanun their lord: "You think David is honoring your father by sending these condolers? No — he sent these men to case the city, to scout it, to bring it down."

[4] So Hanun seized David's envoys, shaved off half of each man's beard, sliced their robes off at the buttocks, and sent them away exposed.

[5] When David heard, he sent messengers to meet them — these were men deeply humiliated — and the king said, "Stay in Jericho until your beards grow back. Then come home."

[6] The Ammonites realized they had made themselves a stench to David. So they hired Aramean mercenaries — twenty thousand foot soldiers from Beth-rehob and Zobah, a thousand men from the king of Maacah, and twelve thousand from Tob.

[7] David heard and dispatched Joab with the entire army — every fighting man he had.

[8] The Ammonites marched out and formed their battle line at the city gate. The Arameans from Zobah and Rehob, with the men from Tob and Maacah, took position separately in the open field.

[9] Joab saw the trap — battle lines in front of him and behind him. He picked Israel's best troops and arrayed them against the Arameans. [10] The rest he handed to his brother Abishai, who set them against the Ammonites. [11] Joab said, "If the Arameans overpower me, you come rescue me. If the Ammonites overpower you, I'll come rescue you. [12] Be strong. Let's fight like men — for our people, for the cities of our God. And let the LORD do what seems right to him."

[13] Joab advanced with his troops against the Arameans, and they broke and ran. [14] When the Ammonites saw the Arameans fleeing, they fled too — straight from Abishai back into the city. So Joab pulled back from the Ammonites and returned to Jerusalem.

[15] The Arameans saw they had been routed by Israel, and they regrouped. [16] Hadadezer sent for the Arameans across the Euphrates, and they came to Helam under Shobach, commander of Hadadezer's army.

[17] Word reached David. He gathered all Israel, crossed the Jordan, and arrived at Helam. The Arameans drew up against him, and the battle began. [18] But the Arameans broke again before Israel. David cut down seven hundred chariot drivers and forty thousand horsemen. He struck down Shobach the commander, and Shobach died there.

[19] When all the kings who served Hadadezer saw they had been beaten by Israel, they made peace with Israel and submitted to them. And the Arameans were finished helping the Ammonites — they were too afraid.

[1] Spring came around — the season when kings march out to war. David sent Joab out with his officers and the whole army of Israel. They shredded the Ammonites and laid siege to Rabbah. David stayed in Jerusalem.

[2] One evening David got up from his bed and walked the roof of the palace. From up there he saw a woman bathing. She was stunning.

[3] He sent someone to find out who she was. The report came back: "That's Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam, wife of Uriah the Hittite."

[4] David sent messengers to bring her. She came. He slept with her — she had just purified herself from her period — and then she went home.

[5] She got pregnant. She sent word to David: "I'm pregnant."

[6] David sent a message to Joab: "Send me Uriah the Hittite." Joab sent him.

[7] When Uriah arrived, David asked him how Joab was doing, how the troops were doing, how the war was going.

[8] Then David said, "Go home. Wash your feet. Relax." Uriah left the palace, and the king sent a gift after him.

[9] But Uriah didn't go home. He slept at the palace gate with the king's servants.

[10] When David heard Uriah hadn't gone home, he asked him, "You've been on the road. Why didn't you go home?"

[11] Uriah said, "The ark is in a tent. Israel and Judah are camped in the open field. My commander Joab and the king's men are sleeping on the ground. And I'm supposed to go home, eat, drink, and sleep with my wife? By your life, I will not do such a thing."

[12] David said, "Stay here one more day. Tomorrow I'll send you back." So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next.

[13] David invited him to dinner. He got him drunk. But that night Uriah still went out and slept among the king's servants — he did not go home.

[14] In the morning David wrote a letter to Joab and sent it by Uriah's own hand.

[15] The letter said: "Put Uriah on the front line where the fighting is fiercest. Then pull back from him so he gets struck down and dies."

[16] So Joab, watching the city, posted Uriah at the spot where he knew the toughest defenders were.

[17] The men of the city came out and fought Joab. Some of David's soldiers fell. Uriah the Hittite died too.

[18] Joab sent David a full report of the battle.

[19] He instructed the messenger: "When you finish telling the king everything about the battle,

[20] if his temper flares and he says, 'Why did you go so close to the city? Didn't you know they'd shoot from the wall?

[21] Who killed Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Wasn't it a woman who dropped a millstone on him from the wall at Thebez? So why did you go that close?' — then say, 'Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead.'"

[22] The messenger went. He told David everything Joab had sent him to say.

[23] He said, "The enemy pushed us back into the open field, but we drove them back to the city gate.

[24] Then the archers shot at us from the wall. Some of the king's men died. Your servant Uriah the Hittite is also dead."

[25] David told the messenger, "Tell Joab this: 'Don't let it shake you. The sword devours one as well as another. Press the attack on the city. Take it down.' Encourage him."

[26] When Uriah's wife heard her husband was dead, she mourned for him.

[27] When the mourning was over, David sent for her and brought her into his house. She became his wife and bore him a son.

But what David had done was evil in the eyes of the LORD.

[1] So the LORD sent Nathan to David. Nathan walked in and said: "Two men lived in the same city. One was rich. One was poor.

[2] The rich man had flocks and herds — more than he could count.

[3] The poor man had nothing. Nothing but a single lamb, small, that he had bought. He raised it. It grew up alongside his children. It ate from his plate, drank from his cup, slept in his arms. It was like a daughter to him.

[4] One day a traveler showed up at the rich man's door. But the rich man couldn't bear to slaughter one of his own animals to feed his guest. So he took the poor man's lamb — and served it to the traveler."

[5] David's blood went hot. He erupted at Nathan: "As the LORD lives, the man who did this deserves to die.

[6] He'll pay for that lamb four times over — because he did this thing, because he had no pity."

[7] Nathan looked at him. "You are the man. This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I anointed you king over Israel. I rescued you from Saul's hand.

[8] I handed you your master's house. I put your master's wives in your arms. I gave you Israel and Judah. And if that wasn't enough, I would have given you more, and more again.

[9] So why did you despise the word of the LORD by doing this evil thing? You struck down Uriah the Hittite with the sword and took his wife to be yours. You killed him with the sword of the Ammonites.

[10] Now the sword will never leave your house — because you despised me and took the wife of Uriah the Hittite to be your own.

[11] This is what the LORD says: I am raising disaster against you from inside your own house. I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to a man close to you. He will sleep with them in broad daylight.

[12] You did it in secret. I will do it in front of all Israel, under the sun."

[13] David said to Nathan, "I have sinned against the LORD." And Nathan said to David, "The LORD has put your sin away. You will not die.

[14] But because by this you have given the LORD's enemies every reason to mock him — the child born to you will die."

[15] Nathan went home. And the LORD struck the child Uriah's wife had borne to David, and the child grew gravely ill.

[16] David begged God for the boy. He fasted. He went into his house and lay on the ground through the night.

[17] The elders of his household stood over him, trying to get him up off the floor. He wouldn't move. He wouldn't eat with them.

[18] On the seventh day the child died. David's servants were afraid to tell him. They whispered to each other, "Look — while the child was alive we spoke to him and he wouldn't listen. How can we tell him the child is dead? He might do something terrible."

[19] But David saw his servants whispering. He understood. He asked them, "Is the child dead?" They said, "He's dead."

[20] David got up off the ground. He washed. He put on oil. He changed his clothes. He went into the house of the LORD and worshiped. Then he went home and asked for food, and they set it before him, and he ate.

[21] His servants said, "What are you doing? While the child was alive, you fasted and wept. Now that he's dead, you get up and eat?"

[22] He said, "While the child was alive I fasted and wept. I thought: Who knows? Maybe the LORD will be gracious to me and let the child live.

[23] But now he's dead. Why should I fast? Can I bring him back? I am going to him. He is not coming back to me."

[24] Then David comforted Bathsheba his wife. He went to her and slept with her, and she gave birth to a son. They named him Solomon. The LORD loved him,

[25] and sent word through Nathan the prophet to call him Jedidiah — Beloved of the LORD — for the LORD's sake.

[26] Meanwhile Joab was fighting against Rabbah of the Ammonites. He captured the royal city.

[27] Joab sent messengers to David: "I have fought against Rabbah. I have taken the city of waters.

[28] Now gather the rest of the army, lay siege to the city, and capture it — before I take it myself and it gets named after me."

[29] So David gathered the whole army, marched to Rabbah, attacked it, and took it.

[30] He lifted the crown from the head of their king — a crown of gold weighing a talent, set with a precious stone — and it was placed on David's head. He hauled out plunder from the city, mountains of it.

[31] He brought out the people and put them to work with saws and iron picks and axes, and set them to making bricks. He did this to every Ammonite city. Then David and the whole army returned to Jerusalem.

[1] Some time later, this happened: Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister named Tamar. And Amnon, David's son, became obsessed with her.

[2] Amnon was so tormented over his sister Tamar that he made himself sick. She was a virgin, and it seemed impossible to Amnon that he could do anything to her.

[3] But Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah. Jonadab was a shrewd man.

[4] He asked Amnon, "Why are you, the king's son, looking more haggard every morning? Tell me." Amnon said, "I'm in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister."

[5] Jonadab said, "Lie down in bed. Pretend to be sick. When your father comes to check on you, say, 'Please let my sister Tamar come and feed me. Let her prepare the food right in front of me so I can watch — and eat from her hand.'"

[6] So Amnon lay down and faked illness. When the king came to see him, Amnon said, "Please — let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in front of me, and I'll eat from her hand."

[7] David sent word to Tamar at the palace: "Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare him some food."

[8] Tamar went to Amnon's house, where he was lying down. She took dough, kneaded it, shaped the cakes in his sight, and baked them.

[9] Then she took the pan and served him — but he refused to eat. "Send everyone out of here," Amnon said. So every servant left.

[10] Amnon said to Tamar, "Bring the food into the bedroom so I can eat from your hand." Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them to her brother Amnon in his bedroom.

[11] But when she brought them close so he could eat, he grabbed her. "Come to bed with me, sister," he said.

[12] "No, brother!" she cried. "Don't force me. This isn't done in Israel. Don't do this disgraceful thing.

[13] Where could I take my shame? And you — you'd be counted among the worst fools in Israel. Please — speak to the king. He won't keep me from you."

[14] But he wouldn't listen. He overpowered her and raped her.

[15] Then Amnon hated her — with a hatred more violent than the lust he'd felt before. "Get up," he said. "Get out."

[16] "No!" she said. "Sending me away is a worse wrong than what you've already done." But he wouldn't listen.

[17] He called his attendant. "Get this woman out of here. Throw her out and bolt the door behind her."

[18] (She was wearing an ornate robe, the kind the king's virgin daughters wore.) The servant put her out and bolted the door.

[19] Tamar poured ashes on her head, tore the ornate robe she was wearing, put her hand on her head, and walked away weeping aloud.

[20] Her brother Absalom said to her, "Has Amnon, your brother, been with you? Keep quiet for now, sister. He's your brother. Don't take this to heart." So Tamar stayed in her brother Absalom's house — a desolate woman.

[21] When King David heard all of this, he was furious — but he did nothing.

[22] Absalom said nothing to Amnon, good or bad. But he hated Amnon for violating his sister Tamar.

[23] Two years later, Absalom was shearing sheep at Baal-hazor near Ephraim, and he invited all the king's sons.

[24] Absalom went to the king and said, "Your servant is shearing sheep. Would the king and his court honor me by coming?"

[25] "No, my son," the king said. "We shouldn't all come — we'd be a burden to you." Absalom pressed him, but David wouldn't go, though he gave him his blessing.

[26] Then Absalom said, "If not you, then please let my brother Amnon come with us." "Why him?" the king asked.

[27] But Absalom pressed him, so David sent Amnon and the rest of the king's sons.

[28] Absalom gave his men their orders: "Watch carefully. When Amnon's heart is light with wine and I say, 'Strike Amnon down' — kill him. Don't be afraid. I'm the one giving the order. Be strong. Be brave."

[29] So Absalom's men did to Amnon what Absalom commanded. Then every one of the king's sons jumped on his mule and fled.

[30] While they were still on the road, the news reached David: "Absalom has killed every one of the king's sons. Not one is left."

[31] The king stood up, tore his clothes, and threw himself on the ground. All his servants stood by, their clothes torn too.

[32] But Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, spoke up: "Don't let my lord think they've killed all the young men, the king's sons. Only Amnon is dead. Absalom has been planning this from the day Amnon raped his sister Tamar.

[33] So don't let my lord the king take this report to heart, thinking all his sons are dead. Only Amnon is dead."

[34] Meanwhile, Absalom had fled. The watchman on duty looked up and saw a crowd of people coming down the road from the west, along the side of the hill.

[35] Jonadab said to the king, "Look — the king's sons. It's just as I said."

[36] As he finished speaking, the king's sons arrived, wailing aloud. And the king and all his servants wept too — wept hard.

[37] Absalom fled to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. And David mourned for his son day after day.

[38] After Absalom fled to Geshur, he stayed there three years.

[39] And King David's spirit longed to go out to Absalom, because he had come to terms with Amnon's death.

[1] Joab son of Zeruiah could read the king. He saw it: David's heart was leaning toward Absalom.

[2] So Joab sent to Tekoa and brought back a clever woman. He told her, "Play the mourner. Dress in grief, no oil, no perfume — be a woman who has wept for the dead a long time.

[3] Go to the king and say what I tell you to say." And he put the words in her mouth.

[4] When the woman from Tekoa came before the king, she dropped face-down to the ground and cried out, "Save me, O king!"

[5] The king said, "What's wrong?" She said, "I'm a widow. My husband is dead.

[6] Your servant had two sons. They quarreled out in the field. No one to pull them apart — one struck the other and killed him.

[7] Now the whole clan has risen against me, demanding, 'Hand over the one who struck his brother. We'll kill him for the life he took — and finish off the heir while we're at it.' They mean to snuff out the last ember I have left, leaving my husband no name, no remnant on the face of the earth."

[8] The king said to the woman, "Go home. I'll take care of it."

[9] But the woman from Tekoa said, "My lord the king, let the guilt fall on me and on my father's house. Let the king and his throne be innocent of it."

[10] The king said, "If anyone gives you trouble, bring him to me — he won't lay a hand on you again."

[11] She said, "Let the king swear by the LORD his God that the blood-avenger won't multiply destruction by killing my son." And he said, "As the LORD lives, not one hair of your son's head will fall to the ground."

[12] Then the woman said, "Let your servant speak one more word to my lord the king." He said, "Speak."

[13] And she said, "Then why have you plotted exactly this kind of thing against God's people? In what you just declared, the king has convicted himself — because he hasn't brought home the son he banished.

[14] We all die. We're like water spilled on the ground, gone, ungathered. But God does not simply take a life. He devises ways — He works it out so the exile is not cast off forever.

[15] I came to say this to my lord the king because the people frightened me. Your servant thought, 'Let me speak to the king. Maybe he'll do what I ask.

[16] Maybe the king will listen and rescue his servant from the hand of the man who would cut me and my son out of God's inheritance.'

[17] And your servant thought, 'May the word of my lord the king bring me rest. For my lord the king is like the angel of God, weighing good and evil.' May the LORD your God be with you."

[18] Then the king answered the woman: "Don't hide anything from me when I ask you this." She said, "Let my lord the king speak."

[19] The king said, "Is Joab's hand in all of this?" The woman answered, "As surely as you live, my lord the king — no one slips left or right of what my lord the king has said. Yes. Your servant Joab is the one. He told me to come. He put every word in my mouth.

[20] He did it to turn the situation around. But my lord has wisdom like the angel of God — he knows everything that happens in the land."

[21] The king said to Joab, "All right. I'll do it. Go. Bring the young man Absalom back."

[22] Joab fell face-down to the ground and blessed the king. "Today your servant knows he has found favor in your eyes, my lord the king — because the king has granted his servant's request."

[23] Then Joab got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.

[24] But the king said, "Let him go to his own house. He is not to see my face." So Absalom went to his own house and did not see the king.

[25] In all Israel there was no one praised for his looks like Absalom. From the sole of his foot to the crown of his head, no flaw.

[26] When he cut his hair — he cut it once a year because it grew heavy on him — he weighed it: two hundred shekels by the royal weight.

[27] Absalom had three sons and a daughter named Tamar. She became a beautiful woman.

[28] Absalom lived in Jerusalem two full years without ever seeing the king's face.

[29] So Absalom sent for Joab to send him to the king. Joab wouldn't come. He sent a second time. Still he wouldn't come.

[30] Absalom said to his servants, "See Joab's field next to mine? He's got barley there. Go burn it." And Absalom's servants set the field on fire.

[31] Then Joab got up and came to Absalom at his house. "Why did your servants set my field on fire?"

[32] Absalom said, "Listen — I sent for you. I said come here, I want to send you to the king to ask, 'Why did I come back from Geshur? Better for me to still be there.' Now let me see the king's face. If there's guilt in me, let him put me to death."

[33] So Joab went to the king and told him. The king summoned Absalom. He came to the king and bowed face-down to the ground before him. And the king kissed Absalom.

[1] Time passed, and Absalom got himself a chariot, horses, and fifty men to run ahead of him.

[2] He made it his habit to rise early and station himself beside the road leading to the city gate. Whenever someone came along with a dispute to bring before the king for judgment, Absalom would call him over and ask, "What town are you from?" The man would answer, "Your servant is from one of the tribes of Israel."

[3] Then Absalom would say, "Look — your case is solid, airtight. But there's no one from the king who'll hear you out."

[4] And he'd add, "If only I were the judge in this land! Anyone with a complaint or a case could come to me, and I'd see that justice was done."

[5] Whenever someone approached to bow before him, Absalom would reach out, take hold of him, and kiss him.

[6] This is how Absalom treated every Israelite who came to the king for judgment. And so he stole the hearts of the people of Israel.

[7] Four years went by. Then Absalom said to the king, "Let me go to Hebron to make good on a vow I swore to the LORD.

[8] When I was living in Geshur in Aram, I made this vow: 'If the LORD really does bring me back to Jerusalem, I'll worship him in Hebron.'"

[9] The king said, "Go in peace." So Absalom got up and went to Hebron.

[10] But Absalom had already sent secret messengers throughout the tribes of Israel: "The moment you hear the trumpet, shout — 'Absalom is king in Hebron!'"

[11] Two hundred men from Jerusalem went with Absalom. They'd been invited as guests and went in good faith — they had no idea what was happening.

[12] While he was offering the sacrifices, Absalom also summoned Ahithophel the Gilonite — David's own counselor — from his hometown of Giloh. The conspiracy gathered strength, and the people streaming to Absalom kept multiplying.

[13] A messenger reached David: "Israel's heart has gone over to Absalom."

[14] David said to all his officials in Jerusalem with him, "On your feet — we have to run. There's no escape from Absalom otherwise. Move fast, before he overtakes us, brings disaster down on us, and puts this city to the sword."

[15] The king's officials answered him, "Whatever our lord the king decides — we're with you."

[16] So the king left, with his whole household behind him. He left ten concubines to look after the palace.

[17] The king went out with all the people following him, and they stopped at the last house.

[18] All his servants marched past him — every Kerethite, every Pelethite, and all six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gath — filing by the king.

[19] The king said to Ittai the Gittite, "Why are you coming with us? Turn back. Stay with the new king. You're a foreigner, an exile from your own land.

[20] You only got here yesterday — should I drag you up and down with us today when I don't even know where I'm going? Go back. Take your countrymen with you. May steadfast love and faithfulness go with you."

[21] But Ittai answered the king, "As the LORD lives, and as my lord the king lives — wherever my lord the king is, whether it means death or life, that's where your servant will be."

[22] David said, "Then come on — march." So Ittai the Gittite marched on, with all his men and all the families with them.

[23] The whole countryside wept aloud as the people passed by. The king crossed the Kidron Valley, and all the people moved out toward the wilderness road.

[24] And there was Zadok too, with all the Levites carrying the ark of the covenant of God. They set the ark of God down — and Abiathar came up — until all the people had finished filing out of the city.
