[7] If a man is caught kidnapping a fellow Israelite, treating him as property or selling him, the kidnapper must die. Burn the evil out from among you.

[8] When skin disease breaks out, watch carefully and follow exactly what the Levitical priests instruct you. Do what I commanded them.

[9] Remember what the Lord your God did to Miriam on the road out of Egypt.

[10] When you make any kind of loan to your neighbor, do not march into his house to seize what he pledged.

[11] Stand outside. Let the man you are lending to bring the pledge out to you.

[12] If he is poor, do not sleep with his pledge in your possession.

[13] Return it to him at sunset, so he can sleep in his own cloak and bless you. That will count as righteousness before the Lord your God.

[14] Do not exploit a hired worker who is poor and needy, whether he is a fellow Israelite or a foreigner living in one of your towns.

[15] Pay him his wages each day before the sun sets. He is poor and counting on it. Otherwise he will cry out to the Lord against you, and you will be guilty.

[16] Parents must not be put to death for their children, nor children for their parents. Each person dies for his own sin.

[17] Do not twist justice against the foreigner or the orphan. Do not seize a widow's cloak as a pledge.

[18] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt, and the Lord your God redeemed you from there. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

[19] When you are harvesting in your field and you forget a sheaf, do not go back for it. Leave it for the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow — so the Lord your God will bless everything your hands do.

[20] When you beat the olives from your trees, do not go over the branches again. What remains belongs to the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow.

[21] When you harvest your vineyard, do not pick it clean. What remains belongs to the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow.

[22] Remember: you were a slave in Egypt. That is why I am commanding you to do this.

[1] When two people have a dispute and bring it to court, the judges will hear the case and rule — vindicating the one in the right, condemning the one in the wrong.

[2] If the guilty party deserves a beating, the judge will have him lie down and be flogged in his presence — the number of lashes matching the severity of the crime.

[3] But the limit is forty. Not one more. Go beyond that and you've degraded a fellow human being in public — and you've degraded your community along with him.

[4] Don't muzzle an ox while it's threshing the grain.

[5] When brothers live together and one of them dies without leaving a son, the widow must not marry a stranger from outside the family. Her husband's brother is to take her as his wife and fulfill the duty of a brother-in-law to her.

[6] The first son she bears will carry the dead brother's name — so his name isn't wiped out of Israel.

[7] But if the man refuses to take his brother's widow, she will go up to the elders at the city gate and say: "My husband's brother won't keep his brother's name alive in Israel. He refuses the duty."

[8] Then the elders of his town will summon him and talk to him. If he stands his ground and says, "I don't want to marry her,"

[9] then his sister-in-law will approach him in front of the elders, pull the sandal off his foot, spit in his face, and declare: "This is what happens to the man who refuses to build up his brother's house."

[10] From that day on, his family in Israel will be known as: "The House of the Unsandaled."

[11] If two men are fighting and the wife of one of them rushes in to rescue her husband from his attacker, and she grabs the attacker by his private parts —

[12] cut off her hand. No mercy.

[13] You must not carry two different weights in your bag — one heavy, one light.

[14] You must not keep two different measures in your house — one large, one small.

[15] Use accurate, honest weights. Use accurate, honest measures. Then you will live long in the land the Lord your God is giving you.

[16] Anyone who cheats with weights and measures is detestable to the Lord your God. Anyone who deals dishonestly — detestable.

[17] Remember what Amalek did to you on the road out of Egypt.

[18] How he ambushed you when you were exhausted and worn down — picking off the stragglers at the back, the weak and the weary. He had no fear of God.

[19] So when the Lord your God gives you rest from all the enemies around you, in the land he's handing you as your inheritance — wipe out the memory of Amalek from under heaven. Don't forget.

[1] When you finally cross into the land the LORD your God is handing you—when you take it, settle in it, make it home—[2] take the first of every harvest the ground gives you, pack it in a basket, and carry it to the place the LORD your God will choose as the dwelling for his name.

[3] Walk up to the priest on duty in those days and say: "Today I declare before the LORD your God that I have arrived in the land he swore to our ancestors he would give us."

[4] The priest will take the basket from your hands and set it before the altar of the LORD your God.

[5] Then, standing there in his presence, you will speak these words:

"My father was a wandering Aramean. He went down into Egypt with almost nothing—just a handful of people—and lived there as an outsider. But there he grew into a nation: great, strong, vast in number. [6] The Egyptians turned on us. They abused us. They crushed us under brutal labor. [7] So we cried out to the LORD, the God of our ancestors—and the LORD heard. He saw our suffering, our exhaustion, our oppression. [8] And the LORD brought us out of Egypt with a fist of iron and an arm stretched wide, with terror and signs and wonders. [9] He led us here and gave us this land—a land flooding with milk and honey. [10] So now, look—I'm bringing the first of the harvest from the soil you, LORD, have given me."

Set the basket down before the LORD your God and bow low before him. [11] Then celebrate. Every good thing the LORD your God has given you and your household—enjoy it. And bring the Levite and the foreigner among you into the celebration.

[12] Every third year—the year of the tithe—when you've finished setting aside a tenth of everything you produced and given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, and the widow, so they can eat their fill in your towns—[13] then say before the LORD your God:

"I've cleared out the sacred portion from my house. I've given it to the Levite, the foreigner, the orphan, the widow—just as you commanded. I haven't broken your commands. I haven't forgotten them. [14] I didn't eat any of it while mourning. I didn't touch it while ritually unclean. I didn't offer any of it to the dead. I obeyed the voice of the LORD my God. I did everything you commanded. [15] Look down from your holy home, from heaven, and bless your people Israel and this soil you've given us—just as you swore to our ancestors—a land flooding with milk and honey."

[16] Today the LORD your God is commanding you to live by these decrees and rulings. Guard them. Do them. With everything in you—heart and soul.

[17] Today you have declared the LORD to be your God—that you will walk his ways, keep his decrees, his commands, his rulings, and listen to his voice.

[18] And today the LORD has declared you to be his own, his treasured people, just as he promised—and that you will keep all his commands. [19] He will lift you high above every nation he has made—high in praise, in fame, in honor—and you will be a people consecrated to the LORD your God, just as he said.

[1] Moses and Israel's elders gave the people their orders: Keep every command I'm laying on you today.

[2] The day you cross the Jordan into the land the LORD your God is giving you, set up massive stones and coat them with plaster.

[3] Write on them every word of this law — the moment you cross over — so you can enter the land the LORD your God is giving you, a land flowing with milk and honey, just as the LORD, the God of your ancestors, promised you.

[4] So when you've crossed the Jordan, plant these stones on Mount Ebal — the ones I'm telling you about right now — and coat them with plaster.

[5] Build an altar there to the LORD your God, an altar of stones. No iron tool touches them.

[6] Build the altar of the LORD your God with uncut stones, and offer burnt offerings on it to the LORD your God.

[7] Sacrifice fellowship offerings there. Eat them. Celebrate before the LORD your God.

[8] And on those stones write every word of this law — etched plain, etched clear.

[9] Then Moses, with the Levitical priests, spoke to all Israel: Quiet, Israel. Listen. Today you have become the people of the LORD your God.

[10] So obey the voice of the LORD your God. Live out his commands and his statutes — the ones I'm giving you today.

[11] That same day Moses gave the people this charge:

[12] When you've crossed the Jordan, these tribes stand on Mount Gerizim to bless the people — Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Joseph, and Benjamin.

[13] And these stand on Mount Ebal to pronounce the curse — Reuben, Gad, Asher, Zebulun, Dan, and Naphtali.

[14] Then the Levites will raise their voices and call out to every Israelite:

[15] Cursed is the one who carves an idol or casts a metal image — a thing the LORD detests, the handiwork of a craftsman — and sets it up in secret. And all the people answer: Amen.

[16] Cursed is the one who treats his father or mother with contempt. And all the people answer: Amen.

[17] Cursed is the one who moves his neighbor's boundary stone. And all the people answer: Amen.

[18] Cursed is the one who sends a blind man wandering off the road. And all the people answer: Amen.

[19] Cursed is the one who twists justice against the immigrant, the orphan, the widow. And all the people answer: Amen.

[20] Cursed is the man who sleeps with his father's wife — he has uncovered his father's bed. And all the people answer: Amen.

[21] Cursed is the one who has sex with any animal. And all the people answer: Amen.

[22] Cursed is the one who sleeps with his sister — his father's daughter or his mother's daughter. And all the people answer: Amen.

[23] Cursed is the one who sleeps with his mother-in-law. And all the people answer: Amen.

[24] Cursed is the one who strikes down his neighbor in secret. And all the people answer: Amen.

[25] Cursed is the one who takes a bribe to kill the innocent. And all the people answer: Amen.

[26] Cursed is the one who refuses to uphold the words of this law by living them out. And all the people answer: Amen.

[1] If you actually listen to the voice of the LORD your God — if you guard and live out every command I'm giving you today — then the LORD your God will lift you high above every nation on earth.

[2] All these blessings will hunt you down and overtake you, because you listened to the voice of the LORD your God:

[3] Blessed in the city. Blessed in the field.

[4] Blessed: the children of your body, the harvest of your soil, the young of your herds, the calves of your cattle, the lambs of your flocks.

[5] Blessed: your basket and your kneading bowl.

[6] Blessed when you walk in. Blessed when you walk out.

[7] The enemies who rise against you — the LORD will shatter them in front of your eyes. They'll march at you down one road and scatter from you down seven.

[8] The LORD will command the blessing to land on your barns, on everything your hand touches. He will bless you in the land the LORD your God is giving you.

[9] The LORD will establish you as his holy people, just as he swore he would, if you keep the commands of the LORD your God and walk in his ways.

[10] Every nation on earth will see that the name of the LORD is spoken over you — and they will be afraid of you.

[11] The LORD will overwhelm you with good things: the fruit of your body, the young of your livestock, the harvest of your soil — in the land the LORD swore to your ancestors he would give you.

[12] The LORD will throw open his good storehouse — the skies — to send rain on your land in its season and to bless everything your hands do. You will lend to many nations and borrow from none.

[13] The LORD will make you the head, not the tail. You'll be on top, never on the bottom — if you listen to the commands of the LORD your God that I'm giving you today, and guard them, and do them,

[14] never veering right or left from any of these words I'm commanding you today, never running off after other gods to serve them.

[15] But if you will not listen to the voice of the LORD your God — if you do not guard and live out every command and statute I'm giving you today — then all these curses will hunt you down and overtake you:

[16] Cursed in the city. Cursed in the field.

[17] Cursed: your basket and your kneading bowl.

[18] Cursed: the children of your body, the harvest of your soil, the young of your herds, the lambs of your flocks.

[19] Cursed when you walk in. Cursed when you walk out.

[20] The LORD will turn loose on you a curse, a panic, a withering — on every venture your hand takes up — until you are destroyed, until you vanish in a hurry, because of the evil you've done in abandoning him.

[21] The LORD will fasten plague to you until it has wiped you off the land you are entering to possess.

[22] The LORD will strike you with consumption, with fever, with inflammation, with scorching heat and drought, with blight and mildew. They will chase you until you perish.

[23] The sky above your head will turn to bronze. The ground beneath your feet, to iron.

[24] The LORD will turn the rain of your land into dust and ash — falling from the sky on you until you are destroyed.

[25] The LORD will hand you over, broken, to your enemies. You'll march at them down one road and scatter from them down seven. You will become a horror to every kingdom on earth.

[26] Your corpses will be food for every bird in the sky and every beast on the ground — and no one will frighten them away.

[27] The LORD will strike you with the boils of Egypt, with tumors, with running sores, with the itch — none of which can be cured.

[28] The LORD will strike you with madness, with blindness, with the panic of a soul coming apart.

[29] At noon you will grope like a blind man fumbling in the dark. Nothing you do will succeed. You will be robbed and beaten down every single day, with no one to rescue you.

[30] You will get engaged to a woman — another man will rape her. You will build a house — you will never live in it. You will plant a vineyard — you will never taste its fruit.

[31] Your ox slaughtered before your eyes — and you'll get nothing of it. Your donkey ripped away in front of you — never returned. Your flock handed over to your enemies — and no one will rescue you.

[32] Your sons and daughters handed over to a foreign people while you watch — eyes aching for them all day long — and your hand powerless.

[33] A people you've never heard of will eat the harvest your soil produced, everything you worked for. You will be nothing but oppressed and crushed, every day of your life.

[34] You will go insane from what your own eyes are forced to see.

[35] The LORD will strike you on the knees and the legs with boils that cannot be cured — from the soles of your feet to the top of your head.

[36] The LORD will haul you off — you and the king you've set over yourselves — to a nation neither you nor your ancestors have ever known. There you will serve other gods, gods of wood and stone.

[37] You will become a horror story, a punchline, a curse-word among every people the LORD drives you to.

[38] You'll carry mountains of seed out to your field — and gather almost nothing back. The locusts will devour it.

[39] You'll plant vineyards and tend them — but you will not drink the wine, you will not gather the grapes. The worm will eat them.

[40] You'll have olive trees across all your territory — but no oil to anoint yourself. Your olives will drop off the branch.

[41] You'll father sons and daughters — but they will not be yours. They'll be marched off into captivity.

[42] All your trees, all the produce of your soil — the insects will claim it.

[43] The foreigner living among you will climb higher and higher above you, and you will sink lower and lower.

[44] He will lend to you — you'll have nothing to lend him. He will be the head; you will be the tail.

[45] All these curses will come down on you. They will chase you and overtake you and destroy you — because you would not listen to the voice of the LORD your God, would not keep the commands and statutes he gave you.

[46] They will brand you and your descendants as a sign and a wonder forever.

[47] Because you did not serve the LORD your God with joy, with gladness of heart, when everything was overflowing —

[48] you will serve enemies the LORD sends against you, in hunger and thirst and nakedness and total destitution. He will fasten an iron yoke on your neck until he has destroyed you.

[49] The LORD will bring a nation against you from far away, from the ends of the earth — swooping in like an eagle — a nation whose language you will not understand,

[50] hard-faced, with no respect for the old and no mercy for the young.

[51] They will eat the young of your livestock and the harvest of your soil — until you are destroyed. They will leave you no grain, no wine, no oil, no calves, no lambs — until you are gone.

[52] They will lay siege to you in every city across your land, until the high fortified walls you trusted come crashing down. They will trap you in every city, in every corner of the land the LORD your God gave you.

[53] In the siege — in the desperate squeeze your enemies will put you in — you will eat the fruit of your own body, the flesh of the sons and daughters the LORD your God gave you.

[54] Even the most refined, the most tender man among you will turn pitiless toward his own brother, toward the wife in his arms, toward the children he has left —

[55] refusing to share with any of them the flesh of his own children he is eating, because nothing else is left to him in the siege, in the squeeze your enemies will put on you in every city.

[56] The most refined, most delicate woman among you — the kind who would not put the sole of her foot on the ground because she was so delicate — will turn pitiless toward the husband in her arms, toward her own son, her own daughter,

[57] hiding the afterbirth that came from between her legs, hiding the children she just bore — to eat them in secret — because nothing else is left in the siege, in the squeeze your enemies will put on you in your cities.

[58] If you will not guard and live out every word of this teaching written in this scroll — if you will not fear this glorious and terrifying name, the LORD your God —

[59] then the LORD will strike you and your descendants with plagues out of nightmare: relentless, savage, and lingering — diseases that drag on and refuse to die.

[60] He will bring back on you every sickness of Egypt that terrified you, and they will cling to you.

[61] Every disease and every plague not even written in this scroll — the LORD will pile them on you until you are destroyed.

[62] You who were once as many as the stars of the sky will be left a handful — because you would not listen to the voice of the LORD your God.

[63] Just as the LORD once delighted to make you flourish and multiply, he will now delight to ruin you and wipe you out. You will be torn off the land you are entering to possess.

[64] The LORD will scatter you among every people, from one end of the earth to the other. There you will serve other gods — gods of wood and stone — gods neither you nor your ancestors ever knew.

[65] Among those nations you will find no rest, no place for the sole of your foot to come down. The LORD will give you there a trembling heart, eyes wasting away, a soul running on empty.

[66] Your life will dangle by a thread in front of you. You will be terrified, night and day, and never sure you'll live to see morning.

[67] In the morning you'll say, "If only it were night." In the night you'll say, "If only it were morning." — because of the dread shaking your heart and the things your eyes are forced to see.

[68] The LORD will haul you back to Egypt in ships — by the very road I told you you would never see again. There you will offer yourselves up as slaves to your enemies — and no one will even buy you.

[1] These are the terms of the covenant the LORD ordered Moses to cut with Israel in the land of Moab—on top of the covenant he had already cut with them at Horeb.

[2] Moses summoned all Israel and said to them: You watched it with your own eyes—everything the LORD did in Egypt to Pharaoh, to his officials, to his whole land.

[3] The brutal tests your eyes witnessed, those staggering signs, those impossible wonders.

[4] But to this day the LORD has not given you a mind that grasps it, eyes that truly see, ears that actually hear.

[5] Forty years I marched you through the wilderness. Your clothes never wore out. The sandals on your feet never gave way.

[6] You ate no bread. You drank no wine, no beer. All so you would know: I am the LORD your God.

[7] When you arrived at this place, Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan came out to fight us—and we crushed them.

[8] We seized their land and handed it over to Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh as their inheritance.

[9] So keep the words of this covenant. Do them. Then you will succeed in everything you set your hand to.

[10] Today every one of you stands here before the LORD your God—your tribal chiefs, your elders, your officials, every man of Israel,

[11] your children, your wives, and the foreigners camped among you, from the woodcutter to the water-carrier—

[12] standing here to enter into covenant with the LORD your God, sworn under oath, the covenant the LORD your God is cutting with you today,

[13] so he can establish you today as his people and be your God—just as he promised you, just as he swore to your ancestors, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

[14] And I am not making this covenant, this sworn agreement, with you alone.

[15] I am making it with everyone standing here today before the LORD our God—and with everyone who is not here with us today.

[16] You know how we lived in Egypt. You know how we passed through the nations on the way here.

[17] You saw their vile things—their gods of wood and stone, silver and gold, propped up among them.

[18] Make sure there is no one here—no man, no woman, no clan, no tribe—whose heart is already turning away from the LORD our God to go worship the gods of those nations. Make sure no root grows among you that bears poison and bitterness as its fruit.

[19] When such a person hears the words of this oath, he congratulates himself in his heart: I'll be fine—I can walk in the stubbornness of my own heart. As if drenching the dry ground along with the wet would somehow save him.

[20] The LORD will not forgive that man. The LORD's anger and jealousy will burn against him like fire. Every curse written in this book will settle on him. The LORD will erase his name from under heaven.

[21] The LORD will single him out of all the tribes of Israel for disaster, in line with every curse of the covenant written in this scroll of instruction.

[22] Then the generations to come—your children who rise up after you, and the foreigner who arrives from a distant land—they will see the catastrophe inflicted on this land, the diseases the LORD has unleashed on it,

[23] and they will say: The whole land—burning sulfur, salt, scorched earth. Nothing planted. Nothing sprouting. Not a blade of green. Like the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah, Admah and Zeboiim, the cities the LORD demolished in his fury.

[24] And all the nations will ask: Why did the LORD do this to this land? Why this blazing, consuming rage?

[25] And the answer will come: Because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD, the God of their ancestors—the covenant he cut with them when he brought them out of Egypt.

[26] They went and served other gods. They bowed to gods they had never known, gods he had never assigned to them.

[27] So the LORD's anger ignited against this land. He poured down on it every curse written in this book.

[28] In burning fury, in towering wrath, the LORD ripped them from their soil and hurled them into another land—where they are to this day.

[29] The hidden things belong to the LORD our God. But what he has revealed belongs to us and our children forever—so we can live out every word of this instruction.

[1] When all of this lands on you — the blessing and the curse I've laid before you — and you're scattered among the nations where the LORD your God has driven you,

[2] and you come back to him, you and your children, with everything in you, listening to his voice the way I'm commanding you today —

[3] then the LORD your God will turn your exile around. He'll have compassion on you. He'll gather you back from every nation where he scattered you.

[4] Even if you've been flung to the far edge of the sky, he'll reach there and pull you back.

[5] He'll bring you home — to the land your ancestors held — and you'll possess it again. He'll do you more good than he ever did them. He'll make you more than they ever were.

[6] And the LORD your God will perform surgery on your heart and on your children's hearts — circumcising it so you can love him with everything you are, with every breath in you, and live.

[7] Then he'll turn every one of these curses onto your enemies — onto the ones who hated you, the ones who hunted you.

[8] As for you — you'll come back. You'll listen to the LORD's voice. You'll do everything he commands you today.

[9] And the LORD your God will overflow you with good in everything your hands touch — your children, your livestock, your harvest. He'll take delight in prospering you the way he delighted in your ancestors,

[10] when you listen to the LORD your God's voice, when you keep his commands and statutes written in this book of instruction, when you turn back to him with everything in you.

[11] Look — this command I'm giving you today isn't too hard for you. It isn't out of reach.

[12] It's not up in the sky, so you'd have to say, "Who'll climb up and bring it down so we can hear it and do it?"

[13] It's not across the sea, so you'd have to say, "Who'll cross over and bring it back so we can hear it and do it?"

[14] No — the word is right next to you. It's in your mouth. It's in your heart. You can do this.

[15] Look — today I'm setting before you life and good, death and ruin.

[16] What I'm commanding you today is this: love the LORD your God, walk in his ways, keep his commands and statutes and rulings — and you will live. You will multiply. The LORD your God will bless you in the land you're about to enter and take.

[17] But if your heart turns away — if you stop listening, if you're seduced into bowing down to other gods and serving them —

[18] I'm telling you straight, today: you will perish. You will not last long in the land you're about to cross the Jordan to enter and possess.

[19] I'm calling heaven and earth as witnesses against you today: I have set before you life and death, blessing and curse. Choose life — so you and your children can actually live —

[20] by loving the LORD your God, by listening to his voice, by holding fast to him. Because he is your life. He is the length of your days. He's the reason you'll live in the land the LORD swore to give to your ancestors — to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

[1] Moses went out and spoke these words to all Israel.

[2] He told them: "I'm a hundred and twenty years old today. I can't lead you in and out anymore. And the LORD has told me plainly: 'You won't cross this Jordan.'

[3] The LORD your God himself will cross over ahead of you. He will wipe out these nations in front of you, and you will take their land. Joshua will lead you across, just as the LORD has said.

[4] The LORD will do to them exactly what he did to Sihon and Og, kings of the Amorites — destroyed them, erased their land.

[5] He will hand them over to you. And when he does, you will deal with them according to every command I've given you.

[6] Be strong. Be brave. Don't flinch. Don't let them terrify you. The LORD your God walks with you. He will not abandon you. He will not let you go."

[7] Then Moses called Joshua and said to him in front of everyone: "Be strong. Be brave. You're the one going with this people into the land the LORD swore to their ancestors. You're the one who will divide it among them as their inheritance.

[8] The LORD himself goes ahead of you. He will be with you. He won't fail you. He won't forsake you. So don't be afraid. Don't lose your nerve."

[9] Moses wrote this law down and gave it to the priests, the sons of Levi — the ones who carry the ark of the LORD's covenant — and to all the elders of Israel.

[10] He ordered them: "Every seven years, at the appointed time — the year for cancelling debts, during the Feast of Tabernacles —

[11] when all Israel comes to appear before the LORD your God at the place he will choose, you will read this law aloud in their hearing. Every word of it.

[12] Gather the people — men, women, children, and the foreigners living in your towns. Let them listen. Let them learn. Let them fear the LORD your God and follow every word of this law with care.

[13] And their children, who don't yet know this law, will hear it too. They will learn to fear the LORD your God for as long as you live in the land you're crossing the Jordan to possess."

[14] The LORD said to Moses: "Your time is almost up. Call Joshua. The two of you, present yourselves at the tent of meeting, and I will commission him." So Moses and Joshua went and stood at the tent of meeting.

[15] The LORD appeared at the tent in a pillar of cloud, and the cloud stood at the entrance.

[16] The LORD said to Moses: "You're about to lie down with your ancestors. And then this people will rise up and prostitute themselves with the foreign gods of the land they're entering. They will abandon me. They will break the covenant I made with them.

[17] When that day comes, my anger will burn against them. I will walk away. I will hide my face. They will be devoured. Disaster after disaster will hunt them down. On that day they'll say, 'Aren't these horrors falling on us because our God isn't with us anymore?'

[18] And I will hide my face — completely — because of all the evil they've done, because they turned to other gods.

[19] Now write down this song. Teach it to the people of Israel. Put it in their mouths, so this song can stand as my witness against them.

[20] Because once I've brought them into the land I swore to their ancestors — land flowing with milk and honey — and they've eaten their fill and grown fat, they will turn to other gods. They will serve them. They will despise me. They will shatter my covenant.

[21] And when disaster after disaster finally finds them, this song will testify against them, face to face — their descendants will never forget it. I know what they're inclined to do, even now, before I've brought them into the land I swore to give them."

[22] So Moses wrote down this song that day and taught it to the Israelites.

[23] Then the LORD commissioned Joshua son of Nun: "Be strong. Be brave. You will bring the Israelites into the land I swore to give them. And I will be with you."

[24] When Moses finished writing every word of this law in a book — start to finish —

[25] he gave this command to the Levites who carry the ark of the LORD's covenant:

[26] "Take this book of the law and place it beside the ark of the covenant of the LORD your God. Let it stand there as a witness against you.

[27] Because I know you. I know how stubborn you are, how you dig in your heels. Look — even while I'm alive and standing with you, you've defied the LORD. How much worse will it be after I'm gone?

[28] Gather to me all the elders of your tribes and your officials. Let me speak these words in their hearing. Let me call heaven and earth as witnesses against them.

[29] Because I know that after I'm gone, you will ruin yourselves. You will turn off the path I commanded you. Disaster will find you in the days to come, because you will do what is evil in the LORD's eyes, provoking him with the work of your hands."

[30] Then Moses spoke the words of this song, from beginning to end, in the hearing of the entire assembly of Israel:

[1] Listen, sky — I'm about to speak.
Earth, catch every word from my mouth.
[2] Let my teaching fall like rain,
my speech settle like dew,
like cloudbursts on tender grass,
like soaking showers on new growth.

[3] I'm calling out the name of the LORD —
give our God the weight he deserves.
[4] He is the Rock. His work is flawless.
Every path he walks is justice.
A God of faithfulness, no crookedness in him —
upright. True.

[5] But his children? They wrecked it.
The corruption is theirs, not his —
a twisted, crooked generation.
[6] Is this how you repay the LORD,
you fools, you brainless people?
Isn't he your Father, the one who bought you,
who made you and set you on your feet?

[7] Remember the old days.
Think back through the generations.
Ask your father — he'll tell you.
Ask the elders — they'll explain.
[8] When the Most High parceled out the nations,
when he split humanity into peoples,
he drew the borders
according to the number of the divine sons.
[9] But the LORD's share was his own people —
Jacob, the lot assigned to him.

[10] He found him in a wasteland,
in a howling emptiness of desert.
He circled around him, watched over him,
guarded him like the pupil of his eye.
[11] Like an eagle stirring its nest,
hovering above its young —
he spread his wings, caught him,
carried him on his pinions.
[12] The LORD alone led him.
No foreign god alongside.

[13] He set him riding high on the land,
fed him the produce of the fields,
nursed him with honey from the rock,
oil from the flint-stone cliffs,
[14] curds from the herd, milk from the flock,
fat lambs, prize rams of Bashan,
goats, and the finest wheat —
and you drank wine, the blood of grapes, foaming red.

[15] But Jeshurun grew fat — and kicked.
You got fat, bloated, stuffed —
and you abandoned the God who made you,
treated the Rock of your rescue like nothing.
[16] You provoked him with foreign gods,
disgusted him with abominations.
[17] You sacrificed to demons — not God —
to gods you'd never known,
newcomers just arrived on the scene,
gods your ancestors never feared.
[18] The Rock who fathered you — you forgot him.
The God who labored to bring you forth — you blanked him out.

[19] The LORD saw it and spurned them —
his own sons and daughters had provoked him.
[20] He said: I'll hide my face from them.
I'll see what becomes of them.
Because they're a generation gone sideways,
children with no faithfulness in them.
[21] They made me jealous with a no-god,
provoked me with their empty idols.
So I'll make them jealous with a no-people,
provoke them with a nation of fools.

[22] A fire has blazed up in my anger —
it burns down to the deepest grave.
It consumes the earth and what it grows.
It scorches the foundations of the mountains.
[23] I'll pile disasters on them.
I'll empty my quiver into them.
[24] Wasted by hunger, devoured by plague,
ravaged by bitter destruction —
I'll send the fangs of beasts against them,
the venom of crawling things in the dust.
[25] Outside, the sword will take their children.
Inside, the houses, terror —
young men, young women,
nursing babies, gray-haired elders.

[26] I said: I'll scatter them to the wind.
I'll erase their memory from humankind.
[27] Except — I dreaded the enemy's gloating,
dreaded their adversaries getting it wrong,
saying, "Our own hand did this.
The LORD had nothing to do with it."

[28] These are a nation without sense,
no discernment in them.
[29] If only they were wise — they'd see this.
They'd understand where it ends.
[30] How could one chase a thousand,
two put ten thousand to flight,
unless their Rock had sold them out,
unless the LORD had handed them over?
[31] Because their rock is not like our Rock —
even our enemies admit it.

[32] Their vine grows out of Sodom's soil,
out of Gomorrah's fields.
Their grapes are poison.
Their clusters, bitter.
[33] Their wine is serpent venom,
the deadly cruelty of cobras.

[34] Isn't this stored up with me,
sealed away in my vaults?
[35] Vengeance is mine. Repayment too.
In time, their foot will slip.
The day of their disaster is racing close.
What's coming for them is hurtling in fast.

[36] The LORD will vindicate his people.
He'll show mercy to his servants
when he sees their strength is spent,
when no one is left — neither slave nor free.
[37] He'll say: Where are their gods now?
The rock they ran to for shelter —
[38] who ate the fat of their sacrifices,
drank the wine of their offerings?
Let them get up and help you.
Let them be your hiding place.

[39] See it now — I, I am he.
There is no god beside me.
I kill and I bring to life.
I wound and I heal.
And no one can wrench anything from my hand.
[40] I raise my hand to heaven and swear:
As I live forever —
[41] when I sharpen my flashing sword,
when my hand grips justice,
I'll bring vengeance on my foes,
I'll repay those who hate me.
[42] My arrows will drink blood till they're drunk,
my sword will devour flesh —
blood of the slain and the captive,
heads of the enemy commanders.

[43] Celebrate, you nations, with his people!

[22] What does a man actually get for all his labor, all the anxious striving under the sun?

[2] Spread your investment seven ways — eight, even. You have no idea what disaster is heading for this land.

[3] When the clouds are heavy, they empty themselves on the earth. When a tree falls — north, south, wherever it lands — that's where it stays.

[4] Watch the wind too closely and you'll never plant. Read the clouds too carefully and you'll never harvest.

[5] You don't understand how the wind moves. You don't understand how bones form inside a pregnant woman. So you certainly don't understand the work of God, who is making all of it.

[6] Plant in the morning. Keep planting into the evening. You don't know which sowing will take — this one, that one, maybe both.

[7] Light is sweet. It's a good thing for the eyes to see the sun.

[8] If a person lives many years, let them enjoy every one of them — but remember: the dark days are coming, and there will be many. Everything ahead is vapor.

[9] So go on, young one — enjoy your youth. Let your heart be glad in the days of your strength. Walk wherever your heart pulls you, see whatever your eyes long to see. But know this: God will bring all of it into the reckoning.

[10] Get the anxiety out of your chest. Get the ache out of your body. Youth and its dark hair — they're vapor too, gone before you can hold them.

[1] Remember the One who made you while you're still young — before the dark days arrive, before the years catch up and you find yourself saying, "There's nothing left I enjoy."

[2] Remember him before the sun goes dim, before the light and the moon and the stars all fade, before the clouds keep rolling back after every rain.

[3] That's the day the guards of the house start to tremble, and the strong men stoop, and the women at the millstones quit because so few are left, and the ones looking through the windows can barely see.

[4] The doors to the street swing shut. The sound of grinding fades to a whisper. A man wakes at the first bird's call, but every song sounds muted to him now.

[5] Heights terrify him. The road itself becomes a threat. His hair turns to almond blossom. He drags himself along like a swollen grasshopper. Desire dies out. Because every one of us is walking toward our eternal home, and the mourners are already gathering in the street.

[6] Remember him — before the silver cord snaps, before the golden bowl shatters, before the pitcher breaks at the spring and the wheel splinters at the well.

[7] The dust returns to the ground where it came from, and the breath returns to God who gave it.

[8] Smoke and vapor, said the Teacher. All of it. Nothing but smoke.

[9] Beyond being wise himself, the Teacher kept teaching the people what he knew. He weighed, he searched, he arranged proverb after proverb with care.

[10] He hunted for the right words — words that would land true, words honest enough to bear the weight of what is real.

[11] The sayings of the wise are like cattle prods, like nails driven deep — collected by one Shepherd, given to drive us forward.

[12] Beyond that, my son, take warning: there's no end to the making of books, and too much study will wear your body out.

[13] Here is the end of the matter. You've heard it all now. Fear God. Keep his commandments. This is what it means to be human.

[14] Because God will bring every act into judgment — every hidden thing, whether it was good or whether it was evil.

[1] Paul, sent by Christ Jesus through God's own will, to the saints in Ephesus — the faithful ones in Christ Jesus: [2] grace to you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

[3] Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has poured over us every blessing the heavenly realms hold — all of it in Christ. [4] Before the world had a foundation, he chose us in him — chose us to stand before him holy, unblemished, drenched in love. [5] He marked us out in advance to be adopted as his own children through Jesus Christ. This was his pleasure, his design, [6] a tribute to the staggering beauty of his grace — grace he lavished on us in the Beloved. [7] In him we are bought back. Through his blood, our trespasses are wiped clean. That is the wealth of his grace — [8] grace he flooded over us, every drop of wisdom and insight included. [9] He let us in on the secret of his will — the mystery he'd been pleased to keep, the plan he'd been holding in Christ — [10] a plan for the fullness of time: to gather everything up under one head, Christ. Everything in the heavens. Everything on the earth. All of it, in him.

[11] In him we were also chosen as his inheritance — set apart in advance by the One who arranges every detail according to the counsel of his will — [12] so that we, the first to hope in Christ, would become a living anthem to his glory. [13] And you — you too were folded in when you heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation. When you believed, you were stamped with the seal: the promised Holy Spirit, [14] who is the down payment on our inheritance until the day we are fully claimed — to the praise of his glory.

[15] So ever since I heard about your faith in the Lord Jesus and the love you have for all the saints, [16] I haven't stopped giving thanks for you. I name you in my prayers, [17] asking that the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, would hand you a spirit of wisdom and revelation — that you would know him deeply, truly. [18] I pray the eyes of your heart would be flooded with light, so you'd see what hope his call carries, what wealth lies in the glory of his inheritance among the saints, [19] and how immeasurably vast his power is toward us who believe — the same explosive strength [20] he unleashed in Christ when he raised him from the dead and seated him at his right hand in the heavenly realms, [21] high above every ruler, every authority, every power, every dominion, every name that can be named — in this age and the one to come. [22] He laid everything beneath his feet and gave him, over all things, to be head of the church — [23] which is his body, the fullness of the One who fills all things in every way.

[1] You were dead. Walking around, breathing, eating, working — but dead. Your trespasses and sins had killed you. [2] You moved through life on autopilot, syncing your steps to the rhythm of this broken age, taking orders from the prince of the air — that spirit still humming through everyone who refuses God. [3] We were all there. Every one of us. Chasing whatever our flesh demanded, whatever flashed across our minds, doing what came naturally — and what came naturally was wrath. We were born deserving it.

[4] But God. Two words that change everything. God, drowning in mercy, undone by the love He had for us — [5] reached into our graves while we were still corpses in our sins and pulled us up alongside the Messiah. Grace. That is what saved you. [6] He raised us with Him. Seated us with Him. Right now we are already there with Jesus in the heavenly places — [7] so that for ages to come the universe will be staring at the unthinkable kindness He poured out on us in Christ Jesus.

[8] Grace saved you. Faith received it. None of this originated with you — it is God's gift, start to finish. [9] Nothing you did. Nothing you could brag about. [10] We are His handiwork — fresh-made in Christ Jesus for the good works God already mapped out for us to walk into.

[11] So remember who you used to be. Gentiles by birth. The circumcised crowd — the ones whose mark was made by human hands — called you "the uncircumcised," like it was a slur. [12] Back then you were cut off from the Messiah, locked out of Israel's citizenship, strangers to the covenants and the promises they carried, drifting through the world with no hope and no God.

[13] But now — in Christ Jesus — you who were a thousand miles off have been brought close by His blood. [14] He Himself is our peace. He took two hostile camps and made them one, demolishing the wall that kept them apart, [15] dismantling in His own flesh the law with its commandments and regulations — so that out of the two He could forge one new humanity, and peace itself. [16] Through the cross He reconciled both groups into one body to God, killing the hostility right there on the wood.

[17] Then He came and announced peace — peace to you who were far away, peace to those who were near — [18] because through Him both of us now walk straight into the Father's presence, carried by one Spirit.

[19] So you are no longer outsiders. No longer foreigners with expired visas. You are fellow citizens with God's holy people, members of His household — [20] built on the foundation the apostles and prophets laid down, with Christ Jesus Himself as the cornerstone holding the whole thing true. [21] In Him the entire structure rises, fitted together joint by joint, growing into a holy temple in the Lord — [22] and in Him you too are being built into the place where God lives by His Spirit.

[1] This is why I, Paul, am Christ Jesus' prisoner — for you, the outsiders.

[2] You must have heard about the assignment God's grace handed me on your behalf — [3] how the mystery was disclosed to me directly, by revelation. I've already written about this briefly. [4] When you read it, you'll catch what I now grasp about Christ's mystery — [5] something hidden from earlier generations, never made known to humanity until now, when the Spirit broke it open to his holy apostles and prophets.

[6] Here it is: the nations are co-heirs, fellow members of the same body, partners in the promise — all of it through Christ Jesus, through the gospel.

[7] I became a servant of this gospel by the gift of God's grace, handed to me by the working of his power. [8] To me — the least of all God's holy ones — this grace was given: to announce to the nations the unsearchable wealth of Christ, [9] and to bring into the light what God has been arranging all along — the mystery hidden for ages in the God who made everything.

[10] The plan? That now, through the church, the layered wisdom of God would be put on display before the rulers and powers in the heavenly realms. [11] This was his eternal purpose, accomplished in Christ Jesus our Lord. [12] In him, through faith in him, we can walk straight up to God — bold, unafraid, with full access.

[13] So please — don't lose heart over what I'm suffering for you. My suffering is your glory.

[14] This is why I drop to my knees before the Father, [15] from whom every family in heaven and on earth takes its name. [16] I'm asking him — out of the vast riches of his glory — to strengthen you with power through his Spirit, deep in your inner being. [17] That Christ would make his home in your hearts through faith. That you'd be rooted, grounded — anchored in love — [18] so you could grasp, alongside all God's people, just how wide and long and high and deep this thing is. [19] To know the love of Christ that outstrips knowledge itself. To be filled — flooded — with all the fullness of God.

[20] Now to the One who can do infinitely more than anything we'd dare ask or even imagine — working by his power inside us — [21] to him be the glory in the church and in Christ Jesus, across every generation, forever and ever. Amen.

[1] So I'm asking you—I, the prisoner in the Lord's service—live a life that actually matches the calling you received. [2] Carry yourselves with humility and gentleness. Be patient. Put up with each other in love. [3] Work hard to guard the unity the Spirit creates—peace is the cord that binds you together. [4] One body. One Spirit. One hope—the same hope you were called into. [5] One Lord. One faith. One baptism. [6] One God and Father over everything, working through everything, present in everything.

[7] But to each of us, grace was given—measured out according to the generosity of Christ. [8] That's why Scripture says: "When he climbed to the heights, he led the captives away in his train and handed out gifts to his people." [9] Now "he climbed"—what does that imply except that he first descended, down into the lowest reaches of the earth? [10] The one who descended is the same one who ascended—higher than every heaven—so he could fill the universe with himself.

[11] And here are the gifts he gave: some to be apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, some shepherds and teachers— [12] the point being to equip God's people for the work of ministry, to build up the body of Christ, [13] until we all arrive together at unity in faith and in knowing the Son of God, until we reach full maturity, the measure of the full stature of Christ himself. [14] No more infants. No more getting tossed like driftwood on every wave, blown around by every fresh doctrine, conned by sleight-of-hand teachers running their schemes. [15] Instead—speak the truth, in love, and grow up into him in every way. He is the head: Christ. [16] From him the whole body draws its life, every joint supplying what the others need, every part doing its work, and the whole thing grows—building itself up in love.

[17] So this is what I'm telling you, and I'm saying it in the Lord's name: stop living the way the pagans live, with their minds chasing nothing. [18] Their thinking has gone dark. They're cut off from the life God gives, because they don't know him—because their hearts have turned to stone. [19] Past feeling anything, they've handed themselves over to indulgence, to every kind of impurity, hungry for more. [20] But that is not the Christ you learned. [21] Assuming you actually heard him and were taught in him—and the truth is in Jesus— [22] you were taught to strip off your old self, the one rotting away under deceitful cravings, [23] to be made new in the way you think, [24] and to put on the new self—the one God created to look like him, in genuine righteousness and holiness.

[25] So drop the lies. Each of you, tell the truth to your neighbor—we belong to one another. [26] Be angry without sinning. Don't let the sun set on your fury. [27] Don't hand the devil an opening. [28] Whoever's been stealing—stop. Get to work instead. Put your hands to honest labor so you have something to share with whoever's in need. [29] Let no rotten word come out of your mouth—only what builds someone up, what fits the moment, what gives grace to whoever's listening. [30] And do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God. He's the one who sealed you for the day of redemption. [31] Get rid of all the bitterness, the rage, the anger, the shouting, the slander—all of it. Out, along with every form of malice. [32] Be kind to each other. Be tender. Forgive one another the way God in Christ forgave you.

[1] So imitate God. You're his beloved children—act like it. [2] Walk the road of love, the same road Christ walked when he gave himself up for us, an offering whose fragrance rose to God like incense.

[3] As for sexual sin, every kind of impurity, every grasping appetite—don't even let these be whispered about among you. That's not who saints are. [4] Crude talk, foolish talk, suggestive jokes—none of it fits. Replace it with gratitude. [5] Know this for certain: the sexually immoral, the impure, the greedy—who is really just an idolater with a different altar—none of them has any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God.

[6] Don't let anyone con you with hollow arguments. These are exactly the things that bring God's wrath down on the disobedient. [7] So don't throw in your lot with them. [8] You used to be darkness. Now you are light in the Lord. So live like children of light— [9] because light produces every kind of goodness, righteousness, and truth— [10] always sorting out what pleases the Lord. [11] Don't share in the sterile works of darkness; expose them instead. [12] What they do in secret is too shameful even to name out loud. [13] But everything dragged into the light becomes visible, [14] and whatever becomes visible becomes light itself. That's why it's said:

*Wake up, sleeper.
Rise from the dead.
Christ will shine on you.*

[15] So watch carefully how you walk—not like fools but like the wise, [16] making the most of every moment, because the days are evil. [17] Don't be reckless. Understand what the Lord wants. [18] And don't get drunk on wine—that road leads to ruin. Instead, be filled with the Spirit. [19] Speak to one another in psalms and hymns and Spirit-songs. Sing. Make music in your hearts to the Lord. [20] Give thanks always, for everything, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, to God the Father.

[21] Submit to one another out of reverence for Christ.

[22] Wives, to your own husbands as to the Lord— [23] because the husband is the head of the wife the way Christ is the head of the church, his body, of which he is the Savior. [24] As the church submits to Christ, so wives to their husbands, in everything.

[25] Husbands—love your wives. Love them the way Christ loved the church: he handed himself over for her. [26] He did it to make her holy, cleansing her by washing in water through the word, [27] so that he could present her to himself radiant—no stain, no wrinkle, nothing of the sort—holy and flawless. [28] In the same way, husbands ought to love their wives as they love their own bodies. The man who loves his wife loves himself. [29] Nobody ever hated his own flesh. He feeds it, cares for it—the way Christ cares for the church, [30] because we are members of his body. [31] *This is why a man will leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife, and the two will become one flesh.* [32] This is a profound mystery—I'm talking about Christ and the church. [33] But back to you: each husband, love your wife as you love yourself; and the wife—see to it that she respects her husband.

[1] Children, listen to your parents in the Lord — this is what's right. [2] "Honor your father and mother" — the first command that comes with a promise attached — [3] "so that things go well for you and your days on this earth are long."

[4] Fathers, don't grind your children down until they're seething. Raise them in the discipline and instruction that comes from the Lord.

[5] Slaves, obey your earthly masters with genuine respect — a trembling, sincere kind — the same way you'd obey Christ. [6] Not the performance of someone trying to look good when the boss is watching, but as servants of Christ doing God's will from the gut. [7] Serve wholeheartedly, as if the Lord himself were the one you're working for, not a human being — [8] knowing this: whatever good anyone does, the Lord pays it back, slave or free.

[9] Masters, do the same for them. Drop the threats. You know that the One who is Master over both you and them sits in heaven, and he plays no favorites.

[10] Finally — draw your strength from the Lord and from the immense force of his might. [11] Put on every piece of armor God provides, so you can hold your ground against the schemes of the devil. [12] Because our fight isn't with flesh and blood. It's with rulers, with authorities, with the cosmic powers running this present darkness, with spiritual forces of evil entrenched in the heavenly realms. [13] So pick up the full armor of God — so that when the evil day hits, you'll be able to stand, and after you've done everything, still be standing.

[14] Stand, then. Buckle truth around your waist. Strap on righteousness like body armor. [15] Lace up your feet with the readiness that comes from the gospel of peace. [16] On top of all this, grab the shield of faith — with it you can extinguish every flaming arrow the evil one fires. [17] Take the helmet of salvation. Take the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God.

[18] Pray. Pray at every moment, in the Spirit, every kind of prayer and request. Stay alert. Keep at it. Intercede for all God's people. [19] And pray for me — that when I open my mouth, the right words will be given, so I can boldly make known the mystery of the gospel — [20] the gospel I'm an ambassador for, in chains. Pray that I'll speak it the way I should: fearlessly.

[21] So that you know how I'm doing and what I'm up to — Tychicus, our dear brother and faithful servant in the Lord, will fill you in on everything. [22] I'm sending him to you for exactly this reason: so you'll know how we are, and so he can encourage your hearts.

[23] Peace to the brothers and sisters, and love joined with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. [24] Grace to everyone who loves our Lord Jesus Christ with a love that never fades.

[1] This happened in the days of Xerxes — the Xerxes whose empire stretched from India to Cush, one hundred and twenty-seven provinces under one crown.

[2] In those days, with King Xerxes seated on his royal throne in the fortress of Susa,

[3] he threw a banquet in the third year of his reign — a banquet for everyone who mattered: his officials, his court, the military command of Persia and Media, the nobles, the provincial governors. All of them, in his presence.

[4] For one hundred and eighty days he put it all on display — the staggering wealth of his kingdom, the glittering splendor of his greatness. Six months of spectacle.

[5] When those days were finished, the king threw another banquet — this one for everyone in the fortress of Susa, from the highest to the lowest. Seven days. In the garden courtyard of the royal palace.

[6] White and violet hangings, fastened with cords of fine linen and purple to silver rings on marble columns. Couches of gold and silver set on a pavement of porphyry, marble, mother-of-pearl, and precious stone.

[7] Drinks served in goblets of gold — every goblet different from the last — and royal wine flowing in abundance, as only a king can pour.

[8] The drinking followed one rule: no compulsion. The king had instructed his stewards to let each guest do as he pleased.

[9] Meanwhile, Queen Vashti was hosting her own banquet for the women, inside the royal palace of King Xerxes.

[10] On the seventh day, when the wine had finally caught up with him, the king summoned his seven eunuchs — Mehuman, Biztha, Harbona, Bigtha, Abagtha, Zethar, and Carkas — the men who served in his presence.

[11] His order: bring Queen Vashti before him wearing the royal crown. He wanted to show off her beauty to the assembled crowd and the officials. She was stunning to look at, and he wanted them to see her.

[12] Queen Vashti refused. She would not come at the king's command, delivered through his eunuchs. The king burned. Rage flared up inside him.

[13] So he turned to his wise men, the ones who read the times — because that was the king's custom, to consult those who knew law and precedent,

[14] the inner circle closest to him: Carshena, Shethar, Admatha, Tarshish, Meres, Marsena, and Memucan — the seven nobles of Persia and Media who saw the king's face and held the top seats in the kingdom.

[15] "By the law," he asked them, "what is to be done with Queen Vashti? She refused to obey King Xerxes' command delivered through the eunuchs."

[16] Then Memucan spoke up before the king and the nobles: "Queen Vashti has not just wronged the king. She has wronged every official and every people in every province of King Xerxes' empire.

[17] Because this story about the queen is going to reach every woman in the kingdom — and they will start looking at their husbands with contempt. They will say, 'King Xerxes ordered Queen Vashti brought before him, and she didn't come.'

[18] This very day, the noblewomen of Persia and Media who hear what the queen did will throw it back at every royal official. Contempt and fury — no end to it.

[19] So, if it pleases the king, let a royal decree go out, written into the laws of Persia and Media so it cannot be revoked: Vashti is never again to enter the presence of King Xerxes. And let the king give her royal position to another woman — one more worthy.

[20] When the king's edict is heard throughout this vast empire, every wife will honor her husband, from the greatest to the least."

[21] The advice landed well with the king and the nobles. The king did exactly what Memucan said.

[22] He sent letters to every royal province — each in its own script, each people in its own language — declaring that every man was to be master in his own house and speak the language of his own people.

[1] When the king's fury finally cooled, his mind drifted back to Vashti — what she had done, what he had decreed against her.

[2] His personal attendants saw the opening and spoke up: "Let a search begin for beautiful young virgins for the king.

[3] Appoint commissioners throughout every province of the empire to gather every stunning young woman to the harem in the citadel of Susa. Place them under Hegai, the king's eunuch, the guardian of the women, and let them receive their beauty treatments.

[4] Then the young woman who captivates the king will replace Vashti as queen." The plan pleased the king, and he set it in motion.

[5] Now in the citadel of Susa there lived a Jew named Mordecai son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish — a Benjaminite,

[6] one of the exiles Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had carried off from Jerusalem alongside Jeconiah king of Judah.

[7] Mordecai had raised his cousin Hadassah — that is, Esther — because she had no father or mother. The girl was breathtaking, stunning in form and face. When her parents died, Mordecai took her in as his own daughter.

[8] When the king's order and decree went out, hundreds of young women were brought to the citadel of Susa under Hegai's authority — and Esther was taken to the palace, placed in Hegai's care.

[9] She captivated him. He showed her favor, fast-tracked her beauty treatments and special diet, assigned her seven hand-picked maids from the palace, and moved her — with her entourage — into the best quarters of the harem.

[10] Esther had not revealed her people or her family — Mordecai had told her not to.

[11] Every single day Mordecai walked the courtyard outside the harem, trying to learn how Esther was and what was happening to her.

[12] Each young woman's turn came after twelve months of prescribed treatments — six months with oil of myrrh, six months with perfumes and cosmetics — only then was she ready to go in to King Xerxes.

[13] When a young woman went to the king, she could take with her from the harem anything she wanted.

[14] She would enter in the evening, and in the morning return to a second harem under Shaashgaz, the king's eunuch in charge of the concubines. She would not go to the king again unless he had delighted in her and summoned her by name.

[15] When Esther's turn came — Esther, daughter of Abihail (Mordecai's uncle), whom he had adopted as his own — she asked for nothing beyond what Hegai, the king's eunuch and guardian of the women, advised. And everyone who saw her was captivated by her.

[16] So Esther was brought to King Xerxes in his royal residence in the tenth month, the month of Tebeth, in the seventh year of his reign.

[17] The king loved Esther more than all the others. She won his favor and devotion above every other woman. He placed the royal crown on her head and made her queen in place of Vashti.

[18] Then the king threw a great banquet for all his officials and servants — Esther's banquet. He declared a holiday across the provinces and distributed gifts with royal generosity.

[19] When the virgins were gathered a second time, Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate.

[20] Esther still had not disclosed her family or her people, just as Mordecai had instructed. She obeyed him exactly as she had when he was raising her.

[21] During those days, while Mordecai sat at the king's gate, two of the king's eunuchs — Bigthan and Teresh, palace guards — grew bitter and plotted to assassinate King Xerxes.

[22] Mordecai got wind of it and told Queen Esther, and Esther reported it to the king on Mordecai's behalf.

[23] An investigation confirmed the plot. The two were hanged on a gallows. And it was all written down in the royal chronicles in the king's presence.

[1] After all this, King Xerxes elevated Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite. He promoted him, set his throne above every other official in the court. [2] Every royal servant at the king's gate bowed low and paid homage to Haman — the king had commanded it. But Mordecai would not bow. Would not kneel. Would not give him an inch.

[3] The other officials at the gate confronted him: "Why are you defying the king's order?" [4] Day after day they pressed him, and day after day he refused to listen. Finally they reported him to Haman — they wanted to see whether Mordecai's reason would hold. He had told them he was a Jew.

[5] When Haman saw it for himself — Mordecai standing straight while everyone else folded — fury surged through him. [6] But killing one man was beneath him. They had told him who Mordecai's people were, and now Haman wanted them all. Every Jew in the empire of Xerxes. Wiped out.

[7] In the first month — the month of Nisan — in the twelfth year of King Xerxes, they cast the pur (that is, the lot) before Haman. Day after day, month after month, until it landed on the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

[8] Haman went to the king. "There is a certain people," he said, "scattered across every province of your empire, separated from everyone else. Their laws differ from every other people's. They do not keep the king's laws. It is not in your interest to tolerate them. [9] If it pleases the king, let an order be written for their destruction. I will pay ten thousand talents of silver into the royal treasury for the men who carry out the work."

[10] The king pulled the signet ring from his finger and handed it to Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite — enemy of the Jews. [11] "Keep your money," the king said. "And do with the people whatever you want."

[12] On the thirteenth day of the first month, the royal scribes were summoned. Everything Haman dictated was written down — to the satraps of the king, to the governors of every province, to the officials of every people — each province in its own script, each people in its own language. It was issued in the name of King Xerxes and sealed with his ring. [13] Couriers carried the dispatches into every province with the order: destroy, kill, annihilate every Jew — young, old, women, children — all in a single day, the thirteenth of the twelfth month, the month of Adar. And plunder their possessions.

[14] A copy of the edict was to be published as law in every province, posted for every people to read, so they would be ready when the day came. [15] The couriers tore out at the king's command, and the decree went up in the citadel of Susa. The king and Haman sat down to drink. The city of Susa reeled.

[1] When Mordecai learned everything that had been done, he tore his clothes, threw on sackcloth and ashes, and walked out into the heart of the city, crying out — a long, bitter, devastating wail.

[2] He went as far as the king's gate, but no further. No one could enter the gate wearing sackcloth.

[3] And in every province, wherever the king's decree and his edict landed, the Jews fell into grief — fasting, weeping, wailing, sackcloth and ashes spread out like a bed beneath them.

[4] Esther's maids and her eunuchs came and told her. The queen was shaken to the core. She sent clothes to dress Mordecai, to get the sackcloth off him. He refused them.

[5] So Esther summoned Hathach, one of the king's eunuchs assigned to her, and sent him to Mordecai to find out what was happening and why.

[6] Hathach went out to Mordecai in the city square in front of the king's gate.

[7] Mordecai told him everything that had happened to him — and the exact sum of silver Haman had promised to pay into the royal treasury for the destruction of the Jews.

[12] Then Mordecai returned to the king's gate. But Haman rushed home, head covered, devastated.

[13] He told his wife Zeresh and all his friends everything that had happened. His advisors and his wife said to him: "If this Mordecai — before whom you've begun to fall — is Jewish, you cannot stand against him. You will fall. You will fall hard."

[14] They were still talking when the king's eunuchs arrived and hurried Haman off to the banquet Esther had prepared.

[1] So the king and Haman came to dine with Queen Esther. [2] And on this second day, as they drank their wine, the king said again, "What is your petition, Queen Esther? It's yours. Whatever you ask, up to half my kingdom—it's done."

[3] Esther answered: "If I have found favor with you, my king, and if it pleases you—grant me my life. That is my petition. And spare my people. That is my request. [4] Because we have been sold—me and my people—handed over to be slaughtered, butchered, wiped out. If we had only been sold into slavery, I would have held my tongue. [7] The king shot to his feet in fury, abandoned his wine, and stormed out into the palace garden. Haman stayed behind to beg Queen Esther for his life—he could see the king had already decided his fate.

"Will he now assault the queen too?" the king roared. "Right here in my own house?"

The words were barely out of his mouth before they covered Haman's face.

[10] So they hanged Haman on the gallows he had built for Mordecai. And the king's rage subsided.

[6] How can I bear to watch the catastrophe fall on my people? How can I bear to watch my kindred destroyed?"

[7] King Ahasuerus said to Queen Esther and to Mordecai the Jew, "Look — I have given Haman's estate to Esther, and they have hanged him on the gallows because he raised his hand against the Jews.

[8] Now you write whatever you want concerning the Jews, in the king's name, and seal it with the king's signet ring — because an edict written in the king's name and sealed with the king's ring cannot be revoked."

[9] So the king's scribes were summoned at that moment — the twenty-third day of the third month, the month of Sivan — and exactly what Mordecai dictated was written to the Jews, and to the satraps, governors, and officials of the provinces from India to Cush — one hundred and twenty-seven provinces — to each province in its own script, to each people in its own language, and to the Jews in their own script and language.

[10] He wrote in the name of King Ahasuerus, sealed it with the king's signet ring, and sent the letters by mounted couriers riding the royal stable's swiftest horses, bred from the king's racing stock.

[11] The king's decree granted the Jews in every city the right to gather and defend their lives — to destroy, kill, and annihilate any armed force of any people or province that attacked them, including women and children, and to plunder their goods —

[12] on a single day, throughout all the provinces of King Ahasuerus: the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

[13] A copy of the decree was to be issued as law in every province, published to all peoples, so the Jews would be ready on that day to take vengeance on their enemies.

[14] The couriers, mounted on the royal racing horses, raced out under the king's urgent command. The decree was published in Susa the citadel.

[15] Mordecai walked out from the king's presence in royal robes of blue and white, a great golden crown on his head, and a cloak of fine linen and purple. The city of Susa erupted in celebration.

[16] For the Jews, it was light and gladness, joy and honor.

[17] In every province, in every city, wherever the king's command and decree reached, the Jews had joy and gladness, feasting and a holiday. And many people of the land became Jews — because the terror of the Jews had fallen on them.

[1] The thirteenth day of the twelfth month — the month of Adar — arrived. The day the king's decree was set to take effect. The day the enemies of the Jews had been waiting for, expecting to crush them. The day flipped. The Jews crushed those who hated them.

[2] Across every province of King Xerxes' empire, the Jews gathered in their cities to strike down anyone seeking their destruction. Nobody could stand against them. Terror of them had fallen on every people.

[3] Every official in every province — the satraps, the governors, the administrators, the royal staff — they all backed the Jews. They had seen which way the wind was blowing. Mordecai had become someone to fear.

[4] Mordecai now held real power in the palace. His reputation spread through every province. The man was rising, and everyone knew it.

[5] So the Jews struck their enemies with the sword. Killed them. Destroyed them. Did with their haters whatever they wanted.

[6] In the fortress of Susa alone, they killed five hundred men.

[7] Among the dead: Parshandatha, Dalphon, Aspatha,

[8] Poratha, Adalia, Aridatha,

[9] Parmashta, Arisai, Aridai, and Vaizatha —

[10] the ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews. They killed them. But they did not touch the plunder.

[11] That same day, the count of those killed in Susa reached the king.

[12] The king said to Queen Esther, "In the fortress of Susa alone, the Jews have killed five hundred men, including Haman's ten sons. What must they have done in the rest of the provinces? Now — what do you want? It's yours. What else are you asking? Done."

[13] Esther said, "If it pleases the king, let the Jews in Susa be granted one more day to act under today's decree. And let Haman's ten sons be hung on the gallows."

[14] The king ordered it done. The decree was issued in Susa, and they hung Haman's ten sons.

[15] The Jews in Susa gathered again on the fourteenth of Adar and killed three hundred men there. But they did not touch the plunder.

[16] Meanwhile, throughout the provinces, the Jews had gathered to defend themselves and get free of their enemies. They killed seventy-five thousand of those who hated them. But they did not touch the plunder.

[17] This happened on the thirteenth of Adar. On the fourteenth they rested — and made it a day of feasting and celebration.

[18] But the Jews in Susa had gathered on the thirteenth and the fourteenth, and rested on the fifteenth — so that became their day of feasting and celebration.

[19] This is why the Jews in rural towns, the unwalled villages, celebrate the fourteenth of Adar as a day of joy, feasting, and sending gifts of food to one another.

[20] Mordecai wrote it all down. Then he sent letters to every Jew in every province of King Xerxes' empire — near and far —

[21] binding them to observe the fourteenth and fifteenth of Adar every year,

[22] as the days when the Jews got free of their enemies, the month when grief flipped to joy and mourning to celebration. They were to make these days into feasting and gladness, sending food to each other and gifts to the poor.

[23] The Jews took up what they had already begun, and what Mordecai had written to them, as a permanent practice.

[24] Because Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, enemy of all the Jews, had plotted to destroy them. He had cast the pur — that is, the lot — to crush them and wipe them out.

[25] But when the matter came before the king, he ordered in writing that the wicked scheme Haman had devised against the Jews come back on his own head. He and his sons were hung on the gallows.

[26] That is why these days are called Purim — from the word pur. Because of everything written in this letter, because of everything they had seen, because of everything that had happened to them,

[27] the Jews bound themselves, their descendants, and everyone who joined them to observe these two days without fail, every year, exactly as written, exactly on time.

[28] These days were to be remembered and kept in every generation, by every family, in every province, in every city. The days of Purim would never fade among the Jews. The memory of them would never die out among their descendants.

[29] Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, together with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter about Purim.

[30] Letters went out to all the Jews in the hundred and twenty-seven provinces of Xerxes' kingdom — words of peace and truth —

[31] to fix the days of Purim at their appointed times, exactly as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had ordained, exactly as the Jews had taken upon themselves and their descendants, including the matter of the fasts and their cries of grief.

[32] Esther's command sealed the regulations of Purim. And it was written down in the book.

[1] King Ahasuerus laid tribute on the land and on the islands of the sea. [3] Mordecai the Jew ranked second only to King Ahasuerus. He was great among the Jews, beloved by the multitude of his kin, working for the good of his people and speaking peace over every last one of them.

[16] No straw is given to us, yet they say, 'Make bricks!' Look — your servants are being beaten, but the fault is with your own people."

[17] He answered, "Lazy. That's what you are. Lazy. That's why you say, 'Let us go sacrifice to the LORD.'

[18] Get back to work. No straw will be given, and you'll still deliver your full quota of bricks."

[19] The Israelite foremen saw the trap they were in when they were told, "You will not reduce your daily brick count by a single brick."

[20] As they came out from Pharaoh's presence, they ran into Moses and Aaron waiting for them —

[22] Moses went back to the LORD and said, "Lord — why have you brought disaster on this people? Why did you ever send me?

[23] From the moment I walked in to speak in your name, Pharaoh has crushed them harder. And you — you have done nothing to rescue your people."

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Now you'll see what I'm about to do to Pharaoh. A mighty hand will drive him to release them. A mighty hand will drive him to throw them out of his land."

[3] I appeared to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob — as God Almighty. But by my name, the LORD — I did not let them know me.
