[10] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[11] "Go. Speak to Pharaoh, king of Egypt. Tell him to let the Israelites walk out of his land."

[12] Moses answered the LORD: "Look — the Israelites won't even listen to me. Why would Pharaoh? And my lips — they stumble."

[13] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron. He commissioned them concerning the Israelites and concerning Pharaoh, king of Egypt — to bring Israel out of the land of Egypt.

[14] These are the heads of their fathers' houses:

The sons of Reuben, Israel's firstborn: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, Carmi. These are the clans of Reuben.

[15] The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar — and Shaul, son of a Canaanite woman. These are the clans of Simeon.

[16] These are the names of Levi's sons by their generations: Gershon, Kohath, Merari. Levi lived 137 years.

[17] The sons of Gershon: Libni and Shimei — by their clans.

[18] The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel. Kohath lived 133 years.

[19] The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of Levi by their generations.

[20] Amram married Jochebed, his father's sister. She bore him Aaron and Moses. Amram lived 137 years.

[21] The sons of Izhar: Korah, Nepheg, Zichri.

[22] The sons of Uzziel: Mishael, Elzaphan, Sithri.

[23] Aaron married Elisheba, daughter of Amminadab, sister of Nahshon. She bore him Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar.

[24] The sons of Korah: Assir, Elkanah, Abiasaph. These are the clans of the Korahites.

[25] Eleazar, son of Aaron, married one of Putiel's daughters. She bore him Phinehas. These are the heads of the Levite fathers' houses by their clans.

[27] These are the ones who confronted Pharaoh, king of Egypt, to bring the Israelites out of Egypt. This Moses. This Aaron.

[28] On the day the LORD spoke to Moses in the land of Egypt —

[29] the LORD said to him: "I am the LORD. Tell Pharaoh, king of Egypt, everything I tell you."

[30] And Moses said before the LORD: "Look — my lips stumble. Why would Pharaoh listen to me?"

[1] The LORD said to Moses: "Look — I'm making you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet. [3] But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and though I multiply my signs and wonders across Egypt, [4] Pharaoh won't listen. [16] Say this to him: 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to tell you: Release my people so they can worship me in the wilderness. And you still haven't listened. [22] But Egypt's magicians did the same thing with their secret arts. So Pharaoh's heart stayed hard. He wouldn't listen — just as the LORD had said. [23] Pharaoh turned and walked back to his palace. He didn't take any of it to heart. [25] Seven full days passed after the LORD struck the Nile.

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: Go to Pharaoh. Tell him: This is what the LORD says—Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[7] But the magicians did the same thing with their secret arts. They brought up frogs over Egypt too.

[11] The frogs will leave you and your houses, your officials and your people. They'll remain only in the Nile.

[19] The magicians said to Pharaoh: This is the finger of God.

[28] Pharaoh said: I'll let you go. You can sacrifice to the LORD your God in the wilderness. Just don't go very far. Now pray for me.

[29] Moses said: The moment I leave you, I'll pray to the LORD. Tomorrow the flies will leave Pharaoh and his officials and his people. Only—Pharaoh must stop playing games. He must not refuse again to let the people go to sacrifice to the LORD.

[30] Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD.

[31] And the LORD did what Moses asked. He removed the flies from Pharaoh, from his officials, from his people. Not one was left.

[1] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Go to Pharaoh and tell him — this is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: Let my people go, so they can worship me.

[2] If you keep refusing — if you keep holding them —

[21] Others shrugged it off and left everything in the open.

[22] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch your hand toward the sky. Let the hail fall across all Egypt — on people, on animals, on every green thing in the fields."

[24] Hail — and fire flashing inside the hail — relentless, terrible, like nothing that nation had seen since it became a nation.

[25] Across all of Egypt the hail struck everything in the open: people, animals, crops. It shredded every tree and flattened every field.

[26] Only in Goshen, where the Israelites lived, was there no hail.

[27] Pharaoh summoned Moses and Aaron. "This time I've sinned," he said. "The LORD is in the right. I and my people are in the wrong.

[29] Moses said, "The moment I leave the city, I'll lift my hands to the LORD. The thunder will stop, the hail will end — so you will know the earth belongs to the LORD.

[30] But I know you. You and your officials still don't fear the LORD God."

[31] (The flax and the barley were destroyed — the barley was already in head, the flax in bloom.

[32] The wheat and the spelt weren't ruined; they ripen later.)

[33] Moses left Pharaoh and the city, lifted his hands to the LORD, and the thunder and hail stopped. The rain stopped pouring down.

[35] Pharaoh's heart was a stone. He would not let the Israelites go — exactly as the LORD had said through Moses.

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Moses: "Go to Pharaoh. I have made his heart heavy, and the hearts of his officials too, so I can plant these signs of mine right in front of him.

[18] Moses left Pharaoh's court and prayed to the LORD.

[15] When Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, the LORD killed every firstborn in Egypt — from human firstborn to animal firstborn. That's why I sacrifice to the LORD every male that first opens the womb, and redeem every firstborn of my sons.'

[16] It will be like a sign on your hand, like a band between your eyes — because the LORD's strong hand brought us out of Egypt."

[17] When Pharaoh finally let the people go, God did not lead them along the Philistine road, even though it was the short route. God thought: "If they face war, they'll change their minds and run back to Egypt."

[18] So God took them the long way — through the wilderness, toward the Red Sea. Israel marched out of Egypt in formation, ready for battle.

[19] Moses carried Joseph's bones with him. Joseph had made the Israelites swear an oath: "When God comes to you — and he will come — carry my bones with you out of here."

[20] They left Succoth and camped at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness.

[21] The LORD went ahead of them — by day in a pillar of cloud to lead the way, by night in a pillar of fire to give them light. They could travel day or night.

[22] The pillar of cloud by day, the pillar of fire by night — never left its place in front of the people.

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites to turn back and pitch camp in front of Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea. Camp right by the water, opposite Baal-zephon.

[3] Pharaoh will look at this and say, 'The Israelites are lost. The wilderness has swallowed them.'

[4] I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and he will chase them. Then I will get my glory through Pharaoh and his entire army — and Egypt will know that I am the LORD."

So they did exactly that.

[5] When word reached the king of Egypt that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his officials flipped. "What have we done, letting Israel walk out on us? We've lost our entire labor force!"

[6] So Pharaoh harnessed his chariot and rallied his army.

[7] He took six hundred elite chariots and every other chariot in Egypt — each one with an officer commanding.

[8] The LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, king of Egypt, and he gave chase. The Israelites had marched out bold and unafraid,

[9] but now the Egyptians came after them — every horse, every chariot, every horseman, the whole army — and caught up to them camped by the sea, near Pi-hahiroth, opposite Baal-zephon.

[10] Pharaoh closed in. The Israelites looked up — and there came Egypt, marching down on them. Terror seized them. They cried out to the LORD.

[11] Then they turned on Moses: "Were there no graves in Egypt? Did you have to drag us into the wilderness to die? What have you done to us, hauling us out of Egypt?

[12] Is this not exactly what we told you back there? 'Leave us alone. Let us serve the Egyptians.' Better to slave for Egypt than die in this wasteland!"

[13] Moses answered the people: "Stop being afraid. Stand your ground. Watch the LORD rescue you today. These Egyptians you see right now — you will never see them again, ever.

[14] The LORD will fight for you. All you have to do is be still."

[15] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Why are you crying out to me? Tell the Israelites to move forward.

[16] Lift your staff. Stretch your hand over the sea and split it open. The Israelites will walk through the sea on dry ground.

[17] And I — I am hardening the hearts of the Egyptians so they go in after them. I will get my glory through Pharaoh, his entire army, his chariots, his horsemen.

[18] Egypt will know I am the LORD when I get my glory through Pharaoh, his chariots, his horsemen."

[19] The angel of God, who had been leading the Israelite camp, moved and took position behind them. The pillar of cloud shifted from in front and stood at their rear,

[20] settling between the Egyptian camp and the Israelite camp. To one side it was darkness. To the other it gave light through the night. Neither side could reach the other all night long.

[21] Then Moses stretched his hand over the sea, and the LORD drove the waters back with a fierce east wind that blew all night. He turned the sea into dry ground. The waters split.

[22] The Israelites walked through the middle of the sea on dry ground — a wall of water on their right, a wall of water on their left.

[23] The Egyptians came after them — every one of Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen — straight into the middle of the sea.

[24] Just before dawn, the LORD looked down on the Egyptian army from the pillar of fire and cloud and threw them into chaos.

[25] He jammed their chariot wheels so they could barely move. The Egyptians screamed, "Get out of here! The LORD is fighting for Israel against Egypt!"

[26] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Stretch your hand over the sea. Let the waters come crashing back onto the Egyptians — their chariots, their horsemen."

[27] Moses stretched his hand over the sea. At daybreak the sea snapped back to its place, and the Egyptians ran straight into it. The LORD swept them into the heart of the sea.

[28] The waters returned, covering the chariots and the horsemen — Pharaoh's entire army that had charged into the sea after Israel. Not one survived.

[29] But the Israelites walked through on dry ground in the middle of the sea, the waters a wall to their right and to their left.

[30] That day the LORD rescued Israel from Egypt's grip. Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the shore.

[31] Israel saw the mighty hand the LORD had brought against Egypt — and the people feared the LORD, and they believed in the LORD and in his servant Moses.

[1] Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to the LORD:

I will sing to the LORD — he has risen up in glory!
Horse and rider — he hurled them into the sea.

[2] The LORD is my strength, my song, my salvation.
This is my God — I will praise him.
My father's God — I will lift him high.

[3] The LORD is a warrior.
The LORD is his name.

[4] Pharaoh's chariots, Pharaoh's army — he flung them into the sea.
The pick of his officers drowned in the Red Sea.

[5] The deep swallowed them.
They sank to the bottom like a stone.

[6] Your right hand, LORD — majestic in power.
Your right hand, LORD — it shatters the enemy.

[7] With towering greatness you broke those who rose against you.
You loosed your fury — it burned them up like straw.

[8] One breath from your nostrils and the waters stacked themselves up,
the currents stood in walls,
the deep froze solid in the heart of the sea.

[9] The enemy boasted: I'll chase. I'll catch. I'll divide the spoil.
My appetite will get its fill of them.
I'll draw my sword. My hand will finish them.

[10] You blew with your breath — the sea covered them.
They sank like lead in the surging waters.

[11] Who is like you among the gods, LORD?
Who is like you — majestic in holiness,
terrible in praises, working wonders?

[12] You stretched out your right hand —
the earth swallowed them whole.

[13] In your steadfast love you led the people you redeemed.
In your strength you guided them to your holy home.

[14] The nations heard. They trembled.
Anguish gripped the inhabitants of Philistia.

[15] The chiefs of Edom panicked.
The leaders of Moab — trembling seized them.
The people of Canaan melted away.

[16] Terror and dread fell on them.
By the greatness of your arm they froze like stone —
until your people passed by, LORD,
until the people you ransomed passed by.

[17] You will bring them in and plant them on your own mountain —
the place, LORD, you made for your dwelling,
the sanctuary, Lord, your hands established.

[18] The LORD will reign — forever and ever.

[19] When Pharaoh's horses and chariots and horsemen plunged into the sea, the LORD brought the waters of the sea crashing back over them. But the Israelites walked through on dry ground in the middle of the sea.

[20] Then Miriam the prophet, Aaron's sister, picked up a tambourine, and all the women followed her out — tambourines in their hands, dancing.

[21] Miriam sang back to them:

Sing to the LORD — he has risen up in glory!
Horse and rider — he hurled them into the sea.

[22] Moses led Israel on from the Red Sea, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur. Three days they walked in that wilderness and found no water.

[23] They reached Marah, but they couldn't drink the water there — it was bitter. (That's why the place is called Marah — "Bitter.")

[24] The people turned on Moses: What are we supposed to drink?

[25] Moses cried out to the LORD, and the LORD showed him a piece of wood. He threw it into the water, and the water turned sweet.

There the LORD gave them a statute and a rule, and there he tested them.

[26] He said: If you'll really listen to the voice of the LORD your God, do what is right in his eyes, pay attention to his commands and keep his statutes — then none of the diseases I unleashed on Egypt will I bring on you. I am the LORD who heals you.

[27] Then they came to Elim — twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees. They camped there beside the water.

[1] They pulled out from Elim, and the whole community of Israel arrived at the wilderness of Sin, the stretch between Elim and Sinai, on the fifteenth day of the second month after leaving Egypt.

[2] Out there in the wilderness, the whole community came at Moses and Aaron with bitter complaints.

[3] "If only the LORD had killed us in Egypt," they said. "At least there we sat by pots of meat and ate bread until we were full. You dragged us into this wilderness to starve every last one of us to death."

[4] The LORD said to Moses, "I'm about to rain bread down from the sky for you. The people will go out each day and gather what they need for that day. This is how I'll test them — to see if they'll walk in my instruction or not.

[5] On the sixth day, when they prepare what they've brought in, it will turn out to be twice what they gather on the other days."

[6] So Moses and Aaron told all Israel, "By evening you will know it was the LORD who brought you out of Egypt,

[7] and in the morning you will see the glory of the LORD — because he has heard your complaints against him. Who are we, that you complain against us?"

[8] Then Moses said, "The LORD will give you meat to eat in the evening and bread to fill you in the morning, because he has heard the complaints you're hurling at him. Who are we? Your complaints aren't against us — they're against the LORD."

[9] Moses said to Aaron, "Tell the whole community of Israel: Come close to the LORD, because he has heard your complaints."

[10] As Aaron was still speaking to the assembled people of Israel, they turned toward the wilderness — and there it was: the glory of the LORD, blazing in the cloud.

[11] The LORD said to Moses,

[12] "I have heard the complaints of the Israelites. Tell them this: At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will have your fill of bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God."

[13] That evening quail came up and blanketed the camp. In the morning, a layer of dew lay all around it.

[14] When the dew burned off, there on the desert floor lay something fine and flaky — fine as frost on the ground.

[15] When the Israelites saw it, they turned to each other and said, "What is that?" — because they had no idea what it was. Moses told them, "It's the bread the LORD has given you to eat.

[16] Here's what the LORD commands: Gather as much as each of you needs to eat — about two quarts per person, based on how many are in your tent."

[17] The Israelites did it. Some gathered more, some less.

[18] But when they measured it out, the one who gathered much had nothing extra, and the one who gathered little had no shortage. Each had gathered exactly what he needed to eat.

[19] Moses told them, "Don't keep any of it until morning."

[20] But some didn't listen to Moses. They kept part of it until morning — and it bred worms and stank. Moses was furious with them.

[21] Every morning they gathered it, each according to his appetite. When the sun grew hot, it melted away.

[22] On the sixth day they gathered twice the bread — four quarts per person — and all the leaders of the community came and reported it to Moses.

[23] He said to them, "This is what the LORD said: Tomorrow is a day of complete rest, a holy sabbath to the LORD. Bake what you want to bake, boil what you want to boil, and whatever is left over, set aside and keep until morning."

[24] So they kept it until morning, as Moses had ordered, and it did not stink — no worms in it.

[25] Moses said, "Eat it today, because today is a sabbath to the LORD. You won't find any out in the field today.

[26] Six days you gather it, but the seventh day — the sabbath — there will be none."

[27] Even so, some of the people went out on the seventh day to gather, and found nothing.

[28] The LORD said to Moses, "How long will you people refuse to keep my commands and my instructions?

[29] Look — the LORD has given you the sabbath. That's why on the sixth day he gives you bread for two. Every one of you, stay where you are on the seventh day. No one is to leave his place."

[30] So the people rested on the seventh day.

[31] Israel called the bread manna. It was white like coriander seed, and it tasted like wafers made with honey.

[32] Moses said, "This is what the LORD commands: Take two quarts of it and keep it for the generations to come, so they can see the bread I fed you in the wilderness when I brought you out of Egypt."

[33] So Moses said to Aaron, "Take a jar, put two quarts of manna in it, and set it before the LORD to be kept for the generations to come."

[34] As the LORD commanded Moses, Aaron set it in front of the covenant tablets to be preserved.

[35] The Israelites ate manna for forty years, until they reached a land where people lived. They ate manna until they came to the edge of Canaan.

[36] (Two quarts — that's a tenth of an ephah.)

[1] The whole community of Israel pulled out of the Sin Wilderness, moving stage by stage as the LORD directed, and pitched camp at Rephidim. But there was no water. None for the people to drink.

[2] So the people came at Moses, swinging: "Give us water. We're thirsty." Moses fired back, "Why are you coming at me? Why are you testing the LORD?"

[3] But thirst gnawed at them, and the people turned on Moses, grumbling: "Why did you drag us out of Egypt? To kill us — us and our children and our livestock — with thirst?"

[4] Moses cried out to the LORD: "What am I supposed to do with these people? Any minute now they'll stone me."

[5] The LORD answered Moses: "Walk out in front of the people. Take some of the elders of Israel with you. Take the staff — the one you struck the Nile with — and go.

[6] I'll be standing there in front of you, on the rock at Horeb. Strike the rock. Water will pour out of it. The people will drink." Moses did exactly that, with the elders of Israel watching.

[7] He named the place Massah and Meribah — Test and Quarrel — because the Israelites had picked a fight with him and put the LORD on trial, asking, "Is the LORD actually with us, or not?"

[8] Then Amalek came. They attacked Israel at Rephidim.

[9] Moses said to Joshua, "Pick our fighters. Go out tomorrow and engage Amalek. I'll be on top of the hill with the staff of God in my hand."

[10] Joshua did as Moses ordered and engaged Amalek, while Moses, Aaron, and Hur climbed the hill.

[11] Here's what happened: as long as Moses held his hand up, Israel was winning. The moment he let it drop, Amalek surged.

[12] Moses' arms grew heavy. So they rolled a stone under him, sat him on it, and Aaron and Hur held his hands up — one on each side — and his hands stayed steady until the sun went down.

[13] And Joshua cut down Amalek and his army with the edge of the sword.

[14] The LORD said to Moses: "Write this in a book as a record, and drill it into Joshua's ears: I will wipe the memory of Amalek clean off the face of the earth."

[15] Moses built an altar there and named it: The LORD Is My Banner.

[16] He said, "A hand has been raised against the throne of the LORD. The LORD is at war with Amalek — generation after generation."

[1] Word reached Jethro, priest of Midian and Moses' father-in-law, about everything God had done for Moses and for Israel his people — how the LORD had marched Israel out of Egypt.

[2] So Jethro took Zipporah, Moses' wife, who had been sent back home,

[3] along with her two sons. One was named Gershom — "I've been a stranger in foreign country" —

[4] and the other Eliezer — "My father's God came through for me; he saved me from Pharaoh's sword."

[5] Jethro came out to Moses in the wilderness, bringing Moses' wife and sons, to the place where Moses was camped at the mountain of God.

[6] He sent word ahead: "I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you — and your wife and her two sons are with me."

[7] Moses went out to meet him, bowed low, kissed him, and they greeted each other warmly before going into the tent.

[8] Then Moses told it all — everything the LORD had done to Pharaoh and Egypt for Israel's sake, every hardship along the road, and every time the LORD had pulled them through.

[9] Jethro was overjoyed at all the good the LORD had done for Israel, rescuing them from Egypt's grip.

[10] "Blessed be the LORD," Jethro said, "who tore you free from Egypt and Pharaoh — who tore this people out from under Egypt's hand.

[11] Now I know it for certain: the LORD towers above every god. In the very thing the Egyptians used to crush them, he crushed the Egyptians."

[12] Then Jethro offered a burnt offering and sacrifices to God, and Aaron came with all the elders of Israel to share a meal with Moses' father-in-law in the presence of God.

[13] The next day Moses sat to judge the people, and they stood around him from morning till evening.

[14] When Jethro saw what Moses was doing for the people, he said, "What is this you're doing? Why are you sitting there alone with everyone standing around you from dawn to dusk?"

[15] Moses answered, "Because the people come to me to inquire of God.

[16] When they have a dispute, they bring it to me. I judge between them and teach them God's statutes and instructions."

[17] His father-in-law said, "This isn't good — what you're doing.

[18] You'll wear yourself out, and these people too. The load is too heavy. You can't carry it alone.

[19] Listen to me — I'll give you counsel, and may God be with you. You stand for the people before God, bringing their cases to him.

[20] Teach them the statutes and instructions. Show them the road to walk and the work to do.

[21] But find capable men from among the people — God-fearing men, trustworthy men who refuse bribes. Set them over the people as leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

[22] Let them judge the people at all times. The hard cases they bring to you; the small ones they handle themselves. Lighten your load. Let them carry it with you.

[23] If you do this — and God commands it — you'll be able to endure, and all these people will go home in peace."

[24] Moses listened to his father-in-law and did everything he said.

[25] He chose capable men out of all Israel and put them in charge — leaders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens.

[26] They judged the people at all times. The hard cases they brought to Moses; the small ones they decided themselves.

[27] Then Moses sent his father-in-law on his way, and Jethro went home to his own country.

[1] Three months to the day after Israel walked out of Egypt, they arrived at the wilderness of Sinai.

[2] They had broken camp at Rephidim, entered the Sinai wilderness, and pitched there in the open country beneath the mountain. Israel camped, and there before the mountain they stayed.

[3] Moses climbed up to God. And the LORD called down to him from the mountain: "Tell the house of Jacob this. Speak it to the children of Israel.

[4] You saw what I did to Egypt. You saw how I carried you on eagles' wings and brought you here to myself.

[5] Now — if you will truly listen to my voice and hold fast to my covenant, you will be my treasured possession out of all peoples. The whole earth is mine,

[6] but you — you will be my kingdom of priests, a holy nation. These are the words. Speak them to Israel."

[7] Moses came down, called the elders of the people together, and laid out before them everything the LORD had commanded.

[8] And the people answered as one voice: "Everything the LORD has spoken — we will do it." Moses carried their words back up to the LORD.

[9] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Listen — I am coming to you in a dense cloud, so the people will hear me speaking with you and trust you from this day forward." And Moses reported the people's words to the LORD.

[10] The LORD said to Moses, "Go down to the people. Consecrate them today and tomorrow. Have them wash their clothes

[11] and be ready for the third day — because on the third day the LORD is coming down on Mount Sinai before the eyes of all the people.

[12] Mark off a boundary around the mountain. Tell them: 'Do not climb it. Do not even touch its edge. Anyone who touches the mountain dies.

[13] No hand shall touch them — they are to be stoned or shot through. Animal or human, they will not live.' Only when the ram's horn blasts a long note may they come up the mountain."

[14] So Moses came down from the mountain to the people, consecrated them, and they washed their clothes.

[15] He said to them, "Be ready for the third day. Do not go near a woman."

[16] When the third day dawned, thunder cracked the sky and lightning split it open. A heavy cloud settled on the mountain and a shofar blast tore through the camp — louder, louder — until everyone there trembled.

[17] Moses led the people out of the camp to meet God, and they stood at the foot of the mountain.

[18] Mount Sinai was wrapped in smoke from end to end, because the LORD had come down on it in fire. Smoke billowed up like smoke from a furnace, and the whole mountain shook violently.

[19] The shofar grew louder and louder. Moses spoke, and God answered him in thunder.

[20] The LORD came down on Mount Sinai, onto the summit, and called Moses up to the top. Moses went up.

[21] The LORD said, "Go down. Warn the people. They must not break through to look at the LORD, or many of them will fall.

[22] Even the priests who approach the LORD must consecrate themselves, or the LORD will break out against them."

[23] Moses said to the LORD, "The people cannot come up Sinai. You yourself warned us — 'Set the boundary around the mountain. Make it holy.'"

[24] The LORD answered, "Go. Go down. Then come back up — you and Aaron with you. But the priests and the people must not break through to come up to the LORD, or he will break out against them."

[25] So Moses went down to the people and spoke to them.

[1] Then God spoke. All these words:

[2] I am the Lord your God. I am the one who pulled you out of Egypt, out of the house where you were slaves.

[3] No other gods. Not in my presence.

[4] Don't carve idols for yourself. No image of anything — nothing up in the sky, nothing down on the earth, nothing in the waters below.

[5] Don't bow to them. Don't serve them. Because I, the Lord your God, am a jealous God. I track the guilt of fathers down through their children — to the third generation, to the fourth — when they reject me.

[6] But I pour out loyal love to the thousandth generation when they love me and keep my commands.

[7] Don't drag my name through the mud. The Lord will not let anyone walk free who treats his name like it's nothing.

[8] Remember the Sabbath day. Keep it holy.

[9] Six days you work. Six days you do everything you have to do.

[10] But the seventh day belongs to the Lord your God. No work. Not you, not your son, not your daughter, not your servants, not your animals, not the foreigner staying inside your gates.

[11] Because in six days the Lord made the heavens, the earth, the sea, everything in them — and on the seventh day he rested. That's why the Lord blessed the Sabbath day and set it apart as holy.

[12] Honor your father and your mother. So your days will stretch long in the land the Lord your God is giving you.

[13] Don't murder.

[14] Don't commit adultery.

[15] Don't steal.

[16] Don't lie about your neighbor in court.

[17] Don't crave your neighbor's house. Don't crave your neighbor's wife, his servant, his maid, his ox, his donkey — anything that belongs to him.

[18] The whole crowd was watching it — thunder cracking, lightning flashing, the trumpet screaming, the mountain smoking. They saw it, and they shook. They stood far off.

[19] They said to Moses, "You talk to us. We'll listen. But don't let God speak to us, or we'll die."

[20] Moses answered, "Don't be afraid. God has come to test you — to put the fear of him in your faces so you won't sin."

[21] The people kept their distance. Moses walked into the thick darkness where God was.

[22] The Lord said to Moses, "Tell the Israelites this: You saw for yourselves that I spoke to you from heaven.

[23] Don't make gods alongside me. No silver gods. No gold gods. Don't make them.

[24] Build me an altar of earth. Sacrifice your burnt offerings and peace offerings on it — your sheep, your cattle. Wherever I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and bless you.

[25] If you build me a stone altar, don't shape the stones with tools. The moment you swing a chisel at them, you've defiled them.

[26] And don't climb steps up to my altar — I won't have your nakedness exposed over it."

[1] These are the rulings you will set before them.

[2] When you buy a Hebrew slave, he serves six years. In the seventh, he walks free — no payment owed.

[3] If he came alone, he leaves alone. If he came with a wife, she walks out with him.

[4] But if his master gave him a wife and she bore him sons or daughters, the woman and her children belong to the master. He leaves alone.

[5] But if the slave declares plainly, "I love my master, my wife, my children — I will not go free,"

[6] then his master brings him before God, stations him at the door or the doorpost, and pierces his ear with an awl. He serves him for life.

[7] When a man sells his daughter as a servant, she does not go free the way the men do.

[8] If she fails to please the master who designated her for himself, he must let her be redeemed. He has no right to sell her to outsiders — he broke faith with her.

[9] If he designated her for his son, he must treat her as a daughter.

[10] If he takes another wife, he must not cut back her food, her clothing, or her marital rights.

[11] If he denies her these three, she walks out free — no payment, no silver.

[12] Whoever strikes a man dead is put to death.

[13] But if it was not premeditated — if God let it happen by his hand — I will appoint a place where he can flee.

[14] But if a man schemes against his neighbor and kills him with cunning, drag him even from my altar. He dies.

[15] Whoever strikes his father or mother is put to death.

[16] Whoever kidnaps a man — whether he has sold him or still holds him — is put to death.

[17] Whoever curses his father or mother is put to death.

[18] If men fight and one strikes the other with a stone or with his fist, and the man does not die but is laid up in bed,

[19] then if he gets back up and walks around outside on his cane, the one who struck him goes free — but he pays for the lost time and sees him fully healed.

[20] If a man strikes his male or female slave with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he must be punished.

[21] But if the slave survives a day or two, there is no punishment — the slave is his property.

[22] If men brawling strike a pregnant woman and her child comes out, but no further harm follows, the offender must pay whatever the husband demands, as the judges assess.

[23] But if there is harm, then you give life for life,

[24] eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot,

[25] burn for burn, wound for wound, bruise for bruise.

[26] If a man strikes the eye of his slave — male or female — and destroys it, he sets the slave free in exchange for the eye.

[27] If he knocks out the tooth of his slave, he sets the slave free in exchange for the tooth.

[28] If an ox gores a man or woman to death, the ox is stoned and its flesh not eaten. The owner is clear.

[29] But if the ox had a history of goring and the owner was warned and did nothing, and it killed a man or woman, the ox is stoned and the owner dies too.

[30] If a ransom is set on him, he pays whatever is demanded to redeem his life.

[31] The same ruling applies whether the ox gores a son or a daughter.

[32] If the ox gores a slave — male or female — the owner pays the master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox is stoned.

[33] If a man uncovers a pit, or digs one and leaves it open, and an ox or donkey falls in,

[34] the owner of the pit pays. He gives silver to the animal's owner, and the dead beast is his.

[35] If one man's ox injures another's so it dies, they sell the living ox and split the silver, and they split the carcass too.

[36] But if the ox was known to gore and the owner did not pen it in, he repays ox for ox — and the dead one is his.

[1] If a man steals an ox or a sheep and slaughters it or sells it, he pays back five oxen for the ox, four sheep for the sheep.

[2] If a thief is caught breaking in at night and gets struck down, no bloodguilt falls on the one who killed him.

[3] But if it happens after sunrise, bloodguilt stands. The thief must make full restitution. If he has nothing, he is sold to cover what he stole.

[4] If the stolen animal is found alive in his possession — ox, donkey, or sheep — he pays back double.

[5] If a man lets his livestock graze loose and they ravage another man's field or vineyard, he repays from the best of his own field and vineyard.

[6] If fire breaks out, catches in thornbushes, and consumes stacked grain or standing grain or a whole field, the one who started the blaze pays in full.

[7] If a man entrusts his neighbor with silver or goods for safekeeping, and they are stolen from the neighbor's house — if the thief is caught, the thief pays double.

[8] If the thief is not found, the homeowner is brought before God to determine whether he laid hands on his neighbor's property.

[9] In every dispute over ownership — ox, donkey, sheep, clothing, anything missing that someone claims as theirs — both parties come before God. The one God condemns pays back double to his neighbor.

[10] If a man entrusts his neighbor with a donkey, ox, sheep, or any animal to keep, and it dies or is injured or carried off with no witness,

[11] an oath before the LORD settles it between them: the keeper swears he did not touch the other man's property. The owner accepts the oath. No restitution.

[12] But if it was stolen from him, he repays the owner.

[13] If it was torn apart by a predator, let him bring the carcass as evidence — he owes nothing for what was savaged.

[14] If a man borrows an animal from his neighbor and it is injured or dies while the owner is absent, he must pay in full.

[15] If the owner was present, no payment. If it was hired, the fee covers it.

[16] If a man seduces a virgin who is not engaged and sleeps with her, he must pay the bride-price and take her as his wife.

[17] If her father absolutely refuses to give her to him, he still pays silver equal to the bride-price of a virgin.

[18] Do not let a sorceress live.

[19] Anyone who has sex with an animal is to be put to death.

[20] Anyone who sacrifices to any god but the LORD alone is to be wiped out under the ban.

[21] Do not mistreat a foreigner. Do not crush him. You were foreigners in Egypt — remember it.

[22] Do not exploit any widow or orphan.

[23] If you do — if they cry out to me, I will hear them. I will hear.

[24] My anger will burn. I will cut you down with the sword. Your wives will become widows. Your children, orphans.

[25] If you lend silver to one of my people, to the poor among you, do not act like a creditor. Do not pile interest on him.

[26] If you take your neighbor's cloak as collateral, return it before the sun goes down.

[27] It is his only covering — the cloak wrapped around his skin. What else will he sleep in? When he cries out to me, I will listen. I am compassionate.

[28] Do not curse God. Do not curse the leader of your people.

[29] Do not hold back the fullness of your harvest or the overflow of your presses. Give me the firstborn of your sons.

[30] Do the same with your oxen and sheep — seven days with its mother, on the eighth day give it to me.

[31] You are to be people set apart for me. Do not eat meat torn by predators in the field. Throw it to the dogs.

[1] Don't spread a false report. Don't lock arms with the wicked by becoming a lying witness.

[2] Don't follow the crowd into evil. Don't shade your testimony in a lawsuit to side with the majority and twist what's right.

[3] And don't tilt toward a poor man in his case just because he's poor.

[4] If you come across your enemy's ox or donkey wandering loose, take it back to him.

[5] If you see the donkey of someone who hates you collapsed under its load, don't walk away. Help him lift it.

[6] Don't bend justice against the poor when they stand before the court.

[7] Stay far from any false charge. Don't kill the innocent or the righteous — I will not acquit the guilty.

[8] Don't take a bribe. Bribes blind those who see clearly and twist the words of the honest.

[9] Don't crush the foreigner. You know what it's like to be one — you were foreigners in Egypt.

[10] For six years plant your land and gather what it produces.

[11] But in the seventh, let it rest. Leave it alone. The poor among you can eat from it, and what they leave the wild animals can have. Do the same with your vineyard and your olive grove.

[12] Six days do your work, but on the seventh stop — so your ox and donkey can rest, and the son of your servant and the foreigner can catch their breath.

[13] Pay attention to everything I've told you. Don't speak the names of other gods — don't let them cross your lips.

[14] Three times a year hold a festival for me.

[15] Keep the Festival of Unleavened Bread. For seven days eat bread without yeast, as I commanded you, at the appointed time in the month of Abib — for that's when you came out of Egypt. And no one shows up before me empty-handed.

[16] Then the Festival of Harvest, the firstfruits of what you planted in the field. And the Festival of Ingathering at the end of the year, when you bring in what the field has yielded.

[17] Three times a year every male among you appears before the Sovereign Lord.

[18] Don't offer the blood of my sacrifice along with anything leavened. And don't let the fat of my festival sit until morning.

[19] Bring the best of the firstfruits of your soil into the house of the Lord your God. Don't boil a young goat in its mother's milk.

[20] Watch this: I'm sending a messenger ahead of you to guard you on the road and bring you to the place I've prepared.

[21] Pay attention to him. Listen to his voice. Don't defy him — he won't pardon your rebellion, because my Name is in him.

[22] But if you really listen to his voice and do everything I say, I will be the enemy of your enemies and the adversary of those who oppose you.

[23] My messenger will go ahead of you and bring you into the land of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hivites, and Jebusites — and I will wipe them out.

[24] Don't bow down to their gods. Don't worship them. Don't imitate what they do. Tear their gods down. Smash their sacred pillars to pieces.

[25] Serve the Lord your God, and he will bless your bread and your water. I will lift sickness out of your midst.

[26] No woman in your land will miscarry. None will be barren. I will give you a full count of days.

[27] I will send my terror ahead of you. I will throw every nation you face into chaos. Every one of your enemies will turn their back and run.

[28] I will send the hornet ahead of you to drive out the Hivites, Canaanites, and Hittites from your path.

[29] But I won't drive them out in a single year — the land would go wild, and the beasts would multiply against you.

[30] Little by little I'll push them out, until you've grown enough to take possession of the land.

[31] I will set your borders from the Red Sea to the Sea of the Philistines, from the wilderness to the River. I will hand over those who live in the land, and you will drive them out from before you.

[32] Make no covenant with them or their gods.

[33] They will not live in your land — or they'll trip you into sinning against me. If you worship their gods, it will be a snare around your neck.

[1] Then he said to Moses: "Climb up to the LORD — you, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and seventy of Israel's elders. Worship from a distance.

[2] Moses alone draws near to the LORD. The others stay back. The people do not come up with him at all."

[3] Moses came down and laid out before the people every word the LORD had spoken, every ruling. And the people answered with one voice: "Everything the LORD has said — we will do it."

[4] So Moses wrote it all down — every word from the LORD. He rose early the next morning and built an altar at the foot of the mountain, with twelve standing stones for the twelve tribes of Israel.

[5] He sent young men of Israel to offer burnt offerings and to sacrifice bulls as peace offerings to the LORD.

[6] Moses took half the blood and poured it into basins. The other half he splashed against the altar.

[7] Then he picked up the scroll of the covenant and read it aloud in the hearing of the people. And they said: "Everything the LORD has spoken — we will do it. We will listen."

[8] Then Moses took the blood, threw it over the people, and said: "This is the blood of the covenant the LORD has cut with you, sealed by every one of these words."

[9] Then Moses went up — Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and the seventy elders of Israel with him.

[10] And they saw the God of Israel. Beneath his feet was something like a pavement of sapphire, clear as the sky itself.

[11] But against the leaders of Israel he did not lift a hand. They saw God — and they ate, and they drank.

[12] Then the LORD said to Moses: "Come up to me on the mountain. Stay there. I will give you the stone tablets — the law, the commandment — what I have written to teach them."

[13] So Moses rose, with Joshua his assistant, and Moses climbed the mountain of God.

[14] To the elders he said: "Wait here for us until we return. Aaron and Hur are with you. Anyone with a dispute — go to them."

[15] Moses climbed the mountain, and the cloud swallowed it.

[16] The glory of the LORD settled on Mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days. On the seventh day he called to Moses out of the cloud.

[17] To the eyes of Israel, the glory of the LORD looked like a consuming fire on the mountaintop.

[18] Moses walked into the cloud and kept climbing. He stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites to bring me an offering. Take it from anyone whose heart moves them to give.

[3] Here is what you'll receive: gold, silver, bronze;

[4] blue, purple, and crimson yarn; fine linen; goat hair;

[5] ram skins dyed red; fine leather; acacia wood;

[6] oil for the lamp; spices for the anointing oil and the fragrant incense;

[7] onyx stones and other gems to set into the ephod and the breastpiece.

[8] Have them build me a sanctuary, and I will live among them.

[9] Build it exactly to the pattern I'm about to show you — the dwelling and every piece of its furniture, down to the last detail.

[10] Start with a chest of acacia wood — three and three-quarter feet long, two and a quarter wide, two and a quarter high.

[11] Overlay it with pure gold, inside and out, and run a gold molding around the top.

[12] Cast four gold rings and fasten them to its four feet — two rings on one side, two on the other.

[13] Make poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold.

[14] Slide the poles through the rings on the sides of the chest so it can be carried.

[15] The poles stay in the rings. They are never removed.

[16] Inside the chest, place the covenant — the testimony I will give you.

[17] Now the cover: pure gold, three and three-quarter feet long, two and a quarter wide. This is the mercy seat — the place of atonement.

[18] At each end of the cover, hammer out two cherubim of gold — one solid piece with the cover itself.

[19] One cherub at this end, one at that end, rising directly out of the mercy seat.

[20] Their wings spread upward, sheltering the cover. They face each other, their gaze fixed on the mercy seat between them.

[21] Set the cover on top of the chest, and place inside the chest the testimony I will give you.

[22] There I will meet you. From above the mercy seat, between the two cherubim over the ark of the covenant, I will speak with you and give you everything I command for the Israelites.

[23] Now make a table of acacia wood — three feet long, eighteen inches wide, two and a quarter feet high.

[24] Overlay it with pure gold and run a gold molding around it.

[25] Frame it with a rim a hand's breadth wide, and edge that rim with gold molding too.

[26] Cast four gold rings and attach them at the four corners, where the legs meet the table.

[27] The rings sit close to the rim, holding the poles that carry the table.

[28] The poles themselves: acacia wood, overlaid with gold.

[29] Make the table's vessels — plates, dishes, pitchers, and bowls for pouring out the drink offerings — all of pure gold.

[30] On this table, always, set the bread of the Presence. It stays before me — without fail.

[31] Now the lampstand: pure gold, hammered out of a single piece. Base, shaft, cups, buds, and blossoms — all one continuous work.

[32] Six branches extend from its sides — three on one side, three on the other.

[33] Each branch carries three almond-blossom cups, each with bud and petal. Six branches, all the same.

[34] The central shaft itself bears four almond-blossom cups, with their buds and petals,

[35] and a bud sits beneath each pair of branches where they meet the shaft — three buds for the six branches.

[36] The buds and the branches are all one piece — one seamless hammered work of pure gold.

[37] Make seven lamps for it. Mount them so their light shines forward.

[38] Its wick trimmers and trays: pure gold.

[39] Use a full talent of pure gold — seventy-five pounds — for the lampstand and all its tools.

[40] And look — look carefully — build everything according to the pattern shown to you on the mountain."

[1] Build the Dwelling from ten panels of finely twisted linen — blue, purple, and crimson — with cherubim woven into them by a master's hand.

[2] Each panel: twenty-eight cubits long, four cubits wide. All ten the same measure.

[3] Join five panels into one set, and the other five into another.

[4] On the edge of the outermost panel of the first set, make loops of blue. Do the same on the outermost panel of the second.

[5] Fifty loops on one panel, fifty on the other, the loops lining up exactly.

[6] Then forge fifty gold clasps and use them to bind the two sets together — and the Dwelling becomes one.

[7] Over that, stretch a tent of goat hair: eleven panels.

[8] Each one thirty cubits long, four wide. All eleven matched.

[9] Join five together, then six together — and fold the sixth panel double across the front of the tent.

[10] Fifty loops on the edge of the last panel of the first set, fifty on the last panel of the second.

[11] Make fifty bronze clasps, hook them through the loops, and pull the tent into a single piece.

[12] The extra half-panel of the goat-hair covering will hang down over the back of the Dwelling.

[13] The extra cubit on each side will drape down its flanks, covering it completely.

[14] Over that tent, two more coverings: rams' skins dyed red, and over those, fine leather.

[15] For the framework, stand up acacia-wood boards.

[16] Each board ten cubits tall, a cubit and a half wide,

[17] with two pegs at the base of each — uniform across every board.

[18] Twenty boards for the south side,

[19] and forty silver sockets beneath them — two sockets under each board, locking onto its two pegs.

[20] Twenty boards for the north side as well,

[21] with their forty silver sockets, two under each board.

[22] For the back wall — the west — six boards,

[23] plus two corner boards to brace the rear corners.

[24] These corner boards are doubled from base to top, joined at a single ring — that is how both corners are built.

[25] So eight boards in all on the back, with sixteen silver sockets — two under each.

[26] Now make crossbars of acacia wood: five for one side of the Dwelling,

[27] five for the other, five for the back wall on the west.

[28] The middle crossbar runs the full length, threading through the boards from end to end.

[29] Sheath the boards in gold, forge gold rings to hold the crossbars, and overlay the crossbars in gold too.

[30] Raise the Dwelling exactly to the pattern you were shown on the mountain.

[31] Now: a veil — blue, purple, crimson, finely twisted linen, with cherubim worked into it by a master.

[32] Hang it on four acacia pillars sheathed in gold, with golden hooks, set into four silver sockets.

[33] Hang the veil from the clasps above. Behind it, place the Ark of the Covenant. The veil will mark the boundary between the Holy Place and the Holy of Holies.

[34] Set the atonement cover on the Ark, there in the Holy of Holies.

[35] Outside the veil: place the table on the north side, the lampstand opposite it on the south.

[36] For the entrance to the tent, make a screen — blue, purple, crimson, finely twisted linen — embroidered with skill.

[37] Hang it on five acacia pillars sheathed in gold, with golden hooks. And cast five bronze sockets to hold them.

[1] Build the altar from acacia wood — seven and a half feet square, four and a half feet tall. A perfect square.

[2] At each of the four corners, shape horns rising from the altar itself, all of one piece. Sheath the whole thing in bronze.

[3] Forge every tool in bronze: the pots for hauling out ashes, the shovels, the basins, the meat forks, the firepans. Every utensil — bronze.

[4] Make a bronze grating, a mesh, and set a bronze ring at each of its four corners.

[5] Mount the grating under the ledge of the altar, so it sits halfway down — submerged inside the structure.

[6] Cut poles for the altar from acacia wood and overlay them in bronze.

[7] Run the poles through the rings on either side so the altar can be carried.

[8] Build it hollow — a frame of boards. Exactly as you were shown on the mountain. Exactly.

[9] Now the courtyard. The south side: hangings of finely twisted linen, a hundred cubits long for that one side.

[10] Twenty posts standing in twenty bronze sockets, with silver hooks and silver bands binding the posts.

[11] The north side, the same — a hundred cubits of hangings, twenty posts, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks, silver bands.

[12] The west end: fifty cubits of hangings, ten posts, ten sockets.

[13] The east end, where the sun rises — also fifty cubits across.

[14] On one side of the entrance, fifteen cubits of hangings, three posts, three sockets.

[15] On the other side, the same: fifteen cubits, three posts, three sockets.

[16] For the gate itself — a curtain of twenty cubits, woven in blue, purple, and crimson on fine twisted linen, the work of an embroiderer. Four posts, four sockets.

[17] Every post around the courtyard banded in silver, hooked in silver, set in bronze.

[18] The courtyard: a hundred cubits long, fifty wide, seven and a half feet tall — linen finely twisted, sockets of bronze.

[19] Every implement used in the Dwelling for any purpose — every tent peg, every peg of the courtyard — bronze.

[20] Command the Israelites to bring you pure olive oil, pressed clear, for the light — so the lamp burns continually.

[21] Inside the Tent of Meeting, outside the curtain that veils the covenant, Aaron and his sons will tend that flame — from evening until morning, before the LORD. A statute forever, generation after generation, on behalf of Israel.

[1] Bring your brother Aaron to your side, along with his sons—Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar—drawn out from among the Israelites to serve me as priests.

[2] Make sacred garments for Aaron your brother—garments of glory, garments of beauty.

[3] Speak to every skilled craftsman, every artisan I have filled with the spirit of wisdom, and have them craft Aaron's vestments. He is being set apart. He is being consecrated to serve me as priest.

[4] These are the garments they will make: a breastpiece, an ephod, a robe, a woven tunic, a turban, a sash. Sacred garments for Aaron your brother and for his sons, so they may serve me as priests.

[5] They will work with gold, with violet and purple and crimson yarn, with fine linen.

[6] The ephod—craft it from gold thread, from violet and purple and crimson, from finely twisted linen, the work of a master weaver.

[7] It will have two shoulder pieces joined at its two edges—joined and bound.

[8] The waistband woven into it must be one piece with it, the same craftsmanship: gold, violet, purple, crimson, finely twisted linen.

[9] Take two onyx stones and engrave on them the names of the sons of Israel—

[10] six names on one stone, the remaining six on the other, ordered by birth.

[11] Engrave them the way a jeweler engraves a signet—the names of Israel's sons cut into stone. Set them in filigree settings of gold.

[12] Fasten the two stones onto the shoulder pieces of the ephod—stones of remembrance for the sons of Israel. Aaron will carry their names on his two shoulders before the LORD. A remembrance.

[13] Make filigree settings of gold,

[14] and two chains of pure gold, twisted like cords—braided work. Attach the braided chains to the settings.

[15] The breastpiece of judgment—craft it with the same skill as the ephod: gold, violet, purple, crimson, finely twisted linen.

[16] Make it square, folded double—a span long, a span wide.

[17] Mount on it four rows of stones. First row: ruby, topaz, emerald.

[18] Second row: turquoise, sapphire, diamond.

[19] Third row: jacinth, agate, amethyst.

[20] Fourth row: beryl, onyx, jasper. Set them in gold filigree.

[21] Twelve stones for the twelve names of Israel's sons—each one engraved like a signet, one name per stone, one stone per tribe.

[22] Make braided chains of pure gold for the breastpiece, twisted like cords.

[23] Make two gold rings for the breastpiece and attach them to its two upper corners.

[24] Run the two gold cords through the rings at the corners of the breastpiece,

[25] and fasten the other ends of the cords to the two filigree settings—then attach those to the shoulder pieces of the ephod at the front.

[26] Make two more gold rings and place them on the two lower corners of the breastpiece, on the inside edge against the ephod.

[27] Two more gold rings—attach them to the shoulder pieces of the ephod at the front, low down, near the seam just above the woven waistband.

[28] Bind the breastpiece by its rings to the rings of the ephod with a cord of violet, so it rests against the waistband and never works loose from the ephod.

[29] Aaron will carry the names of Israel's sons on the breastpiece of judgment over his heart whenever he enters the holy place—a constant remembrance before the LORD.

[30] Into the breastpiece of judgment, place the Urim and the Thummim. They will rest over Aaron's heart when he comes before the LORD. Aaron will carry the judgment of Israel's sons over his heart, before the LORD, always.

[31] The robe of the ephod—make it entirely of violet.

[32] An opening for the head at its center, with a woven binding around the opening like the collar of armor, so it cannot tear.

[33] Around the hem, work pomegranates of violet, purple, and crimson yarn—all around—with gold bells between them,

[34] alternating: gold bell, pomegranate, gold bell, pomegranate—all the way around the hem.

[35] Aaron must wear it when he ministers. The sound of it will be heard as he enters the holy place before the LORD and as he leaves—so he does not die.

[36] Make a medallion of pure gold and engrave on it, like a signet: HOLY TO THE LORD.

[37] Fasten it with a violet cord onto the turban—at the front of the turban it will sit.

[38] It will rest on Aaron's forehead, and Aaron will bear the guilt connected to the sacred offerings the Israelites consecrate—all their holy gifts. It will always be on his forehead, securing their acceptance before the LORD.

[39] Weave the tunic of fine linen. Make the turban of fine linen. And the sash—the work of an embroiderer.

[40] For Aaron's sons—make tunics, sashes, headbands. Glory and beauty.

[41] Clothe your brother Aaron and his sons with these. Anoint them. Ordain them. Consecrate them. They will serve me as priests.

[42] Make them linen undergarments to cover their nakedness, reaching from waist to thigh.

[43] Aaron and his sons must wear these whenever they enter the tent of meeting or approach the altar to minister in the holy place—so they incur no guilt and do not die. A lasting statute, for him and for his descendants after him.

[1] Here is what you will do to consecrate them as priests serving me. Take one young bull and two rams without defect. [2] Bake unleavened bread, unleavened cakes mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers brushed with oil — all from fine wheat flour. [3] Place them in a single basket and present them in that basket alongside the bull and the two rams.

[4] Bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance of the tent of meeting and wash them with water. [5] Then take the garments and dress Aaron in the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod itself, and the breastpiece. Fasten the ephod to him with its skillfully woven waistband. [6] Set the turban on his head and attach the sacred crown to the turban. [7] Take the anointing oil, pour it over his head, and anoint him.

[8] Bring his sons forward and dress them in tunics. [9] Wrap sashes around them — Aaron and his sons — and tie the headbands on them. The priesthood belongs to them by perpetual decree. This is how you ordain Aaron and his sons.

[10] Lead the bull to the front of the tent of meeting, and Aaron and his sons will press their hands onto its head. [11] Slaughter the bull before the Lord at the entrance of the tent of meeting. [12] Take some of the bull's blood, smear it with your finger on the horns of the altar, and pour out the rest at the base of the altar. [13] Take all the fat covering the entrails, the lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat, and burn them on the altar. [14] But the bull's flesh, its hide, and its dung — burn them outside the camp. This is a sin offering.

[15] Then take one of the rams, and Aaron and his sons will press their hands onto its head. [16] Slaughter it, collect its blood, and splash it against every side of the altar. [17] Cut the ram into pieces, wash the entrails and legs, and arrange them with the head and the other pieces. [18] Then burn the entire ram on the altar. It is a burnt offering to the Lord — a fragrance that pleases him, an offering by fire.

[19] Take the second ram now, and Aaron and his sons will press their hands onto its head. [20] Slaughter it. Take some of its blood and put it on the lobe of Aaron's right ear, on the lobes of his sons' right ears, on the thumbs of their right hands, and on the big toes of their right feet. Then splash the rest of the blood against every side of the altar. [21] Take some blood from the altar and some of the anointing oil and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments, and on his sons and their garments. He and his sons and their garments will be made holy.

[22] Take the fat of the ram, the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails, the lobe of the liver, both kidneys with their fat, and the right thigh — for this is the ram of ordination. [23] From the basket of unleavened bread before the Lord, take one loaf, one cake of oil-bread, and one wafer. [24] Place all of it in the hands of Aaron and his sons, and have them lift it up before the Lord as a wave offering. [25] Then take it from their hands and burn it on the altar along with the burnt offering — a fragrance that pleases the Lord, an offering by fire.

[26] Take the breast of the ordination ram and lift it before the Lord as a wave offering. That portion is yours. [27] Set apart the breast that was waved and the thigh that was lifted — the portions from the ordination ram belonging to Aaron and his sons. [28] These belong to Aaron and his sons by perpetual statute from the Israelites, because they are contributions — the Israelites' contributions from their peace offerings, their offering to the Lord.

[29] Aaron's sacred garments will pass to his sons after him, to be anointed and ordained in. [30] The son who succeeds him as priest and enters the tent of meeting to serve in the sanctuary will wear them for seven days.

[31] Take the ordination ram and boil its meat in a holy place. [32] Aaron and his sons will eat the meat of the ram and the bread in the basket at the entrance of the tent of meeting. [33] They alone will eat what was used to make atonement for them at their ordination and consecration. No outsider may eat it — it is holy. [34] If any of the ordination meat or bread is left until morning, burn what remains. It must not be eaten — it is holy.

[35] This is exactly what you will do for Aaron and his sons — everything I have commanded. The ordination will take seven days. [36] Each day offer a bull as a sin offering to make atonement. Purify the altar by making atonement for it, and anoint it to consecrate it. [37] For seven days you will make atonement for the altar and consecrate it. Then the altar will be most holy — whatever touches it becomes holy.

[38] This is what you will offer on the altar: two year-old lambs each day, continuously. [39] One lamb in the morning, the other at twilight. [40] With the first lamb, offer a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter hin of beaten oil, and a quarter hin of wine as a drink offering. [41] Offer the second lamb at twilight with the same grain and drink offering as in the morning — a fragrance that pleases, an offering by fire to the Lord. [42] This burnt offering will continue from generation to generation at the entrance of the tent of meeting before the Lord, where I will meet you and speak with you.

[43] There I will meet with the Israelites, and the place will be made holy by my glory. [44] I will consecrate the tent of meeting and the altar. I will consecrate Aaron and his sons to serve me as priests. [45] I will dwell among the Israelites and be their God. [46] And they will know that I am the Lord their God, who brought them out of Egypt so that I could dwell among them. I am the Lord their God.

[1] Build an altar for burning incense. Make it from acacia wood. [2] One cubit by one cubit, square — two cubits high — with horns rising from its corners, all of a piece. [3] Plate it in pure gold: the top, the sides all around, the horns. Run a gold molding around it. [4] Beneath the molding, on two opposite sides, set two gold rings to hold the carrying poles. [5] Make the poles from acacia and overlay them with gold.

[6] Place this altar in front of the veil that shields the ark of the covenant — directly before the atonement cover, where I will meet you.

[7] Every morning, when Aaron tends the lamps, he is to burn fragrant incense on it. [8] And again at twilight, when he sets the lamps burning — incense, perpetually, before the LORD, through every generation. [9] No unauthorized incense. No burnt offering. No grain offering. No drink offering poured out on it. [10] Once a year Aaron will purify its horns with the blood of the sin offering of atonement. Once a year, every year, through every generation. This altar is holy of holies to the LORD.

[11] The LORD spoke to Moses: [12] When you take a census of Israel, every man counted must pay the LORD a ransom for his life — so no plague breaks out among them in the counting. [13] Each one counted will give half a shekel by the sanctuary weight (twenty gerahs to the shekel) — a half-shekel offering to the LORD. [14] Everyone twenty years old and up, when they pass through the count, gives this offering. [15] The rich do not give more, the poor do not give less. Half a shekel — the ransom for your lives. [16] Take this atonement money from Israel and dedicate it to the service of the tent of meeting. It will stand as a memorial before the LORD — a ransom for your lives.

[17] The LORD spoke to Moses: [18] Make a bronze basin with a bronze stand for washing. Place it between the tent of meeting and the altar, and fill it with water. [19] Aaron and his sons will wash their hands and feet there. [20] Before entering the tent of meeting — wash, or die. Before approaching the altar to serve, to send up an offering by fire to the LORD — wash, or die. [21] They will wash their hands and feet so they do not die. This is a lasting ordinance for Aaron and his descendants through every generation.

[22] The LORD spoke to Moses: [23] Take the finest spices: five hundred shekels of liquid myrrh, half as much sweet cinnamon — two hundred fifty — and two hundred fifty of fragrant cane, [24] five hundred of cassia, all by the sanctuary weight, and a hin of olive oil. [25] Blend it into a sacred anointing oil — a perfumer's masterwork. This is the holy anointing oil.

[26] Use it to anoint the tent of meeting, the ark of the covenant, [27] the table and all its vessels, the lampstand and all its vessels, the incense altar, [28] the altar of burnt offering and all its vessels, the basin and its stand. [29] Consecrate them — make them most holy. Whatever touches them becomes holy. [30] Anoint Aaron and his sons too, and consecrate them to serve me as priests.

[31] Tell Israel: This will be my holy anointing oil through every generation. [32] Do not pour it on ordinary skin. Do not make anything like it using the same formula. It is holy — treat it as holy. [33] Anyone who blends a copy, anyone who puts it on an outsider — cut off from the people.

[34] The LORD said to Moses: Take fragrant spices — gum resin, onycha, galbanum — and pure frankincense, equal parts of each, [35] and have a perfumer compound them into incense: salted, pure, holy. [36] Grind some of it fine and place it before the covenant in the tent of meeting, where I will meet you. Treat it as most holy. [37] Do not make incense like this for yourselves. Treat it as holy to the LORD. [38] Anyone who makes a copy to enjoy its fragrance — cut off from the people.

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Look — I've called him by name: Bezalel, son of Uri, son of Hur, from the tribe of Judah.

[3] I've filled him with the Spirit of God — with wisdom, with understanding, with knowledge, with every kind of craft —

[4] to design intricate work, to shape gold and silver and bronze,

[5] to cut and set stones, to carve wood, to master every craft.

[6] And listen — I've appointed Oholiab, son of Ahisamach from the tribe of Dan, to work alongside him. I've placed skill in the heart of every gifted artisan, so they can build everything I've commanded you:

[7] the Tent of Meeting, the Ark of the Covenant and the mercy seat that covers it, every furnishing of the Tent,

[8] the table and its vessels, the pure-gold lampstand with all its tools, the altar of incense,

[9] the altar of burnt offering with its equipment, the basin and its stand,

[10] the woven garments — the sacred vestments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve in,

[11] the anointing oil, and the fragrant incense for the sanctuary. They will do everything exactly as I've commanded you."

[12] The LORD said to Moses:

[13] "Tell the Israelites this: 'Keep my Sabbaths. This is the sign between me and you, generation after generation — so you'll know that I am the LORD who sets you apart.

[14] Keep the Sabbath. It is sacred to you. Anyone who profanes it dies. Anyone who works on it — that person is cut off from the people.

[15] Six days for work. The seventh is Sabbath — a holy rest, sacred to the LORD. Anyone who works on the Sabbath day dies.

[16] The Israelites must guard the Sabbath, observing it generation after generation as an eternal covenant.

[17] Between me and the Israelites it is a sign forever, because in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth, and on the seventh day he stopped — and breathed.'"

[18] When he finished speaking with Moses on Mount Sinai, he handed him the two tablets of the covenant — stone, inscribed by the finger of God.

[1] When the people saw that Moses was taking forever to come down from the mountain, they swarmed around Aaron and said, "Get up. Make us gods who will go ahead of us. This Moses, the man who hauled us out of Egypt — we have no idea what happened to him."

[2] Aaron said, "Strip the gold rings from the ears of your wives, your sons, your daughters, and bring them to me."

[3] So every person tore off their gold earrings and handed them over to Aaron.

[4] He took the gold, melted it down, worked it with a tool, and shaped it into a calf. And they shouted, "These are your gods, Israel — the ones who brought you up out of Egypt!"

[5] When Aaron saw it, he built an altar in front of the calf and announced, "Tomorrow — a festival to the LORD!"

[6] Up early the next morning, they offered burnt offerings and brought peace offerings. The people sat down to eat and drink, then got up to play.

[7] The LORD said to Moses, "Go. Get down there. Your people — the ones you brought up out of Egypt — have ruined themselves.

[8] They've bolted off the path I commanded, fast. They've made themselves a cast-metal calf, they're bowing to it, sacrificing to it, saying, 'These are your gods, Israel, who brought you up out of Egypt.'"

[9] The LORD said to Moses, "I've watched these people. Stiff-necked, every one of them.

[10] Now leave me alone. Let my anger burn through them and consume them. Then I will make a great nation out of you."

[11] But Moses pressed his face into the presence of the LORD his God and pleaded: "LORD — why does your anger blaze against your own people, the people you rescued from Egypt with overwhelming power, with a fist that nothing could resist?

[12] Why give the Egyptians the chance to say, 'He led them out to murder them in the mountains, to wipe them off the face of the earth'? Turn back from your burning anger. Relent. Don't bring this disaster on your people.

[13] Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel — your servants. You swore to them by your own self: 'I will multiply your descendants like the stars in the sky, and all this land I promised — I will give it to your children forever.'"

[14] And the LORD relented. The disaster he had spoken of — he did not bring it.

[15] Moses turned and went down the mountain, the two tablets of the covenant in his hands — inscribed on both sides, front and back.

[16] The tablets were God's work. The writing was God's writing, carved into the stone.

[17] Joshua heard the roar of the people below and said to Moses, "That's the sound of war in the camp."

[18] Moses said: "That's not the shout of victory. That's not the cry of defeat. What I hear is singing."

[19] As he came near the camp and saw the calf and the dancing, fury erupted in him. He hurled the tablets from his hands and shattered them at the foot of the mountain.

[20] He seized the calf they had made, threw it into the fire, ground it to powder, scattered the powder on water, and made the Israelites drink it.

[21] Moses turned on Aaron: "What did these people do to you that you dragged them into a sin this enormous?"

[22] Aaron said, "Don't be angry, my lord. You know these people — you know how bent on evil they are.

[23] They said to me, 'Make us gods to go ahead of us. As for this Moses, the man who brought us up out of Egypt — we have no idea what happened to him.'

[24] So I told them, 'Whoever has gold, take it off.' They handed it to me. I threw it into the fire — and out came this calf."

[25] Moses saw that the people were out of control — Aaron had let them run wild, an embarrassment in front of their enemies.

[26] So Moses stood at the entrance of the camp and shouted, "Whoever is for the LORD — to me!" And every Levite gathered to him.

[27] He said to them, "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Every man strap a sword to his side. Go through the camp, gate to gate, back and forth. Cut down brother, friend, neighbor."

[28] The Levites did exactly what Moses said. That day, about three thousand fell.

[29] Moses said, "Today you have set yourselves apart for the LORD — even at the cost of your own son, your own brother. He has poured blessing on you this day."

[30] The next morning Moses said to the people, "You have committed a massive sin. Now I will climb back up to the LORD. Maybe I can make atonement for what you've done."

[31] So Moses returned to the LORD and said, "These people — they have sinned, a sin of staggering weight. They made themselves gods of gold.

[32] And yet — forgive their sin. But if you will not — then blot me out of the book you have written."

[33] The LORD said to Moses, "The one who sins against me — that's the one I blot out of my book.

[34] Now go. Lead the people to the place I told you. My angel will go before you. But on the day I come to settle accounts, I will settle it for their sin."

[35] And the LORD struck the people with a plague because of what they had done with the calf — the one Aaron made.

[1] The LORD said to Moses: "Get moving. You and the people you brought up out of Egypt — leave this place. Head for the land I swore to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the land I promised their descendants.

[2] I'll send an angel ahead of you. I'll drive them out — Canaanite, Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, Jebusite.

[3] Go up to a land flowing with milk and honey. But I won't go with you. You're a stiff-necked people, and if I traveled in your midst I might consume you on the way."

[4] When the people heard this devastating word, they entered mourning. No one put on a single ornament.

[5] The LORD had told Moses to tell them: "You are a stiff-necked people. If I walked among you for even a moment, I'd destroy you. Strip off your ornaments. Then I'll decide what to do with you."

[6] So from Mount Horeb on, the Israelites stripped themselves of their ornaments.

[7] Moses would take the tent and pitch it outside the camp — far outside — and he called it the Tent of Meeting. Anyone seeking the LORD would go out to that tent beyond the camp.

[8] Whenever Moses walked out toward the tent, every person in the camp would rise and stand at their tent door, watching him until he disappeared inside.

[9] When Moses entered, the pillar of cloud would descend and stand at the entrance, and the LORD would speak with Moses.

[10] The people would see the pillar of cloud standing at the tent's entrance, and every one of them would rise and bow down at their own tent door.

[11] And the LORD spoke to Moses face to face, the way a man speaks to his friend. Then Moses would return to camp, but his young assistant Joshua son of Nun never left the tent.

[12] Moses said to the LORD: "Look — you keep telling me, 'Bring this people up,' but you haven't told me who you're sending with me. And yet you've said, 'I know you by name. You've found favor in my eyes.'

[13] If I really have found favor with you, then show me your ways. Let me know you — actually know you — so I can keep finding favor with you. And remember: this nation is your people."

[14] He answered: "My presence will go with you. I will give you rest."

[15] Moses said: "If your presence doesn't go with us, don't send us up from here.

[16] How will anyone know we've found favor in your eyes — me and your people — unless you go with us? That's what sets us apart, me and your people, from every other people on the face of the earth."

[17] The LORD said to Moses: "This very thing you asked — I'll do it. You've found favor in my eyes, and I know you by name."

[18] Then Moses said: "Show me your glory."

[19] And he said: "I'll make all my goodness pass before you, and I'll proclaim the name — the LORD — in your hearing. I'll be gracious to whom I'll be gracious. I'll show mercy to whom I'll show mercy.

[20] But," he said, "you cannot see my face. No human sees me and lives.

[21] Here," the LORD said, "stand on this rock, near me.

[22] When my glory passes, I'll set you in a cleft of the rock and shield you with my hand until I've gone by.

[23] Then I'll lift my hand, and you'll see my back. But my face — my face will not be seen."

[1] The LORD said to Moses: "Carve out two stone tablets like the first ones. I'll write on them the words that were on the originals you shattered. [2] Be ready by morning. Climb Mount Sinai at sunrise and meet me there on the summit. [3] No one comes with you. No one is to be seen anywhere on the mountain. Even the flocks and herds stay away from its base."

[4] So Moses cut two stone tablets like the first ones, rose before dawn, and climbed Sinai as the LORD had commanded, the two tablets in his hands.

[5] The LORD descended in the cloud, stood there with him, and proclaimed his name: YHWH. [6] He passed in front of Moses and called out:

"YHWH, YHWH —
God of mercy and grace,
slow to anger,
overflowing with loyal love and truth,
[7] keeping that love steady for thousands,
forgiving wrongdoing, rebellion, and sin —
yet not letting the guilty walk free,
calling children and grandchildren to account
for the sins of their parents,
to the third and fourth generation."

[8] Moses dropped to the ground and worshiped. [9] "Lord," he said, "if I've found favor with you — please, walk with us. Yes, these people are stubborn. Forgive our wrongdoing, our sin. Take us as your own."
