[10] The LORD answered: "I'm cutting a covenant. In front of all your people I will perform wonders that have never been done anywhere on earth, in any nation. The people surrounding you will see what the LORD does — and what I do through you will be terrifying.

[11] "Pay attention to what I'm commanding you today. I'm driving out before you the Amorite, Canaanite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite. [12] Watch yourself. Don't cut any covenant with the people of the land you're entering — they'll become a trap baited and set right under your feet. [13] Tear down their altars. Smash their sacred pillars. Cut down their Asherah poles. [14] You will not bow to any other god — because the LORD's very name is Jealous. He is a jealous God.

[15] "Don't make any treaty with the people of the land. When they whore after their gods and sacrifice to them, they'll invite you, and you'll eat from their sacrifice. [16] You'll take their daughters for your sons, and those daughters will whore after their gods and pull your sons into the same prostitution.

[17] "Don't cast metal gods for yourselves.

[18] "Keep the Festival of Unleavened Bread. Seven days you eat bread without yeast, as I commanded you, at the appointed time in the month of Abib — because that was the month you came out of Egypt.

[19] "Every firstborn belongs to me — every firstborn male of your livestock, whether ox or sheep. [20] Redeem the firstborn donkey with a lamb. If you won't redeem it, break its neck. Redeem every firstborn son. No one comes before me empty-handed.

[21] "Six days you work. On the seventh, you rest — even during plowing and harvest, you rest.

[22] "Keep the Festival of Weeks with the first wheat of the harvest, and the Festival of Ingathering at the turn of the year. [23] Three times a year every male among you is to appear before the Sovereign, the LORD, the God of Israel. [24] I'll drive out nations before you and expand your borders, and no one will covet your land while you go up to appear before the LORD your God three times a year.

[25] "Don't offer the blood of my sacrifice with anything leavened. Don't let any of the Passover sacrifice remain until morning.

[26] "Bring the best of the first fruits of your soil into the house of the LORD your God.

"Do not boil a young goat in its mother's milk."

[27] The LORD said to Moses: "Write these words down. On the basis of these words I am cutting a covenant with you and with Israel."

[28] Moses was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights. He ate no bread and drank no water. And on the tablets he wrote the words of the covenant — the Ten Words.

[29] Moses came down from Mount Sinai. The two tablets of the covenant were in his hands, and he didn't know that the skin of his face was radiant — because he had been speaking with God. [30] When Aaron and the Israelites saw Moses — saw his face shining — they shrank back. They were afraid to come near him.

[31] But Moses called to them. Aaron and the leaders of the community came back, and Moses spoke with them. [32] After that, all the Israelites came near, and he gave them every command the LORD had given him on Mount Sinai.

[33] When Moses finished speaking with them, he covered his face with a veil. [34] Whenever he entered the LORD's presence to speak with him, he removed the veil until he came out. Then he would emerge and tell the Israelites what he'd been commanded. [35] They would see Moses' face — the skin of his face blazing — and Moses would put the veil back on until he went in again to speak with the LORD.

[1] So Bezalel and Oholiab and every skilled craftsman — everyone the LORD had filled with wisdom and the instinct to know how — went to work on the sanctuary, doing everything exactly as the LORD had commanded.

[2] Moses called Bezalel and Oholiab forward, along with every gifted artisan whose heart the LORD had stirred and who was ready to step up and do the work.

[3] He handed them all the contributions the Israelites had brought for building the sanctuary. And still, morning after morning, the people kept bringing more — freely, voluntarily.

[4] Finally, every craftsman doing the sacred work stopped what he was doing and came to Moses,

[5] and they said it: "The people are bringing far more than we need. The work the LORD ordered is already oversupplied."

[6] So Moses gave the order, and it was announced throughout the camp: "No one — man or woman — needs to make any more offerings for the sanctuary." The people were stopped from giving.

[7] What they already had was more than enough. It was overflowing.

[8] Then every skilled worker among them began constructing the Dwelling — ten curtains of finely twisted linen, dyed blue and purple and crimson, with cherubim woven into them by an artist's hand.

[9] Each curtain measured twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide — every one of them identical.

[10] They joined five curtains together into one set, and the other five into another.

[11] Then they made loops of blue along the edge of the outermost curtain in each set,

[12] fifty loops on one panel and fifty on the matching edge of the other — the loops aligned exactly across from each other.

[13] They forged fifty gold clasps and used them to link the two sets of curtains together, so the Dwelling became a single, unified whole.

[14] Then they wove eleven curtains of goat hair to serve as a tent over the Dwelling.

[15] Each one was thirty cubits long and four wide — all eleven cut to the same measure.

[16] They joined five into one set, and six into another.

[17] They made fifty loops along the edge of the outermost curtain of each set,

[18] and forged fifty bronze clasps to fasten the tent together as one.

[19] Over all of this they laid a covering of ram skins dyed red, and above that, a covering of fine leather.

[20] For the framework of the Dwelling, they made upright planks of acacia wood.

[21] Each plank was ten cubits tall and a cubit and a half wide,

[22] with two tenons at the base of each one for joining — the same design across every board.

[23] For the south side, they made twenty planks,

[24] and forty silver bases to set beneath them — two bases under each plank, locking into the tenons.

[25] For the north side, they made twenty more planks,

[26] with forty silver bases — two beneath each.

[27] For the back of the Dwelling, facing west, they made six planks,

[28] and two extra planks for the rear corners,

[29] doubled at the base and joined together at the top with a single ring — corner pieces, both of them, shaped the same way.

[30] So the back wall had eight planks in total and sixteen silver bases — two beneath each plank.

[31] Then they cut crossbars of acacia wood: five for the planks on one side of the Dwelling,

[32] five for the other side, and five for the rear wall facing west.

[33] The middle crossbar ran the full length of the wall, from one end to the other, threading through the heart of the planks.

[34] They overlaid all the planks with gold, forged gold rings to hold the crossbars, and overlaid the crossbars with gold as well.

[35] Then they made the veil — blue, purple, and crimson on finely twisted linen — with cherubim woven into it by a master's hand.

[36] They made four acacia posts for it, overlaid them with gold, fitted them with gold hooks, and cast four silver bases for them.

[37] For the entrance to the tent, they made a screen — blue, purple, and crimson on finely twisted linen — the work of an embroiderer.

[38] And five posts to hold it, with their hooks. They overlaid the tops and bands of the posts with gold, and cast five bronze bases for them.

[1] Bezalel built the ark from acacia wood — three feet nine inches long, two feet three inches wide, two feet three inches high.

[2] He plated it with pure gold inside and out and ran a gold molding around its edge.

[3] He cast four gold rings and fixed them to its four feet — two rings on one side, two on the other.

[4] He made carrying poles of acacia wood, sheathed them in gold,

[5] and slid them through the rings on the sides of the ark so it could be carried.

[6] He made the atonement cover from pure gold — three feet nine inches long, two feet three inches wide.

[7] He hammered two cherubim out of gold — beaten from a single piece — one at each end of the cover.

[8] One cherub at this end, one at that end, both worked from the same gold as the cover itself.

[9] The cherubim spread their wings upward, sheltering the cover, faces angled toward each other, gazes fixed on the place of atonement.

[10] He built the table from acacia wood — three feet long, eighteen inches wide, two feet three inches high.

[11] He overlaid it with pure gold and edged it with a gold molding.

[12] He framed it with a rim a handsbreadth wide and crowned that rim with gold molding too.

[13] He cast four gold rings and attached them at the four corners where the legs met the table.

[14] The rings sat close to the rim — holders for the poles that carried the table.

[15] He made the poles from acacia wood, plated them in gold, and used them to carry the table.

[16] Then he crafted the vessels for the table out of pure gold — plates, dishes, bowls, and pitchers for pouring out offerings.

[17] He made the lampstand from pure gold, hammered from a single piece — base, shaft, cups, buds, blossoms, all one.

[18] Six branches extended from the sides — three on one side, three on the other.

[19] Each branch carried three almond-shaped cups, each cup with bud and blossom — the same pattern repeated down all six branches.

[20] On the central shaft itself, four almond cups bloomed, complete with buds and blossoms.

[21] A bud sat beneath each pair of branches where they joined the shaft — three buds, six branches, all of a piece.

[22] Buds, branches, shaft — every part hammered from a single block of pure gold.

[23] He made the seven lamps, along with the wick trimmers and trays — all pure gold.

[24] The lampstand and its tools were forged from seventy-five pounds of pure gold.

[25] He built the incense altar from acacia wood — eighteen inches square, three feet tall — with horns rising from its corners as one piece with the altar.

[26] He overlaid it with pure gold — top, sides, horns — and ran a gold molding around it.

[27] He set two gold rings below the molding on opposite sides to hold the poles for carrying it.

[28] The poles were acacia wood, sheathed in gold.

[29] And he compounded the holy anointing oil and the pure, fragrant incense — the work of a master perfumer.

[1] He built the altar for burnt offerings out of acacia wood — square, seven and a half feet on each side, four and a half feet tall. [2] He shaped horns at each of the four corners, all of one piece with the altar, then sheathed the whole thing in bronze. [3] He forged every tool the altar would need: pots for the ashes, shovels, basins for catching blood, meat hooks, and fire pans — all of them bronze. [4] He fitted the altar with a bronze grating, a mesh that sat halfway up under the ledge. [5] He cast four bronze rings for the four corners of the grating to hold the carrying poles. [6] He carved the poles from acacia wood and overlaid them in bronze, [7] then slid them through the rings on either side so the altar could be lifted and moved. He built it hollow — just planks.

[8] He made the bronze basin and its bronze stand from the mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

[9] Then he built the courtyard. The south side ran a hundred and fifty feet of finely woven linen curtains, [10] hung on twenty posts in twenty bronze sockets, with silver hooks and silver bands. [11] The north side matched it — a hundred and fifty feet, twenty posts, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks, silver bands. [12] The west side stretched seventy-five feet of curtains across ten posts in ten sockets, with silver hooks and silver bands. [13] The east side, facing the sunrise, was also seventy-five feet across — [14] twenty-two and a half feet of curtains hanging on three posts with three sockets on one side of the entrance, [15] and the same on the other side: twenty-two and a half feet of curtains, three posts, three sockets. [16] Every curtain around the entire courtyard was finely woven linen. [17] The sockets were bronze, the hooks and bands silver, and the tops of the posts were sheathed in silver. Every post around the courtyard was banded with silver.

[18] The curtain for the courtyard's entrance was the work of an embroiderer — blue, purple, scarlet, and finely woven linen. Thirty feet wide and seven and a half feet tall, matching the height of the courtyard curtains. [19] It hung on four posts set in four bronze sockets, with silver hooks, silvered tops, and silver bands. [20] Every peg around the tabernacle and the courtyard was bronze.

[21] Here is the accounting for the tabernacle — the tabernacle of the covenant — which Moses ordered drawn up by the Levites under Ithamar son of Aaron the priest. [22] Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made everything the LORD had commanded Moses. [23] With him worked Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan — engraver, designer, embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet and fine linen.

[24] All the gold used for the sanctuary, every ounce of the wave offering: 2,193 pounds, by the sanctuary standard.

[25] The silver collected from those counted in the census: 7,544 pounds, by the sanctuary standard — [26] one beka per head, half a shekel by the sanctuary weight, from every man twenty years old and up who crossed over to be counted. That came to 603,550 men. [27] Of this silver, 7,500 pounds went into casting the sockets for the sanctuary and the curtain — a hundred sockets at seventy-five pounds each. [28] The remaining 44 pounds Bezalel used for the hooks on the posts, the overlay on their tops, and the silver bands.

[29] The bronze of the wave offering: 5,310 pounds. [30] He used it for the sockets at the entrance to the tent of meeting, the bronze altar with its bronze grating and all its tools, [31] the sockets around the courtyard, the sockets at the courtyard's entrance, and every peg of the tabernacle and every peg of the surrounding court.

[1] From the blue, purple, and crimson yarn they wove the woven garments for service in the sanctuary, and they made the sacred vestments for Aaron — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[2] He made the ephod from gold, from blue, purple, and crimson yarn, and from finely twisted linen.

[3] They hammered the gold into thin sheets and cut it into threads, then worked it into the blue, the purple, the crimson, and the linen — the craftsmanship of a master.

[4] They made shoulder pieces for it, joined at its two edges so it could be fastened together.

[5] The waistband of the ephod, woven as one piece with it, was made of the same materials: gold, blue, purple, crimson, and finely twisted linen — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[6] They set the onyx stones in filigree settings of gold, engraved like a signet with the names of the sons of Israel.

[7] He fastened them on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as memorial stones for the sons of Israel — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[8] He made the breastpiece — the craftsmanship of a master — in the same style as the ephod: gold, blue, purple, crimson, and finely twisted linen.

[9] It was square and folded double — a span long, a span wide, doubled over.

[10] They set four rows of stones in it. The first row: carnelian, topaz, emerald.

[11] The second row: turquoise, sapphire, diamond.

[12] The third row: jacinth, agate, amethyst.

[13] The fourth row: beryl, onyx, jasper. All of them held in filigree settings of gold.

[14] Twelve stones for twelve names — the sons of Israel — each engraved like a signet, one name per stone, for the twelve tribes.

[15] They made chains for the breastpiece, twisted like cords, of pure gold.

[16] They made two gold filigree settings and two gold rings, and fastened the rings to the two upper corners of the breastpiece.

[17] They threaded the two golden cords through the two rings at the corners of the breastpiece,

[18] and the other ends of the cords they fastened to the two filigree settings, attaching them to the shoulder pieces of the ephod at the front.

[19] Then they made two more gold rings and set them at the two lower corners of the breastpiece, on its inner edge against the ephod.

[20] And two more gold rings — these they fastened to the front of the ephod, low down, near the seam, just above the woven waistband.

[21] They bound the breastpiece by its rings to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue, so it would sit firmly against the waistband and not come loose from the ephod — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[22] He made the robe of the ephod entirely of blue, the work of a weaver,

[23] with an opening at the top in the center, like the collar of a coat of mail, with a woven border around the opening so it would not tear.

[24] On the hem of the robe they made pomegranates of blue, purple, and crimson yarn, twisted tight.

[25] They made bells of pure gold and set them between the pomegranates all around the hem —

[26] a bell, then a pomegranate, a bell, then a pomegranate — circling the hem of the robe for ministry, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[27] They wove tunics of fine linen for Aaron and his sons,

[28] and the turban of fine linen, the ornate caps of fine linen, the linen undergarments of finely twisted linen,

[29] and the sash of finely twisted linen embroidered with blue, purple, and crimson — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[30] They made the medallion — the holy crown — of pure gold, and engraved on it like a signet: HOLY TO THE LORD.

[31] They tied it to the turban with a cord of blue, fastening it to the front — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[32] So all the work on the Tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting, was finished. The Israelites had done it all — every detail exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses. Exactly.

[33] Then they brought the Tabernacle to Moses: the tent and all its furnishings — its clasps, its frames, its crossbars, its posts, its bases;

[34] the covering of ram skins dyed red, the covering of fine leather, and the curtain that screened the Holy Place;

[35] the ark of the covenant with its poles and its atonement cover;

[36] the table with all its utensils and the Bread of the Presence;

[37] the pure gold lampstand with its row of lamps and all its accessories, and the oil for the light;

[38] the gold altar, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance of the tent;

[39] the bronze altar with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand;

[40] the hangings for the courtyard, its posts and bases, the screen for the courtyard gate, its ropes and pegs, and every implement needed for service in the Tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting;

[41] and the woven garments for service in the sanctuary — the sacred vestments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve in.

[42] The Israelites had done all the work exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[43] Moses inspected everything. They had done it — every piece of it — just as the LORD had commanded. So Moses blessed them.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[3] Set the Ark of the Covenant inside it and screen the Ark with the veil.

[4] Bring in the table and arrange what belongs on it. Bring in the lampstand and light its lamps.

[5] Place the gold altar of incense in front of the Ark and hang the screen at the entrance to the dwelling.

[6] Position the altar of burnt offering in front of the entrance to the Tent of Meeting,

[8] Erect the courtyard around it all, and hang the screen at the courtyard gate.

[9] Take the anointing oil and anoint the dwelling and everything inside it. Consecrate it and all its furnishings, and it will be holy.

[17] So on the first day of the first month of the second year, the dwelling was raised.

[18] Moses set it up: he laid the bases, framed the boards, ran the crossbars through, and stood the pillars.

[19] He stretched the tent over the dwelling, laid the covering on top—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[20] He took the Testimony and placed it inside the Ark, slid the poles through the rings, and set the mercy seat on top.

[21] He carried the Ark into the dwelling, hung the veil to screen it off, and shielded the Ark of the Testimony—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[22] He set the table inside the Tent of Meeting, on the north side, outside the veil,

[23] and arranged the rows of bread on it before the LORD—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[24] He placed the lampstand in the Tent of Meeting opposite the table, on the south side,

[25] and lit the lamps before the LORD—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[26] He set the gold altar in the Tent of Meeting in front of the veil

[27] and burned fragrant incense on it—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[29] He placed the altar of burnt offering at the entrance and offered the burnt offering and grain offering on it—just as the LORD had commanded him.

[30] He set the basin between the Tent of Meeting and the altar and filled it with water for washing.

[31] Moses, Aaron, and his sons washed their hands and feet at it.

And so Moses finished the work.

[34] Then the cloud covered the Tent of Meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the dwelling.

[35] Moses could not enter the Tent of Meeting—the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the place.

[36] Whenever the cloud lifted from above the dwelling, Israel would set out, every stage of their journey.

[37] If the cloud did not lift, they did not move—they waited until it did.

[38] For the cloud of the LORD rested on the dwelling by day, and fire burned in it by night, in the sight of all Israel, through every stage of their journey.

[1] In my thirtieth year, fourth month, fifth day — I was among the exiles by the Kebar Canal when the heavens cracked open and I saw God.

[2] It was the fifth day of the month, the fifth year of King Jehoiachin's deportation.

[3] The word of the LORD came hard and direct to Ezekiel son of Buzi, the priest, there in the land of the Chaldeans by the Kebar. The hand of the LORD came down on him.

[4] I looked — and a storm was rolling in from the north: a massive cloud, fire flashing inside it, a glow wrapped all around it. And at its core, where the fire burned hottest, something gleamed like molten metal.

[5] Out of the heart of it came four living creatures. Their shape was human —

[6] but each had four faces. Each had four wings.

[7] Their legs ran straight down, their feet like a calf's hoof, polished and burning like bronze fresh from the forge.

[8] Under each of their four wings, human hands. All four of them — faces and wings —

[9] their wings touching the next creature's wings. They never turned as they moved. Each one went straight forward, wherever it was headed.

[10] Their faces: a human face in front, a lion's face on the right, a bull's face on the left, an eagle's face behind. All four had all four.

[11] Their wings stretched upward — two reaching out to touch the wings of the creatures beside them, two covering their bodies.

[12] Each one moved straight ahead. Wherever the Spirit went, they went, never turning, never breaking stride.

[13] Between the creatures: something like burning coals, like torches weaving back and forth between them. The fire was brilliant, and lightning shot out of it.

[14] The creatures themselves flashed back and forth like bolts of lightning.

[15] Then I looked — and beside each of the four creatures, on the ground, a wheel.

[16] The wheels gleamed like topaz. All four identical. And their construction: a wheel intersecting a wheel, somehow.

[17] They could roll in any of four directions without turning.

[18] Their rims were high and terrifying — and the rims of all four were covered with eyes, eyes everywhere.

[19] When the creatures moved, the wheels moved with them. When the creatures lifted off the ground, the wheels lifted too.

[20] Wherever the Spirit wanted to go, they went — wheels rising right alongside them, because the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

[21] When the creatures moved, the wheels moved. When they stopped, the wheels stopped. When they rose, the wheels rose — locked together, one spirit running through them all.

[22] Spread out above the heads of the creatures: a vast dome, glittering like ice, terrifying to look at, stretched over them like a sky.

[23] Beneath that dome their wings reached toward each other. Two more wings on each one covered their bodies.

[24] And the sound of those wings when they flew — like crashing water, like the voice of the Almighty, like the roar of an army on the march. When they stopped, the wings folded down.

[25] A voice came from above the dome over their heads. They had stopped, wings down, when it spoke.

[26] Above the dome — something that looked like a throne, lapis lazuli, sapphire blue. And seated on the throne, the figure of a man.

[27] From what looked like his waist up, he gleamed like molten metal, fire wrapped all around him. From his waist down, fire — and a radiance surrounding him on every side,

[28] glowing like a rainbow on a rainy day. That was the appearance of the glory of the LORD.

I saw it — and I fell on my face. And then I heard a voice speaking.

[1] He said to me: "Son of man, get on your feet. I have something to say to you."

[2] As he spoke, breath rushed into me, lifted me, set me upright — and I heard him.

[3] He said: "Son of man, I am sending you to Israel — a nation of rebels who have rebelled against me. They and their fathers have been breaking faith with me right up to this very day.

[4] The children are stubborn-faced and hard-hearted. I am sending you to them, and you will tell them: 'This is what the Lord God says.'

[5] Whether they listen or refuse — and they are a house of rebellion — they will know a prophet has been among them.

[6] As for you, son of man — do not be afraid of them. Do not be afraid of their words. Briers will surround you, scorpions will be your neighbors — do not fear what they say, do not flinch at their faces. They are a house of rebellion.

[7] You will speak my words to them whether they listen or not — because rebellion is what they are.

[8] But you, son of man — listen to what I am telling you. Do not be rebellious like that rebellious house. Open your mouth and eat what I give you."

[9] I looked, and a hand was reaching toward me, and in the hand was a scroll.

[10] He unrolled it in front of me. It was written on both sides — front and back — with funeral songs, mourning, and grief.

[1] Then he said to me: "Son of man, eat what's in front of you. Eat this scroll. Then go—speak to the house of Israel."

[2] So I opened my mouth, and he fed me the scroll.

[3] "Son of man," he said, "feed your belly with this. Fill your gut with the scroll I'm giving you." I ate it. And in my mouth it was sweet as honey.

[4] Then he said: "Son of man, go. Go to the house of Israel and speak my words to them.

[5] You're not being sent to some foreign people with thick speech and a tongue you can't decipher. You're being sent to Israel.

[6] Not to a crowd of nations with garbled accents and impossible languages—people whose words you couldn't even understand. If I had sent you to *them*, they would have listened.

[7] But the house of Israel? They won't listen to you—because they refuse to listen to me. The whole house is stubborn-browed and stone-hearted.

[8] So look—I am making your face as hard as their faces, your forehead as hard as their foreheads.

[9] I'm making your forehead diamond, harder than flint. Don't fear them. Don't flinch at their faces. They are a house of rebellion."

[10] Then he said: "Son of man, take every word I speak to you—take it deep, into your heart, into your ears.

[11] Then go. Go to the exiles, to your own people, and speak. Tell them, 'This is what the Lord GOD says'—whether they listen or refuse."

[12] Then the Spirit lifted me, and behind me I heard a great rumbling roar: *Blessed be the glory of the LORD from his place.*

[13] The sound of the living creatures' wings brushing against each other, the sound of the wheels beside them—a great rumbling roar.

[14] The Spirit lifted me and carried me away. I went bitter, my spirit on fire, the hand of the LORD pressing hard on me.

[15] I came to the exiles at Tel-abib, the ones living by the Chebar canal—I came to where they were sitting. And there I sat among them. Seven days. Stunned silent.

[16] At the end of those seven days, the word of the LORD came to me:

[17] "Son of man, I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel. When you hear a word from my mouth, warn them from me.

[18] When I tell the wicked, 'You will die,' and you don't warn him—you don't speak up to turn him from his wicked road and save his life—that wicked man will die in his guilt, and I will hold you accountable for his blood.

[19] But if you warn the wicked man and he doesn't turn from his wickedness, from his wicked road—he dies in his guilt, but you have saved your own life.

[20] And when a righteous man turns from his righteousness and does wrong, and I lay a stumbling block before him—he will die. Because you didn't warn him, he will die in his sin. The righteous things he did won't be remembered. And I will hold you accountable for his blood.

[21] But if you warn the righteous man not to sin, and he doesn't sin—he will live, because he took the warning. And you will have saved your own life."

[22] The hand of the LORD came on me there. He said, "Get up. Go out to the valley. I will speak to you there."

[23] So I got up and went out to the valley—and the glory of the LORD was standing there, the same glory I had seen by the Chebar canal. I fell on my face.

[24] Then the Spirit entered me, set me on my feet, and spoke to me. He said: "Go. Shut yourself inside your house.

[25] As for you, son of man—they will put ropes on you, bind you with them, and you will not go out among them.

[26] I will make your tongue cleave to the roof of your mouth. You will be mute. You will not be the one who rebukes them—because they are a house of rebellion.

[27] But when I speak to you, I will open your mouth, and you will say to them, 'This is what the Lord GOD says.' Let the one who will listen, listen. Let the one who refuses, refuse. They are a house of rebellion."

[1] "Now you, son of man—take a brick. Set it in front of you and carve a city on it. Jerusalem. [2] Then lay siege to it. Build siege walls against it. Throw up a ramp. Pitch enemy camps around it. Plant battering rams on every side. [3] And take an iron pan—set it up like an iron wall between you and the city. Lock your face onto it. The city is under siege, and you are the one besieging it. This is a sign for the house of Israel.

[4] "Then lie down on your left side. Take the guilt of the house of Israel onto yourself. For as many days as you lie there, you carry their guilt. [5] I am assigning you a day for each year of their guilt—three hundred and ninety days. That long you will bear it. [6] When you finish, turn over—lie on your right side this time, and bear the guilt of the house of Judah. Forty days. A day for a year. I have measured it out for you.

[7] "Lock your face toward the siege of Jerusalem. Bare your arm. Prophesy against her. [8] I am binding you with ropes—you will not turn from one side to the other until the days of your siege are complete.

[9] "Now take wheat, barley, beans, lentils, millet, and spelt. Pour them into a single jar and make bread from them. Eat this during the three hundred and ninety days you are lying on your side. [10] Weigh your food—twenty shekels a day. Eat it at set times. [11] Measure your water too—a sixth of a hin. Drink it at set times. [12] Eat it like a barley cake. And bake it in their sight over human excrement."

[13] The LORD said: "This is how the people of Israel will eat their bread—defiled—among the nations where I scatter them."

[14] I said, "No—Lord GOD, no. I have never defiled myself. From childhood until now I have never eaten anything that died on its own or was torn by beasts. No tainted meat has ever touched my mouth."

[15] So he said, "All right. I will let you use cow dung instead of human dung. Bake your bread over that."

[16] Then he said to me: "Son of man, I am about to break the staff of bread in Jerusalem. They will eat their bread by weight, anxious. They will drink their water by measure, in horror. [17] Bread gone, water gone—each one staring at his brother in shock, wasting away in their guilt."

[1] Now, son of man, take a sharp sword and use it as a barber's razor. Pass it over your head and your beard. Then take scales and divide the hair into thirds.

[2] When the days of the siege are complete, burn a third in the fire at the center of the city. Take another third and slash it with the sword all around the city. Scatter the last third to the wind — and I will unsheathe a sword to chase them down.

[3] But pluck out a few strands and bind them into the fold of your robe.

[4] Then take even some of those, throw them into the fire, and let them burn — from there, fire will leap out against the whole house of Israel.

[5] This is what the Lord GOD says: This is Jerusalem. I set her at the center of the nations, with countries circling her on every side.

[6] And she rebelled against my rulings — more wickedly than the nations, more defiantly than the lands around her. She threw out my judgments. She refused to walk in my statutes.

[7] So this is what the Lord GOD says: Because you have been more turbulent than the nations around you — refusing to walk in my statutes, refusing to keep my judgments, failing even to live by the standards of the nations encircling you —

[8] therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: I am against you. I myself. And I will execute judgments in your midst with the nations watching.

[9] Because of all your detestable acts, I will do to you what I have never done before and will never do again.

[10] Fathers will eat their sons in your streets, and sons will eat their fathers. I will pass sentence on you, and whatever remnant survives I will scatter to every wind.

[11] So as I live — declares the Lord GOD — because you defiled my sanctuary with all your vile things and all your abominations, I too will shave you down. My eye will not pity. I will not spare.

[12] A third of you will die by plague or starve to death inside your walls. A third will fall by the sword outside your walls. And a third I will scatter to every wind, and I will unsheathe a sword to hunt them.

[13] Then my anger will spend itself, my fury against them will settle, and I will be satisfied. And when my rage is exhausted on them, they will know that I, the LORD, spoke in my jealousy.

[14] I will make you a ruin and a mockery among the nations around you, in full view of everyone who passes by.

[15] You will become a taunt, a curse, a warning, a horror to the nations around you — when I execute judgments on you in anger, in fury, in scalding rebuke. I, the LORD, have spoken.

[16] When I loose against them the deadly arrows of famine — arrows meant to destroy, arrows I send to ruin you — I will pile famine on you and break your staff of bread.

[17] I will send famine against you, and wild beasts that will leave you childless. Plague and bloodshed will sweep through you, and I will bring the sword down on you. I, the LORD, have spoken.

[1] The word of the LORD hit me:

[2] Son of man, turn your face toward the mountains of Israel and prophesy against them.

[3] Say this: Mountains of Israel, hear the word of the Lord GOD. This is what the Lord GOD says to the mountains and the hills, to the ravines and the valleys — I am bringing a sword against you, and I will demolish your high places.

[4] Your altars will be rubble. Your incense stands will be smashed. I will throw your slain down in front of your filthy idols.

[5] I will scatter the corpses of Israel's children before their idols and strew your bones around your altars.

[6] Everywhere you live, the cities will be wreckage and the high places hollowed out — so your altars lie in ruins and stand condemned, so your idols are shattered and finished, so your incense stands are cut down and everything you made is wiped away.

[8] But I will leave some alive. A remnant will escape the sword among the nations when you are scattered across the lands.

[9] And those who escape will remember me out there among the nations where they were dragged into captivity — how I was broken by their whoring heart that turned from me, by their eyes that lusted after idols. They will loathe themselves for the evils they did, for every disgusting thing.

[10] Then they will know that I am the LORD — that I did not speak this disaster against them for nothing.

[11] This is what the Lord GOD says: Clap your hands, stamp your feet, and cry out — Ah! — over every wicked, vile thing the house of Israel has done. They will fall by sword, by famine, by plague.

[12] The one far off dies by plague. The one nearby falls by the sword. The one who survives and is left will starve to death. I will spend my fury on them.

[13] Then you will know that I am the LORD — when their slain lie scattered among their idols around their altars, on every high hill, on every mountaintop, under every green tree and every leafy oak — wherever they offered sweet aromas to all their idols.

[14] I will stretch out my hand against them and turn the land into desolation — a wasteland from the wilderness all the way to Diblah, wherever they live. Then they will know that I am the LORD.

[1] The word of the LORD hit me hard:

[2] Son of man, this is what the Lord GOD says to the land of Israel — The end. The end has come on the four corners of the land.

[3] The end is on you now. I am unleashing my anger on you. I will judge you by your own roads, lay every one of your filthy deeds at your feet.

[4] No pity from me. No sparing. I will pay you back for the way you have lived, and your obscenities will sit right in the middle of you — and then you will know I am the LORD.

[5] This is what the Lord GOD says: Disaster. One disaster — look, it is coming.

[6] The end is here. The end has arrived. It has woken up against you. Look — it has come.

[7] Your doom has come for you, you who live in this land. The time is here. The day is near — panic, not joyful shouting, on the mountains.

[8] Any moment now I pour out my fury on you, finish off my anger against you. I will judge you by your roads and lay every obscenity right at your feet.

[9] No pity. No sparing. I will give you back exactly what your roads have earned, and your obscenities will sit in the middle of you — and you will know it is I, the LORD, who strikes.

[10] Look — the day. Look — it is coming. Your doom has stepped out. The rod has bloomed. Arrogance has put out buds.

[11] Violence has grown up into a rod of wickedness. Nothing of them will remain — not their crowds, not their wealth, not anything that mattered to them.

[12] The time has come. The day has arrived. Let the buyer not celebrate, let the seller not mourn — because wrath is on the whole roaring crowd of them.

[13] The seller will never get back what he sold, even if they both live. The vision against this whole noisy mob will not be revoked. No one will hold onto his life through his own corruption.

[14] They blow the trumpet, ready everything — and no one goes to battle. My fury is on the whole crowd.

[15] The sword outside. Plague and famine inside. Whoever is in the field dies by the sword. Whoever is in the city — famine and plague will eat them.

[16] Survivors will escape into the mountains like doves of the valleys, all of them moaning, each one over his own guilt.

[17] Every hand will hang limp. Every knee will run like water.

[18] They will wrap themselves in sackcloth, shudder head to foot, every face shamed, every head shaved bare.

[19] They will fling their silver into the streets. Their gold will be filth to them. Their silver, their gold — none of it can rescue them on the day of the LORD's fury. It will not fill their bellies or feed their hunger, because it was the very thing that tripped them into ruin.

[20] They took the beauty of their ornaments and turned it into pride — used it to make their disgusting images, their loathsome things. So I am making it filth to them.

[21] I am handing it over to foreigners as loot, to the wicked of the earth as plunder, and they will desecrate it.

[22] I will turn my face away from them, and they will desecrate my treasured place. Raiders will walk in and defile it.

[23] Forge the chain — because the land is full of blood-crimes and the city is full of violence.

[24] I will bring in the worst of the nations and they will seize their houses. I will shut down the pride of the strong, and their holy places will be desecrated.

[25] Terror is coming. They will look for peace — there is none.

[26] Disaster on disaster. Rumor on rumor. They will hunt a vision from a prophet — but instruction will vanish from the priest, counsel from the elders.

[27] The king will mourn. The prince will dress in despair. The hands of the people of the land will tremble. I will do to them what they have done, judge them by their own standards — and they will know that I am the LORD.

[1] In the sixth year, sixth month, fifth day, I was sitting in my house with the elders of Judah seated before me, and the hand of the Sovereign LORD fell on me there.

[14] He brought me to the entrance of the north gate of the LORD's house. And there sat the women, weeping for Tammuz.

[15] He said, "Do you see this, son of man? Keep watching. You'll see worse."
