[17] So now, if it pleases the king, let a search be made in the royal archives in Babylon to see whether King Cyrus actually issued a decree to rebuild this house of God in Jerusalem. Then let the king send us his decision on the matter.

[1] So King Darius gave the order, and they searched the archives in Babylon where the treasures were stored.

[2] But it was at Ecbatana — the fortress in the province of Media — that they found the scroll. Here is what it said:

[3] In the first year of King Cyrus, Cyrus issued this decree concerning the house of God in Jerusalem: Let the house be rebuilt as a place where sacrifices are offered, and let its foundations be solidly laid. Its height: ninety feet. Its width: ninety feet.

[4] Three courses of massive stones, then a course of fresh timber. The cost comes from the royal treasury.

[5] And the gold and silver vessels of the house of God — the ones Nebuchadnezzar carried off from the temple in Jerusalem and hauled to Babylon — return them. Every piece back to its place in the temple in Jerusalem. Set them in the house of God.

[6] Therefore — Tattenai, governor of the province Beyond the River, and Shethar-bozenai, and your associates, the officials of that region — keep your distance.

[7] Leave the work on this house of God alone. Let the governor of the Jews and the elders of the Jews rebuild this house of God on its original site.

[8] And here is my further decree about what you must do for these elders of the Jews as they rebuild the house of God: the full cost of construction is to be paid to these men — promptly, no delay — from the royal treasury, from the tribute of the province Beyond the River.

[9] Whatever they need — young bulls, rams, lambs for burnt offerings to the God of heaven, wheat, salt, wine, oil — whatever the priests in Jerusalem request, give it to them. Every day. Without fail.

[10] So they may offer sacrifices pleasing to the God of heaven and pray for the life of the king and his sons.

[11] And I issue this decree as well: if anyone alters this order, a beam shall be ripped from his house, and he shall be impaled on it. His house shall be reduced to a pile of rubble.

[12] May the God who has caused his name to dwell there overthrow any king or people who lifts a hand to alter this — to destroy this house of God in Jerusalem. I, Darius, have spoken. Let it be done with precision.

[13] Then Tattenai the governor, Shethar-bozenai, and their associates carried it out — exactly, immediately — just as King Darius had ordered.

[14] And the elders of the Jews kept building, and the work prospered, propelled by the prophecy of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah son of Iddo. They finished the building by the command of the God of Israel and by decree of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes, kings of Persia.

[15] The house was completed on the third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of the reign of King Darius.

[16] Then the people of Israel — the priests, the Levites, the rest of the exiles who had returned — celebrated the dedication of the house of God with joy.

[17] For this dedication they offered a hundred bulls, two hundred rams, four hundred lambs, and as a sin offering for all Israel, twelve male goats — one for each tribe of Israel.

[18] They installed the priests in their divisions and the Levites in their orders for the service of God in Jerusalem, just as it is written in the book of Moses.

[19] On the fourteenth day of the first month, the exiles kept the Passover.

[20] The priests and Levites had purified themselves together — every one of them, clean. They slaughtered the Passover lamb for all the returned exiles, for their fellow priests, and for themselves.

[21] So the people of Israel who had come back from exile ate it — along with everyone who had separated themselves from the defilement of the surrounding nations to join them in seeking the LORD, the God of Israel.

[22] And they kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days, full of joy — because the LORD had turned the heart of the king of Assyria toward them, strengthening their hands in the work on the house of God, the God of Israel.

[1] Years passed. Then, during the reign of Artaxerxes king of Persia, Ezra stepped onto the stage — son of Seraiah, son of Azariah, son of Hilkiah, [2] son of Shallum, son of Zadok, son of Ahitub, [3] son of Amariah, son of Azariah, son of Meraioth, [4] son of Zerahiah, son of Uzzi, son of Bukki, [5] son of Abishua, son of Phinehas, son of Eleazar, son of Aaron the chief priest himself.

[6] This Ezra came up from Babylon. He was a scribe with the Law of Moses running through his veins — the Law that the LORD, the God of Israel, had given. And because the hand of the LORD his God rested on him, the king gave him everything he asked for.

[7] In the seventh year of King Artaxerxes, a fresh wave headed up to Jerusalem — Israelites, priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers, and temple servants. [8] Ezra reached Jerusalem in the fifth month of that seventh year. [9] He had launched out from Babylon on the first day of the first month and arrived in Jerusalem on the first day of the fifth month — the good hand of his God carrying him the whole way. [10] Because Ezra had set his heart like flint: to study the Law of the LORD, to live it, and to teach Israel its statutes and rulings.

[11] Here is the letter King Artaxerxes handed to Ezra the priest, the scribe — expert in the commandments and statutes the LORD had given Israel:

[12] "Artaxerxes, king of kings, to Ezra the priest, scholar of the Law of the God of heaven: greetings.

[13] "I hereby decree that any of the people of Israel in my realm — priests and Levites included — who wish to go to Jerusalem with you, may go. [14] You are sent by the king and his seven counselors to investigate Judah and Jerusalem according to the Law of your God, which is in your hand, [15] and to deliver the silver and gold that the king and his counselors have freely given to the God of Israel, whose dwelling is in Jerusalem — [16] along with all the silver and gold you can collect across the province of Babylon, together with the freewill offerings of the people and priests for the house of their God in Jerusalem.

[17] "With this money, move quickly: buy bulls, rams, lambs, with their grain and drink offerings, and offer them on the altar of the house of your God in Jerusalem. [18] Whatever is left over — do with it what seems right to you and your fellow priests, according to the will of your God.

[19] "The vessels entrusted to you for the service of the house of your God — deliver them in full before the God of Jerusalem. [20] And whatever else is needed for the house of your God that falls to you to provide, draw it from the royal treasury.

[21] "I, King Artaxerxes, hereby issue this order to every treasurer in the province Beyond the River: whatever Ezra the priest, scholar of the Law of the God of heaven, requests of you — give it to him. Promptly. [22] Up to a hundred talents of silver, a hundred kors of wheat, a hundred baths of wine, a hundred baths of oil, and salt without limit. [23] Whatever the God of heaven commands — let it be done with precision for the house of the God of heaven. Why should wrath fall on the realm of the king and his sons?

[24] "Be advised: no tax, tribute, or toll may be imposed on any priest, Levite, singer, gatekeeper, temple servant, or worker in this house of God.

[25] "And you, Ezra — according to the wisdom of your God that is in your hand — appoint magistrates and judges to govern all the people of the province Beyond the River, anyone who knows the laws of your God. And whoever does not know them — teach them. [26] Anyone who refuses to obey the law of your God and the law of the king — let justice be carried out without delay: death, banishment, confiscation of goods, or imprisonment."

[27] Blessed be the LORD, the God of our ancestors, who planted this purpose in the king's heart — to beautify the house of the LORD in Jerusalem — [28] and who extended steadfast love to me before the king, his counselors, and all his mighty officials. The hand of the LORD my God was on me, and I was strengthened. I gathered leaders from Israel to go up with me.

[1] Here are the heads of the ancestral houses who came up with me from Babylon during the reign of King Artaxerxes, traced by their family lines:

[2] From the sons of Phinehas — Gershom. From the sons of Ithamar — Daniel. From the sons of David — Hattush,

[3] of the sons of Shecaniah. From the sons of Parosh — Zechariah, and registered with him, one hundred fifty men.

[4] From the sons of Pahath-moab — Eliehoenai son of Zerahiah, and with him two hundred men.

[5] From the sons of Shecaniah — the son of Jahaziel, and with him three hundred men.

[6] From the sons of Adin — Ebed son of Jonathan, and with him fifty men.

[7] From the sons of Elam — Jeshaiah son of Athaliah, and with him seventy men.

[8] From the sons of Shephatiah — Zebadiah son of Michael, and with him eighty men.

[9] From the sons of Joab — Obadiah son of Jehiel, and with him two hundred eighteen men.

[10] From the sons of Bani — Shelomith son of Josiphiah, and with him one hundred sixty men.

[11] From the sons of Bebai — Zechariah son of Bebai, and with him twenty-eight men.

[12] From the sons of Azgad — Johanan son of Hakkatan, and with him one hundred ten men.

[13] From the sons of Adonikam — the last ones, named Eliphelet, Jeuel, and Shemaiah, and with them sixty men.

[14] From the sons of Bigvai — Uthai and Zaccur, and with them seventy men.

[15] I gathered them at the river that runs toward Ahava, and we camped there three days. When I scanned the people and the priests, I found no Levites among them — not one.

[16] So I sent for Eliezer, Ariel, Shemaiah, Elnathan, Jarib, Elnathan, Nathan, Zechariah, and Meshullam — leaders all — and for Joiarib and Elnathan, men of insight.

[17] I dispatched them to Iddo, the chief at the place called Casiphia. I put words in their mouths to say to Iddo and his kin, the temple servants at Casiphia: send us ministers for the house of our God.

[18] And because the good hand of our God was on us, they brought us a man of understanding from the sons of Mahli, son of Levi, son of Israel — Sherebiah, with his sons and brothers, eighteen in all.

[19] Also Hashabiah, and with him Jeshaiah of the sons of Merari, with his brothers and their sons — twenty in all.

[20] And from the temple servants, whom David and his officials had assigned to serve the Levites: two hundred twenty of them, every one named.

[21] There at the Ahava River I called a fast. We humbled ourselves before our God and asked him for safe passage — for ourselves, our children, and all we carried.

[22] I was ashamed to ask the king for soldiers and horsemen to guard us against bandits on the road. We had told him: the hand of our God rests in favor on everyone who seeks him, but his power and his anger fall on all who abandon him.

[23] So we fasted. We begged our God for this. And he heard us.

[24] Then I set apart twelve of the leading priests — Sherebiah, Hashabiah, and ten of their kin —

[25] and I weighed out to them the silver, the gold, and the vessels — the offering for the house of our God that the king, his counselors, his officers, and every Israelite present had contributed.

[26] Into their hands I weighed: six hundred fifty talents of silver, silver vessels worth one hundred talents, one hundred talents of gold,

[27] twenty gold bowls valued at a thousand darics, and two vessels of fine polished bronze, precious as gold.

[28] I told them: you are holy to the LORD. These vessels are holy. The silver and gold are a freewill offering to the LORD, the God of your fathers.

[29] Guard them. Watch them. Until you weigh them out in the chambers of the LORD's house in Jerusalem, before the chief priests and Levites and the heads of Israel's families.

[30] So the priests and Levites took charge of the weighed silver, gold, and vessels, to carry them to Jerusalem, to the house of our God.

[31] On the twelfth day of the first month we broke camp at the Ahava River and set out for Jerusalem. The hand of our God was on us. He rescued us from the grip of enemies and from ambush along the way.

[32] We reached Jerusalem and rested there three days.

[33] On the fourth day, in the house of our God, the silver, the gold, and the vessels were weighed into the hands of Meremoth son of Uriah the priest, with Eleazar son of Phinehas beside him, and with them the Levites Jozabad son of Jeshua and Noadiah son of Binnui.

[34] Everything counted. Everything weighed. Every gram recorded that very day.

[35] Then those who had come back from exile offered burnt sacrifices to the God of Israel: twelve bulls for all Israel, ninety-six rams, seventy-seven lambs, and twelve male goats as a sin offering — all of it a burnt offering to the LORD.

[36] They also delivered the king's commissions to the royal satraps and governors of the province Beyond the River, and these men gave their backing to the people and to the house of God.

[1] When all this was finished, the officials came to me with a report: "The people of Israel — including the priests and Levites — have not kept themselves separate from the surrounding peoples. They have absorbed the detestable practices of the Canaanites, Hittites, Perizzites, Jebusites, Ammonites, Moabites, Egyptians, and Amorites.

[2] They have taken these foreign women as wives for themselves and their sons. The holy seed has been mixed into the populations around them. And the leaders and officials have led the way in this betrayal."

[3] When I heard it, I ripped my tunic and my cloak, tore hair from my head and beard, and collapsed there, shattered.

[4] Everyone who trembled at the words of the God of Israel gathered around me — appalled by what the exiles had done. And I sat there, devastated, until the evening offering.

[5] At the time of the evening offering, I rose from my self-imposed grief — clothes still torn — and dropped to my knees, spreading my hands toward the LORD my God.

[6] I prayed: "My God, I am too ashamed and humiliated to lift my face to you, my God. Our sins have risen higher than our heads. Our guilt towers to the heavens.

[7] From the days of our ancestors until this very moment, our guilt has been immense. Because of our sins, we — our kings, our priests — have been handed over to foreign kings, to the sword, to captivity, to plunder, to public shame. And that is exactly where we stand today.

[8] But now, for a brief moment, the LORD our God has shown us grace. He left us a remnant. He gave us a foothold in his holy place. Our God lit up our eyes again and granted us a little relief in our slavery.

[9] Yes — we are slaves. But our God has not abandoned us in our slavery. He has extended his loyal love to us before the kings of Persia. He gave us breathing room — enough to rebuild the house of our God, to restore its ruins, to give us a wall of protection in Judah and Jerusalem.

[10] And now, our God — what can we possibly say after this? We have walked away from your commands —

[11] the commands you gave through your servants the prophets, when you said: 'The land you are entering to possess is a polluted land, defiled by the filth of its peoples — their detestable practices have saturated it from one end to the other with impurity.

[12] So do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons. Never seek peace or prosperity with them — so that you may grow strong, eat the good of the land, and leave it as an inheritance to your children forever.'

[13] After everything that has crashed down on us because of our evil deeds and our staggering guilt — even though you, our God, have punished us less than our sins deserved and have left us this remnant —

[14] are we really going to break your commands again? Are we going to intermarry with people whose practices are this corrupt? Would you not be furious enough to wipe us out entirely, leaving no remnant, no survivor?

[15] LORD, God of Israel — you are just. That is the only reason any of us are still here today, a survivor. Look at us — standing in your presence in our guilt. No one can stand before you like this."

[1] While Ezra was praying and confessing, weeping and throwing himself down before the house of God, a massive crowd gathered around him—men, women, children, all of Israel—and they wept with him, wept hard.

[2] Then Shecaniah son of Jehiel, of the line of Elam, spoke up to Ezra: "We've betrayed our God. We took foreign women from the peoples around us as wives. But even now, there's still hope for Israel.

[3] Let's cut a covenant with our God right now—send away every one of these women and the children born to them. Do it the way my lord counsels, the way those who tremble at God's command counsel. Do it by the book.

[4] Stand up. This is on you. We're with you. Be strong. Act."

[5] So Ezra rose. He made the chief priests, the Levites, and all Israel swear they would do exactly this. And they swore it.

[6] Then Ezra got up from in front of God's house and went to the chamber of Jehohanan son of Eliashib. He went in but ate no bread and drank no water—still in mourning over the exiles' faithlessness.

[7] Word went out through Judah and Jerusalem to every returned exile: assemble in Jerusalem.

[8] Anyone who failed to show within three days—by decision of the officials and elders—would forfeit all his property and be cut off from the assembly of the exiles.

[9] So every man of Judah and Benjamin gathered in Jerusalem within three days. It was the ninth month, the twentieth day. The whole population sat in the open square in front of God's house, shaking—both from the gravity of the moment and from the pouring rain.

[10] Ezra the priest stood and addressed them: "You have broken faith. You married foreign women and added to Israel's guilt.

[11] Now make confession to the LORD, the God of your fathers, and do his will. Separate yourselves from the peoples of the land and from the foreign wives."

[12] The whole assembly answered back, loud: "Yes. We'll do exactly what you say.

[13] But there are a lot of people here, and it's the rainy season—we can't just stand outside. And this isn't a one-day job, or two. We've sinned big on this one.

[14] Let our officials represent the whole assembly. Everyone in our towns who has married a foreign woman should come at appointed times, with the elders and judges of each town, until our God's burning anger over this matter is turned away from us."

[15] Only Jonathan son of Asahel and Jahzeiah son of Tikvah opposed this, with Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite backing them.

[16] But the returned exiles went ahead with the plan. Ezra the priest selected men—heads of families, each named by name—and on the first day of the tenth month they sat down to investigate.

[17] By the first day of the first month they had finished with every man who had married a foreign woman.

[18] Among the priests found to have married foreign women: from the sons of Jeshua son of Jozadak and his brothers—Maaseiah, Eliezer, Jarib, Gedaliah.

[19] They pledged on their hands to send their wives away, and as guilty men they offered a ram from the flock for their guilt.

[20] From the sons of Immer: Hanani and Zebadiah.

[21] From the sons of Harim: Maaseiah, Elijah, Shemaiah, Jehiel, Uzziah.

[22] From the sons of Pashhur: Elioenai, Maaseiah, Ishmael, Nethanel, Jozabad, Elasah.

[23] Among the Levites: Jozabad, Shimei, Kelaiah (also called Kelita), Pethahiah, Judah, Eliezer.

[24] Among the singers: Eliashib. Among the gatekeepers: Shallum, Telem, Uri.

[25] And from Israel in general— From the sons of Parosh: Ramiah, Izziah, Malchijah, Mijamin, Eleazar, Malchijah, Benaiah.

[26] From the sons of Elam: Mattaniah, Zechariah, Jehiel, Abdi, Jeremoth, Elijah.

[27] From the sons of Zattu: Elioenai, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Jeremoth, Zabad, Aziza.

[28] From the sons of Bebai: Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, Athlai.

[29] From the sons of Bani: Meshullam, Malluch, Adaiah, Jashub, Sheal, Jeremoth.

[30] From the sons of Pahath-Moab: Adna, Chelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, Binnui, Manasseh.

[31] From the sons of Harim: Eliezer, Isshijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon,

[32] Benjamin, Malluch, Shemariah.

[33] From the sons of Hashum: Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, Shimei.

[34] From the sons of Bani: Maadai, Amram, Uel,

[35] Benaiah, Bedeiah, Cheluhi,

[36] Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib,

[37] Mattaniah, Mattenai, Jaasu.

[38] From the sons of Binnui: Shimei,

[39] Shelemiah, Nathan, Adaiah,

[40] Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai,

[41] Azarel, Shelemiah, Shemariah,

[42] Shallum, Amariah, Joseph.

[43] From the sons of Nebo: Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jaddai, Joel, Benaiah.

[44] Every one of these had married foreign women, and some of them had had children with these women.

[1] Paul, an apostle—not from any human committee, not handed this commission by a man, but commissioned by Jesus the Messiah and God the Father, who raised him from the dead—

[2] along with every brother here with me, to the churches of Galatia:

[3] Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus the Messiah,

[4] who gave himself away for our sins to drag us out of this present evil age—exactly what our God and Father willed.

[5] To him belongs the glory, forever and ever. Amen.

[6] I am stunned. You are bailing—this fast—on the One who called you by the grace of the Messiah, and chasing a different gospel.

[7] Not that there is another one. There are just people unsettling you, trying to twist the gospel of the Messiah into something else.

[8] But listen: if we ourselves—or an angel straight from heaven—were to preach a gospel different from the one we preached to you, let him be damned.

[9] I said it before, I will say it again: if anyone preaches you a gospel other than the one you received, let him be damned.

[10] Does this sound like I am angling for human approval? Or God's? Am I trying to charm people? If I were still in the business of pleasing people, I would not be a servant of the Messiah.

[11] Let me make this clear, brothers: the gospel I preached is not a human product.

[12] I did not get it from any person. Nobody taught it to me. It came through a revelation of Jesus the Messiah.

[13] You have heard the story of my old life in Judaism—how viciously I went after God's church, how I tried to tear it apart.

[14] I was outpacing my peers in Judaism, racing past my own generation, fanatically devoted to the traditions of my ancestors.

[15] But then God—who had set me apart from the womb and called me by his grace—

[16] was pleased to reveal his Son in me, so that I would announce him among the nations. I did not run to consult flesh and blood.

[17] I did not head up to Jerusalem to meet the apostles who came before me. I went to Arabia instead. Then back to Damascus.

[18] Three years later, I finally went up to Jerusalem to meet Cephas. Stayed with him fifteen days.

[19] I saw none of the other apostles—only James, the Lord's brother.

[20] Before God, I am telling you the truth. I am not making this up.

[21] After that, I went off into the regions of Syria and Cilicia.

[22] The churches of Judea that belong to the Messiah—they did not even know my face.

[23] All they kept hearing was: "The man who used to hunt us down is now preaching the very faith he tried to destroy."

[24] And they glorified God because of me.

[1] Fourteen years later I went back up to Jerusalem, this time with Barnabas, and I brought Titus along too. [2] I went because of a revelation. I laid out for them the gospel I've been preaching among the Gentiles — privately, before the leaders — because I wanted to make sure I wasn't running, or hadn't run, for nothing. [3] And not even Titus, who was there with me, was forced to be circumcised, though he's Greek. [4] The issue came up because of infiltrators — false brothers who slipped in to spy on the freedom we have in Christ Jesus, looking to drag us back into slavery. [5] We didn't give them an inch. Not for a moment. We held the line so the truth of the gospel would stay intact for you.

[6] As for the so-called pillars — whatever they were makes no difference to me; God doesn't play favorites — they added nothing to my message. [7] On the contrary: they saw that I had been entrusted with the gospel for the uncircumcised, just as Peter had been entrusted with the gospel for the circumcised. [8] The same God who worked through Peter for the apostleship to the Jews worked through me for the Gentiles. [9] So James, Cephas, and John — the supposed pillars — recognized the grace given to me and shook hands on it with Barnabas and me. A partnership: we to the Gentiles, they to the Jews. [10] Their only request was that we remember the poor — which is exactly what I was already determined to do.

[11] But when Cephas came to Antioch, I stood up to him face to face, because he was dead wrong. [12] Here's what happened: before certain men arrived from James, he ate with the Gentiles. But the moment they showed up, he pulled back and separated himself, afraid of the circumcision crowd. [13] The other Jewish believers fell in line with his hypocrisy — even Barnabas got swept up in it. [14] When I saw they weren't walking straight toward the truth of the gospel, I told Cephas in front of everyone: "If you're a Jew and you've been living like a Gentile, how can you turn around and pressure Gentiles to live like Jews?"

[15] We're Jews by birth — not "Gentile sinners," as the saying goes — [16] yet we know that no one is set right with God by keeping the law. It happens through faith in Jesus Christ. So we put our faith in Christ Jesus, in order to be set right by faith in Christ and not by the works of the law. Because by works of the law no flesh will ever be justified.

[17] Now, if in seeking to be justified in Christ we ourselves turn out to be sinners, does that make Christ a servant of sin? Absolutely not. [18] But if I rebuild what I tore down, I prove myself a lawbreaker. [19] Through the law I died to the law — so that I could live to God. [20] I was crucified with Christ. I'm no longer the one living; Christ lives in me. The life I now live in this body, I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and handed himself over for me. [21] I refuse to nullify the grace of God. Because if righteousness comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing.

[1] You mindless Galatians — who put you under a spell? Jesus Christ was painted right in front of your eyes, crucified, and you saw it. [2] Answer me one question: did the Spirit come to you because you kept the law, or because you heard and believed? [3] Are you really this foolish? You started in the Spirit — and now you're trying to finish in the flesh? [4] Did you go through all that for nothing? Because it was for nothing, if this is where you end up. [5] When God pours out his Spirit on you and works miracles among you, what's the engine — your law-keeping, or your faith in what you heard?

[6] Think of Abraham: he trusted God, and that trust was counted as righteousness. [7] So understand this — the true children of Abraham are the people who live by faith. [8] Scripture saw it coming. It saw that God would set the nations right through faith, and it preached the gospel to Abraham ahead of time: "Through you, every nation will be blessed." [9] So everyone who lives by faith is blessed right alongside Abraham, the man who believed.

[10] But everyone banking on law-keeping is under a curse. Scripture is blunt: "Cursed is anyone who fails to do every single thing written in the book of the law." [11] No one gets right with God by law — that's obvious, because "the righteous will live by faith." [12] And law is not about faith — law says, "Do these things and you'll live by them." [13] Christ bought us out from under the curse of the law by becoming the curse for us — Scripture says, "Cursed is everyone hung on a tree" — [14] so that Abraham's blessing could spill out to the nations through Christ Jesus, and we could receive the promised Spirit through faith.

[15] Let me put this in human terms, brothers and sisters: once a person's will is ratified, no one can void it or add a codicil. [16] Now the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed. Notice — Scripture doesn't say "and to seeds," as if many, but "and to your seed," singular. That seed is Christ. [17] Here's my point: the law, which showed up four hundred and thirty years later, cannot annul a covenant God already ratified — it cannot cancel the promise. [18] Because if the inheritance comes by law, it no longer comes by promise — but God gave it to Abraham as a gift, through a promise.

[19] So what was the law for? It was added because of transgressions, a temporary measure until the Seed came — the one the promise was about. It was put in place through angels, by the hand of a mediator. [20] And a mediator implies more than one party — but God is one.

[21] So is the law fighting against God's promises? Absolutely not. If a law had been given that could actually generate life, then yes — righteousness would come through law. [22] But Scripture locked everything up under sin, so that what was promised — given through faith in Jesus Christ — could be handed to those who believe.

[23] Before this faith arrived, we were held in custody under the law, locked down, waiting for the faith that was about to be revealed. [24] So the law became our guardian leading us to Christ, so that we could be made right by faith. [25] But now that faith has come, we're no longer under that guardian. [26] You are all sons and daughters of God through faith in Christ Jesus. [27] Every one of you who was baptized into Christ has put Christ on like a garment. [28] There is no more Jew or Greek, no more slave or free, no more male and female — you are all one in Christ Jesus. [29] And if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed — heirs, just as the promise said.

[1] Here is what I mean: as long as the heir is a child, he's no different from a slave — even though he owns the whole estate. [2] He's under guardians and managers until the date his father set. [3] It's the same with us. When we were spiritually underage, we were enslaved to the elemental forces of this world. [4] But when the fullness of time arrived, God sent his Son — born of a woman, born under the law — [5] to buy out everyone trapped under the law, so we could be adopted as sons. [6] And because you are sons, God sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying out, "Abba! Father!" [7] So you're not a slave anymore — you're a son. And if a son, then an heir, through God.

[8] Back then, when you didn't know God, you were enslaved to things that aren't gods at all. [9] But now that you know God — or rather, now that God knows you — how can you turn back to those weak, bankrupt forces? Do you want to be enslaved all over again? [10] You're obsessing over sacred days, months, seasons, years. [11] I'm afraid for you. I'm afraid all my work on you was wasted.

[12] Brothers and sisters, I'm begging you — become like me, because I became like you. You haven't wronged me. [13] You remember: it was a physical illness that first brought me to you with the gospel. [14] And though my condition was a trial for you, you didn't despise me or recoil. You welcomed me as if I were an angel of God — as if I were Christ Jesus himself. [15] So where did all that joy go? I can testify — back then, if you could have, you'd have torn out your own eyes and handed them to me. [16] So now I'm your enemy? For telling you the truth?

[17] Those people are courting you, but not for any good reason. They want to shut you out so you'll have to court them back. [18] Being pursued for a good purpose is fine — anytime, not just when I'm with you. [19] My children — I'm in labor over you all over again, until Christ takes shape inside you. [20] I wish I were there with you right now, so I could change my tone. I'm at a loss with you.

[21] Tell me — you who want to live under the law — do you actually hear what the law says? [22] It's written: Abraham had two sons. One by the slave woman, one by the free woman. [23] The slave woman's son was born the ordinary way, by flesh. The free woman's son was born through promise. [24] This is an allegory. The two women are two covenants. One is Mount Sinai, birthing children into slavery — that's Hagar. [25] Hagar represents Mount Sinai in Arabia, and corresponds to the present Jerusalem, because she's in slavery with her children. [26] But the Jerusalem above is free — and she is our mother. [27] As Scripture says:

*Rejoice, barren woman who never gave birth!
Burst into song, you who never knew labor pains!
For the desolate woman has more children
than the one with a husband.*

[28] You, brothers and sisters, like Isaac, are children of promise. [29] And just as back then the son born by the flesh persecuted the son born by the Spirit — it's the same now. [30] But what does Scripture say? *Drive out the slave woman and her son. The slave woman's son will never share the inheritance with the free woman's son.* [31] So, brothers and sisters — we are not children of the slave. We are children of the free.

[1] Freedom — that is what Christ set you free for. So plant your feet. Do not let anyone strap that yoke of slavery back onto your shoulders.

[2] Hear me on this — I, Paul, am telling you: if you submit to circumcision, Christ becomes worthless to you.

[3] I will say it again to every man who goes under the knife: you have just signed up to keep the entire Law. All of it.

[4] You who think the Law can make you right with God — you have severed yourself from Christ. You have fallen clean out of grace.

[5] But we — we wait for the righteousness we hope for, and we wait by the Spirit, through faith.

[6] In Christ Jesus, circumcision means nothing. Uncircumcision means nothing. What counts is faith working itself out through love.

[7] You were running so well. Who cut in on you and made you stop trusting the truth?

[8] That kind of persuasion did not come from the One who called you.

[9] A little yeast works its way through the whole batch of dough.

[10] Still, I am confident in the Lord that you will see this my way. And the one stirring you up — whoever he is — will answer for it.

[11] As for me, brothers — if I am still preaching circumcision, why am I still being hunted? If that were my message, the offense of the cross would have evaporated.

[12] Honestly? I wish the ones unsettling you would go the whole way and castrate themselves.

[13] You were called to freedom, brothers. Just do not turn that freedom into a license for the flesh. Instead — through love — make yourselves servants to one another.

[14] The whole Law gets folded up into a single sentence: Love your neighbor as yourself.

[15] But if you keep biting and devouring each other, watch out — you will consume one another whole.

[16] Here is what I am saying: walk by the Spirit, and you will not give the flesh what it craves.

[17] Because the flesh wants what is against the Spirit, and the Spirit wants what is against the flesh. They are locked in opposition — which is why you cannot just do whatever you feel like doing.

[18] But if the Spirit leads you, the Law is no longer over you.

[19] The works of the flesh? Obvious. Sexual immorality, filth, unbridled appetite,

[20] idol-worship, sorcery, hatreds, fights, jealousy, rage, selfish ambition, factions, splinter groups,

[21] envy, drunkenness, wild parties — and the list goes on. I am warning you now, like I warned you before: people who live this way will not inherit God's kingdom.

[22] But the Spirit grows fruit: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness,

[23] gentleness, self-control. No law stands against any of this.

[24] And those who belong to Christ Jesus have crucified the flesh — with its passions and cravings nailed up there with it.

[25] If the Spirit is our life, then let the Spirit set our pace.

[26] No more strutting. No more provoking each other. No more envy.

[1] Family — if someone gets caught in a sin, you who walk by the Spirit, restore that person. Gently. And watch yourself, because you could be next.

[2] Carry each other's heavy loads — this is how you fulfill what Christ asked of you.

[3] Anyone who thinks they're somebody when they're nobody is fooling themselves.

[4] Test your own work. Then whatever pride you have will be earned, measured against yourself — not against someone else.

[5] Each person carries their own pack.

[6] If someone is teaching you the word, share everything good you have with them.

[7] Don't kid yourself. God isn't mocked. Whatever a person plants is exactly what they harvest.

[8] Plant in the soil of your own appetites and you'll harvest decay. Plant in the soil of the Spirit and you'll harvest life that never ends.

[9] Don't get tired of doing what's right. The harvest comes if we don't quit.

[10] So whenever you get the chance, do good to everyone — especially to the family of faith.

[11] Look at the size of these letters I'm writing you with my own hand.

[12] The ones pressuring you to get circumcised — they're just trying to look impressive in the flesh. They want to dodge the persecution that comes with the cross of Christ.

[13] Even the circumcised ones don't actually keep the law. They want you cut so they can brag about your body.

[14] As for me — I will never brag about anything except the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ. Through that cross, the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world.

[15] Circumcision means nothing. Uncircumcision means nothing. What matters is new creation.

[16] Peace and mercy on everyone who lines up with this rule — on the true Israel of God.

[17] From now on, let no one give me grief. I carry the scars of Jesus on my body.

[18] The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit, brothers and sisters. Amen.

[1] In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.

[2] The earth was chaos — formless, empty, drowned in darkness. And the Spirit of God hovered over the face of the deep waters like a bird brooding over its nest.

[3] God spoke: "Light." And light blazed into being.

[4] God saw the light — it was good. He drew a line between the light and the dark.

[5] He named the light Day and the dark Night. Evening came, then morning. Day one.

[6] God spoke: "Let there be a vault in the middle of the waters, splitting waters from waters."

[7] So God built the vault, parting the waters below from the waters above. And it held.

[8] He named the vault Sky. Evening came, then morning. Day two.

[9] God spoke: "Let the waters under the sky gather into one place — let dry ground appear." And it happened.

[10] He named the dry ground Land and the gathered waters Seas. God saw it — good.

[11] God spoke: "Let the land sprout green — seed-bearing plants and fruit trees of every kind, each carrying its own seed within itself." And it happened.

[12] The land erupted in green — plants seeding themselves, trees bearing fruit packed with seed, every kind reproducing its own. God saw it — good.

[13] Evening came, then morning. Day three.

[14] God spoke: "Let lights ignite across the dome of the sky to divide day from night — let them mark the seasons, the days, the years.

[15] Let them shine down on the earth." And it happened.

[16] God forged two great lights — the greater to govern the day, the lesser to govern the night — and the stars.

[17] He set them in the dome of the sky to pour light on the earth,

[18] to rule day and night, to split light from dark. God saw it — good.

[19] Evening came, then morning. Day four.

[20] God spoke: "Let the waters swarm with living things, and let birds soar across the dome of the sky."

[21] So God created the great sea-monsters and every creature that moves and teems through the waters — every kind — and every winged bird — every kind. God saw it — good.

[22] He blessed them: "Be fruitful. Multiply. Fill the seas. Let birds multiply on the earth."

[23] Evening came, then morning. Day five.

[24] God spoke: "Let the land bring forth living creatures of every kind — livestock, crawling things, wild animals — each after its own kind." And it happened.

[25] God made the wild animals, the livestock, every creature that creeps along the ground — each after its kind. God saw it — good.

[26] Then God said: "Let us make humanity in our image, after our likeness. Let them rule the fish of the sea, the birds of the sky, the livestock, the whole earth, and every creature that moves across it."

[27] So God created humanity in his image —
in the image of God he created them —
male and female he created them.

[28] God blessed them and said: "Be fruitful. Multiply. Fill the earth and master it. Rule the fish of the sea, the birds of the sky, every living thing that moves on the earth."

[29] God said: "Look — I'm giving you every seed-bearing plant on the face of the whole earth, and every tree with seed-bearing fruit. They are yours to eat.

[30] And to every wild animal, every bird of the sky, every creature that crawls — everything with the breath of life in it — I give every green plant for food." And it happened.

[31] God looked over everything he had made — and it was very good. Evening came, then morning. Day six.

[1] So the skies and the earth were finished, every last detail in place. [2] By the seventh day God had completed the work he set out to do, and on that seventh day he rested from all of it. [3] He blessed the seventh day and set it apart as sacred, because on it he rested from the work of creation he had been doing.

[4] This is the story of how the skies and the earth came to be, on the day the LORD God made earth and sky.

[5] No shrub had yet broken through the soil, no grain stood in the fields — the LORD God had not yet sent rain, and there was no one to work the ground. [6] But a mist rose up from the earth and drenched the whole surface of the soil. [7] Then the LORD God formed the man from the dust of the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life — and the man became a living soul.

[8] The LORD God planted a garden in the east, in Eden, and there he placed the man he had formed. [9] From the soil he made every tree grow — beautiful to look at, good to eat from. And in the middle of the garden stood the tree of life, and the tree of the knowledge of good and evil.

[10] A river flowed out of Eden to water the garden, and from there it split into four headwaters. [11] The first is the Pishon, winding through the whole land of Havilah, where there is gold — [12] and the gold of that land is good. Bdellium is there, and onyx stone. [13] The second river is the Gihon, winding through the whole land of Cush. [14] The third is the Tigris, running east of Asshur. And the fourth is the Euphrates.

[15] The LORD God took the man and settled him in the garden of Eden to tend it and watch over it. [16] And the LORD God gave the man this command: "From every tree in the garden you are free to eat. [17] But the tree of the knowledge of good and evil — from that one you must not eat. The day you eat from it, you will surely die."

[18] Then the LORD God said, "It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make him a partner who matches him." [19] So the LORD God formed from the ground every animal of the field and every bird of the sky, and brought them to the man to see what he would call them. Whatever the man called each living creature, that became its name. [20] The man named every domestic animal, every bird of the sky, every wild creature. But for the man himself, no partner was found to match him.

[21] So the LORD God sent the man into a deep sleep, and while he slept took one of his ribs and closed up the flesh in its place. [22] From the rib he had taken out of the man, the LORD God built a woman, and brought her to the man.

[23] And the man said:
"This — at last — is bone of my bone
and flesh of my flesh.
She will be called Woman,
because she was taken out of Man."

[24] This is why a man leaves his father and mother and clings to his wife, and the two become one flesh.

[25] The man and his wife were both naked, and they felt no shame.

[1] Now the serpent was the most cunning of all the wild creatures the LORD God had made. He slid up to the woman: "Did God actually say you can't eat from any tree in the garden?"

[2] The woman answered, "We can eat from the trees of the garden.

[3] It's just the tree in the middle — God said, 'Don't eat from it, don't even touch it, or you'll die.'"

[4] "You won't die," the serpent hissed.

[5] "God knows the moment you eat from it your eyes will fly open and you'll be like God — knowing good, knowing evil."

[6] The woman looked. The tree was good for food. It was a feast to the eyes. It promised wisdom. She took. She ate. She handed some to her husband, right there beside her, and he ate too.

[7] And their eyes did open — and what they saw was themselves, naked. They stitched fig leaves together and made coverings.

[8] Then they heard him — the LORD God, walking through the garden in the cool of the day. And they hid. The man and his wife crouched among the trees, hiding from the face of the LORD God.

[9] The LORD God called to the man: "Where are you?"

[10] "I heard you in the garden," he answered, "and I was afraid because I was naked. So I hid."

[11] "Who told you you were naked? Did you eat from the tree I commanded you not to eat from?"

[12] The man said, "The woman — the one you gave to be with me — she handed me the fruit, and I ate."

[13] The LORD God turned to the woman: "What have you done?" She said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."

[14] Then the LORD God said to the serpent: "Because you have done this, cursed are you above every animal, above every wild creature. On your belly you will crawl. Dust you will eat, all the days of your life.

[15] I will put hostility between you and the woman, between your offspring and hers. He will crush your head, and you will strike his heel."

[16] To the woman he said: "I will multiply your pain in childbearing. In agony you will bring forth children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you."

[17] To the man he said: "Because you listened to your wife and ate from the tree I forbade you — cursed is the ground because of you. In painful toil you will eat from it all the days of your life.

[18] Thorns and thistles it will throw up at you, and you will eat the plants of the field.

[19] By the sweat of your face you will eat bread, until you return to the ground — for from it you were taken. Dust you are, and to dust you will return."

[20] The man named his wife Eve, because she would become the mother of all the living.

[21] And the LORD God made garments of skin for the man and his wife, and clothed them.

[22] Then the LORD God said: "Look — the human has become like one of us, knowing good and evil. Now what if he reaches out his hand, takes from the tree of life, eats, and lives forever?"

[23] So the LORD God expelled him from the garden of Eden, to work the ground from which he had been taken.

[24] He drove the man out. And east of the garden of Eden he stationed cherubim and a flashing, whirling sword — to guard the way to the tree of life.

[1] Adam knew Eve, his wife, and she conceived. She gave birth to Cain and said, "I have brought forth a man — with the LORD's help." [2] Later she gave birth to his brother Abel. Abel kept the flocks. Cain worked the soil.

[3] In time, Cain brought an offering to the LORD from the produce of the ground. [4] Abel also brought one — the choicest portions from the firstborn of his flock. The LORD looked with favor on Abel and his offering, [5] but on Cain and his offering he did not look. Cain burned with anger. His face fell.

[6] The LORD said to Cain, "Why are you burning? Why has your face fallen? [7] If you do what is right, won't you be lifted up? But if you don't — sin is crouching at the door. It wants you. You have to master it."

[8] Cain said to his brother Abel, "Let's go out to the field." And while they were there, Cain rose against his brother and killed him.

[9] The LORD said to Cain, "Where is your brother Abel?"

"I don't know," he said. "Am I my brother's keeper?"

[10] The LORD said, "What have you done? Listen — your brother's blood is screaming to me from the ground. [11] Now you are cursed, driven from this soil that opened its mouth to drink your brother's blood from your hand. [12] When you work the ground, it will no longer yield to you. You will be a restless wanderer on the earth."

[13] Cain said to the LORD, "My punishment is more than I can carry. [14] You are driving me off the land today, hiding me from your presence. I'll be a restless wanderer, and whoever finds me will kill me."

[15] The LORD said to him, "No — anyone who kills Cain will suffer vengeance seven times over." And the LORD placed a mark on Cain so that no one who found him would strike him down.

[16] Cain went out from the LORD's presence and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

[17] Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to Enoch. Cain was building a city at the time, and he named it after his son — Enoch. [18] Enoch fathered Irad. Irad fathered Mehujael. Mehujael fathered Methushael. Methushael fathered Lamech.

[19] Lamech took two wives — one named Adah, the other Zillah. [20] Adah gave birth to Jabal; he was the father of those who live in tents and raise livestock. [21] His brother was Jubal; he was the father of all who play the harp and pipe. [22] Zillah also bore a son — Tubal-Cain, who forged every kind of tool from bronze and iron. His sister was Naamah.

[23] Lamech said to his wives:

"Adah and Zillah, hear my voice.
Wives of Lamech, listen to what I say:
I have killed a man for wounding me,
a young man for striking me.
[24] If Cain is avenged seven times,
then Lamech — seventy-seven."

[25] Adam knew his wife again, and she gave birth to a son. She named him Seth, saying, "God has appointed me another offspring in place of Abel, since Cain killed him." [26] Seth also had a son, and he named him Enosh.

That was when people began to call on the name of the LORD.

[1] This is the record of Adam's line. The day God created humankind, he made them in the likeness of God. [2] Male and female he created them, blessed them, and named them Humanity on the day of their making.

[3] Adam lived 130 years and fathered a son in his own likeness, after his own image, and named him Seth. [4] After Seth's birth Adam lived another 800 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [5] All told, Adam's life ran 930 years. Then he died.

[6] Seth lived 105 years and fathered Enosh. [7] After Enosh he lived another 807 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [8] Seth's life came to 912 years. Then he died.

[9] Enosh lived 90 years and fathered Kenan. [10] After Kenan he lived another 815 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [11] Enosh's life came to 905 years. Then he died.

[12] Kenan lived 70 years and fathered Mahalalel. [13] After Mahalalel he lived another 840 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [14] Kenan's life came to 910 years. Then he died.

[15] Mahalalel lived 65 years and fathered Jared. [16] After Jared he lived another 830 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [17] Mahalalel's life came to 895 years. Then he died.

[18] Jared lived 162 years and fathered Enoch. [19] After Enoch he lived another 800 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [20] Jared's life came to 962 years. Then he died.

[21] Enoch lived 65 years and fathered Methuselah. [22] After Methuselah, Enoch walked with God for 300 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [23] Enoch's life came to 365 years. [24] Enoch walked with God — and then he was gone, because God took him.

[25] Methuselah lived 187 years and fathered Lamech. [26] After Lamech he lived another 782 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [27] Methuselah's life came to 969 years. Then he died.

[28] Lamech lived 182 years and fathered a son. [29] He named him Noah, saying, "This one will bring us relief from our labor, from the painful work of our hands on the ground the LORD has cursed." [30] After Noah, Lamech lived another 595 years and fathered more sons and daughters. [31] Lamech's life came to 777 years. Then he died.

[32] Noah was 500 years old when he fathered Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

[1] Humanity multiplied across the face of the earth, and daughters were born to them.

[2] The sons of God saw the daughters of men — saw that they were beautiful — and took as wives any they chose.

[3] Then the LORD said: "My Spirit will not contend with humanity forever. They are flesh. Their days are numbered: one hundred twenty years."

[4] The Nephilim walked the earth in those days — and after — when the sons of God went in to the daughters of men and they bore them children. These were the mighty ones of old, the men whose names still echo.

[5] The LORD looked at what humanity had become and saw it everywhere: a species drenched in evil, every thought of every heart bent toward ruin, all day, every day.

[6] And the LORD grieved that he had made them. The pain of it cut him to the heart.

[7] So the LORD said: "I will wipe them from the face of the earth — every human I created, and the animals with them, the things that crawl, the birds in the sky. I am sorry I made them."

[8] But Noah — Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.

[9] This is the story of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, blameless among his generation. Noah walked with God.

[10] He fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

[11] The earth was rotten in God's sight, swollen with violence.

[12] God looked at the earth — and there it was, ruined. Every living thing had corrupted its way.

[13] So God said to Noah: "I have decided to end all flesh. Violence fills the earth because of them. I am going to destroy them — and the earth with them.

[14] Build yourself an ark of cypress wood. Make rooms inside it. Seal it with pitch, inside and out.

[15] These are the dimensions: three hundred cubits long, fifty wide, thirty high.

[16] Build a roof, leaving a cubit's gap at the top. Put the door in the side. Three decks — lower, middle, upper.

[17] Listen — I am bringing a flood of water over the earth, to destroy every living thing under the sky that has the breath of life in it. Everything on the earth will die.

[18] But I will establish my covenant with you. You will enter the ark — you, your sons, your wife, your sons' wives — together.

[19] Of every living creature, bring two of each into the ark to keep them alive with you. Male and female.

[20] Birds of every kind, animals of every kind, every creeping thing of the ground — two of each will come to you, to live.

[21] And take with you every kind of food that is eaten. Store it. It will be food for you and for them."

[22] Noah did it. Everything God commanded him — he did it.

[1] Then the LORD said to Noah, "Get into the ark — you and your whole household. You're the one I've seen as righteous in this generation, the only one.

[2] Take seven pairs of every clean animal, male and mate, and one pair of every unclean animal, male and mate.

[3] And seven pairs of every bird in the sky too, male and female — keep their kinds alive across the face of the earth.

[4] Because in seven days I'm opening the sky. Forty days. Forty nights. Rain that doesn't stop. Every living thing I made — I'm wiping it off the ground."

[5] Noah did it. Everything the LORD told him, exactly.

[6] He was six hundred years old when the flood came — water surging over the earth.

[7] Noah went in. His sons with him, his wife, his sons' wives — into the ark, ahead of the waters.

[8] The clean animals and the unclean, the birds and everything that crawls the ground —

[9] they came to Noah, into the ark, two by two, male and female, just as God had commanded.

[10] Seven days passed. Then the floodwaters hit the earth.

[11] In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, the seventeenth day of the second month — that day — the deep cracked open. Every fountain of the great abyss burst up from below. The floodgates of the sky tore loose from above.

[12] Forty days. Forty nights. Rain.

[13] On that very day Noah walked into the ark — Noah, his sons Shem, Ham, and Japheth, his wife, his three daughters-in-law — all of them together.

[14] And with them every wild animal by its kind, every herd animal by its kind, every creature that crawls the ground by its kind, every bird by its kind — everything with wings.

[15] They came to Noah, into the ark, two by two — every creature with the breath of life in it.

[16] Male and female of every kind, exactly as God had commanded. Then the LORD shut him in.

[17] The flood ran forty days across the earth. The waters rose, lifted the ark, raised it high off the ground.

[18] The waters surged. They piled higher and higher. The ark drifted on the surface.

[19] Higher still. The waters overwhelmed the earth — every high mountain under the whole sky vanished beneath them.

[20] Twenty feet above the peaks — that's how deep. The mountains were gone.

[21] And everything that moved on the earth died — birds, herds, wild animals, the swarming creatures that swarmed the ground, and every human being.

[22] Everything with the breath of life in its nostrils, everything that lived on dry land — dead.

[23] He wiped out every living thing on the face of the earth — human, animal, crawling creature, bird of the sky. All erased. Only Noah was left, and those with him in the ark.

[24] And the waters held the earth under for a hundred and fifty days.

[1] But God remembered Noah — every wild thing, every animal in the ark with him. He sent a wind sweeping across the earth, and the waters began to drop. [2] The deep's springs sealed shut. The sky's floodgates closed. The downpour from heaven stopped. [3] The waters pulled back from the land, steady, slow — and after a hundred fifty days they had dropped low. [4] On the seventeenth day of the seventh month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat. [5] The waters kept falling until the tenth month. On the first day of that month, the mountaintops broke the surface.

[6] Forty days later, Noah opened the window he had built into the ark [7] and released a raven. It flew out, back and forth, until the waters had dried from the earth. [8] Then he sent out a dove to see if the waters had eased off the ground. [9] But the dove found no place to set her foot. She came back to him in the ark — water still covered everything. He reached out, took her, and brought her in. [10] He waited another seven days and sent the dove out again. [11] She returned to him at evening — and there in her beak: a freshly plucked olive leaf. That was how Noah knew the waters had drained from the earth. [12] He waited seven more days and sent her out once more. This time she did not come back.

[13] In Noah's six hundred and first year, on the first day of the first month, the waters were gone from the earth. Noah pulled back the covering of the ark and looked — and the ground was dry. [14] By the twenty-seventh day of the second month, the earth was completely dry.

[15] Then God spoke to Noah: [16] "Come out of the ark — you, your wife, your sons, your sons' wives. [17] Bring out every living thing with you — birds, animals, every creature that moves along the ground. Let them swarm the earth, be fruitful, multiply across it." [18] So Noah came out, with his sons and his wife and his sons' wives. [19] Every animal, every creeping thing, every bird — everything that moves on the earth — came out of the ark by its kinds.

[20] Then Noah built an altar to the LORD. He took some of every clean animal, every clean bird, and offered burnt offerings on it. [21] The LORD breathed in the pleasing aroma, and said in his heart: *Never again. Never again will I curse the ground because of humanity — even though the bent of the human heart is toward evil from the start. Never again will I strike down every living thing the way I have just done.*

[22] *As long as the earth endures:
seedtime and harvest,
cold and heat,
summer and winter,
day and night —
they will not cease.*

[1] God blessed Noah and his sons. He told them: "Be fruitful. Multiply. Fill the earth.

[2] Every animal on land, every bird in the sky, everything that moves on the ground, every fish in the sea — they will live in dread of you. They are placed in your hand.

[3] Every living thing that moves is now your food. I give it all to you, as freely as I once gave you the green plants.

[4] But one rule: do not eat flesh with its life still in it — that is, with its blood.

[5] And for your own blood — the blood of your lives — I will demand a reckoning. From every animal I will demand it. From every human being I will demand it. If anyone takes a human life, I will require that life back.

[6] Whoever sheds human blood, by humans shall their blood be shed — because humanity was made in the image of God.

[7] As for you: be fruitful, multiply, swarm across the earth, multiply on it."

[8] Then God spoke to Noah and his sons:

[9] "Listen — I am establishing my covenant with you, and with your descendants after you,

[10] and with every living creature that came out of the ark with you — the birds, the livestock, every wild animal — every living thing on earth.

[11] Here is my covenant: never again will all life be wiped out by floodwaters. Never again will a flood destroy the earth.

[12] This," God said, "is the sign of the covenant I am making between me and you and every living creature with you — a covenant for all generations to come, forever:

[13] I have set my bow in the clouds. It will be the sign of the covenant between me and the earth.

[14] When I gather clouds over the earth and the bow appears in them,

[15] I will remember my covenant — the one between me and you and every living creature of every kind. Never again will the waters become a flood to destroy all flesh.

[16] When the bow is in the clouds, I will see it and remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of every kind on the earth."

[17] God said to Noah: "This is the sign of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh on the earth."

[18] The sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth. Ham was the father of Canaan.

[19] These three were Noah's sons, and from them the whole earth was repopulated.

[20] Noah, a man of the soil, was the first to plant a vineyard.

[21] He drank the wine, got drunk, and lay naked inside his tent.

[22] Ham, the father of Canaan, saw his father's nakedness and went outside and told his two brothers.

[23] Shem and Japheth took a cloak, laid it across both their shoulders, walked in backward, and covered their father. Their faces were turned away. They did not see him exposed.

[24] When Noah woke from his wine and learned what his youngest son had done to him,

[25] he said: "Cursed be Canaan. The lowest of slaves he will be to his brothers."

[26] And he said: "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem. May Canaan be his slave.

[27] May God enlarge Japheth — let him live in the tents of Shem. And may Canaan be his slave."

[28] After the flood, Noah lived three hundred and fifty years.

[29] All his days came to nine hundred and fifty years. Then he died.

[1] Here is the family tree of Noah's sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — and the sons born to them after the flood.

[2] Japheth's line: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, Tiras.

[3] Gomer's sons: Ashkenaz, Riphath, Togarmah.

[4] Javan's sons: Elishah, Tarshish, the Kittim, the Dodanim.

[5] From these the coastland peoples spread out across their territories — each clan with its own tongue, its own land, its own nation.

[6] Ham's line: Cush, Egypt, Put, Canaan.

[7] Cush's sons: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, Sabteca. Raamah's sons: Sheba and Dedan.

[8] Cush also fathered Nimrod, the first man on earth to wield real power.

[9] He was a hunter without equal, fierce before the LORD himself — which is why people still say, "Like Nimrod, a hunter without equal before the LORD."

[10] His kingdom began with Babel, Erech, Akkad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.

[11] From there he pushed into Assyria and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah,

[12] and Resen — the great city — between Nineveh and Calah.

[13] Egypt fathered the Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim,

[14] Pathrusim, Casluhim (the Philistines came from there), and Caphtorim.

[15] Canaan fathered Sidon, his firstborn, then Heth,

[16] then the Jebusites, the Amorites, the Girgashites,

[17] the Hivites, the Arkites, the Sinites,

[18] the Arvadites, the Zemarites, the Hamathites. Eventually the Canaanite clans scattered,

[19] and their territory stretched from Sidon down toward Gerar as far as Gaza, then east toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, reaching to Lasha.

[20] These are Ham's descendants — by clan, by language, by land, by nation.

[21] Shem also had sons. He was Japheth's older brother and the father of every descendant of Eber.

[22] Shem's line: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, Aram.

[23] Aram's sons: Uz, Hul, Gether, Mash.

[24] Arpachshad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.

[25] Eber had two sons. One was named Peleg — "division" — because in his days the earth was split apart. His brother was Joktan.

[26] Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,

[27] Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,

[28] Obal, Abimael, Sheba,

[29] Ophir, Havilah, Jobab — all sons of Joktan.

[30] Their land ran from Mesha toward Sephar, into the hill country of the east.

[31] These are Shem's descendants — by clan, by language, by land, by nation.

[32] These were the clans of Noah's sons, traced through their generations, nation by nation. From them the nations of the earth spread out after the flood.

[1] Once, the whole earth shared one tongue, one set of words.

[2] As people drifted east, they came upon a plain in the land of Shinar and settled there.

[3] They said to each other, "Come on — let's mold bricks and fire them hard." Bricks became their stone, tar their mortar.

[4] Then they said, "Come on — let's build ourselves a city, with a tower whose top punches into the sky. Let's make a name for ourselves, so we're not scattered across the face of the earth."

[5] The Lord came down to see the city and the tower these human beings were building.

[6] And the Lord said, "Look at them — one people, one language, and this is only the beginning. Nothing they set their minds to will be beyond their reach.

[7] Come — let us go down and tangle their language, so they can't understand a word the other is saying."

[8] So the Lord scattered them from there across the face of the whole earth, and the city sat unfinished.

[9] That is why it is called Babel — because there the Lord babbled the language of the whole earth, and from there the Lord scattered them across the face of all the earth.

[10] This is the line of Shem. Shem was a hundred years old when he fathered Arpachshad, two years after the flood.

[11] After Arpachshad was born, Shem lived another five hundred years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[12] Arpachshad was thirty-five when he fathered Shelah.

[13] After Shelah, Arpachshad lived another four hundred and three years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[14] Shelah was thirty when he fathered Eber.

[15] After Eber, Shelah lived another four hundred and three years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[16] Eber was thirty-four when he fathered Peleg.

[17] After Peleg, Eber lived another four hundred and thirty years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[18] Peleg was thirty when he fathered Reu.

[19] After Reu, Peleg lived another two hundred and nine years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[20] Reu was thirty-two when he fathered Serug.

[21] After Serug, Reu lived another two hundred and seven years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[22] Serug was thirty when he fathered Nahor.

[23] After Nahor, Serug lived another two hundred years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[24] Nahor was twenty-nine when he fathered Terah.

[25] After Terah, Nahor lived another hundred and nineteen years and fathered more sons and daughters.

[26] Terah was seventy when he fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran.

[27] This is the line of Terah. Terah fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran. Haran fathered Lot.

[28] Haran died in the lifetime of his father Terah, in the land of his birth — Ur of the Chaldeans.

[29] Abram and Nahor both took wives. Abram's wife was Sarai. Nahor's wife was Milcah, daughter of Haran — the father of Milcah and Iscah.

[30] Sarai was barren. She had no child.

[31] Terah gathered his son Abram, his grandson Lot (Haran's boy), and his daughter-in-law Sarai (Abram's wife), and they set out from Ur of the Chaldeans, bound for the land of Canaan. But when they reached Haran, they settled there.

[32] Terah lived two hundred and five years, and he died in Haran.

[1] The LORD said to Abram: Leave. Walk away from your country, from your kin, from your father's house — go to the land I will show you.

[2] I will make you into a great nation. I will bless you. I will make your name weighty, and you yourself will be a blessing.

[3] Those who bless you, I will bless. Whoever curses you, I will curse. And through you, every family on earth will find its blessing.

[4] So Abram went, just as the LORD had told him, and Lot went with him. Abram was seventy-five when he walked out of Haran.

[5] He took Sarai his wife, Lot his nephew, everything they had accumulated, and every person they had gathered in Haran — and they set out for Canaan. They arrived in Canaan.

[6] Abram crossed the land as far as Shechem, to the oak of Moreh. The Canaanites were already there, holding the country.

[7] The LORD appeared to Abram and said: To your offspring I will give this land. So Abram built an altar there to the LORD, who had shown himself to him.

[8] From there he moved on to the hill country east of Bethel and pitched his tent — Bethel to the west, Ai to the east. He built another altar to the LORD and called on his name.

[9] Then Abram pulled up stakes again, journeying south, stage by stage, toward the Negev.

[10] Famine struck the land. It hit hard. So Abram went down to Egypt to wait it out as a foreigner.

[11] As he was about to enter Egypt, he said to Sarai his wife: Listen — I know how beautiful you are.

[12] The moment the Egyptians see you, they'll say, "That's his wife," and they'll kill me and keep you.

[13] So tell them you're my sister. Then it'll go well for me because of you, and I'll stay alive.

[14] Sure enough, when Abram entered Egypt, the Egyptians saw the woman — saw she was stunning.

[15] Pharaoh's officials saw her, raved about her to Pharaoh, and the woman was taken into Pharaoh's palace.

[16] Pharaoh treated Abram well on her account — he ended up with sheep, cattle, donkeys, male and female servants, female donkeys, camels.

[17] But the LORD struck Pharaoh and his household with devastating plagues because of Sarai, Abram's wife.

[18] Pharaoh summoned Abram. What have you done to me? he demanded. Why didn't you tell me she was your wife?

[19] Why did you say, "She's my sister," so that I took her as my own? Here — take her. Go.

[20] Pharaoh gave his men orders, and they escorted Abram out — him, his wife, and everything he owned.

[1] So Abram pulled up from Egypt — he and his wife and everything he owned, with Lot beside him — and headed back into the Negev.

[2] Abram was heavy with wealth: livestock, silver, gold.

[3] He moved by stages out of the Negev, working his way back to Bethel, to the very spot where his tent had stood before, between Bethel and Ai —

[4] the place where he had built the altar at the start. And there Abram called on the name of the LORD.

[5] Lot, traveling with Abram, also had flocks and herds and tents of his own.

[6] But the land couldn't carry them both. Their holdings were too vast — they couldn't stay together.

[7] Friction broke out between Abram's herdsmen and Lot's. (The Canaanites and the Perizzites were living in the land at the time.)

[8] So Abram said to Lot, "Let's not have bad blood between us — between your men and mine. We're family.

[9] The whole land is right here in front of you. Let's split. If you go left, I'll go right. If you go right, I'll go left."

[10] Lot lifted his eyes and surveyed the Jordan Valley — every inch of it well-watered, lush as the garden of the LORD, rich as the land of Egypt — this was before the LORD wiped out Sodom and Gomorrah — all the way to Zoar.

[11] So Lot chose the Jordan Valley for himself and headed east. The two of them parted ways.

[12] Abram settled in Canaan; Lot settled among the cities of the plain and pitched his tents as far as Sodom.

[13] And the men of Sodom were vile — sinners against the LORD, deeply.

[14] After Lot had gone, the LORD said to Abram, "Lift your eyes from where you're standing. Look north, south, east, west.

[15] Every piece of land you can see — I'm giving it to you and to your descendants forever.

[16] I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth. If anyone could count the dust, then your descendants could be counted too.

[17] Get up. Walk the land — its length, its breadth. It's yours. I'm giving it to you."

[18] So Abram moved his tents and came to settle by the oaks of Mamre at Hebron. There he built an altar to the LORD.

[1] In the days of Amraphel king of Shinar, Arioch king of Ellasar, Chedorlaomer king of Elam, and Tidal king of Goiim — [2] these four marched out to war against Bera king of Sodom, Birsha king of Gomorrah, Shinab king of Admah, Shemeber king of Zeboiim, and the king of Bela (which is Zoar).

[3] The five kings joined forces in the Valley of Siddim — the Salt Sea now covers it. [4] For twelve years they had bent the knee to Chedorlaomer. In the thirteenth, they rebelled. [5] So in the fourteenth, Chedorlaomer and his allies came crashing down. They crushed the Rephaim at Ashteroth-karnaim, the Zuzim at Ham, the Emim on the plain of Kiriathaim, [6] and the Horites in the hill country of Seir, driving them clear out to El-paran on the edge of the wilderness. [7] Then they wheeled back to En-mishpat (that is, Kadesh), and laid waste to the whole territory of the Amalekites, and the Amorites living at Hazazon-tamar too.

[8] That's when the king of Sodom marched out — with the kings of Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela (Zoar). They drew up battle lines in the Valley of Siddim [9] against Chedorlaomer king of Elam, Tidal king of Goiim, Amraphel king of Shinar, and Arioch king of Ellasar — four kings against five.

[10] But the Valley of Siddim was riddled with tar pits. When the kings of Sodom and Gomorrah broke and ran, men tumbled into them. The survivors fled to the hills. [11] The invaders stripped Sodom and Gomorrah bare — every possession, every scrap of food — and pulled out. [12] And they took Lot, Abram's nephew, with them. He'd been living in Sodom. They took everything he owned.

[13] One man escaped and brought the news to Abram the Hebrew. Abram was camped by the oaks of Mamre the Amorite, who was brother to Eshcol and Aner — all of them bound to Abram in alliance.

[14] When Abram heard his kinsman had been taken, he mustered his trained men — three hundred and eighteen of them, born in his own household — and gave chase as far as Dan. [15] Under cover of night he split his force, fell on the enemy, and routed them. He drove them all the way to Hobah, north of Damascus. [16] He brought back everything — all the plunder, his nephew Lot and his possessions, the women, the people, all of it.

[17] When Abram came back from defeating Chedorlaomer and the kings with him, the king of Sodom rode out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh (the King's Valley).

[18] And Melchizedek, king of Salem, brought out bread and wine. He was priest of God Most High. [19] He blessed Abram, saying:

"Blessed be Abram by God Most High,
maker of heaven and earth.
[20] And blessed be God Most High,
who delivered your enemies into your hand."

And Abram gave him a tenth of everything.

[21] Then the king of Sodom said to Abram, "Give me the people. Keep the goods for yourself."

[22] But Abram answered, "I have raised my hand to the LORD, God Most High, maker of heaven and earth, and sworn this: [23] not a thread, not a sandal strap, nothing of yours will I take. You will never say, 'I made Abram rich.' [24] Nothing for me — only what my men have already eaten, and the share belonging to Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre, who marched with me. Let them take their portion."

[1] After all this, the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision: "Don't be afraid, Abram. I am your shield. Your reward will be enormous."

[2] Abram answered, "Lord GOD, what could you possibly give me? I'm walking through life childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus."

[3] He pressed in: "You haven't given me offspring. A servant born in my house will inherit everything I have."

[4] Then the word of the LORD came back: "That man will not be your heir. Your own flesh and blood will be."

[5] He took Abram outside and said, "Look up. Count the stars — if you can." Then: "That's your offspring."

[6] Abram trusted the LORD, and the LORD counted it to him as righteousness.

[7] He said to him, "I am the LORD who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land as your inheritance."

[8] Abram asked, "Lord GOD, how will I know I'm going to possess it?"

[9] He answered, "Bring me a heifer three years old, a goat three years old, a ram three years old, a turtledove, and a young pigeon."

[10] Abram brought all of them. He split each animal down the middle and laid the halves opposite each other. The birds he did not divide.

[11] Vultures dropped down on the carcasses, and Abram drove them off.

[12] As the sun was setting, a heavy sleep fell on Abram — and with it, a darkness, thick and terrifying, pressed in on him.

[13] Then the LORD said: "Know this for certain — your descendants will live as strangers in a land that isn't theirs. They'll be enslaved and crushed for four hundred years.

[14] But I will bring judgment on the nation that enslaves them, and afterward they'll walk out loaded with wealth.

[15] As for you — you'll go to your fathers in peace. You'll be buried in a good old age.

[16] In the fourth generation your descendants will come back here, because the wickedness of the Amorites is not yet ripe enough for judgment."

[17] When the sun had gone down and darkness covered everything, suddenly a smoking firepot and a blazing torch passed between the pieces.

[18] That day the LORD cut a covenant with Abram: "To your offspring I give this land — from the river of Egypt to the great river, the Euphrates —

[19] the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,

[20] the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaim,

[21] the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites."

[1] Sarai, Abram's wife, had given him no children. But she had an Egyptian slave-woman named Hagar.
