[22] Lotan's sons: Hori and Hemam. Lotan's sister was Timna.

[23] Shobal's sons: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, Onam.

[24] Zibeon's sons: Aiah and Anah. This Anah is the one who discovered the hot springs in the wilderness while grazing his father's donkeys.

[25] Anah's children: Dishon, and Oholibamah, Anah's daughter.

[26] Dishon's sons: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran.

[27] Ezer's sons: Bilhan, Zaavan, Akan.

[28] Dishan's sons: Uz and Aran.

[29] These were the Horite chiefs: Chief Lotan, Chief Shobal, Chief Zibeon, Chief Anah,

[30] Chief Dishon, Chief Ezer, Chief Dishan — the Horite chiefs, clan by clan, in the land of Seir.

[31] These are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king ever ruled over Israel:

[32] Bela son of Beor reigned in Edom, and his city was Dinhabah.

[33] When Bela died, Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah took the throne.

[34] When Jobab died, Husham from the land of the Temanites took the throne.

[35] When Husham died, Hadad son of Bedad took the throne — the one who crushed Midian in the fields of Moab. His city was Avith.

[36] When Hadad died, Samlah from Masrekah took the throne.

[37] When Samlah died, Shaul from Rehoboth on the River took the throne.

[38] When Shaul died, Baal-Hanan son of Achbor took the throne.

[39] When Baal-Hanan son of Achbor died, Hadar took the throne. His city was Pau. His wife was Mehetabel, daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-Zahab.

[40] These are the names of Esau's chiefs by clan and territory: Chief Timna, Chief Alvah, Chief Jetheth,

[41] Chief Oholibamah, Chief Elah, Chief Pinon,

[42] Chief Kenaz, Chief Teman, Chief Mibzar,

[43] Chief Magdiel, Chief Iram. These were Edom's chiefs, marked by where they settled in the land they held. This was Esau — father of Edom.

[1] Jacob settled down in the land where his father had lived as a stranger — the land of Canaan.

[2] This is the story of Jacob's family.

Joseph, seventeen years old, was out with his brothers tending the flocks — working alongside the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah, his father's wives. And Joseph brought their father a bad report about them.

[3] Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because he was the child of his old age. He had a special robe made for him — a thing of color and craftsmanship.

[4] His brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them. They hated him for it. They couldn't even speak to him civilly.

[5] Then Joseph had a dream. He told his brothers about it — and they hated him even more.

[6] "Listen to this dream I had," he said.

[7] "We were out in the field, tying up bundles of grain. Suddenly my bundle stood up straight — and your bundles gathered around it and bowed down to mine."

[8] His brothers shot back, "So you're going to be king over us? You're going to rule us?" And they hated him still more — for his dreams, for his words.

[9] Then he had another dream, and told that one too. "Listen — I had another dream. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me."

[10] When he told his father along with his brothers, his father rebuked him. "What kind of dream is this? Are your mother and I and your brothers actually going to come and bow down to the ground in front of you?"

[11] His brothers burned with jealousy. But his father kept the matter in mind.

[12] His brothers had gone off to graze their father's flocks near Shechem.

[13] Israel said to Joseph, "Your brothers are pasturing the flock at Shechem. Come — I'm sending you to them."

"I'm ready," Joseph said.

[14] "Go find out how your brothers are doing," he said, "and how the flocks are doing. Bring me word." So he sent him off from the valley of Hebron, and Joseph made his way to Shechem.

[15] A man found him wandering in the open country. "What are you looking for?" the man asked.

[16] "My brothers," Joseph said. "Tell me — where are they pasturing the flock?"

[17] "They've moved on," the man said. "I heard them say, 'Let's go to Dothan.'" So Joseph followed his brothers and found them at Dothan.

[18] They spotted him in the distance. And before he reached them, they plotted to kill him.

[19] "Here comes the dreamer," they said to each other.

[20] "Let's kill him and throw him into one of these pits. We'll say a wild animal devoured him. Then we'll see what becomes of his dreams."

[21] But Reuben heard this and tried to save him. "Let's not take his life," he said.

[22] "Don't shed blood. Throw him into this pit out here in the wilderness — but don't lay a hand on him." He was planning to rescue him later and bring him back to his father.

[23] So when Joseph arrived, they stripped him of his robe — the special one, the one of color and craftsmanship —

[24] and they grabbed him and threw him into the pit. The pit was empty. No water in it.

[25] Then they sat down to eat.

Looking up, they saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead — camels loaded with spices, balm, and myrrh, heading down to Egypt.

[26] Judah spoke up. "What do we gain by killing our brother and covering up his blood?

[27] Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites. Don't let our own hands touch him — he's our brother, our own flesh." His brothers agreed.

[28] When the Midianite traders passed by, the brothers pulled Joseph up out of the pit and sold him to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. And they took Joseph down to Egypt.

[29] When Reuben came back to the pit and saw Joseph wasn't there, he tore his clothes.

[30] He went back to his brothers. "The boy is gone! Where can I turn?"

[31] So they took Joseph's robe, slaughtered a goat, and dipped the robe in the blood.

[32] They sent the robe of color and craftsmanship to their father with the message: "We found this. See if it's your son's robe or not."

[33] He recognized it. "It's my son's robe! A wild animal has devoured him. Joseph has been torn to pieces."

[34] Jacob tore his clothes, wrapped himself in sackcloth, and mourned his son for many days.

[35] All his sons and daughters came to comfort him, but he refused all comfort. "No," he said. "I will go down to the grave mourning for my son." And he wept for him.

[36] Meanwhile, in Egypt, the Midianites sold Joseph to Potiphar — an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard.

[1] Around that time Judah pulled away from his brothers and settled near a man from Adullam named Hirah. [2] There Judah noticed the daughter of a Canaanite named Shua. He took her, slept with her, [3] and she conceived. She bore a son, and he named him Er. [4] She conceived again, bore another son, and named him Onan. [5] Then she bore one more — Shelah. She was at Kezib when he was born.

[6] Judah arranged a wife for Er, his firstborn. Her name was Tamar. [7] But Er was wicked in the LORD's sight, so the LORD took his life.

[8] Then Judah said to Onan, "Go to your brother's widow. Do your duty as her brother-in-law and raise up offspring for your brother." [9] But Onan knew the child wouldn't be counted as his. So every time he slept with her, he spilled his seed on the ground rather than give his brother an heir. [10] What he did was evil in the LORD's sight, and the LORD took his life too.

[11] Then Judah said to Tamar, his daughter-in-law, "Go back to your father's house. Live as a widow until my son Shelah is grown." Because he was thinking: he might die like his brothers. So Tamar went home to her father.

[12] Years passed. Judah's wife — Shua's daughter — died. When the mourning was over, Judah went up to Timnah with his friend Hirah the Adullamite, to the men shearing his sheep.

[13] Word reached Tamar: "Your father-in-law is on his way to Timnah to shear his sheep." [14] So she stripped off her widow's clothes, wrapped herself in a veil, and sat at the entrance to Enaim, on the road to Timnah. Because she could see it now — Shelah was grown, and still she hadn't been given to him.

[15] Judah saw her and took her for a prostitute, since her face was covered. [16] He turned aside to her by the road and said, "Come — let me sleep with you." He didn't know she was his daughter-in-law.

She said, "What will you give me for it?"

[17] "I'll send you a young goat from the flock."

"Will you give me a pledge until you send it?"

[18] "What kind of pledge?"

"Your seal, your cord, and the staff in your hand."

He gave them to her, slept with her, and she conceived. [19] Then she got up, walked away, took off the veil, and put her widow's clothes back on.

[20] Judah sent the young goat by his friend the Adullamite, to retrieve the pledge from the woman. But he couldn't find her. [21] He asked the men of the town, "Where's the shrine prostitute — the one who was at Enaim, by the road?"

"There's been no shrine prostitute here," they said.

[22] So he came back to Judah: "I couldn't find her. The locals say there's never been a shrine prostitute in that place."

[23] Judah said, "Let her keep the things. We'll be a laughingstock. I tried — I sent the goat. You couldn't find her. That's the end of it."

[24] About three months later, the word came to Judah: "Your daughter-in-law Tamar has prostituted herself. She's pregnant from it."

Judah said, "Bring her out. Burn her."

[25] As they were dragging her out, she sent word to her father-in-law: "I'm pregnant by the man who owns these. Look closely — whose seal is this? Whose cord? Whose staff?"

[26] Judah recognized them. He said, "She's more in the right than I am — because I didn't give her to my son Shelah." And he never slept with her again.

[27] When her time came to give birth — twins were in her womb. [28] During labor one stretched out a hand, and the midwife tied a scarlet thread on it and said, "This one came first." [29] But then he drew his hand back, and out came his brother. She said, "What a breach you've made for yourself!" So they named him Perez. [30] Then his brother came out, the scarlet thread on his hand. They named him Zerah.

[1] Joseph had been hauled down to Egypt. Potiphar — an Egyptian, one of Pharaoh's officers, captain of the guard — bought him from the Ishmaelites who'd brought him there.

[2] But the LORD was with Joseph, and everything turned to gold in his hands. He lived in the house of his Egyptian master,

[3] and his master could see it — could see that the LORD was with him, that the LORD made everything Joseph touched succeed.

[4] So Joseph won his favor and became his personal attendant. Potiphar put him in charge of the household and handed over everything he owned.

[5] From the moment he gave Joseph the keys, the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house because of Joseph — the blessing of the LORD spilled over everything Potiphar had, in the house and in the fields.

[6] He left it all in Joseph's care and didn't concern himself with anything except the food he ate. Now Joseph was striking — well-built and good-looking.

[7] After a while, his master's wife started eyeing him. "Sleep with me," she said.

[8] He refused. "Look," he told her, "with me here, my master doesn't worry about a single thing in this house. Everything he owns, he's put in my hands.

[9] No one in this house ranks higher than I do. He's kept nothing back from me — except you, because you're his wife. How could I do something so monstrous? How could I sin against God?"

[10] Day after day she pressed him, and day after day he refused — wouldn't sleep with her, wouldn't even be near her.

[11] Then one day he went into the house to do his work, and none of the household staff was inside.

[12] She grabbed him by his cloak. "Sleep with me." He bolted, leaving the cloak in her hand, and ran outside.

[13] When she saw the cloak in her hand and Joseph gone,

[14] she called for the household servants. "Look at this!" she said. "He brought a Hebrew into the house to make fools of us. He came in here to sleep with me — but I screamed.

[15] When he heard me screaming, he dropped his cloak and ran."

[16] She kept the cloak beside her until his master came home,

[17] and she told him the same story: "That Hebrew slave you brought us — he came at me to make sport of me.

[18] But when I screamed, he dropped his cloak and ran."

[19] When Potiphar heard his wife's story — "this is what your slave did to me" — his fury burned.

[20] He had Joseph arrested and thrown into the prison where the king's prisoners were held. So there Joseph was, in prison.

[21] But the LORD was with Joseph. He extended steady kindness to him and gave him favor in the eyes of the warden.

[22] The warden put every prisoner in the place under Joseph's authority — whatever happened there, Joseph was the one running it.

[23] The warden didn't bother checking on anything Joseph handled, because the LORD was with him. Whatever he did, the LORD made it succeed.

[1] Sometime after this, the king of Egypt's cupbearer and his baker crossed their master. They offended Pharaoh, king of Egypt.

[2] Pharaoh erupted at both officials — the chief cupbearer and the chief baker —

[3] and threw them into custody at the captain of the guard's house, the same prison where Joseph was held.

[4] The captain of the guard assigned Joseph to attend to them, and he served them. They sat in custody for some time.

[5] One night, both men — the king of Egypt's cupbearer and baker, held in that prison — dreamed. Each had his own dream, and each dream carried its own meaning.

[6] When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw they were shaken.

[7] He asked Pharaoh's officials, locked up with him in his master's house, "Why the troubled faces today?"

[8] "We've had dreams," they told him, "and there's no one to interpret them." Joseph said, "Interpretations belong to God. Tell me."

[9] So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream. "In my dream, a vine stood in front of me,

[10] and on the vine were three branches. As soon as it budded, blossoms appeared, and the clusters ripened into grapes.

[11] Pharaoh's cup was in my hand. I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh's cup, and placed the cup in Pharaoh's hand."

[12] Joseph said, "Here's what it means. The three branches are three days.

[13] In three days, Pharaoh will lift your head — restore you to your post. You'll place the cup in Pharaoh's hand just like before, when you were his cupbearer.

[14] But remember me when it goes well for you. Show me kindness — mention me to Pharaoh. Get me out of this place.

[15] I was stolen from the land of the Hebrews, and here too I've done nothing to deserve being thrown in this pit."

[16] When the chief baker saw the interpretation was favorable, he said to Joseph, "I had a dream too. Three baskets of white bread were on my head.

[17] The top basket held every kind of baked good for Pharaoh, and birds were eating it right off my head."

[18] Joseph answered, "Here's what it means. The three baskets are three days.

[19] In three days, Pharaoh will lift your head — off your shoulders. He'll hang you on a tree, and the birds will pick the flesh from your bones."

[20] Three days later was Pharaoh's birthday. He threw a feast for all his officials and lifted the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker in front of them.

[21] The chief cupbearer he restored to his role, and the cup went back into Pharaoh's hand.

[22] The chief baker he hanged — exactly as Joseph had said.

[23] But the chief cupbearer forgot Joseph. He never gave him another thought.

[1] Two years dragged by. Then Pharaoh dreamed.

He was standing beside the Nile.

[2] Out of the river climbed seven cows — sleek, well-fed — and they grazed in the reed grass.

[3] Then seven more cows climbed out behind them — gaunt, skeletal, the worst he had ever seen — and they stood beside the first seven on the riverbank.

[4] And the gaunt cows ate the sleek ones. Devoured them whole.

Pharaoh jolted awake.

[5] He drifted back to sleep and dreamed a second time.

Seven heads of grain rose on a single stalk — plump, golden, full.

[6] Then seven more heads sprouted after them — withered, scorched by the east wind, shriveled to nothing.

[7] And the withered heads swallowed the seven plump, full heads.

Pharaoh woke. A dream.

[8] By morning his spirit was in turmoil. He summoned every magician in Egypt, every wise man he could find, and told them his dreams. Not one of them could crack it.

[9] Then the chief cupbearer spoke up to Pharaoh: "Today I have to confess my failures.

[10] Pharaoh was furious with his servants once and threw me into the prison at the captain of the guard's house — me and the chief baker.

[11] One night we both dreamed — each of us a dream with its own meaning.

[12] A young Hebrew was there with us, a slave of the captain of the guard. We told him our dreams, and he interpreted them — each of us, exactly to the meaning.

[13] And it played out precisely as he said: I was restored to my position, and the baker was hanged."

[14] Pharaoh sent for Joseph. They rushed him out of the dungeon. He shaved, changed his clothes, and stood before Pharaoh.

[15] Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I had a dream, and no one can interpret it. But I've heard about you — that when you hear a dream, you can interpret it."

[16] Joseph answered, "It is not me. God will give Pharaoh the answer he needs."

[17] So Pharaoh told him: "In my dream I was standing on the bank of the Nile.

[18] Seven cows came up out of the river — fat, well-formed — and they grazed in the reeds.

[19] Then seven more came up after them — wretched, gaunt, the worst I have ever seen in all of Egypt.

[20] And the lean, ugly cows ate the seven fat cows.

[21] But when they had eaten them, you couldn't tell — they looked just as terrible as before. Then I woke up.

[22] I dreamed again. Seven heads of grain came up on one stalk, full and golden.

[23] Then seven more heads sprouted after them — shriveled, thin, scorched by the east wind.

[24] And the thin heads swallowed the seven full heads. I told my magicians, but no one could explain it."

[25] Joseph said to Pharaoh: "Pharaoh's two dreams are one dream. God is telling Pharaoh what he is about to do.

[26] The seven good cows are seven years. The seven good heads of grain are seven years. It is one dream.

[27] The seven gaunt and ugly cows that came up after them are seven years, and so are the seven empty heads scorched by the east wind — seven years of famine.

[28] This is what I told Pharaoh: God has shown Pharaoh what he is about to do.

[29] Seven years of overwhelming abundance are coming to all of Egypt.

[30] But seven years of famine will rise up after them, and all that abundance will be forgotten. The famine will consume the land.

[31] No one will even remember there was plenty — that's how crushing the famine will be.

[32] And the reason the dream came twice to Pharaoh: the matter is fixed by God, and God is about to make it happen — fast.

[33] So let Pharaoh find a man — discerning, wise — and put him in charge of Egypt.

[34] Let Pharaoh act. Appoint overseers throughout the land. Take a fifth of Egypt's harvest during the seven years of abundance.

[35] Gather every scrap of food from these coming good years. Stockpile grain under Pharaoh's authority. Store it in the cities. Guard it.

[36] That reserve will carry the land through the seven years of famine that are coming on Egypt. The country will not perish."

[37] Pharaoh liked the plan. So did all his officials.

[38] Pharaoh said to them, "Could we find another man like this — a man with the Spirit of God in him?"

[39] Then Pharaoh turned to Joseph: "Since God has shown you all this, no one is as discerning and wise as you.

[40] You will run my house. My people will move at your command. Only the throne itself will set me above you."

[41] Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Look. I am placing you over all of Egypt."

[42] Pharaoh pulled the signet ring from his hand and slid it onto Joseph's. He dressed him in robes of fine linen and hung a gold chain around his neck.

[43] He had him ride in the second chariot of the realm, and runners went ahead of him shouting, "Bow down!" That is how Pharaoh set him over all of Egypt.

[44] Pharaoh said to Joseph, "I am Pharaoh — but without your word, no one in all of Egypt lifts a hand or takes a step."

[45] Pharaoh named him Zaphenath-paneah and gave him Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, as his wife. Joseph went out over the land of Egypt.

[46] Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh, king of Egypt. He left Pharaoh's presence and traveled the length and breadth of Egypt.

[47] During the seven years of abundance, the land produced in fistfuls.

[48] Joseph gathered every bit of surplus from those seven years and stored it in the cities. Into each city he put the grain from the fields surrounding it.

[49] Joseph piled up grain like sand on the seashore — so much they stopped counting. It was beyond measure.

[50] Before the years of famine arrived, Joseph had two sons by Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.

[51] Joseph named the firstborn Manasseh, saying, "God has made me forget all my hardship and all my father's house."

[52] The second he named Ephraim, saying, "God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction."

[53] The seven years of abundance in Egypt ended.

[54] And the seven years of famine began — exactly as Joseph had said. Famine struck every country. But in all of Egypt, there was bread.

[55] When Egypt too began to starve, the people cried out to Pharaoh for food. Pharaoh told all of Egypt: "Go to Joseph. Do whatever he tells you."

[56] The famine spread across the face of the earth. Joseph opened every storehouse and sold grain to the Egyptians. The famine bore down hard on Egypt.

[57] And every nation came to Egypt — to Joseph — to buy grain, because the famine had swallowed the world.

[1] When Jacob saw that grain was available in Egypt, he turned on his sons: "Why are you just standing there looking at each other?"

[2] He pressed in: "I've heard there's grain in Egypt. Get down there. Buy us some. We live, or we die — that simple."

[3] So ten of Joseph's brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.

[4] But Benjamin, Joseph's full brother, Jacob kept back. He couldn't bear the thought of disaster touching him.

[5] So Israel's sons arrived among the crowds streaming in to buy grain — the famine had taken hold of Canaan too.

[6] Joseph was the man in charge. He was the one selling grain to every nation that came. His brothers came in and bowed low, faces to the ground.

[7] Joseph saw them. He recognized them instantly. But he played the stranger, spoke harshly: "Where are you from?" "From Canaan," they said. "To buy food."

[8] He knew them. They had no idea who he was.

[9] Then the dreams came back to him — the ones he had dreamed about them, years ago. And he said, "You're spies. You've come to scout out the weak points of the land."

[10] "No, my lord," they protested. "Your servants came to buy food.

[11] We're all sons of one man. We're honest men. Your servants are no spies."

[12] He pushed back: "No. You've come to see where the land lies exposed."

[13] They said, "Your servants are twelve brothers, sons of one man in the land of Canaan. The youngest is with our father today. One is no more."

[14] Joseph said, "It's just as I told you. You're spies.

[15] Here's how you'll be tested: by Pharaoh's life, you're not leaving this place unless your youngest brother comes here.

[16] Send one of you to get him. The rest of you stay locked up. We'll see if there's any truth in you. If not — by Pharaoh's life — you're spies."

[17] And he threw them all into custody. Three days.

[18] On the third day Joseph said to them, "Do this and live — I'm a man who fears God.

[19] If you're honest men, let one brother stay behind in prison. The rest of you, go. Carry grain home to feed your starving households.

[20] But bring your youngest brother to me. Your words will be verified. You won't die." They agreed.

[21] Then they turned to each other: "We're paying for what we did to our brother. We saw the anguish in his soul when he begged us, and we wouldn't listen. That's why this anguish has come on us."

[22] Reuben answered them: "Didn't I tell you? Didn't I say, 'Don't sin against the boy'? You wouldn't hear it. Now — now there's a reckoning for his blood."

[23] They didn't know Joseph understood every word — he had been using an interpreter.

[24] He turned away from them and wept. Then he came back, spoke with them, and took Simeon from among them. He had him bound before their eyes.

[25] Joseph ordered their sacks filled with grain, each man's silver returned to his sack, and provisions for the journey. It was done.

[26] They loaded the grain on their donkeys and left.

[27] At the place they stopped for the night, one of them opened his sack to feed his donkey — and there was his silver, sitting right at the mouth of the sack.

[28] He said to his brothers, "My silver — it's been put back. Look, here it is in my sack!" Their hearts dropped. They turned to one another, shaking: "What has God done to us?"

[29] They came to Jacob their father in Canaan and told him everything that had happened:

[30] "The man — the lord of the land — spoke harshly with us. Treated us like spies in his country.

[31] We told him, 'We're honest men. We're not spies.

[32] We're twelve brothers, sons of one father. One is no more. The youngest is with our father today in Canaan.'

[33] Then the man, the lord of the land, said to us, 'Here's how I'll know you're honest: leave one brother with me. Take grain for your starving households. Go.

[34] But bring your youngest brother to me. Then I'll know you're not spies — that you're honest men. I'll release your brother to you, and you can move freely in this land.'"

[35] Then they started emptying their sacks — and there it was: every man's pouch of silver in his sack. They saw the pouches, their father saw them, and fear took them all.

[36] Jacob their father said to them, "You're stripping me of my children. Joseph is gone. Simeon is gone. Now you want to take Benjamin. Everything is against me."

[37] Reuben spoke up: "Put my two sons to death if I don't bring him back to you. Hand him over to me. I'll return him."

[38] But Jacob said, "My son is not going down with you. His brother is dead. He's all I have left. If harm comes to him on the road you're traveling, you'll send my gray head down to the grave in grief."

[1] The famine bore down on the land, relentless.

[2] When they had eaten through the grain they brought back from Egypt, their father said, "Go again. Buy us a little food."

[3] But Judah answered him, "The man warned us, point-blank: 'You will not see my face unless your brother is with you.'

[4] If you'll send our brother with us, we'll go down and buy you food.

[5] But if you won't send him, we're not going. The man told us flat out — 'No brother, no audience.'"

[6] Israel said, "Why did you do this to me? Why did you tell the man you had another brother?"

[7] They said, "The man interrogated us — about us, about our family. 'Is your father still living? Do you have another brother?' We just answered his questions. How were we supposed to know he'd say, 'Bring your brother down here'?"

[8] Then Judah said to Israel his father, "Send the boy with me. Let's get up and go — so we live and don't die, all of us, you, us, our children.

[9] I'll guarantee him myself. Hold me responsible. If I don't bring him back and stand him in front of you, the blame is mine forever.

[10] If we hadn't dragged our feet, we could have been there and back twice by now."

[11] Their father Israel said, "If it must be, then do this: take the best of the land in your packs — bring the man a gift. A little balm, a little honey, spices, myrrh, pistachios, almonds.

[12] Take double the silver. The silver that came back in your sacks — take it back. It may have been a mistake.

[13] Take your brother. Get up. Go back to the man.

[14] May God Almighty grant you mercy in front of him, so he releases your other brother and Benjamin. As for me — if I am bereaved, I am bereaved."

[15] So they took the gift, took double the silver, took Benjamin, and went down to Egypt. They stood before Joseph.

[16] When Joseph saw Benjamin with them, he said to his house steward, "Take these men inside. Slaughter an animal and prepare it. These men will eat with me at noon."

[17] The steward did as Joseph said and brought the men into Joseph's house.

[18] The men were terrified at being brought into Joseph's house. They said, "It's about the silver — the silver that came back in our sacks the first time. He's bringing us in to spring on us, overpower us, take us as slaves, take our donkeys."

[19] So they approached Joseph's steward and spoke to him at the doorway of the house.

[20] They said, "Please, sir — we came down once before to buy food.

[21] On the way back, when we stopped for the night and opened our sacks — there was each man's silver, right at the mouth of his sack. The full amount. We've brought it back with us.

[22] And we've brought more silver to buy food. We have no idea who put the silver in our sacks."

[23] He said, "Peace to you. Don't be afraid. Your God, the God of your father, must have slipped treasure into your sacks. I received your payment." Then he brought Simeon out to them.

[24] The steward brought the men into Joseph's house, gave them water to wash their feet, and gave their donkeys feed.

[25] They laid out the gift to be ready for when Joseph came at noon — they had heard they would eat there.

[26] When Joseph came into the house, they presented the gift they'd carried in, and they bowed to him, faces to the ground.

[27] He asked how they were. He said, "Is your old father well — the one you mentioned? Is he still alive?"

[28] They said, "Your servant our father is well. He's still alive." And they bowed low again.

[29] He looked up and saw his brother Benjamin — his mother's son — and said, "Is this your youngest brother, the one you told me about?" Then he said, "God be gracious to you, my son."

[30] And Joseph rushed out — the sight of his brother undid him, he had to weep. He went into his chamber and wept there.

[31] Then he washed his face, came out, pulled himself together, and said, "Serve the food."

[32] They served him at his own table, his brothers at theirs, and the Egyptians eating with him at theirs — because Egyptians could not eat with Hebrews, it was unclean to them.

[33] The brothers were seated in front of him in order — oldest to youngest, by birthright exact. They stared at each other in disbelief.

[34] Portions were carried from Joseph's table to them — and Benjamin's portion was five times the others'. They drank with him and drank deep.

[1] Then Joseph gave the order to his house steward: "Fill these men's sacks with all the grain they can carry, and slip each man's silver back into the mouth of his sack. [2] And my cup — the silver one — put it in the mouth of the youngest one's sack, along with his grain money." The steward did exactly as Joseph said.

[3] At first light, the brothers were sent off with their donkeys. [4] They had barely cleared the city when Joseph said to his steward: "Move. Chase them down. When you catch them, say this: 'Why have you repaid kindness with treachery? [5] Isn't this the cup my master drinks from — the very one he uses for divination? You have done a wicked thing.'"

[6] He overtook them and delivered the words verbatim. [7] They were stunned. "Why would our lord say such a thing? Your servants would never dream of doing something like this. [8] Look — the silver we found in our sacks last time, we brought it back to you all the way from Canaan. Why would we now steal silver or gold from your master's house? [9] If any of your servants is found with it, he dies. And the rest of us will be your lord's slaves."

[10] "Fair enough," the steward replied, "but only on my terms: the one caught with it becomes my slave. The rest of you walk free."

[11] Each man hurried to lower his sack to the ground and open it. [12] The steward searched — starting with the oldest, ending with the youngest. The cup turned up in Benjamin's sack.

[13] They tore their clothes. Every one of them reloaded his donkey and turned back toward the city.

[14] Judah and his brothers walked into Joseph's house — he was still there — and threw themselves on the ground before him. [15] Joseph said to them, "What have you done? Did you not know a man like me can read what's hidden?"

[16] Judah answered: "What can we say to my lord? What words are left? How can we prove we're innocent? God has uncovered your servants' guilt. We are now your slaves, my lord — all of us, and the one in whose hand the cup was found."

[17] "Never," Joseph said. "Far be it from me. Only the man caught with the cup will be my slave. The rest of you — go in peace to your father."

[18] Then Judah stepped forward. "Please, my lord — let your servant speak one word in my lord's hearing. Don't burn with anger at your servant. You are Pharaoh's equal. [19] My lord asked his servants, 'Do you have a father, a brother?' [20] And we told you: 'We have an aging father, and there's a young son born to him in his old age. His brother is dead. He alone is left of his mother's children, and his father loves him.'

[21] "Then you said to your servants, 'Bring him down to me. I want to see him with my own eyes.' [22] We said to my lord, 'The boy cannot leave his father. If he leaves him, his father will die.' [23] But you told your servants, 'Unless your youngest brother comes down with you, you will not see my face again.'

[24] "So we went back up to your servant, our father, and told him what my lord had said. [25] And when our father said, 'Go back and buy us a little more food,' [26] we told him, 'We can't go down. Only if our youngest brother is with us — otherwise we cannot show our faces to that man.'

[27] "Then your servant, our father, said to us: 'You know my wife bore me two sons. [28] One of them walked out and never came back. I said, "Surely he's been torn to pieces" — and I haven't seen him since. [29] If you take this one from me too, and harm comes to him, you will send my gray head down to the grave in grief.'

[30] "So now — if I come back to your servant, my father, without the boy — and his very soul is bound up with the boy's soul — [31] when he sees the boy is not with us, he will die. Your servants will have sent our father's gray head down to the grave in sorrow.

[32] "Your servant pledged himself for the boy to my father. I said, 'If I don't bring him back, I'll carry the blame before my father as long as I live.'

[33] "So please — let me stay as my lord's slave in the boy's place. Let the boy go back with his brothers. [34] How can I face my father if the boy isn't with me? I cannot bear to watch the grief that would break him."

[1] Joseph could not hold it together any longer. Not in front of all those attendants. He cried out, "Everyone out! Clear the room!" So no one was with him when Joseph revealed himself to his brothers.

[2] But he wept so loudly the Egyptians heard him. Word reached Pharaoh's house.

[3] Joseph said to his brothers, "I'm Joseph. Is my father — is he really still alive?" His brothers couldn't answer. They were stunned silent, faces frozen in front of him.

[4] "Come closer," Joseph said. They came closer. "I'm Joseph. Your brother. The one you sold into Egypt.

[5] Don't be devastated. Don't be furious with yourselves for what you did. God sent me ahead of you to save lives.

[6] The famine has gripped this land for two years now, and five more are coming — no plowing, no harvest.

[7] God sent me ahead of you to preserve a remnant on the earth, to keep you alive through a great deliverance.

[8] So it wasn't you who sent me here. It was God. He made me a father to Pharaoh, master of his entire household, ruler over all Egypt.

[9] Hurry — go back to my father and tell him: 'This is what your son Joseph says: God has made me lord of all Egypt. Come down to me. Don't delay.

[10] You'll settle in the land of Goshen, close to me — you, your children, your grandchildren, your flocks, your herds, everything you have.

[11] I'll provide for you there. Five more years of famine are coming. I won't let you, your family, or your livestock fall into ruin.'

[12] Look at me — your own eyes can see, and my brother Benjamin's eyes too — it's really me speaking to you.

[13] Tell my father everything — all my glory in Egypt, everything you've seen. Hurry. Bring my father down here."

[14] Then he threw his arms around Benjamin's neck and wept, and Benjamin wept on his shoulder.

[15] He kissed each of his brothers and wept over them. After that, his brothers found their voices and talked with him.

[16] News rippled through Pharaoh's palace: "Joseph's brothers have come!" Pharaoh was pleased. So were his servants.

[17] Pharaoh told Joseph, "Tell your brothers: 'Do this — load up your animals and head back to Canaan.

[18] Get your father and your families and come to me. I'll give you the best of Egypt. You'll eat the richness of this land.'

[19] And tell them this too: 'Take wagons from Egypt for your little ones and your wives. Bring your father. Come.

[20] Don't worry about your belongings — the best of all Egypt is yours.'"

[21] Israel's sons did it. Joseph gave them wagons, just as Pharaoh ordered, and supplies for the road.

[22] To each brother he gave a new set of clothes. To Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five sets of clothes.

[23] To his father he sent ten donkeys loaded with the finest goods of Egypt, and ten female donkeys carrying grain and bread and provisions for the journey.

[24] As he sent his brothers off, he told them, "Don't quarrel on the way."

[25] So they left Egypt and came to their father Jacob in Canaan.

[26] They told him, "Joseph is still alive! He's the ruler over all Egypt!" Jacob's heart went numb. He couldn't believe them.

[27] But when they relayed everything Joseph had told them, and when he saw the wagons Joseph had sent to carry him — the spirit of their father Jacob came back to life.

[28] Israel said, "Enough. My son Joseph is still alive. I will go see him before I die."

[1] So Israel set out with everything he owned, and when he reached Beersheba he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac.

[2] That night God spoke to Israel in visions: "Jacob. Jacob."

"I'm here," he answered.

[3] "I am God — the God of your father. Don't be afraid to go down to Egypt. I will make you into a great nation there.

[4] I will go down with you, and I will bring you back up again. And Joseph's own hand will close your eyes."

[5] Jacob rose from Beersheba. His sons loaded their father, their children, and their wives onto the wagons Pharaoh had sent to carry him.

[6] They took their livestock and everything they had acquired in Canaan, and Jacob went down to Egypt — he and all his descendants with him —

[7] his sons and grandsons, his daughters and granddaughters. He brought every one of them with him to Egypt.

[8] These are the names of Israel's family who came to Egypt — Jacob and his sons. Reuben, Jacob's firstborn.

[9] Reuben's sons: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

[10] Simeon's sons: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul (whose mother was Canaanite).

[11] Levi's sons: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

[12] Judah's sons: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah — though Er and Onan died in Canaan. Perez had sons of his own: Hezron and Hamul.

[13] Issachar's sons: Tola, Puvah, Job, and Shimron.

[14] Zebulun's sons: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.

[15] These were Leah's sons — born to Jacob in Paddan Aram — along with his daughter Dinah. Thirty-three sons and daughters in all.

[16] Gad's sons: Zephon, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.

[17] Asher's sons: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah — and their sister Serah. Beriah's sons: Heber and Malkiel.

[18] These were Zilpah's children — the maid Laban gave to his daughter Leah — sixteen souls she bore to Jacob.

[19] The sons of Jacob's wife Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.

[20] In Egypt, Joseph fathered Manasseh and Ephraim by Asenath, daughter of Potiphera, priest of On.

[21] Benjamin's sons: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.

[22] These were Rachel's descendants through Jacob — fourteen in all.

[23] Dan's son: Hushim.

[24] Naphtali's sons: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.

[25] These were Bilhah's children — the maid Laban gave to his daughter Rachel — seven souls she bore to Jacob.

[26] Everyone who came with Jacob to Egypt — his direct descendants, not counting the wives of his sons — numbered sixty-six.

[27] Add Joseph's two sons born in Egypt, and the house of Jacob entering Egypt totaled seventy.

[28] Jacob sent Judah ahead to Joseph to get directions to Goshen. When they arrived in the region,

[29] Joseph harnessed his chariot and rode up to meet his father. The moment he saw him, he threw his arms around him and wept on his shoulder a long, long time.

[30] Israel said to Joseph, "Now I can die. I have seen your face. You are alive."

[31] Joseph said to his brothers and his father's household, "I'll go up and tell Pharaoh — I'll say, 'My brothers and my father's household have come to me from Canaan.

[32] They are shepherds. They keep livestock. They've brought their flocks and herds and everything they own.'

[33] When Pharaoh summons you and asks what you do for a living,

[34] tell him: 'Your servants have been keeping livestock since we were boys — just like our fathers before us.' Say this so he will settle you in Goshen — because every shepherd is detestable to the Egyptians."

[1] Joseph went and reported to Pharaoh: "My father and brothers have arrived from Canaan with their flocks, their herds, and everything they own. They're in Goshen right now." [2] He had brought five of his brothers with him and presented them before Pharaoh.

[3] Pharaoh asked the brothers, "What's your line of work?"

They answered, "Your servants are shepherds, like our fathers before us." [4] Then they added, "We've come to live here as foreigners. There's no pasture left in Canaan — the famine is brutal. Please, let your servants settle in Goshen."

[5] Pharaoh turned to Joseph: "Your father and your brothers have come to you. [6] Egypt is open to you. Settle your family in the best part of the land — give them Goshen. And if you know any capable men among them, put them in charge of my own livestock."

[7] Then Joseph brought in his father Jacob and presented him to Pharaoh. Jacob blessed Pharaoh. [8] Pharaoh asked him, "How old are you?"

[9] Jacob answered, "The years of my wandering total a hundred and thirty. Few and hard — nothing like the long lives my fathers lived in their own wandering." [10] Jacob blessed Pharaoh again and went out from his presence.

[11] So Joseph settled his father and brothers in Egypt, giving them property in the best of the land — the district of Rameses — exactly as Pharaoh had ordered. [12] And Joseph kept his father, his brothers, and his entire extended family supplied with food, down to the smallest child.

[13] But there was no bread anywhere. The famine was crushing. Egypt and Canaan were wasting away. [14] Joseph collected every coin in Egypt and Canaan in exchange for grain and deposited it all in Pharaoh's treasury.

[15] When the money ran out in both lands, the Egyptians came to Joseph: "Give us bread! Are we going to die in front of you just because the silver's gone?"

[16] Joseph said, "Bring your livestock. I'll trade you bread for animals if the money's gone." [17] So they brought their livestock to Joseph, and he gave them bread in exchange for horses, sheep, cattle, and donkeys. That year he fed them in trade for all their animals.

[18] When that year ended, they came back the next year: "We can't hide it from our lord — the silver is spent, the herds are yours. There's nothing left for our lord except our bodies and our land. [19] Why should we die before your eyes — we and our soil along with us? Buy us and our land for bread. We'll become Pharaoh's slaves and our land his property. Just give us seed so we can live and not die, so the land doesn't turn to dust."

[20] So Joseph bought up every field in Egypt for Pharaoh. Every Egyptian sold his land — the famine had broken them. The land became Pharaoh's. [21] As for the people, he relocated them to cities from one end of Egypt to the other. [22] Only the priests' land he did not buy — they had a fixed allowance from Pharaoh and lived off it, so they didn't have to sell.

[23] Joseph announced to the people: "Today I've bought you and your land for Pharaoh. Here's seed — plant the ground. [24] When harvest comes, give a fifth to Pharaoh. The other four-fifths is yours — seed for your fields, food for your households, food for your children."

[25] They said, "You've saved our lives. If our lord is pleased with us, we'll gladly be Pharaoh's slaves."

[26] So Joseph made it law — still on the books in Egypt to this day — that a fifth belongs to Pharaoh. Only the priests' land was exempt; it never became Pharaoh's.

[27] Meanwhile, Israel settled in Egypt, in the region of Goshen. They acquired property there, were fruitful, and multiplied like wildfire.

[28] Jacob lived in Egypt seventeen years. The full span of his life came to a hundred and forty-seven years. [29] When the time drew near for Israel to die, he called his son Joseph and said, "If I've found favor with you, put your hand under my thigh and swear it: deal with me in loyalty and truth. Do not bury me in Egypt. [30] When I lie down with my fathers, carry me out of Egypt and bury me in their grave."

Joseph said, "I'll do as you say."

[31] "Swear it to me," Jacob said. So Joseph swore. And Israel bowed in worship at the head of his bed.

[1] Sometime later, word reached Joseph: "Your father is failing." So he took his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim, and set out.

[2] When they told Jacob, "Your son Joseph has come to you," Israel gathered his strength and sat up in bed.

[3] Jacob said to Joseph, "El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and there he blessed me.

[4] He told me, 'I'm going to make you fruitful. I'll multiply you, turn you into a gathering of peoples, and hand this land to your offspring after you as a possession that won't end.'

[5] So now — your two sons, the ones born to you in Egypt before I came down here, they're mine. Ephraim and Manasseh — they belong to me the same way Reuben and Simeon do.

[6] Any children you father after them are yours. They'll be counted under their brothers' names when it comes to their inheritance.

[7] As for me — when I was coming from Paddan, Rachel died on me, in the land of Canaan, on the road, still some distance from Ephrath. I buried her there, beside the road to Ephrath." (That's Bethlehem.)

[8] Then Israel saw Joseph's sons and asked, "Who are these?"

[9] "They're my sons," Joseph answered, "the ones God has given me here." Jacob said, "Bring them to me. Let me bless them."

[10] Israel's eyes had grown heavy with age — he could barely see. Joseph brought the boys close, and Jacob kissed them and held them tight.

[11] "I never expected to see your face again," Israel said to Joseph, "and look — God has shown me your children too."

[12] Joseph drew them back from Jacob's knees and bowed face to the ground.

[13] Then he positioned them — Ephraim at his right (toward Israel's left) and Manasseh at his left (toward Israel's right) — and brought them close.

[14] But Israel crossed his arms. He reached out his right hand and laid it on Ephraim's head — the younger — and his left on Manasseh's head — the firstborn. He did it on purpose.

[15] Then he blessed Joseph:

"The God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked,
the God who has been my shepherd my whole life until this day,

[16] the Messenger who rescued me from every evil —
may he bless these boys.
Let my name live in them,
and the names of my fathers Abraham and Isaac.
Let them multiply into a multitude in the middle of the earth."

[17] When Joseph saw his father's right hand resting on Ephraim's head, he didn't like it. He took hold of his father's hand to move it from Ephraim to Manasseh.

[18] "Not like that, Father," Joseph said. "This one's the firstborn. Put your right hand on his head."

[19] But his father refused. "I know, son. I know. He too will become a people, and he too will be great. But his younger brother will be greater, and his offspring will swell into a multitude of nations."

[20] So that day Jacob blessed them with these words:

"By you Israel will pronounce blessing, saying,
'May God make you like Ephraim and like Manasseh.'"

And he set Ephraim before Manasseh.

[21] Then Israel said to Joseph, "I'm about to die. But God will be with you, and he'll bring you back to the land of your fathers.

[22] As for you — I'm giving you one ridge more than your brothers, the slope I took from the Amorite with my sword and my bow."

[1] Then Jacob called his sons and said: "Gather close. Let me tell you what's coming for you in the days ahead.

[2] Come in, sons of Jacob. Listen. Listen to Israel your father.

[3] Reuben — my firstborn. You are my strength, the first proof of my manhood, ranked highest, with power to match.

[4] But you churn like floodwater — and you will not rise to the top. Because you climbed into your father's bed. You climbed onto my couch and defiled it.

[5] Simeon and Levi — brothers in every sense. Their blades are weapons of cruelty.

[6] I will not enter their council. I will not join their assembly. In their rage they killed men. For sport they crippled oxen.

[7] Cursed be their rage — it is savage. Cursed be their fury — it is brutal. I will scatter them through Jacob. I will disperse them across Israel.

[8] Judah — your brothers will praise you. Your hand will be on the neck of your enemies. Your father's sons will bow down to you.

[9] Judah is a lion's cub. You rose from the kill, my son. He crouches, he stretches out like a lion — like a lioness — who dares to stir him?

[10] The scepter will not leave Judah, nor the ruler's staff slip from between his feet, until the one it belongs to comes — and the nations will fall in line behind him.

[11] He tethers his donkey to the vine, his colt to the choicest branch. He washes his clothes in wine, his robes in the blood of grapes.

[12] His eyes are darker than wine. His teeth are whiter than milk.

[13] Zebulun will settle by the sea. He will be a harbor for ships. His border will reach toward Sidon.

[14] Issachar is a thick-boned donkey, sprawled out between the saddlebags.

[15] He saw that rest was good, that the land was pleasant — and he bent his shoulder to the load and became a slave at hard labor.

[16] Dan will judge his people, one of the tribes of Israel.

[17] Dan will be a snake on the road, a viper on the path, biting the horse's heel so the rider tumbles backward.

[18] I wait for your rescue, LORD.

[19] Gad — raiders will raid him, but he will raid at their heels.

[20] Asher's bread will be rich. He will serve up food fit for kings.

[21] Naphtali is a doe set loose, bearing beautiful fawns.

[22] Joseph is a fruitful vine — a fruitful vine by a spring, branches climbing over the wall.

[23] Archers came at him hard. They shot at him. They hated him.

[24] But his bow held steady. His arms stayed strong — by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob, by the name of the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel,

[25] by the God of your father who helps you, by the Almighty who blesses you — blessings from the heavens above, blessings from the deep that lies below, blessings of breast and womb.

[26] Your father's blessings outstrip the blessings of the ancient mountains, the bounty of the everlasting hills. Let them rest on Joseph's head, on the crown of the one set apart from his brothers.

[27] Benjamin is a wolf that tears its prey. In the morning he devours the catch. At evening he divides the spoil."

[28] These are the twelve tribes of Israel, and this is what their father said to them when he blessed them — each one with the blessing that fit him.

[29] Then he gave them his final orders: "I am about to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers, in the cave on the field of Ephron the Hittite,

[30] the cave in the field of Machpelah, near Mamre in Canaan, the field Abraham bought from Ephron the Hittite to be his burial ground.

[31] There they buried Abraham and his wife Sarah. There they buried Isaac and his wife Rebekah. There I buried Leah —

[32] the field and the cave that came with it, bought from the Hittites."

[33] When Jacob finished giving instructions to his sons, he pulled his feet up into the bed, drew his last breath, and was gathered to his people.

[1] Joseph collapsed onto his father's face, wept over him, kissed him. [2] Then he ordered the physicians in his service to embalm Israel, and they did — [3] forty days, the full embalming term, and Egypt mourned him seventy.

[4] When the mourning days were spent, Joseph went to Pharaoh's court and said, "If I've found favor with you, carry this word to Pharaoh: [5] My father made me swear an oath. He said, 'I'm about to die. Bury me in the tomb I carved out for myself in the land of Canaan.' Let me go up and bury my father. Then I'll return."

[6] Pharaoh answered, "Go. Bury your father, as he made you swear."

[7] So Joseph went up to bury his father, and with him went all Pharaoh's officials — the elders of the palace, every elder of Egypt — [8] Joseph's whole household, his brothers, his father's household. Only the little ones and the flocks and the herds stayed behind in Goshen. [9] Chariots and horsemen rode with him. It was a vast procession.

[10] When they reached the threshing floor of Atad, just beyond the Jordan, they held a funeral — heavy, immense. Joseph mourned his father seven days there. [11] The Canaanites living in the land watched the mourning at Atad and said, "Egypt is grieving hard today." That's why the place is called Abel-mizraim — "Egypt's Mourning" — across the Jordan.

[12] So Jacob's sons did exactly as he had charged them: [13] they carried him into Canaan and buried him in the cave at Machpelah, the field east of Mamre that Abraham had bought from Ephron the Hittite as a burial holding. [14] Then Joseph returned to Egypt — he, his brothers, everyone who had gone with him to bury his father.

[15] With their father gone, Joseph's brothers panicked. "What if Joseph holds the grudge after all? What if he pays us back in full for everything we did to him?" [16] So they sent word to Joseph: "Before he died, your father left instructions. [17] He said this: 'Tell Joseph — please — forgive your brothers' offense, their sin, the evil they did to you.' So now: please forgive the offense of the servants of your father's God."

Joseph wept when the message reached him.

[18] His brothers themselves came and threw themselves down before him. "We are your slaves," they said.

[19] But Joseph answered, "Don't be afraid. Am I in the place of God? [20] You meant evil against me — God meant it for good. He aimed it at this very day, at keeping a great many people alive. [21] So don't be afraid. I'll provide for you and your children." And he comforted them, speaking straight to their hearts.

[22] Joseph stayed in Egypt — he and his father's house. He lived to be a hundred and ten. [23] He saw Ephraim's children to the third generation. The sons of Machir, Manasseh's son, were born on Joseph's knees.

[24] Joseph said to his brothers, "I'm about to die. But God will come for you. He will come, and he will bring you up out of this land to the land he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, to Jacob." [25] And Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath: "When God comes for you — carry my bones up from here."

[26] Joseph died, a hundred and ten years old. They embalmed him and laid him in a coffin in Egypt.

and there, in that hand, his power lies hidden.

Pestilence trails at his heels.

your chariots of salvation?

You split the earth open with rivers.

[10] The mountains saw you and writhed.

and lifted its hands to the sky.

[11] Sun and moon froze in their high houses
at the glint of your flying arrows,
at the lightning-flash of your spear.

to save your anointed one.

[1] In the second year of Darius the king, on the first day of the sixth month, the word of the LORD broke through the prophet Haggai to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest:

[2] This is what the LORD of Armies says: These people keep saying, "The time hasn't come. Not yet. Not yet to rebuild the LORD's house."

[3] Then the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet:

[4] So it's time for you to live in your paneled mansions while my house lies in ruins?

[5] Now hear what the LORD of Armies says: Take a hard look at your lives.

[6] You plant much and harvest little. You eat and never feel full. You drink and stay thirsty. You bundle up and can't get warm. The worker earns wages only to pour them into a bag full of holes.

[7] This is what the LORD of Armies says: Take a hard look at your lives.

[8] Climb the hills. Bring back timber. Build the house. I'll take pleasure in it, and I'll be honored — says the LORD.

[9] You expected abundance, and look — barely anything. What little you brought home, I blew away. Why? — declares the LORD of Armies. Because of my house, which lies in ruins, while every one of you runs to tend his own.

[10] That's why the sky above you withholds its dew and the ground withholds its harvest.

[11] I summoned drought against the land, against the hills, against the grain, the new wine, the olive oil, against everything the ground brings forth, against people and livestock, against every labor of your hands.

[12] Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, along with all the remnant of the people, listened to the voice of the LORD their God — to the words of Haggai the prophet, because the LORD their God had sent him. And the people stood in awe before the LORD.

[13] Then Haggai, the LORD's messenger, delivered the LORD's message to the people: I am with you — declares the LORD.

[14] And the LORD stirred the spirit of Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and the spirit of Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and the spirit of all the remnant of the people. They came and went to work on the house of the LORD of Armies, their God,

[15] on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month, in the second year of Darius the king.

[1] On the twenty-first day of the seventh month, the word of the LORD came through Haggai the prophet:

[2] "Speak to Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, to Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and to everyone left of the people. Ask them this:

[4] But be strong now, Zerubbabel — the LORD declares it. Be strong, Joshua son of Jehozadak, high priest. Be strong, all you people of the land — the LORD declares it. Get to work. I am with you — the LORD of Hosts declares it.

[6] For this is what the LORD of Hosts says: 'A little while longer, and one more time I will shake the heavens and the earth, the sea and the dry land.

[7] I will shake every nation, and the treasures of all the nations will come pouring in. I will fill this house with glory,' says the LORD of Hosts.

[8] 'The silver is mine. The gold is mine,' declares the LORD of Hosts.

[10] On the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month, in the second year of Darius, the word of the LORD came to Haggai the prophet:

[11] "This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Put a legal question to the priests. Ask them:

[13] Then Haggai said, "And if someone defiled by contact with a corpse touches any of these — does it become unclean?" The priests answered, "Yes. It becomes unclean."

[14] Then Haggai answered: "So it is with this people, this nation in my sight — declares the LORD. So it is with everything their hands produce. Whatever they offer here — it's unclean.

[16] When someone came expecting twenty measures of grain, there were ten. When someone came to the winepress to draw fifty, there were twenty.
