[6] The waters of Nimrim — dried up.
The grass — withered. The new growth — gone.
Not a green thing left.

[7] So they haul off everything they ever earned,
every stash they ever saved,
and drag it across the Wadi of the Willows.

[8] The wailing has gone around the whole border of Moab.
The lament reaches Eglaim. It reaches Beer-elim.

[9] The waters of Dimon run red with blood —
and I am not finished with Dimon yet.
A lion is coming for whoever escaped Moab,
for whoever is still left in the land.

[1] Send the lambs to the ruler of the land — from Sela across the wilderness, up to the mountain of the Daughter of Zion.

[2] Like birds shaken from the nest, scattering in panic, the women of Moab huddle at the fords of the Arnon.

[3] "Give us counsel. Make a decision. At high noon, throw your shadow over us like night itself. Hide the hunted. Don't betray the runaway.

[4] Let Moab's refugees stay with you. Be their shelter from the destroyer." — Because the oppressor will end. The destruction will stop. The trampler will vanish from the land.

[5] And a throne will be set up in steadfast love. On it will sit — in the tent of David — a judge who hunts down justice and runs fast toward righteousness.

[6] We've heard about Moab's pride — how massive it is — its arrogance, its conceit, its rage. But its boasting is hollow.

[7] So Moab wails for Moab — every last one of them wailing. Mourn for the raisin-cakes of Kir-hareseth — utterly crushed.

[8] The fields of Heshbon are withered. The vine of Sibmah — whose tendrils once reached Jazer, wandered into the wilderness, whose shoots stretched out and crossed the sea — the lords of the nations have hacked down its choicest branches.

[9] So I weep with Jazer for the vine of Sibmah. I drench you with my tears, Heshbon and Elealeh — because over your summer fruit and your harvest, the war-shout has fallen.

[10] Joy is stripped from the orchards. Gladness is gone. In the vineyards — no singing, no cheering. No one treads wine in the presses. The shouting is silenced.

[11] My insides moan for Moab like a harp string. My core aches for Kir-hareseth.

[12] When Moab shows up at the high place, exhausting itself in worship, going into its sanctuary to pray — it accomplishes nothing.

[13] This is the word the LORD spoke against Moab in time past.

[14] But now the LORD speaks again: Within three years — counted exactly, like a hired worker counts his days — Moab's glory will be reduced to nothing, despite all its noise. The survivors will be few, weak, and irrelevant.

[1] A verdict against Damascus:

Look at it — Damascus, finished as a city.
A heap of rubble, nothing more.

[2] The towns of Aroer, abandoned.
Left to the flocks.
Sheep will lie down there with no one to scare them off.

[3] Ephraim loses its fortress.
Damascus loses its kingdom.
And what's left of Aram will match the glory of Israel's exiled sons —
which is to say, nothing at all.
So says the LORD of Armies.

[4] On that day Jacob's weight will waste away.
The fat of his body will melt off his bones.

[5] It will be like a harvester gathering standing grain —
his arm sweeping in the heads.
Like a gleaner picking through the Valley of Rephaim,
finding only what the reapers missed.

[6] A few stragglers will be left —
the way an olive tree, beaten down,
keeps two or three olives at the very top,
four or five clinging to the outer branches.
So says the LORD, the God of Israel.

[7] On that day, people will finally look up to their Maker.
Their eyes will turn toward the Holy One of Israel.

[8] They'll stop staring at the altars their own hands built.
They'll stop admiring the sacred poles and incense stands their fingers crafted.

[9] On that day, their fortified cities will look like the abandoned ruins of the Hivites and Amorites —
places left empty when Israel first came through.
Desolation, top to bottom.

[10] Because you forgot the God who saves you.
You never remembered the Rock who shelters you.
So go ahead — plant your lovely gardens,
set out your imported vines.

[11] The day you plant them, watch them sprout.
The morning you sow, watch the blossoms come.
But the harvest will vanish
on the day of disease and incurable pain.

[12] Listen — the roar of nations,
roaring like the roar of the sea.
The rumble of peoples,
rumbling like mighty waters thundering.

[13] Yes, nations thunder like crashing waves —
but God rebukes them, and they run far away,
scattered like chaff blown off mountain ridges,
like tumbleweed spinning before a storm.

[14] At dusk — terror.
By dawn — gone.
This is the portion of those who plunder us,
the lot of those who loot us.

[1] Trouble for the land of buzzing wings beyond the rivers of Cush —

[2] the land that sends envoys by sea, skimming the waters in papyrus skiffs. Go, swift messengers, to a nation tall and bronzed, to a people feared near and far, a nation of relentless force, the land carved by rivers.

[3] All you inhabitants of the world, every dweller on earth — when the banner lifts on the mountains, look. When the trumpet sounds, listen.

[4] This is what the LORD told me: From where I sit, I will watch in stillness — like heat shimmering in the sun, like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest.

[5] Because before the harvest, when the bloom is done and the flower becomes a ripening grape, He will slice off the shoots with pruning knives, hack away the spreading branches.

[6] All of it left for the mountain birds of prey and the wild beasts of the land — the birds will feast on it through the summer, the beasts will winter on its remains.

[7] At that time, a gift will be brought to the LORD of Armies from a people tall and bronzed, from a people feared near and far, a nation of relentless force, the land carved by rivers — brought to the place where the name of the LORD of Armies dwells, to Mount Zion.

[1] A dispatch concerning Egypt:

Watch — the LORD comes riding a swift cloud, bearing down on Egypt. The idols of Egypt shudder before his face, and the heart of Egypt liquefies inside her.

[2] "I will turn Egyptian against Egyptian — brother knifing brother, neighbor stabbing neighbor, city warring against city, kingdom against kingdom.

[3] Egypt's spirit will drain out of her. I will swallow her strategy whole. They will run to their idols, their mutterers, their mediums, their fortune-tellers — anything.

[4] And I will hand Egypt over to a brutal master. A savage king will rule them." So declares the Sovereign, the LORD of Armies.

[5] The waters of the sea will fail. The river will run dry — bone dry.

[6] The canals will reek. The Nile's branches will shrink and turn fetid. Reeds and rushes will rot.

[7] The plants along the Nile, along the riverbanks, every sown field — withered, blown away, gone.

[8] The fishermen will weep. Every man who casts a hook into the Nile will mourn. Those who spread nets across the water — their livelihoods will collapse.

[9] The linen workers will despair. The weavers of fine cloth will go pale.

[10] The pillars of the land will be crushed. Every wage earner will be sick at heart.

[11] The princes of Zoan are pure fools. Pharaoh's wisest counselors give counsel that is sheer stupidity. How can you say to Pharaoh, "I am a son of the sages, a son of ancient kings"?

[12] Where are they now — these wise men of yours? Let them tell you, let them figure out what the LORD of Armies has decreed against Egypt.

[13] The princes of Zoan have played the fool. The princes of Memphis have been duped. The chieftains of her tribes have led Egypt staggering off course.

[14] The LORD has poured a spirit of vertigo into her. They have sent Egypt reeling through every act she attempts — like a drunk slipping in his own vomit.

[15] Nothing Egypt can do will work — not head, not tail, not palm branch, not reed.

[16] In that day, Egypt will be like women — trembling, terrified at the lifted hand of the LORD of Armies, the hand he raises against her.

[17] And the land of Judah will become Egypt's nightmare. Just the mention of it will throw them into panic, because of what the LORD of Armies is plotting against them.

[18] In that day, five cities in Egypt will speak the language of Canaan and swear allegiance to the LORD of Armies. One of them will be called City of the Sun.

[19] In that day, an altar to the LORD will stand in the heart of Egypt, and a pillar to the LORD at her border —

[20] a sign and a witness to the LORD of Armies in Egypt. When they cry out to the LORD under their oppressors, he will send them a savior, a champion, and he will rescue them.

[21] The LORD will make himself known to Egypt. The Egyptians will know the LORD in that day. They will worship with sacrifice and offering. They will make vows to the LORD and keep them.

[22] The LORD will strike Egypt — strike and heal. They will turn back to the LORD, and he will hear them, and he will heal them.

[23] In that day, a highway will run from Egypt to Assyria. Assyrians will travel into Egypt, Egyptians into Assyria, and Egypt will worship alongside Assyria.

[24] In that day, Israel will be the third with Egypt and Assyria — a blessing in the middle of the earth,

[25] whom the LORD of Armies has blessed, saying: "Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Israel my inheritance."

[1] The year the Assyrian commander — sent by Sargon, king of Assyria — marched on Ashdod, attacked it, and took it,

[2] in that year the LORD spoke through Isaiah son of Amoz. He said, "Strip the sackcloth off your body. Take the sandals off your feet." Isaiah obeyed — walking stripped and barefoot.

[3] Then the LORD said: "My servant Isaiah has gone naked and barefoot for three years. He is a sign. He is an omen. Against Egypt. Against Cush.

[4] This is how the king of Assyria will drive the captives of Egypt and the exiles of Cush — young and old, stripped naked, barefoot, backsides exposed. Egypt's shame, marched off into the world.

[5] And those who pinned their hopes on Cush, who bragged about Egypt — they will be shattered. Humiliated.

[6] On that day the people who live along this coast will say: 'Look at them. This is where we ran for help. This is who we counted on to save us from the king of Assyria. And now — what hope is left for us?'"

[1] An oracle against the Wasteland by the Sea:

Like storms tearing across the Negev, it comes — out of the desert, out of a land that terrifies. [2] A brutal vision has been shown to me: the betrayer betrays, the destroyer destroys. Rise up, Elam. Lay siege, Media. Every groan she caused — I am ending it.

[3] So my body convulses. Pain seizes me like a woman in labor. I am too wrecked to hear, too shattered to see. [4] My mind reels. Terror grips me. The twilight I once loved has become a horror to me.

[5] They set the table. They spread the rug. They eat. They drink. — Get up, you officers! Oil your shields!

[6] This is what the Lord said to me: Go. Post a watchman. Have him report what he sees. [7] When he spots riders in pairs, columns of donkeys, columns of camels — tell him to listen hard, listen with everything in him.

[8] Then the watchman cried out: My lord, I stand on the tower all day long. Night after night I keep my post. [9] And look — here they come! A man in a chariot, riders in pairs. And he calls back the answer:

Fallen. Fallen is Babylon. Every image of her gods — smashed across the ground.

[10] O my threshed people, child of the threshing floor — what I have heard from the Lord of Armies, the God of Israel, I have told you.

[11] An oracle against Dumah:

Someone calls to me from Seir: Watchman, what's left of the night? Watchman, how much night remains?

[12] The watchman answers: Morning is coming — and night again. If you want to ask, ask. Come back. Ask again.

[13] An oracle against Arabia:

You will camp in the scrublands of Arabia, caravans of Dedan. [14] Bring water to the thirsty, you who live in Tema. Meet the refugee with bread. [15] They are fleeing — fleeing the sword, the drawn blade, the bent bow, the full weight of war.

[16] This is what the Lord told me: Within one year — counted exactly, the way a hired worker counts his days — all the splendor of Kedar will be finished. [17] The archers left, the warriors of Kedar — only a handful will remain.

The Lord, the God of Israel, has spoken.

[1] A dispatch concerning the Valley of Vision:

What's wrong with you?
Why has the whole city climbed to the rooftops,
[2] you city of uproar, you town drunk on noise,
you party that won't quit?
Your dead weren't killed by the sword.
They didn't fall in battle.
[3] Your commanders fled together —
captured without a single bow drawn.
Every fugitive found was rounded up,
no matter how far they'd run.

[4] So leave me alone. Let me weep my eyes out.
Don't try to comfort me
over the ruin of my people.

[5] Because the Lord — the Lord of Armies — has a day:
a day of panic, of trampling, of confusion
in the Valley of Vision.
Walls breaking down,
screams reaching the mountains.

[6] Elam picked up the quiver,
chariots and horsemen on the move,
Kir uncovered the shield.
[7] Your finest valleys filled with chariots.
Cavalry posted at the city gate.

[8] He stripped away Judah's last defense.

That day you turned your eyes to the armory in the House of the Forest.
[9] You counted the cracks in the City of David —
so many of them.
You stockpiled water from the lower pool.
[10] You took inventory of the houses in Jerusalem,
tearing some down to reinforce the wall.
[11] You dug a reservoir between the two walls
for the water of the old pool.

But you never looked at the One who made all this happen.
You never paid attention to the One who planned it long ago.

[12] That day the Lord — the Lord of Armies — called you
to weeping, to wailing,
to shaved heads and sackcloth.
[13] Instead — look at you:
celebration, party, slaughtering cattle, butchering sheep,
meat and wine flowing.
"Eat, drink — tomorrow we die!"

[14] The Lord of Armies revealed himself in my ears:
"This sin will not be wiped clean until you're dead."
So says the Lord, the Lord of Armies.

[15] This is what the Lord — the Lord of Armies — says:

Go. Confront this steward, this Shebna,
the one running the palace:
[16] What are you doing here? Who do you think you are,
carving yourself a tomb up high,
chiseling a resting place into the rock?
[17] Watch. The Lord is about to hurl you, strong man —
hurl you violently,
[18] wind you up like a ball
and throw you into a vast, open country.
There you'll die.
There your glory chariots will lie —
you, the shame of your master's house.
[19] I'm pulling you from your post.
You'll be torn out of your station.

[20] That day I will summon my servant Eliakim son of Hilkiah.
[21] I'll dress him in your robe.
I'll fasten your sash on him.
I'll hand him your authority.
He'll be a father to the people of Jerusalem
and to the house of Judah.
[22] I'll lay the key to the house of David on his shoulder —
what he opens, no one shuts.
What he shuts, no one opens.
[23] I'll drive him in like a tent peg in solid ground.
He'll become a throne of honor for his father's house.
[24] And they'll hang everything on him —
the whole weight of the family,
all the offspring and offshoots,
every cup and pitcher.

[25] In that day — declares the Lord of Armies —
the peg driven into solid ground will give way.
It will be sheared off and fall,
and everything hanging on it will crash down.

The Lord has spoken.

[1] A verdict against Tyre.

Wail, you ships of Tarshish—your harbor is gone, your home port wiped out. Word reached them on the way back from Cyprus.

[2] Fall silent, you who live on the coast, you merchants of Sidon whose envoys crossed the sea and made you rich.

[3] Across the deep waters came the grain of the Nile, the harvest of the great river—Tyre was the marketplace of nations.

[4] Be ashamed, Sidon. The sea itself speaks, the fortress of the sea: "I never went into labor, never gave birth, never raised young men or brought up daughters."

[5] When the news reaches Egypt, they will writhe at the report from Tyre.

[6] Run for Tarshish. Wail, you who live on the coast.

[7] Is this your party city? The one whose roots run back to ancient days, whose feet carried her to colonize distant shores?

[8] Who planned this against Tyre, the city that handed out crowns, whose traders were princes, whose merchants were the honored of the earth?

[9] The LORD of Heaven's Armies planned it—to strip the pride from every glory, to humiliate everyone the earth holds in honor.

[10] Spread out across your land like the Nile in flood, daughter of Tarshish—the harbor that held you is gone.

[11] He stretched his hand over the sea and made kingdoms tremble. The LORD has issued the order against Canaan: tear down her strongholds.

[12] He said: "No more partying for you, ravaged virgin daughter of Sidon. Get up, cross over to Cyprus—even there you'll find no rest."

[13] Look at the land of the Chaldeans—this people that no longer exists. Assyria turned it over to desert creatures. They raised their siege towers, stripped her palaces bare, reduced her to rubble.

[14] Wail, you ships of Tarshish. Your fortress is destroyed.

[15] On that day Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years—the lifespan of a king. After seventy years, what happens to Tyre will be like the song about the prostitute:

[16] "Take up your harp, walk the city, forgotten whore. Play your sweetest, sing your songs—maybe someone will remember you."

[17] At the end of seventy years, the LORD will pay attention to Tyre. She'll return to her trade, sell herself again to every kingdom on the face of the earth.

[18] But this time her profits and her earnings will be set apart for the LORD—not hoarded, not stockpiled. Her trade will provide for those who live in the LORD's presence: abundant food and elegant clothing.

[1] Watch — the LORD is about to gut the earth, strip it bare, twist its face, scatter everyone who lives on it.

[2] No one gets a pass. Priest or peasant, master or slave, mistress or maid, seller or buyer, lender or borrower, creditor or debtor — same fate for all.

[3] The earth will be stripped to nothing, plundered to the bone. The LORD has spoken it. That settles it.

[4] The earth withers and wastes away. The world languishes, fading. Even the proud heights of humanity collapse.

[5] The earth lies poisoned beneath those who walk it — they trampled the teachings, trashed the statutes, shattered the ancient covenant.

[6] So a curse devours the land, and those who live there bear the guilt. The inhabitants burn away, and only a handful are left.

[7] The new wine dries up. The vine wilts. Everyone who used to laugh is sighing now.

[8] The tambourines have gone silent. The party is over. The harp has nothing to say.

[9] No one sings over wine anymore. The beer turns bitter in the mouth of the drinker.

[10] The city of chaos lies broken. Every house is shut tight — no one comes in.

[11] Out in the streets they cry for wine. Every joy has turned to dusk. The earth's gladness is exiled.

[12] What remains of the city is wreckage. Its gates are pounded into ruin.

[13] This is how it will be across the earth, among the nations — like an olive tree shaken empty, like gleanings after the grape harvest is done.

[14] But then — voices. Singing. From the west the survivors shout, lifting their voices for the majesty of the LORD.

[15] So glorify the LORD in the east. In the coastlands of the sea — lift up the name of the LORD, the God of Israel.

[16] From the ends of the earth we hear songs: "Glory to the Righteous One." But I say, "I waste away, I waste away — woe to me. The traitors betray. The traitors keep on betraying."

[17] Terror and pit and trap are coming for you, inhabitant of the earth.

[18] Whoever runs from the sound of terror will fall into the pit. Whoever climbs out of the pit will be caught in the trap. The floodgates of the sky are opened. The foundations of the earth are quaking.

[19] The earth is breaking apart, splitting open, staggering.

[20] It reels like a drunk, sways like a shack in a storm. Its rebellion lies heavy on it — it falls, and it will not get up again.

[21] On that day the LORD will bring judgment on the host of heaven above and on the kings of the earth below.

[22] They will be herded together like prisoners into a pit, locked in a dungeon — and after long days, called to account.

[23] The moon will blush. The sun will hide its face. Because the LORD of Armies will reign on Mount Zion, in Jerusalem — and his elders will see his glory blazing.

[1] LORD, you are my God. I will lift you high, I will praise your name — because you have done the impossible, plans laid down in ages past, faithful and sure, every one of them coming true.

[2] You turned the city into a pile of stones, the fortress into ruins. The proud citadel of strangers is a city no more — it will never be rebuilt.

[3] So the nations of muscle will honor you. The cities of ruthless people will fear you.

[4] You have been a shelter for the poor, a shelter for the desperate when the storm hit, shade when the heat scorched. Because the breath of the ruthless is like rain pounding against a wall —

[5] like desert heat. You silence the roar of outsiders. The way clouds cool a burning afternoon, you cut down the victory song of the ruthless.

[6] On this mountain, the LORD of armies will throw a feast for all peoples — aged wine and rich food, the finest cuts, the wine strained clear.

[7] On this mountain he will tear away the shroud draped over every nation, the burial cloth covering every people.

[8] He will swallow death forever. The Lord GOD will wipe the tears from every face. He will lift the disgrace of his people off the whole earth. The LORD has spoken.

[9] On that day they will say: "Look — this is our God. We waited for him, and he saved us. This is the LORD. We waited for him. Let's celebrate. Let's rejoice in his rescue."

[10] The LORD's hand will rest on this mountain. But Moab will be trampled where it stands, the way straw is trampled into a manure pit.

[11] They will stretch out their arms inside it like a swimmer reaching through water — and God will bring down their pride along with every clever motion of their hands.

[12] The high walls of your fortress — he will knock them down, level them, smash them into the dust.

[1] On that day, this song will rise in the land of Judah:

We have a fortress city.
God himself sets up its walls and ramparts as our salvation.

[2] Throw open the gates.
Let the righteous nation enter —
the one that keeps faith.

[3] The mind anchored in you,
you guard in perfect peace,
because it trusts you.

[4] Trust the LORD — always, forever.
The LORD himself is the Rock that outlasts every age.

[5] He brings down those who live on the heights.
The proud city — he flattens it.
He flattens it to the ground,
grinds it into the dust.

[6] Feet trample it now —
the feet of the oppressed,
the footsteps of the poor.

[7] The path of the righteous runs level.
You who are upright — you make their way smooth.

[8] Yes, LORD, walking the road of your judgments,
we wait for you.
Your name, the memory of you —
that is what our souls crave.

[9] My soul reaches for you in the night;
my spirit inside me searches for you at dawn.
For when your judgments touch the earth,
the world's inhabitants learn what righteousness is.

[10] Show grace to the wicked, and he never learns righteousness.
Even in a land of justice, he keeps twisting the truth,
blind to the majesty of the LORD.

[11] LORD, your hand is raised — and they refuse to see it.
Let them see. Let them be put to shame
by your zeal for your people.
Let the fire reserved for your enemies devour them.

[12] LORD, you will appoint peace for us,
because everything we have accomplished,
you accomplished for us.

[13] LORD our God,
other masters besides you have ruled us,
but we invoke only your name.

[14] They are dead — they will not live.
Shades — they will not rise.
You came against them, you wiped them out,
erased every trace of their memory.

[15] You expanded the nation, LORD —
you expanded the nation, made yourself glorious,
pushed back every border of the land.

[16] LORD, in their distress they searched for you,
poured out a whispered prayer
when your discipline pressed down on them.

[17] Like a pregnant woman writhing and crying out in her labor,
that is what we were before you, LORD.

[18] We were pregnant, we writhed —
and gave birth to wind.
We brought no salvation to the earth,
and no inhabitants of the world were born.

[19] But your dead will live.
Their bodies will rise.
Wake up and sing for joy, you who lie in the dust!
For your dew is the dew of light,
and the earth will give birth to the long-dead.

[20] Come, my people — enter your rooms,
shut the doors behind you.
Hide yourselves a little while,
until the fury passes through.

[21] Look: the LORD is leaving his place
to call the inhabitants of the earth to account for their guilt.
The earth will expose the blood spilled on her;
she will no longer cover her dead.

[1] On that day, the LORD will draw his sword — heavy, honed, lethal — and bring it down on Leviathan, the serpent that twists and bolts, the dragon coiled in the sea. He will kill the monster.

[2] On that day — sing it: A vineyard of wine, the kind you long for.

[3] I, the LORD, tend it. I water it every moment. I guard it night and day so no one can touch it.

[4] I'm not angry anymore. But if briars and thorns come at me, I'll march straight through and burn them to ash.

[5] Unless they grab hold of my protection — then let them make peace with me, peace, yes, with me.

[6] Days are coming when Jacob will sink down roots, when Israel will bud and bloom and fill the whole earth with fruit.

[7] Did the LORD strike Israel the way he struck those who struck Israel? Did he slaughter them the way he slaughtered their killers?

[8] No — measured, exiled, sent away. He drove them out with his fierce breath, a desert wind on a brutal day.

[9] And here is how Jacob's guilt gets paid down, the full price of his sin removed: when he grinds every pagan altar into chalk dust, when no Asherah pole stands, no incense altar remains.

[10] The fortified city sits alone, abandoned, deserted like wilderness. Calves graze there, lie down there, stripping the branches bare.

[11] When the boughs dry out, they snap off, and women come gather them for kindling. These people have no understanding — so their Maker shows them no compassion, the one who formed them grants them no favor.

[12] On that day, the LORD will thresh from the Euphrates to the Wadi of Egypt — and you, Israel, will be gathered up one by one.

[13] On that day, a great trumpet will blast — and those lost in Assyria, those scattered in Egypt, will come home and bow down before the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem.

[1] Disaster waits for the proud crown of Ephraim's drunks, for the wilting flower of their glory perched atop the lush valley — that crown worn by men knocked sideways with wine.

[2] Watch. The Lord has someone — strong, overwhelming. Like a hailstorm. Like a wind that splits trees. Like a flood of water rising, surging, smashing everything to the ground with his hand.

[3] That proud crown of Ephraim's drunks — it will be trampled underfoot.

[4] And the wilting flower of their glory, perched above the rich valley, will be like the first ripe fig before summer arrives — the moment someone spots it, they swallow it whole.

[5] On that day, the Lord of Hosts himself will be the crown of beauty, the diadem of splendor, for what remains of his people.

[6] He will be a spirit of justice for the one who sits in judgment, and strength for those who push the battle back to the gate.

[7] But these too stagger from wine and stumble from drink — priest and prophet alike, reeling, swallowed up, drowned in the stuff, lost in their visions, tripping over their verdicts.

[8] Every table is covered in vomit. Filth everywhere. Not a clean spot left.

[9] "Who does he think he's teaching?" they sneer. "Who's supposed to understand this message? Babies just weaned? Toddlers pulled from the breast?

[10] Blah blah blah, yada yada yada, a little here, a little there."

[11] Fine. Then God will speak to this people in stammering lips and a foreign tongue —

[12] the same God who once told them: "Here is rest — let the weary rest. Here is repose." And they would not listen.

[13] So the word of the Lord will come to them: blah blah blah, yada yada yada, a little here, a little there — until they walk off and fall backward, broken, snared, and caught.

[14] So listen, you mockers, you rulers of this people in Jerusalem —

[15] you who brag, "We cut a deal with death. We signed a contract with the grave. When the raging flood sweeps through, it won't touch us — we've made lies our refuge, deception our hiding place."

[16] Here is what the Lord God says: I am laying a stone in Zion — a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a foundation that holds. Whoever trusts it will not panic.

[17] I will stretch justice as the measuring line and righteousness as the plumb. Hail will sweep away your refuge of lies. Water will flood your hiding place.

[18] Your deal with death? Cancelled. Your contract with the grave? Void. When the raging flood comes through, you will be trampled flat beneath it.

[19] Every time it passes, it will seize you — morning after morning, day and night — and just understanding the message will be sheer terror.

[20] The bed is too short to stretch out on. The blanket too narrow to wrap around you.

[21] The Lord will rise up the way he did at Mount Perazim, rage the way he did in the Valley of Gibeon — to do his work, his strange work, to perform his deed, his alien deed.

[22] So drop the mockery, or your chains will tighten. I have heard it from the Lord, the Lord of Hosts: destruction is decreed, decided, for the whole land.

[23] Listen — hear my voice. Pay attention — hear what I am saying.

[24] Does the farmer plow all day long to plant? Does he keep breaking up the soil and harrowing it forever?

[25] No — once he levels the surface, he scatters dill, sows cumin, plants wheat in rows, barley in its place, spelt at the border.

[26] His God instructs him. His God teaches him the right way.

[27] You don't thresh dill with a sledge or roll a cart wheel over cumin — you beat dill with a stick and cumin with a rod.

[28] Grain for bread gets crushed — but not endlessly. The thresher's wheels roll over it, the horses tread it — but he doesn't grind it to dust.

[29] This too comes from the Lord of Hosts: his counsel a wonder, his wisdom magnificent.

[1] Disaster waits for you, Ariel — Ariel, the city where David made his camp. Pile year on year, let the festivals cycle through their rounds —

[2] then I will tighten the noose around Ariel. There will be grief, there will be groaning, and she will become to me what her name means: an altar-hearth running with blood.

[3] I will set my camp around you on every side. I will throw up siegeworks against you and raise ramparts to hem you in.

[4] You will be driven low — speaking from the dirt, your words muffled in the dust. Your voice will rise from the ground like a ghost's whisper, a thin sound seeping up from below.

[5] But the swarm of your enemies will turn to dust blown fine, the mob of tyrants like chaff scattered on the wind. Suddenly. In an instant.

[6] The LORD of Armies will come for you — with thunder, with earthquake, with deafening roar, with whirlwind and storm and flames that consume.

[7] And the horde of all those nations marching against Ariel — every one who attacked her, who built siegeworks, who pressed her hard — they will vanish like a dream, like a vision in the night.

[8] Like a starving man who dreams he's eating, then wakes still hollow. Like a parched man who dreams he's drinking, then wakes still cracked with thirst — that is how it will be for the horde of nations massed against Mount Zion.

[9] Stop in your tracks and be stunned. Blind yourselves and stay blind. Drunk — but not on wine. Staggering — but not from beer.

[10] The LORD has poured over you a spirit of deep sleep. He has sealed shut your eyes — the prophets. He has covered your heads — the seers.

[11] This whole vision has become for you nothing but words in a sealed scroll. Hand it to someone who can read and say, "Read this," and he will say, "I can't — it's sealed."

[12] Hand it to someone who can't read and say, "Read this," and he will say, "I don't know how to read."

[13] The Lord says: These people come close with their mouths, they honor me with their lips — but their hearts have wandered far from me. Their reverence for me is nothing but a script they've memorized, a rule taught by men.

[14] So watch — I am about to do something stunning to this people. Stunning and strange. The wisdom of their wise will collapse. The cleverness of their clever ones will vanish.

[15] Disaster waits for those who burrow deep to hide their plans from the LORD, who do their work in the dark and say, "Who sees us? Who knows?"

[16] You have it all backwards. Shall the potter be reckoned as clay? Shall the thing that was made say of its maker, "He didn't make me"? Shall the pot say of the potter, "He has no idea what he's doing"?

[17] Just a little while longer — and Lebanon will turn into farmland, and the farmland will look like wild forest.

[18] On that day the deaf will hear the words of the scroll. Out of the gloom and darkness, the eyes of the blind will see.

[19] The humble will overflow with joy in the LORD. The poorest among the people will exult in the Holy One of Israel.

[20] Because the tyrant will be finished. The mocker will be gone. Everyone who lay awake plotting evil will be cut off —

[21] those who frame an innocent man with a word, who set traps for the one defending justice at the city gate, who twist the truth to ruin the righteous with lies.

[22] So this is what the LORD — who redeemed Abraham — says about the house of Jacob: Jacob will not be shamed any longer. His face will not go pale again.

[23] When he sees his children — the work of my hands among him — he will hold my name as holy. He will hold the Holy One of Jacob as holy. He will stand in awe of the God of Israel.

[24] Those whose spirits wandered off will come to understanding. Those who grumbled will learn the lesson.

[1] Disaster, says the LORD, on these defiant children—drawing up plans that aren't mine, cutting deals not breathed by my Spirit, stacking sin on top of sin.

[2] They head down to Egypt without asking me a thing, running to Pharaoh for cover, hiding in the shadow of Egypt.

[3] Pharaoh's protection will be your humiliation. Egypt's shadow will be your shame.

[4] Their envoys have already reached Zoan. Their delegates have made it to Hanes.

[5] But all of them will be disgraced over a people who can't help them—who bring no aid, no advantage, only shame and reproach.

[6] An oracle concerning the beasts of the Negev: Through a land of trouble and torment—of lioness and roaring lion, viper and darting serpent—they haul their wealth on the backs of donkeys, their treasures on the humps of camels, to a people who deliver nothing.

[7] Egypt's help is empty wind. So I call her Rahab the Do-Nothing.

[8] Now go. Write it on a tablet in their presence. Inscribe it on a scroll, so it stands as a witness for the days to come, forever and ever.

[9] These are rebel children. Lying children. Children who refuse to hear the LORD's instruction.

[10] To the seers they say: "Stop seeing." To the prophets: "Don't tell us the truth. Tell us flattering things. Prophesy illusions.

[11] Get off the path. Turn aside from the road. And get the Holy One of Israel out of our faces."

[12] So this is what the Holy One of Israel says: Because you've spurned this word and put your trust in oppression and crookedness, and leaned on them—

[13] this guilt will be like a crack spreading through a high wall, bulging out, until suddenly, in a single instant, it collapses.

[14] It will shatter like a clay pot smashed without mercy—not one fragment large enough to scoop coals from a fire or dip water from a cistern.

[15] For this is what the Lord GOD, the Holy One of Israel, says: In turning back and resting you will be saved. In quietness and trust is your strength. But you wanted none of it.

[16] You said: "No—we'll ride out on horses." Then ride you will—straight into flight. You said: "We'll mount swift steeds." So swift will be your pursuers.

[17] A thousand of you will run from the threat of one. From the threat of five you will all flee, until you're left like a flagpole on a mountaintop, a lone banner on a hill.

[18] And yet—the LORD waits to be gracious to you. He rises up to show you mercy. For the LORD is a God of justice. Blessed are all who wait for him.

[19] People of Zion, who live in Jerusalem—you will weep no more. He will be gracious the moment he hears you cry. The instant he hears, he answers.

[20] Though the Lord has fed you the bread of adversity and the water of affliction, your Teacher will no longer hide. Your eyes will see your Teacher.

[21] And whenever you veer right or left, your ears will hear a voice behind you saying: "This is the way. Walk in it."

[22] You'll take your silver-plated idols and your gold-coated images and treat them as filth. "Get out," you'll say to them, as if to a menstrual rag.

[23] Then he'll send rain for the seed you sow, and the bread from your land will be rich and abundant. In that day your livestock will graze in wide-open pastures.

[24] The oxen and donkeys that work your soil will eat seasoned fodder, winnowed with shovel and fork.

[25] On every high mountain and every lofty hill, streams will run with water—on the day of great slaughter, when the towers fall.

[26] The moon will shine like the sun, and the sun's light will blaze seven times brighter—the light of seven days in one—on the day the LORD binds up his people's wounds and heals the bruises of his blow.

[27] Look—the Name of the LORD comes from far away, burning with anger, dense with smoke. His lips overflow with fury. His tongue is a devouring fire.

[28] His breath is a flood-tide rising to the neck, sifting the nations in the sieve of destruction, slipping a bridle into the jaws of the peoples to lead them astray.

[29] But for you—there will be singing, like the night of a sacred festival. Your heart will rejoice like one who marches to the flute up to the mountain of the LORD, to the Rock of Israel.

[30] The LORD will make his majestic voice heard. He will bring down his arm with raging anger and devouring flame—with cloudburst and tempest and hailstones.

[31] At the LORD's voice, Assyria will be shattered—struck down by his rod.

[32] And every stroke of the staff of judgment the LORD lays on him will fall to the beat of tambourines and lyres, as he swings his arm and fights against them.

[33] The pyre has been ready for ages—prepared, in fact, for the king. Deep and wide, piled high with fire and timber. The breath of the LORD, like a torrent of burning sulfur, sets it ablaze.

[1] Disaster waits for those who run to Egypt for help — who bet everything on horses, who trust chariots because there are so many of them, who count the cavalry but never look to the Holy One of Israel, never consult the LORD.

[2] But he is wise too — and he brings disaster, and he does not take back his words. He will rise against the house of evildoers, against the help that comes from men who do evil.

[3] The Egyptians are men, not God. Their horses are flesh, not spirit. When the LORD stretches out his hand, the helper stumbles, the one being helped collapses, and they all go down together.

[4] This is what the LORD said to me: As a lion growls — a great lion over its kill — and a whole company of shepherds called out against it cannot frighten it by their shouting or make it flinch at their noise, so the LORD of Hosts will come down to wage war on Mount Zion, on its heights.

[5] Like birds circling overhead, the LORD of Hosts will shield Jerusalem — shielding and rescuing, passing over and delivering.

[6] Come back, children of Israel — come back to the One you have so deeply betrayed.

[7] On that day every one of you will throw away the silver idols and the gold idols your own sinful hands made for you.

[8] Assyria will fall — but not by a human sword. A sword no man wields will devour them. They will flee before the blade, and their young warriors will be put to forced labor.

[9] Their rock will dissolve in terror; their officers will panic and abandon the banner. So declares the LORD — whose fire burns in Zion, whose furnace blazes in Jerusalem.

[1] Watch for it: a king is coming who will reign in righteousness, and princes who will rule with justice.

[2] Each one will be a shelter from the wind, a hiding place from the storm — streams of water in a desert, the shadow of a massive rock in a land burned dry.

[3] Then the eyes of those who see will not be sealed shut, and the ears of those who hear will actually listen.

[4] The reckless heart will learn discernment, and the stammering tongue will speak fluently, clearly.

[5] No more will the fool be called noble, no more will the swindler be praised as generous.

[6] Because the fool speaks foolishness, and his mind churns out evil — practicing godlessness, peddling lies about the Lord, leaving the hungry empty and cutting off water from the thirsty.

[7] The swindler's methods are wicked. He plots schemes to destroy the poor with lies, even when the needy plead a just case.

[8] But the noble person plans noble things, and stands by what is noble.

[9] Get up, you women lounging in comfort — listen to me. You daughters who feel so secure — hear what I'm saying.

[10] In a little over a year, you complacent ones will tremble. The grape harvest will fail. No fruit will come in.

[11] Shudder, you comfortable ones. Tremble, you who feel so safe. Strip yourselves bare. Tie sackcloth around your waists.

[12] Beat your breasts for the pleasant fields, for the fruitful vines —

[13] for the soil of my people, where thorns and briars will rise up, for every happy house in the joyful city.

[14] The palace is abandoned. The crowded city is empty. Fortress and watchtower become a wasteland forever — wild donkeys' delight, a pasture for flocks.

[15] Until the Spirit is poured out on us from above, and the wilderness becomes orchard, and what we now call orchard looks like a forest.

[16] Then justice will move into the wilderness, and righteousness will live in the orchard.

[17] The work of righteousness will be peace. The result of righteousness — calm and confidence, forever.

[18] My people will live in peaceful homes, in secure dwellings, in untroubled places of rest.

[19] Though the forest comes crashing down in hail, and the city is leveled to the ground —

[20] you will be blessed, you who sow beside every stream, who let your ox and donkey roam free.

[1] Doom to you, destroyer—you who were never destroyed. Doom to you, betrayer—you whom no one ever betrayed. When you finish destroying, you will be destroyed. When you stop betraying, you will be betrayed.

[2] Lord, show us mercy. We have waited for you. Be our strength every morning, our deliverance when the trouble closes in.

[3] At the thunder of your voice, peoples scatter. When you rise up, nations fly apart.

[4] Your plunder is gathered like locusts gathering grain—men swarm over it like a horde of insects on a field.

[5] The Lord is lifted high—he lives in the heights. He fills Zion with justice and right.

[6] He will be the steady ground beneath your days, an abundance of rescue, wisdom, knowledge. Reverence for the Lord—that is the treasure.

[7] Listen—the warriors weep in the streets. The peace envoys break down sobbing.

[8] The highways lie empty. No traveler walks them. Treaties shattered. Witnesses despised. No one counts as human anymore.

[9] The land mourns and withers. Lebanon stands humiliated, rotting. Sharon has become a wasteland. Bashan and Carmel shake their leaves to the ground.

[10] Now I rise, says the Lord. Now I am lifted up. Now I show my full height.

[11] You conceive chaff. You give birth to stubble. Your own breath is a fire that will eat you alive.

[12] Whole peoples will burn to lime—thornbushes hacked down and thrown in the blaze.

[13] You far away—hear what I have done. You close at hand—acknowledge my power.

[14] In Zion the sinners are terrified. Trembling has seized the godless: Who among us can live with a consuming fire? Who among us can survive flames that never go out?

[15] The one who walks straight and speaks true, who refuses the profits of oppression, who shakes the bribe out of his hand, who stops his ears against bloody schemes and shuts his eyes against evil—

[16] that one will live on the heights. Rock fortresses will be his refuge. His bread will be given him. His water never fails.

[17] Your eyes will see the King in his beauty. They will gaze on a land that stretches far.

[18] Your mind will turn back to the old terror and ask: Where is the bureaucrat who counted us? Where is the one who weighed the tribute? Where is the one who tallied the towers?

[19] You will not see that brutal people again—the people whose speech you could not catch, whose stammering tongue made no sense.

[20] Look at Zion, our festival city. Your eyes will see Jerusalem—a quiet home, a tent that will never be moved, whose stakes will never be pulled, whose ropes will never snap.

[21] There the Lord himself will be our magnificent presence—a place of wide rivers and streams, where no enemy galley rows, where no warship passes through.

[22] For the Lord is our judge. The Lord is our lawgiver. The Lord is our King. He is the one who will save us.

[23] Your rigging hangs slack. It cannot hold the mast. It cannot spread the sail. Then plunder beyond counting will be divided up—even the lame will haul off the loot.

[24] No resident will say, I am sick. The people who live there will have their guilt lifted away.

[1] Come close, nations — listen. Peoples, pay attention. Let the earth hear, and everything that fills it; the world, and everything it produces.

[2] The LORD's fury is loose against all nations, his wrath against every army they've raised. He has marked them for destruction, handed them over to the slaughter.

[3] Their dead will be flung out unburied. The stench will rise from their corpses, and the mountains will run with their blood.

[4] The stars will rot in the sky. The heavens will roll up like a scroll. Every star will fall — like a withered leaf off a vine, like a shriveled fig dropping from the tree.

[5] My sword has drunk its fill in the heavens. Now watch — it comes down on Edom, on the people I have already sentenced.

[6] The LORD's sword runs red — dripping with blood, slick with fat — the blood of lambs and goats, the fat from the kidneys of rams. The LORD is holding a sacrifice in Bozrah, a great slaughter in the land of Edom.

[7] Wild oxen will fall with them, young bulls with the mighty bulls. The ground will be drunk on blood, the soil saturated with fat.

[8] This is the LORD's day of reckoning — a year of payback for Zion's cause.

[9] Edom's streams will turn to tar, its soil to sulfur, its land a burning pitch.

[10] Night and day it will not go out. Smoke will rise from it forever. From one generation to the next it will lie in ruins — no one passing through, not ever again.

[11] The desert owl and the screech owl will take it over. The great owl and the raven will nest there. God will stretch over it the measuring line of chaos, the plumb stones of emptiness.

[12] No nobles will be left to call out a kingdom. Every prince — gone.

[13] Thorns will overrun its palaces, nettles and brambles its strongholds. It will be a haunt for jackals, a courtyard for ostriches.

[14] Desert creatures will meet hyenas there. Wild goats will call to each other. There the night-demon will settle and find herself a resting place.

[15] The owl will make her nest there — lay her eggs, hatch them, gather her brood under her shadow. There the hawks will gather, each with its mate.

[16] Search the LORD's scroll and read: not one of these will be missing, none without its mate. His mouth has spoken it — his Spirit will gather them in.

[17] He himself has cast the lot for them. His hand has measured out their portion with the line. They will possess it forever, generation after generation making their home there.

[1] The wilderness will throw a party. The wasteland will burst into bloom — crocus everywhere, riotous color where there was only dust.

[2] It will bloom hard, sing out loud, drunk on joy. The glory of Lebanon will be handed to it, the splendor of Carmel and Sharon. They will see the glory of the LORD — the sheer magnificence of our God.

[3] So put strength back into the slack hands. Steady the knees that have given out.

[4] Tell the ones whose hearts are racing with fear: Hold on. Be strong. Don't be afraid. Look — your God is coming. Vengeance is coming with him. Divine payback. He himself is coming to rescue you.

[5] Then blind eyes will fly open. Deaf ears will be unstopped.

[6] The lame will leap like a deer. The mute tongue will break into singing. Water will erupt in the wilderness, streams cutting through the desert.

[7] The mirage will become a real lake. The parched ground, bubbling springs. Where jackals used to den down in the dust, grass will grow — reeds and rushes.

[8] And there will be a road there. A highway. They'll call it the Way of Holiness. The unclean won't travel it. It belongs to those who walk the way — and even a fool can't get lost on it.

[9] No lions there. No predator sets foot on it. Only the redeemed walk that road.

[10] The ransomed of the LORD will come home — back to Zion with singing, joy crowning their heads like a wreath that won't wilt. Gladness and joy will catch up to them, and sorrow and sighing will turn and run.

[1] In the fourteenth year of King Hezekiah's reign, Sennacherib king of Assyria came up against every fortified city in Judah and took them.

[2] From Lachish, the king of Assyria dispatched his field commander to Jerusalem with a heavy force to confront King Hezekiah. The commander took up position by the aqueduct of the upper pool, on the road to the Launderer's Field.

[3] Out came Eliakim son of Hilkiah, who managed the palace, along with Shebna the scribe and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder.

[4] The field commander said to them: "Tell Hezekiah this — from the Great King, the king of Assyria: What is this confidence you're leaning on?

[5] You think empty words count as strategy and strength in war? Who exactly are you trusting that you'd rebel against me?

[6] Egypt? That cracked reed of a staff? Lean on it and it punctures your hand. That's Pharaoh king of Egypt — to everyone who leans on him.

[7] And if you say to me, 'We trust in the LORD our God' — wasn't that the very one whose high places and altars Hezekiah tore down, telling Judah and Jerusalem, 'You'll worship before this one altar'?

[8] So make a wager with my master the king of Assyria: I'll hand you two thousand horses — if you can even find riders to put on them.

[9] How could you push back even the lowest-ranking officer of my master's servants? And you're trusting Egypt for chariots and horsemen?

[10] Did I come up to wreck this land without the LORD? The LORD himself told me: 'Go up against this land and destroy it.'"

[11] Then Eliakim, Shebna, and Joah said to the field commander: "Please — speak Aramaic to your servants. We understand it. Don't speak Hebrew with us in earshot of the people on the wall."

[12] But the commander shot back: "Did my master send me to speak these words to your master and to you? No — to the men sitting on the wall, who are about to eat their own dung and drink their own urine with you."

[13] Then he stood and shouted in Hebrew at the top of his lungs: "Listen to the words of the Great King, the king of Assyria!

[14] The king says: Don't let Hezekiah deceive you. He cannot save you.

[15] Don't let Hezekiah make you trust in the LORD by saying, 'The LORD will surely rescue us. This city will not be handed to the king of Assyria.'

[16] Don't listen to Hezekiah. The king of Assyria says: Make peace with me. Come out to me. Every one of you will eat from your own vine and your own fig tree and drink water from your own well —

[17] until I come and take you to a land like your own — a land of grain and new wine, a land of bread and vineyards.

[18] Don't let Hezekiah seduce you with 'The LORD will rescue us.' Did any god of any nation rescue his land from the hand of the king of Assyria?

[19] Where are the gods of Hamath and Arpad? Where are the gods of Sepharvaim? Did they rescue Samaria from my hand?

[20] Of all the gods of all these lands — which one rescued his land from my hand, that the LORD should rescue Jerusalem from my hand?"

[21] The people kept silent. Not a word answered him — the king's order had been clear: "Don't answer him."

[22] Then Eliakim son of Hilkiah, the palace manager, Shebna the scribe, and Joah son of Asaph, the recorder, came to Hezekiah with their clothes torn and reported the field commander's words.

[1] When King Hezekiah heard it, he tore his clothes, wrapped himself in sackcloth, and went into the house of the LORD.

[2] He sent Eliakim, who ran the palace, Shebna the scribe, and the senior priests — all draped in sackcloth — to the prophet Isaiah, son of Amoz.

[3] They told him: "Hezekiah says this — today is a day of agony, of rebuke, of disgrace. Children have come to the moment of birth, and there is no strength to push them out.

[4] Maybe the LORD your God heard what the Rabshakeh said — sent by the king of Assyria, his master, to mock the living God — and maybe he will answer the words your God heard. So lift up a prayer for the remnant that still survives."

[5] When Hezekiah's officials came to Isaiah,

[6] Isaiah told them: "Tell your master this — the LORD says: Don't be afraid of what you heard, those words the lackeys of the king of Assyria used to blaspheme me.

[7] Watch — I am about to put a spirit in him. He will hear a rumor, return to his own land, and there I will cut him down with the sword."

[8] The Rabshakeh went back and found the king of Assyria attacking Libnah — he had heard the king had left Lachish.

[9] Then word reached Sennacherib about Tirhakah, king of Cush: "He has marched out to fight you." When he heard it, he sent messengers to Hezekiah again with this message:

[10] "Say this to Hezekiah, king of Judah — don't let the God you trust deceive you when he says Jerusalem won't fall into the hand of the king of Assyria.

[11] You've heard what the kings of Assyria have done to every country — wiped them out. And you think you'll be spared?

[12] Did the gods of the nations my fathers destroyed save them? Gozan, Haran, Rezeph, the people of Eden in Telassar — gone.

[13] Where is the king of Hamath, the king of Arpad, the king of the city of Sepharvaim, of Hena, of Ivvah?"

[14] Hezekiah took the letter from the messengers' hands and read it. Then he went up to the house of the LORD and spread it out before the LORD.

[15] And Hezekiah prayed to the LORD:

[16] "LORD of Hosts, God of Israel, enthroned above the cherubim — you alone are God over every kingdom on earth. You made the heavens and the earth.

[17] Bend your ear, LORD, and listen. Open your eyes, LORD, and see. Hear every word Sennacherib sent to taunt the living God.

[18] It's true, LORD — the kings of Assyria have laid waste to all those nations and their lands,

[19] and thrown their gods into the fire. But those weren't gods — just wood and stone, the work of human hands. So of course they were destroyed.

[20] Now, LORD our God, rescue us from his hand, so every kingdom on earth will know that you alone are the LORD."

[21] Then Isaiah, son of Amoz, sent word to Hezekiah: "The LORD, the God of Israel, says: Because you have prayed to me about Sennacherib, king of Assyria —

[22] this is the word the LORD has spoken against him:

She despises you, she laughs at you —
the virgin daughter of Zion.
She shakes her head behind your back —
the daughter of Jerusalem.

[23] Who have you mocked? Who have you reviled?
Against whom have you raised your voice
and lifted your eyes in arrogance?
Against the Holy One of Israel.

[24] Through your servants you taunted the Lord and said:
'With my swarm of chariots
I climbed the mountain heights,
the far recesses of Lebanon.
I cut down its tallest cedars,
its choicest cypresses.
I reached its remotest peak,
its densest forest.

[25] I dug wells and drank foreign water.
I dried up every river of Egypt
with the soles of my feet.'

[26] Haven't you heard?
Long ago I planned it.
In days of old I shaped it.
Now I have brought it to pass —
that you should crush fortified cities into heaps of rubble.

[27] Their people, drained of strength,
were shattered and ashamed.
They were grass in the field, tender green shoots,
weeds on the roof,
scorched before they could grow.

[28] But I know where you sit,
when you come and when you go,
and how you rage against me.

[29] Because of your raging,
because your arrogance has reached my ears,
I will put my hook in your nose,
my bit in your mouth,
and turn you back the way you came.

[30] And this will be the sign for you, Hezekiah: This year you will eat what grows on its own. Next year, what springs up from that. But in the third year — sow and reap, plant vineyards, eat their fruit.

[31] The surviving remnant of the house of Judah will sink roots downward and bear fruit upward.

[32] Out of Jerusalem will come a remnant, out of Mount Zion survivors. The zeal of the LORD of Hosts will do this.

[33] So this is what the LORD says about the king of Assyria:

He will not enter this city.
He will not shoot an arrow here.
He will not face it with a shield
or pile up a siege ramp against it.

[34] By the way he came, he will return.
He will not enter this city —
declares the LORD.

[35] I will defend this city and save it,
for my own sake,
and for the sake of David my servant."

[36] Then the angel of the LORD went out and struck down a hundred and eighty-five thousand in the Assyrian camp. When the rest woke at dawn — they were corpses, every one of them.

[37] So Sennacherib, king of Assyria, broke camp and went home. He stayed in Nineveh.

[38] And while he was worshiping in the temple of his god Nisroch, his own sons — Adrammelech and Sharezer — cut him down with the sword and fled to the land of Ararat. His son Esarhaddon took the throne.

[1] In those days Hezekiah fell deathly ill. The prophet Isaiah son of Amoz came to him and said, "This is what the LORD says: Put your house in order. You are going to die. You will not recover."

[2] Hezekiah turned his face to the wall and prayed to the LORD.

[3] "LORD," he said, "remember — please remember — how I walked before you faithfully, with a whole heart, doing what was good in your sight." And Hezekiah broke down and wept, hard.

I moan like a dove.

this is how my spirit finds its life:
you restored me. You let me live.

[17] Look — the bitterness was for my good.
You loved me back from the pit of annihilation.
You hurled all my sins
behind your back.

Death cannot sing.
Those going down into the pit
have no more hope in your faithfulness.

[19] The living, the living — they praise you,

about your faithfulness.

and we will play music all the days of our lives

[7] You told yourself, "Forever — I'll reign forever." You never took it to heart, never saw what was coming, never thought about how this ends.

[8] So listen now, you lover of luxury, lounging in your false security, telling yourself, "I am, and there is no one else. I'll never be a widow. I'll never lose my children."

[9] Both are coming for you. In a single day. In a single instant — widowhood and the loss of your children. They will hit you in full force, despite all your spells, despite the staggering power of your enchantments.

[10] You trusted in your wickedness. You said, "Nobody sees me." Your wisdom and your knowledge — they twisted you, and you told yourself, "I am, and there is no one else."

[14] Look at them — straw. Fire takes them. They can't even save themselves from the flame. This isn't a cozy hearth to sit by. This isn't a fire to warm your hands.

[1] Listen to this, house of Jacob — you who go by the name Israel, who came out of Judah's womb, who swear by the name of the LORD and invoke the God of Israel, but not in truth, not with integrity.

[1] Listen to me, you islands. Pay attention, peoples far away. The LORD called me before I was born. While I was still in my mother's womb, he spoke my name.

[1] Listen to me, you who chase after righteousness, you who hunt for the LORD. Look at the rock you were cut from, the quarry you were dug out of.

[1] Who believed what we heard? Who saw the arm of the LORD revealed in this?

[8] They dragged him through a rigged trial and hauled him off, and who in his generation even noticed? He was cut out of the land of the living, struck down for the rebellion of my people.

[9] They gave him a grave with criminals — and a rich man's tomb when he died — though he had done no violence and never spoke a lie.

[14] then you'll find your joy in the LORD. I'll lift you up over the high places of the earth. I'll feed you the inheritance of Jacob your father. The mouth of the LORD has spoken it.

[1] Look — the LORD's arm isn't too short to save. His ear isn't too dull to hear. [2] It's your crimes that have driven a wedge between you and your God. Your sins have made him turn his face away. He won't listen.

[3] Your hands are stained with blood. Your fingers drip with guilt. Your lips spew lies. Your tongue mutters poison. [4] Nobody files an honest lawsuit. Nobody pleads a case in good faith. They rely on empty arguments and speak fraud. They conceive trouble. They give birth to ruin.

[5] They hatch snake eggs and weave spider webs. Eat one of those eggs and you die. Crack one open and a viper slithers out. [6] Their webs make no clothing — you can't cover yourself in what they spin. Their work is violence. Their hands are full of it.

[7] Their feet sprint toward evil. They race to spill innocent blood. Their thoughts churn out destruction. Wreckage and ruin are their roadmap. [8] They don't know the road to peace. There's no justice in the ruts they've worn. They've twisted every path they walk — anyone following them never finds peace.

[9] So justice stays far from us. Righteousness never catches up. We hoped for light — only darkness. We waited for dawn — and walked in shadow. [10] We grope along walls like the blind, feeling our way like men with no eyes. We stumble at noon as if it were dusk. Among the strong, we're already dead. [11] We growl like bears. We moan like doves. We strain for justice — there is none. For rescue — and it stays out of reach.

[12] Our defiance against you piles high. Our sins testify against us in open court. Our betrayals follow us everywhere. We know what we've done. [13] Treason against the LORD. Denial. Turning our backs on our God. Plotting oppression. Stirring up rebellion. Conceiving lies in our hearts and pushing them out through our mouths.

[14] So justice gets shoved aside. Righteousness stands at a distance. Truth has collapsed in the public square. Honesty can't even find a way in. [15] Truth has gone missing. Anyone who refuses evil becomes prey.

The LORD saw it. It disgusted him — that justice had vanished. [16] He saw that no one would step up. He was stunned that no one would intervene. So his own arm brought the rescue. His own righteousness held him steady.

[17] He strapped on righteousness like body armor. He set salvation on his head like a helmet. He wrapped himself in vengeance and pulled zeal around him like a cloak. [18] He will repay according to what they've done — fury to his enemies, payback to his foes. To the distant coastlands, he will deliver their due.

[19] From the west they will fear the name of the LORD. From the rising sun they will tremble at his glory. He comes like a river in flood, driven by the breath of the LORD.

[20] A Redeemer is coming to Zion — to those in Jacob who turn from rebellion. That's the LORD's word.

[21] "As for me, this is my covenant with them," says the LORD. "My Spirit, which rests on you, and my words, which I've placed in your mouth — they will not leave your mouth, or your children's mouths, or your grandchildren's mouths. Not now. Not ever." That's what the LORD says.

[1] Get up. Shine. Your light has come, and the glory of the LORD is rising over you like the sun. [2] Look — darkness blankets the earth, thick night wraps the nations, but over you the LORD breaks like dawn, and his glory becomes visible on you. [3] Nations will travel toward your light. Kings will come to the brightness of your sunrise.

[4] Lift your eyes. Look around. They are gathering — all of them — coming home to you. Your sons arrive from far away, your daughters carried on the hip. [5] You will see it and your face will glow, your heart will pound and swell, because the wealth of the sea will pour into your lap, the riches of nations will arrive at your door. [6] A flood of camels will cover your land — young camels from Midian and Ephah, caravans from Sheba, every one of them loaded with gold and frankincense, every one of them announcing the praise of the LORD. [7] Every flock of Kedar will be gathered to you, the rams of Nebaioth will be at your service, climbing my altar as welcome offerings — and I will make my glorious house even more glorious.

[8] Who are these, drifting in like a cloud, like doves homing to their roosts? [9] The coastlands are waiting for me. The ships of Tarshish lead the way, bringing your children from far off, their silver and gold with them, all for the name of the LORD your God — the Holy One of Israel — because he has made you beautiful.

[10] Foreigners will rebuild your walls. Their kings will serve you. I struck you in my anger, yes — but in my favor I have shown you mercy. [11] Your gates will stand open day and night and never close, so that the wealth of nations can be brought in, with their kings led in procession. [12] Any nation or kingdom that refuses to serve you will perish — those nations will be ruined, completely.

[13] The glory of Lebanon will come to you — cypress, pine, and box tree together — to beautify the place of my sanctuary, and I will make the place where my feet rest magnificent. [14] The sons of those who crushed you will come bowing at your feet. Everyone who scorned you will fall down at your soles and call you the City of the LORD, Zion of the Holy One of Israel.

[15] Once you were abandoned and hated, with no one passing through. I will make you the pride of the ages, the joy of every generation. [16] You will drink the milk of nations, nurse at the breasts of kings — and you will know that I, the LORD, am your Savior, your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob.

[17] Instead of bronze I will bring you gold. Instead of iron, silver. Instead of wood, bronze. Instead of stones, iron. I will appoint peace as your overseer and righteousness as your taskmaster. [18] No more reports of violence in your land, no more wreckage and ruin inside your borders. You will name your walls Salvation and your gates Praise.

[19] You will not need the sun for daylight anymore, nor the moon to light your nights. The LORD himself will be your everlasting light. Your God will be your glory. [20] Your sun will never set again. Your moon will never wane. The LORD will be your everlasting light, and your days of mourning will be finished.

[21] Your people — all of them — will be righteous. They will inherit the land forever, the shoot I planted with my own hands, the work that puts my glory on display. [22] The smallest will become a thousand. The least, a mighty nation. I am the LORD. When the time comes, I will make it happen — fast.

[1] The Spirit of the Sovereign LORD is on me — because the LORD has anointed me. He sent me to carry good news to the wrecked and the poor, to bind up the ones whose hearts are in pieces, to announce liberation to captives and the prison doors flung open for those locked in the dark.

[2] To declare the year the LORD says yes — and the day our God settles accounts. To comfort everyone who mourns.

[3] To give the grieving of Zion something better than ash: a crown. Oil of joy instead of funeral weeping. A cloak of praise instead of a spirit collapsed in despair. And they will be called oaks of righteousness — planted by the LORD himself, to show off his glory.
