[5] Foreigners will tend your flocks. Outsiders will work your fields and your vineyards.

[6] But you — you will be called priests of the LORD. Ministers of our God, they will name you. You will feast on the wealth of nations. Their splendor will be yours to inherit.

[7] Instead of your shame, a double portion. Instead of disgrace, you will shout over your inheritance. In your own land you will own twice what you lost — joy that never ends will be yours.

[8] Because I — the LORD — love justice. I hate robbery and wrong. I will pay them in full, faithfully. I will cut an everlasting covenant with them.

[9] Their descendants will be known among the nations. Their children among the peoples. Everyone who sees them will recognize it: these are the people the LORD has blessed.

[10] I will sing my heart out over the LORD. My whole soul will dance in my God. Because he has dressed me in the clothes of salvation, wrapped me in a robe of righteousness — like a bridegroom crowned like a priest, like a bride decked out in her jewels.

[11] As the earth pushes up its green shoots, as a garden makes its seeds break open and grow — that's how the Sovereign LORD will make righteousness break open, and praise rise up, in front of every nation watching.

[1] For Zion's sake I will not shut my mouth. For Jerusalem's sake I will not rest — not until her vindication breaks like dawn, not until her rescue blazes like a torch in the dark.

[2] The nations will see your righteousness. Every king will see your glory. And you'll be called by a new name — a name spoken by the mouth of the LORD himself.

[3] You will be a crown of beauty in the hand of the LORD, a royal diadem in the palm of your God.

[4] No one will call you Abandoned anymore. No one will call your land Desolate. You'll be named My Delight Is in Her, and your land will be called Married — because the LORD delights in you, and your land has a husband now.

[5] As a young man marries a young woman, so your sons will marry you. As a bridegroom rejoices over his bride, so your God will rejoice over you.

[6] On your walls, Jerusalem, I have stationed watchmen. Day and night, they will never fall silent. You who call on the LORD — take no rest,

[7] and give him no rest, until he establishes Jerusalem and makes her the praise of all the earth.

[8] The LORD has sworn it — by his right hand, by his mighty arm: Never again will I hand your grain to your enemies for food. Never again will foreigners drink the wine you sweated to make.

[9] The ones who harvest it will eat it and praise the LORD. The ones who gather the grapes will drink it in the courts of my sanctuary.

[10] Move! Move through the gates! Clear the road for the people. Build it up, build it up — pull out every stone. Lift a banner for the nations to see.

[11] Listen — the LORD has sent word to the ends of the earth: Tell Daughter Zion, Look — your Savior is coming. His reward is with him. His recompense walks ahead of him.

[12] And they will be called The Holy People, The Redeemed of the LORD. And you — you will be called Sought After, A City No Longer Forsaken.

[1] Who is this coming from Edom, robes stained crimson from Bozrah? Who is this, splendid in his garments, striding forward in the fullness of his strength?

"It is I — speaking justice, mighty to save."

[2] Why is your clothing red? Why do your garments look like someone who has been treading grapes?

[3] "I trampled the winepress alone. From the nations, no one was with me. I trampled them in my fury, ground them down in my wrath. Their lifeblood splashed my robes; I stained every garment I wore.

[4] The day of vengeance was burning in my heart, and the year of redemption had come.

[5] I looked — no one to help. I was stunned — no one to hold me up. So my own arm brought the victory. My own fury sustained me.

[6] I trampled the peoples in my rage, broke them apart in my wrath, poured their lifeblood out on the ground."

[7] I will tell of the loyal love of the LORD — his praises, everything he has done for us, the flood of goodness he poured out on the house of Israel from his deep compassion, his unfailing mercy.

[8] He said, "Surely they are my people, children who will not betray me." And he became their Savior.

[9] In every moment of their distress, he was distressed. The angel of his presence rescued them. In his love and pity he redeemed them. He lifted them, carried them, all the days of long ago.

[10] But they rebelled. They grieved his Holy Spirit. So he became their enemy — he himself fought against them.

[11] Then his people remembered the old days — the days of Moses. Where is the one who brought them up out of the sea with the shepherds of his flock? Where is the one who set his Holy Spirit among them,

[12] who sent his glorious arm to march at Moses' right hand, who split the waters before them to make himself an everlasting name,

[13] who led them through the depths? Like a horse galloping across open country, they did not stumble.

[14] Like cattle going down to a quiet valley, the Spirit of the LORD gave them rest. This is how you led your people — and made for yourself a name of splendor.

[15] Look down from heaven. See — from your high and holy and beautiful throne. Where is your zeal? Where is your power? The stirring of your heart, your compassion toward me — held back. Why?

[16] You are our Father. Abraham doesn't know us; Israel doesn't claim us. You, LORD, you are our Father, our Redeemer from ancient days — that is your name.

[17] LORD, why do you let us wander from your ways? Why do you harden our hearts so we no longer fear you? Come back — for the sake of your servants, the tribes that belong to you.

[18] For a little while your holy people possessed the land. Then our enemies trampled your sanctuary into the dust.

[19] We have become like people you never ruled, like those who were never called by your name.

[1] If only you would tear the heavens open and come down — mountains buckling at the sight of you, [2] the way fire devours brushwood, the way fire makes water boil — come, make your name known to your enemies, make the nations tremble at your face. [3] When you did the unthinkable, things we never saw coming, you came down, and the mountains melted before you.

[4] From the beginning of time no ear has heard, no eye has seen any god but you — a God who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. [5] You meet the one who delights in doing right, the one who remembers your ways. But look — you were angry, and we kept sinning. We have been at this so long. Can we still be saved?

[6] We have all become like something unclean, and every righteous deed of ours is a filthy rag. We wither like leaves, and our sins, like wind, sweep us away. [7] Nobody calls on your name. Nobody rouses themselves to grab hold of you. You hid your face from us. You handed us over to our own corruption.

[8] And yet — Lord, you are our Father. We are the clay, you are the potter. We are all the work of your hand. [9] Don't stay furious, Lord. Don't hold our sins against us forever. Look at us — please — we are your people, all of us.

[10] Your holy cities have become wilderness. Zion is wilderness. Jerusalem — a wasteland. [11] Our holy and beautiful house, where our ancestors praised you — burned to the ground. Everything we treasured lies in ruins.

[12] After all this, Lord — will you hold yourself back? Will you stay silent and crush us beyond bearing?

[1] I made myself available to people who never asked for me. I let myself be found by people who never went looking. To a nation that never called my name, I said: Here I am. Here I am.

[2] All day long I stretched out my hands to a stubborn people — people walking a road that leads nowhere good, chasing their own ideas,

[3] a people who insult me to my face, nonstop — sacrificing in their backyard gardens, burning incense on bricks like it means something,

[4] crouching in graveyards, holding all-night vigils in secret places, eating pig flesh, broth of unclean things simmering in their pots,

[5] then telling everyone else, "Stay back — don't come near me, I'm holier than you." These people are smoke in my nostrils, a fire that burns all day.

[6] Look — it's written down in front of me. I won't stay quiet. I will pay it back. I will pay it back in full, right into their laps —

[7] your sins and your ancestors' sins together, says the Lord. They burned incense on the mountains, mocked me on the hills — so I will measure out exactly what their conduct earned, straight into their laps.

[8] This is what the Lord says: When you find good juice still in a cluster, someone says, "Don't destroy it — there's a blessing in there." That's how I'll handle my servants. I won't destroy everything.

[9] I will bring offspring out of Jacob, an heir to my mountains out of Judah. My chosen ones will inherit the land. My servants will live there.

[10] Sharon will be pastureland for flocks again. The Valley of Achor will be a resting place for cattle — for my people who searched for me.

[11] But as for you — you who abandoned the Lord, who forgot my holy mountain, who set tables for the god of Luck and filled cups for the god of Fate —

[12] I will assign you to the sword. All of you will kneel for the slaughter. Because I called and you didn't answer. I spoke and you didn't listen. You did what I hate. You chose what I refuse.

[13] So here is what the Lord God says: Look — my servants will eat, and you will go hungry. My servants will drink, and you will go thirsty. My servants will celebrate, and you will be humiliated.

[14] My servants will sing from the joy in their hearts, and you will scream from heartbreak, howl from a shattered spirit.

[15] Your name will become a curse my chosen ones spit when they swear. The Lord God will kill you. But his servants — he will give them a new name.

[16] Whoever blesses himself on earth will bless himself by the God of truth. Whoever takes an oath on earth will swear by the God of truth. Because the old griefs are forgotten. They are hidden from my eyes.

[17] Look — I am creating new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered. They won't even cross the mind.

[18] So rejoice. Be wildly glad forever in what I am creating. Look — I am creating Jerusalem as joy itself, her people as delight.

[19] I will rejoice over Jerusalem. I will take delight in my people. The sound of weeping will not be heard in her anymore, the sound of crying — gone.

[20] No more infants who only live a few days. No more elders who don't get to finish their years. A person who dies at a hundred will be considered young. Anyone who falls short of a hundred will be considered cursed.

[21] They will build houses and actually live in them. They will plant vineyards and actually eat the fruit.

[22] They won't build for someone else to move in. They won't plant for someone else to eat. My people will live as long as trees live. My chosen ones will wear out what their hands have made.

[23] They won't work for nothing. They won't bring children into the world for catastrophe. They are the offspring of the Lord's blessing — them and their descendants together.

[24] Before they call, I'll answer. While they're still talking, I'll hear.

[25] Wolf and lamb will graze side by side. The lion will eat straw like an ox. And the serpent? Dust will be its food. Nothing will harm. Nothing will destroy on my entire holy mountain — says the Lord.

[1] This is what the LORD says: The sky is my throne. The earth is the stool beneath my feet. So what house could you possibly build for me? What place could ever contain my rest?

[2] My own hand made all of this. Every last thing exists because I willed it. But here is who catches my eye — the one bowed low, the one whose spirit is shattered, the one who trembles at my word.

[3] Slaughter a bull? You might as well murder a man. Sacrifice a lamb? You might as well break a dog's neck. Bring a grain offering? It reeks like pig's blood. Burn incense in tribute? You're praising an idol. You chose your own roads. Your souls feast on what disgusts me.

[4] So I will choose too. I'll choose the harsh treatment for you. I'll bring down on you the very thing that terrifies you. Because I called and nobody answered. I spoke and nobody listened. You did what I hate. You picked what I refuse.

[5] Listen — you who tremble at his word: Your own brothers despise you. They cast you out for the sake of my name. They sneer, "Let the LORD reveal his glory, then, so we can watch you celebrate." But they are the ones who will be shamed.

[6] A sound — listen — uproar from the city, a voice thundering from the temple. It is the voice of the LORD repaying his enemies what they have earned.

[7] Before she even felt the first pang, she gave birth. Before the contractions hit, she delivered a son.

[8] Who has ever heard of such a thing? Who has ever seen it? Can a country be born in a single day? Can a nation come forth in one moment? But the instant Zion's labor began, she brought her children into the world.

[9] Would I bring a child to the brink of birth and then refuse to deliver? says the LORD. Would I open the womb and slam it shut? says your God.

[10] Celebrate with Jerusalem! Rejoice for her, all of you who love her. Let your joy run wild — all of you who once mourned over her.

[11] So you can nurse and be filled at her comforting breast. So you can drink deep and delight in her glorious abundance.

[12] Because this is what the LORD says: I am rerouting peace toward her like a river, the glory of the nations like a flooding stream. You will nurse there. You will be carried on her hip. You will be bounced on her knees.

[13] The way a mother comforts her child — that is how I will comfort you. In Jerusalem you will find your comfort.

[14] You will see this and your heart will leap. Your bones will bloom like fresh grass. The LORD's hand will be known by his servants — and his fury, by his enemies.

[15] Look — the LORD comes wrapped in fire, his chariots a hurricane, to release his anger in full heat and his rebuke in flames of fire.

[16] With fire the LORD will execute judgment. With his sword he will judge all flesh. The slain of the LORD will be many.

[17] Those who consecrate themselves and purify themselves to slip into the gardens, following the one in the middle, eating pig's flesh and vermin and rats — they will all meet their end together. This is the LORD's word.

[18] I know what they do. I know what they think. The hour is coming to gather every nation and every language. They will come, and they will see my glory.

[19] I will set a sign among them. I will send the survivors to the nations — to Tarshish, to Put and Lud (the famous archers), to Tubal and Javan, to the distant coastlands that have never heard a whisper of my name or seen a flicker of my glory. They will proclaim my glory to the nations.

[20] And they will bring all your brothers and sisters back from every nation as an offering to the LORD — on horses, in chariots, in wagons, on mules, on camels — to my holy mountain, Jerusalem, says the LORD. They'll bring them the way the Israelites bring grain offerings in clean vessels to the house of the LORD.

[21] And from among them — yes, from these foreigners — I will take some to be priests and Levites, says the LORD.

[22] Because just as the new heavens and the new earth I am making will endure before me — this is the LORD's word — so your name and your descendants will endure.

[23] And it will happen — every new moon, every Sabbath, all humanity will come to bow before me, says the LORD.

[24] Then they will walk out and look at the corpses of those who rebelled against me. Their worm will not die. Their fire will not go out. They will be a horror to all flesh.

[1] James — slave of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ — to the twelve tribes scattered across the nations: greetings.

[2] Brothers and sisters, when life ambushes you with trials of every kind, count it pure joy. [3] You know what's happening: the testing of your faith is forging endurance in you. [4] Let that endurance finish its work, so you emerge mature, complete, lacking nothing.

[5] If any of you is short on wisdom, ask God — he hands it out freely, without making you feel small for asking — and it will be given. [6] But ask in faith, with no inner double-mindedness. The doubter is a wave at sea, whipped and tossed by the wind. [7] That kind of person should not expect to receive anything from the Lord — [8] divided in mind, unstable in every step.

[9] Let the believer with nothing boast in being lifted high, [10] and the rich one boast in being brought low — because wealth withers like a wildflower. [11] The sun rises, the heat scorches, the bloom falls, the beauty is gone. So it is with the rich, fading mid-stride through their busy lives.

[12] Blessed is the one who endures testing. Having stood the trial, that person will receive the crown of life — promised to all who love him.

[13] When tempted, no one should say, "God is tempting me." God cannot be tempted by evil, and he tempts no one. [14] Every person is tempted when their own appetite drags them off and baits the hook. [15] Then desire conceives and gives birth to sin; and sin, fully grown, breeds death.

[16] Don't be deceived, dear brothers and sisters. [17] Every good gift, every perfect gift, comes down from above — from the Father of lights, in whom there is no flicker, no shifting shadow. [18] He chose to birth us by the word of truth, making us the firstfruits of all he has made.

[19] Hold this close, my dear friends: be quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to anger — [20] because human rage never produces the righteousness of God. [21] So strip off every moral filth and the runoff of evil, and welcome with humility the implanted word that has the power to save your souls.

[22] Become doers of the word, not just hearers who con themselves. [23] Anyone who hears the word but doesn't act on it is like someone who studies their face in a mirror — [24] glances at themselves, walks away, and immediately forgets what they look like. [25] But the one who leans in and stays with the perfect law, the law that sets people free — not a forgetful listener but an active doer — that person will be blessed in everything they do.

[26] If anyone thinks they're religious but can't bridle their tongue, they're fooling themselves. Their religion is worthless. [27] Pure religion, untainted before God the Father, looks like this: caring for orphans and widows in their distress, and refusing to let the world stain you.

[1] My brothers and sisters, if you actually believe in our glorious Lord Jesus the Messiah, stop playing favorites.

[2] Picture this: a man walks into your gathering wearing gold rings and designer clothes. Right behind him, a poor man shuffles in wearing rags.

[3] You fawn over the well-dressed one — "Here, take this seat of honor" — while you tell the poor man, "Stand over there," or "Sit on the floor by my feet."

[4] Do you see what you've done? You've split people into categories and made yourselves judges driven by twisted motives.

[5] Listen to me, brothers and sisters I love: hasn't God deliberately chosen the poor of this world to be rich in faith and to inherit the kingdom he promised to those who love him?

[6] But you — you humiliate the poor. Meanwhile, isn't it the rich who crush you, who drag you into court,

[7] who sneer at the beautiful name you bear?

[8] Now, if you actually live out the royal law that Scripture spells out — *Love your neighbor as yourself* — you're doing it right.

[9] But the moment you show favoritism, you're sinning. The law itself convicts you as a lawbreaker.

[10] Because whoever keeps the whole law but trips on a single point is guilty of breaking all of it.

[11] The same God who said "Do not commit adultery" also said "Do not murder." Skip the adultery but commit murder, and you're still a lawbreaker.

[12] So speak and act like people who will be judged by the law of freedom.

[13] Because judgment falls without mercy on anyone who refused to show mercy. Mercy laughs in judgment's face.

[14] What good is it, brothers and sisters, if someone claims to have faith but has nothing to show for it? Can that kind of faith save anyone?

[15] Imagine a brother or sister with no clothes on their back and no food on the table.

[16] And one of you says, "Hey, God bless — stay warm, eat well!" — but you give them nothing. What good is that?

[17] That's faith for you: alone, with no action behind it. Dead. Stone-cold dead.

[18] Someone will object: "You have faith, I have works." Fine — show me your faith without the works. I'll show you my faith *by* my works.

[19] You believe God is one? Good for you. The demons believe that too — and they shudder.

[20] Do you want proof, you empty-headed person, that faith without works is useless?

[21] Wasn't our father Abraham declared righteous by what he did when he laid his son Isaac on the altar?

[22] You see — his faith was working alongside his actions, and his faith was completed by what he did.

[23] That's when the Scripture came true: *Abraham believed God, and it was credited to him as righteousness* — and he was called God's friend.

[24] So you see: a person is justified by what they do, not by faith alone.

[25] Same with Rahab the prostitute. Wasn't she justified by what she did — taking in the spies, sending them out a different way?

[26] A body without breath is a corpse. Faith without works is the same thing.

[1] Not many of you should rush to become teachers, my brothers. Understand this: those of us who teach will face a stricter reckoning.

[2] We all stumble — constantly, in countless ways. But the one who never trips over their words? That's a complete human being, capable of bridling the whole body.

[3] Slip a bit into a horse's mouth and you steer the entire animal wherever you want.

[4] Or take ships: massive vessels driven by violent winds, yet a tiny rudder turns them wherever the pilot decides.

[5] The tongue works the same way — a small thing that boasts enormous power. Think how a tiny spark sets a whole forest ablaze.

[6] The tongue is fire. A whole world of corruption planted right inside us. It stains the entire body, torches the course of our existence, and the flame itself is lit straight from hell.

[7] Humans have tamed — and continue to tame — every species: beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures.

[8] But the tongue? No human can tame it. It's a restless evil, brimming with lethal poison.

[9] With it we bless the Lord and Father, and with the same mouth we curse people made in God's image.

[10] Out of the same opening pours blessing and cursing. My brothers, this should not be happening.

[11] Does a spring gush fresh water and brackish water from the same source?

[12] Can a fig tree produce olives, my brothers? Can a grapevine bear figs? Neither can a salt spring give fresh water.

[13] Who among you is wise and understanding? Prove it. Show it by the way you live — actions marked by the gentleness that wisdom breeds.

[14] But if you're harboring bitter jealousy and selfish ambition in your heart, don't strut around claiming wisdom. You're lying against the truth.

[15] That kind of "wisdom" doesn't come down from above. It's earthbound, animal, demonic.

[16] Wherever jealousy and selfish ambition take root, you get chaos and every kind of rotten thing.

[17] But the wisdom that comes from above? First of all, it's pure. Then peaceable, considerate, willing to yield, overflowing with mercy and good fruit, undivided, free of pretense.

[18] And those who plant peace will harvest a crop of righteousness from seeds sown in peace.

[1] Where do the fights come from? Where do the feuds among you come from? Right here — from the appetites that wage war inside you. [2] You crave, and you have nothing. You kill and burn with envy, and you still can't get what you want. You brawl, you battle — and you have nothing, because you never ask. [3] And when you do ask, you don't receive, because you're asking with rotten motives — wanting only to spend it on your pleasures.

[4] Adulterers. Don't you understand that flirting with the world means declaring war on God? Anyone who decides to be the world's friend has made themselves God's enemy. [5] Or do you think Scripture speaks for nothing when it says the Spirit God planted in us yearns for us with a fierce, jealous love? [6] And he gives more grace still. That's why it says: God blocks the arrogant, but pours grace on the humble.

[7] So surrender to God. Stand your ground against the devil, and he will run. [8] Come close to God, and he will come close to you. Clean your hands, sinners. Purify your hearts, you double-souled. [9] Be miserable. Mourn. Weep. Let your laughter turn to grief and your celebration to sorrow. [10] Bow low before the Lord, and he will lift you high.

[11] Stop slandering each other, brothers and sisters. Whoever tears down a brother or judges a brother tears down the law and judges the law. And if you're judging the law, you're not keeping it — you've installed yourself as its judge. [12] There is only one Lawgiver and Judge — the one who can save and destroy. Who do you think you are, judging your neighbor?

[13] Now listen, you who say, "Today or tomorrow we're heading to this city or that one, spending a year there, running our business, turning a profit" — [14] you don't even know what tomorrow holds. What is your life? A puff of mist, here for a moment, then gone. [15] What you should be saying is: "If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that." [16] Instead, you swagger around boasting about your plans. All such boasting is evil. [17] So then: anyone who knows the good they ought to do, and doesn't do it — that's sin.

[1] Now a word for you, the rich: weep. Wail over the misery rushing toward you. [2] Your wealth has rotted. Your fine clothes are moth-eaten rags. [3] Your gold and silver are corroded, and that corrosion will testify against you — it will eat your flesh like fire. You stockpiled treasure in the final hour. [4] Listen — the wages you cheated out of the workers who mowed your fields are crying out. The cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of Heaven's Armies. [5] You lived in luxury here on earth, you indulged every appetite — you fattened your own hearts for the day of slaughter. [6] You condemned the righteous one, you murdered him, and he offered no resistance.

[7] So be patient, brothers and sisters, until the Lord's return. Think of the farmer waiting for the precious harvest from the soil — patient through the early rains and the late rains. [8] Be patient like that. Hold your ground. The Lord's coming is close. [9] Stop grumbling against each other, brothers and sisters, or you'll be the ones judged. The Judge is standing right at the door.

[10] When you need a model of endurance under suffering, look at the prophets who spoke in the name of the Lord. [11] We call them blessed because they held on. You've heard about Job's perseverance, and you've seen how the Lord brought it to its end — because the Lord is overflowing with compassion and mercy.

[12] Above everything, brothers and sisters, do not swear oaths — not by heaven, not by earth, not by anything. Let your yes be yes and your no be no. Otherwise you fall under judgment.

[13] Is anyone among you suffering? Pray. Is anyone cheerful? Sing. [14] Is anyone among you sick? Call the elders of the church. Let them pray over the person and anoint them with oil in the name of the Lord. [15] The prayer offered in faith will save the one who is sick, and the Lord will raise them up. And if they have sinned, they will be forgiven. [16] So confess your sins to one another, and pray for one another, so that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person is powerful — it gets things done. [17] Elijah was a human being just like us. He prayed, fervently, that it would not rain — and for three and a half years not a drop fell. [18] Then he prayed again, and the sky opened, and the earth produced its harvest.

[19] Brothers and sisters, if any of you wanders from the truth and someone brings them back, [20] understand this: whoever turns a sinner from the wrong road saves that soul from death and covers a multitude of sins.

[30] I disciplined your children for nothing. They wouldn't take correction. Your own sword devoured your prophets like a ravenous lion.

[31] You, this generation—pay attention to the word of the LORD:

Have I been a desert to Israel? A land of suffocating darkness? Then why do my people say, "We're free to roam—we're done coming to you"?

[33] How skilled you've become at chasing love. Even the worst women could learn from you.

[34] And on your clothes—the blood of the innocent poor. You didn't catch them breaking in. It's just there, on everything.

[35] And still you say, "I'm innocent. His anger has surely turned away from me." Watch me bring you to trial for saying, "I have not sinned."

[36] How casually you switch your loyalties. Egypt will shame you the way Assyria already did.

[37] You'll leave that place too, hands on your head in grief—because the LORD has rejected the ones you trusted. They will not help you.

[8] Judah saw that I divorced faithless Israel and handed her a certificate of divorce because of her endless adulteries. But Judah — that treacherous sister — wasn't afraid. She went out and prostituted herself too.

[9] Israel's whoring was so casual she defiled the land itself, committing adultery with stones and wooden idols.

[10] And after all of this, her treacherous sister Judah didn't come back to me with her whole heart — only with a performance. This is the LORD speaking.

[11] Then the LORD said to me: Faithless Israel looks righteous compared to treacherous Judah.

[21] Listen — a sound rising from the barren heights: the weeping and pleading of Israel's children. They twisted their path. They forgot the LORD their God.

[23] The hills were a lie. The mountain orgies were noise and nothing. Only in the LORD our God is the salvation of Israel.

[24] From the time we were young, that shameful thing has devoured everything our ancestors worked for — their flocks and herds, their sons and daughters.

[25] Let us lie down in our shame. Let our disgrace cover us like a blanket. We've sinned against the LORD our God — we and our fathers, from the time we were young to this very day. We never listened to the voice of the LORD our God."

[20] My tent is in ruins. Every cord, snapped. My children have walked out on me — they're gone. No one left to set up my tent again, no one to hang the curtains.

[21] The shepherds have gone stupid. They didn't seek the LORD. That's why they failed, and why their whole flock is scattered.

[22] Listen — a rumor. A roar coming. A massive convulsion out of the northern country, coming to turn the cities of Judah into rubble, a den for jackals.

[23] LORD, I know — a person's life is not their own. No one walking out the door has the power to direct his own steps.

[24] Discipline me, LORD — but with justice, not in your anger, or you'll grind me to nothing.

[23] Not one will be left. I am bringing catastrophe on the men of Anathoth — the year of their reckoning.

[4] Take that loincloth you bought — the one wrapped around you — and head for the Euphrates. When you get there, hide it in a crevice in the rocks.

[1] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[2] Do not take a wife. Do not father sons or daughters in this place.

[3] Because this is what the LORD says about the children born here, and about the mothers who carry them, and the fathers who beget them in this land:

[4] They will die of diseases that gut them. No one will mourn them. No one will bury them. They will lie on the ground like dung. Sword and famine will finish them, and their corpses will feed the birds of the sky and the beasts of the earth.

[5] This is what the LORD says: Do not enter a house of mourning. Do not go to grieve. Do not offer comfort. Because I have pulled my peace away from this people — my loyal love, my mercy — gone.

[6] Great and small will die in this land. No burial. No mourning. No cutting the skin. No shaving the head for them.

[7] No one will break bread with the bereaved to console them for the dead. No cup of comfort will be raised for a lost father or mother.

[8] And do not enter a house of feasting either. Do not sit down to eat and drink with them.

[9] Because this is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, says: Watch. Right before your eyes, in your own lifetime, I am silencing this place — the voice of joy and the voice of gladness, the voice of the groom and the voice of the bride. Silence.

[10] When you deliver all this to the people, they will ask you: Why? Why has the LORD pronounced this disaster against us? What did we do wrong? What sin did we commit against the LORD our God?

[11] Then tell them: Because your ancestors abandoned me — declares the LORD — and chased after other gods, served them, bowed to them. They walked away from me. They did not keep my instruction.

[12] And you — you have done worse than your ancestors. Look at you, each one trailing the stubborn cravings of his own evil heart, refusing to listen to me.

[13] So I am hurling you out of this land into a country neither you nor your ancestors have known. There you can serve other gods day and night to your heart's content — because I will show you no favor.

[14] Yet the days are coming — declares the LORD — when people will no longer swear, "As the LORD lives, who brought Israel up out of Egypt,"

[15] but instead, "As the LORD lives, who brought Israel up out of the north country and out of every land where he had banished them." I will bring them back to their own soil — the soil I gave their ancestors.

[16] Right now, though — I am sending for fishermen, declares the LORD, and they will haul them in. Then I will send for hunters, and they will track them down on every mountain, every hill, every crevice in the rocks.

[17] My eyes are on every road they walk. They cannot hide from me. Their guilt is not concealed from my sight.

[18] First I will repay them double for their guilt, their sin — because they polluted my land with the carcasses of their vile idols, and stuffed my inheritance full of their abominations.

[19] LORD — my strength, my stronghold, my refuge in the day of distress — the nations will come to you from the ends of the earth and say: Our ancestors inherited nothing but lies, hollow gods that do nothing.

[20] Can a person make gods for himself? Those are not gods.

[21] So now — watch — I am going to teach them. This time, I will make them know my hand and my power. They will know that my name is the LORD.

[1] Judah's sin is engraved with an iron stylus, etched with a diamond point — carved deep into the tablet of their hearts, chiseled onto the horns of their altars. [2] Even their children remember the altars and the Asherah poles standing beside every leafy tree, on every high hill, [3] on the mountains of the open country. Your wealth, every treasure you've hoarded — I am handing it all over as plunder, along with your high places, payment for sin sown through your borders. [4] You will lose your grip on the inheritance I gave you. I will make you slaves to your enemies in a land you've never seen — because you have set my anger on fire, and it will burn forever.

[5] This is what the LORD says: Cursed is the one who leans on mere humans, who makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from the LORD. [6] He will be like a shriveled bush in the wasteland, blind to any good thing coming — planted in the salt-burnt places of the desert, in a land where no one lives.

[7] Blessed is the one who trusts the LORD, whose confidence rests entirely in him. [8] He is like a tree planted by water, sending its roots toward the stream. It does not flinch when the heat comes; its leaves stay green. It does not panic in a year of drought, and it never stops bearing fruit.

[9] The heart — more crooked than anything, sick beyond cure. Who can possibly understand it? [10] I, the LORD, search the heart. I test the gut. I give each person exactly what their life has earned, exactly what their actions deserve.

[11] Like a partridge sitting on eggs she didn't lay — that's the person who builds a fortune by injustice. Halfway through his life it abandons him, and in the end he is exposed as a fool.

[12] A throne of glory, lifted high from the beginning — that is the place of our sanctuary. [13] LORD, hope of Israel — all who abandon you will be shamed. Those who turn from you will be written in the dust, because they have forsaken the LORD, the fountain of living water.

[14] Heal me, LORD, and I will be healed. Save me, and I will be saved. You are the one I praise. [15] Listen to them taunting me: "Where is this word from the LORD? Let's see it come true." [16] But I haven't run from being your shepherd; I have not longed for the day of disaster — you know this. Every word that left my lips was spoken in your presence. [17] Don't become a terror to me. You are my refuge in the day of catastrophe. [18] Let my persecutors be the ones shamed — not me. Let them be shattered — not me. Bring the day of disaster down on them. Crush them — double.

[19] This is what the LORD said to me: Go and stand at the People's Gate, where the kings of Judah enter and leave, and then at every gate of Jerusalem. [20] Say to them: Hear the word of the LORD, kings of Judah, all of Judah, every resident of Jerusalem who walks through these gates. [21] This is what the LORD says: Watch yourselves. Do not haul any load on the Sabbath day. Do not bring it through the gates of Jerusalem. [22] Do not carry a load out of your houses on the Sabbath. Do no work at all. Keep the Sabbath holy, as I commanded your ancestors. [23] But they didn't listen, didn't lean in — they stiffened their necks against hearing, refused to learn anything.

[24] But if you actually listen to me, says the LORD — if you stop hauling loads through these city gates on the Sabbath, if you keep the day holy and do no work on it — [25] then through these gates will come kings and princes who sit on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses, accompanied by their officials, by the people of Judah and the residents of Jerusalem. And this city will be inhabited forever. [26] People will stream in from the towns of Judah, from the villages around Jerusalem, from the territory of Benjamin, from the foothills, from the hill country, from the Negev — bringing burnt offerings, sacrifices, grain offerings, frankincense, thank offerings to the house of the LORD.

[27] But if you will not listen to me — if you refuse to keep the Sabbath holy, if you keep hauling loads through the gates of Jerusalem on the Sabbath — then I will set fire to those gates. The flames will consume the citadels of Jerusalem, and nothing will put them out.

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD:

[2] "Get up. Go down to the potter's house. I'll speak to you there."

[3] So I went down to the potter's house, and there he was, working at the wheel.

[4] The vessel he was shaping collapsed in his hands — clay gone wrong. So he started over. Reworked it into something else. Whatever seemed right to him.

[5] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[6] "House of Israel — can't I do with you what this potter does with his clay? Look at you: clay in my hand. That's all. Just clay in my hand.

[7] One moment I declare that a nation, a kingdom, will be uprooted, torn down, destroyed —

[8] but if that nation turns from its evil, I relent. I do not bring the disaster I had planned.

[9] And one moment I declare that a nation, a kingdom, will be built up and planted —

[10] but if it does evil in my sight and refuses to listen to my voice, I reconsider the good I intended to do for it.

[11] So now — say this to the men of Judah and the people of Jerusalem: 'This is what the LORD says: I am shaping disaster against you. Drawing up a plan. So turn — every one of you — from your evil road. Straighten out your ways and what you're doing.'"

[12] But they'll say: "No use. We're going to follow our own plans. Every one of us will do whatever his stubborn, evil heart wants."

[13] So this is what the LORD says:

Ask among the nations —
who has ever heard of such a thing?
Virgin Israel has done something
unspeakable.

[14] Does the snow of Lebanon ever leave
the rocky slopes?
Do the cold streams from distant mountains
ever run dry?

[15] But my people — my people have forgotten me.
They burn incense to nothing.
They've stumbled off the ancient roads,
wandered onto side paths,
trails that go nowhere.

[16] They've turned their land into a horror,
a thing people whistle at in disgust forever.
Anyone passing through will be shocked,
will shake his head in disbelief.

[17] Like a hot east wind I'll scatter them
before the enemy.
My back, not my face, is what they'll see
on the day of their ruin.

[18] Then they said: "Let's plot against Jeremiah. The priests will still have their instruction, the wise their counsel, the prophets their word. None of that depends on him. Come on — let's attack him with our tongues. Ignore everything he says."

[19] Pay attention to me, LORD!
Listen to what my accusers are saying!

[20] Is evil really the way to repay good?
They've dug a pit for me.
Remember — I stood before you
and spoke up on their behalf,
trying to turn your anger away from them.

[21] So now — hand their children over to famine.
Let the sword do its work on them.
Make their wives childless widows.
Strike down their men with plague,
their young men cut down in battle.

[22] Let screaming break out from their houses
when you bring raiders against them without warning,
because they dug a pit to trap me,
hid snares for my feet.

[23] But you, LORD — you know
every plan they've made to kill me.
Don't cover their guilt.
Don't wipe their sin from your sight.
Let them be thrown down right there before you.
Deal with them in the heat of your anger.

[1] This is what the LORD said: Go buy a clay jar from the potter. Take with you some of the elders of the people and the senior priests. [2] Walk out to the Valley of Ben-Hinnom, to the entrance of the Potsherd Gate, and there proclaim the words I give you.

[3] Say this: Hear the word of the LORD, kings of Judah, people of Jerusalem. This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, declares — I am about to bring catastrophe on this place, and every ear that hears of it will ring.

[4] Because they abandoned me. They turned this place into something foreign. They burned incense here to gods they never knew — they, their ancestors, the kings of Judah — and they soaked this ground with the blood of innocents. [5] They built shrines to Baal and threw their own children into the fire as offerings — something I never commanded, never spoke of, something that never crossed my mind.

[6] So watch — the days are coming, declares the LORD, when this place will no longer be called Topheth or the Valley of Ben-Hinnom. It will be called the Valley of Slaughter.

[7] Right here, in this place, I will unravel every plan Judah and Jerusalem ever made. I will drop them by the sword in front of their enemies, by the hand of those who hunt their lives. I will give their corpses to the vultures and the wild dogs. [8] I will turn this city into a horror, a thing people whistle at as they pass — anyone who walks by will gasp and shake his head at the wounds of it. [9] I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and their daughters. They will devour one another in the siege — when their enemies, the ones hunting their lives, press in and crush them.

[10] Then smash the jar — right there, in front of the men who walked out with you. [11] And say to them: This is what the LORD of Armies declares — I will shatter this people and this city the way a potter's jar is shattered, beyond all repair. They will bury their dead in Topheth until there is no more room to bury.

[12] That is what I will do to this place and to those who live in it, says the LORD. I will make this city itself a Topheth. [13] Every house in Jerusalem, every palace of Judah's kings — every place where they climbed to the rooftops to burn incense to the whole army of heaven and pour out drink offerings to other gods — every one of them will be defiled, every one like Topheth.

[14] Then Jeremiah came back from Topheth, where the LORD had sent him to prophesy, and he stood in the courtyard of the LORD's house and said to all the people: [15] This is what the LORD of Armies, the God of Israel, declares — listen — I am bringing on this city and on every town around it every disaster I spoke against it, because they stiffened their necks and refused to hear my words.

[1] Pashhur son of Immer, the priest who served as chief officer in the LORD's house, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things.

[2] So Pashhur had the prophet Jeremiah beaten and locked him in the stocks at the Upper Benjamin Gate of the LORD's house.

[3] The next morning, when Pashhur released him from the stocks, Jeremiah told him: "The LORD is no longer calling you Pashhur. Your new name is Terror-on-Every-Side.

[4] Because this is what the LORD says: I am turning you into a thing of terror — to yourself and to everyone you love. You will watch them cut down by enemy swords, and your eyes will not look away. I am handing all of Judah over to the king of Babylon — he will drag them off into exile, he will strike them down.

[5] Every treasure of this city, every hoard of wealth, every precious thing, every royal stockpile of Judah's kings — I am giving it all to their enemies. They will loot it. They will seize it. They will haul it to Babylon.

[6] And you, Pashhur — you and everyone in your house — you will go into exile. You will reach Babylon. You will die there. You will be buried there. You and every friend you ever lied to with your false prophecies."

[7] You seduced me, LORD, and I let myself be seduced. You overpowered me — you won. Now I am the joke of the town, the punchline they pass around all day.

[8] Every time I open my mouth, I'm screaming — violence! ruin! Your word has made me a laughingstock, insulted from sunrise to sunset.

[9] So I told myself: I'm done. I won't mention him. I won't speak his name again. But then it's there — fire shut up in my bones, a blaze in my chest. I wear myself out trying to hold it in. I can't.

[10] I hear them whispering — Terror everywhere! Turn him in! Let's turn him in! Even my so-called friends are watching for me to slip — Maybe he'll trip up, then we'll have him, then we'll get our revenge.

[11] But the LORD is with me like a soldier nobody messes with. So my hunters will stumble. They will not win. Their failure will be permanent — a humiliation no one ever forgets.

[12] LORD of armies — you who test the righteous, who see straight through to the heart and gut — let me watch you settle the score with them. I have laid my case at your feet.

[13] Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD! He pulls the life of the needy out of the hands of those who would crush it.

[14] Curse the day I was born. The day my mother delivered me — let it not be blessed.

[15] Curse the man who brought my father the news, who made him glad with the words: A son! You have a son!

[16] Let that man be like the cities the LORD overthrew without mercy. Let him hear screams in the morning and war cries at noon —

[17] because he didn't kill me in the womb, didn't make my mother's belly my grave, her womb forever swollen with me inside.

[18] Why? Why did I come out of the womb at all — just to see trouble, just to drown in grief, just to spend my days drenched in shame?

[1] The word came to Jeremiah from the LORD when King Zedekiah sent Pashhur son of Malkijah and Zephaniah the priest, son of Maaseiah, with this message:

[2] "Consult the LORD for us. Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is laying siege to us. Maybe the LORD will do something extraordinary, the kind of thing he's done before, and force Babylon to pull back."

[3] Jeremiah answered them: "Tell Zedekiah this —

[4] The LORD, the God of Israel, says: I am turning your own weapons against you. The swords you're holding to fight Babylon and the Chaldeans surrounding your walls — I'm going to pile them up inside this city.

[5] And I myself will fight against you, with an outstretched hand and a powerful arm, with fury and rage and overwhelming wrath.

[6] I will strike down everyone living in this city — people and animals alike. A devastating plague will sweep through.

[7] After that, says the LORD, I will hand Zedekiah king of Judah and his officials and whatever's left of this city — whoever survives the plague, the sword, the starvation — into the hands of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, into the hands of their enemies, into the hands of those hunting their lives. He will cut them down without mercy. No pity. No compassion.

[8] And to this people you will say: The LORD says — listen carefully. I am placing before you the road to life and the road to death.

[9] Whoever stays in this city will die — by the sword, by famine, by plague. But whoever walks out and surrenders to the Chaldeans laying siege to you will live. Their life will be the only spoil they carry out.

[10] I have set my face against this city for catastrophe, not for good, says the LORD. It will be handed over to the king of Babylon, and he will torch it.

[11] And to the royal house of Judah, hear the word of the LORD —

[12] House of David, the LORD says: Render justice every morning. Rescue the one being robbed from the hand of the oppressor. Or my fury will break out like fire and burn with no one to put it out — all because of what you've done.

[13] I am against you, you who sit enthroned above the valley, rock of the plain, says the LORD. You who say, 'Who could ever come down on us? Who could break into our stronghold?'

[14] I will deal with you according to the fruit of your deeds, says the LORD. I will kindle a fire in your forest, and it will devour everything around you."

[1] This is what the LORD said: Go down to the palace of the king of Judah. Stand there and deliver this message.

[2] Say: Listen to the word of the LORD, king of Judah, you who sit on David's throne — you, your officials, your people who pass through these gates.

[3] This is what the LORD demands: Do justice. Rescue the robbed from the hand of the oppressor. Don't exploit the immigrant, the orphan, the widow. Don't shed innocent blood in this place.

[4] If you actually do this, kings will keep coming through these palace gates — kings on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses, the king himself with his officials and his people.

[5] But if you ignore these words, I swear by myself, says the LORD, this palace will become rubble.

[6] This is what the LORD says about the royal house of Judah: You are Gilead to me, the peak of Lebanon — but I swear I'll make you a wasteland, a city no one lives in.

[7] I'm consecrating destroyers against you, each with his weapon. They'll cut down your finest cedars and throw them into the fire.

[8] Nations will pass by this city and ask each other: Why did the LORD do this to such a great city?

[9] And the answer will come: Because they abandoned the covenant of the LORD their God. They bowed to other gods. They served them.

[10] Don't weep for the dead. Don't mourn him. Weep instead for the one who is leaving — he'll never come back, never see the land of his birth again.

[11] This is what the LORD says about Shallum son of Josiah, king of Judah, who took the throne after his father and then was taken from this place: He will not return.

[12] He'll die in the place they exiled him to. He'll never see this land again.

[13] Disaster on the man who builds his house with injustice, his upper rooms with theft — who works his neighbor for nothing and refuses to pay him.

[14] Who says: I'll build myself a grand palace with spacious upper rooms. Who cuts out windows, panels it in cedar, paints it red.

[15] Does cedar make you a king? Your father ate and drank too — but he did justice and righteousness, and it went well with him.

[16] He defended the poor and the needy — and it went well. That's what it means to know me, says the LORD.

[17] But your eyes, your heart — they're set on nothing but your own gain, on shedding innocent blood, on oppression and extortion.

[18] So this is what the LORD says about Jehoiakim son of Josiah, king of Judah: No one will mourn him — no Alas, my brother! No Alas, my sister! No Alas, lord! No Alas, his majesty!

[19] He'll be buried like a donkey — dragged out and dumped beyond the gates of Jerusalem.

[20] Climb Lebanon and scream. Lift your voice in Bashan. Wail from Abarim — every one of your lovers is broken.

[21] I spoke to you when you were comfortable — you said: I'm not listening. This has been your pattern since you were young. You've never obeyed my voice.

[22] The wind will herd away your shepherds. Your lovers will march into exile. Then, finally, you'll be ashamed — humiliated by all your wickedness.

[23] You who nest in Lebanon, perched in cedar — how you'll groan when the labor pains hit, the agony of a woman in childbirth.

[24] As I live, says the LORD, even if you, Coniah son of Jehoiakim, king of Judah, were the signet ring on my right hand — I'd rip you off.

[25] I'm handing you over to those who want you dead, the ones you're terrified of — Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and the Chaldeans.

[26] I'm hurling you and the mother who bore you into a land neither of you was born in. That's where you'll die.

[27] The land you long to return to — you'll never set foot in it again.

[28] Is this man Coniah some shattered, despised pot, some piece of junk nobody wants? Why are he and his children thrown out, flung into a land they don't know?

[29] Land, land, land — hear the word of the LORD!

[30] This is what the LORD says: Record this man as childless — a man who failed in his lifetime. None of his offspring will succeed. None will sit on David's throne or rule again in Judah.

[1] Disaster on the shepherds who scatter and shred the sheep of my pasture — this is the LORD's word.

[2] So here is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says to the shepherds tending my people: You scattered my flock. You drove them off. You never once checked on them. Now I will check on you — for the evil of what you have done. This is the LORD's word.

[3] And I myself will gather what is left of my flock from every country where I drove them, and I will bring them home to their own pasture. They will be fruitful. They will multiply.

[4] I will raise up shepherds over them who will actually shepherd them, and they will never again be afraid, never again be terrified, never again go missing. This is the LORD's word.

[5] Look — the days are coming, says the LORD, when I will raise up for David a Branch, a righteous one. A king who will reign with wisdom and execute justice and righteousness across the land.

[6] In his days Judah will be saved and Israel will live in safety. And this is the name they will call him: *The LORD Is Our Righteousness.*

[7] So the days are coming, declares the LORD, when people will no longer swear, "As surely as the LORD lives who brought Israel up out of Egypt" —

[8] but instead, "As surely as the LORD lives who brought the offspring of Israel up out of the north, out of all the countries where he had driven them." And they will live on their own soil again.

[9] About the prophets:

My heart is shattered inside me.
Every bone in me trembles.
I am like a drunk man,
like a man overcome by wine —
because of the LORD,
because of his holy words.

[10] The land is crawling with adulterers. Under the curse the land lies parched, the desert pastures dried to dust. Their course is evil. Their power is misused.

[11] Even prophet and priest are godless — I have found their wickedness right inside my own house, declares the LORD.

[12] So their path will turn slick beneath them — they will be shoved into the dark and fall. I am bringing disaster down on them in the year of their reckoning. This is the LORD's word.

[13] Among the prophets of Samaria I saw something disgusting: they prophesied by Baal and led my people Israel astray.

[14] But among the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen something horrifying: they commit adultery. They walk in lies. They prop up the hands of evildoers so that no one turns back from his wickedness. To me they have all become like Sodom — and the people there like Gomorrah.

[15] So this is what the LORD of Hosts says about the prophets:

I will feed them bitter herbs
and make them drink poisoned water,
because from the prophets of Jerusalem
godlessness has spread out
into every corner of the land.

[16] This is what the LORD of Hosts says: Do not listen to the words of the prophets who prophesy to you. They are filling you with empty hope. The vision they speak comes from their own minds — not from the mouth of the LORD.

[17] They keep telling those who despise the LORD's word, "You'll have peace." And to everyone walking in the stubbornness of his own heart, they say, "No harm will come to you."

[18] But who has stood in the council of the LORD to see and hear his word? Who has paid attention to his word and listened?

[19] Look — the storm of the LORD! Fury has broken loose, a whirling storm bursting over the heads of the wicked.

[20] The LORD's anger will not turn back until he has done it — until he has carried out the purposes of his heart. In days to come you will understand it clearly.

[21] I did not send these prophets — but they ran. I did not speak to them — but they prophesied.

[22] If they had stood in my council, they would have proclaimed my words to my people and turned them back from their evil road, from the evil of what they were doing.

[23] Am I only a God up close, says the LORD — and not a God far off?

[24] Can anyone hide in some secret place where I cannot see him? declares the LORD. Do I not fill heaven and earth? This is the LORD's word.

[25] I have heard what the prophets are saying — the ones who prophesy lies in my name. "I had a dream! I had a dream!"

[26] How long will this go on — in the hearts of prophets who prophesy lies, prophets of their own self-deception?

[27] They think they can make my people forget my name with the dreams they keep telling each other — the same way their fathers forgot my name for Baal.

[28] Let the prophet who had a dream tell his dream. But let the one who has my word speak my word faithfully. What does straw have to do with grain? declares the LORD.

[29] Is not my word like fire — declares the LORD — like a hammer that shatters rock?

[30] So I am against the prophets, declares the LORD — the ones who steal my words from one another.

[31] I am against the prophets, declares the LORD — the ones who wag their tongues and announce, "The LORD declares."

[32] I am against those who prophesy lying dreams, declares the LORD — who tell them and lead my people astray with their lies and their reckless boasting. I never sent them. I never commissioned them. They are no help at all to this people. This is the LORD's word.

[33] When this people — or a prophet or a priest — asks you, "What is the burden of the LORD?" — you tell them: *You* are the burden. And I am throwing you off, declares the LORD.

[34] As for the prophet or priest or anyone of the people who says, "The burden of the LORD" — I will punish that man and his household.

[35] Here is what each of you should say to his neighbor and his brother: "What did the LORD answer? What has the LORD spoken?"

[36] But never again mention "the burden of the LORD" — because the burden turns out to be each man's own word. You are twisting the words of the living God, the LORD of Hosts, our God.

[37] This is what you say to a prophet: "What did the LORD answer you? What has the LORD spoken?"

[38] But if you say, "The burden of the LORD," then this is what the LORD says: Because you keep using that phrase — "the burden of the LORD" — after I sent you word: *do not say it* —

[39] then look: I will lift you up, completely. I will hurl you out of my presence, you and the city I gave to you and your fathers.

[40] And I will fasten on you an everlasting disgrace, a humiliation that will never be forgotten.

[1] After Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon hauled Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim — king of Judah — into exile from Jerusalem, along with the officials of Judah, the craftsmen, and the metalworkers, dragging them off to Babylon, the LORD showed me something. Two baskets of figs, set out in front of the temple of the LORD.

[2] One basket held figs so good they looked like the season's first ripe pick. The other basket held figs so rotten you couldn't put them in your mouth.

[3] The LORD said to me, "Jeremiah, what do you see?" I said, "Figs. The good ones, very good. The bad ones, very bad — too foul to eat."

[4] Then the word of the LORD came to me:

[5] "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: Like these good figs, that is how I will regard the exiles of Judah — the ones I sent out from this place into the land of the Babylonians. For their good.

[6] I will fix my eyes on them with favor. I will bring them home to this land. I will build them up and not tear them down. I will plant them and not pull them up.

[7] I will give them a heart that knows me — knows that I am the LORD. They will be my people, and I will be their God, because they will turn back to me with everything in them.

[8] But like the rotten figs — too foul to eat — that is how I will treat Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, what's left of Jerusalem still in this land, and the ones who took refuge in Egypt. So says the LORD.

[9] I will make them a horror, a disaster, to every kingdom on earth. A byword. A punchline. A curse and a taunt wherever I scatter them.

[10] I will send sword, famine, and plague chasing them down until they are wiped clean off the soil I gave their ancestors — and gave to them."
