[28] Arrows can't make him run. Slingstones are husks of chaff. [29] Clubs feel like twigs. He laughs at the whistle of a javelin. [30] His underside is jagged pottery, dragging through the mud like a threshing sled. [31] He turns the deep into a boiling pot, churns the sea into a perfumer's mixing bowl. [32] He leaves a wake of foam that gleams white — you'd think the deep had gone gray-haired. [33] Nothing on earth is his equal. He was made without fear. [34] He looks down on everything that towers. He is king over all the proud.

[1] Then Job answered the LORD:

[2] "I see it now. You can do anything. Nothing you intend can be blocked.

[3] You asked: Who is this clouding my counsel with ignorance? That was me. I spoke about things I never understood — wonders too high, too vast, beyond my reach.

[4] You said: Listen now, and I will speak. I will question you, and you will answer me.

[5] I had heard of you — rumors, secondhand reports. But now my own eyes have seen you.

[6] So I take back every word. I sit down in the dust and ashes and I am done."

[7] After the LORD finished speaking to Job, he turned to Eliphaz the Temanite: "My anger burns against you and your two friends. You did not speak the truth about me — Job did.

[8] So now — take seven bulls and seven rams. Go to my servant Job. Offer them as a burnt offering for yourselves. And Job — my servant Job — will pray for you. I will accept his prayer and not give you what your foolishness deserves. Because you did not speak rightly about me, the way my servant Job did."

[9] So Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did what the LORD told them. And the LORD accepted Job's prayer for them.

[10] When Job prayed for his friends, the LORD restored him. Everything Job had lost — the LORD gave back double.

[11] Then all his brothers, all his sisters, everyone who had known him before, came to his house. They ate with him. They mourned with him. They comforted him over all the disaster the LORD had brought upon him. And each one gave him a piece of silver and a ring of gold.

[12] The LORD blessed the rest of Job's life more than the beginning. He owned fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand donkeys.

[13] He fathered seven sons and three daughters.

[14] He named the first Jemimah — Dove. The second, Keziah — Cinnamon. The third, Keren-happuch — Horn of Beauty.

[15] Nowhere in the land were women as stunning as Job's daughters. And their father gave them an inheritance alongside their brothers — unheard of.

[16] Job lived a hundred and forty years after this. He saw his children, and his children's children — four generations.

[17] Then Job died, old and full of days.

[1] The word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel:

[2] Listen, you elders. Pay attention, every soul in the land. Has anything like this ever happened in your lifetime? Or in your ancestors'?

[3] Tell your children. Have them tell theirs. And theirs the generation after.

[4] What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust devoured. What the swarming locust left, the hopping locust devoured. What the hopping locust left, the destroying locust finished off.

[5] Wake up, drunkards. Weep. Wail, every wine-lover — the new wine is ripped from your lips.

[6] A nation has invaded my land — massive, beyond counting. Its teeth are a lion's teeth. Its fangs, a lioness.

[7] It has stripped my vine bare, splintered my fig tree, peeled off the bark and thrown it down. The branches gleam white, exposed.

[8] Mourn like a young woman in sackcloth weeping for the man she was promised to.

[9] Grain offering and drink offering — gone from the house of the LORD. The priests are in mourning, the ministers of the LORD.

[10] The fields are wrecked. The ground itself mourns. The grain is destroyed, the new wine dried up, the olive oil failing.

[11] Be ashamed, you farmers. Wail, you vinedressers — for the wheat, for the barley, for the harvest of the field, all of it lost.

[12] The vine is dry. The fig tree withers. Pomegranate, palm, apple — every tree in the field has shriveled. Joy itself has dried up in the children of men.

[13] Put on sackcloth, priests. Mourn. Wail, you who serve at the altar. Come, spend the night in sackcloth, ministers of my God — grain offering and drink offering are cut off from the house of your God.

[14] Call a sacred fast. Summon a solemn assembly. Gather the elders, gather every inhabitant of the land into the house of the LORD your God — and cry out to the LORD.

[15] That day. The day. The day of the LORD is close — and it comes like devastation from the Almighty.

[16] Has the food not been ripped away right in front of us? Joy and gladness — ripped from the house of our God?

[17] The seeds shrivel beneath their clods. The storehouses lie ruined, the granaries torn down. The grain has failed.

[18] Listen — the cattle groan. The herds wander confused, no pasture anywhere. Even the flocks of sheep are suffering.

[19] To you, LORD, I cry — fire has eaten the open pastures, flame has scorched every tree in the field.

[20] Even the wild animals pant for you. The streams have run dry. Fire has devoured the open pastures.

[1] Sound the alarm on Zion. Blast the trumpet on my holy mountain. Every soul in the land — tremble. The Day of the LORD is coming. It's almost here.

[2] A day of darkness and gloom. A day of clouds and black sky. Like dawn spreading across the mountains — a vast army, an overwhelming force. Nothing like it has ever existed. Nothing like it will come again, not for generations beyond counting.

[3] Fire devours ahead of them. Flames consume behind. The land in front of them is Eden. The land behind them is wasteland. Nothing escapes.

[4] They look like horses. They charge like cavalry.

[5] Listen — the rumble across the mountaintops, like chariots, like fire crackling through stubble, like an army in battle formation.

[6] Nations writhe at the sight of them. Every face goes pale.

[7] They run like warriors. They scale walls like soldiers. Each one holds his line. None breaks formation.

[8] They don't crowd each other. Each marches his own path. They crash through weapons and don't even slow down.

[9] They swarm the city. They sprint along the walls. They climb into houses. They come through the windows like thieves.

[10] The earth quakes before them. The heavens shudder. Sun and moon go dark. Stars withhold their light.

[11] And the LORD thunders at the head of his army. His ranks are massive. Mighty are those who carry out his word. The Day of the LORD is huge. It is terrifying. Who can endure it?

[12] Even now — this is the LORD speaking — turn back to me with everything you've got. Fast. Weep. Mourn.

[13] Tear your hearts, not your clothes. Come back to the LORD your God. He is gracious. He is merciful. He is slow to anger and overflowing with steadfast love. He relents from disaster.

[14] Who knows? He may turn. He may relent. He may leave a blessing behind him — grain and wine for the offering to the LORD your God.

[15] Sound the trumpet on Zion. Call a fast. Convene a sacred assembly.

[16] Gather the people. Sanctify the congregation. Bring in the elders. Bring in the children, even the nursing infants. The bridegroom — pull him from his chamber. The bride — from her wedding canopy.

[17] Let the priests, who serve the LORD, weep between the entrance and the altar. Let them cry out: Spare your people, LORD. Don't make your inheritance a punchline, a proverb among the nations. Why should anyone be allowed to say, "Where is their God?"

[18] Then the LORD became jealous for his land. He had compassion on his people.

[19] The LORD answered them: Listen — I'm sending you grain, new wine, fresh oil, enough to satisfy you. I will never again make you a disgrace among the nations.

[20] The army from the north — I'll drive it far away from you. I'll push it into a parched and desolate land, its front ranks into the eastern sea, its rear into the western sea. Its stench will rise. Its rot will reek. Because it tried to do the unthinkable.

[21] Soil, don't be afraid. Rejoice. Be glad. The LORD has done magnificent things.

[22] Wild animals, don't be afraid. The pastures are turning green again. The trees are bearing fruit. The fig tree and the vine are giving their richness.

[23] Children of Zion — celebrate. Rejoice in the LORD your God. He has given you the early rain to vindicate you. He pours down the rains for you — both the early and the late — as he did before.

[24] The threshing floors will overflow with grain. The vats will burst with new wine and oil.

[25] I will pay you back for the years the locusts ate — the swarming locust, the hopping locust, the destroying locust, the cutting locust — my great army that I unleashed against you.

[26] You'll eat your fill. You'll be satisfied. You will praise the name of the LORD your God, who has done wonders for you. My people will never be put to shame again.

[27] You will know that I am right here in the middle of Israel, that I am the LORD your God, and there is no other. My people will never be put to shame.

[28] And after all that — I will pour out my Spirit on every kind of flesh. Your sons and daughters will prophesy. Your old men will dream dreams. Your young men will see visions.

[29] Even on the servants — male and female — in those days I'll pour out my Spirit.

[30] I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth — blood and fire and columns of smoke.

[31] The sun will turn to darkness. The moon will turn to blood. Before the great and terrifying Day of the LORD arrives.

[32] And everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be rescued. On Mount Zion and in Jerusalem there will be deliverance — just as the LORD promised — and among the survivors, the ones the LORD himself is calling.

[1] In those days, at that hour — when I reverse the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem —

[2] I will gather every nation. I will march them down into the Valley of Jehoshaphat. And there I will put them on trial — for what they did to my people, my inheritance, Israel. They scattered them among the nations. They carved up my land.

[3] They rolled dice for my people. They sold a boy for the price of a prostitute. They traded a girl for a jug of wine — and drank it down.

[4] And you — Tyre, Sidon, every district of Philistia — what do you think you're doing to me? Settling some score? Try it. I will turn it back on your own heads, fast and final.

[5] You looted my silver and gold. You hauled my treasures into your temples.

[6] You sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks — shipped them far from their own soil.

[7] Watch. I am pulling them back from every place you sold them off to — and I am turning your deeds back on your own heads.

[8] I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hands of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans — a nation at the ends of the earth. The LORD has spoken.

[9] Announce it among the nations: Sanctify war! Wake the warriors! Let every fighting man muster and march!

[10] Hammer your plows into swords. Beat your pruning hooks into spears. Let the weakling say, "I am a warrior."

[11] Hurry, all you nations — converge from every side. — LORD, send down your warriors there.

[12] Let the nations rouse themselves. Let them climb to the Valley of Jehoshaphat. Because there — there I will take my seat to judge every nation on every side.

[13] Swing the sickle — the harvest is ripe. Come, tread — the winepress is full, the vats overflow — their wickedness has grown that vast.

[14] Crowds, crowds, in the Valley of Decision! The day of the LORD is closing in on the Valley of Decision.

[15] Sun and moon go black. The stars pull back their light.

[16] The LORD roars from Zion. From Jerusalem he gives voice — and heaven and earth shudder. But for his people, the LORD is shelter. For the children of Israel, a fortress.

[17] Then you will know: I am the LORD your God, the one who lives on Zion, my holy mountain. And Jerusalem will be holy ground. Strangers will never trample her again.

[18] On that day — the mountains will drip with sweet wine, the hills will run with milk, every gully in Judah will flow with water, and a spring will well up from the house of the LORD and water the Valley of Acacias.

[19] Egypt will be a wasteland. Edom, a desert of ruins — payback for the violence they did to the children of Judah, whose innocent blood they spilled on their own ground.

[20] But Judah will be lived in forever. Jerusalem — generation after generation.

[21] I will avenge their blood — blood I had not yet avenged. And the LORD makes his home in Zion.

[1] In the beginning — the Word. The Word with God. The Word *was* God. [2] He was there, with God, from the start. [3] Everything came through him. Nothing that exists came into being without him. [4] In him: life. And that life was the light blazing through humanity. [5] The light burns in the dark, and the dark has never been able to swallow it.

[6] A man arrived. Sent from God. His name was John. [7] He came as a witness — to testify about the light, so that everyone might believe through him. [8] He wasn't the light himself. He came to point to it.

[9] The real light — the one that illuminates every human being — was breaking into the world. [10] He was in the world. The world came into being through him. And the world had no idea who he was. [11] He came to his own people, and his own people refused him. [12] But to everyone who did take him in, who put their trust in his name, he handed them the right to become children of God — [13] children born not of blood, not of biological urge, not of human decision, but born of God.

[14] And the Word became flesh. Pitched his tent among us. We saw his glory with our own eyes — the glory of a Father's one and only Son — overflowing with grace, overflowing with truth.

[15] John shouted his testimony about him: "This is the one I told you about — 'The one coming after me has overtaken me, because he existed before me.'"

[16] From the fullness pouring out of him, we have all received — grace stacked on grace. [17] The law was given through Moses; grace and truth arrived in person through Jesus the Messiah. [18] No one has ever laid eyes on God. The one and only Son, himself God, pressed close to the Father's heart — he has made him known.

[19] Here's the testimony John gave when the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem dispatched priests and Levites to ask him point-blank: "Who are you?"

[20] He didn't dodge. He confessed openly: "I am not the Messiah."

[21] "Then what?" they pressed. "Are you Elijah?"

"I am not."

"The Prophet?"

"No."

[22] "Then tell us — who? We need an answer to take back. What do you say about yourself?"

[23] He answered with Isaiah's words: "I am a voice. Shouting in the wilderness: *Make the road straight for the Lord.*"

[24] Some of the questioners were Pharisees. [25] They pushed harder: "If you're not the Messiah, not Elijah, not the Prophet — then why are you baptizing?"

[26] John answered, "I baptize with water. But standing right here among you is someone you don't recognize — [27] the one coming after me. I'm not even worthy to untie the strap of his sandal."

[28] All this happened in Bethany, across the Jordan, where John was baptizing.

[29] The next day, John saw Jesus walking toward him and said: "Look — the Lamb of God. The one who lifts away the sin of the world. [30] This is the one I meant when I said, 'A man is coming after me who has overtaken me, because he existed before me.' [31] I didn't know who he was. But the reason I came baptizing with water was so he could be revealed to Israel."

[32] Then John testified: "I watched the Spirit come down out of heaven like a dove and settle on him. [33] I wouldn't have known him myself, but the One who sent me to baptize with water told me: 'The man you see the Spirit descending on and resting upon — he is the one who baptizes with the Holy Spirit.' [34] I saw it. And I testify: this is the Son of God."

[35] The next day, John was standing there again with two of his disciples. [36] Watching Jesus pass by, he said, "Look — the Lamb of God."

[37] The two disciples heard it and started following Jesus.

[38] Jesus turned, saw them trailing him, and asked, "What are you looking for?"

They said, "Rabbi" — which means Teacher — "where are you staying?"

[39] "Come and see," he said.

So they came. They saw where he was staying. And they spent the rest of the day with him. It was about four in the afternoon.

[40] One of the two who had heard John and followed Jesus was Andrew — Simon Peter's brother. [41] The first thing Andrew did was find his brother Simon and tell him, "We have found the Messiah" — that is, the Anointed One. [42] He brought him to Jesus.

Jesus locked eyes on him and said, "You are Simon, son of John. You will be called Cephas" — which translates as Peter. *The Rock.*

[43] The next day, Jesus decided to head for Galilee. He found Philip and said, "Follow me." [44] Philip was from Bethsaida — Andrew and Peter's hometown.

[45] Philip went and found Nathanael: "We've found him — the one Moses wrote about in the law, the one the prophets pointed to. Jesus of Nazareth, son of Joseph."

[46] Nathanael shot back, "Nazareth? Can anything good come out of *Nazareth*?"

Philip said, "Come and see."

[47] Jesus saw Nathanael coming toward him and announced, "Now here's a true Israelite. No deception in him."

[48] "How do you know me?" Nathanael asked.

Jesus said, "Before Philip called you — when you were under the fig tree — I saw you."

[49] Nathanael said, "Rabbi — you are the Son of God. You are the King of Israel."

[50] Jesus answered, "You believe because I told you I saw you under the fig tree? You're going to see things far bigger than that." [51] Then he said: "I tell you the truth — you will all see heaven thrown wide open, and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man."

[1] Three days later, a wedding in Cana of Galilee. Jesus' mother was there. [2] Jesus and his disciples had also been invited. [3] The wine ran out. His mother turned to him: "They have no wine."

[4] "Why is this my concern, woman? My hour has not arrived."

[5] She turned to the servers: "Whatever he tells you, do it."

[6] Six stone jars stood nearby — the kind used for the Jewish purification rites — each one holding twenty or thirty gallons. [7] "Fill them with water," Jesus said. They filled them to the brim. [8] "Now draw some out and take it to the master of the feast." They did.

[9] The master of the feast tasted the water — now wine — with no idea where it had come from, though the servers who'd drawn it knew. He called the bridegroom over [10] and said, "Everyone serves the good wine first, and brings out the cheap stuff once the guests are drunk. You've saved the best for last."

[11] This — the first of his signs — Jesus performed in Cana of Galilee. He revealed his glory, and his disciples put their trust in him.

[12] After this he went down to Capernaum with his mother, his brothers, and his disciples. They stayed only a few days.

[13] Passover was near, so Jesus went up to Jerusalem. [14] In the temple courts he found them — the cattle dealers, the sheep sellers, the dove merchants, the money-changers seated at their tables. [15] He braided a whip out of cords and drove them all out — sheep, cattle, the lot. He sent the coins of the money-changers flying and flipped their tables. [16] To the dove sellers he said, "Get these out of here. Stop turning my Father's house into a marketplace."

[17] His disciples remembered what was written: *Zeal for your house will consume me.*

[18] The Jewish leaders confronted him: "What sign can you show us to justify doing this?"

[19] Jesus answered, "Tear down this temple, and in three days I'll raise it up."

[20] "Forty-six years it took to build this temple — and you'll raise it in three days?" [21] But the temple he meant was his body. [22] After he was raised from the dead, his disciples remembered he'd said this, and they believed the Scripture and the words he had spoken.

[23] While he was in Jerusalem at the Passover festival, many believed in his name — they had seen the signs he was doing. [24] But Jesus didn't entrust himself to them. He knew them all. [25] He needed no one to tell him about people. He knew what was in them.

[1] There was a man — a Pharisee, Nicodemus by name, one of the rulers of the Jews.

[2] He came to Jesus at night and said, "Rabbi, we know you've come from God as a teacher. No one could do the signs you're doing unless God were with him."

[3] Jesus answered, "I'll tell you the truth — unless someone is born from above, they cannot see the kingdom of God."

[4] Nicodemus said, "Born? How can a grown man be born? He can't crawl back into his mother's womb and start over."

[5] Jesus answered, "I'm telling you the truth. Unless someone is born of water and Spirit, they cannot enter the kingdom of God.

[6] Flesh gives birth to flesh. Spirit gives birth to spirit.

[7] Don't be shocked that I said you must be born from above.

[8] The wind goes where it wants. You hear it moving, but you can't tell where it came from or where it's going. That's how it is with everyone born of the Spirit."

[9] Nicodemus said, "How is any of this possible?"

[10] Jesus answered, "You're the teacher of Israel and you don't grasp this?

[11] I'm telling you the truth — we speak what we know, we testify to what we've seen, and your people will not receive what we say.

[12] If I've told you earthly things and you don't believe, how will you believe when I tell you heavenly ones?

[13] No one has ascended into heaven except the one who descended from heaven — the Son of Man.

[14] And just as Moses lifted up the snake in the wilderness, the Son of Man must be lifted up,

[15] so that everyone who trusts him will have life that never ends.

[16] Because this is how God loved the world: he gave his only Son, so that everyone who trusts him will not be destroyed but will have life that never ends.

[17] God didn't send his Son into the world to condemn the world. He sent him so the world could be rescued through him.

[18] Whoever trusts him isn't condemned. Whoever refuses to trust stands condemned already — because they have not trusted the name of God's only Son.

[19] And here's the verdict: the light came into the world, and people loved the darkness more than the light, because what they were doing was evil.

[20] Everyone who lives in evil hates the light and won't come near it, terrified their deeds will be exposed.

[21] But whoever lives in the truth comes to the light, so it becomes obvious that what they've done has been done in God.

[22] After this, Jesus went with his disciples into the Judean countryside. He stayed there with them and was baptizing.

[23] John, too, was baptizing — at Aenon near Salim, because the water there was plentiful. People kept coming to be baptized.

[24] (This was before John was thrown into prison.)

[25] A dispute broke out between John's disciples and a certain Jew about purification.

[26] They came to John and said, "Rabbi — the one who was with you across the Jordan, the one you testified about — look, he's baptizing, and everyone's going to him."

[27] John answered, "No one can receive anything unless it's been given to him from heaven.

[28] You yourselves can vouch for me — I said I'm not the Messiah. I was sent ahead of him.

[29] The bride belongs to the bridegroom. The friend of the bridegroom stands by, listens for his voice, and is overjoyed just to hear it. That joy — that's mine, and it's complete.

[30] He has to grow greater. I have to grow smaller.

[31] The one who comes from above is over everything. The one who comes from the earth belongs to the earth and speaks from the earth. The one who comes from heaven is over everything.

[32] He testifies to what he has seen and heard — and no one accepts his testimony.

[33] Whoever does accept it has certified that God is true.

[34] The one God sent speaks God's words, because God gives the Spirit without measure.

[35] The Father loves the Son and has placed everything in his hands.

[36] Whoever trusts the Son has life that never ends. Whoever refuses the Son will not see life — God's wrath rests on them."

[1] Word reached Jesus that the Pharisees had heard he was making and baptizing more disciples than John— [2] though it wasn't actually Jesus doing the baptizing; his disciples were. [3] So he left Judea and headed back toward Galilee.

[4] The route took him through Samaria. [5] He came to a Samaritan town called Sychar, near the parcel of land Jacob had given his son Joseph. [6] Jacob's well was there. Jesus, worn out from the road, sat down right at the well. It was about noon.

[7] A Samaritan woman came to draw water. Jesus said, "Give me a drink." [8] His disciples had gone into town to buy food.

[9] The Samaritan woman said, "You're a Jew. I'm a Samaritan woman. How is it you're asking me for a drink?" (Jews don't share cups with Samaritans.)

[10] Jesus answered, "If you knew what God is offering—if you knew who is asking you for a drink—you'd have asked him, and he'd have given you living water."

[11] "Sir," she said, "you don't even have a bucket, and the well is deep. Where exactly do you get this living water? [12] You're not greater than our father Jacob, are you? He gave us this well. He drank from it himself. So did his sons. So did his livestock."

[13] Jesus said, "Everyone who drinks this water will be thirsty again. [14] But whoever drinks the water I give will never thirst again—ever. The water I give becomes a spring inside them, welling up into eternal life."

[15] "Sir," she said, "give me this water. Then I won't get thirsty. I won't have to keep coming out here to draw."

[16] He said, "Go call your husband and come back."

[17] "I don't have a husband," she said.

Jesus said, "Right—'I don't have a husband.' [18] You've had five. The man you're with now isn't your husband. You spoke the truth."

[19] "Sir," she said, "I see you're a prophet. [20] Our ancestors worshiped on this mountain, but you people say Jerusalem is the place where worship belongs."

[21] Jesus said, "Believe me—an hour is coming when you'll worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem. [22] You worship what you don't know. We worship what we know, because salvation comes from the Jews. [23] But the hour is coming—it's already here—when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and in truth. These are the worshipers the Father is looking for. [24] God is spirit. Those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth."

[25] The woman said, "I know Messiah is coming—the one called Christ. When he comes, he'll explain everything to us."

[26] Jesus said, "I am he—the one speaking to you right now."

[27] Just then his disciples returned. They were stunned to find him talking with a woman, but no one asked, "What do you want?" or "Why are you talking with her?"

[28] The woman left her water jar behind, ran into town, and said to the people, [29] "Come see a man who told me everything I've ever done. Could this be the Messiah?" [30] They came out of the town and started making their way toward him.

[31] Meanwhile his disciples were urging him, "Rabbi, eat something."

[32] He said, "I have food to eat you don't know about."

[33] The disciples said to each other, "Did someone bring him food?"

[34] Jesus said, "My food is doing the will of the one who sent me—finishing his work. [35] Don't you say, 'Four more months till harvest'? Look around. Lift your eyes. The fields are ripe right now—ready to be cut. [36] The reaper is already drawing his wages, gathering a crop for eternal life, so that sower and reaper celebrate together. [37] The saying holds: one sows, another reaps. [38] I sent you to reap what cost you nothing. Others did the hard labor; you've stepped into their work."

[39] Many Samaritans from that town believed in him because of the woman's testimony: "He told me everything I've ever done." [40] When the Samaritans came to him, they begged him to stay. He stayed two days. [41] Many more believed because of his own words. [42] They told the woman, "We don't believe just because of what you said anymore. We've heard him ourselves. We know—this man really is the Savior of the world."

[43] After the two days he left for Galilee. [44] (Jesus himself had said a prophet gets no honor in his own hometown.) [45] When he arrived in Galilee, the Galileans welcomed him. They'd seen everything he'd done in Jerusalem at the festival—they had been there themselves.

[46] He came again to Cana in Galilee, where he had turned the water into wine. A royal official was there whose son was sick in Capernaum. [47] When he heard Jesus had come from Judea into Galilee, he went to him and begged him to come down and heal his son, who was about to die.

[48] Jesus said, "Unless you people see signs and wonders, you simply will not believe."

[49] The official said, "Sir, come down before my child dies."

[50] Jesus said, "Go. Your son lives."

The man took Jesus at his word and started home. [51] While he was still on the road, his servants met him with the news—the boy was alive. [52] He asked them what hour he'd begun to recover. They said, "The fever broke yesterday at one in the afternoon." [53] The father realized that was the exact hour Jesus had said, "Your son lives." He believed—he and his whole household.

[54] This was the second sign Jesus did after coming from Judea into Galilee.

[1] After this, there was a Jewish festival, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.

[2] By the Sheep Gate there's a pool — Bethesda in Aramaic — ringed by five covered colonnades.

[3] Under them lay a crowd of broken people: blind, lame, paralyzed.

[4] —

[5] One man had been there thirty-eight years, locked in his sickness.

[6] Jesus saw him lying there. He knew how long it had been. He asked him, "Do you want to be well?"

[7] The man answered, "Sir, I have no one to put me in the pool when the water stirs. Every time I try, someone else gets there first."

[8] Jesus said, "Get up. Pick up your mat. Walk."

[9] Instantly the man was whole. He picked up his mat and started walking. It was the Sabbath.

[10] The Jewish leaders confronted the healed man: "It's the Sabbath. You're not allowed to carry that mat."

[11] He told them, "The man who healed me — he told me to pick it up and walk."

[12] "Who?" they demanded. "Who told you to carry it?"

[13] But the man had no idea — Jesus had slipped away into the crowd.

[14] Later, Jesus found him in the temple. "Look at you — whole again. Now stop sinning, or something worse may come."

[15] The man went and told the leaders it was Jesus who had healed him.

[16] And so the persecution began — they came after Jesus for doing this on the Sabbath.

[17] His answer: "My Father is still working. So am I."

[18] That sent them over the edge. Now they wanted him dead — not just for breaking Sabbath, but for calling God his own Father, making himself equal with God.

[19] So Jesus told them: "I'm telling you the truth. The Son can't do anything on his own. He only does what he sees the Father doing. Whatever the Father does, the Son does the same.

[20] The Father loves the Son. He shows him everything he's doing. And he's going to show him even greater works — works that will leave you stunned.

[21] Just as the Father raises the dead and gives them life, the Son gives life to whoever he chooses.

[22] The Father doesn't judge anyone. He's handed all judgment to the Son,

[23] so that everyone will honor the Son the way they honor the Father. Refuse to honor the Son, and you've refused to honor the Father who sent him.

[24] Listen to me: whoever hears my words and trusts the One who sent me — that person has eternal life. No judgment waiting. They've already crossed from death into life.

[25] The hour is coming — it's here now — when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear will live.

[26] The Father has life in himself. He's granted the Son the same — life in himself.

[27] And he's given him authority to judge, because he is the Son of Man.

[28] Don't be amazed at this. An hour is coming when everyone in the graves will hear his voice

[29] and come out — those who did good rising to life, those who did evil rising to judgment.

[30] I can't do anything on my own. I judge by what I hear. My judgment is just, because I'm not chasing my own will — I'm chasing the will of the One who sent me.

[31] If I testify about myself alone, my testimony doesn't count.

[32] But there's another who testifies about me, and I know his testimony is true.

[33] You sent people to John. He told you the truth.

[34] I don't need human testimony — I'm only bringing this up so you might be saved.

[35] John was a lamp, burning and shining, and for a moment you were happy to bask in his light.

[36] But I have testimony heavier than John's. The works the Father gave me to finish — the very works I'm doing — they're the proof. They shout that the Father sent me.

[37] And the Father himself, the One who sent me, has testified about me. You've never heard his voice. You've never seen his form.

[38] His word doesn't live in you, because you don't believe the One he sent.

[39] You comb through the Scriptures thinking they'll give you eternal life. Those Scriptures are about me —

[40] and you refuse to come to me to have life.

[41] I don't need applause from people.

[42] But I know you. The love of God isn't in you.

[43] I came in my Father's name, and you won't receive me. Someone else will come in his own name — him you'll welcome with open arms.

[44] How could you believe? You feed off each other's approval and have no appetite for the glory that comes from the only God.

[45] Don't think I'll be the one accusing you to the Father. Your accuser is Moses — the very one you've staked your hope on.

[46] If you actually believed Moses, you'd believe me — he wrote about me.

[47] But if you don't believe what he wrote, how will you ever believe what I say?"

[1] After this, Jesus crossed to the far side of the Sea of Galilee — the lake of Tiberias. [2] A massive crowd trailed after him because they had seen what he was doing for the sick, the healings rolling out one after another. [3] He climbed the hillside and sat down there with his disciples. [4] Passover, the Jewish feast, was close at hand.

[5] Jesus looked up and saw the crowd surging toward him. He turned to Philip: "Where are we going to buy bread to feed all these people?" [6] He said it to test him. He already knew what he was going to do. [7] Philip answered, "Eight months' wages wouldn't buy enough bread to give each person even a bite." [8] Andrew — Simon Peter's brother — spoke up: [9] "There's a boy here with five barley loaves and two small fish. But what's that against a crowd like this?"

[10] "Get the people to sit down," Jesus said. The grass was thick there, and they settled in — about five thousand men. [11] Jesus took the loaves, gave thanks, and started distributing them to everyone seated — as much as they wanted — and the fish the same way. [12] When they had eaten their fill, he told the disciples, "Gather up what's left. Waste nothing." [13] They collected twelve baskets of fragments from those five barley loaves — leftovers from what the crowd had eaten.

[14] When the people saw the sign he had performed, they started saying, "This has to be the Prophet — the one who was supposed to come into the world." [15] Jesus knew they were about to seize him and make him king by force, so he slipped away again, alone, up into the mountain.

[16] Evening came, and the disciples went down to the lake. [17] They got into a boat and started across toward Capernaum. It was already dark, and Jesus still hadn't joined them. [18] A heavy wind kicked up, and the water turned rough. [19] They had rowed three or four miles when they saw Jesus walking on the water, coming toward the boat — and they were terrified. [20] "It's me," he said. "Don't be afraid." [21] They wanted to take him into the boat, and immediately the boat was at the shore where they were headed.

[22] The next day the crowd that had stayed on the other side of the lake realized something: only one boat had been there, and Jesus hadn't gotten into it with his disciples — they had left without him. [23] But other boats from Tiberias had pulled in near the place where they'd eaten the bread after the Lord gave thanks. [24] When the crowd saw that Jesus wasn't there, and neither were his disciples, they piled into the boats and headed for Capernaum to find him.

[25] They found him on the other side of the lake. "Rabbi," they asked, "when did you get here?"

[26] Jesus answered, "I'll tell you the truth — you're not looking for me because you saw signs. You're looking for me because you ate the bread and got full. [27] Stop working for food that spoils. Work for food that lasts — food that carries you into eternal life. The Son of Man will give you that food. God the Father has put his seal on him."

[28] "What do we have to do," they asked, "to perform the works God requires?"

[29] Jesus answered, "This is the work of God: trust the one he sent."

[30] "Then what sign are you going to give us so we can see it and believe you?" they pressed. "What will you do? [31] Our ancestors ate manna in the wilderness. As Scripture says, 'He gave them bread from heaven to eat.'"

[32] Jesus said, "Truth: it wasn't Moses who gave you bread from heaven. It's my Father who gives you the real bread from heaven. [33] The bread of God is the one who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world."

[34] "Sir," they said, "give us this bread — always."

[35] Jesus said: "I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry. Whoever trusts me will never thirst. [36] But I told you — you've seen me, and still you don't believe. [37] Everyone the Father gives me will come to me, and whoever comes to me I will never throw out. [38] I came down from heaven not to do what I want, but to do what the one who sent me wants. [39] And here is his will: that I lose none of those he has given me — that I raise them all on the last day. [40] This is what my Father wants: that everyone who looks at the Son and trusts him has eternal life, and I will raise them up on the last day."

[41] The Jewish leaders started grumbling about him because he'd said, "I am the bread that came down from heaven." [42] "Isn't this Jesus, Joseph's son?" they said. "We know his father and mother. How can he say, 'I came down from heaven'?"

[43] "Stop muttering among yourselves," Jesus said. [44] "No one can come to me unless the Father who sent me draws them. And I will raise them up on the last day. [45] The prophets wrote: 'They will all be taught by God.' Everyone who has listened to the Father and learned from him comes to me. [46] Not that anyone has seen the Father — only the one who is from God has seen him. [47] I'm telling you the truth: whoever believes has eternal life. [48] I am the bread of life. [49] Your ancestors ate the manna in the wilderness, and they died. [50] This is the bread that comes down from heaven so that anyone who eats it won't die. [51] I am the living bread that came down from heaven. Anyone who eats this bread will live forever. The bread I will give is my flesh — for the life of the world."

[52] The leaders started arguing fiercely among themselves: "How can this man give us his flesh to eat?"

[53] Jesus said: "I'm telling you the truth — unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, there is no life in you. [54] Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise them up on the last day. [55] My flesh is real food. My blood is real drink. [56] Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood lives in me, and I live in them. [57] Just as the living Father sent me and I live because of the Father, so the one who feeds on me will live because of me. [58] This is the bread that came down from heaven — not like the bread your ancestors ate before dying. Whoever feeds on this bread will live forever."

[59] He said this while teaching in the synagogue at Capernaum.

[60] When many of his disciples heard it, they said, "This is brutal. Who can listen to this?"

[61] Jesus knew his disciples were grumbling about it. "Is this what trips you up?" he asked. [62] "Then what about when you see the Son of Man ascending to where he was before? [63] The Spirit gives life. The flesh counts for nothing. The words I've spoken to you — they are spirit, and they are life. [64] But some of you don't believe." Jesus had known from the start which ones wouldn't believe and which one would betray him. [65] He went on: "This is why I told you no one can come to me unless the Father grants it."

[66] From that moment, many of his disciples turned back and stopped following him.

[67] "You don't want to leave too, do you?" Jesus asked the Twelve.

[68] Simon Peter answered, "Lord, where would we go? You have the words of eternal life. [69] We have come to believe — we know — you are the Holy One of God."

[70] Jesus answered, "Didn't I choose you, the Twelve? And one of you is a devil." [71] He meant Judas, son of Simon Iscariot — one of the Twelve, the one who would hand him over.

[1] After this, Jesus moved through Galilee. He refused to set foot in Judea—the authorities there wanted him dead.

[2] The Festival of Shelters was approaching,

[3] and his brothers pushed him: "Get out of here. Go to Judea. Your followers there need to see what you're doing.

[4] Nobody who wants to be known operates in the shadows. If you're really doing these things, show yourself to the world."

[5] Even his own brothers didn't believe in him.

[6] Jesus said, "My moment hasn't arrived. But for you, any moment works.

[7] The world has no reason to hate you. It hates me because I expose what it does—and what it does is evil.

[8] You go up to the festival. I'm not going—not yet. My time isn't ripe."

[9] He said this and stayed in Galilee.

[10] But once his brothers had left for the festival, he went too—quietly, hidden, no announcement.

[11] At the festival, the authorities were already hunting for him. "Where is he?" they kept asking.

[12] The crowds were buzzing. Some said, "He's a good man." Others: "No—he's deceiving the people."

[13] But nobody spoke openly. Fear of the authorities saw to that.

[14] Halfway through the festival, Jesus walked into the temple and began to teach.

[15] The authorities were stunned. "How does this man know so much? He's never studied."

[16] Jesus answered, "What I teach isn't mine. It comes from the one who sent me.

[17] Anyone willing to do God's will can tell whether this teaching comes from God or whether I'm just spinning my own ideas.

[18] A man pushing his own message wants his own glory. But someone seeking the glory of the one who sent him—he tells the truth. There's nothing crooked in him.

[19] Moses gave you the law. None of you keeps it. So why are you trying to kill me?"

[20] The crowd answered, "You're out of your mind. Who's trying to kill you?"

[21] Jesus said, "I did one work, and you're all reeling.

[22] Think about it: Moses gave you circumcision—not that it came from him, but from the patriarchs—and you'll circumcise a boy on the Sabbath.

[23] If a boy can be circumcised on the Sabbath without breaking Moses' law, why are you furious that I healed an entire human being on the Sabbath?

[24] Stop judging by appearances. Judge with honesty."

[25] Some of the Jerusalemites started saying, "Isn't this the man they're trying to kill?

[26] Yet here he is, speaking in the open, and they say nothing. Could it be that the leaders have finally figured out he's the Messiah?

[27] But no—we know where this man is from. When the Messiah comes, no one will know his origin."

[28] Then Jesus, still teaching in the temple, raised his voice: "So you know me. You know where I'm from. But I haven't come on my own. The one who sent me is real—and you don't know him.

[29] I know him. I came from him. He sent me."

[30] They tried to arrest him. But no one laid a hand on him. His hour had not yet come.

[31] Many in the crowd believed in him. "When the Messiah arrives," they said, "will he do more signs than this man has done?"

[32] The Pharisees caught wind of the whispering. They and the chief priests sent temple guards to arrest him.

[33] Jesus said, "I'm with you a little longer. Then I go back to the one who sent me.

[34] You'll look for me—and you won't find me. Where I am going, you cannot come."

[35] The authorities muttered among themselves: "Where does he plan to go that we can't find him? Is he heading to the Greek diaspora to teach Gentiles?

[36] What did he mean—'You'll look for me and not find me,' and 'Where I am, you cannot come'?"

[37] On the festival's last and greatest day, Jesus stood and cried out: "Anyone thirsty—come to me. Drink.

[38] Whoever believes in me—as Scripture says—rivers of living water will pour out from deep inside them."

[39] He meant the Spirit, which those who believed in him were about to receive. The Spirit hadn't yet been given, because Jesus hadn't yet been glorified.

[40] Some in the crowd, hearing this, said, "This really is the Prophet."

[41] Others: "He's the Messiah." But others pushed back: "The Messiah doesn't come from Galilee.

[42] Scripture says the Messiah comes from David's line, from Bethlehem, David's village."

[43] So the crowd split over him.

[44] Some wanted to seize him. But no one touched him.

[45] The guards came back to the chief priests and Pharisees empty-handed. "Why didn't you bring him in?" they demanded.

[46] The guards answered, "No one has ever spoken like this man."

[47] The Pharisees snapped, "Don't tell us you've been taken in too.

[48] Has a single ruler believed in him? A single Pharisee?

[49] This rabble—this crowd that knows nothing of the law—they're cursed."

[50] Then Nicodemus—the one who had come to Jesus earlier, one of their own—spoke up:

[51] "Does our law condemn a man without first hearing him out, finding out what he's done?"

[52] They turned on him: "Are you from Galilee too? Look into it. No prophet comes out of Galilee."

[53] And they all went home.

[1] Jesus went to the Mount of Olives.

[2] At dawn he was back in the temple courts. The whole crowd gathered around him, so he sat down and began to teach.

[3] The scribes and Pharisees dragged in a woman caught in adultery. They stood her in front of everyone

[4] and said, "Teacher, this woman was caught in the act of adultery.

[5] Moses' law commands us to stone women like this. What do you say?"

[6] They were baiting him, hunting for something to charge him with. Jesus bent down and began writing in the dirt with his finger.

[7] When they kept pressing him, he straightened up and said, "Whoever among you has never sinned — let him throw the first stone."

[8] Then he bent down again and kept writing in the dirt.

[9] One by one they walked away, the oldest first, until Jesus was left alone with the woman still standing there.

[10] He stood up and said, "Woman — where are they? Did no one condemn you?"

[11] "No one, Lord," she said. "Then neither do I condemn you," Jesus said. "Go. From now on, stop sinning."

[12] Jesus spoke to them again: "I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness — they will carry the light of life."

[13] The Pharisees shot back, "You're testifying about yourself. Your testimony doesn't count."

[14] Jesus answered, "Even when I testify about myself, my testimony holds — because I know where I came from and where I'm going. You don't know either one.

[15] You judge by surface appearances. I judge no one.

[16] But even if I do judge, my verdict is true, because I'm not alone in it — the Father who sent me stands with me.

[17] Your own law says the testimony of two witnesses is valid.

[18] I am one witness to myself, and the Father who sent me is the other."

[19] "Where is this father of yours?" they demanded. Jesus answered, "You don't know me, and you don't know my Father. If you knew me, you would know him too."

[20] He said all this near the treasury as he was teaching in the temple. No one arrested him — his hour had not yet come.

[21] He told them again, "I'm leaving. You'll search for me, and you'll die in your sin. Where I'm going, you cannot come."

[22] The Jewish leaders muttered, "Is he going to kill himself? Is that what he means by 'Where I'm going, you cannot come'?"

[23] He said to them, "You're from below. I'm from above. You belong to this world. I do not.

[24] That's why I told you you'll die in your sins. Unless you believe that I AM — you will die in your sins."

[25] "Who are you, then?" they asked. Jesus answered, "Exactly what I've been telling you from the start.

[26] I have much to say about you, much to judge. But the One who sent me speaks truth, and what I've heard from him is what I tell the world."

[27] They didn't understand he was talking about the Father.

[28] So Jesus said, "When you lift up the Son of Man, then you'll know that I AM, and that I do nothing on my own — I speak exactly what the Father taught me.

[29] The One who sent me is with me. He hasn't left me alone, because I always do what pleases him."

[30] As he spoke these things, many believed in him.

[31] Jesus said to the Jews who had believed him, "If you hold to my word, you really are my disciples.

[32] You will know the truth, and the truth will set you free."

[33] They answered, "We're Abraham's children. We've never been slaves to anyone. How can you say we'll be 'set free'?"

[34] Jesus replied, "I'm telling you the truth — everyone who sins is a slave to sin.

[35] A slave doesn't stay in the house forever. A son does.

[36] So if the Son sets you free, you'll be free for real.

[37] I know you're Abraham's descendants. But you're trying to kill me, because my word has no room in you.

[38] I speak what I've seen with my Father. You do what you've heard from yours."

[39] "Abraham is our father," they answered. Jesus said, "If you were Abraham's children, you'd act like Abraham.

[40] Instead, you're trying to kill me — a man who told you the truth I heard from God. Abraham never did anything like that.

[41] You're doing the works of your real father." They said, "We're not illegitimate. We have one Father — God himself."

[42] Jesus told them, "If God were your Father, you'd love me, because I came from God and I'm here now. I didn't come on my own — he sent me.

[43] Why can't you understand what I'm saying? Because you can't bear to hear my word.

[44] Your father is the devil, and you want to do what your father wants. He was a murderer from the start. He never stood in the truth, because there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks his native tongue — he is a liar and the father of lies.

[45] But because I tell the truth, you don't believe me.

[46] Which one of you can convict me of sin? If I'm telling the truth, why don't you believe me?

[47] Whoever belongs to God hears what God says. You don't hear, because you don't belong to God."

[48] The Jews answered, "Aren't we right to say you're a Samaritan and you're demon-possessed?"

[49] Jesus said, "I'm not demon-possessed. I honor my Father — and you dishonor me.

[50] I'm not chasing my own glory. There is One who seeks it and judges.

[51] I'm telling you the truth — whoever keeps my word will never see death."

[52] The Jews said, "Now we know you've got a demon. Abraham died. The prophets died. And you say, 'Whoever keeps my word will never taste death.'

[53] Are you greater than our father Abraham? He died. The prophets died. Who do you think you are?"

[54] Jesus answered, "If I glorify myself, my glory is nothing. The One who glorifies me is my Father — the One you call your God.

[55] You don't know him. I do. If I said I didn't, I'd be a liar like you. But I do know him, and I keep his word.

[56] Your father Abraham rejoiced at the thought of seeing my day. He saw it, and he was glad."

[57] The Jews said, "You're not even fifty, and you've seen Abraham?"

[58] Jesus told them, "I'm telling you the truth — before Abraham was, I AM."

[59] At that, they picked up stones to throw at him. But Jesus slipped away and walked out of the temple.

[1] Walking along, Jesus saw a man — blind from the day he was born. [2] His disciples asked, "Rabbi, whose sin caused this? His own, or his parents'?"

[3] "Neither," Jesus said. "This isn't about sin. This is so God's work can show up in him. [4] While daylight lasts, we have to do the work of the One who sent me. Night is coming, and then no one can work. [5] As long as I'm in the world, I am the world's light."

[6] He spat on the ground, mixed mud with the saliva, and smeared it across the man's eyes. [7] "Go," he said. "Wash in the Pool of Siloam." (Siloam means *Sent*.) The man went. He washed. He came back seeing.

[8] His neighbors — and everyone who'd watched him beg for years — started asking, "Isn't this the guy who used to sit and beg?" [9] Some said yes. Others said, "No, just someone who looks like him." He kept insisting, "It's me."

[10] "Then how?" they pressed. "How did your eyes open?"

[11] He said, "The man called Jesus made mud, put it on my eyes, and told me to go wash at Siloam. I went. I washed. I could see."

[12] "Where is he?" they asked.

"I don't know."

[13] They hauled the formerly blind man to the Pharisees. [14] It was a Sabbath, the day Jesus had made the mud and opened his eyes. [15] So the Pharisees ran their own interrogation: How did you receive your sight?

He told them: "He put mud on my eyes. I washed. I see."

[16] Some of the Pharisees said, "This man isn't from God — he doesn't keep the Sabbath." Others countered, "How could a sinner do signs like this?" They were split down the middle.

[17] So they turned back to the blind man: "What do *you* say about him? They're your eyes."

"He's a prophet," he said.

[18] The Jewish authorities refused to believe he'd actually been blind and now could see — until they brought in his parents. [19] "Is this your son? The one you say was born blind? Then how is he seeing now?"

[20] His parents answered, "Yes, he's our son. Yes, he was born blind. [21] How he sees now, we don't know. Who opened his eyes, we don't know. Ask him. He's an adult. He can speak for himself." [22] They said this because they were terrified of the authorities, who had already decided that anyone confessing Jesus as Messiah would be thrown out of the synagogue. [23] That's why they said, "He's old enough — ask him."

[24] So a second time they called the man in. "Give glory to God," they said. "We know this man is a sinner."

[25] He answered, "Whether he's a sinner, I have no idea. One thing I know: I was blind. Now I see."

[26] "What did he do to you?" they pushed. "How did he open your eyes?"

[27] "I already told you," he said, "and you didn't listen. Why do you want to hear it again? You don't want to become his disciples too, do you?"

[28] They erupted on him: "*You're* his disciple. We are disciples of Moses. [29] We know God spoke to Moses. This one? We don't even know where he comes from."

[30] The man shot back, "Now *that* is remarkable. You don't know where he comes from — and he opened my eyes. [31] We all know God doesn't listen to sinners. He listens to the person who honors him and does his will. [32] In all of history, no one has ever opened the eyes of someone born blind. [33] If this man weren't from God, he couldn't do a thing."

[34] "You were born drowning in sin," they snarled, "and you're going to lecture *us*?" And they threw him out.

[35] Jesus heard they'd thrown him out. He found him and asked, "Do you believe in the Son of Man?"

[36] "Who is he, sir? Tell me, so I can believe in him."

[37] "You've seen him," Jesus said. "He's the one speaking to you right now."

[38] "Lord, I believe." And he dropped to his knees and worshiped him.

[39] Then Jesus said, "I came into this world for judgment — so the blind would see, and those who think they see would go blind."

[40] Some Pharisees standing nearby heard this. "Are you saying *we're* blind?"

[41] Jesus said, "If you were blind, you'd be innocent. But you keep insisting, 'We see' — so your guilt remains."

[1] Listen carefully. Anyone who refuses the gate and climbs the fence into the sheep pen — that one is a thief. A bandit. [2] The shepherd comes through the gate. [3] The gatekeeper opens for him, the sheep recognize his voice, and he calls his own by name and leads them out. [4] Once he has brought them all out, he walks ahead of them, and they follow — because they know his voice. [5] They will never follow a stranger. They scatter from him, because his voice is foreign to them.

[6] Jesus told them this, but they had no idea what he was saying.

[7] So he said it again, plainer this time: I am telling you the truth — I am the gate for the sheep. [8] Every one who came before me was a thief, a bandit — and the sheep refused to listen. [9] I am the gate. Anyone who comes in through me will be saved — coming in, going out, finding pasture. [10] The thief shows up for one reason: to steal, to slaughter, to destroy. I came so they could have life — life overflowing.

[11] I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays his life down for the sheep. [12] The hired hand is no shepherd. The sheep are not his. When the wolf appears, he abandons them and runs — and the wolf tears into the flock and scatters them. [13] He runs because he is hired help. The sheep mean nothing to him.

[14] I am the good shepherd. I know mine, and mine know me — [15] just as the Father knows me and I know the Father — and I lay down my life for the sheep. [16] I have other sheep that are not from this pen. I must bring them in too. They will hear my voice. One flock. One shepherd.

[17] This is why the Father loves me — because I lay down my life only to take it up again. [18] No one takes it from me. I give it up of my own accord. I have the authority to lay it down and the authority to take it back. This is the command I received from my Father.

[19] His words split the crowd again. [20] Many were saying, He has a demon. He is out of his mind. Why even listen? [21] Others said, These are not the words of a man possessed. Can a demon open the eyes of the blind?

[22] Then came Hanukkah in Jerusalem. It was winter. [23] Jesus was walking inside the temple, in the colonnade of Solomon, [24] when the Judeans surrounded him and demanded: How long are you going to keep us in suspense? If you are the Messiah, say it plainly.

[25] Jesus answered: I told you, and you do not believe. The works I do in my Father's name — they testify about me. [26] But you do not believe, because you are not among my sheep. [27] My sheep hear my voice. I know them. They follow me. [28] I give them eternal life. They will never perish. No one will tear them out of my hand. [29] My Father, who gave them to me, is greater than all — and no one can tear anything out of the Father's hand. [30] The Father and I are one.

[31] They reached for stones again — ready to kill him.

[32] Jesus said: I have shown you many good works from the Father. For which of them do you intend to stone me?

[33] We are not stoning you for a good work, they answered. We are stoning you for blasphemy. You — a man — make yourself out to be God.

[34] Jesus answered: Is it not written in your own Law, *I said, you are gods*? [35] If he called them gods — those to whom the word of God came — and Scripture cannot be broken — [36] how can you accuse the one the Father set apart and sent into the world of blasphemy for saying, I am God's Son? [37] If I am not doing my Father's works, then do not believe me. [38] But if I am — even if you refuse to believe me, believe the works. Then you will know, you will understand: the Father is in me, and I am in the Father.

[39] Once more they tried to seize him. He slipped through their hands.

[40] He went back across the Jordan, to the place where John had first been baptizing, and stayed there. [41] Crowds came to him. They were saying: John never performed a sign — but everything John said about this man was true. [42] And there, many believed in him.

[1] A man was sick — Lazarus of Bethany, the village where Mary and her sister Martha lived. [2] (This was the same Mary who would later pour perfume on the Lord and wipe his feet with her hair. Lazarus, the sick man, was her brother.) [3] The sisters sent word to Jesus: "Lord, the one you love is sick."

[4] When Jesus heard it, he said, "This sickness is not headed toward death. It's for God's glory — so that the Son of God will be glorified through it."

[5] Jesus loved Martha and her sister and Lazarus. [6] So when he heard Lazarus was sick — he stayed where he was. Two more days.

[7] Then he said to the disciples, "Let's go back to Judea."

[8] "Rabbi," they said, "the Jews there were just trying to stone you. And you want to go back?"

[9] Jesus answered, "Aren't there twelve hours of daylight? A man who walks in the day doesn't stumble — he sees by the light of this world. [10] But the man who walks at night stumbles, because the light isn't in him."

[11] Then he added, "Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep. I'm going to wake him."

[12] The disciples said, "Lord, if he's sleeping, he'll be fine."

[13] Jesus had been speaking of his death. They thought he meant ordinary sleep. [14] So he told them plainly: "Lazarus is dead. [15] And for your sake I'm glad I wasn't there — so you can believe. Let's go to him."

[16] Then Thomas — called the Twin — said to the others, "Let's go too. We can die with him."

[17] When Jesus arrived, Lazarus had already been in the tomb four days. [18] Bethany was close to Jerusalem — less than two miles — [19] and many of the Jews had come to console Martha and Mary over their brother.

[20] When Martha heard Jesus was coming, she went out to meet him. Mary stayed home.

[21] Martha said to Jesus, "Lord — if you had been here, my brother would not have died. [22] But even now, I know: whatever you ask of God, God will give you."

[23] Jesus said, "Your brother will rise."

[24] "I know," Martha said. "He'll rise — at the resurrection, on the last day."

[25] Jesus said to her, "I am the resurrection and the life. The one who believes in me will live, even if he dies. [26] And whoever lives and believes in me will never die — never. Do you believe this?"

[27] "Yes, Lord," she said. "I believe you are the Christ, the Son of God — the one coming into the world."

[28] Then she went and called her sister Mary aside. "The Teacher is here," she whispered. "He's asking for you."

[29] The moment Mary heard it, she got up and ran to him. [30] Jesus had not yet entered the village — he was still where Martha had met him. [31] The Jews in the house, the ones comforting Mary, saw her leap up and rush out. They followed, assuming she was going to the tomb to weep there.

[32] When Mary reached Jesus and saw him, she dropped at his feet. "Lord — if you had been here, my brother would not have died."

[33] Jesus saw her weeping. He saw the Jews who came with her weeping. And something inside him broke open — a deep, shuddering grief, a fury at death itself. [34] "Where have you laid him?" he asked.

"Lord, come and see."

[35] Jesus wept.

[36] The Jews said, "Look how he loved him."

[37] But some of them said, "This man opened the eyes of the blind. Couldn't he have kept this one from dying?"

[38] Jesus, the storm still raging in him, came to the tomb. It was a cave with a stone laid across the entrance.

[39] "Take away the stone," he said.

Martha — the dead man's sister — said, "Lord, by now the smell. He's been in there four days."

[40] Jesus said, "Didn't I tell you that if you believed, you would see the glory of God?"

[41] So they moved the stone. Jesus looked up and said, "Father, thank you. You've heard me. [42] I know you always hear me — but I'm saying it now for the sake of the crowd, so they'll believe you sent me."

[43] Then he shouted — voice ringing off the rock — "Lazarus! Come out!"

[44] And the dead man came out. Wrapped hand and foot in burial cloths. Face still bound in the cloth they'd tied around it.

Jesus said, "Unbind him. Let him go."

[45] Many of the Jews who had come with Mary — who saw what Jesus did — believed in him. [46] But some of them went straight to the Pharisees and reported it.

[47] So the chief priests and Pharisees convened the Council. "What are we doing?" they said. "This man is performing one sign after another. [48] If we let him go on, everyone will believe in him — and the Romans will come and take away both our place and our nation."

[49] One of them, Caiaphas, high priest that year, said, "You know nothing. [50] You don't grasp that it's better for one man to die for the people than for the whole nation to be destroyed."

[51] He did not say this on his own. As high priest that year, he prophesied — Jesus was about to die for the nation. [52] And not for the nation only, but to gather into one the scattered children of God.

[53] From that day on, they plotted to kill him.

[54] So Jesus no longer moved openly among the Jews. He withdrew to a region near the wilderness — to a town called Ephraim — and stayed there with the disciples.

[55] The Passover of the Jews was near, and many went up from the countryside to Jerusalem before the festival, to purify themselves. [56] They kept looking for Jesus, asking each other in the temple courts, "What do you think? He won't come to the festival, will he?"

[57] The chief priests and Pharisees had given the order: anyone who knew where he was had to report it — so they could arrest him.

[1] Six days out from Passover, Jesus came to Bethany — home of Lazarus, the one he'd raised from the dead.

[2] They put on a dinner for him. Martha served. Lazarus was at the table with him.

[3] Then Mary took a pound of pure nard — staggeringly expensive — and poured it over Jesus' feet, then wiped them with her hair. The whole house filled with the scent of it.

[4] Judas Iscariot — one of the disciples, the one who would betray him — spoke up:

[5] "Why wasn't this perfume sold? Three hundred denarii. Given to the poor."

[6] He didn't say this because he cared about the poor. He was a thief. He kept the money bag and skimmed from it.

[7] Jesus said, "Leave her alone. She's been keeping this for the day of my burial.

[8] The poor will always be with you. I won't."

[9] Word got out that Jesus was there, and a huge crowd of Jews came — not just for him but to see Lazarus, the one he'd brought back from the dead.

[10] So the chief priests started plotting to kill Lazarus too,

[11] because too many Jews were defecting on account of him, putting their faith in Jesus.

[12] The next day, the massive Passover crowd heard Jesus was coming into Jerusalem.

[13] They grabbed palm branches and went out to meet him, shouting: "Hosanna! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord — the King of Israel!"

[14] Jesus found a young donkey and rode in on it — exactly as it's written:

[15] "Don't be afraid, daughter of Zion. Look — your King is coming, riding on a donkey's colt."

[16] His disciples didn't catch any of this at the time. But later, after Jesus was glorified, they remembered: this had all been written about him, and they had been living it out.

[17] The crowd that had been there when he called Lazarus out of the tomb kept telling the story.

[18] That's why the crowd came out to meet him — they'd heard about the sign.

[19] The Pharisees said to each other, "We're getting nowhere. Look — the whole world has run off after him."

[20] Among the pilgrims who'd come up to worship at the feast were some Greeks.

[21] They approached Philip — the one from Bethsaida in Galilee — with a request: "Sir, we'd like to see Jesus."

[22] Philip went to Andrew. Andrew and Philip went to Jesus.

[23] Jesus answered them: "The hour has come. The Son of Man will be glorified.

[24] Listen to me: unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it stays a single grain. But if it dies — it multiplies.

[25] Cling to your life and you'll lose it. Hate your life in this world and you'll keep it — forever.

[26] Anyone who serves me has to follow me. Wherever I am, my servant will be there too. Whoever serves me — the Father will honor."

[27] "My soul is in turmoil right now. What should I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'? No — this is exactly why I came to this hour.

[28] Father, glorify your name." A voice came out of heaven: "I have glorified it. I will glorify it again."

[29] The crowd standing there heard it and said it was thunder. Others said an angel had spoken to him.

[30] Jesus said, "That voice came for your sake, not mine.

[31] Now is the judgment of this world. Now the ruler of this world gets thrown out.

[32] And I — when I'm lifted up from the earth — I will pull everyone toward myself."

[33] He said this to signal what kind of death he was going to die.

[34] The crowd pushed back: "We've heard from the Law that the Messiah stays forever. So how can you say the Son of Man has to be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man?"

[35] Jesus told them, "The light is with you a little while longer. Walk while you have the light, before the darkness catches up to you. The one walking in darkness doesn't know where he's going.

[36] While you have the light, trust the light — become children of light." After saying this, Jesus left and hid himself from them.

[37] Even after he'd done all these signs right in front of them, they wouldn't believe in him —

[38] fulfilling what Isaiah the prophet had said: "Lord, who has believed what we reported? To whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?"

[39] They couldn't believe. Isaiah had also said:

[40] "He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts — so they wouldn't see with their eyes, or understand with their hearts, or turn — and I would heal them."

[41] Isaiah said this because he saw his glory. He was speaking about him.

[42] Still — even among the rulers, many believed in him. But because of the Pharisees, they wouldn't say so out loud, afraid of being thrown out of the synagogue.

[43] They loved the praise of people more than the praise of God.

[44] Jesus cried out: "Whoever believes in me isn't believing in me — but in the one who sent me.

[45] Whoever sees me sees the one who sent me.

[46] I came as light into the world, so that no one who trusts me would stay in the dark.

[47] If anyone hears my words and doesn't keep them, I don't judge them. I didn't come to judge the world. I came to save it.

[48] Whoever rejects me and refuses my words has a judge already: the word I've spoken — that's what will judge them on the last day.

[49] Because I haven't spoken on my own. The Father who sent me — he gave me the command: what to say, how to say it.

[50] And I know his command is eternal life. So whatever I say — I say it exactly as the Father told me."

[1] Passover was about to begin. Jesus knew his hour had come — time to leave this world and go to the Father. He had loved his own in this world, and now he would love them to the end, all the way through.

Jesus said, "If I don't wash you, you have no part with me."

[9] "Then Lord," Peter said, "not just my feet — my hands and my head too!"

[10] Jesus said, "Someone who's bathed only needs his feet washed — he's clean all over. And you are clean. Though not all of you."

[11] He knew who would betray him. That's why he said, "Not all of you."

[14] So if I — the Lord, the Teacher — washed your feet, you owe each other the same.

[15] I gave you a pattern. Do for each other what I did for you.

[16] I'm telling you the truth: a servant isn't greater than his master. A messenger isn't greater than the one who sent him.

[17] Now you know this. You'll be blessed if you actually do it.

[18] I'm not talking about all of you. I know the ones I chose. But the Scripture has to be fulfilled: *The one who shared my bread has lifted his heel against me.*

[19] I'm telling you now, before it happens, so that when it does, you'll believe I am who I am.

[20] The truth is this: whoever welcomes someone I send welcomes me. And whoever welcomes me welcomes the One who sent me."

[21] Saying this, Jesus was shaken to the core. He spoke it plainly: "I'm telling you the truth — one of you is going to betray me."

[22] The disciples looked at each other, bewildered. Which one?

[23] One of them — the one Jesus loved — was leaning back against Jesus.

[24] Simon Peter motioned to him: *Ask him. Who does he mean?*

[26] Jesus said, "The one I hand this piece of bread to, after I dip it." He dipped the bread and handed it to Judas, son of Simon Iscariot.

[27] The moment Judas took it, Satan entered him.

"Whatever you're going to do," Jesus said, "do it fast."

[28] No one at the table understood why he said this.

[29] Some thought, since Judas kept the money bag, that Jesus was telling him to buy what they needed for the feast, or to give something to the poor.

[30] Judas took the bread and went out immediately. It was night.

[31] When he was gone, Jesus said: "Now the Son of Man is glorified. And God is glorified in him.

[32] If God is glorified in him, God will glorify him in himself — and will do it now.

[34] A new command: love each other. The way I have loved you — love each other like that.

[35] This is how everyone will know you're mine. By the love between you."

[36] Simon Peter said, "Lord, where are you going?"

Jesus answered, "Where I'm going, you can't follow me now. But you will follow later."

[37] "Lord, why can't I follow you now?" Peter asked. "I'd lay down my life for you."

[38] Jesus said, "You'd lay down your life for me? I'm telling you the truth — before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times."

[1] Don't let your hearts come undone. You trust God — trust me too.

[2] My Father's house has plenty of room. If it didn't, would I be telling you I'm going there to prepare a place for you?

[3] And once I've gone and gotten everything ready, I'm coming back. I'll bring you to myself, so that wherever I am, you'll be there with me.

[8] Philip said, "Lord, just show us the Father. That's all we need."

[9] Jesus said, "Philip — after all this time together, you still don't see me? Whoever has seen me has seen the Father. So how can you say, 'Show us the Father'?

[10] Don't you believe I am in the Father and the Father is in me? The words I speak aren't mine alone. The Father, living in me, is the one doing the work.

[11] Take me at my word: I am in the Father, and the Father is in me. Or if you can't, at least believe because of the works themselves.

[12] Here is the truth: whoever trusts me will do the same works I've been doing — and even greater ones, because I am going to the Father.

[13] Whatever you ask in my name, I will do — so that the Father is glorified in the Son.

[14] Ask me anything in my name, and I will do it.

[15] If you love me, you'll keep my commands.

[16] And I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate to be with you forever —

[18] I won't leave you orphaned. I'm coming to you.

[19] In a little while the world won't see me anymore. But you will see me. Because I live, you also will live.

[20] On that day you'll understand: I am in my Father, you are in me, and I am in you.

[21] Whoever holds my commands and keeps them — that's the one who loves me. And whoever loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love them, and I will show myself to them."

[22] Judas — not Iscariot — asked, "Lord, why are you about to reveal yourself to us and not to the world?"

[23] Jesus answered, "If anyone loves me, they'll keep my word. My Father will love them, and we will come to them and make our home with them.

[24] Whoever doesn't love me won't keep my words. And the word you're hearing isn't mine — it belongs to the Father who sent me.

[25] I'm telling you all this while I'm still with you.

[26] But the Advocate — the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name — he will teach you everything and bring back to your mind every word I've spoken to you.

[29] I'm telling you now, before it happens, so that when it does, you'll believe.

[30] I won't be talking with you much longer. The ruler of this world is coming. He has no claim on me —

[31] but the world has to see that I love the Father and that I do exactly what the Father commands. Get up. Let's go from here."

[1] I am the true vine. My Father is the gardener.

[2] Every branch in me that produces nothing, he cuts away. Every branch that bears fruit, he prunes — so it can bear more.

[3] You are already clean because of the word I have spoken to you.

[4] Stay in me, and I will stay in you. A branch cannot bear fruit on its own — it has to remain connected to the vine. Neither can you, unless you remain in me.

[5] I am the vine. You are the branches. The one who stays in me, and I in him, bears fruit in abundance. Apart from me, you can do nothing.

[6] Anyone who does not stay in me gets thrown out like a severed branch — and withers. Branches like that get gathered up, tossed into the fire, burned.

[8] This is how my Father is glorified: that you bear fruit in abundance and prove to be my disciples.

[10] If you keep my commands, you will remain in my love — just as I have kept my Father's commands and remain in his love.

[11] I have told you this so that my joy will be in you, and your joy will be complete.

[12] This is my command: love each other the way I have loved you.

[13] No one has greater love than this — to lay down his life for his friends.

[14] You are my friends if you do what I command.

[15] I do not call you servants anymore. A servant has no idea what his master is doing. I have called you friends — because everything I heard from my Father, I have made known to you.

[17] This is what I command you: love each other.

[18] If the world hates you, remember — it hated me first.

[19] If you belonged to the world, the world would love you as its own. But you do not belong to it. I picked you out of it. That is why the world hates you.

[22] If I had not come and spoken to them, they would have no sin. Now they have no excuse for their sin.

[23] Whoever hates me hates my Father too.

[24] If I had not done among them the works no one else has ever done, they would have no sin. But now they have seen — and hated — both me and my Father.

[25] This fulfills what is written in their law: They hated me for no reason.

[26] When the Advocate comes — the one I will send to you from the Father, the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father — he will testify about me.
