[1] I'm telling you all this so you won't collapse when it comes. [2] They'll throw you out of the synagogues. In fact, the hour is coming when anyone who kills you will think he's performing a sacred duty for God. [3] They'll do it because they never knew the Father, and they never knew me. [4] I'm telling you now so that when the hour arrives, you'll remember I warned you.

I didn't say these things at the start because I was with you. [5] But now I'm going to the one who sent me — and not one of you asks where I'm going. [6] Instead, grief has flooded your hearts because of what I've said.

[7] But hear me: it's better for you that I leave. If I don't go, the Advocate won't come to you. If I go, I'll send him. [10] Righteousness — because I'm going to the Father and you won't see me anymore. [11] Judgment — because the ruler of this world has already been sentenced.

[12] There's so much more I want to tell you, but you can't carry it now. [13] When he comes — the Spirit of truth — he'll guide you into the whole truth. [1] Jesus said all this, then lifted his eyes to the sky and spoke: "Father, the hour is here. Glorify your Son so the Son can glorify you. I'm asking you to protect them from the evil one. [16] They don't belong here. Neither do I. [17] Set them apart by the truth. Your word is truth. [18] The way you sent me into the world — that's how I'm sending them. [19] And for their sake I set myself apart, so they too can be made holy in truth.

[20] I'm not just praying for these here. I'm praying for everyone who will believe in me because of what they say — [21] that all of them would be one. The way you, Father, are in me and I am in you — that they would be in us. So the world might finally believe you sent me. [22] The glory you gave me, I gave to them — so they could be one the way we are one. [23] Me in them, you in me — fused into complete oneness. So the world will know you sent me, and that you loved them the same way you loved me.

[24] Father — I want them with me. The ones you gave me. I want them where I am, so they can see my glory — the glory you gave me because you loved me before the world was even a thought. [25] Righteous Father — the world doesn't know you. But I do. And these here know that you sent me. [26] I made your name known to them, and I'll keep making it known — so the love you have for me would live inside them, and I would live in them too."

[1] When Jesus finished speaking, he crossed the Kidron ravine with his disciples. On the far side was a garden, and they went in.

[2] Judas — the betrayer — knew the place. Jesus had gone there often with his disciples.

[3] So Judas came. He brought a detachment of soldiers and a squad of temple police sent by the chief priests and Pharisees. Torches. Lanterns. Weapons.

[4] Jesus knew everything coming for him. He stepped forward and said, "Who is it you want?"

[5] "Jesus of Nazareth," they answered. "I am he," he said. Judas, the betrayer, was standing right there with them.

[6] The moment he said "I am he," they staggered back and fell to the ground.

[7] He asked them again: "Who is it you want?" "Jesus of Nazareth."

[8] "I told you — I am he. If it's me you want, let these others go."

[9] This was so the word he had spoken would hold: "I have not lost one of those you gave me."

[10] Then Simon Peter, who had a sword, drew it. He swung at the high priest's servant and sliced off his right ear. The servant's name was Malchus.

[11] Jesus said to Peter, "Put the sword away. The cup the Father has handed me — am I not going to drink it?"

[12] So the detachment, with its commander and the temple police, seized Jesus and bound him.

[13] They took him first to Annas — father-in-law of Caiaphas, who was high priest that year.

[14] Caiaphas was the one who had told the Jewish leaders it would be better for one man to die for the people.

[15] Simon Peter followed Jesus, along with another disciple. That disciple knew the high priest, so he went into the high priest's courtyard with Jesus,

[16] while Peter stood outside at the gate. The other disciple, the one the high priest knew, came back out, spoke to the girl watching the gate, and brought Peter in.

[17] The girl looked at Peter. "Aren't you one of this man's disciples?" "I am not," he said.

[18] It was cold. The servants and the police had built a charcoal fire and were standing around it warming themselves. Peter stood with them, warming his hands.

[19] Meanwhile the high priest questioned Jesus about his disciples and his teaching.

[20] Jesus answered, "I have spoken openly to the world. I taught in synagogues, in the temple, where every Jew gathers. I said nothing in secret.

[21] Why are you asking me? Ask the people who heard me. They know what I said."

[22] One of the officers standing there slapped him across the face. "Is that how you answer the high priest?"

[23] Jesus said, "If I spoke wrongly, name the wrong. If I spoke rightly — why hit me?"

[24] Annas sent him, still bound, to Caiaphas the high priest.

[25] Peter was still standing by the fire, warming himself. They asked him, "Aren't you one of his disciples?" He denied it. "I am not."

[26] One of the high priest's servants — a relative of the man whose ear Peter had cut off — said, "Didn't I see you in the garden with him?"

[27] Peter denied it again. And right then a rooster crowed.

[28] From Caiaphas they took Jesus to the governor's headquarters. It was early morning. They themselves did not enter the building — they didn't want to be defiled, since they wanted to eat the Passover.

[29] So Pilate came out to them. "What charge are you bringing against this man?"

[30] "If he weren't a criminal," they answered, "we wouldn't have handed him over to you."

[31] Pilate said, "Take him yourselves. Judge him by your own law." The Jewish leaders said, "We aren't allowed to execute anyone."

[32] This was so Jesus' words would hold — the words pointing to the kind of death he was going to die.

[33] Pilate went back inside, called for Jesus, and said, "Are you the king of the Jews?"

[34] Jesus answered, "Are you asking on your own, or did others tell you about me?"

[35] "Am I a Jew?" Pilate shot back. "Your own people, your own chief priests — they handed you over to me. What did you do?"

[36] Jesus said, "My kingdom is not from this world. If it were, my people would be fighting right now to keep me out of Jewish hands. But my kingdom is not from here."

[37] "So you are a king, then?" Pilate said. Jesus answered, "You said it — I am a king. This is why I was born. This is why I came into the world: to bear witness to the truth. Everyone who belongs to the truth listens to my voice."

[38] "Truth?" Pilate said. "What is that?" Then he walked back out to the Jews and told them, "I find no case against him.

[39] But you have a custom: I release one prisoner to you at Passover. Want me to release the king of the Jews?"

[40] They shouted back, "Not him! Barabbas!" Barabbas was a revolutionary.

[1] So Pilate took Jesus and had him flogged. [2] The soldiers wove a crown out of thorns and jammed it on his head. They threw a purple robe around him [3] and started parading up to him: "Hail, King of the Jews!" — and slapping him across the face.

[4] Pilate went out again. "Look — I'm bringing him out to you so you'll know I find no case against him." [5] Jesus stepped out wearing the thorn crown and the purple robe. Pilate said, "Here. Look at the man."

[6] The moment the chief priests and the temple guards laid eyes on him, they erupted: "Crucify! Crucify!" Pilate shot back, "You take him and crucify him. I find no case against him." [7] The Jewish leaders answered, "We have a law, and by that law he has to die — because he made himself out to be the Son of God."

[8] When Pilate heard that, his fear deepened. [9] He went back into the palace and pressed Jesus: "Where do you come from?" Jesus gave him nothing. [10] "You won't speak to me?" Pilate said. "Don't you realize I have the authority to release you and the authority to crucify you?" [11] Jesus answered, "You'd have no authority over me at all if it hadn't been handed down to you from above. That's why the one who delivered me to you carries the greater guilt."

[12] From that point on, Pilate kept trying to release him. But the Jewish leaders kept shouting, "Release this man and you're no friend of Caesar's! Anyone who claims to be a king sets himself against Caesar."

[13] When Pilate heard that, he brought Jesus out and took his seat on the judge's bench at the place called The Stone Pavement — Gabbatha in Aramaic. [14] It was the day of Preparation for Passover, around noon. He said to the Jews, "Here is your king." [15] They roared back, "Take him away! Take him away! Crucify him!" Pilate said, "Crucify your king?" The chief priests answered, "We have no king but Caesar."

[16] So he handed Jesus over to them to be crucified. They took him. [17] Carrying his own cross, he walked out to the place called The Skull — Golgotha in Aramaic. [18] There they crucified him, two others with him, one on each side, Jesus in the middle.

[19] Pilate had a sign made and fixed to the cross. It read: JESUS OF NAZARETH, KING OF THE JEWS. [20] Many of the Jews read that sign, because the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city, and it was written in Aramaic, Latin, and Greek. [21] The chief priests of the Jews protested to Pilate: "Don't write 'The King of the Jews.' Write that he *claimed* to be King of the Jews." [22] Pilate answered, "What I have written, I have written."

[23] After the soldiers crucified Jesus, they took his clothes and split them four ways, one share per soldier. The tunic was left over — seamless, woven in a single piece from top to bottom. [24] So they said to each other, "Let's not tear it. Let's roll for it." This was to fulfill the Scripture: *They divided my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.* That is exactly what the soldiers did.

[25] Near the cross of Jesus stood his mother, his mother's sister, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene. [26] Jesus saw his mother standing there with the disciple he loved beside her. He said to his mother, "Woman, here is your son." [27] Then to the disciple: "Here is your mother." From that hour, the disciple took her into his own home.

[28] After this, knowing now that everything had been brought to completion, and so that Scripture might reach its fulfillment, Jesus said, "I'm thirsty." [29] A jar of sour wine was sitting there. They soaked a sponge in it, fixed it on a stalk of hyssop, and lifted it to his mouth. [30] When Jesus had taken the wine, he said, "It is finished." He bowed his head and gave up his spirit.

[31] Because it was the Preparation, and the Jewish leaders didn't want bodies left on the crosses during the Sabbath — that particular Sabbath being a high day — they asked Pilate to have the legs broken and the bodies taken down. [32] So the soldiers came and broke the legs of the first man, then the other one crucified with Jesus. [33] But when they got to Jesus and saw he was already dead, they didn't break his legs. [34] Instead, one of the soldiers drove a spear into his side, and immediately blood and water poured out.

[35] The one who saw it has testified — his testimony is true, and he knows he is telling the truth — so that you also may believe. [36] These things happened to fulfill the Scripture: *Not one of his bones will be broken.* [37] And another Scripture says: *They will look on the one they pierced.*

[38] After all this, Joseph of Arimathea — a disciple of Jesus, but secretly, because he feared the Jewish leaders — asked Pilate if he could take the body away. Pilate gave permission. So he came and took the body. [39] Nicodemus came too — the one who had first come to Jesus at night — bringing about seventy-five pounds of myrrh and aloes mixed together. [40] They took the body of Jesus and wrapped it in linen with the spices, the way Jews bury their dead.

[41] At the place where he was crucified there was a garden, and in the garden a new tomb where no one had ever been laid. [42] Because it was the Jewish Preparation, and the tomb was close, they laid Jesus there.

[1] Sunday, before dawn, while darkness still clung to the world, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb and saw the stone dragged aside.

[2] She ran. Found Simon Peter and the other disciple — the one Jesus loved — and gasped it out: "They've taken the Master out of the tomb. We don't know where they put him."

[3] Peter took off. The other disciple too. Both of them racing for the grave.

[4] They ran together, but the other disciple was faster. He reached the tomb first.

[5] Bending down, he saw the linen wrappings lying there. But he didn't go in.

[6] Then Peter came up behind him and went straight in. He saw the linens lying there,

[7] and the cloth that had covered Jesus' face — not with the linens, but folded up by itself in its own place.

[8] Then the other disciple, the one who got there first, finally went in. He saw. And he believed.

[9] (They still hadn't grasped what Scripture said — that he had to rise from the dead.)

[10] Then the disciples went home.

[11] But Mary stood outside the tomb, weeping. Through her tears she bent down and looked in,

[12] and saw two angels in white, sitting where the body of Jesus had been — one at the head, one at the feet.

[13] "Woman, why are you crying?" they asked. "Because they've taken my Lord," she said, "and I don't know where they've put him."

[14] She turned around. There stood Jesus. She didn't recognize him.

[15] "Woman, why are you weeping?" he said. "Who are you looking for?" Thinking he was the gardener, she said, "Sir, if you carried him off, tell me where you put him and I'll get him."

[16] Jesus said, "Mary." She spun toward him and cried out in Aramaic, "Rabboni!" — Teacher.

[17] "Don't hold onto me," Jesus said. "I haven't yet ascended to the Father. Go to my brothers. Tell them: I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God."

[18] So Mary Magdalene went and announced to the disciples: "I have seen the Lord." And she told them everything he had said.

[19] That evening — the first day of the week — the disciples were locked behind closed doors. They were afraid of the Jewish leaders. Jesus came and stood among them. "Peace to you," he said.

[20] He showed them his hands and his side. The disciples erupted in joy when they saw the Lord.

[21] Again he said, "Peace to you. As the Father sent me, I am sending you."

[22] Then he breathed on them. "Receive the Holy Spirit," he said.

[23] "If you release anyone's sins, they are released. If you hold them bound, they stay bound."

[24] Thomas — one of the Twelve, the one called the Twin — wasn't there when Jesus came.

[25] The others kept telling him, "We have seen the Lord!" But he said, "Unless I see the nail marks in his hands, and put my finger where the nails were, and put my hand into his side — I will not believe it."

[26] Eight days later, the disciples were inside again, and Thomas was with them. The doors were locked. Jesus came and stood among them. "Peace to you," he said.

[27] Then to Thomas: "Put your finger here. Look at my hands. Reach out your hand and put it into my side. Stop doubting. Believe."

[28] Thomas answered him, "My Lord and my God."

[29] Jesus said, "You believe because you have seen me. Blessed are those who have not seen — and still believe."

[30] Jesus did many other signs in front of his disciples that aren't recorded in this book.

[31] But these are written so you may believe that Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God — and that by believing, you may have life in his name.

[1] Later, Jesus showed himself again to the disciples by the Sea of Tiberias. Here is how it happened.

[2] Simon Peter, Thomas (called the Twin), Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two others were together.

[3] Peter said, "I'm going fishing." They said, "We're coming with you." They went out, climbed into the boat, and caught nothing all night.

[4] Dawn broke. Jesus stood on the shore — but the disciples didn't recognize him.

[5] He called out, "Friends, caught anything?" "No," they answered.

[6] "Throw the net off the right side of the boat," he said. "You'll find some." They threw it — and now they couldn't haul it in, the catch was so heavy with fish.

[7] The disciple Jesus loved said to Peter, "It's the Lord." The moment Peter heard "It's the Lord," he threw on his outer garment — he'd stripped down to work — and dove into the sea.

[8] The others followed in the boat, dragging the net full of fish. They weren't far from shore — maybe a hundred yards out.

[9] When they stepped onto the beach, they saw a charcoal fire burning, with fish laid on it, and bread.

[10] "Bring some of the fish you just caught," Jesus said.

[11] Peter climbed back aboard and hauled the net to land — packed with one hundred fifty-three large fish. And though there were so many, the net didn't tear.

[12] "Come and have breakfast," Jesus said. None of them dared ask, "Who are you?" They knew. It was the Lord.

[13] Jesus came over, took the bread, and gave it to them — and the fish the same way.

[14] This was now the third time Jesus had appeared to the disciples after rising from the dead.

[15] When they had finished eating, Jesus turned to Simon Peter. "Simon, son of John — do you love me more than these?" "Yes, Lord," he said. "You know I'm devoted to you." "Then feed my lambs."

[16] He asked again. "Simon, son of John — do you love me?" "Yes, Lord. You know I'm devoted to you." "Then shepherd my sheep."

[17] A third time: "Simon, son of John — are you devoted to me?" Peter felt the sting of being asked three times. "Lord, you know everything. You know I'm devoted to you." "Then feed my sheep.

[18] Listen — when you were young, you dressed yourself and went wherever you wanted. But when you grow old, you'll stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you don't want to go."

[19] He said this to signal the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God. Then he said, "Follow me."

[20] Peter turned and saw the disciple Jesus loved walking behind them — the one who had leaned against Jesus at the supper and asked, "Lord, who is going to betray you?"

[21] Peter saw him and asked, "Lord, what about him?"

[22] Jesus answered, "If I want him to remain until I come, what's that to you? You — follow me."

[23] That's why the rumor spread among the brothers that this disciple wouldn't die. But Jesus hadn't said he wouldn't die. He said, "If I want him to remain until I come, what's that to you?"

[24] This is the disciple bearing witness to these things, the one who wrote them down. And we know his testimony is true.

[25] There's more — so much more Jesus did. If every act were written out, one by one, I suspect the world itself couldn't hold the books that would be written.

[1] The word of the LORD struck Jonah son of Amittai: [2] "Get up. Go to Nineveh — that massive city — and call them out. Their evil has climbed into my face." [3] So Jonah got up — and ran. The opposite direction. Toward Tarshish, away from the LORD. He went down to Joppa, found a ship bound for Tarshish, paid the fare, and climbed aboard to sail with them — anywhere but where God was sending him.

[4] But the LORD hurled a wind onto the sea. A storm so violent the ship thought itself into pieces. [5] The sailors panicked. Each one screamed to his own god. They started heaving cargo overboard to lighten the load. And Jonah? Jonah had gone below deck, stretched out, and fallen into a dead sleep. [6] The captain found him there. "What are you doing sleeping? Get up! Call on your god! Maybe your god will give us a thought, and we won't die."

[7] The sailors turned to each other: "Let's cast lots. Find out whose fault this is." They cast — and the lot landed on Jonah. [8] So they piled the questions on him: "Tell us — who's responsible for this disaster? What's your business? Where are you from? What country? What people?" [9] He told them: "I'm a Hebrew. I worship the LORD, the God of the heavens — the one who made the sea and the dry land."

[10] The men went cold with fear. "What have you done?" They knew already he was running from the LORD — he'd told them. [11] "What do we do with you," they asked, "to make this sea let us go?" The waves were climbing higher. [12] "Pick me up," he said, "and throw me into the sea. Then it will calm down. I know this storm is on me."

[13] But the sailors rowed harder, trying to make land. They couldn't. The sea kept rising against them, wilder by the minute. [14] So they cried out to the LORD: "Please, LORD — don't let us die over this man's life. Don't put innocent blood on us. You're the LORD. You've done what you wanted." [15] Then they lifted Jonah and threw him into the sea. And the sea stopped raging. [16] The men were seized with awe of the LORD. They offered him a sacrifice. They made vows.

[17] And the LORD appointed a great fish to swallow Jonah. He was inside the fish three days and three nights.

[1] From the belly of the fish, Jonah prayed to the LORD his God.

[2] He said:

I called out from the squeeze of my own ruin,
and you answered.
From the gut of the grave I screamed,
and you heard my voice.

[3] You hurled me into the deep,
into the heart of the sea —
the current wrapped around me,
your breakers, your waves,
rolled over me.

[4] I thought: I'm cut off,
banished from your sight.
Yet somehow I will look again
toward your holy temple.

[5] The waters closed in, throat-deep.
The deep swallowed me whole.
Seaweed coiled around my head.

[6] Down I sank to the roots of the mountains,
the earth bolting its gates behind me forever —
but you, LORD my God,
hauled my life up from the pit.

[7] When my life was draining out of me,
I remembered the LORD,
and my prayer climbed up to you,
to your holy temple.

[8] Those who chase after empty idols
walk away from the love that was theirs.

[9] But I — I will sacrifice to you
with a song of thanks.
What I vowed, I will pay.
Rescue belongs to the LORD.

[10] Then the LORD spoke to the fish, and it vomited Jonah onto dry land.

[1] The word of the LORD came to Jonah a second time:

[2] "Get up. Go to Nineveh, that massive city, and deliver the message I give you."

[3] This time Jonah went. He set out for Nineveh, just as the LORD said. Nineveh was a city of staggering size — three days just to cross it.

[4] Jonah pushed into the city, walking a single day, and started shouting his message: "Forty days. Then Nineveh falls."

[5] And the people of Nineveh believed God. They called a fast. From the wealthiest to the poorest, every last one of them put on sackcloth.

[6] Word reached the king of Nineveh. He stepped down from his throne, stripped off his royal robes, wrapped himself in sackcloth, and sat in ashes.

[7] Then he sent out a decree throughout Nineveh, issued by the king and his nobles: "No one — human or animal, herd or flock — is to taste anything. No food. No water.

[8] Cover everyone in sackcloth — even the livestock. Cry out to God with everything you have. Every person must turn from their evil and from the violence in their hands.

[9] Who knows? Maybe God will turn. Maybe he'll relent, pull back the heat of his anger, and we won't be destroyed."

[10] And God saw what they did — saw them turn from their evil road. So God relented. The disaster he had spoken of, he did not bring.

[1] This wrecked Jonah. He was furious — burning with anger.

[2] He prayed to the LORD: "This. This is exactly what I said would happen back home. This is why I bolted for Tarshish in the first place. I knew it. I knew you — gracious, compassionate, slow to anger, overflowing with loyal love, the kind of God who relents from sending disaster.

[3] So just kill me, LORD. Take my life. I'd rather be dead than watch this."

[4] The LORD said, "Are you right to be this angry?"

[5] Jonah stormed out of the city, parked himself east of it, threw together a makeshift shelter, and sat in its shade — waiting to see what would happen to Nineveh.

[6] Then the LORD God arranged a plant. It shot up over Jonah, threw shade across his head, and pulled him out of his misery. Jonah was thrilled with the plant. Absolutely thrilled.

[7] But at dawn the next day, God arranged a worm. The worm attacked the plant, and the plant withered.

[8] When the sun climbed, God arranged a scorching east wind. The sun hammered Jonah's head until he was faint, and he begged to die. "Better dead than alive," he said.

[9] God said to Jonah, "Are you right to be this angry — over a plant?"

"Yes," Jonah snapped. "Angry enough to die."

[10] The LORD said: "You're broken up over a plant. A plant you didn't plant. A plant you didn't grow. It sprouted overnight and died overnight.

[11] And I — am I not supposed to care about Nineveh? That massive city. More than 120,000 people in it who can't tell their right hand from their left. And all those animals too."

[1] After Moses—the LORD's servant—was dead, the LORD spoke to Joshua son of Nun, Moses's right hand:

[2] "Moses is gone. Now get up. Cross this Jordan, you and every last one of these people, into the land I am handing over to them—to Israel.

[3] Every patch of ground the sole of your foot touches, I have given you. I said it to Moses. I'm saying it now.

[4] From the wilderness south to the Lebanon range north, from the great river—the Euphrates—across all the Hittite country, to the Mediterranean where the sun sets: this is your territory.

[5] No one will stand against you. Not one day of your life. The way I was with Moses, I will be with you. I will not let go of you. I will not walk away.

[6] Be strong. Be courageous. You are the one who will hand this land out to this people as their inheritance—the land I swore to their ancestors I would give them.

[7] Only—be strong. Be very courageous. Guard every word of the law Moses my servant laid on you. Don't drift from it, not right, not left. Then you will succeed wherever you go.

[8] This book of the law—do not let it leave your mouth. Chew on it day and night. Live it out, every word written in it. Then your road will open up before you. Then you will succeed.

[9] Haven't I commanded you? Be strong. Be courageous. Do not be afraid. Do not fall apart. The LORD your God is with you wherever you go."

[10] So Joshua gave the order to the officers of the people:

[11] "Move through the camp. Tell the people: 'Get your supplies ready. In three days you cross this Jordan to go in and take the land—the land the LORD your God is giving you to possess.'"

[12] To the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, Joshua said:

[13] "Remember what Moses the servant of the LORD told you: 'The LORD your God is giving you rest. He is giving you this land.'

[14] Your wives, your children, your livestock—they stay here, in the land Moses gave you east of the Jordan. But every fighting man among you crosses over armed, ahead of your brothers. You back them up.

[15] You stay with them until the LORD gives them rest, the way he gave it to you, until they too possess the land the LORD your God is giving them. Then—then—you come back to your own land, the inheritance Moses the servant of the LORD gave you east of the Jordan, where the sun rises. And you possess it."

[16] They answered Joshua: "Everything you command us, we will do. Wherever you send us, we will go.

[17] Just as we listened to Moses in everything, so we will listen to you. Only—may the LORD your God be with you the way he was with Moses.

[18] Anyone who defies your orders, anyone who refuses to listen to a single word you command—he dies. Just be strong. Be courageous."

[1] Joshua, son of Nun, sent two spies out from Shittim on the quiet. "Go," he told them. "Scout the land — especially Jericho." So they went. They came to the house of a prostitute named Rahab and stayed the night there.

[2] Word reached the king of Jericho: "Listen — Israelite men slipped in tonight to scout the land."

[3] The king sent a message to Rahab: "Hand over the men who came to you — the ones in your house. They're here to scout the whole country."

[4] But Rahab had already hidden them. She said, "Yes, the men came to me. I didn't know where they were from.

[5] At dusk, when the gate was about to close, they left. I have no idea where they went. Hurry — chase them down. You can still catch them."

[6] She had taken them up to the roof and tucked them beneath stalks of flax she had laid out there.

[7] The king's men chased the spies down the road toward the Jordan, all the way to the fords. The moment they were out, the city gate slammed shut behind them.

[8] Before the spies bedded down, Rahab climbed up to the roof.

[9] "I know," she said. "I know the LORD has given you this land. Terror of you has fallen on us. Every person living here is melting in fear because of you.

[10] We've heard how the LORD dried up the Red Sea in front of you when you came out of Egypt. We've heard what you did to Sihon and Og, those two Amorite kings east of the Jordan — how you wiped them out completely.

[11] When we heard, our hearts liquefied. No one has any spirit left to face you. Because the LORD your God — he is God. Up in the heavens. Down here on earth.

[12] So swear to me now, by the LORD — since I've shown you kindness, you'll show kindness to my father's house. Give me a sign I can trust.

[13] Spare my father, my mother, my brothers, my sisters — everyone who belongs to them. Save us from death."

[14] "Our lives for yours," the men said. "If you don't betray what we're doing, when the LORD gives us this land we'll treat you with faithfulness and kindness."

[15] Then she let them down through her window by a rope — her house was built into the city wall; she lived in the wall itself.

[16] "Head for the hills," she told them, "or the search party will find you. Hide there three days, until they come back. Then go your way."

[17] The men said to her, "Here's how this oath you made us swear works.

[18] When we invade the land, tie this scarlet cord in the window you let us down through. Gather your father, your mother, your brothers — your whole family — into your house.

[19] If anyone steps outside your door, their blood is on their own head. We're not responsible. But anyone inside with you — if a hand touches them, their blood is on us.

[20] And if you breathe a word of what we're doing, the oath you made us swear — we're free of it."

[21] "Agreed," she said. "Just as you've said." She sent them off, and they were gone. Then she tied the scarlet cord in the window.

[22] They went up into the hills and stayed three days, until the search party gave up. The pursuers had combed the whole road and found nothing.

[23] Then the two men came down out of the hills, crossed the Jordan, and came to Joshua son of Nun. They told him everything that had happened to them.

[24] "The LORD has handed the whole land over to us," they said. "Every person living there is melting in fear at the sight of us."

[1] Before dawn, Joshua got the people moving. They broke camp at Shittim and marched to the edge of the Jordan, and there they stopped, waiting, before crossing over.

[2] Three days in, the officers walked through the camp

[3] with their orders for the people: "When you see the Ark of the Covenant of the LORD your God being carried by the Levitical priests, pull up stakes. Follow it.

[4] But keep your distance — about a thousand yards back. Don't crowd it. You've never walked this road before, so let the Ark show you the way."

[5] Joshua told the people, "Consecrate yourselves. Tomorrow the LORD is going to do wonders in your midst."

[6] Then to the priests: "Lift the Ark of the Covenant. Lead the people across." So they hoisted it and went out ahead.

[7] The LORD said to Joshua, "Today I begin elevating you in the eyes of all Israel, so they will know that I am with you the same way I was with Moses.

[8] Tell the priests carrying the Ark: when you reach the edge of the Jordan's waters, stop there. Stand in the river."

[9] Joshua gathered Israel: "Come close. Listen to the words of the LORD your God."

[10] Then he said, "Here is how you'll know the living God is right here among you, and that he will absolutely drive out the Canaanites, Hittites, Hivites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Amorites, and Jebusites before you:

[11] watch. The Ark of the Covenant of the Lord of all the earth is about to step into the Jordan ahead of you.

[12] Pick twelve men, one from each tribe.

[13] The moment the soles of the priests' feet — the priests carrying the Ark of the LORD, the Lord of all the earth — touch the water of the Jordan, the river will be cut. The water flowing down from upstream will pile up in a single heap."

[14] So the people broke camp to cross the Jordan, the priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant out in front.

[15] Now the Jordan was over its banks — it was harvest season, the river was running full — but the moment the priests' feet hit the water's edge,

[16] the river upstream stopped. It rose up in a wall, way back at a town called Adam near Zarethan. The water heading down to the Sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea, was completely cut off. And the people crossed, opposite Jericho.

[17] The priests carrying the Ark of the Covenant of the LORD stood there — feet planted on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan — while all of Israel walked across on dry ground. They stood until the entire nation had finished crossing.

[1] Once the whole nation had finished crossing the Jordan, the LORD spoke to Joshua:

[2] "Pick twelve men from the people, one from each tribe,

[3] and give them this order: Lift twelve stones from right here in the middle of the Jordan, from the exact spot where the priests' feet are planted. Carry them across and set them down where you camp tonight."

[4] So Joshua called the twelve men he had chosen from Israel, one per tribe,

[5] and told them: "Cross back into the Jordan, in front of the ark of the LORD your God. Each of you, hoist a stone onto your shoulder — twelve stones, one for every tribe of Israel.

[6] This is a sign among you. When your children ask one day, 'What do these stones mean to you?'

[7] you tell them: The Jordan's current was cut off in front of the ark of the covenant of the LORD. When it crossed the Jordan, the waters were cut off. These stones are a memorial to the people of Israel, forever."

[8] The Israelites did exactly what Joshua commanded. They lifted twelve stones out of the middle of the Jordan — one for each tribe, just as the LORD had told Joshua — carried them across to the camp, and set them down there.

[9] Joshua also planted twelve stones in the middle of the Jordan itself, at the place where the feet of the priests carrying the ark had stood. They are still there to this day.

[10] The priests carrying the ark stood in the middle of the Jordan until every command the LORD had given Joshua for the people was carried out — just as Moses had charged Joshua. And the people hurried across.

[11] When every last person had crossed, the ark of the LORD crossed too, and the priests took their place at the front of the people.

[12] The men of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh crossed armed for battle ahead of the Israelites, exactly as Moses had told them.

[13] About forty thousand strong, equipped for war, they marched in the LORD's presence onto the plains of Jericho, ready to fight.

[14] That day the LORD made Joshua great in the eyes of all Israel. They held him in awe, just as they had held Moses in awe — all the days of his life.

[15] The LORD said to Joshua,

[16] "Order the priests carrying the ark of the testimony to come up out of the Jordan."

[17] So Joshua gave the order: "Come up out of the Jordan."

[18] The moment the priests carrying the ark of the covenant of the LORD stepped up out of the riverbed and their feet touched dry ground, the waters of the Jordan rushed back to their place and overflowed their banks as before.

[19] The people came up out of the Jordan on the tenth day of the first month and camped at Gilgal, on the eastern edge of Jericho.

[20] And there at Gilgal, Joshua set up the twelve stones they had carried out of the Jordan.

[21] He said to the Israelites: "When your children ask their fathers someday, 'What are these stones?'

[22] tell your children this: Israel crossed this Jordan on dry ground.

[23] The LORD your God dried up the waters of the Jordan in front of you until you crossed — the same way the LORD your God did with the Red Sea, drying it up before us until we crossed —

[24] so that every people on earth would know the hand of the LORD is mighty, and so that you would stand in awe of the LORD your God forever."

[1] When the kings of the Amorites west of the Jordan and the kings of the Canaanites along the coast heard how the LORD had dried up the Jordan in front of Israel until they crossed over — their hearts liquefied. Every last bit of fight drained out of them at the sight of Israel.

[2] Then the LORD said to Joshua, "Make yourself flint knives and circumcise the Israelites — this generation, now."

[3] So Joshua made the flint knives and circumcised the Israelites at the Hill of Foreskins.

[4] Here is why: every male who came out of Egypt — every fighting-age man — had died in the wilderness on the journey out.

[5] The men who left Egypt had been circumcised, but none of those born during the forty-year trek through the wilderness ever were.

[6] Israel walked that wilderness for forty years until the whole generation of warriors who came out of Egypt was gone — wiped out because they would not listen to the LORD's voice. The LORD had sworn they would never see the land he promised their fathers — the land flowing with milk and honey.

[7] So he raised up their sons in their place. These were the ones Joshua circumcised — uncircumcised until that moment, because no one had done it on the road.

[8] Once the whole nation had been circumcised, they stayed put in the camp until they healed.

[9] Then the LORD said to Joshua, "Today I have rolled the disgrace of Egypt off your backs." So the place is called Gilgal — Rolling — to this day.

[10] The Israelites camped at Gilgal and kept the Passover there on the fourteenth day of the month, in the evening, on the plains of Jericho.

[11] The day after Passover — that very day — they ate from the produce of the land: unleavened bread and roasted grain.

[12] The manna stopped the morning after they ate the land's produce. There was no more manna for the Israelites. That year they ate from the harvest of Canaan.

[13] Joshua was near Jericho when he looked up and saw a man standing in front of him, sword drawn, ready. Joshua walked straight to him and demanded, "Are you for us or for our enemies?"

[14] "Neither," he said. "I am the commander of the LORD's army. I have come." Joshua dropped face-down to the ground in worship and said, "What does my Lord command his servant?"

[15] The commander of the LORD's army said, "Take off your sandals. The ground you are standing on is holy." And Joshua did.

[1] Jericho had locked itself down tight against the Israelites. No one going out. No one coming in.

[2] The LORD said to Joshua, "Look — I've handed Jericho over to you. The king, the warriors, all of it.

[3] Here's what you do: march around the city. All your fighting men, one lap around. Do this for six days.

[4] Seven priests carry seven ram's-horn trumpets in front of the ark. On day seven, circle the city seven times, with the priests blasting the horns.

[5] When you hear that long, drawn-out blast — when every horn lets loose — every soldier in earshot lets out a war cry that splits the sky. The walls will collapse where they stand. Your men walk straight in."

[6] So Joshua son of Nun called the priests together. "Pick up the ark of the covenant. Seven of you, grab the seven ram's-horn trumpets and lead it out."

[7] Then he turned to the people: "Move out. Circle the city. The armed guard goes ahead of the ark of the LORD."

[8] When Joshua finished speaking, the seven priests with the seven horns stepped out before the LORD, blowing as they marched, and the ark of the covenant followed.

[9] The armed guard went in front of the priests with the trumpets. The rear guard followed the ark. The horns never stopped sounding.

[10] But Joshua had ordered the people: "No shouting. No raising your voice. Not a word out of your mouths — until the day I tell you, 'Shout.' Then let it rip."

[11] So the ark of the LORD circled the city once. Then back to camp. They slept there.

[12] At dawn Joshua was up. The priests lifted the ark.

[13] The seven priests with the seven horns walked in front of the ark, sounding the blast as they went. The armed guard ahead, the rear guard behind, the trumpets steady and unbroken.

[14] Day two: one lap around the city, back to camp. Same on day three. Day four. Day five. Day six.

[15] On the seventh day they rose at first light and circled the city the same way — but this time seven times. Seven laps. Only on this day.

[16] On the seventh lap, when the priests sounded the horns, Joshua roared to the people: "SHOUT! The LORD has given you the city!

[17] The city is under the ban — devoted to the LORD, everything in it. Only Rahab the prostitute lives, and everyone in her house, because she hid the men we sent.

[18] But keep your hands off the devoted things — or you'll bring the ban down on yourselves. Touch one piece of it and you've poisoned the camp of Israel and brought disaster on us all.

[19] Every scrap of silver and gold, every bronze and iron vessel — sacred to the LORD. Into the LORD's treasury it goes."

[20] So they shouted. The horns blew. And when the people heard that blast and let out the war cry — the wall caved in where it stood. Every soldier charged straight in, and the city was theirs.

[21] They put it all under the ban — every living thing inside. Men, women, young, old, cattle, sheep, donkeys. Edge of the sword.

[22] To the two spies, Joshua said: "Go to the prostitute's house. Bring her out — her and everyone with her — like you swore to her."

[23] So the young men who had scouted the city went in and brought out Rahab, her father, her mother, her brothers, everyone connected to her — the whole family. They led them out and settled them outside Israel's camp.

[24] Then they torched the city. Everything in it. Only the silver, gold, bronze, and iron went into the treasury of the LORD's house.

[25] But Rahab the prostitute — Joshua spared her, and her father's house, and everyone she had. She lives among Israel to this day, because she hid the messengers Joshua sent to scout Jericho.

[26] Then Joshua laid down a curse, then and there: "Cursed before the LORD is the man who dares rebuild this city, Jericho. He lays the foundation at the cost of his firstborn. He sets the gates at the cost of his youngest."

[27] And the LORD was with Joshua. His name spread across the land.

[1] But Israel broke faith over what was devoted to destruction. Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, from the tribe of Judah, helped himself to some of it. And the LORD's anger burned hot against Israel.

[2] Joshua sent men from Jericho up to Ai — the town near Beth-aven, just east of Bethel — and told them, "Go scout the land." So they went up and reconnoitered Ai.

[3] They came back to Joshua and reported: "No need to send the whole army. Two or three thousand can take Ai. Don't wear out the troops — there's hardly anyone there."

[4] So about three thousand went up. And they ran. They ran from the men of Ai.

[5] The defenders of Ai cut down thirty-six of them, chasing them from the city gate all the way to the quarries, striking them down on the slope. And the heart of the people melted — it ran like water.

[6] Joshua ripped his clothes and fell facedown before the ark of the LORD until evening — he and the elders of Israel — dirt heaped on their heads.

[7] Joshua said, "Oh, Lord GOD — why did you drag this people across the Jordan at all? To hand us over to the Amorites? To wipe us out? If only we'd been content to stay on the other side!

[8] Lord — what am I supposed to say, now that Israel has turned tail and run from its enemies?

[9] The Canaanites will hear of this. Every nation around us will. They'll close in. They'll erase our name from the earth. And then what will you do for your own great name?"

[10] The LORD said to Joshua: "Get up. Why are you facedown like this?

[11] Israel has sinned. They've broken the covenant I commanded them to keep. They've taken what was devoted to me. They've stolen. They've lied. They've hidden it among their own gear.

[12] That's why Israel cannot stand against its enemies. That's why they run. They have become the thing devoted to destruction. I will not be with you again unless you tear this evil out from among you.

[13] Get up. Consecrate the people. Tell them: Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, because this is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says — There is devoted plunder among you, Israel. You will not stand before your enemies until you remove it.

[14] In the morning you'll come forward by tribes. The tribe the LORD seizes will come forward by clans. The clan the LORD seizes will come forward by families. The family the LORD seizes will come forward man by man.

[15] And the one caught with the devoted things will be burned with fire — he and everything he has — because he broke the covenant of the LORD and committed an outrage in Israel."

[16] Joshua was up early. He brought Israel forward tribe by tribe — and Judah was taken.

[17] He brought the clans of Judah forward — the clan of the Zerahites was taken. He brought the Zerahites forward family by family — Zabdi was taken.

[18] He brought Zabdi's family forward man by man — and Achan son of Carmi, son of Zabdi, son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was taken.

[19] Joshua said to him, "My son — give glory to the LORD, the God of Israel. Confess to him. Tell me what you did. Don't hide it from me."

[20] Achan answered: "It's true. I sinned against the LORD, the God of Israel. This is what I did.

[21] I saw, in the spoil, a beautiful robe from Shinar, two hundred shekels of silver, and a bar of gold weighing fifty shekels. I wanted them. I took them. They're buried in the ground inside my tent — silver underneath."

[22] Joshua sent runners. They sprinted to the tent — and there it was, hidden inside, silver underneath.

[23] They brought it all out — out of the tent, to Joshua and to all the Israelites — and spread it on the ground before the LORD.

[24] Then Joshua, with all Israel, took Achan son of Zerah — the silver, the robe, the gold, his sons, his daughters, his ox, his donkey, his sheep, his tent, everything he had — and brought them up to the Valley of Achor.

[25] Joshua said, "Why did you bring this disaster on us? Today the LORD brings disaster on you." And all Israel stoned him. They burned them and stoned them with stones.

[26] They piled a great heap of stones over him — it stands there to this day. And the LORD turned from his burning anger. That's why the place is called the Valley of Achor — the Valley of Trouble — to this day.

[1] Then the LORD said to Joshua: "Don't be afraid. Don't lose your nerve. Take every fighting man you have and go up against Ai. I've handed you the king, his people, his city, his land — all of it.

[2] Do to Ai and her king exactly what you did to Jericho and her king — except this time, you keep the plunder and the livestock for yourselves. Set an ambush behind the city."

[3] So Joshua moved out, the whole army with him, ready to march on Ai. He picked thirty thousand of his best warriors and sent them ahead under cover of night,

[4] with these orders: "Listen carefully. You're going to lie in ambush behind the city — not too far off. Every one of you stay sharp and ready.

[5] I'll take the rest of the men and approach the city from the front. When they come pouring out to meet us like they did before, we'll turn and run.

[6] They'll chase us. They'll think — just like last time — 'They're running from us again.' So we'll run, drawing them away from the city.

[7] Then you spring from the ambush and seize the city. The LORD your God is handing it to you.

[8] Once the city is yours, set it on fire. Do what the LORD commanded. These are your orders."

[9] Joshua sent them off, and they took position in ambush — between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city. Joshua spent that night with the troops.

[10] Early the next morning, Joshua mustered the army. He and the elders of Israel marched at the head of the troops toward Ai.

[11] The entire force advanced and arrived in front of the city, making camp on the north side of Ai — a valley between them and the city.

[12] He'd already taken about five thousand men and placed them in ambush between Bethel and Ai, on the west side of the city.

[13] So the army was deployed: the main force north of the city, the rear guard west of it. Joshua spent that night down in the valley.

[14] When the king of Ai saw this, he and the men of the city scrambled out at dawn to engage Israel in battle — heading for a spot facing the Arabah. He had no idea an ambush was waiting behind him.

[15] Joshua and all Israel played the part — beaten men in retreat, fleeing toward the wilderness.

[16] Every man in Ai was called out to chase them. They chased Joshua — and got pulled clean away from the city.

[17] Not a man was left in Ai or Bethel who didn't run out after Israel. They left the city wide open and gave chase.

[18] Then the LORD said to Joshua: "Stretch out the javelin in your hand toward Ai. I'm giving the city into your hand." Joshua stretched it out toward the city.

[19] The moment his hand went up, the men in ambush sprang from their position, raced into the city, captured it, and immediately set it ablaze.

[20] The men of Ai turned and looked back — and there it was: smoke from their city climbing into the sky. They had nowhere to run. The Israelites who'd been fleeing toward the wilderness now wheeled around on their pursuers.

[21] When Joshua and all Israel saw that the ambush had taken the city and the smoke was rising, they turned back and cut down the men of Ai.

[22] The ambush force came out of the city to meet them, so Ai's soldiers were trapped — Israelites on both sides. They struck them down until not one was left alive or escaped.

[23] But the king of Ai they took alive and brought to Joshua.

[24] When Israel finished killing every inhabitant of Ai in the open fields and the wilderness where they'd been pursued — every last one falling by the sword — all Israel returned to Ai and put it to the sword.

[25] The total dead that day, men and women, was twelve thousand — everyone in Ai.

[26] Joshua never lowered the hand holding the javelin until he had destroyed every inhabitant of Ai under the ban.

[27] Israel did keep the livestock and plunder for themselves — exactly what the LORD had told Joshua.

[28] Joshua burned Ai to the ground and made it a permanent ruin, a wasteland — and so it stands to this day.

[29] He hanged the king of Ai on a tree until evening. At sunset, on Joshua's order, they took his body down from the tree, threw it at the city gate, and piled a great heap of stones over it — still there to this day.

[30] Then Joshua built an altar to the LORD, the God of Israel, on Mount Ebal,

[31] just as Moses, the servant of the LORD, had commanded the Israelites — as written in the Book of the Law of Moses: an altar of uncut stones, untouched by any iron tool. On it they offered burnt offerings to the LORD and sacrificed peace offerings.

[32] There, in the presence of the Israelites, Joshua copied onto the stones the law of Moses — a copy that Moses had written.

[33] All Israel — elders, officers, judges, foreigners and native-born alike — stood on either side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD, facing the Levitical priests who carried it. Half the people faced Mount Gerizim, half faced Mount Ebal, exactly as Moses, the servant of the LORD, had commanded — to bless the people of Israel.

[34] Then Joshua read every word of the law — the blessing and the curse — exactly as written in the Book of the Law.

[35] Of all that Moses had commanded, Joshua left nothing unread before the entire assembly of Israel — including the women, the children, and the foreigners living among them.

[1] When news of all this reached the kings west of the Jordan—those in the hill country, the lowlands, and along the entire Mediterranean coast as far north as Lebanon: the Hittites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites— [2] they banded together to fight Joshua and Israel as one united front.

[3] But the people of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and Ai, [4] and they decided to play it smart. They went out as a delegation in disguise—loaded their donkeys with worn-out sacks and cracked, patched-up wineskins, [5] wore beat-up sandals held together with stitching, threw on tattered clothes, and packed bread that had gone dry and crumbly. [6] In this state they showed up at Joshua's camp at Gilgal and said to him and the men of Israel, "We've come from a distant land. Cut a treaty with us."

[7] The men of Israel eyed the Hivites and said, "How do we know you don't live right next door? If you do, we can't make any treaty with you."

[8] They turned to Joshua: "We are your servants."

Joshua asked them, "Who are you? Where do you come from?"

[9] They answered, "Your servants have traveled from a far-off country—because of the reputation of the LORD your God. We've heard the reports: everything he did in Egypt, [10] and what he did to the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan—Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan in Ashtaroth. [11] So our elders and everyone back home told us, 'Pack supplies for the road and go meet them. Tell them: We are your servants. Make a treaty with us.' [12] Look at this bread. It was hot from the oven the day we packed it. Now it's dry and crumbling. [13] These wineskins were new when we filled them—now they're split. Our clothes and sandals are falling apart from the long journey."

[14] The Israelites sampled the provisions—but did not consult the LORD. [15] Joshua made peace with them, cutting a treaty to let them live, and the leaders of the assembly swore the oath sealing it.

[16] Three days after the treaty was signed, Israel learned the truth: these people were neighbors, living right in the area. [17] The Israelites marched out and on the third day reached their cities—Gibeon, Kephirah, Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim. [18] But Israel didn't attack, because the leaders had sworn by the LORD, the God of Israel.

The whole community grumbled against the leaders, [19] but the leaders held the line: "We've sworn an oath by the LORD, the God of Israel. We cannot touch them. [20] Here's what we'll do: let them live, or wrath will fall on us for breaking the oath we swore." [21] The leaders added, "Let them live—but they'll cut wood and haul water for the whole community." So the leaders kept their word.

[22] Joshua summoned the Gibeonites and confronted them: "Why did you lie to us? Why did you say, 'We live far away,' when you live right here among us? [23] Now you're under a curse. From this day on, you will always be slaves—woodcutters and water-haulers for the house of my God."

[24] They answered Joshua, "Your servants were told plainly that the LORD your God had commanded his servant Moses to give you this entire land and wipe out everyone living in it. We were terrified for our lives because of you. That's why we did it. [25] Now we're in your hands. Do whatever seems right and fair to you."

[26] So Joshua spared them. He kept Israel from killing them, [27] and that day he made them woodcutters and water-haulers for the community and for the altar of the LORD, at the place the LORD would choose—a role they hold to this day.

[1] Adoni-Zedek, king of Jerusalem, got word that Joshua had taken Ai and wiped it out — done to Ai and its king exactly what he did to Jericho and its king — and that the people of Gibeon had cut a peace deal with Israel and were now living among them.

[2] Fear shot through him. Gibeon was no small town — it was a royal city, bigger than Ai, and its men were warriors.

[3] So Adoni-Zedek sent word to Hoham king of Hebron, Piram king of Jarmuth, Japhia king of Lachish, and Debir king of Eglon:

[4] "Come up here. Help me. Let's strike Gibeon — they made peace with Joshua and the Israelites."

[5] The five Amorite kings — Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, Eglon — joined forces, marched out with all their armies, surrounded Gibeon, and attacked.

[6] The Gibeonites sent an urgent message to Joshua at the Gilgal camp: "Don't abandon your servants. Come fast. Save us. Help us. Every Amorite king from the hill country has joined against us."

[7] Joshua moved out from Gilgal — his whole army with him, every fighting man.

[8] The LORD said to Joshua, "Don't be afraid of them. I've handed them over to you. Not one of them will stand against you."

[9] Joshua hit them by surprise — marched all night from Gilgal and came down on them.

[10] The LORD threw them into chaos before Israel. Joshua struck them with a crushing blow at Gibeon, chased them up the road to Beth-horon, and cut them down all the way to Azekah and Makkedah.

[11] As they fled before Israel, running down the Beth-horon descent, the LORD hurled massive hailstones on them from the sky, all the way to Azekah. More died from the hail than from Israelite swords.

[12] On that day — the day the LORD handed the Amorites over to Israel — Joshua spoke to the LORD in front of everyone:

"Sun — stand still over Gibeon.
Moon — stop over the Valley of Aijalon."

[13] And the sun stood still. The moon froze in place. Until the nation finished avenging itself on its enemies.

It's written in the Book of Jashar. The sun halted in the middle of the sky and refused to set for nearly a whole day.

[14] There has never been a day like it — before or since — when the LORD listened to the voice of a man. The LORD was fighting for Israel.

[15] Then Joshua, with all Israel, returned to the camp at Gilgal.

[16] Meanwhile, the five kings had run for it and hidden themselves in a cave at Makkedah.

[17] Word came to Joshua: "We found the five kings — hiding in the cave at Makkedah."

[18] Joshua said, "Roll big stones over the cave mouth. Post guards there.

[19] Don't stop. Chase your enemies down. Strike their rear guard. Don't let them reach their cities — the LORD your God has given them into your hand."

[20] Joshua and the Israelites finished off the enemy with a slaughter so complete it nearly wiped them out — only a handful made it inside the fortified cities.

[21] The whole army returned to Joshua at the camp at Makkedah in peace. No one dared even speak against the Israelites after that.

[22] Then Joshua said, "Open the cave. Bring me those five kings."

[23] They did — dragged them out: the kings of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon.

[24] When they brought them to Joshua, he summoned every man of Israel and told the commanders who had gone with him, "Come here. Put your feet on the necks of these kings." They came forward and pressed their feet on the kings' necks.

[25] Joshua said to them, "Don't be afraid. Don't lose your nerve. Be strong. Be bold. This is what the LORD will do to every enemy you fight."

[26] Then Joshua struck them dead and hung their bodies on five trees. They hung there until evening.

[27] At sunset Joshua gave the order. They took the bodies down, threw them into the cave where the kings had hidden, and piled great stones over the mouth — stones that are there to this day.

[28] That same day Joshua took Makkedah. He put the city and its king to the sword, devoted everyone in it to destruction — left no survivor. He did to the king of Makkedah what he had done to the king of Jericho.

[29] Joshua and all Israel moved on from Makkedah to Libnah and attacked it.

[30] The LORD handed it over — city and king both. Joshua put every soul in it to the sword. No survivor. He did to its king what he had done to the king of Jericho.

[31] On from Libnah to Lachish. Joshua and all Israel laid siege and attacked.

[32] The LORD gave Lachish to Israel. Joshua took it on the second day, put it to the sword, killed every soul in it — exactly as he had done at Libnah.

[33] Horam king of Gezer came up to help Lachish. Joshua struck him down — him and his army — until no survivor was left.

[34] From Lachish, Joshua and all Israel pressed on to Eglon. Siege. Attack.

[35] Took it that same day. Put it to the sword. Devoted every soul in it to destruction that day — exactly as he had done at Lachish.

[36] From Eglon, Joshua and all Israel went up to Hebron and attacked.

[37] They took it. Put it to the sword — its king, all its towns, every soul. No survivor — just as he had done at Eglon. He devoted the city and everyone in it to destruction.

[38] Then Joshua and all Israel turned back to Debir and attacked.

[39] He took it — city, king, every town belonging to it. Put them to the sword. Devoted every soul to destruction. No survivor. What he had done to Hebron, what he had done to Libnah and its king — he did to Debir and its king.

[40] So Joshua struck the whole land — the hill country, the Negev, the lowlands, the slopes — and every king. No survivor. Every breathing thing devoted to destruction, just as the LORD, the God of Israel, had commanded.

[41] Joshua struck them from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, the whole region of Goshen, all the way to Gibeon.

[42] He captured all these kings and their lands in one campaign — because the LORD, the God of Israel, was fighting for Israel.

[43] Then Joshua, with all Israel, returned to the camp at Gilgal.

[1] When Jabin king of Hazor got word of what had happened, he sent messengers racing to Jobab king of Madon, to the kings of Shimron and Achshaph, [2] and to the kings up north in the highlands, in the Arabah south of Chinneroth, in the lowlands, and along the slopes of Dor on the western coast. [3] He called out the Canaanites east and west, the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, and Jebusites of the hill country, and the Hivites beneath Hermon in the land of Mizpah.

[4] They came — every last one of them — armies swarming like sand on the seashore, with horses and chariots beyond counting. [5] All these kings linked up, marched together, and camped at the waters of Merom to fight Israel.

[6] The LORD said to Joshua, "Don't be afraid of them. By this time tomorrow, I'm handing every one of them over to Israel — dead men. Hamstring their horses. Burn their chariots."

[7] Joshua hit them without warning. He and his whole fighting force descended on them at the waters of Merom and fell on them hard. [8] The LORD delivered them into Israel's hand, and Israel cut them down, chasing them all the way to Greater Sidon, to Misrephoth-maim, and east into the Valley of Mizpeh. They struck them until not one survivor was left. [9] Joshua did exactly what the LORD told him: he hamstrung their horses and burned their chariots.

[10] Then Joshua doubled back and took Hazor, killing its king with the sword — because Hazor had been the head of all these kingdoms. [11] They put every living thing in it to the sword, devoting them to destruction. Nothing that breathed was left alive. Then he burned Hazor to the ground.

[12] Joshua took all the cities of these kings — every king with them — and put them to the sword, wiping them out, just as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded. [13] But Israel did not burn the cities standing on their mounds — Hazor alone Joshua burned. [14] Israel took the plunder and livestock for themselves, but every person they killed with the sword until none were left. They left nothing alive that breathed.

[15] What the LORD commanded Moses, Moses commanded Joshua, and Joshua did it. He left nothing undone of everything the LORD commanded Moses.

[16] Joshua took the whole land: the hill country, all the Negev, all the region of Goshen, the lowlands, the Arabah, and the highlands of Israel with their lowlands — [17] from Mount Halak rising toward Seir all the way to Baal-gad in the Valley of Lebanon beneath Mount Hermon. He captured every one of their kings, struck them down, and killed them. [18] Joshua waged war against all those kings for a long time. [19] Not a single city made peace with Israel — except the Hivites who lived in Gibeon. Everything else they took by force. [20] This was the LORD's doing: he hardened their hearts to come out and fight Israel, so that they might be destroyed without mercy — wiped out completely, as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[21] During that campaign Joshua also moved against the Anakim — those giants — in the hill country: at Hebron, at Debir, at Anab, and throughout the hills of Judah and Israel. Joshua devoted them and their cities to destruction. [22] No Anakim remained in the land of Israel. Only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod did any survive.

[23] So Joshua took the whole land — all of it — exactly as the LORD had promised Moses. Joshua gave it to Israel as their inheritance, parceled out tribe by tribe. And the land had rest from war.

[1] This is the inheritance the Israelites received in Canaan — the land Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the family heads of the tribes parceled out to them.

[6] Then the men of Judah came to Joshua at Gilgal, and Caleb son of Jephunneh — Caleb the Kenizzite — stepped forward and said: "You know what the LORD told Moses, the man of God, about you and me back at Kadesh-barnea.

[7] I was forty years old when Moses, the servant of the LORD, sent me out from Kadesh-barnea to scout the land. I came back and told him what I'd seen — straight, no spin, the truth as I saw it.

[8] My brothers who went up with me melted the people's courage. But I stayed locked in, fully with the LORD my God.

[9] That day Moses made me a promise: 'The ground your feet walked on will belong to you and your children forever, because you stayed all-in with the LORD my God.'

[10] And look — the LORD has kept me alive, just as he said. Forty-five years have passed since he spoke that word to Moses, while Israel wandered the wilderness. Today I stand here, eighty-five years old,

[11] and I am as strong as I was the day Moses sent me out. Whatever strength I had then for war and for the road, I have now.

[12] So give me this hill country the LORD promised me that day. You heard the report — the Anakim are up there, fortified cities, giants. But if the LORD is with me, I'll drive them out, just as he said."

[13] Joshua blessed him and handed Hebron over to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his inheritance.

[14] Hebron has belonged to Caleb's descendants ever since, because he stayed all-in with the LORD, the God of Israel.

[15] Hebron used to be called Kiriath-arba — Arba being the greatest of the Anakim. And the land finally had peace from war.

[1] The territory allotted to the tribe of Judah, clan by clan, stretched south to the border of Edom, all the way down to the Wilderness of Zin at the far edge of the Negev.

[4] continued on to Azmon, came out at the Wadi of Egypt, and ran out at the sea. That was their southern boundary.

[18] On the day she came to him, she urged him to ask her father for a field. When she dismounted from her donkey, Caleb said, "What do you want?"

[19] She answered, "Give me a blessing — you've put me in the Negev, the dry south. Give me springs of water too." So Caleb gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

[14] Joseph's descendants came to Joshua with a complaint: "Why have you given us just one allotment, one portion? We're a huge people — the LORD has blessed us that much." [15] Joshua answered: "If you're so numerous, then go up into the forest country and clear ground for yourselves in the territory of the Perizzites and the Rephaim — since the hill country of Ephraim is too cramped for you."

[16] Joseph's descendants pushed back: "The hill country isn't enough for us, and every Canaanite living in the valley has iron chariots — the ones in Beth-shean and its villages, the ones in the Valley of Jezreel."

[17] Then Joshua said to the house of Joseph — to Ephraim and Manasseh both: "You are a great people with great strength. You won't have just one allotment. [18] The hill country will be yours. Yes, it's forest — clear it. Every last inch of it will belong to you. And you will drive out the Canaanites, even with their iron chariots, even with their strength. You will."

[1] The whole community of Israel gathered at Shiloh and pitched the Tent of Meeting there. The land lay subdued before them.

[2] But seven tribes still had no inheritance — no portion staked out, no soil to call their own.

[3] So Joshua confronted them: "How long are you going to sit on your hands? How long before you take possession of the land the LORD, the God of your fathers, has already handed you?

[4] Pick three men from each tribe. I'll send them out to walk the land, map it, and write up an inventory of their inheritance. Then bring it back to me.

[5] They'll divide it into seven portions. Judah holds its territory in the south, the house of Joseph holds its territory in the north.

[6] You survey the rest — seven shares — and bring the map to me. Then I'll cast lots for you here, before the LORD our God.

[7] But the Levites get no share among you — their inheritance is the priesthood of the LORD. And Gad, Reuben, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have already taken their land east of the Jordan, the portion Moses, the servant of the LORD, gave them."

[8] The surveyors set out. Joshua's orders to them as they left: "Walk the land. Map it. Come back to me here, and I'll cast lots for you before the LORD at Shiloh."

[9] So the men went. They crossed the land, recorded it town by town in seven sections in a book, and returned to Joshua at the camp in Shiloh.

[10] Joshua cast lots for them at Shiloh in the presence of the LORD, and there he divided the land among the Israelites — to each tribe its assigned share.

[11] The first lot came up for the tribe of Benjamin, clan by clan. Their territory landed between the sons of Judah and the sons of Joseph.

[12] On the north side, the border started at the Jordan, climbed up the slope above Jericho on the north, pushed west into the hill country, and ended at the wilderness of Beth-aven.

[13] From there it ran south to Luz — the slope of Luz, that is, Bethel — then dropped down to Ataroth-addar on the hill south of Lower Beth-horon.

[14] On the western side, the border bent south from the hill facing Beth-horon and finished at Kiriath-baal — also known as Kiriath-jearim, a town of Judah. That was the western boundary.

[15] The southern boundary started at the edge of Kiriath-jearim, headed west, and reached the spring of the waters of Nephtoah.

[16] Then it dropped to the foot of the hill overlooking the Valley of Ben-Hinnom, north of the Valley of Rephaim. It continued down the Hinnom Valley along the southern slope of the Jebusite ridge, and on down to En-rogel.

[17] From there it curved north to En-shemesh, ran to Geliloth across from the Ascent of Adummim, then dropped to the Stone of Bohan son of Reuben.

[18] It passed along the northern slope facing the Arabah and descended into the Arabah.

[19] Then the border ran along the northern slope of Beth-hoglah and ended at the north bay of the Salt Sea, at the south end of the Jordan. That was the southern border.

[20] The Jordan formed the eastern border. This was the inheritance of the children of Benjamin, with its borders all around — clan by clan.

[21] The cities of the tribe of Benjamin, clan by clan, were: Jericho, Beth-hoglah, Emek-keziz,

[22] Beth-arabah, Zemaraim, Bethel,

[23] Avvim, Parah, Ophrah,

[24] Chephar-ammoni, Ophni, and Geba — twelve cities, with their villages.

[25] Also Gibeon, Ramah, Beeroth,

[26] Mizpeh, Chephirah, Mozah,

[27] Rekem, Irpeel, Taralah,

[3] Hazar-shual, Balah, Ezem,

[4] Eltolad, Bethul, Hormah,

[5] Ziklag, Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susah,
