[11] It climbed westward to Maralah, brushed Dabbesheth, and reached the ravine east of Jokneam.

[12] From Sarid it turned east toward the sunrise, to the border of Chisloth-tabor, then out to Daberath and up to Japhia.

[13] From there it crossed eastward to Gath-hepher and Eth-kazin, came out at Rimmon, and bent toward Neah.

[14] The boundary then circled north of Hannathon and ended in the Valley of Iphtah-el,

[15] including Kattath, Nahalal, Shimron, Idalah, and Bethlehem — twelve towns with their villages.

[16] This was the inheritance of the tribe of Zebulun by its clans — these towns and their villages.

[17] The fourth lot came up for Issachar, for the tribe of Issachar by its clans.

[18] Their territory included: Jezreel, Chesulloth, Shunem,

[19] Hapharaim, Shion, Anaharath,

[20] Rabbith, Kishion, Ebez,

[21] Remeth, En-gannim, En-haddah, and Beth-pazzez.

[22] The boundary touched Tabor, Shahazumah, and Beth-shemesh, and ended at the Jordan — sixteen towns with their villages.

[23] This was the inheritance of the tribe of Issachar by its clans — the towns and their villages.

[24] The fifth lot came up for the tribe of Asher by its clans.

[25] Their territory included: Helkath, Hali, Beten, Achshaph,

[26] Allammelech, Amad, and Mishal. On the west it reached Carmel and Shihor-libnath.

[27] Then it turned eastward to Beth-dagon, touched Zebulun and the Valley of Iphtah-el on the north, ran up to Beth-emek and Neiel, and continued north to Cabul.

[28] It included Ebron, Rehob, Hammon, and Kanah, all the way to Great Sidon.

[29] The boundary swung back toward Ramah and the fortified city of Tyre, turned toward Hosah, and ended at the sea in the region of Achzib,

[30] along with Ummah, Aphek, and Rehob — twenty-two towns with their villages.

[31] This was the inheritance of the tribe of Asher by its clans — these towns and their villages.

[32] The sixth lot came up for Naphtali, for the tribe of Naphtali by its clans.

[33] Their boundary ran from Heleph and the oak at Zaanannim, through Adami-nekeb and Jabneel, to Lakkum, and ended at the Jordan.

[34] It turned west to Aznoth-tabor, then continued to Hukkok. It touched Zebulun on the south, Asher on the west, and Judah at the Jordan on the east.

[35] The fortified towns were Ziddim, Zer, Hammath, Rakkath, Chinnereth,

[36] Adamah, Ramah, Hazor,

[37] Kedesh, Edrei, En-hazor,

[38] Iron, Migdal-el, Horem, Beth-anath, and Beth-shemesh — nineteen towns with their villages.

[39] This was the inheritance of the tribe of Naphtali by its clans — the towns and their villages.

[40] The seventh lot came up for the tribe of Dan by its clans.

[41] The territory of their inheritance included Zorah, Eshtaol, Ir-shemesh,

[42] Shaalabbin, Aijalon, Ithlah,

[43] Elon, Timnah, Ekron,

[44] Eltekeh, Gibbethon, Baalath,

[45] Jehud, Bene-berak, Gath-rimmon,

[46] Me-jarkon, and Rakkon, along with the territory facing Joppa.

[47] But Dan's territory slipped from their grasp. So the Danites marched north, attacked Leshem, took it by the sword, and seized it. They settled there and renamed Leshem after their ancestor Dan.

[48] This was the inheritance of the tribe of Dan by its clans — these towns and their villages.

[49] When they finished dividing the land by its borders, the Israelites gave Joshua son of Nun an inheritance among them.

[50] At the LORD's command they gave him the town he asked for — Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim. He rebuilt it and made his home there.

[51] These were the inheritances that Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the family heads of the Israelite tribes assigned by lot at Shiloh, before the LORD, at the entrance of the tent of meeting. And so the dividing of the land was finished.

[1] The LORD spoke to Joshua:

[2] "Tell Israel — designate the cities of refuge I described through Moses.

[3] A place where someone who kills accidentally, without intent, can run. Sanctuary from the avenger of blood.

[4] When he reaches one of these cities, he stops at the entrance gate and lays out his case before the elders of that city. They take him in, give him a place among them, and he lives there.

[5] If the avenger of blood comes hunting him, they will not hand him over — because the killing was unintentional, no prior hatred between them.

[6] He stays in that city until he stands trial before the assembly, until the death of whoever is high priest at that time. Then he can go home — back to the town he fled from."

[7] So they set apart Kedesh in Galilee, up in the hills of Naphtali. Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim. Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — in the hills of Judah.

[8] And east of the Jordan, across from Jericho: Bezer out in the wilderness, up on the plateau, from the tribe of Reuben. Ramoth in Gilead, from Gad. Golan in Bashan, from Manasseh.

[9] These were the appointed cities for every Israelite and for any outsider living among them — anywhere a person could run after killing someone by accident, escape the avenger of blood, and get a fair hearing before the assembly.

[1] The heads of the Levite households came to Eleazar the priest, to Joshua son of Nun, and to the family heads of the Israelite tribes [2] at Shiloh in Canaan, and made their case: "The LORD commanded through Moses that we be given towns to live in, with pastureland for our livestock."

[3] So the Israelites, following the LORD's order, gave the Levites these towns and pastures out of their own inheritance.

[4] The lot came up first for the Kohathite clans. The Levites descended from Aaron the priest drew thirteen towns from the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin. [5] The rest of the Kohathites drew ten towns from the clans of Ephraim, Dan, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.

[6] The Gershonites drew thirteen towns from the clans of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and the half-tribe of Manasseh in Bashan.

[7] The Merarites, clan by clan, drew twelve towns from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.

[8] So Israel assigned these towns and their pasturelands to the Levites by lot, exactly as the LORD had commanded through Moses.

[9] From Judah and Simeon they handed over the following towns, each named here: [10] These went to the descendants of Aaron from the Kohathite clans of Levi, since the first lot fell to them.

[11] They received Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the hill country of Judah, along with its surrounding pastures. Arba was the ancestor of Anak. [12] But the fields and villages around the town had already been given to Caleb son of Jephunneh as his holding.

[13] To the descendants of Aaron the priest they gave Hebron — a city of refuge for anyone guilty of manslaughter — along with its pastures, plus Libnah, [14] Jattir, Eshtemoa, [15] Holon, Debir, [16] Ain, Juttah, and Beth-shemesh — nine towns with their pastures, drawn from these two tribes.

[17] From the tribe of Benjamin: Gibeon, Geba, [18] Anathoth, and Almon — four towns with their pastures.

[19] All together, the towns of Aaron's priestly line came to thirteen, each with its surrounding land.

[20] The rest of the Kohathite clans — Levites not descended from Aaron — drew their lot from the tribe of Ephraim. [21] They received Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim — a city of refuge for the manslayer — along with Gezer, [22] Kibzaim, and Beth-horon — four towns with their pastures.

[23] From the tribe of Dan: Elteke, Gibbethon, [24] Aijalon, and Gath-rimmon — four towns with their pastures.

[25] From the half-tribe of Manasseh: Taanach and Gath-rimmon — two towns with their pastures.

[26] In total, the remaining Kohathite clans received ten towns with their pastures.

[27] The Gershonite clans of the Levites received from the half-tribe of Manasseh: Golan in Bashan — a city of refuge for the manslayer — and Beeshterah — two towns with their pastures.

[28] From the tribe of Issachar: Kishion, Daberath, [29] Jarmuth, and En-gannim — four towns with their pastures.

[30] From the tribe of Asher: Mishal, Abdon, [31] Helkath, and Rehob — four towns with their pastures.

[32] From the tribe of Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee — a city of refuge for the manslayer — along with Hammoth-dor and Kartan — three towns with their pastures.

[33] All told, the Gershonite clans had thirteen towns with their pastures.

[34] To the rest of the Levites — the Merarite clans — they gave, from the tribe of Zebulun: Jokneam, Kartah, [35] Dimnah, and Nahalal — four towns with their pastures.

[36] From the tribe of Reuben: Bezer, Jahaz, [37] Kedemoth, and Mephaath — four towns with their pastures.

[38] From the tribe of Gad: Ramoth in Gilead — a city of refuge for the manslayer — and Mahanaim, [39] Heshbon, and Jazer — four towns in all, with their pastures.

[40] In total, the Merarite clans — the remaining Levites — received twelve towns by lot.

[41] All the Levite towns within Israelite territory came to forty-eight, each with its surrounding pastureland. [42] Every one of these towns had its own pasture ringing it — the same pattern, town after town.

[43] So the LORD gave Israel every inch of land he had sworn to their ancestors. They took it, and they settled it. [44] He gave them rest on every side, just as he had promised their forefathers. Not a single enemy could stand against them — the LORD delivered every adversary into their hands. [45] Of all the good things the LORD had promised the house of Israel, not one word fell short. Every promise landed.

[1] Then Joshua called the men of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh together [2] and told them: "You have done everything Moses, the servant of the LORD, commanded you. You listened to me in everything I asked. [3] All this long stretch of time — right up to today — you have not abandoned your brothers. You have kept the charge the LORD your God laid on you. [4] Now the LORD your God has given your brothers rest, exactly as he promised. So turn around. Go home. Go to your own land — the territory Moses gave you across the Jordan. [5] But guard this carefully: live out the command and the law Moses gave you. Love the LORD your God. Walk every road he walks. Keep his commands. Hold tight to him. Serve him with everything you are — every heartbeat, every breath."

[6] Then Joshua blessed them and sent them off, and they headed home.

[7] To half the tribe of Manasseh, Moses had given land in Bashan; to the other half, Joshua had given land west of the Jordan with their brothers. As Joshua sent them off with his blessing, [8] he said: "Go home rich — herds piled high, silver, gold, bronze, iron, mountains of clothing. Share the plunder of your enemies with your brothers."

[9] So the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh left the rest of Israel at Shiloh in Canaan and headed back to Gilead — the land that belonged to them, the land Moses had assigned them by the word of the LORD.

[10] But when they reached Geliloth, on the Canaan side of the Jordan, the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh built an altar there by the river — a massive, impressive altar.

[11] Word reached the other Israelites: "Look — the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar right at the edge of Canaan, at Geliloth by the Jordan, on the Israelite side." [12] When Israel heard it, the entire assembly gathered at Shiloh to march out and make war on them.

[13] First they sent Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest across to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh in Gilead. [14] With him went ten chiefs — one from each tribal house in Israel, each man the head of his clan. [15] They went to Gilead, to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh, and confronted them:

[16] "This is what the whole assembly of the LORD demands to know: What is this betrayal you have committed against the God of Israel? Turning your backs on the LORD today by building yourselves an altar — rebellion against the LORD right now, today! [17] Was the sin at Peor not enough? We still have not washed ourselves clean of it — and a plague struck the LORD's own people because of it. [18] And now you turn your backs on the LORD again? If you rebel against the LORD today, tomorrow his fury will fall on the whole assembly of Israel. [19] If the land you possess is unclean, then cross over into the LORD's land — where his tabernacle stands — and take ground among us. But do not rebel against the LORD. Do not rebel against us by building yourselves another altar besides the altar of the LORD our God. [20] When Achan son of Zerah committed his treachery with the devoted things, wrath fell on all Israel. He was not the only one who died for his sin."

[21] Then the Reubenites, Gadites, and half-tribe of Manasseh answered the heads of Israel's clans:

[22] "The Mighty One. God. The LORD. The Mighty One. God. The LORD — he knows! And let Israel know too! If this was rebellion, if this was betrayal of the LORD — do not spare us today. [23] If we built that altar to turn from the LORD, or to offer burnt offerings and grain offerings and peace offerings on it — may the LORD himself call us to account.

[24] "No — we did this out of fear. Fear of what your children might one day say to ours: 'What do you have to do with the LORD, the God of Israel? [25] The LORD set a border between us and you, sons of Reuben, sons of Gad — the Jordan. You have no share in the LORD.' And your children would shut our children out from worshiping the LORD.

[26] "So we said: let us build an altar — not for burnt offerings, not for sacrifices, [27] but as a witness between us and you, and between the generations to come, that we too have the right to worship the LORD in his presence — with our burnt offerings, our sacrifices, our peace offerings. So your children can never say to ours, 'You have no share in the LORD.'

[28] "We thought: if they ever do say that to us or our descendants, we will answer, 'Look at this replica of the LORD's altar that our fathers made — not for burnt offerings, not for sacrifices, but as a witness between us and you.'

[29] "Rebel against the LORD? Turn from him today by building an altar for burnt offerings and grain offerings and sacrifices — apart from the altar of the LORD our God that stands before his tabernacle? Never. Never us."

[30] When Phinehas the priest and the leaders of the assembly — the heads of Israel's clans — heard what the Reubenites, Gadites, and Manassites said, they were satisfied. [31] Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest told them: "Today we know the LORD is among us — because you did not commit this treachery against him. You have rescued the Israelites from the LORD's hand."

[32] Then Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest and the leaders left the Reubenites and Gadites in Gilead and returned to Canaan, to the Israelites, and brought back the report. [33] The report pleased Israel. They blessed God and dropped all talk of war — no more marching out to devastate the land where the Reubenites and Gadites lived.

[34] The Reubenites and Gadites named the altar: A Witness Between Us — That the LORD is God.

[1] Years passed. The LORD had given Israel rest from every enemy ringing them in. Joshua was old now, deep into his years.

[2] So he summoned them all — Israel's elders, its chiefs, its judges, its officers — and said: "I'm old. My days are nearly spent.

[3] You've seen with your own eyes what the LORD your God did to every one of these nations on your account. He's the one who fought for you — your God himself.

[4] Look — I've allotted these remaining nations to you as an inheritance, tribe by tribe, along with all the ones I've already cut down, everything from the Jordan to the Great Sea in the west.

[5] The LORD your God will drive them out before you. He'll shove them out of your sight, and you will take their land — exactly as he promised.

[6] So hold the line. Be relentless in keeping everything written in the book of Moses' instruction. Don't drift from it — not right, not left.

[7] Don't mix with these nations still left among you. Don't even speak the names of their gods. Don't swear by them. Don't serve them. Don't bow to them.

[8] Cling to the LORD your God — the way you've done up to this day.

[9] The LORD has driven out nations vast and powerful in front of you. To this day, not a soul has been able to stand against you.

[10] One of you chases a thousand — because the LORD your God himself is the one fighting for you, exactly as he promised.

[11] So watch yourselves. Guard your souls. Love the LORD your God.

[12] But if you turn back — if you bond yourself to what's left of these nations among you, intermarry with them, blend your lives with theirs and theirs with yours —

[13] then know this for certain: the LORD your God will stop driving them out for you. They'll become a snare, a trap, a whip across your ribs, thorns in your eyes — until you vanish from this good ground the LORD your God has given you.

[14] Today I'm walking the road every mortal walks. And you know this in your bones, every part of you: not one promise the LORD your God made you has failed. Every word landed. Not one fell to the ground.

[15] But just as every good word he spoke has come true for you, so the LORD can bring every evil word down on you — until he wipes you off this good ground he gave you.

[16] If you break the covenant the LORD your God commanded you — if you go and serve other gods and bow down to them — the LORD's fury will blaze against you, and you will disappear, fast, from the good land he gave you."

[1] Joshua gathered every tribe of Israel to Shechem. He summoned the elders, the leaders, the judges, the officers — they presented themselves before God.

[2] Joshua spoke to all the people: "This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: 'Long ago your ancestors lived on the far side of the Euphrates — Terah, the father of Abraham and Nahor — and they served other gods.

[3] I took your father Abraham from beyond that river and led him across the whole land of Canaan. I multiplied his descendants. I gave him Isaac.

[4] To Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. I handed Esau the hill country of Seir to possess, but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.

[5] Then I sent Moses and Aaron. I struck Egypt with everything I did inside it — and after that, I brought you out.

[6] I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, and you came to the sea. The Egyptians chased your ancestors with chariots and horsemen all the way to the Red Sea.

[7] They cried out to the LORD, and he set darkness between you and the Egyptians. He brought the sea crashing down on them and buried them. Your own eyes saw what I did in Egypt. Then you lived in the wilderness for a long stretch of years.

[8] I brought you into the land of the Amorites who lived east of the Jordan. They fought you. I handed them over. You took their land. I wiped them out in front of you.

[9] Then Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, rose up to fight Israel. He sent for Balaam son of Beor to curse you —

[10] but I refused to listen to Balaam. He blessed you instead, again and again. I rescued you from his hand.

[11] You crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho. The men of Jericho fought you — Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, Jebusites — and I handed every one of them over to you.

[12] I sent the hornet ahead of you. It drove them out before you — the two kings of the Amorites. Not your sword. Not your bow.

[13] I gave you a land you did not work for, cities you did not build — and you live in them. Vineyards and olive groves you did not plant — and you eat from them.'

[14] So now: fear the LORD. Serve him with everything in you, in truth. Throw out the gods your ancestors served beyond the Euphrates and in Egypt. Serve the LORD.

[15] And if serving the LORD seems wrong to you — choose today whom you will serve. The gods your ancestors served back across the river? The gods of the Amorites whose land you now live on? Choose. But as for me and my household — we will serve the LORD."

[16] The people answered: "Never. We would never abandon the LORD to serve other gods.

[17] The LORD our God — he is the one who brought us and our ancestors up out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery. He performed those great signs right in front of our eyes. He guarded us on every road we traveled, through every nation we passed.

[18] The LORD drove out all those peoples, including the Amorites who lived in this land. We too will serve the LORD. He is our God."

[19] Joshua said to the people: "You cannot serve the LORD. He is a holy God. He is a jealous God. He will not forgive your rebellions and your sins.

[20] If you abandon the LORD and serve foreign gods, he will turn on you. He will bring ruin on you and finish you — after all the good he has done for you."

[21] The people answered Joshua: "No. We will serve the LORD."

[22] Joshua said: "You are witnesses against yourselves — you have chosen the LORD, to serve him." They said: "Witnesses."

[23] "Then throw out the foreign gods among you. Turn your hearts to the LORD, the God of Israel."

[24] The people answered Joshua: "The LORD our God we will serve. His voice we will obey."

[25] That day Joshua cut a covenant for the people at Shechem. He set down for them a statute and a ruling.

[26] Joshua wrote these words into the book of the law of God. He took a massive stone and set it up there under the oak in the sanctuary of the LORD.

[27] He told all the people: "Look — this stone is a witness against us. It has heard every word the LORD spoke to us. It will be a witness against you, so you cannot lie to your God."

[28] Then Joshua sent the people away, each to his own inheritance.

[29] After all this, Joshua son of Nun, servant of the LORD, died — a hundred and ten years old.

[30] They buried him within the borders of his inheritance at Timnath-serah, in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.

[31] Israel served the LORD throughout the lifetime of Joshua and throughout the lifetime of the elders who outlived him — those who had seen with their own eyes everything the LORD had done for Israel.

[32] The bones of Joseph, which the Israelites had carried up from Egypt, they buried at Shechem — in the plot of ground Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for a hundred pieces of silver. It became the inheritance of Joseph's descendants.

[33] Eleazar son of Aaron also died. They buried him at Gibeah, the town that had been given to his son Phinehas in the hill country of Ephraim.

[1] From Jude, a slave of Jesus the Messiah and brother of James — to those who are called, who are loved by God the Father and kept safe in Jesus the Messiah:

[2] Mercy, peace, and love — may they flood over you.

[3] My beloved, I was eager to write to you about the salvation we share, but I find I must instead write urging you to fight for the faith — the faith handed down once and for all to God's holy people.

[4] Certain men have slipped in among you unnoticed — long ago marked out for this judgment. They are godless. They twist the grace of our God into a license for filth, and they deny our only Master and Lord, Jesus the Messiah.

[5] You already know all this, but let me remind you: the Lord rescued his people out of Egypt — and afterward destroyed the ones who refused to believe.

[6] And the angels — the ones who would not stay in their assigned place but abandoned their proper home — he has locked them in everlasting chains, in darkness, awaiting the judgment of the great Day.

[7] Same with Sodom and Gomorrah and the towns nearby. They gave themselves over to sexual corruption, chased after flesh that was not theirs — and now they lie there as the public exhibit of what eternal fire looks like.

[8] Yet these dreamers do the very same thing. They defile their bodies. They despise authority. They sneer at glorious beings.

[9] Even Michael the archangel — when he was disputing with the devil over Moses' body — did not dare to throw a slanderous verdict at him. All he said was, "The Lord rebuke you."

[10] But these people slander whatever they do not understand. And whatever they do understand — by raw instinct, like animals — is exactly what destroys them.

[11] Hell awaits them. They have walked the road of Cain. They have thrown themselves headlong into Balaam's error for profit. They have perished in Korah's rebellion.

[12] They are the hidden reefs at your love feasts, eating shamelessly alongside you, feeding only themselves. Clouds without rain, blown along by the wind. Trees in late autumn — no fruit, twice dead, uprooted.

[13] Wild waves of the sea, foaming up their own disgrace. Wandering stars, for whom the blackest darkness has been kept in reserve forever.

[14] Enoch — the seventh from Adam — prophesied about these men too: "Look — the Lord comes with thousands upon thousands of his holy ones,

[15] to bring judgment on everyone, to convict every soul of every godless deed they committed in their godlessness, and of every harsh word godless sinners have spoken against him."

[16] These are the grumblers, the complainers, chasing their own appetites. Their mouths spew arrogance. They flatter people when there is something to be gained.

[17] But you, my beloved — remember what the apostles of our Lord Jesus the Messiah said would happen.

[18] They told you: "In the last days, mockers will come, chasing after their own godless cravings."

[19] These are the ones who split the community, ruled by instinct, with no Spirit in them.

[20] But you, my beloved — build yourselves up on your most holy faith. Pray in the Holy Spirit.

[21] Keep yourselves anchored in the love of God as you wait for the mercy of our Lord Jesus the Messiah, which leads to eternal life.

[22] Have mercy on those who waver.

[23] Snatch others out of the fire and save them. To still others, show mercy — but with fear, hating even the clothing stained by their flesh.

[24] Now to the One who can keep you from falling and present you before his glorious presence — flawless and overflowing with joy —

[25] to the only God our Savior, through Jesus the Messiah our Lord, be glory, majesty, power, and authority — before all time, now, and into every age to come. Amen.

[1] After Joshua died, Israel put the question to the LORD: "Who goes up first to fight the Canaanites for us?"

[2] The LORD answered: "Judah goes. I've handed the land over to him."

[3] Judah turned to his brother Simeon: "Come up with me into my allotted territory. We'll fight the Canaanites together, and I'll go with you into yours." So Simeon went with him.

[4] Judah marched out, and the LORD delivered the Canaanites and Perizzites into their hands. At Bezek they cut down ten thousand men.

[5] They ran into Adoni-Bezek there and took him on, hammering the Canaanites and Perizzites.

[6] Adoni-Bezek ran. They chased him down, caught him, and hacked off his thumbs and big toes.

[7] Adoni-Bezek said: "Seventy kings with their thumbs and big toes cut off used to scavenge scraps under my table. What I did, God has done back to me." They hauled him to Jerusalem, and there he died.

[8] The men of Judah attacked Jerusalem and took it — put it to the sword and torched the city.

[9] After that, Judah headed south to fight the Canaanites holding the hill country, the Negev, and the western foothills.

[10] Judah moved against the Canaanites in Hebron — formerly called Kiriath-Arba — and struck down Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai.

[11] From there they marched on the inhabitants of Debir, once known as Kiriath-Sepher.

[12] Caleb threw down a challenge: "Whoever attacks Kiriath-Sepher and captures it — I'll give him my daughter Acsah as his wife."

[13] Othniel, son of Caleb's younger brother Kenaz, captured it. So Caleb gave him Acsah.

[14] When she came to him, she pressed him to ask her father for a field. As she dismounted from her donkey, Caleb asked, "What is it you want?"

[15] She said, "Give me a blessing. You've placed me in the dry south — give me springs of water too." So Caleb gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

[16] The descendants of Moses' father-in-law, the Kenite, went up from the City of Palms with the people of Judah into the wilderness of Judah south of Arad, and they settled in among the people there.

[17] Then Judah went with his brother Simeon, and they cut down the Canaanites living in Zephath. They devoted the city to destruction and renamed it Hormah — "Annihilation."

[18] Judah also took Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron, each with the territory around it.

[19] The LORD was with Judah. He took the hill country. But the people of the plains — Judah couldn't drive them out. They had iron chariots.

[20] As Moses had promised, Hebron went to Caleb, who drove out the three sons of Anak from it.

[21] The Benjaminites, though — they never dislodged the Jebusites living in Jerusalem. The Jebusites have lived there alongside Benjamin ever since.

[22] The house of Joseph went up against Bethel, and the LORD went with them.

[23] They sent scouts to Bethel — a city once known as Luz.

[24] The scouts spotted a man coming out of the city. "Show us a way in," they said, "and we'll treat you kindly."

[25] He showed them how to get in. They put the city to the sword but let the man and his whole family go.

[26] The man traveled to the land of the Hittites, founded a city, and named it Luz. That's still its name.

[27] Manasseh didn't drive out the people of Beth-Shean, Taanach, Dor, Ibleam, or Megiddo, or their surrounding villages. The Canaanites dug in and stayed in that land.

[28] When Israel finally grew strong, they put the Canaanites to forced labor — but never drove them out completely.

[1] The messenger of the LORD climbed up from Gilgal to Bochim and said: "I pulled you out of Egypt. I led you into the land I swore to your fathers. They wouldn't give up a single one of their practices, wouldn't let go of their stubborn ways.

[20] So the LORD's anger blazed against Israel, and he said: "Because this nation has violated the covenant I commanded their fathers — because they will not listen to my voice — [21] I will no longer drive out before them any of the nations Joshua left when he died. [1] These are the nations the LORD left in place to test Israel — everyone who hadn't lived through the wars for Canaan. [3] The five Philistine warlords, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites who held the Lebanon range from Mount Baal-hermon to Lebo-hamath. [9] Then Israel cried out to the LORD, and the LORD raised up a rescuer for them: Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother. [10] The Spirit of the LORD came over him. [15] Then Israel cried out to the LORD, and the LORD raised up a rescuer: Ehud son of Gera, a Benjaminite — a left-handed man. The Israelites sent him with their tribute to Eglon king of Moab. [16] Ehud forged himself a double-edged dagger, a foot and a half long, and strapped it to his right thigh under his clothes. [17] He brought the tribute to Eglon, king of Moab — and Eglon was an enormously fat man. [18] After Ehud presented the tribute, he sent away the men who had carried it. [19] But he himself turned back at the stone idols near Gilgal and said, "I have a secret message for you, O king."

The king said, "Silence!" — and every attendant left the room.

[20] Ehud approached him as he sat alone in his cool upper chamber. Ehud said, "I have a message from God for you." The king rose from his throne. [21] Then Ehud reached with his left hand, drew the dagger from his right thigh, and drove it into Eglon's belly. [22] The hilt went in after the blade. The fat closed over it. Ehud didn't pull the sword out. The filth came out. [23] Ehud slipped out to the porch, shut the doors of the upper room behind him, and locked them.

[8] Barak said, "If you go with me, I'll go. If you won't go with me, I won't go."

[9] "Fine," she said. "I'll go with you. But understand this: the glory of this campaign will not be yours. The LORD is going to deliver Sisera into the hands of a woman." Then Deborah got up and went with Barak to Kedesh.

[14] Deborah said to Barak: "Move. This is the day. The LORD has handed Sisera over to you. The LORD himself has gone out ahead of you." Barak came down from Mount Tabor with his ten thousand behind him.

[17] But Sisera ran — straight to the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite. There was peace between Jabin's house and Heber's clan.

[18] Jael stepped out to meet him. "Come in, my lord. Come in. Don't be afraid." He went in. She covered him with a rug.

[19] He said, "Please — a little water. I'm thirsty." She opened a skin of milk, gave him a drink, and covered him again.

[20] He told her, "Stand at the tent opening. If anyone comes asking whether there's a man inside, say no."

[21] Then Jael, Heber's wife, took a tent peg in one hand, a hammer in the other, crept up to him as he slept — exhausted — and drove the peg through his temple into the ground. He never woke. He died.

[22] Just then Barak came chasing after Sisera. Jael went out to meet him. "Come," she said. "I'll show you the man you're looking for." He stepped inside — and there was Sisera, sprawled out dead, the peg through his skull.

[23] That day God broke Jabin, king of Canaan, before the Israelites.

[24] And Israel's hand pressed down on him — harder, harder — until Jabin, king of Canaan, was finished.

[1] That day Deborah and Barak son of Abinoam sang this song:

[2] When leaders lead in Israel, when the people answer the call — bless the LORD!

And the land had rest for forty years.

[1] Israel did evil in the LORD's sight again, and he handed them over to Midian for seven years.

[2] Midian's grip crushed Israel. To escape them, the Israelites carved out hideouts in the mountains—caves and rock fortresses.

[3] Every time Israel planted, Midian came up. Midian, Amalek, the people of the east—they swept in together.

[4] They camped on Israel's land and ravaged the crops all the way to Gaza. They left nothing alive in Israel—no sheep, no ox, no donkey.

[5] They came up like a swarm of locusts, men and camels beyond counting, invading the land to destroy it.

[6] Midian ground Israel into the dust. So Israel cried out to the LORD.

[7] When the Israelites cried out to the LORD because of Midian,

[10] I told you, 'I am the LORD your God. Do not worship the gods of the Amorites whose land you now live in.' But you would not listen to me."

[11] Then the angel of the LORD came and sat under the oak at Ophrah, which belonged to Joash the Abiezrite. His son Gideon was threshing wheat in a winepress—hiding it from Midian.

[12] The angel of the LORD appeared to him and said, "The LORD is with you, mighty warrior."

[13] Gideon answered, "Please, sir—if the LORD is with us, then why has all this happened to us? Where are all his wonders our fathers told us about—how he brought us up from Egypt? Now the LORD has abandoned us and dropped us into Midian's fist."

[14] The LORD turned to him and said, "Go with the strength you have, and rescue Israel from Midian. I am sending you."

[15] Gideon said, "Please, Lord—how can I rescue Israel? My clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the runt of my father's house."

[16] The LORD said, "I will be with you. You will strike down Midian as if they were a single man."

[17] Gideon said, "If I've found favor with you, give me a sign that it's really you speaking to me.

[18] Don't leave until I come back with my offering and set it before you." He said, "I'll wait until you return."

[19] Gideon went in and prepared a young goat and unleavened bread from an ephah of flour. He put the meat in a basket, the broth in a pot, and brought them out to him under the oak. He set them down.

[20] The angel of God said to him, "Take the meat and the unleavened bread, place them on this rock, and pour out the broth." Gideon did it.

[21] The angel of the LORD reached out with the tip of the staff in his hand and touched the meat and the bread. Fire leaped up from the rock and consumed it all. Then the angel of the LORD vanished from his sight.

[22] Gideon realized—this was the angel of the LORD. He gasped, "Oh no—Sovereign LORD! I have seen the angel of the LORD face to face!"

[23] But the LORD said to him, "Peace. Don't be afraid. You will not die."

[24] Gideon built an altar there to the LORD and called it "The LORD is Peace." To this day it stands in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

[25] That same night the LORD said to him, "Take your father's bull—the second one, seven years old. Tear down the altar of Baal that belongs to your father, and cut down the Asherah pole beside it.

[26] Build a proper altar to the LORD your God on top of this stronghold. Then take the second bull and offer it as a burnt offering, using the wood of the Asherah pole you cut down."

[27] So Gideon took ten of his servants and did what the LORD told him. But he was too afraid of his father's household and the men of the town to do it by day—so he did it at night.

[28] When the men of the town got up in the morning, there it was: Baal's altar smashed, the Asherah beside it chopped down, and the second bull offered up on the newly built altar.

[29] They said to each other, "Who did this?" They investigated, asked around, and concluded: "Gideon son of Joash did it."

[30] The men of the town demanded of Joash, "Bring out your son. He has to die. He tore down Baal's altar and cut down the Asherah beside it."

[31] But Joash said to the mob pressing against him, "Are you going to fight Baal's battles? Are you going to save him? Anyone who fights for Baal will be dead by morning. If Baal is really a god, let him defend himself—it was his altar that got torn down."

[32] So that day they named Gideon "Jerubbaal"—meaning, Let Baal contend with him—because he tore down Baal's altar.

[33] Then all Midian and Amalek and the people of the east joined forces, crossed the Jordan, and camped in the Valley of Jezreel.

[34] The Spirit of the LORD clothed Gideon. He blew the trumpet, and the Abiezrites rallied behind him.

[35] He sent messengers throughout Manasseh, and they too came to him. He sent messengers to Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali, and they marched up to meet him.

[36] Gideon said to God, "If you really are going to rescue Israel through me, as you said—

[38] And that's what happened. He got up early the next morning, squeezed the fleece, and wrung out a bowl full of water.

[39] Then Gideon said to God, "Don't be angry with me. Let me ask just once more. Let me test once more with the fleece—this time, let the fleece be dry, and let the ground be covered with dew."

[40] God did it that night. The fleece alone was dry; dew covered all the ground.

[1] Jerubbaal — that is, Gideon — was up before dawn with everyone who followed him, and they camped at the spring of Harod. The Midianite camp lay to their north, in the valley below the hill of Moreh.

[7] The LORD said to Gideon, "With these three hundred who lapped, I will rescue you and deliver Midian into your hand. Send the rest home."

[25] They said, "Gladly." They spread out a cloak, and every man tossed in an earring from his plunder.

[26] The weight of the gold earrings he asked for came to seventeen hundred shekels of gold — not counting the crescents, the pendants, the purple robes worn by the kings of Midian, or the chains from the necks of their camels.

[27] Gideon made it into an ephod and set it up in his town of Ophrah. All Israel prostituted themselves to it there. It became a trap for Gideon and his house.

[28] So Midian was crushed before the Israelites and never lifted its head again. The land had quiet for forty years, all the days of Gideon.

[29] Jerubbaal son of Joash went home and lived in his own house.

[30] Gideon had seventy sons of his own — born from him, because he had many wives.

[31] His concubine in Shechem also bore him a son, and he named him Abimelech.

[32] Gideon son of Joash died at a good old age and was buried in the tomb of Joash his father in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

[33] But the moment Gideon was in the ground, the Israelites turned. They prostituted themselves to the Baals and made Baal-Berith their god.

[34] They did not remember Yahweh their God — the one who had rescued them from the hand of every enemy on every side.

[35] And they showed no loyalty to the household of Jerubbaal — Gideon — for all the good he had done for Israel.

[1] Abimelech, son of Jerubbaal, went to Shechem — to his mother's brothers and the whole clan of his mother's family — and made his pitch:

[2] "Ask every man of Shechem this: what's better for you — seventy men, all sons of Jerubbaal, ruling over you, or one man? And remember — I'm your bone and your blood."

[3] His mother's brothers carried the message to the leaders of Shechem, word for word. And their hearts swung toward Abimelech. They said, "He's our brother."

[4] So they handed him seventy pieces of silver out of the temple of Baal-Berith, and Abimelech used it to hire a crew of reckless, worthless men who followed him wherever he went.

[5] He marched to his father's house in Ophrah and slaughtered his brothers — Jerubbaal's seventy sons — on a single stone. Only Jotham, the youngest, survived. He had hidden himself.

[6] Then all the leaders of Shechem and the whole house of Millo gathered at the oak by the pillar in Shechem and crowned Abimelech king.

[7] Word reached Jotham. He climbed to the top of Mount Gerizim, lifted his voice, and shouted down to them: "Listen to me, men of Shechem — and may God listen to you.

[8] Once the trees set out to anoint a king over themselves. They said to the olive tree, 'Reign over us.'

[9] But the olive tree answered, 'Should I give up my rich oil — by which gods and men are honored — just to sway above the trees?'

[10] So the trees said to the fig, 'You come and reign over us.'

[11] But the fig tree said, 'Should I abandon my sweetness, my excellent fruit, just to sway above the trees?'

[12] So the trees said to the vine, 'You come and reign over us.'

[13] And the vine said, 'Should I forsake my wine — the joy of gods and men — just to sway above the trees?'

[14] Then all the trees said to the thornbush, 'You come and reign over us.'

[15] And the thornbush told the trees: 'If you're truly anointing me king over you, then come — take shelter in my shade. But if not — let fire come out of the thornbush and devour the cedars of Lebanon.'

[16] So now — if you acted in good faith and integrity when you made Abimelech king, if you've treated Jerubbaal and his house well, if you've given him what his hands deserved —

[17] my father fought for you, threw his life on the line, and rescued you from Midian's grip.

[18] But today you've risen up against my father's house. You butchered his sons — seventy men on one stone — and crowned Abimelech, son of his slave-girl, king over the leaders of Shechem, because he's your brother.

[19] If today you've acted in good faith and integrity toward Jerubbaal and his house — then enjoy Abimelech, and may he enjoy you.

[20] But if not — let fire blaze out of Abimelech and consume the leaders of Shechem and the house of Millo, and let fire blaze out of the leaders of Shechem and the house of Millo and consume Abimelech."

[21] Then Jotham bolted. He fled to Beer and settled there, beyond the reach of his brother Abimelech.

[22] Abimelech ruled Israel for three years.

[23] Then God sent a poisonous spirit between Abimelech and the leaders of Shechem, and the leaders of Shechem turned on Abimelech —

[24] so that the violence done to Jerubbaal's seventy sons would come home, their blood landing on Abimelech, their brother who killed them, and on the leaders of Shechem who put the strength in his hand to do it.

[25] The leaders of Shechem set ambushers in the hills against him, robbing everyone who passed by on the road. Word reached Abimelech.

[26] Then Gaal son of Ebed arrived in Shechem with his brothers — and the leaders of Shechem put their trust in him.

[27] They went out into the fields, gathered the grapes, trod them, and threw a festival. They entered the temple of their god, ate and drank, and cursed Abimelech.

[28] And Gaal son of Ebed said, "Who is Abimelech, and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? Isn't he the son of Jerubbaal? And isn't Zebul his deputy? Serve the men of Hamor, Shechem's father — why should we serve Abimelech?

[29] If only this people were under my hand — I'd get rid of Abimelech." Then he sent word to Abimelech: "Beef up your army and come out."

[30] When Zebul, the ruler of the city, heard Gaal son of Ebed's words, his anger flared.

[31] He sent messengers to Abimelech in secret: "Look — Gaal son of Ebed and his brothers have come to Shechem, and they're stirring up the city against you.

[32] So get up tonight, you and your men, and lie in ambush in the fields.

[33] In the morning, the moment the sun rises, charge the city. When Gaal and his men come out against you, do to him whatever your hand finds to do."

[34] So Abimelech rose by night, his whole force with him, and they set ambushes against Shechem in four companies.

[35] Gaal son of Ebed walked out and stood at the entrance of the city gate. And Abimelech rose from his ambush — he and his men with him.

[36] When Gaal saw them he said to Zebul, "Look — people are coming down from the mountain peaks." Zebul replied, "You're seeing the shadows of the mountains as if they were men."

[37] Gaal spoke again: "Look — people are coming down from the navel of the land, and another company is coming from the direction of the Diviners' Oak."

[38] Then Zebul said to him, "Where's your big mouth now? You said, 'Who is Abimelech that we should serve him?' Aren't these the people you mocked? Go on — go out and fight them."

[39] So Gaal marched out at the head of the leaders of Shechem and fought Abimelech.

[40] Abimelech chased him, and Gaal ran. Many fell wounded, all the way to the entrance of the gate.

[41] Abimelech stayed in Arumah, and Zebul drove Gaal and his brothers out — they could no longer live in Shechem.

[42] The next day the people went out to the fields. Word reached Abimelech.

[43] He took his men, split them into three companies, and set an ambush in the fields. When he saw the people leaving the city, he sprang up and struck them down.

[44] Abimelech and the company with him rushed forward and took position at the entrance of the city gate. The other two companies charged everyone in the fields and cut them down.

[45] All that day Abimelech fought against the city. He captured it, killed everyone in it, demolished the city, and salted the ground.

[46] When the leaders of the tower of Shechem heard, they took refuge in the stronghold of the temple of El-Berith.

[47] Abimelech was told that all the leaders of the tower of Shechem had gathered there.

[48] So Abimelech climbed Mount Zalmon with all his men. He took an axe, cut a branch from a tree, hoisted it on his shoulder, and said to his men, "What you've seen me do — hurry, do the same."

[49] So every man cut his own branch, and they followed Abimelech. They piled the branches against the stronghold and set the place on fire over the heads of those inside. Everyone in the tower of Shechem died — about a thousand men and women.

[50] Then Abimelech went to Thebez, camped against it, and seized it.

[51] But there was a strong tower in the middle of the city, and all the men and women — all the city's leaders — fled there, locked themselves in, and climbed up to the roof.

[52] Abimelech reached the tower, attacked it, and approached the entrance to burn it down.

[53] Then a woman dropped an upper millstone on Abimelech's head and crushed his skull.

[54] He called to his young armor-bearer in a rush: "Draw your sword and kill me — don't let them say of me, 'A woman killed him.'" The young man ran him through, and he died.

[55] When the Israelites saw that Abimelech was dead, they each went home.

[56] So God repaid the wickedness of Abimelech — what he had done to his father by murdering his seventy brothers.

[57] And all the wickedness of the men of Shechem — God brought it back on their own heads. The curse of Jotham son of Jerubbaal landed.

[1] After Abimelech's reign of blood, a man rose to deliver Israel — Tola son of Puah, son of Dodo, from the tribe of Issachar. He lived in Shamir, in the hill country of Ephraim.

[2] He judged Israel for twenty-three years, then died and was buried in Shamir.

[3] After him came Jair the Gileadite. He judged Israel for twenty-two years.

[4] He had thirty sons who rode thirty donkeys and ruled thirty towns in Gilead — towns still called Havvoth-Jair to this day.

[5] Jair died and was buried in Kamon.

[6] Then Israel went back to what Israel always does — evil in the eyes of the LORD. They chased the Baals and the Ashtaroth, the gods of Aram, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of Ammon, the gods of the Philistines. They abandoned the LORD. They stopped serving him entirely.

[7] So the LORD's anger blazed against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines and the Ammonites.

[8] That year, and for eighteen years after, those enemies crushed and ground down every Israelite east of the Jordan, in the land of the Amorites, in Gilead.

[9] The Ammonites even crossed the Jordan to wage war against Judah, Benjamin, and the house of Ephraim. Israel was in agony.

[10] So Israel cried out to the LORD: "We've sinned against you. We abandoned our God and served the Baals."

[11] And the LORD answered Israel: "When the Egyptians, the Amorites, the Ammonites, the Philistines —

[12] when the Sidonians, the Amalekites, the Maonites — when any of them crushed you and you cried to me, didn't I rescue you from their hand?

[13] But you — you abandoned me. You served other gods. So I'm done rescuing you.

[14] Go cry to the gods you chose. Let them save you in your hour of agony."

[15] But Israel said to the LORD: "We've sinned. Do to us whatever seems right in your eyes — only, please, rescue us today."

[16] Then they threw out the foreign gods from their midst and served the LORD. And the LORD could not bear Israel's misery any longer.

[17] The Ammonites mustered and camped in Gilead. Israel gathered and camped at Mizpah.

[18] The leaders of Gilead said to each other: "Whoever steps up first to fight the Ammonites — he will be head over everyone living in Gilead."

[1] Jephthah the Gileadite was a warrior — a man of brutal capability. His mother was a prostitute, and Gilead had fathered him.

[2] But Gilead's wife bore him legitimate sons, and when they grew up, they ran Jephthah off. "You won't inherit a thing in our father's house," they told him. "You're the son of another woman."

[3] So Jephthah fled from his brothers and settled in the land of Tob. A band of restless men gathered around him, and they rode out with him on raids.

[4] Some time later, the Ammonites went to war against Israel.

[5] When the fighting started, the elders of Gilead went looking for Jephthah in the land of Tob.

[6] "Come back," they said. "Be our commander. Fight the Ammonites for us."

[7] Jephthah threw it in their faces: "Weren't you the ones who hated me? Who drove me out of my father's house? Now you come crawling when you're in trouble?"

[8] The elders didn't flinch. "That's exactly why we're here. Come with us. Fight the Ammonites. And you'll be head over everyone in Gilead — every last one of us."

[9] Jephthah pressed them: "If you bring me home to fight the Ammonites, and the LORD hands them over to me — I'm your head. For real."

[10] "The LORD hears us," the elders said. "He's our witness. We'll do exactly what you say."

[11] So Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people made him head and commander over them. And Jephthah laid every word of it out before the LORD at Mizpah.

[12] Then Jephthah sent messengers to the king of Ammon: "What's your quarrel with me? Why have you come to invade my land?"

[13] The king of Ammon answered: "Israel stole my land when they came up from Egypt — from the Arnon to the Jabbok and all the way to the Jordan. Give it back. Peacefully."

[14] Jephthah sent messengers back again with this:

[15] "Here is what Jephthah says: Israel did not seize Moab's land or Ammon's land.

[16] When Israel came up from Egypt, they traveled through the wilderness to the Red Sea and arrived at Kadesh.

[17] Then Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom: 'Let us pass through your land.' Edom refused. They sent word to the king of Moab too — same answer. So Israel stayed at Kadesh.

[18] Then they trekked through the wilderness, skirted Edom and Moab, came around to the east of Moab, and camped on the far side of the Arnon. They never set foot inside Moab's border — the Arnon was the line.

[19] Then Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon: 'Let us pass through your land to our destination.'

[20] But Sihon didn't trust Israel to pass through. He mustered his whole army at Jahaz and attacked.

[21] And the LORD, the God of Israel, gave Sihon and all his men into Israel's hand. Israel struck them down and took possession of all the Amorite land in that country —

[22] from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the wilderness to the Jordan. Every inch of it.

[23] So the LORD, the God of Israel, dispossessed the Amorites for the sake of his people Israel — and now you want to take it back?

[24] Don't you keep whatever Chemosh your god gives you? Then whatever the LORD our God dispossesses for us — that's ours.

[25] Are you any better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever pick a fight with Israel? Did he ever go to war with us?

[26] Israel has lived in Heshbon and its villages, in Aroer and its villages, in every town along the Arnon — for three hundred years. Why didn't you reclaim them then?

[27] I haven't wronged you. You're the one wronging me by making war. Let the LORD, the Judge, decide today between Israel and Ammon."

[28] But the king of Ammon ignored every word Jephthah sent him.

[29] Then the Spirit of the LORD came over Jephthah. He swept through Gilead and Manasseh, passed through Mizpah of Gilead, and from Mizpah of Gilead he advanced on the Ammonites.

[30] And Jephthah made a vow to the LORD: "If you really hand the Ammonites over to me —

[31] then whatever comes out of the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the Ammonites will be the LORD's. I will offer it up as a burnt offering."

[32] So Jephthah crossed over against the Ammonites to fight, and the LORD gave them into his hand.

[33] He struck them down from Aroer all the way to Minnith — twenty cities — and on to Abel-keramim. A devastating blow. And Ammon was crushed before Israel.

[34] Then Jephthah came home to Mizpah. And out of the house — out came his daughter to meet him, with tambourines and dancing. She was his only child. He had no other son, no other daughter. Just her.

[35] The moment he saw her, he ripped his clothes and cried out, "My daughter — you've brought me to my knees! You — of all people — the source of my ruin! I opened my mouth to the LORD. I cannot take it back."

[36] And she said to him, "Father — you opened your mouth to the LORD. Do to me what you said. The LORD avenged you on your enemies, on the Ammonites — do what you vowed."

[37] Then she said, "Grant me this one thing: give me two months. Let me go up into the hills and weep — with my friends — over the fact that I will never marry."

[38] "Go," he said. He sent her away for two months. And she went with her companions into the hills and wept that she would never know a husband.

[39] At the end of the two months, she returned to her father. And he did to her what he had vowed. She had never known a man. And it became a custom in Israel —

[40] that year after year the daughters of Israel go out for four days to commemorate the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite.

[1] The men of Ephraim mobilized, crossed over to Zaphon, and confronted Jephthah: "Why did you march out against the Ammonites and not call us to fight with you? We'll burn your house down around you."

[2] Jephthah shot back: "My people and I were locked in a bitter struggle with the Ammonites. I called for you — you didn't come. You left me hanging.

[3] When I saw no rescue coming from you, I took my life in my own hands, crossed over against the Ammonites, and the LORD handed them to me. So why are you showing up today to fight me?"

[4] Then Jephthah rallied every man of Gilead and went to war with Ephraim. And the men of Gilead cut Ephraim down — because Ephraim had sneered, "You Gileadites are nothing but Ephraim's runaways, stranded between Ephraim and Manasseh."

[5] Gilead seized the fords of the Jordan that led back to Ephraim. Whenever an Ephraimite fugitive begged, "Let me cross," the men of Gilead would ask, "Are you from Ephraim?" If he said no,

[6] they'd order him: "Say 'Shibboleth.'" He'd say "Sibboleth" — couldn't form the sound right — and they'd grab him and kill him at the fords. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites fell that day.

[7] Jephthah judged Israel six years. Then Jephthah the Gileadite died and was buried in one of the towns of Gilead.

[8] After him, Ibzan of Bethlehem judged Israel.

[9] He had thirty sons and thirty daughters. He married the daughters off outside the clan and brought in thirty women from outside for his sons. He judged Israel seven years.

[10] Then Ibzan died and was buried in Bethlehem.

[11] After him, Elon the Zebulunite judged Israel for ten years.

[12] Then Elon the Zebulunite died and was buried at Aijalon in the territory of Zebulun.

[13] After him, Abdon son of Hillel, from Pirathon, judged Israel.

[14] He had forty sons and thirty grandsons who rode on seventy donkeys. He judged Israel eight years.

[15] Then Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite died and was buried at Pirathon in the land of Ephraim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.

[1] Israel kept doing what was evil in the LORD's sight, so he handed them over to the Philistines for forty years.

[2] There was a man from Zorah, from the clan of Dan. His name was Manoah. His wife was barren — she had never borne a child.

[3] The angel of the LORD appeared to her and said, "You're barren. You've never had a child. But you're going to conceive. You're going to bear a son.

[4] So watch yourself — no wine, no strong drink, nothing unclean.

[5] Because you will conceive, and you will bear a son, and no razor is ever to touch his head. The boy will be a Nazirite to God from the womb. He's the one who will begin to rescue Israel from the grip of the Philistines."

[6] The woman ran to her husband. "A man of God came to me," she said. "His face — it was like the face of the angel of God. Terrifying. I didn't ask where he came from, and he didn't tell me his name.

[7] But he said: 'You're going to conceive. You're going to bear a son. So now — no wine, no strong drink, nothing unclean. Because the boy will be a Nazirite to God from the womb until the day he dies.'"

[8] Manoah prayed to the LORD: "Please, Lord — let the man of God you sent come back to us. Teach us what to do with the boy who's going to be born."

[9] God listened. The angel of God came back to the woman while she was sitting in the field. Manoah wasn't with her.

[10] She bolted, running to find her husband. "He's here!" she said. "The man who came to me the other day — he's here!"

[11] Manoah got up and followed his wife. He came to the man and said, "Are you the one who spoke to my wife?" "I am," he said.

[12] Manoah said, "When your words come true — what's the rule for the boy? What's his work?"

[13] The angel of the LORD said, "Everything I told your wife — she must follow it.

[14] Nothing that comes from the grapevine. No wine. No strong drink. Nothing unclean. Everything I commanded her — she must keep."

[15] Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, "Let us keep you here. Let us prepare a young goat for you."

[16] The angel of the LORD said, "Even if you keep me, I won't eat your food. But if you want to prepare a burnt offering — offer it to the LORD." Manoah still didn't realize he was speaking to the angel of the LORD.

[17] Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, "What is your name? When your words come true, we want to honor you."

[18] The angel of the LORD said to him, "Why do you ask my name? It is beyond understanding."

[19] So Manoah took the young goat and the grain offering and offered them on the rock to the LORD. And the LORD did something astonishing while Manoah and his wife watched.

[20] As the flame leapt up from the altar toward the sky — the angel of the LORD went up in the flame. Manoah and his wife saw it. They dropped, faces to the ground.

[21] The angel of the LORD did not appear again to Manoah and his wife. Then Manoah knew — this had been the angel of the LORD.

[22] Manoah said to his wife, "We're going to die. We've seen God."

[23] But his wife said, "If the LORD had wanted to kill us, he wouldn't have taken our burnt offering and grain offering from our hands. He wouldn't have shown us all this. He wouldn't have told us what he just told us."

[24] The woman bore a son. She named him Samson. The boy grew, and the LORD blessed him.

[25] And the Spirit of the LORD began to stir in him at Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.

[1] Samson went down to Timnah and there, among the Philistine women, he saw her.

[2] He came back home and told his father and mother: "I saw a woman in Timnah—Philistine. Get her for me. I want her as my wife."

[3] His father and mother pushed back. "Is there really not one woman among your own people? Not one in all Israel? You have to go to those uncircumcised Philistines for a wife?" But Samson said to his father, "Get her for me. She's the one I want."

[4] His parents had no idea this was from the LORD—that He was looking for an opening against the Philistines. In those days, Philistia ruled Israel.

[5] So Samson went down to Timnah with his father and mother. As they approached the vineyards of Timnah, a young lion came roaring at him.

[6] The Spirit of the LORD rushed on him, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands—as easily as ripping a young goat. He told no one, not his father, not his mother, what he had done.

[7] He went down and talked with the woman, and she was everything he wanted.

[8] Later, returning to marry her, he turned aside to look at the lion's carcass—and there inside it was a swarm of bees, and honey.

[9] He scooped the honey into his hands and ate as he walked. When he reached his father and mother, he gave them some, and they ate too. He never told them he had taken it from the body of a lion.

[10] His father went down to the woman, and Samson threw a feast there—the kind young men throw.

[11] When the locals saw him, they brought thirty companions to be with him.

[12] Samson said to them, "Let me put a riddle to you. If you can crack it during the seven days of the feast—solve it—I'll give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothes.

[13] But if you can't, you give them to me." They said, "Pose your riddle. Let's hear it."

[14] So he said: "Out of the eater came something to eat. Out of the strong came something sweet." Three days passed and they couldn't solve it.

[15] On the fourth day they came to Samson's wife: "Coax the riddle out of your husband, or we'll burn you and your father's house to the ground. Did you invite us here to rob us?"

[16] So she came to Samson weeping. "You don't love me. You hate me. You set a riddle for my people and didn't even tell me the answer." He said, "I haven't told my own father and mother. Why would I tell you?"

[17] She wept on him the rest of the seven days of the feast. On the seventh day he finally caved—she had worn him down—and she told her people.

[18] Before sundown on the seventh day, the men of the town said to him: "What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?" He shot back: "If you hadn't plowed with my heifer, you wouldn't have solved my riddle."

[19] Then the Spirit of the LORD rushed on him. He went down to Ashkelon, struck down thirty men, stripped them, and gave the clothes to those who had answered the riddle. Burning with fury, he went back to his father's house.

[20] And his wife was given to the companion who had stood beside him at the wedding.

[1] Sometime later, during the wheat harvest, Samson went to visit his wife, bringing a young goat as a gift. "I want to go to her in her room," he said. But her father blocked the door.

[2] "I was sure you hated her," he said. "So I gave her to your best man. Look — her younger sister is more beautiful anyway. Take her instead."

[3] Samson said, "This time, when I devastate the Philistines, no one can blame me."

[4] He went out, caught three hundred foxes, tied them tail to tail in pairs, and fastened a torch between every pair of tails.

[5] He lit the torches and let the foxes loose in the standing grain of the Philistines. He burned everything — the stacked sheaves, the standing wheat, even the vineyards and olive groves.

[6] "Who did this?" the Philistines demanded. The answer came back: "Samson — son-in-law of the Timnite. The Timnite gave Samson's wife to his best man." So the Philistines went up and burned her and her father alive.

[7] Samson said to them, "If this is how you act, I will not stop until I have my revenge on you."

[8] He attacked them, hip and thigh, slaughtering them in a great massacre. Then he went down and lived in the cleft of the rock of Etam.

[9] The Philistines marched up, made camp in Judah, and spread out near Lehi.

[10] The men of Judah asked, "Why have you come up against us?" They answered, "We came to bind Samson — to do to him what he did to us."

[11] Three thousand men of Judah went down to the cleft of the rock of Etam. They said to Samson, "Don't you understand? The Philistines rule over us. What have you done to us?" He answered, "I did to them what they did to me."

[12] They said, "We have come to bind you and hand you over to the Philistines." Samson said, "Swear to me you will not strike me down yourselves."

[13] "We won't," they said. "We will only bind you and hand you over. We will not kill you." So they tied him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.

[14] As he reached Lehi, the Philistines came rushing at him, shouting in triumph. Then the Spirit of the LORD rushed on him. The ropes on his arms became like flax burned with fire — the bonds melted off his hands.

[15] He spotted a fresh jawbone of a donkey, grabbed it, and struck down a thousand men.

[16] Then Samson said: "With the jawbone of a donkey — heap upon heap. With the jawbone of a donkey — I struck down a thousand men."

[17] When he finished speaking, he threw the jawbone away. That place was called Ramath-Lehi — Jawbone Hill.

[18] Then he was desperately thirsty. He cried out to the LORD: "You gave your servant this great victory. Will you now let me die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?"

[19] So God split open the hollow place at Lehi, and water came pouring out. Samson drank, his strength returned, and he revived. That spring is called En-hakkore — the spring of the one who cried out — and it is still there in Lehi to this day.

[20] Samson led Israel for twenty years in the days of the Philistines.

[1] Samson went to Gaza. He saw a prostitute there and went in to her.

[2] Word spread through the city: "Samson is here." So they surrounded the place, lay in wait for him all night at the city gate, and stayed quiet through the dark hours, telling themselves, "At dawn we kill him."

[3] But Samson slept only till midnight. At midnight he rose, grabbed the doors of the city gate along with the two posts, ripped them up — bar and all — hoisted them onto his shoulders, and carried them to the top of the hill that faces Hebron.

[4] After this he fell in love with a woman in the Sorek Valley. Her name was Delilah.

[5] The Philistine rulers came to her and said, "Coax him. Find out what makes him so strong, how we can overpower him, tie him up, break him. Do this and each of us will give you eleven hundred pieces of silver."

[6] So Delilah said to Samson, "Tell me — please — what makes you so strong? What could tie you down and break you?"

[7] Samson told her, "If they bind me with seven fresh bowstrings, never dried out, I'll be as weak as anyone else."

[8] The Philistine rulers brought her seven fresh bowstrings, still moist, and she tied him up with them.

[9] With men hidden in the inner room, she shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He snapped the bowstrings like a strand of yarn near a flame. The secret of his strength stayed hidden.

[10] Delilah said to Samson, "You've played me. You lied. Now tell me — what can really tie you down?"

[11] He said, "If they bind me tight with brand-new ropes, never used, I'll be as weak as anyone else."

[12] So Delilah got new ropes and tied him up, then shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" The men were waiting in the inner room. He snapped the ropes off his arms like thread.

[13] Delilah said, "You're still mocking me. Still lying. Tell me — what can tie you down?" He said, "If you weave the seven braids of my hair into the fabric on the loom and pin it tight—"

[14] So while he slept, she wove his braids into the loom, pinned them tight, and shouted, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He woke up and tore loose — pin, loom, fabric, and all.

[15] Then she said, "How can you say you love me when your heart isn't with me? Three times you've mocked me. Three times you've refused to tell me what makes you so strong."

[16] Day after day she pressed him. Nagged him. Wore him down until he was sick to death of it.

[17] So he told her everything. "No razor has ever touched my head. I have been set apart for God from the womb. If my head is shaved, my strength leaves me. I become weak. I become like any other man."

[18] Delilah knew this time he had told her the truth. She sent for the Philistine rulers: "Come up — this time. He's told me everything." So they came, silver in hand.

[19] She lulled him to sleep on her lap. Then she called for a man, and he shaved off the seven braids of Samson's hair. She began to torment him. His strength was gone.

[20] She cried, "Samson — the Philistines are on you!" He woke from sleep and thought, "I'll get out of this like every other time. I'll shake myself free." He did not know the LORD had left him.

[21] The Philistines seized him. Gouged out his eyes. Marched him down to Gaza, bound him with bronze shackles, and set him grinding grain in the prison.

[22] But the hair on his head began to grow back after it had been shaved.

[23] The Philistine rulers gathered for a great sacrifice to their god Dagon — a celebration. They chanted: "Our god has handed Samson, our enemy, over to us."

[24] When the people saw him, they praised their god: "Our god has handed our enemy over to us — the destroyer of our land, the one who piled up our dead."

[25] In the high of the party they shouted, "Bring out Samson — let him entertain us." So they hauled him from the prison and he performed for them. They stood him between the pillars.

[26] Samson said to the boy holding his hand, "Put me where I can feel the pillars that hold this place up. I want to lean on them."

[27] The temple was packed — men, women, every Philistine ruler there. Three thousand more on the roof watching Samson perform.

[28] Samson cried out to the LORD: "Lord GOD — remember me. Strengthen me, God — just this once more. Let me pay back the Philistines with one blow for my two eyes."

[29] Samson reached for the two central pillars holding up the temple — one with his right hand, one with his left — and braced himself against them.

[30] He said, "Let me die with the Philistines." He pushed with everything he had, and the temple collapsed on the rulers and everyone in it. The dead he killed in his death outnumbered all he had killed in his life.

[31] His brothers and his father's whole household came down, took his body, carried him up, and buried him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of Manoah his father. He had led Israel twenty years.

[1] In the hill country of Ephraim there was a man named Micah.

[2] He said to his mother, "Those eleven hundred pieces of silver that were taken from you — the silver you cursed the thief over, the curse I heard with my own ears — look, I have it. I took it."

His mother said, "May the LORD bless you, my son."

[3] He handed the eleven hundred pieces of silver back to his mother, and she said, "I solemnly consecrate this silver to the LORD on behalf of my son — to make a carved image and a cast idol. So here, I'm giving it back to you."

[4] He returned the silver to his mother anyway, and she took two hundred pieces of it and gave it to a silversmith, who shaped it into a carved image and a cast idol. It stood in Micah's house.

[5] Micah had himself a shrine. He made an ephod and some household gods, and he ordained one of his sons to serve as his priest.

[6] In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did whatever seemed right in his own eyes.

[7] There was a young man from Bethlehem in Judah, from the clan of Judah — a Levite living there as a stranger.

[8] He left Bethlehem looking for somewhere to settle, wherever he could find it. Traveling through, he came to Micah's house in the hill country of Ephraim.

[9] Micah asked him, "Where are you from?"

He answered, "I'm a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah. I'm looking for a place to live."

[10] Micah said, "Stay with me. Be a father and a priest to me. I'll give you ten pieces of silver a year, a set of clothes, and your food." So the Levite went in.

[11] The Levite agreed to stay with the man, and the young man became like one of his own sons to him.

[12] Micah ordained the Levite, and the young man became his priest and lived in Micah's house.

[13] Then Micah said, "Now I know the LORD will treat me well — I have a Levite for a priest."

[1] Israel had no king in those days. And the tribe of Dan was still searching for a place to call home — they hadn't yet received an inheritance among the tribes of Israel.

[2] So the Danites sent out five fighting men from their clans, scouts from Zorah and Eshtaol, to comb the land and size it up. "Go," they said. "Survey the territory." The men made their way into the hill country of Ephraim, to Micah's house, and lodged there for the night.

[3] As they got close, they recognized the voice of the young Levite. They turned in and asked him, "Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place? What's your business?"

[4] He told them what Micah had done for him — "He hired me. I serve as his priest."

[5] So they said to him, "Ask God for us, then. We want to know if this journey of ours will succeed."

[6] The priest told them, "Go in peace. The LORD is watching over the road you're traveling."

[7] The five men moved on and came to Laish. They found the people there living in confidence — quiet, unsuspecting, after the manner of the Sidonians. No one in the land harassed them or held power over them. They were far from Sidon and had no dealings with anyone.

[8] When the scouts came back to their brothers at Zorah and Eshtaol, the others asked, "Well? What's your report?"

[9] "Up," they answered. "Let's move on them. We've seen the land — it's good. And you're just standing here? Don't hesitate. Go. Take possession.

[10] When you arrive, you'll find a people at ease and a land wide open. God has placed it in your hands. It's a place that lacks nothing on earth."

[11] So six hundred men from the clan of Dan, armed for war, set out from Zorah and Eshtaol.

[12] They marched up and camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah — which is why that place is called Mahaneh-dan to this day. It sits just west of Kiriath-jearim.

[13] From there they pushed on into the hill country of Ephraim and arrived at Micah's house.

[14] Then the five who had scouted out Laish spoke up to their brothers: "Listen — do you know what's in these houses? An ephod, household gods, a carved image, a cast idol. Think about what you should do."

[15] So they turned aside and entered the young Levite's quarters at Micah's house and greeted him.

[16] The six hundred armed Danites stood at the gate.

[17] The five scouts went up inside and took the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, the cast idol — while the priest stood at the gate with the six hundred fighting men.

[18] When the priest saw them carrying off the carved image, the ephod, the household gods, and the cast idol, he said, "What are you doing?"

[19] They answered, "Quiet. Put your hand over your mouth. Come with us — be a father and priest to us. Which is better: serving as priest for one man's household, or for an entire tribe and clan in Israel?"

[20] The priest's heart leapt at this. He picked up the ephod, the household gods, and the carved image and joined the company.

[21] They turned and went on their way, putting the children, the livestock, and their possessions in front of them.

[22] They were already some distance from Micah's house when the men in the houses near Micah's were called together. They overtook the Danites.

[23] They shouted after them. The Danites turned around and said to Micah, "What's wrong with you, mustering a crowd like this?"

[24] He said, "You've taken the gods I made, and the priest, and walked off. What do I have left? And you ask me, 'What's wrong'?"

[25] The Danites told him, "Don't raise your voice around us — or some hot-tempered men might fall on you, and you'll lose your life and the lives of your household."

[26] Then the Danites went on their way. Micah saw they were too strong for him, so he turned and went back home.

[27] They took what Micah had made, and the priest who had served him, and arrived at Laish — at a quiet, unsuspecting people. They put the city to the sword and burned it down.

[28] No one came to the rescue, because Laish was far from Sidon and had no dealings with anyone. The city sat in the valley near Beth-rehob. The Danites rebuilt it and settled there.

[29] They renamed it Dan, after their ancestor Dan, son of Israel — though originally the city had been called Laish.

[30] The Danites set up the carved image for themselves. And Jonathan son of Gershom, son of Moses, along with his sons, served as priests for the tribe of Dan — right up until the day the land went into exile.

[31] They kept Micah's carved image standing the whole time the house of God was at Shiloh.

[1] In those days, when Israel had no king, a Levite living deep in the hill country of Ephraim took a concubine for himself from Bethlehem in Judah. [2] But she was unfaithful to him and left, going back to her father's house in Bethlehem. She stayed there four months. [3] Then her husband went after her, hoping to win her back. He brought his servant and two donkeys. She brought him into her father's house, and when her father saw him, he welcomed him with delight.

[4] His father-in-law — the girl's father — would not let him go. He stayed three days, eating, drinking, sleeping under that roof. [5] On the fourth day they rose early, and the Levite got ready to leave. But the girl's father said to his son-in-law, "Eat something first. Strengthen yourself for the road. Then you can go." [6] So the two of them sat down and ate and drank together. Then the father said, "Stay tonight. Please. Enjoy yourself."
