[18] John had kept telling him, "It's not lawful for you to have your brother's wife." [19] Herodias nursed a grudge and wanted him dead — but couldn't pull it off, [20] because Herod feared John. He knew the man was righteous and holy, and he protected him. When he listened to John he was deeply unsettled — and yet he kept listening, gladly.

[21] Then her moment came. Herod's birthday. He threw a banquet for his court, his officers, the leading men of Galilee. [22] Herodias's daughter walked in and danced, and she captivated Herod and his guests. The king said to the girl, "Ask me for anything you want, and it's yours." [23] He swore it: "Whatever you ask, I'll give you — up to half my kingdom."

[24] She went out to her mother. "What should I ask for?" "The head of John the Baptizer." [25] She rushed back to the king and said, "I want you to give me, right now, the head of John the Baptist on a platter."

[26] The king was devastated. But because of his oaths, and because of his guests, he couldn't bring himself to refuse her. [27] He dispatched an executioner immediately, with orders to bring John's head. The man went, beheaded John in the prison, [28] brought the head back on a platter, and gave it to the girl. The girl gave it to her mother.

[29] When John's disciples heard, they came and took his body and laid it in a tomb.

[30] The apostles regrouped around Jesus and reported everything — all they had done, all they had taught. [31] He said, "Come away with me, just us, somewhere quiet. Rest." Because people were swarming, coming and going constantly, and they hadn't even had a chance to eat.

[32] So they took off in the boat to a deserted place by themselves. [33] But people saw them leaving, recognized them, and a crowd ran on foot from all the towns and got there first. [34] When Jesus stepped out of the boat and saw the massive crowd, his heart broke for them — they were like sheep without a shepherd. So he started teaching them, and he taught them a long time.

[35] By now it was getting late, and his disciples came to him. "This place is deserted. It's already late. [36] Send them away so they can go to the surrounding farms and villages and buy themselves something to eat."

[37] He answered, "You give them something to eat." They said, "Should we go spend two hundred denarii on bread and give it to them to eat?" [38] He said, "How many loaves do you have? Go check." They came back: "Five. And two fish."

[39] He told them to have everyone sit down in groups on the green grass. [40] They settled in, in groups of hundreds and fifties. [41] He took the five loaves and two fish, looked up to heaven, blessed them, broke the loaves, and started handing them to the disciples to distribute. He divided the two fish among them all. [42] Everyone ate. Everyone was full. [43] They picked up twelve baskets full of leftover bread and fish. [44] The men who ate the bread numbered five thousand.

[45] Right after, he made his disciples get in the boat and head across to Bethsaida while he dismissed the crowd. [46] After saying goodbye to them, he climbed the mountain to pray.

[47] When evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and he was alone on land. [48] He could see they were straining at the oars — the wind was against them. Sometime between three and six in the morning, he came to them, walking on the sea. He was about to pass by them. [49] When they saw him walking on the sea, they thought he was a ghost and screamed — [50] every one of them saw him and lost it. He spoke immediately: "Take heart. It's me. Don't be afraid." [51] He climbed into the boat with them, and the wind died. They were absolutely undone, [52] because they hadn't understood about the loaves. Their hearts were hard.

[53] They crossed over and landed at Gennesaret, anchoring there. [54] The moment they got out of the boat, people recognized him. [55] They tore through the whole region, carrying the sick on mats to wherever they heard he was. [56] Wherever he went — villages, towns, farms — they laid the sick in the marketplaces and begged him to let them just touch the fringe of his cloak. And everyone who touched him was healed.

[1] The Pharisees gathered around him, along with some scholars of the law who had come down from Jerusalem. [2] They noticed that some of his disciples were eating bread with hands that were—by their reckoning—defiled. Unwashed. [3] (The Pharisees, like all Jews, will not eat until they have washed their hands a certain ritual way, clinging to the tradition handed down by the elders. [4] When they come back from the market, they don't eat until they've immersed themselves. And there's a whole catalog of other customs they've inherited—washings of cups, pitchers, kettles, dining couches.) [5] So the Pharisees and scholars pressed him: "Why don't your disciples walk according to the tradition of the elders? Why do they eat with defiled hands?"

[6] He answered them: "Isaiah nailed you hypocrites perfectly when he wrote:

*'This people honors me with their lips,
but their heart is nowhere near me.
[7] Their worship is empty—
they teach human rules as if they were my commands.'*

[8] You abandon God's command and clutch human tradition instead."

[9] He pressed harder: "You've got a real talent for shoving aside the command of God so you can keep your tradition intact. [10] Moses said, 'Honor your father and your mother,' and 'Whoever curses father or mother must be put to death.' [11] But you say—if a man tells his father or mother, 'Whatever support you might have gotten from me is *corban*' (meaning, dedicated to God)— [12] then you no longer let him do a single thing for his father or mother. [13] You've gutted the word of God with the tradition you keep passing down. And that's just one example. You do this kind of thing constantly."

[14] He called the crowd back in close. "Listen to me, all of you. Understand this. [15] Nothing outside a person can defile them by going in. What defiles is what comes out." [16] *[Whoever has ears, let them hear.]*

[17] Once he was away from the crowd and back in a house, his disciples asked him about the riddle. [18] He said, "Are you really this slow too? Don't you see? Nothing that enters a person from outside can defile them— [19] because it doesn't reach the heart. It goes into the stomach and out into the latrine." (With that, he declared all foods clean.)

[20] Then he said: "What comes out of a person—that's what defiles them. [21] From inside, from the human heart, that's where the rot crawls out: corrupt thoughts, sexual sin, theft, murder, [22] adultery, greed, malice, deceit, indecency, envy, slander, arrogance, recklessness. [23] All of this filth comes from inside. That's what defiles a person."

[24] He left that place and traveled to the region of Tyre. He entered a house and wanted no one to know he was there—but he couldn't stay hidden. [25] A woman whose little girl had an unclean spirit heard about him and came immediately, falling at his feet. [26] She was Greek, Syrophoenician by birth. She begged him to cast the demon out of her daughter.

[27] He said to her, "Let the children eat first. It isn't right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs."

[28] She answered, "True, Lord—but even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs."

[29] He said, "For an answer like that—go. The demon has left your daughter." [30] She went home and found her child lying on the bed. The demon was gone.

[31] He left Tyre, traveled through Sidon, and came back to the Sea of Galilee through the region of the Decapolis. [32] They brought him a man who was deaf and could barely speak, and they begged Jesus to lay his hand on him. [33] He took the man aside, away from the crowd. He put his fingers in the man's ears. He spit and touched his tongue. [34] He looked up to heaven, let out a deep sigh, and said, *"Ephphatha"*—Open up. [35] And just like that, the man's ears were opened, the knot on his tongue came loose, and he spoke clearly.

[36] Jesus told them not to tell anyone. But the more he told them, the more they spread the news. [37] People were stunned past words. "He does everything well," they kept saying. "He makes the deaf hear and the mute speak."

[1] Around that same time, another massive crowd had gathered, and they had nothing to eat. Jesus called his disciples over and said, [2] "My heart breaks for these people. They've been with me three days now, and they have no food. [3] If I send them home hungry, they'll collapse on the road. Some of them came from a long way off."

[4] His disciples shot back, "Out here in this wasteland? Where is anyone supposed to find enough bread to feed a crowd like this?"

[5] "How many loaves do you have?" he asked.

"Seven," they said.

[6] He told the crowd to sit down on the ground. Then he took the seven loaves, gave thanks, broke them, and handed them to his disciples to distribute. They served the crowd. [7] They also had a few small fish. He blessed those too and told the disciples to pass them around. [8] The people ate until they were full. Afterward, the disciples gathered up seven baskets of leftovers. [9] About four thousand had eaten. He sent them home, [10] climbed into the boat with his disciples, and crossed over to the region of Dalmanutha.

[11] The Pharisees showed up and started arguing with him, demanding a sign from heaven — a test. [12] He sighed deep in his spirit and said, "Why does this generation keep hunting for a sign? I'm telling you the truth — no sign will be handed to this generation." [13] He left them standing there, got back in the boat, and crossed to the other side.

[14] The disciples had forgotten to bring bread. They had exactly one loaf with them in the boat. [15] Jesus warned them, "Watch out. Be on your guard against the yeast of the Pharisees and the yeast of Herod."

[16] They started muttering to each other, "He's saying this because we didn't bring bread."

[17] Jesus heard them and said, "Why are you talking about bread? Don't you see it yet? Don't you get it? Are your hearts that hard? [18] You have eyes — can't you see? Ears — can't you hear? Don't you remember? [19] When I broke five loaves for five thousand, how many baskets of leftovers did you pick up?"

"Twelve," they said.

[20] "And when I broke seven for four thousand, how many baskets?"

"Seven."

[21] "Then how is it," he asked, "that you still don't understand?"

[22] They arrived in Bethsaida. Some people brought a blind man to him and begged him to touch the man. [23] Jesus took the blind man by the hand and led him outside the village. He spat on his eyes, laid his hands on him, and asked, "Do you see anything?"

[24] The man looked up. "I see people — they look like trees walking around."

[25] Jesus placed his hands on the man's eyes again. This time the man stared hard, and his sight came back — sharp, clear, everything in focus. [26] Jesus sent him home with the instruction, "Don't even go back into the village."

[27] Jesus and his disciples set out for the villages around Caesarea Philippi. On the road he asked them, "Who do people say I am?"

[28] "Some say John the Baptist," they told him. "Others say Elijah. Still others, one of the prophets."

[29] "And you?" he asked. "Who do you say I am?"

Peter answered, "You are the Messiah."

[30] Jesus told them in no uncertain terms not to breathe a word about him to anyone.

[31] Then he began teaching them — the Son of Man had to suffer terribly, be rejected by the elders, the chief priests, and the scribes, be killed, and after three days rise again. [32] He laid it out plainly. Peter pulled him aside and started to rebuke him.

[33] Jesus turned around, looked at his disciples, and rebuked Peter to his face. "Get behind me, Satan. You're not thinking the thoughts of God. You're thinking the thoughts of men."

[34] He called the crowd over with his disciples and said, "If anyone wants to come after me, they have to deny themselves, pick up their cross, and follow me. [35] Whoever tries to save their life will lose it. Whoever loses their life for me and for the good news will save it. [36] What does it profit you to gain the whole world and forfeit your soul? [37] What could you possibly trade for your soul? [38] If anyone is ashamed of me and my words in this faithless, sin-soaked generation, the Son of Man will be ashamed of them when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."

[1] And he told them, "I'm telling you the truth — some standing right here will not taste death before they see God's kingdom arrive in power."

[2] Six days later, Jesus pulled Peter, James, and John aside and led them up a high mountain, just the four of them. There, in front of them, he transformed.

[3] His clothes turned blinding white — whiter than any bleach on earth could make them.

[4] Then Elijah appeared, with Moses, and the two of them were deep in conversation with Jesus.

[5] Peter blurted out, "Rabbi — it's good we're here. Let's build three shelters: one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah."

[6] He didn't know what he was saying. They were terrified.

[7] Then a cloud rolled in and swallowed them, and a voice came out of the cloud: "This is my Son. The one I love. Listen to him."

[8] They looked around — and suddenly no one was there but Jesus, alone with them.

[9] Coming down the mountain, he ordered them to keep what they'd seen to themselves until the Son of Man had risen from the dead.

[10] They held onto it, but kept asking each other what "rising from the dead" was supposed to mean.

[11] Then they asked him, "Why do the teachers of the law insist Elijah has to come first?"

[12] He answered, "Elijah does come first, and he sets everything right. But then — why does Scripture say the Son of Man must suffer terribly and be treated like garbage?

[13] I'm telling you, Elijah has already come. And they did to him exactly what they wanted — just like Scripture said they would."

[14] When they got back to the other disciples, a huge crowd was around them, and the teachers of the law were arguing with them.

[15] The moment the crowd spotted Jesus, they were stunned — and they ran to greet him.

[16] "What are you arguing about?" he asked.

[17] A man in the crowd answered, "Teacher, I brought my son to you. He has a spirit that won't let him speak.

[18] Whenever it seizes him, it slams him to the ground. He foams at the mouth, grinds his teeth, goes rigid. I asked your disciples to drive it out. They couldn't."

[19] "You faithless generation," Jesus said. "How long do I have to be with you? How long do I have to put up with you? Bring him here."

[20] They brought the boy. The instant the spirit saw Jesus, it threw the boy into convulsions — he hit the ground, rolling, foaming.

[21] "How long has this been happening to him?" Jesus asked the father. "Since he was a child," he said.

[22] "It's tried to kill him over and over — thrown him into fire, into water. If you can do anything, please — have mercy on us. Help us."

[23] "*If* I can?" Jesus said. "Everything is possible for the one who believes."

[24] Instantly the father cried out, "I do believe! Help my unbelief!"

[25] Jesus saw the crowd swelling and rebuked the unclean spirit: "You deaf and mute spirit — I command you, come out of him, and never enter him again."

[26] It shrieked, convulsed him violently, and came out. The boy lay there like a corpse — so still that people were saying, "He's dead."

[27] But Jesus took him by the hand, lifted him up, and he stood.

[28] Later, inside, the disciples asked him privately, "Why couldn't we drive it out?"

[29] "This kind," he said, "only comes out by prayer."

[30] They left and traveled through Galilee. Jesus didn't want anyone to know where he was,

[31] because he was teaching his disciples. He kept telling them, "The Son of Man is going to be handed over. They'll kill him. Three days later, he'll rise."

[32] They didn't understand what he meant — and they were afraid to ask.

[33] They came to Capernaum. Once they were inside, he asked, "What were you arguing about on the road?"

[34] They went silent. On the road they'd been fighting over which of them was the greatest.

[35] He sat down, called the twelve, and said, "If anyone wants to be first, he has to be last of all and servant of all."

[36] He took a small child, set him in the middle of them, wrapped his arms around him, and said,

[37] "Whoever welcomes a child like this in my name welcomes me. And whoever welcomes me — isn't really welcoming me, but the One who sent me."

[38] John spoke up: "Teacher, we saw someone driving out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him because he wasn't one of us."

[39] "Don't stop him," Jesus said. "No one who performs a miracle in my name is going to turn around and curse me.

[40] Whoever isn't against us is for us.

[41] And listen — anyone who gives you even a cup of water because you belong to the Messiah, that person will not lose their reward.

[42] But anyone who trips up one of these little ones who trusts in me — better for him to have a millstone hung around his neck and be thrown into the sea.

[43] If your hand makes you fall, cut it off. Better to enter life maimed than to keep both hands and go to hell, into the fire that never dies.

[44]

[45] If your foot makes you fall, cut it off. Better to enter life crippled than to keep both feet and be thrown into hell.

[46]

[47] If your eye makes you fall, tear it out. Better to enter God's kingdom one-eyed than to keep both eyes and be thrown into hell —

[48] where the worm never dies and the fire never goes out.

[49] Everyone will be salted with fire.

[50] Salt is good — but if salt loses its bite, how do you make it salty again? Keep salt in yourselves. And be at peace with each other."

[1] He left there and headed into Judea, crossing to the far side of the Jordan. Crowds gathered around him again, and as always, he taught them.

[2] Some Pharisees came to test him. "Is it legal," they asked, "for a man to divorce his wife?"

[3] He turned the question back: "What did Moses command you?"

[4] "Moses permitted a man to write a certificate of divorce and send her away," they answered.

[5] Jesus said, "He wrote that rule because your hearts are hard.

[6] But from the beginning of creation, God made them male and female.

[7] That is why a man leaves his father and mother and clings to his wife,

[8] and the two become one flesh. They are no longer two — they are one.

[9] What God has joined, no human being gets to tear apart."

[10] Back inside the house, the disciples pressed him on it again.

[11] He told them: "Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against her.

[12] And if she divorces her husband and marries another, she commits adultery."

[13] People started bringing children to him so he could touch them, but the disciples shooed them away.

[14] When Jesus saw it, he was furious. "Let the children come to me. Don't block them. The kingdom of God belongs to people like these.

[15] I'm telling you — anyone who doesn't receive God's kingdom like a child will never get in."

[16] Then he gathered the children into his arms, laid his hands on them, and blessed them.

[17] As he was setting out on the road, a man ran up, dropped to his knees in front of him, and asked, "Good Teacher — what do I have to do to inherit eternal life?"

[18] Jesus said, "Why are you calling me good? No one is good — only God.

[19] You know the commandments: don't murder, don't commit adultery, don't steal, don't lie, don't defraud, honor your father and mother."

[20] "Teacher," the man said, "I've kept all of these since I was a boy."

[21] Jesus looked at him — really looked — and loved him. "One thing you're missing," he said. "Go. Sell everything you own and give it to the poor. You'll have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me."

[22] The man's face fell. He walked away crushed, because he owned a great deal.

[23] Jesus looked around at his disciples and said, "How hard it is for the wealthy to enter God's kingdom."

[24] They were stunned. But Jesus repeated himself: "Children, how hard it is to enter the kingdom of God.

[25] It's easier for a camel to squeeze through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter God's kingdom."

[26] Now they were really shaken. "Then who can possibly be saved?" they asked each other.

[27] Jesus locked eyes with them. "For humans, impossible. But not for God. With God, everything is possible."

[28] Peter spoke up: "Look — we've left everything to follow you."

[29] Jesus said, "I'm telling you the truth — no one who has left house or brothers or sisters or mother or father or children or fields for my sake and for the gospel

[30] will fail to receive a hundred times as much in this present age — houses, brothers, sisters, mothers, children, fields, along with persecutions — and in the age to come, eternal life.

[31] But many who are first will end up last, and the last first."

[32] They were on the road, climbing toward Jerusalem, with Jesus walking out ahead of them. The disciples were unnerved. Those following behind were afraid. He took the Twelve aside again and told them what was coming:

[33] "Listen. We're going up to Jerusalem. The Son of Man is going to be handed over to the chief priests and the scribes. They'll condemn him to death and turn him over to the Gentiles.

[34] They'll mock him, spit on him, flog him, and kill him. And after three days, he will rise."

[35] James and John, Zebedee's sons, came up to him. "Teacher, we want you to do something for us."

[36] "What do you want?" he asked.

[37] "Let one of us sit at your right and the other at your left when you come into your glory."

[38] Jesus said, "You don't have any idea what you're asking. Can you drink the cup I'm about to drink? Can you be plunged into the baptism I'm about to undergo?"

[39] "We can," they said. Jesus replied, "You will drink the cup I drink. You will be baptized with my baptism.

[40] But the seats at my right and left — those aren't mine to assign. They belong to those for whom they've been prepared."

[41] When the other ten heard about it, they were livid with James and John.

[42] Jesus called them all together. "You know how it works with the Gentiles — their so-called rulers lord it over them, and their big men throw their weight around.

[43] That's not how it works with you. Whoever wants to be great among you must become your servant.

[44] Whoever wants to be first must become the slave of all.

[45] Because even the Son of Man didn't come to be served. He came to serve — and to give his life as a ransom for many."

[46] They came to Jericho. As Jesus was leaving the city with his disciples and a sizable crowd, a blind beggar named Bartimaeus — son of Timaeus — was sitting by the road.

[47] When he heard it was Jesus of Nazareth, he started shouting: "Jesus! Son of David! Have mercy on me!"

[48] People scolded him, told him to shut up. He just shouted louder: "Son of David! Have mercy on me!"

[49] Jesus stopped. "Call him over." They went to the blind man: "Take heart. Get up. He's calling for you."

[50] He threw off his cloak, sprang up, and came to Jesus.

[51] "What do you want me to do for you?" Jesus asked. "Rabbi," he said, "I want to see."

[52] "Go," Jesus said. "Your faith has healed you." Instantly he could see — and he followed Jesus down the road.

[1] As they got close to Jerusalem — coming up on Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives — Jesus sent two of his disciples ahead [2] with these instructions: "Head into that village in front of you. The second you walk in, you'll spot a colt tied up — one nobody's ever ridden. Untie it. Bring it back. [3] If anyone challenges you — 'What do you think you're doing?' — just say, 'The Lord needs it. He'll send it right back.'"

[4] They went. They found the colt tied to a door out on the street, and they untied it. [5] Sure enough, some of the people standing there said, "Hey — what are you doing, untying that colt?" [6] The disciples answered exactly as Jesus had told them, and the people let them go.

[7] They brought the colt to Jesus, threw their cloaks over its back, and he climbed on. [8] The crowd carpeted the road with their cloaks. Others spread leafy branches they'd cut from the fields. [9] Those leading the way and those trailing behind were shouting:

"Hosanna!
Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord!
[10] Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David!
Hosanna in the highest!"

[11] He entered Jerusalem and went into the temple. He looked around at everything. And since it was already late, he left for Bethany with the Twelve.

[12] The next day, leaving Bethany, he was hungry. [13] From a distance he saw a fig tree in full leaf and went to see if it had anything on it. When he got there — nothing but leaves. It wasn't the season for figs. [14] He spoke to the tree: "May no one ever eat fruit from you again." His disciples heard him.

[15] They arrived in Jerusalem. Jesus walked into the temple courts and started driving people out — the sellers, the buyers. He flipped the tables of the money changers and the benches of the dove dealers. [16] He wouldn't let anyone haul merchandise through the temple courts. [17] And he taught them: "Doesn't Scripture say, *My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations*? But you — you've turned it into a hideout for thieves."

[18] The chief priests and the scribes heard about it and started looking for a way to kill him. They were afraid of him — because the whole crowd was electrified by his teaching.

[19] When evening came, Jesus and his disciples left the city.

[20] Walking back in the morning, they passed the fig tree. It was withered down to the roots. [21] Peter remembered: "Rabbi — look! The tree you cursed. It's dead."

[22] Jesus answered, "Have faith in God. [23] I'm telling you the truth — anyone who says to this mountain, 'Get up and throw yourself into the sea,' and doesn't waver inside but actually trusts that what he says is happening — it will happen for him. [24] So I'm telling you: whatever you ask for in prayer, trust that you've already received it, and it's yours. [25] And when you stand to pray, if you're holding something against someone, let it go — so your Father in heaven can let go of what you've done."

[27] They came back to Jerusalem. As Jesus walked through the temple, the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders confronted him. [28] "By what authority are you doing all this? Who gave you the right?"

[29] Jesus said, "I'll ask you one question. Answer me, and I'll tell you by what authority I do these things. [30] John's baptism — was it from heaven, or from people? Answer me."

[31] They huddled. "If we say 'from heaven,' he'll say, 'Then why didn't you believe him?' [32] But if we say 'from people' —" They were afraid of the crowd, because everyone was convinced John was a real prophet. [33] So they answered Jesus, "We don't know."

And Jesus said, "Then I'm not telling you by what authority I do these things either."

[1] He started telling them stories. "A man planted a vineyard. He built a wall around it, dug a pit for the winepress, raised a watchtower. Then he leased it to tenant farmers and traveled abroad.

[2] When harvest came, he sent a servant to collect his share of the fruit.

[3] They grabbed him, beat him, and sent him back empty-handed.

[4] He sent another servant. This one they cracked over the head and humiliated.

[5] He sent a third. They killed him. And many more after that — some beaten, some murdered.

[6] He had one card left to play: his son, his beloved. He sent him last, thinking, 'Surely they'll respect my son.'

[7] But the tenants said to each other, 'This is the heir. Kill him and the inheritance is ours.'

[8] So they seized him, killed him, and dumped his body outside the vineyard.

[9] What will the owner of the vineyard do? He'll come. He'll destroy those tenants. And he'll give the vineyard to others.

[10] Haven't you read the Scripture? 'The stone the builders threw away became the cornerstone.

[11] This is the Lord's doing, and it stuns our eyes.'"

[12] They wanted to arrest him on the spot — they knew the story was aimed at them — but they were afraid of the crowd. So they left him and walked away.

[13] Then they sent some Pharisees and Herodians to trap him in his own words.

[14] They came up and said, "Teacher, we know you're honest. You don't bend to anyone. You don't care about appearances — you teach God's way straight. Tell us: Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar? Should we pay, or shouldn't we?"

[15] He saw right through the act. "Why are you testing me? Bring me a denarius. Let me look at it."

[16] They handed him one. He asked, "Whose face is this? Whose name?" They said, "Caesar's."

[17] Jesus said, "Then give Caesar what belongs to Caesar — and give God what belongs to God." They were left speechless.

[18] Then some Sadducees showed up — the ones who say there's no resurrection — with a question.

[19] "Teacher, Moses wrote that if a man's brother dies leaving a wife but no children, the man must marry the widow and raise up offspring for his brother.

[20] Now then. Seven brothers. The first married a woman and died childless.

[21] The second married her, died childless. Same with the third.

[22] All seven — none of them had children. Finally, the woman died too.

[23] In the resurrection — whenever that is — whose wife will she be? She belonged to all seven."

[24] Jesus said, "You're wrong, and here's why: you don't know the Scriptures, and you don't know the power of God.

[25] When people rise from the dead, they don't marry or get married off. They're like the angels in heaven.

[26] As for the dead being raised — haven't you read in the book of Moses, at the burning bush, how God said to him, 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob'?

[27] He's not the God of the dead. He's the God of the living. You're badly mistaken."

[28] One of the scribes had been listening. He saw Jesus answer well and stepped up with his own question: "Of all the commandments, which one is first?"

[29] Jesus said, "The first is this: 'Listen, Israel — the Lord our God, the Lord is one.

[30] You shall love the Lord your God with everything you are — every fiber of your heart, every breath of your soul, every thought of your mind, every ounce of your strength.'

[31] The second is this: 'Love your neighbor as yourself.' No commandment is greater than these."

[32] The scribe said, "Beautiful, Teacher. You've spoken the truth: God is one, and there is no other.

[33] To love him with the whole heart, the whole understanding, the whole strength — and to love your neighbor as yourself — this is worth more than every burnt offering and sacrifice combined."

[34] Jesus saw that he had answered with wisdom and said, "You're not far from the kingdom of God." After that, no one dared question him.

[35] While teaching in the temple, Jesus pressed back: "How can the scribes say the Messiah is the son of David?

[36] David himself, speaking by the Holy Spirit, said: 'The Lord said to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet.'

[37] David himself calls him Lord. So how is he his son?" The crowd was loving every minute of it.

[38] As he taught, he said, "Watch out for the scribes. They love parading around in long robes, soaking up greetings in the marketplace,

[39] grabbing the best seats in synagogues and the places of honor at banquets.

[40] They devour widows' homes and cover it with long, showy prayers. Their judgment will be severe."

[41] Jesus sat down across from the temple treasury and watched the crowd dropping money into the collection box. Many of the rich poured in large amounts.

[42] Then a poor widow came up and dropped in two small copper coins — barely worth a penny.

[43] He called his disciples over and said, "I'm telling you the truth: this poor widow has put in more than all the others combined.

[44] They gave from their surplus. She gave from her poverty — everything she had. Her whole life."

[1] As he was leaving the temple, one of his disciples said, "Teacher — look at these stones. Look at these buildings."

[2] Jesus answered, "You see all this? Not one stone will be left on another. Every last one will come down."

[3] Later, while he sat on the Mount of Olives looking across at the temple, Peter, James, John, and Andrew came to him alone.

[4] "Tell us — when does this happen? What's the sign that it's all about to come undone?"

[5] Jesus began: "Watch yourselves. Don't let anyone deceive you.

[6] Plenty will come using my name, saying, 'I'm the one' — and they'll pull people in by the crowd.

[7] When you hear of wars, when you hear the rumors of wars — don't panic. It has to happen. But it's not the end yet.

[8] Nation will rise against nation, kingdom against kingdom. Earthquakes in place after place. Famines. These are the first contractions — labor pains, nothing more.

[9] Keep your eyes open. They will hand you over to councils. They will whip you in synagogues. You'll stand before governors and kings because of me — and that itself will be your testimony to them.

[10] The good news has to be announced to every nation first.

[11] When they arrest you and drag you in, don't agonize beforehand about what to say. Whatever is given to you in that moment — say it. It won't be you speaking. It'll be the Holy Spirit.

[12] Brother will betray brother to death. Father will betray child. Children will rise against their parents and have them killed.

[13] Everyone will hate you because of my name. But the one who holds on to the end — that one will be saved.

[14] When you see the obscenity that desecrates standing where it has no business standing — reader, understand what this means — then those in Judea need to run for the hills.

[15] If you're on the roof, don't go back inside to grab anything.

[16] If you're out in the field, don't go back for your coat.

[17] God help the pregnant women in those days. God help the nursing mothers.

[18] Pray it doesn't happen in winter.

[19] Because those days will bring suffering unlike anything since God made the world — and nothing like it will ever come again.

[20] If the Lord hadn't cut those days short, no one would survive. But for the sake of the chosen — the ones he picked — he has cut them short.

[21] In that moment, if anyone tells you, 'Look — the Messiah is here,' or 'There he is,' don't believe it.

[22] False messiahs will surface. False prophets will surface. They'll perform signs and wonders convincing enough to fool even the chosen, if that were possible.

[23] So stay alert. I've told you everything ahead of time.

[24] In those days, after that suffering — the sun will go dark, the moon will stop shining,

[25] the stars will fall from the sky, and the powers in the heavens will shake.

[26] Then they'll see the Son of Man coming in the clouds with overwhelming power and glory.

[27] And he'll send the angels out to gather his chosen from the four winds — from the farthest edge of earth to the farthest edge of sky.

[28] Learn the lesson the fig tree teaches: when its branch turns tender and leaves push out, you know summer is close.

[29] Same with this. When you see these things happening, know that he is near — right at the door.

[30] I'm telling you the truth: this generation will not pass away until all of this happens.

[31] Heaven and earth will pass away. My words will not.

[32] But the day, the hour — nobody knows. Not the angels in heaven. Not the Son. Only the Father.

[33] Stay alert. Stay awake. You don't know when the time is.

[34] It's like a man going on a journey — he leaves his house, puts his servants in charge, gives each one a job, and tells the doorkeeper to keep watch.

[35] So keep watch. You don't know when the master of the house is coming — evening, midnight, the rooster's crow, or dawn —

[36] in case he shows up suddenly and finds you asleep.

[37] What I'm telling you, I'm telling everyone: stay awake.

[1] Two days out from Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread, the chief priests and the scribes were hunting for a way to grab Jesus quietly and kill him. [2] "Not during the festival," they said. "The crowd will riot."

[3] He was in Bethany, reclining at the table in the house of Simon the leper, when a woman walked in with an alabaster jar of pure nard — costly, the real thing. She broke the jar open and poured it over his head.

[4] Some of them at the table boiled over. "What a waste. [5] That perfume could have sold for three hundred denarii — a year's wages — and the money given to the poor." They turned on her.

[6] But Jesus said, "Leave her alone. Why are you harassing her? She has done something beautiful for me. [7] The poor are always with you, and whenever you want, you can do good for them. But you will not always have me. [8] She did what she could. She anointed my body in advance, for burial. [9] I'm telling you — wherever the good news is preached in this whole world, what she did will be told, in memory of her."

[10] Then Judas Iscariot, one of the Twelve, went to the chief priests to hand him over. [11] They were thrilled when they heard it, and promised him silver. So he started watching for the moment.

[12] On the first day of Unleavened Bread, when the Passover lamb was being slaughtered, his disciples asked him, "Where do you want us to go and prepare the Passover for you to eat?"

[13] He sent two of them ahead. "Go into the city. A man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him. [14] Wherever he goes in, tell the owner of the house, 'The Teacher asks: Where is my guest room, where I can eat the Passover with my disciples?' [15] He will show you a large upstairs room, furnished and ready. Prepare it there."

[16] The disciples went into the city, found everything exactly as he had said, and prepared the Passover.

[17] Evening came. He arrived with the Twelve. [18] As they were reclining and eating, Jesus said, "I'm telling you the truth — one of you will betray me. One of you eating with me right now."

[19] They were gutted. One after another they asked him, "Surely not me?"

[20] He said, "One of the Twelve — the one dipping into the bowl with me. [21] The Son of Man is going, just as it's written about him. But woe to the man who hands him over. Better for that man if he had never been born."

[22] As they ate, he took bread, blessed it, broke it, and gave it to them. "Take it. This is my body."

[23] He took a cup, gave thanks, and handed it to them. They all drank from it. [24] He said, "This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many. [25] I'm telling you — I will not drink the fruit of the vine again until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God."

[26] They sang the hymn and walked out to the Mount of Olives.

[27] Jesus said, "All of you will fall away. It is written:

*I will strike the shepherd,
and the sheep will scatter.*

[28] But after I'm raised, I'll go ahead of you into Galilee."

[29] Peter said, "Even if everyone else falls away — not me."

[30] Jesus answered, "I'm telling you the truth — tonight, before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times."

[31] Peter doubled down. "Even if I have to die with you, I will never deny you." And all the rest said the same.

[32] They came to a place called Gethsemane. He said to his disciples, "Sit here while I pray." [33] He took Peter, James, and John with him, and a horror came over him, a crushing dread. [34] He said to them, "My soul is drowning in grief, to the point of death. Stay here. Stay awake."

[35] He went a little farther, fell to the ground, and prayed that, if it were possible, the hour might pass from him. [36] "Abba — Father — everything is possible for you. Take this cup away from me. But not what I want. What you want."

[37] He came back and found them sleeping. He said to Peter, "Simon, are you sleeping? You couldn't stay awake for one hour? [38] Stay awake. Pray. So you don't fall into the trial. The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak."

[39] He went away again and prayed the same words. [40] He came back and found them sleeping again — their eyes were heavy. They didn't know what to say to him.

[41] He came a third time. "Still sleeping? Still resting? Enough. The hour has come. Look — the Son of Man is being handed over into the hands of sinners. [42] Get up. Let's go. The one betraying me is here."

[43] He was still speaking when Judas — one of the Twelve — showed up, and with him a crowd carrying swords and clubs, sent by the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders. [44] The betrayer had given them a signal: "The one I kiss — that's him. Seize him and lead him away under guard."

[45] He went straight to Jesus. "Rabbi." And he kissed him. [46] They put their hands on him and arrested him. [47] One of the bystanders pulled out a sword, swung, and cut off the ear of the high priest's servant.

[48] Jesus said to them, "You came out with swords and clubs to take me, like I'm some kind of bandit? [49] I was with you every day in the temple, teaching, and you never laid a hand on me. But the Scriptures had to be fulfilled."

[50] Then everyone deserted him and ran.

[51] A young man was following him, wearing nothing but a linen cloth over his bare body. They grabbed him, [52] and he left the cloth behind and bolted naked into the night.

[53] They took Jesus to the high priest. All the chief priests, elders, and scribes gathered. [54] Peter followed at a distance, all the way into the courtyard of the high priest. He sat with the guards, warming himself by the fire.

[55] The chief priests and the whole council were searching for testimony against Jesus that would put him to death — and finding none. [56] Plenty of people lied about him, but their stories didn't match. [57] Some stood up and falsely accused him: [58] "We heard him say, 'I'll tear down this temple made with hands, and in three days build another, not made with hands.'" [59] But even on that, their stories didn't line up.

[60] The high priest stood up in the middle of them and questioned Jesus: "Have you no answer? What is this they're accusing you of?" [61] But he stayed silent. Said nothing.

Again the high priest asked him, "Are you the Messiah, the Son of the Blessed?"

[62] Jesus said, "I am. And you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power, coming with the clouds of heaven."

[63] The high priest tore his robes. "Why do we need any more witnesses? [64] You heard the blasphemy. What's your verdict?"

They all condemned him as deserving death.

[65] Some began to spit on him. They blindfolded him, hit him with their fists, and said, "Prophesy!" The guards took him and beat him.

[66] Peter was below in the courtyard. One of the high priest's servant girls came over, [67] saw him warming himself, and looked him over. "You were with that Nazarene too. Jesus."

[68] He denied it. "I don't know — I have no idea what you're talking about." He went out to the entryway. A rooster crowed.

[69] The servant girl saw him again and started telling the bystanders, "This one is one of them." [70] He denied it again.

A little later the bystanders said to Peter, "You're definitely one of them. You're a Galilean."

[71] He started cursing and swearing oaths: "I do not know this man you're talking about."

[72] Immediately the rooster crowed a second time. And Peter remembered what Jesus had said: *Before the rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times.* And he broke. He wept.

[1] At first light, the chief priests held their council with the elders, the scribes, and the whole Sanhedrin. They bound Jesus, marched him out, and handed him over to Pilate.

[2] Pilate asked him, "You — are you the king of the Jews?" Jesus answered, "Those are your words."

[3] The chief priests piled accusation on accusation.

[4] Pilate pressed him again: "Aren't you going to answer? Look at all they're charging you with."

[5] But Jesus said nothing more. Pilate was stunned.

[6] Now at festival time, Pilate had a custom — he would release one prisoner, whoever the crowd asked for.

[7] A man called Barabbas was sitting in chains with the rebels who had committed murder in the uprising.

[8] The crowd surged up and started demanding that Pilate do what he always did.

[9] Pilate threw it back at them: "You want me to release the king of the Jews?"

[10] He could see plainly that envy was what had driven the chief priests to hand Jesus over.

[11] But the chief priests worked the crowd, urging them to call for Barabbas instead.

[12] So Pilate tried again: "Then what should I do with the one you call king of the Jews?"

[13] They roared back: "Crucify him!"

[14] "Why?" Pilate said. "What has he done wrong?" But they only screamed louder: "Crucify him!"

[15] Pilate, wanting to keep the crowd quiet, released Barabbas. Jesus he had flogged, then handed over to be crucified.

[16] The soldiers hauled him inside the palace — the praetorium — and called the whole cohort together.

[17] They threw a purple robe on him, twisted together a crown of thorns, and jammed it on his head.

[18] Then they started saluting him: "Hail, king of the Jews!"

[19] They beat his head with a reed, spat on him, and dropped to their knees in mock worship.

[20] When they had finished playing with him, they stripped off the purple, put his own clothes back on him, and led him out to crucify him.

[21] A man was passing by, coming in from the countryside — Simon of Cyrene, father of Alexander and Rufus. They grabbed him and forced him to carry the cross.

[22] They brought Jesus to the place called Golgotha — which means Skull Place.

[23] They offered him wine laced with myrrh. He refused it.

[24] Then they crucified him. They divided his clothes among themselves, rolling dice to see who got what.

[25] It was nine in the morning when they nailed him up.

[26] The charge against him was posted above: THE KING OF THE JEWS.

[27] They crucified two bandits with him, one on his right, one on his left.

[29] The people passing by hurled abuse, shaking their heads: "So — you're the one who was going to tear down the temple and rebuild it in three days?

[30] Save yourself! Come down from the cross!"

[31] The chief priests and scribes were mocking him too, trading jokes among themselves: "He saved others. He can't save himself.

[32] Let the Messiah, the king of Israel, come down from the cross right now — then we'll see, then we'll believe." Even the men crucified beside him were throwing insults.

[33] At noon, darkness fell over the whole land. It lasted until three in the afternoon.

[34] At three, Jesus cried out with everything he had: "Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" — which means, "My God, my God, why have you abandoned me?"

[35] Some of the bystanders heard it and said, "Listen — he's calling for Elijah."

[36] One of them ran, soaked a sponge in sour wine, stuck it on a stick, and held it up for him to drink. "Wait," he said. "Let's see if Elijah comes to take him down."

[37] Then Jesus let out one final cry and breathed his last.

[38] And the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom.

[39] The centurion standing right in front of him saw how he died. He said: "This man really was the Son of God."

[40] Women were watching from a distance — Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James the younger and of Joses, and Salome.

[41] They had followed him in Galilee and looked after him. Many other women were there too, the ones who had come up with him to Jerusalem.

[42] Evening was coming on. Because it was the Day of Preparation — the day before the Sabbath —

[43] Joseph of Arimathea worked up his courage and went to Pilate. He was a respected member of the council, a man waiting for the kingdom of God. He asked for the body of Jesus.

[44] Pilate was surprised he was already dead. He summoned the centurion and asked if it had really been that long.

[45] Once the centurion confirmed it, Pilate released the body to Joseph.

[46] Joseph bought a linen cloth, took him down, wrapped him, and laid him in a tomb cut from the rock. Then he rolled a stone across the entrance.

[47] Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses watched where he was laid.

[1] When the Sabbath was done, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices to anoint him.

[2] Early — first light, the first day of the week — they came to the tomb as the sun broke the horizon.

[3] They were asking each other, "Who's going to roll the stone back for us?"

[4] Then they looked up — and the stone, massive as it was, had already been rolled away.

[5] They stepped inside the tomb and saw a young man seated on the right, dressed in white. It rattled them.

[6] He said, "Stop being afraid. You're looking for Jesus the Nazarene, the crucified one. He's been raised. He's not here. Look — this is the place where they laid him.

[7] Now go. Tell his disciples — and Peter — that he's going ahead of you into Galilee. You'll see him there, just as he told you."

[8] They bolted from the tomb, shaking, undone. They said nothing to anyone — they were that afraid.

[9] When he rose, early on the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene — the one he'd freed from seven demons.

[10] She went and told the ones who had been with him, the ones now grieving, weeping.

[11] When they heard he was alive and that she had seen him, they refused to believe it.

[12] After that he appeared in a different form to two of them walking out into the countryside.

[13] They came back and told the others. No one believed them either.

[14] Finally he appeared to the Eleven themselves while they were eating. He laid into them for their unbelief, for the hardness of heart that kept them from trusting the ones who had seen him risen.

[15] Then he said: "Go — into all the world. Announce the good news to every creature alive.

[16] Whoever trusts and is immersed will be saved. Whoever refuses to trust stands condemned.

[17] These signs will travel with the ones who believe: in my name they will drive out demons, they will speak in new tongues,

[18] they will pick up serpents in their hands, and if they drink anything deadly it will not harm them. They will lay hands on the sick, and the sick will recover."

[19] So the Lord Jesus, after speaking with them, was taken up into heaven and took his seat at the right hand of God.

[20] And they went out and preached everywhere, the Lord working alongside them, confirming the message through the signs that followed. Amen.

[1] The record of Jesus the Messiah—son of David, son of Abraham.

[2] Abraham fathered Isaac. Isaac fathered Jacob. Jacob fathered Judah and his brothers. [3] Judah fathered Perez and Zerah by Tamar. Perez fathered Hezron. Hezron fathered Ram. [4] Ram fathered Amminadab. Amminadab fathered Nahshon. Nahshon fathered Salmon. [5] Salmon fathered Boaz by Rahab. Boaz fathered Obed by Ruth. Obed fathered Jesse. [6] Jesse fathered David the king.

David fathered Solomon by the woman who had been Uriah's wife. [7] Solomon fathered Rehoboam. Rehoboam fathered Abijah. Abijah fathered Asaph. [8] Asaph fathered Jehoshaphat. Jehoshaphat fathered Joram. Joram fathered Uzziah. [9] Uzziah fathered Jotham. Jotham fathered Ahaz. Ahaz fathered Hezekiah. [10] Hezekiah fathered Manasseh. Manasseh fathered Amos. Amos fathered Josiah. [11] Josiah fathered Jeconiah and his brothers—right as the exile to Babylon broke over them.

[12] After Babylon: Jeconiah fathered Shealtiel. Shealtiel fathered Zerubbabel. [13] Zerubbabel fathered Abiud. Abiud fathered Eliakim. Eliakim fathered Azor. [14] Azor fathered Zadok. Zadok fathered Achim. Achim fathered Eliud. [15] Eliud fathered Eleazar. Eleazar fathered Matthan. Matthan fathered Jacob. [16] Jacob fathered Joseph—the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, the one called Messiah.

[17] So count it out: fourteen generations from Abraham to David. Fourteen from David to the exile. Fourteen from the exile to the Messiah.

[18] Here is how the birth of Jesus the Messiah happened. His mother Mary was pledged to Joseph—but before they came together, she was found to be carrying a child by the Holy Spirit. [19] Joseph, her husband, was a righteous man and did not want to drag her through public shame, so he resolved to end the engagement quietly.

[20] But while he was turning it over in his mind, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream. "Joseph, son of David, don't be afraid to take Mary as your wife. What has been conceived in her is from the Holy Spirit. [21] She will give birth to a son, and you will name him Jesus—because he will rescue his people from their sins."

[22] All of this happened to fulfill what the Lord had spoken through the prophet:

[23] *Look—the virgin will conceive and bear a son,
and they will call him Immanuel*—
which means, *God with us*.

[24] Joseph woke up and did exactly what the angel of the Lord had told him to do. He took Mary as his wife. [25] But he had no sexual relations with her until she gave birth to a son. And he named him Jesus.

[1] After Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea, during the reign of King Herod, magi from the east arrived in Jerusalem [2] asking, "Where is the one born king of the Jews? We saw his star rising in the east, and we've come to bow before him."

[3] When King Herod heard this, it shook him — and all Jerusalem with him. [4] He summoned every chief priest and scribe of the people and pressed them: where was the Messiah to be born? [5] "Bethlehem of Judea," they told him. "That's what the prophet wrote:

[6] *And you, Bethlehem, in the land of Judah,
are no least thing among Judah's rulers —
for out of you will come a ruler
who will shepherd my people Israel.*"

[7] Then Herod called the magi in secret and extracted from them the exact time the star had appeared. [8] He sent them to Bethlehem with instructions: "Search carefully for the child. When you find him, report back to me so I can come and bow before him too."

[9] They listened to the king and set out. And the star they had seen rising went ahead of them until it stopped over the place where the child was. [10] When they saw the star, joy erupted in them — overwhelming, uncontainable. [11] They entered the house and saw the child with Mary his mother, and they fell to their knees and worshiped him. They opened their treasures and laid before him gold, frankincense, and myrrh. [12] Then, warned in a dream not to return to Herod, they took a different road home.

[13] After they left, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream. "Get up. Take the child and his mother and flee to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you. Herod is going to hunt the child down to kill him."

[14] Joseph got up in the dead of night, took the child and his mother, and left for Egypt. [15] He stayed there until Herod's death — fulfilling what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: *Out of Egypt I called my son.*

[16] When Herod realized the magi had outmaneuvered him, fury consumed him. He gave the order: kill every boy in Bethlehem and the surrounding region, two years old and under — calculated by the time he had pried from the magi. [17] Then what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah came true:

[18] *A voice was heard in Ramah,
weeping and bitter lamentation —
Rachel weeping for her children,
refusing to be comforted,
because they are no more.*

[19] After Herod died, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt [20] and said, "Get up. Take the child and his mother and go back to the land of Israel. The ones who wanted the child dead are dead themselves."

[21] So he got up, took the child and his mother, and entered the land of Israel. [22] But when he heard that Archelaus was reigning over Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there. Warned in a dream, he withdrew to the region of Galilee [23] and settled in a town called Nazareth — fulfilling what was spoken through the prophets: *He will be called a Nazarene.*

[1] Then came John the Baptizer, crashing into the wilderness of Judea with a message:

[2] "Turn around. The kingdom of heaven is closing in."

[3] This was the man Isaiah saw centuries before — the voice ringing out from the wasteland: *Clear the road for the Lord. Make his paths straight.*

[4] John wore camel hair belted with leather. He ate locusts and wild honey.

[5] And they came to him — pouring out of Jerusalem, out of every village in Judea, out of the whole Jordan Valley —

[6] confessing what they had done, going under in the river at his hands.

[7] But when he saw the Pharisees and Sadducees showing up for the baptism, he turned on them: "Brood of vipers — who tipped you off to run from the fire that's coming?

[8] Show me fruit. Show me a life that matches the turning.

[9] And don't start telling yourselves, 'Abraham is our father.' I'm telling you, God can raise children for Abraham out of these rocks at my feet.

[10] The axe is already laid against the root. Every tree that fails to bear good fruit gets chopped down and thrown into the fire.

[11] I baptize you with water — it's a turning. But someone is coming after me who is stronger than I am. I'm not fit to carry his sandals. He will baptize you in the Holy Spirit and in fire.

[12] His winnowing fork is in his hand. He will sweep his threshing floor clean — gather the wheat into his barn, and burn the chaff in a fire nothing puts out."

[13] Then Jesus came from Galilee, walking up to the Jordan to be baptized by John.

[14] John tried to stop him. "I should be baptized by you — and you come to me?"

[15] Jesus answered, "Let it happen this way. This is how we fulfill all that's right." So John let him.

[16] The moment Jesus came up out of the water — the heavens tore open. He saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, settling on him.

[17] And a voice from the heavens: *This is my Son. The one I love. My delight rests on him.*

[1] Then the Spirit led Jesus into the wilderness — straight into the testing ground, straight into the devil's reach.

[2] Forty days. Forty nights. No food. By the end, hunger was eating him alive.

[3] The tempter showed up and said, "If you're really the Son of God, tell these stones to turn into bread."

[4] Jesus answered, "It's written: humans don't survive on bread alone, but on every word coming out of God's mouth."

[5] Next, the devil hauled him into the holy city, set him on the pinnacle of the temple,

[6] and said, "If you're really the Son of God, throw yourself down. After all, it's written: *He'll order his angels concerning you*, and *they'll catch you in their hands so you don't even bruise your foot on a stone*."

[7] Jesus shot back, "It's also written: *Don't put the Lord your God to the test*."

[8] One more time. The devil took him up — this time to a mountain so high you could see every kingdom on earth, every empire in its full glory —

[9] and said, "All of it. Yours. Just bow down and worship me."

[10] Jesus said, "Get out, Satan. It's written: *Worship the Lord your God. Serve him. Him alone.*"
