[11] A day is coming to rebuild your walls — a day when your borders push outward.

[12] On that day they'll come streaming to you — from Assyria, from the cities of Egypt, from Egypt to the Euphrates, from sea to sea, from mountain to mountain.

[13] But the earth will become a wasteland because of those who live on it — the fruit of what they've done.

[14] Shepherd your people with your staff, the flock of your inheritance, dwelling alone in a forest in the middle of fertile land. Let them graze in Bashan and Gilead like in the old days.

[15] As in the days you came out of Egypt — I'll show you wonders.

[16] The nations will see it and be ashamed of all their power. They'll clap their hands over their mouths; their ears will go deaf.

[17] They'll lick dust like a snake, like things that crawl in the dirt. They'll come trembling out of their strongholds to the LORD our God, terrified, afraid of you.

[18] Who is a God like you — lifting guilt, walking past the rebellion of what remains of his inheritance? He doesn't hold onto his anger forever, because mercy is what he delights in.

[19] He'll come back to us with compassion. He'll stomp our sins underfoot. He'll hurl every one of our wrongs into the depths of the sea.

[20] You'll keep faith with Jacob, steady love with Abraham — the promise you swore to our fathers in the days of old.

[1] The verdict against Nineveh. The vision of Nahum of Elkosh.

[2] The LORD is a jealous God — and He settles accounts. The LORD settles accounts, and His fury runs deep. He pays back His enemies. He stockpiles wrath for those who hate Him.

[3] The LORD is patient — but never weak. He will not let the guilty walk free. His path runs through whirlwind and storm; the clouds are dust kicked up by His feet.

[4] He barks at the sea and it dries up. He drains every river. Bashan and Carmel wither. The blossoms of Lebanon turn brown.

[5] Mountains quake in His presence. Hills dissolve. The earth itself buckles before His face — the whole world and everyone living on it.

[6] Who can stand when He's enraged? Who can endure the heat of His fury? His wrath pours out like fire. Boulders shatter where He walks.

[7] But the LORD is good — a stronghold when trouble comes. He knows the ones who run to Him.

[8] And the ones who oppose Him? He'll wash them away in a flood that drowns the city itself. He'll chase His enemies into darkness.

[9] Whatever you're plotting against the LORD — He'll end it. Trouble will not rise a second time.

[10] Tangled like thornbushes, soaked in their own drink, they'll burn like dry stubble — every last one.

[11] Nineveh — from you came the one who schemed evil against the LORD, the architect of ruin.

[12] This is what the LORD says: Though they stand strong and many, they will be cut down and pass away. I have afflicted you, Judah — but I won't do it again.

[13] Now I shatter the yoke off your neck. I rip your chains apart.

[14] And to you, Nineveh, the LORD has issued the order: your name dies with you. No more children to carry it. I will gut your temples of every idol, every image. I am digging your grave — because you are contemptible.

[15] Look — there on the mountains — the feet of the messenger bringing good news, announcing peace. Celebrate your festivals, Judah. Pay your vows. The wicked will never march through you again. He's been wiped out — completely.

[1] The shatterer is climbing toward you, Nineveh. Man the walls. Watch the road. Brace yourself. Gather every ounce of strength you have left.

[2] The LORD is restoring Jacob's glory — yes, Israel's glory too — because plunderers stripped them bare and shredded the vines that gave them life.

[3] His warriors carry shields of blood-red. His soldiers blaze in scarlet. The chariots flash like fire on the day he calls them out, and the cypress spears are brandished, hungry.

[4] Chariots tear through the streets. They careen across the squares. They look like torches. They strike like lightning.

[5] He summons his officers — they stumble as they run, scrambling to reach the wall. The siege shield is locked into place.

[6] The river gates burst open. The palace dissolves.

[7] It is decreed: she is stripped, she is hauled away. Her handmaids moan like doves, beating their breasts.

[8] Nineveh is a pool whose water is draining out. "Stop! Stop!" — but no one turns back.

[9] Plunder the silver! Plunder the gold! There is no end to the treasure, wealth heaped on wealth from every precious thing.

[10] Emptied. Stripped. Ravaged. Hearts melt, knees buckle, every body trembles, every face goes ashen.

[11] So where is the lions' den now? Where is the feeding ground of the young lions, where the lion and lioness prowled, where their cubs played and nothing made them afraid?

[12] The lion that tore prey enough for his cubs, that strangled meat for his mate, that filled his caves with carcasses and his lairs with shredded flesh —

[13] Look at me, says the LORD of armies. I am against you. I will burn your chariots to smoke. The sword will devour your young lions. I will cut off your prey from the earth. The voice of your messengers — never heard again.

[1] City of blood — soaked through with lies, packed wall-to-wall with plunder, never empty of prey.

[2] Listen! The crack of whips. The thunder of wheels. Horses pounding, chariots leaping —

[3] cavalry charging, swords flashing, spears like lightning. Bodies heaped. Corpses stacked. Dead beyond counting — they trip over them as they go.

[4] All because of her — the seductress, beautiful and deadly, mistress of dark arts, who sold nations into slavery with her body and peoples with her witchcraft.

[5] I'm coming for you, says the LORD of Armies. I'll yank your skirts up over your face. I'll show the nations everything underneath — every kingdom will see your shame.

[6] I'll bury you in filth, treat you like garbage, set you up as a spectacle for the world to gawk at.

[7] Anyone who sees you will recoil and say: Nineveh is a ruin — who's left to mourn her? Where would I even find someone to comfort you?

[8] Are you any better than Thebes, perched on the Nile, water wrapped around her, the river her wall, the sea her defense?

[9] Cush and Egypt were her endless strength. Put and the Libyans were her allies.

[10] And still — she was dragged off. Marched into exile. Her infants smashed at every street corner. Lots cast for her noblemen. Her great men shackled in chains.

[11] You're next. You'll stagger drunk. You'll go into hiding. You'll grope for shelter from the enemy.

[12] Every fortress of yours is a fig tree heavy with first-ripe fruit — shake the branch and the fruit drops straight into the mouth of whoever's hungry.

[13] Look at your troops — women, every one of them. Your gates stand wide open to your enemies. Fire has eaten through the bars.

[14] Go ahead — draw water for the siege. Fortify your defenses. Get into the clay, tread the mortar, grab the brick mold!

[15] Fire will devour you there anyway. The sword will cut you down. It will eat you like locusts eat. Multiply yourselves like locusts. Swarm like grasshoppers!

[16] You stacked up merchants thicker than stars in the sky — but locusts shed their skin and fly away.

[17] Your officials are a locust swarm, your officers a cloud of grasshoppers settled on the walls on a cold day — the sun rises, they scatter, and nobody knows where they went.

[18] King of Assyria — your shepherds are asleep. Your nobles lie down and never get up. Your people are scattered across the mountains with no one to gather them.

[19] There's no healing for your wound. The blow is fatal. Everyone who hears the news claps their hands over you — because who hasn't felt the lash of your endless cruelty?

[1] The words of Nehemiah son of Hakaliah.

In the month of Kislev, in the twentieth year, I was in the citadel of Susa [2] when Hanani — one of my brothers — arrived from Judah with some men. I pressed them for news: What about the Jews who survived, the ones who escaped the exile? What about Jerusalem?

[3] Their answer cut me down: "The remnant back in the province are in deep trouble. Disgraced. The wall of Jerusalem lies in ruins. Its gates — burned to ash."

[4] When I heard it, I sank to the ground and wept. For days I mourned. I fasted. I prayed before the God of heaven.

[5] I said:

"Lord, God of heaven — great, terrifying, the one who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commands — [6] open your ears. Open your eyes. Hear the prayer of your servant praying before you now, day and night, for your servants, the people of Israel. I confess the sins of Israel — sins we have sinned against you. My own family and I, we have sinned too. [7] We've wrecked it. We've trampled the commands, the statutes, the rulings you gave to your servant Moses.

[8] "Remember what you told Moses: 'If you betray me, I will scatter you among the nations. [9] But if you turn back to me, if you guard my commands and live them out — even if your exiles are at the far edge of the sky, I will gather them from there and bring them to the place I have chosen as the home for my name.'

[10] "These are your servants. Your people. The ones you rescued with raw power and a strong hand. [11] Lord, please — let your ear be tuned to the prayer of this servant, and to the prayer of every servant who delights to honor your name. Give your servant success today. Grant me favor in the sight of this man."

I was the cupbearer to the king.

[1] In the month of Nisan, in the twentieth year of King Artaxerxes, wine was set before him. I picked it up and handed it to the king. I had never looked sad in his presence before.

[2] The king said to me, "Why the long face? You're not sick. This is something deeper — this is grief."

I was terrified.

[3] But I answered him, "Long live the king. How can my face not be heavy when the city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins, its gates burned to ash?"

[4] The king said, "What are you asking for?"

I prayed — fast, silent — to the God of heaven.

[5] Then I said to the king, "If it pleases the king, and if your servant has earned your favor, send me to Judah, to the city where my ancestors are buried, so I can rebuild it."

[6] The king — the queen sitting beside him — asked, "How long will your trip take? When will you be back?" It pleased him to send me, so I gave him a date.

[7] Then I said, "If it pleases the king, give me letters for the governors of the province Beyond the River, granting me safe passage to Judah.

[8] And a letter for Asaph, keeper of the king's forest, so he'll give me timber — beams for the gates of the temple fortress, for the city wall, and for the house I'll be living in." The king granted it all, because the gracious hand of my God was on me.

[9] So I went to the governors of Beyond the River and delivered the king's letters. The king had also sent army officers and cavalry with me.

[10] When Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite official heard about it, they were furious — disgusted — that someone had come to seek the good of the people of Israel.

[11] I arrived in Jerusalem. Three days passed.

[12] Then I got up in the night with a few men. I had told no one what my God had put in my heart to do for Jerusalem. The only animal with me was the one I was riding.

[13] I rode out by night through the Valley Gate, past the Dragon Spring, to the Dung Gate, inspecting the walls of Jerusalem — broken, scattered — and the gates burned hollow.

[14] I went on to the Fountain Gate and the King's Pool, but there was no room for my mount to get through.

[15] So I went up the valley by night, examining the wall, then turned back and re-entered through the Valley Gate.

[16] The officials had no idea where I'd gone or what I was doing. I hadn't yet said anything to the Jews — not to the priests, the nobles, the officials, or anyone else who would do the work.

[17] Then I told them: "You see the trouble we're in. Jerusalem lies in ruins. Her gates are burned. Come — let's rebuild the wall of Jerusalem and end this disgrace."

[18] I told them how the hand of my God had been good to me, and what the king had said. They said, "Let's start building." And they put their hands to the good work.

[19] But when Sanballat the Horonite, Tobiah the Ammonite official, and Geshem the Arab heard, they mocked us and sneered, "What is this you're doing? Are you rebelling against the king?"

[20] I answered them: "The God of heaven — He is the one who will give us success. We, His servants, are going to start building. As for you — you have no share, no claim, no memory in Jerusalem."

[1] When Sanballat heard we were rebuilding the wall, he lost it. He raged, he mocked, he came after the Jews with everything he had.

[2] In front of his cronies and the army of Samaria, he sneered: "What do these pathetic Jews think they're doing? Are they going to fortify the place? Offer sacrifices? Finish in a day? Resurrect stones out of heaps of rubble — burnt rubble at that?"

[3] Tobiah the Ammonite, standing next to him, piled on: "That wall of theirs? A fox could jump on it and the whole thing would collapse."

[4] Hear us, our God — we're being treated like garbage. Turn their insults back on their own heads. Hand them over as plunder in a land where they'll be the captives.

[5] Don't cover their guilt. Don't wipe their sin from your sight. They've thrown their contempt straight at the builders.

[6] So we kept building. The wall came together — every gap closing, height rising to half its full measure — because the people had heart for the work.

[7] But when Sanballat, Tobiah, the Arabs, the Ammonites, and the Ashdodites heard the repairs were advancing and the breaches were closing, fury took them.

[8] They conspired together — all of them — to march on Jerusalem and throw the work into chaos.

[9] So we prayed to our God. And we posted a guard, day and night, against them.

[10] Meanwhile, the people of Judah were saying: "The strength of the workers is failing. There's too much rubble. We can't rebuild this wall."

[11] And our enemies were saying: "They won't see us coming. They won't know what hit them. We'll be in the middle of them, killing, before the work even stops."

[12] Then the Jews who lived near them came to us — ten times over, from every direction — warning: "They're coming for you. From everywhere."

[13] So I stationed people in the lowest places behind the wall, in the exposed spots. I positioned them by family — with swords, with spears, with bows.

[14] I looked around. Then I stood up and said to the nobles, to the officials, to everyone: "Don't be afraid of them. Remember the Lord — great, terrifying — and fight for your brothers, your sons, your daughters, your wives, your homes."

[15] When our enemies heard we were onto them — that God had wrecked their plan — we all went back to the wall, every one of us, back to the work.

[16] From that day on, half my men did the building. The other half held spears, shields, bows, and armor. The officers stood behind the whole house of Judah —

[17] every builder on the wall, every laborer hauling materials, did the work with one hand and gripped a weapon with the other.

[18] Every builder had his sword strapped to his side as he worked. And the man with the trumpet stayed beside me.

[19] I told the nobles, the officials, and the rest of the people: "The work is vast. We're spread out along the wall, far from one another.

[20] Wherever you hear the trumpet, gather to us there. Our God will fight for us."

[21] So we worked. Half the men holding spears from the crack of dawn until the stars came out.

[22] I also told everyone: "Every man and his helper — stay inside Jerusalem at night. By night, they guard us. By day, they work."

[23] None of us took off our clothes — not me, not my brothers, not my men, not the guards at my side. Every man kept his weapon, even when he went for water.

[1] Then a massive outcry erupted — the people and their wives crying out against their own Jewish brothers.

[2] Some were saying: "We have huge families. We need grain just to eat and stay alive."

[3] Others were saying: "We're mortgaging our fields, our vineyards, our houses — anything to get grain during this famine."

[4] And still others: "We've had to borrow money against our fields and vineyards just to pay the king's tax.

[5] We're the same flesh and blood as our brothers — our children are just as good as theirs — yet here we are, forced to sell our sons and daughters into slavery. Some of our daughters have already been taken. And we're powerless, because our fields and vineyards belong to someone else now."

[6] When I heard their outcry and these words, fury burned through me.

[7] I thought it over carefully, then confronted the nobles and officials head-on. I told them: "You're charging interest to your own brothers — bleeding your own people." So I called a public assembly to deal with them

[8] and said: "We've been buying back our Jewish brothers who were sold to foreigners — doing everything we can. And now *you're* selling your brothers, only to have us buy them back again?" They went silent. Not a word.

[9] Then I said: "What you're doing is wrong. Shouldn't you walk in the fear of our God, if only to silence the mockery of the nations watching us?

[10] I myself, my brothers, my men — we've all been lending money and grain. Let's drop the interest. Now.

[11] Give it back to them — today. Their fields, vineyards, olive groves, houses. And the interest you've been charging on the money, the grain, the new wine, the oil — return it."

[12] They answered: "We'll give it back. We won't demand anything from them. We'll do exactly what you say." Then I called the priests and made the nobles and officials swear an oath to follow through.

[13] I also shook out the folds of my robe and declared: "May God shake out like this every man who fails to keep this promise — shake him out of his house, out of his property, leaving him empty." The whole assembly said, "Amen," and praised the Lord. And the people did what they had promised.

[14] On top of that — from the day I was appointed governor of Judah, from year twenty to year thirty-two of King Artaxerxes, twelve full years — neither I nor my brothers ever ate the food allotted to the governor.

[15] The governors before me had laid heavy burdens on the people, taking forty shekels of silver from them on top of food and wine. Even their assistants threw their weight around. But I didn't do that. I feared God.

[16] Instead, I threw myself into the work on this wall. We bought no land. Every one of my men was gathered there for the work.

[17] And at my table — a hundred and fifty Jews and officials, plus those who came to us from the surrounding nations — I fed them all.

[18] Every day: one ox, six choice sheep, poultry, and every ten days a fresh supply of wine in abundance. Yet through all of this, I never demanded the governor's food allowance — the burden on these people was already crushing.

[19] Remember me, my God, for good — for everything I've done for this people.

[1] Word reached Sanballat, Tobiah, Geshem the Arab, and the rest of our enemies that I had rebuilt the wall — that no breach remained, though I had not yet hung the doors in the gates.

[2] So Sanballat and Geshem sent word: "Come — let's meet in one of the villages on the plain of Ono." They meant to harm me.

[3] I sent messengers back: "I'm doing important work. I can't come down. Why should the work stop while I leave it to come to you?"

[4] Four times they sent the same message. Four times I answered the same way.

[5] The fifth time, Sanballat sent his aide with the same offer — but this time with an open letter in his hand.

[6] It read: "Word is going around among the nations — and Geshem confirms it — that you and the Jews are plotting rebellion. That's why you're building the wall. According to these reports, you intend to crown yourself king,

[8] I sent back: "Nothing you're saying is true. You're making it up out of thin air."

[9] They were all trying to scare us, thinking, "Their hands will go slack, the work will collapse." But I prayed: God — strengthen my hands.

[10] Then I went to the house of Shemaiah son of Delaiah, son of Mehetabel — he was shut in at home. He said, "Let's meet at the house of God, inside the temple. Let's lock the doors. They're coming to kill you — tonight. They're coming for your life."

[11] I said: "Should a man like me run? Should someone like me dive into the temple to save his own skin? I won't go."

[12] I saw it clearly: God had not sent him. He had prophesied against me because Tobiah and Sanballat had bought him.

[13] He was hired to scare me into sinning — to give them a story they could smear me with, to ruin my name.

[14] My God — remember Tobiah and Sanballat for what they've done. And the prophetess Noadiah and the rest of the prophets who tried to intimidate me — remember them too.

[15] The wall was finished on the twenty-fifth of Elul. Fifty-two days.

[16] When our enemies heard, when all the surrounding nations saw it, they lost their nerve — they realized this work had been done by our God.

[17] During those days the nobles of Judah were sending letter after letter to Tobiah, and Tobiah's letters kept arriving back.

[18] Many in Judah were sworn to him — he was son-in-law to Shecaniah son of Arah, and his son Jehohanan had married the daughter of Meshullam son of Berechiah.

[19] They kept praising his good deeds to my face and reporting my words back to him. And Tobiah kept sending letters to intimidate me.

[61] These came up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addon, and Immer — but they could not prove their fathers' houses or whether they were Israelite by blood:

[66] The whole assembly came to 42,360,

[67] not counting their 7,337 male and female servants. They also had 245 singers, men and women.

[68] Their horses — 736. Their mules — 245.

[69] Their camels — 435. Their donkeys — 6,720.

[70] Some of the family heads chipped in for the work. The governor gave to the treasury 1,000 gold darics, 50 basins, and 530 priestly garments.

[71] Some of the family heads gave 20,000 gold darics and 2,200 minas of silver to the work fund.

[72] The rest of the people gave 20,000 gold darics, 2,000 minas of silver, and 67 priestly garments.

[73] So the priests, the Levites, the gatekeepers, the singers, some of the people, the temple servants, and all Israel settled back into their towns. And when the seventh month rolled around, Israel was home.

[1] When the seventh month came, all the people gathered as one in the open square in front of the Water Gate. They called for Ezra the scribe to bring out the scroll of the Law of Moses—the Law the LORD had laid on Israel.

[2] So Ezra the priest carried the Law before the assembly: men, women, everyone old enough to understand. This was the first day of the seventh month.

[3] From first light until midday, facing the square at the Water Gate, he read it aloud to them—men, women, and all who could grasp it. And every ear was tuned to the scroll of the Law.

[4] Ezra stood on a wooden platform built for the occasion. Beside him on his right stood Mattithiah, Shema, Anaiah, Uriah, Hilkiah, and Maaseiah; on his left, Pedaiah, Mishael, Malchijah, Hashum, Hashbaddanah, Zechariah, and Meshullam.

[5] Ezra opened the scroll where everyone could see—he was higher than the crowd—and the moment he opened it, every person rose to their feet.

[6] Ezra blessed the LORD, the great God, and the whole assembly lifted their hands and answered, "Amen! Amen!" Then they bowed low, faces to the ground, and worshiped the LORD.

[7] The Levites—Jeshua, Bani, Sherebiah, Jamin, Akkub, Shabbethai, Hodiah, Maaseiah, Kelita, Azariah, Jozabad, Hanan, Pelaiah—moved through the crowd helping the people understand the Law as they stood there.

[8] They read from the scroll of God's Law, making it clear, giving the sense, so the people could grasp what was being read.

[9] Then Nehemiah the governor, Ezra the priest and scribe, and the Levites who were teaching the people said to them all: "This day is holy to the LORD your God. Don't mourn. Don't weep." Because every person there was weeping as they heard the words of the Law.

[10] He told them, "Go. Eat the richest food. Drink something sweet. Send portions to anyone who has nothing ready. This day is holy to our Lord. Don't be crushed by grief—the joy of the LORD is your stronghold."

[11] The Levites quieted the people, saying, "Hush. This day is holy. Don't grieve."

[12] So the people went off to eat and drink, to send portions out, to celebrate with enormous joy—because now they understood the words that had been taught to them.

[13] On the second day, the heads of the families, the priests, and the Levites gathered around Ezra the scribe to study the words of the Law more closely.

[14] And there in the Law—commanded by the LORD through Moses—they found it written: the Israelites were to live in booths during the festival of the seventh month,

[15] and they were to spread the word through all their towns and through Jerusalem: "Go up to the hills. Bring back olive and wild olive branches, myrtle, palm, and shade trees—build booths, as it is written."

[16] So the people went out and brought the branches and built themselves booths—each on their own roof, in their courtyards, in the courts of God's house, in the square at the Water Gate, in the square at the Gate of Ephraim.

[17] The whole assembly—everyone who had come back from exile—built booths and lived in them. Israel hadn't done this since the days of Joshua son of Nun. The joy was overwhelming.

[18] Day after day, from the first day to the last, Ezra read from the scroll of God's Law. They kept the festival for seven days, and on the eighth day held a solemn assembly, exactly as prescribed.

[1] On the twenty-fourth day of that same month, the Israelites gathered—fasting, dressed in sackcloth, dirt smeared on their heads.

[2] Those of Israelite descent cut themselves off from every foreigner and stood there confessing their own sins and the corruption of their ancestors.

[3] For a quarter of the day they stood in place and read aloud from the scroll of the Law of the LORD their God. For another quarter they confessed and worshiped the LORD their God.

[4] On the platform stood the Levites—Jeshua, Bani, Kadmiel, Shebaniah, Bunni, Sherebiah, Bani, Kenani—and they cried out to the LORD their God, voices loud.

[5] Then the Levites—Jeshua, Kadmiel, Bani, Hashabneiah, Sherebiah, Hodiah, Shebaniah, Pethahiah—said: "Stand up. Bless the LORD your God from forever to forever. Bless his glorious name, which towers above every blessing and every word of praise."

[6] "You alone are the LORD. You made the heavens—the highest heavens and all their armies, the earth and everything on it, the seas and everything in them. You give life to it all. The armies of heaven bow down to you.

[7] You are the LORD God who chose Abram—pulled him out of Ur of the Chaldeans and gave him a new name: Abraham.

[8] You found his heart faithful before you. You cut a covenant with him to give his descendants the land of the Canaanite, Hittite, Amorite, Perizzite, Jebusite, and Girgashite. And you kept your word—because you are righteous.

[9] You saw our ancestors crushed in Egypt. You heard them crying out at the Red Sea.

[10] You unleashed signs and wonders against Pharaoh, against all his officials, against everyone in his land—because you knew how arrogantly they treated our people. You made a name for yourself that still stands today.

[11] You split the sea wide open in front of them. They walked through on dry ground. Their pursuers you hurled into the depths like a stone into raging waters.

[12] By day you led them with a pillar of cloud, by night with a pillar of fire, lighting the road they had to travel.

[13] You came down on Mount Sinai. You spoke to them from heaven. You gave them just regulations, reliable instructions, good decrees and commands.

[14] You made known to them your holy Sabbath and through your servant Moses you handed down commands, decrees, and laws.

[15] When they were hungry, you gave them bread from heaven. When they were thirsty, you brought water out of rock. You told them: Go in. Take possession of the land I swore with raised hand to give you.

[16] But they—our ancestors—turned arrogant. They stiffened their necks and refused to listen to your commands.

[17] They refused to obey. They forgot the wonders you had worked among them. They hardened their necks and chose a leader to march them right back into slavery. But you—you are a forgiving God, gracious and compassionate, slow to anger, overflowing with steadfast love. You did not abandon them.

[18] Even when they cast for themselves a metal calf and said, 'This is your god who brought you up out of Egypt,' and blasphemed you to your face—

[19] still, in your overwhelming compassion, you did not abandon them in the wilderness. The pillar of cloud never left them by day; the pillar of fire kept lighting the night and showing them the way.

[20] You gave them your good Spirit to teach them. You did not withhold manna from their mouths. You gave them water for their thirst.

[21] For forty years you sustained them in the wilderness. They lacked nothing. Their clothes did not wear out. Their feet did not swell.

[22] You handed them kingdoms and peoples, parceling out every corner. They took possession of Sihon's land—the land of the king of Heshbon—and of Og king of Bashan.

[23] You multiplied their children like the stars of heaven and brought them into the land you had told their ancestors to enter and possess.

[24] So the children went in and took the land. You crushed the Canaanites who lived there—handed kings and peoples over to them, theirs to deal with as they saw fit.

[25] They captured fortified cities and rich soil. They took over houses stocked with every good thing—cisterns already dug, vineyards, olive groves, fruit trees beyond counting. They ate. They were full. They grew fat. They luxuriated in your massive goodness.

[26] But then they defied you. They rebelled. They threw your Law behind their backs. They murdered your prophets—the ones who confronted them, trying to turn them back to you. They blasphemed without restraint.

[27] So you handed them over to enemies who ground them down. Then, in their distress, they cried out to you. And from heaven you heard. In your immense compassion you sent deliverers who rescued them from the hand of their enemies.

[28] But the moment they had rest, they did evil again before you. So you abandoned them to their enemies, who took control of them. They cried out again, and you heard again, and time after time, in your compassion, you rescued them.

[29] You warned them, trying to bring them back to your Law. But they swelled with arrogance. They refused your commands. They sinned against the very rulings that bring life to whoever obeys them. They turned a stubborn shoulder, stiffened their neck, would not listen.

[30] Year after year you bore with them. By your Spirit you warned them through your prophets. They paid no attention. So you handed them over to the peoples of the lands.

[31] But in your overwhelming compassion you did not finish them off. You did not abandon them. Because you are a gracious and compassionate God.

[32] So now, our God—the great, the mighty, the awesome God who keeps covenant and steadfast love—do not minimize all this hardship that has come upon us: on our kings, our officials, our priests, our prophets, our ancestors, all your people, from the days of the kings of Assyria until this very day.

[33] In everything that has come down on us, you have been just. You acted faithfully. We were the ones in the wrong.

[34] Our kings, our officials, our priests, our ancestors—none of them kept your Law. They ignored your commands and the warnings you pressed upon them.

[35] Even in their own kingdom, in the lavish goodness you poured out on them, in the spacious and fertile land you set before them—they did not serve you. They did not turn from their evil ways.

[36] And look at us now: slaves. Slaves in the very land you gave our ancestors to enjoy its fruit, its abundance—and we are slaves on it.

[37] Its rich harvests pile up for the kings you set over us because of our sins. They rule over our bodies and our livestock as they please. We are in deep distress.

[38] Because of all this, we are making a binding agreement, putting it in writing. Our leaders, our Levites, and our priests are setting their seals to it."

[1] These are the names sealed to the covenant: Nehemiah the governor, son of Hacaliah, and Zedekiah. [2] Then Seraiah, Azariah, Jeremiah, [3] Pashhur, Amariah, Malchijah, [4] Hattush, Shebaniah, Malluch, [5] Harim, Meremoth, Obadiah, [6] Daniel, Ginnethon, Baruch, [7] Meshullam, Abijah, Mijamin, [8] Maaziah, Bilgai, Shemaiah — these were the priests.

[9] The Levites: Jeshua son of Azaniah, Binnui of the line of Henadad, Kadmiel, [10] and their associates — Shebaniah, Hodiah, Kelita, Pelaiah, Hanan, [11] Mica, Rehob, Hashabiah, [12] Zaccur, Sherebiah, Shebaniah, [13] Hodiah, Bani, Beninu.

[14] The leaders of the people: Parosh, Pahath-moab, Elam, Zattu, Bani, [15] Bunni, Azgad, Bebai, [16] Adonijah, Bigvai, Adin, [17] Ater, Hezekiah, Azzur, [18] Hodiah, Hashum, Bezai, [19] Hariph, Anathoth, Nebai, [20] Magpiash, Meshullam, Hezir, [21] Meshezabel, Zadok, Jaddua, [22] Pelatiah, Hanan, Anaiah, [23] Hoshea, Hananiah, Hasshub, [24] Hallohesh, Pilha, Shobek, [25] Rehum, Hashabnah, Maaseiah, [26] Ahiah, Hanan, Anan, [27] Malluch, Harim, Baanah.

[28] As for the rest of us — the people, the priests, the Levites, the gatekeepers, the singers, the temple servants, every soul who had broken with the peoples of the surrounding lands to take hold of God's law, along with our wives, our sons, our daughters, everyone old enough to understand — [29] we joined our leaders, our kin, our nobles, and bound ourselves with an oath under curse: we will walk in God's law, given through Moses his servant, and keep every command, ruling, and decree of the LORD our Sovereign.

[30] We will not give our daughters to the peoples of the land or take their daughters for our sons.

[31] When the peoples of the land bring goods or grain to sell on the Sabbath, we will not buy from them on the Sabbath or on any holy day. Every seventh year we will let the land rest and cancel every debt owed to us.

[32] We take it on ourselves as binding: a third of a shekel each year, every one of us, for the service of the house of our God — [33] for the bread set out, the continual grain offering, the continual burnt offering, the offerings on Sabbaths, new moons, and appointed feasts, for the holy gifts, for the sin offerings that make atonement for Israel, and for all the work of the house of our God.

[34] We cast lots — priests, Levites, and people alike — for the wood offering: each family bringing wood to the house of our God at appointed times year by year, to burn on the altar of the LORD our God, as the law requires.

[35] We commit to bring the firstfruits of our soil and the firstfruits of every tree, year by year, to the house of the LORD; [36] and the firstborn of our sons and our livestock — as the law requires — and the firstborn of our herds and flocks brought to the house of our God, to the priests who serve there.

[37] We will bring to the priests, into the storerooms of the house of our God, the first of our dough, our grain offerings, the fruit of every tree, the new wine and the oil. And to the Levites we will bring the tithes of our soil — they are the ones who collect the tithes in all the towns where we work the land.

[38] A priest from Aaron's line will be with the Levites when they collect, and the Levites will bring a tenth of the tithes up to the house of our God, into the storerooms of the treasury.

[39] To those storerooms the people of Israel and the Levites are to bring the contributions of grain, new wine, and oil. That is where the sacred vessels are kept, where the priests on duty serve, and the gatekeepers and singers.

We will not neglect the house of our God.

[1] The leaders of the people settled in Jerusalem. The rest cast lots — one out of every ten would move into the holy city, the other nine stayed in their hometowns. [2] And the people blessed everyone who volunteered to live in Jerusalem.

[3] These are the heads of the province who took up residence in Jerusalem — though throughout the towns of Judah, people lived on their own ancestral land: Israelites, priests, Levites, temple servants, and descendants of Solomon's servants.

[4] In Jerusalem, some of the sons of Judah and some of the sons of Benjamin settled.

From Judah: Athaiah son of Uzziah, son of Zechariah, son of Amariah, son of Shephatiah, son of Mahalalel — from the line of Perez. [5] And Maaseiah son of Baruch, son of Col-hozeh, son of Hazaiah, son of Adaiah, son of Joiarib, son of Zechariah — from the line of Shelah. [6] All the sons of Perez living in Jerusalem totaled 468 — fighting men.

[7] From Benjamin: Sallu son of Meshullam, son of Joed, son of Pedaiah, son of Kolaiah, son of Maaseiah, son of Ithiel, son of Jeshaiah, [8] and after him Gabbai and Sallai — 928 in all. [9] Joel son of Zichri was their overseer, and Judah son of Hassenuah was second in command over the city.

[10] From the priests: Jedaiah son of Joiarib, Jachin, [11] and Seraiah son of Hilkiah, son of Meshullam, son of Zadok, son of Meraioth, son of Ahitub — the chief officer of God's house. [12] Their kinsmen who carried out the work of the temple numbered 822. Also Adaiah son of Jeroham, son of Pelaliah, son of Amzi, son of Zechariah, son of Pashhur, son of Malchijah, [13] along with his kinsmen — heads of families — 242 in all. And Amashsai son of Azarel, son of Ahzai, son of Meshillemoth, son of Immer, [14] with his kinsmen, mighty warriors — 128. Their commander was Zabdiel son of Haggedolim.

[15] From the Levites: Shemaiah son of Hasshub, son of Azrikam, son of Hashabiah, son of Bunni. [16] Shabbethai and Jozabad — Levite chiefs who handled the outside business of God's house. [17] Mattaniah son of Mica, son of Zabdi, son of Asaph, who led the thanksgiving in prayer. Bakbukiah, second among his brothers. And Abda son of Shammua, son of Galal, son of Jeduthun. [18] All the Levites in the holy city: 284.

[19] The gatekeepers: Akkub, Talmon, and their kinsmen who kept watch at the gates — 172.

[20] The rest of Israel — priests and Levites — were spread across the towns of Judah, each on his ancestral inheritance.

[21] The temple servants lived on the hill of Ophel, with Ziha and Gishpa over them.

[22] The overseer of the Levites in Jerusalem was Uzzi son of Bani, son of Hashabiah, son of Mattaniah, son of Mica — from the sons of Asaph, the singers responsible for the worship in God's house. [23] A royal decree governed them, fixing a daily allotment for the singers.

[24] Pethahiah son of Meshezabel, from the sons of Zerah son of Judah, served as the king's deputy in every matter touching the people.

[25] As for the villages with their surrounding fields — some from Judah settled in Kiriath-arba and its outlying towns, Dibon and its towns, Jekabzeel and its villages, [26] in Jeshua, Moladah, Beth-pelet, [27] Hazar-shual, Beersheba and its towns, [28] Ziklag, Meconah and its towns, [29] En-rimmon, Zorah, Jarmuth, [30] Zanoah, Adullam and their villages, Lachish and its fields, Azekah and its towns. They settled the land from Beersheba all the way to the Valley of Hinnom.

[31] The Benjaminites settled from Geba onward — in Michmash, Aija, Bethel and its towns, [32] Anathoth, Nob, Ananiah, [33] Hazor, Ramah, Gittaim, [34] Hadid, Zeboim, Neballat, [35] Lod, Ono, and the Valley of the Craftsmen.

[36] Some divisions of the Levites from Judah were attached to Benjamin.

[1] These are the priests and Levites who came up with Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and with Jeshua: Seraiah, Jeremiah, Ezra, [2] Amariah, Malluch, Hattush, [3] Shecaniah, Rehum, Meremoth, [4] Iddo, Ginnethoi, Abijah, [5] Mijamin, Maadiah, Bilgah, [6] Shemaiah, Joiarib, Jedaiah, [7] Sallu, Amok, Hilkiah, Jedaiah. These led the priests and their brothers in the days of Jeshua.

[8] The Levites: Jeshua, Binnui, Kadmiel, Sherebiah, Judah, and Mattaniah, who together with his brothers directed the songs of thanksgiving. [9] Bakbukiah and Unni, their brothers, stood across from them in the rotation.

[10] Jeshua fathered Joiakim, Joiakim fathered Eliashib, Eliashib fathered Joiada, [11] Joiada fathered Jonathan, and Jonathan fathered Jaddua.

[12] In the days of Joiakim, these were the priests who served as heads of families: of Seraiah, Meraiah; of Jeremiah, Hananiah; [13] of Ezra, Meshullam; of Amariah, Jehohanan; [14] of Malluchi, Jonathan; of Shebaniah, Joseph; [15] of Harim, Adna; of Meraioth, Helkai; [16] of Iddo, Zechariah; of Ginnethon, Meshullam; [17] of Abijah, Zichri; of Miniamin and Moadiah, Piltai; [18] of Bilgah, Shammua; of Shemaiah, Jehonathan; [19] of Joiarib, Mattenai; of Jedaiah, Uzzi; [20] of Sallai, Kallai; of Amok, Eber; [21] of Hilkiah, Hashabiah; of Jedaiah, Nethanel.

[22] The Levites in the days of Eliashib, Joiada, Johanan, and Jaddua were recorded as heads of families, as were the priests during the reign of Darius the Persian. [23] The descendants of Levi who were family heads were entered in the book of the chronicles down to the days of Johanan son of Eliashib.

[24] The leaders of the Levites — Hashabiah, Sherebiah, Jeshua son of Kadmiel, and their brothers stationed opposite them — sang praise and thanksgiving in answering choirs, just as David the man of God had commanded: section answering section. [25] Mattaniah, Bakbukiah, Obadiah, Meshullam, Talmon, and Akkub kept watch as gatekeepers at the storerooms beside the gates. [26] These served in the days of Joiakim son of Jeshua son of Jozadak, and in the days of Nehemiah the governor and Ezra the priest and scribe.

[27] When the time came to dedicate the wall of Jerusalem, they hunted down the Levites wherever they were living and brought them to the city to lead the celebration — a dedication bursting with thanksgiving and song, with cymbals, harps, and lyres. [28] The singers gathered from the surrounding region — from villages near Jerusalem, from the settlements of the Netophathites, [29] from Beth-gilgal, and from the open country around Geba and Azmaveth, for the singers had built themselves villages all around Jerusalem.

[30] The priests and Levites purified themselves, then purified the people, the gates, and the wall.

[31] I led the officials of Judah up onto the wall and arranged two massive choirs to give thanks. One marched along the top of the wall to the right, toward the Dung Gate. [32] Behind them went Hoshaiah and half the officials of Judah, [33] along with Azariah, Ezra, Meshullam, [34] Judah, Benjamin, Shemaiah, and Jeremiah, [35] and certain priests with trumpets — Zechariah son of Jonathan son of Shemaiah son of Mattaniah son of Micaiah son of Zaccur son of Asaph, [36] and his brothers Shemaiah, Azarel, Milalai, Gilalai, Maai, Nethanel, Judah, and Hanani, carrying the musical instruments of David the man of God. Ezra the scribe walked at the head of them. [37] At the Fountain Gate they kept going straight ahead, climbing the stairs of the City of David where the wall rises, past David's palace, all the way to the Water Gate on the east.

[38] The second choir marched in the opposite direction, and I followed with the other half of the people along the top of the wall, past the Tower of the Ovens to the Broad Wall, [39] over the Gate of Ephraim, past the Jeshanah Gate and the Fish Gate, past the Tower of Hananel and the Tower of the Hundred, all the way to the Sheep Gate; they came to a stop at the Gate of the Guard.

[40] Then both choirs took their places at the house of God — and so did I, with half the officials beside me, [41] and the priests Eliakim, Maaseiah, Miniamin, Micaiah, Elioenai, Zechariah, and Hananiah with their trumpets, [42] along with Maaseiah, Shemaiah, Eleazar, Uzzi, Jehohanan, Malchijah, Elam, and Ezer. The singers sang at full voice with Jezrahiah leading them.

[43] That day they offered sacrifices on a staggering scale and rejoiced — God had filled them with joy beyond measure. The women rejoiced, the children rejoiced, and the sound of Jerusalem's gladness carried for miles.

[44] On that day men were appointed to oversee the storerooms for the contributions, firstfruits, and tithes, gathering into them from the fields around each town the portions set aside by the Law for the priests and Levites. Judah delighted in the priests and Levites standing in service, [45] who carried out the duties of their God and the duties of purification, as did the singers and gatekeepers, according to the command of David and his son Solomon. [46] For long ago, in the days of David and Asaph, there had been directors of song and songs of praise and thanksgiving to God. [47] So in the days of Zerubbabel and again in the days of Nehemiah, all Israel gave the daily portions for the singers and gatekeepers. They set apart what belonged to the Levites, and the Levites set apart what belonged to the descendants of Aaron.

[1] That day they read aloud from the scroll of Moses while the people listened, and they found it written there: no Ammonite or Moabite is ever to enter the assembly of God— [2] because they refused to meet the Israelites with bread and water and hired Balaam to curse them. But our God flipped the curse into a blessing.

[3] When the people heard this Law, they cut every foreigner out of Israel.

[4] But before all this, Eliashib the priest — appointed over the storerooms of the house of our God and a close associate of Tobiah — [5] had cleared out a large room for him. The same room that used to hold the grain offerings, the incense, the temple vessels, and the tithes of grain, wine, and oil prescribed for the Levites, singers, and gatekeepers, along with the priests' portions.

[6] I was not in Jerusalem when this happened. In the thirty-second year of Artaxerxes king of Babylon I had returned to the king. After some time I requested leave again, [7] and when I got back to Jerusalem I saw the evil Eliashib had done for Tobiah — providing him a room in the very courts of God's house.

[8] I was furious. I threw every piece of Tobiah's household property out of the room. [9] I ordered the room purified and brought back the temple vessels, the grain offerings, and the incense.

[10] I also learned that the Levites had not been given their portions, so they and the singers responsible for the service had abandoned their posts and gone back to their fields. [11] I confronted the officials: "Why has the house of God been abandoned?" Then I gathered the Levites and singers and put them back at their stations.

[12] And all Judah brought in the tithes of grain, wine, and oil to the storerooms. [13] I put Shelemiah the priest, Zadok the scribe, and Pedaiah the Levite in charge of the storerooms, with Hanan son of Zaccur, son of Mattaniah, as their assistant — men I considered trustworthy. Their job was distribution to their brothers.

[14] Remember me for this, my God. Do not erase what I have faithfully done for your house and its service.

[15] In those days I saw people in Judah treading winepresses on the Sabbath, hauling in heaps of grain and loading them onto donkeys — along with wine, grapes, figs, every kind of cargo — and bringing it into Jerusalem on the Sabbath day. I warned them on the spot about selling food then.

[16] Men from Tyre were living in the city, importing fish and every kind of merchandise and selling it on the Sabbath to the people of Judah — in Jerusalem itself.

[17] I confronted the nobles of Judah: "What is this evil you're doing — desecrating the Sabbath? [18] Did your ancestors not do exactly this? And did our God not bring all this disaster on us and on this city? Now you're pouring more fury on Israel by profaning the Sabbath."

[19] So I gave the order: as soon as the gates of Jerusalem began to throw shadows before the Sabbath, the doors were to be shut and not opened until the Sabbath was over. I stationed some of my own men at the gates to make sure no load got in on the Sabbath day.

[20] Once or twice the merchants and sellers of every kind of goods spent the night outside Jerusalem. [21] I warned them: "Why are you camping in front of the wall? Do it again and I'll lay hands on you." From then on, they did not come on the Sabbath.

[22] Then I ordered the Levites to purify themselves and come guard the gates, keeping the Sabbath day holy.

Remember this too, my God. Spare me according to your overwhelming love.

[23] Also in those days I saw Jews who had married women from Ashdod, Ammon, and Moab. [24] Half their children spoke the language of Ashdod or one of the other peoples — they couldn't even speak the language of Judah.

[25] I confronted them, I cursed them, I struck some of them, I yanked out their hair. I made them swear by God: "You will not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons — or for yourselves. [26] Was it not over precisely this that Solomon king of Israel sinned? Among the nations there was no king like him. He was loved by his God, and God made him king over all Israel. Even him — foreign women dragged into sin. [27] Are we now supposed to listen to you and commit this whole massive evil — betraying our God by marrying foreign women?"

[28] One of the sons of Joiada son of Eliashib the high priest had become son-in-law to Sanballat the Horonite. I drove him away from me.

[29] Remember them, my God — they defiled the priesthood and the covenant of the priesthood and the Levites.

[30] So I cleansed them of everything foreign and assigned the priests and Levites their duties, each to his task, [31] and I arranged for the wood offering at appointed times and for the firstfruits.

Remember me, my God, for good.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:
