[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Command the Israelites to send out of the camp anyone with a skin disease, anyone with a discharge, anyone defiled by contact with a corpse.

[3] Male or female, send them out. Put them beyond the camp so they will not contaminate the place where I dwell among them."

[4] The Israelites did it. They sent them outside the camp, exactly as the LORD had told Moses.

[5] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[6] "Tell the Israelites: when a man or woman commits any wrong against another person, they have broken faith with the LORD. They are guilty.

[7] They must confess what they did. They must make full restitution — the original amount plus a fifth — and hand it over to the one they wronged.

[8] If that person has no surviving relative to receive the restitution, it belongs to the LORD and goes to the priest, along with the ram of atonement that covers the offender.

[9] Every sacred contribution the Israelites bring to the priest is his.

[10] What anyone consecrates is theirs to give, but once it is given to the priest, it belongs to him."

[11] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[12] "Speak to the Israelites. Tell them: suppose a man's wife strays. She is unfaithful to him.

[13] Another man sleeps with her — secretly, hidden from her husband. She defiled herself but there was no witness, no one caught her in the act.

[14] Now suspicion seizes the husband. Jealousy burns in him against his wife — and she is guilty. Or jealousy burns in him — and she is innocent.

[15] He must bring her to the priest, along with an offering on her behalf: a tenth of an ephah of barley flour. No oil poured on it. No incense added. This is a grain offering of jealousy, a reminder offering — it brings sin to the surface.

[16] The priest brings her forward and stands her before the LORD.

[17] He takes holy water in a clay vessel, scoops up dust from the floor of the tabernacle, and stirs it into the water.

[18] He stands the woman before the LORD, uncovers her hair, places the reminder offering in her hands — the grain offering of jealousy — while he himself holds the bitter water that brings a curse.

[19] Then the priest puts her under oath. He says: 'If no man has slept with you, if you have not strayed and defiled yourself while married to your husband, may this bitter water of cursing leave you untouched.

[20] But if you have strayed while married to your husband, if you have defiled yourself, if another man has slept with you—'

[21] here the priest brings the woman under the oath of the curse, and he tells her: 'may the LORD make you a curse and an oath among your people. May the LORD make your thigh waste away and your belly swell.

[22] May this water that brings a curse enter your body, make your belly swell, your thigh waste away.' And the woman says, 'Amen. Amen.'

[23] The priest writes these curses on a scroll and washes them off into the bitter water.

[24] He makes the woman drink the bitter water that brings a curse — the water enters her, bitter.

[25] Then the priest takes the grain offering of jealousy from her hands, lifts it before the LORD, and brings it to the altar.

[26] He scoops up a handful as the memorial portion and burns it on the altar. After that he makes her drink the water.

[27] When she has drunk it: if she has defiled herself, if she has betrayed her husband, the water that brings the curse will enter her and turn bitter. Her belly will swell. Her thigh will waste away. She will become a curse among her people.

[28] But if the woman is innocent — if she is clean — she will be unharmed and able to conceive.

[29] This is the law of jealousy. It applies when a wife strays while married to her husband and defiles herself,

[30] or when jealousy seizes a man and he suspects his wife. He stands her before the LORD, and the priest carries out everything this law requires.

[31] The husband is clear of guilt. The woman bears her own."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites this. When a man or woman makes a special vow—the vow of a Nazirite—to set themselves apart for the LORD,

[3] they must abstain from wine and strong drink. No wine vinegar, no strong drink vinegar, no grape juice, no grapes—fresh or dried.

[4] For the entire duration of their consecration, nothing that comes from the grapevine touches their lips—not even the seeds, not even the skins.

[5] As long as the vow stands, no razor touches their head. Until the days of their separation to the LORD are complete, they remain holy. The hair of their head grows wild and free.

[6] For the entire time they are set apart to the LORD, they must not go near a dead body.

[7] Not for father, not for mother, not for brother, not for sister—even at their death, the Nazirite stays clean. The mark of consecration to God is on their head.

[8] Every day of their separation, they belong wholly to the LORD.

[9] But if someone suddenly drops dead beside them and defiles the consecrated head, they must shave it on the day of their cleansing—the seventh day they shave it.

[10] On the eighth day, they bring two doves or two young pigeons to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

[11] The priest offers one as a purification offering and the other as a burnt offering, making atonement for the defilement contracted near the corpse. That same day, they reconsecrate their head.

[12] They restart the days of separation to the LORD from scratch and bring a year-old male lamb as a reparation offering. The previous days don't count—the vow was broken by the defilement.

[13] Here is the ritual when the Nazirite's days are complete. They are brought to the entrance of the tent of meeting,

[14] and they present their offering to the LORD: one unblemished year-old male lamb for a burnt offering, one unblemished year-old female lamb for a purification offering, one unblemished ram for a peace offering,

[15] a basket of unleavened bread—loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, wafers of unleavened bread brushed with oil—along with the grain offering and drink offering that go with them.

[16] The priest brings all of it before the LORD and offers the purification offering and burnt offering.

[17] Then he offers the ram as a peace offering to the LORD, along with the basket of unleavened bread, and presents the grain offering and drink offering.

[18] At the entrance of the tent of meeting, the Nazirite shaves the consecrated head. They take the hair of consecration and throw it into the fire beneath the peace offering.

[19] The priest takes the boiled shoulder of the ram, one unleavened loaf from the basket, and one unleavened wafer, and places them in the hands of the Nazirite—after they have shaved their consecrated head.

[20] The priest elevates them as a wave offering before the LORD. They are holy and belong to the priest, along with the breast that was waved and the thigh that was lifted. After this, the Nazirite may drink wine.

[21] These are the regulations for the Nazirite who vows an offering to the LORD for their consecration—beyond whatever else they can afford. Whatever they vowed, they must perform, in full, according to the law of their consecration."

[22] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[23] "Tell Aaron and his sons: this is how you will bless the Israelites. Say to them—

[24] May the LORD bless you and keep you.

[25] May the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you.

[26] May the LORD turn his face toward you and give you peace.

[27] So they will place my name on the Israelites, and I myself will bless them."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: [2] "Tell Aaron this: When you mount the lamps, all seven must throw their light forward, toward the face of the lampstand."

[3] Aaron did exactly that. He set the lamps so their light fell forward, just as the LORD had commanded Moses. [4] The lampstand itself was hammered gold — one piece, from base to blossom, every curve beaten out by hand. Moses built it from the pattern the LORD had shown him on the mountain.

[5] The LORD spoke to Moses: [6] "Take the Levites out from among the rest of Israel and cleanse them. [7] Here is how: Sprinkle the water of purification on them. Then have them shave their entire bodies, wash their clothes, and present themselves clean. [8] They are to bring a young bull with its grain offering — fine flour mixed with oil — and a second bull for a sin offering.

[9] "Gather the Levites before the tent of meeting and assemble the whole community of Israel around them. [10] Bring the Levites before the LORD, and let the Israelites lay their hands on them. [11] Aaron will then present the Levites before the LORD as a wave offering from the people of Israel — set apart for the LORD's service.

[12] "The Levites will lay their hands on the heads of the bulls. Offer one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering to the LORD, to make atonement for the Levites. [13] Station the Levites in front of Aaron and his sons, and lift them up as a wave offering to the LORD.

[14] "This is how you separate the Levites from the rest of Israel. The Levites are mine. [15] After you have cleansed them and offered them as a wave offering, they will go in to serve at the tent of meeting. [16] They are wholly given to me — handed over from Israel. I have taken them in place of every firstborn male, every womb-opener in Israel. [17] For every firstborn in Israel belongs to me — human and animal alike — from the day I struck down every firstborn in Egypt. I consecrated them for myself then. [18] But I have taken the Levites in place of every firstborn son of Israel. [19] I have given the Levites as a gift to Aaron and his sons, drawn from among the Israelites, to do the work at the tent of meeting on Israel's behalf and to make atonement for them — so no plague will fall on Israel when they come near the sanctuary."

[20] So Moses, Aaron, and the whole community of Israel did with the Levites exactly what the LORD had commanded Moses. The Israelites did it all. [21] The Levites purified themselves and washed their clothes. Aaron presented them as a wave offering before the LORD and made atonement to cleanse them. [22] After that, the Levites went in to perform their service at the tent of meeting before Aaron and his sons. Everything the LORD had commanded Moses about the Levites — they did.

[23] The LORD spoke to Moses: [24] "This applies to the Levites: from age twenty-five and up, a Levite enters the workforce at the tent of meeting. [25] At fifty, he retires from active service. [26] After that, he can assist his fellow Levites at the tent — keeping watch — but he no longer does the labor himself. This is how you arrange the duties of the Levites."

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses in the Sinai wilderness, the first month of the second year after Egypt was behind them. He said:

[2] Let Israel keep the Passover at its appointed time.

[3] The fourteenth day of this month, at twilight — that is when. Keep it according to every statute, every rule.

[4] So Moses told Israel to keep the Passover,

[5] and they did — in the Sinai wilderness, on the fourteenth of the first month, at twilight. Everything the LORD commanded Moses, Israel carried out.

[6] But there were some men who had touched a corpse and were ritually unclean. They couldn't keep the Passover that day. So they came before Moses and Aaron

[7] and said: "We're unclean from contact with the dead — but why should that lock us out? Why are we cut off from bringing the LORD's offering at the appointed time, with everyone else in Israel?"

[8] Moses said: "Wait here. Let me hear what the LORD commands about you."

[9] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[10] Tell Israel this: If any of you — now or in generations to come — is unclean from contact with a corpse, or away on a long journey, you may still keep the Passover to the LORD.

[11] You'll keep it the second month, on the fourteenth day, at twilight. Eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs.

[12] Leave nothing till morning. Break no bone of it. Follow every statute of the Passover when you keep it.

[13] But anyone who is clean and not on a journey, who fails to keep the Passover — that person is cut off from the people. They didn't bring the LORD's offering at the appointed time. They will bear their guilt.

[14] If a foreigner living among you wants to keep the Passover to the LORD, let them — by the statute of the Passover, by its rule. One law for the native-born and the foreigner alike.

[15] The day the tabernacle was raised, the cloud covered it — the tent of the testimony. From evening until morning, the cloud over the tabernacle looked like fire.

[16] That was the pattern. By day, the cloud. By night, the look of fire.

[17] When the cloud lifted from the tent, Israel marched. Where the cloud settled, Israel camped.

[18] At the LORD's command, they moved. At the LORD's command, they camped. As long as the cloud rested over the tabernacle, they stayed put.

[19] Even when the cloud lingered over the tabernacle for many days, Israel kept the LORD's watch and did not move.

[20] Sometimes the cloud rested only a few days over the tabernacle. By the LORD's command they camped; by the LORD's command they marched.

[21] Sometimes the cloud stayed only from evening to morning — and when it lifted in the morning, they marched. Whether by day or by night, when the cloud lifted, they moved.

[22] Two days, a month, a year — however long the cloud lingered over the tabernacle, Israel stayed camped and did not march. When it lifted, they moved.

[23] By the LORD's command they camped. By the LORD's command they marched. They kept the LORD's watch — at the LORD's command, through Moses.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Hammer out two trumpets of silver — beat them from a single piece. They're for summoning the assembly and breaking camp.

[3] When both sound, the whole community gathers to you at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

[4] When only one sounds, the chiefs — the heads of Israel's clans — assemble to you.

[5] A short blast: the camps on the east pull out.

[6] A second short blast: the camps on the south move. Short blasts mean march.

[7] To gather the assembly, sound a steady note — not the blast.

[8] The trumpets belong to the sons of Aaron, the priests. This is a permanent statute, generation after generation.

[9] When you go to war in your own land against an enemy pressing you, blast the trumpets — and the LORD your God will remember you, and you'll be rescued from your enemies.

[10] On your days of joy, your appointed feasts, your new moons — sound the trumpets over your burnt offerings and peace offerings. They'll bring you to remembrance before your God. I am the LORD your God."

[11] In the second year, the second month, on the twentieth day, the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle of the testimony.

[12] And Israel pulled out — stage by stage — from the wilderness of Sinai, until the cloud settled in the wilderness of Paran.

[13] This was the first march, by the word of the LORD through Moses.

[14] Judah's standard led the way, division by division — Nahshon son of Amminadab at their head.

[15] Over the tribe of Issachar: Nethanel son of Zuar.

[16] Over the tribe of Zebulun: Eliab son of Helon.

[17] Then the tabernacle was taken down, and the sons of Gershon and Merari hauled it forward.

[18] Reuben's standard moved next, division by division — Elizur son of Shedeur leading.

[19] Over Simeon: Shelumiel son of Zurishaddai.

[20] Over Gad: Eliasaph son of Deuel.

[21] Then the Kohathites set out, carrying the holy things. The tabernacle would be standing again by the time they arrived.

[22] Ephraim's standard moved next, division by division — Elishama son of Ammihud at their head.

[23] Over Manasseh: Gamaliel son of Pedahzur.

[24] Over Benjamin: Abidan son of Gideoni.

[25] Last of all, bringing up the rear guard of every camp, came Dan's standard, division by division — Ahiezer son of Ammishaddai leading.

[26] Over Asher: Pagiel son of Ochran.

[27] Over Naphtali: Ahira son of Enan.

[28] This was the marching order of Israel, division by division, when they pulled out.

[29] Moses said to Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite — Moses' father-in-law — "We're heading to the place the LORD promised us. Come with us. We'll treat you well. The LORD has spoken good over Israel."

[30] But Hobab said, "No. I'm going back to my own country, to my own kin."

[31] "Please don't leave us," Moses pressed. "You know where to camp in the wilderness — you can be our eyes.

[32] Come with us, and whatever good the LORD does for us, we'll do for you."

[33] So they set out from the mountain of the LORD — a three-day journey. The ark of the covenant of the LORD went ahead of them those three days, searching out a resting place.

[34] And the cloud of the LORD hung over them by day as they broke camp and moved.

[35] Whenever the ark set out, Moses would cry: "Rise up, LORD! Scatter your enemies! Let those who hate you flee before your face!"

[36] And when it came to rest, he said: "Return, LORD, to the countless thousands of Israel."

[1] The people started complaining—really complaining—and the LORD heard it. His anger flared. Fire from the LORD broke out and consumed the edges of the camp.

[2] The people cried out to Moses. Moses prayed to the LORD, and the fire died down.

[3] They named that place Taberah—Burning—because the LORD's fire had burned among them.

[4] Then the rabble traveling with them developed a craving, and soon the Israelites were sobbing too: "If only we had meat to eat!

[5] Remember the fish we ate in Egypt—free for the taking? The cucumbers, the melons, the leeks, the onions, the garlic?

[6] Now we're wasting away. Nothing to look at but this manna—nothing."

[7] (The manna was like coriander seed, the color of resin.

[8] The people would wander around and gather it, grind it on millstones or crush it in mortars, boil it in pots and make it into cakes. It tasted like pastry baked in oil.

[9] When the dew fell on the camp at night, the manna fell with it.)

[10] Moses heard the people wailing—every family at the entrance of its tent. The LORD's anger blazed hot, and to Moses it looked like disaster.

[11] Moses said to the LORD: "Why have You done this to Your servant? Why have I lost favor in Your eyes, that You would dump the weight of this entire people on me?

[12] Did I conceive them? Did I give birth to them? Why would You tell me to carry them at my chest like a nurse carries an infant—all the way to the land You swore to their ancestors?

[13] Where am I supposed to get meat for all these people? They're sobbing in my face: 'Give us meat to eat!'

[14] I cannot carry this people alone. The load is too heavy for me.

[15] If this is how You're going to treat me, then do me a favor—kill me right now. If I've found any favor in Your eyes, end it. Don't make me watch my own ruin."

[16] The LORD said to Moses: "Bring me seventy of Israel's elders—men you know to be true elders and officers of the people. Bring them to the tent of meeting and have them stand there with you.

[17] I'll come down and speak with you there. I'll take some of the Spirit that's on you and place it on them. They'll carry the burden of the people with you—you won't carry it alone.

[18] And tell the people: Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow. You're going to eat meat. You wept in the LORD's hearing—'If only we had meat to eat! We were better off in Egypt!'—so the LORD is going to give you meat. And you will eat.

[19] Not just one day. Not two. Not five, not ten, not twenty.

[20] A whole month. Until it's coming out of your nostrils and you can't stand the sight of it—because you rejected the LORD who is right here among you, and wept in His face, asking, 'Why did we ever leave Egypt?'"

[21] Moses said, "I'm standing in the middle of six hundred thousand foot soldiers, and You're saying You'll give them meat for a whole month?

[22] If we slaughtered every flock and herd we have, would it be enough? If we netted every fish in the sea, would that satisfy them?"

[23] The LORD answered Moses: "Is the LORD's arm too short? Now you'll see whether My word holds up or not."

[24] So Moses went out and told the people what the LORD had said. He gathered seventy of the elders and stationed them around the tent.

[25] The LORD descended in the cloud and spoke to him. He took some of the Spirit that was on Moses and placed it on the seventy elders. When the Spirit settled on them, they prophesied—but only that once.

[26] Two men had stayed behind in the camp—Eldad and Medad. They were on the list, but they hadn't gone out to the tent. The Spirit settled on them too, right where they were, and they prophesied in the camp.

[27] A young man ran and told Moses: "Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp!"

[28] Joshua son of Nun, who had served Moses since he was young, said, "My lord Moses, stop them!"

[29] But Moses said, "Are you jealous on my behalf? I wish every one of the LORD's people were prophets—that the LORD would put His Spirit on all of them."

[30] Then Moses returned to the camp with the elders of Israel.

[31] A wind went out from the LORD. It drove quail in from the sea and dropped them all around the camp—a day's walk this way, a day's walk that way—piled about two cubits deep on the ground.

[32] The people were up all that day, all night, and all the next day gathering quail. The one with the least gathered ten homers. They spread them out around the camp to dry.

[33] The meat was still between their teeth—hadn't even been chewed—when the LORD's anger blazed against the people. He struck them with a devastating plague.

[34] They named that place Kibroth-hattaavah—Graves of Craving—because that's where they buried the people who had craved.

[35] From Kibroth-hattaavah the people moved on to Hazeroth, and stayed there.

[1] Miriam and Aaron started talking against Moses—about the Cushite woman he had married. Yes, he had married a Cushite. [2] "Has the LORD spoken only through Moses?" they said. "Hasn't he spoken through us too?" And the LORD heard it.

[3] Now Moses was a man of profound humility—more so than anyone else on the face of the earth.

[4] Suddenly the LORD spoke to Moses, Aaron, and Miriam: "The three of you—out to the tent of meeting. Now." So the three of them went. [5] The LORD came down in a pillar of cloud, stood at the entrance of the tent, and called out, "Aaron. Miriam." The two of them stepped forward, [6] and he said:

"Listen carefully to my words.
When a prophet of the LORD is among you,
I make myself known to him in visions,
I speak to him in dreams.
[7] But not with my servant Moses.
He is faithful in all my house.
[8] With him I speak face to face—
clearly, not in riddles.
He sees the very form of the LORD.
So how did you find the nerve
to speak against my servant Moses?"

[9] The LORD's anger blazed against them, and he was gone.

[10] When the cloud lifted from the tent, there stood Miriam—skin white as snow with leprosy. Aaron turned and saw her, leprous, [11] and said to Moses, "Please, my lord—don't hold this sin against us. We were fools. We sinned. [12] Don't let her be like a stillborn child whose flesh is half eaten away when it leaves the womb."

[13] Moses cried out to the LORD: "God, please—heal her. Please."

[14] The LORD answered Moses, "If her father had spit in her face, wouldn't she carry the shame for seven days? Shut her outside the camp for seven days. Then bring her back."

[15] So Miriam was shut outside the camp for seven days, and the people did not move on until she was brought back.

[16] After that, the people broke camp at Hazeroth and pitched in the wilderness of Paran.

[1] The LORD said to Moses: [2] "Send men to scout out the land of Canaan — the land I'm giving to the people of Israel. Send one man from each ancestral tribe, every one of them a leader."

[3] So Moses dispatched them from the Wilderness of Paran on the LORD's orders — every one of them a head of the people of Israel. [4] These are their names:

From the tribe of Reuben — Shammua son of Zaccur.
[5] From the tribe of Simeon — Shaphat son of Hori.
[6] From the tribe of Judah — Caleb son of Jephunneh.
[7] From the tribe of Issachar — Igal son of Joseph.
[8] From the tribe of Ephraim — Hoshea son of Nun.
[9] From the tribe of Benjamin — Palti son of Raphu.
[10] From the tribe of Zebulun — Gaddiel son of Sodi.
[11] From the tribe of Joseph (specifically Manasseh) — Gaddi son of Susi.
[12] From the tribe of Dan — Ammiel son of Gemalli.
[13] From the tribe of Asher — Sethur son of Michael.
[14] From the tribe of Naphtali — Nahbi son of Vophsi.
[15] From the tribe of Gad — Geuel son of Machi.

[16] These were the names of the men Moses sent to scout the land. And Moses renamed Hoshea son of Nun — he called him Joshua.

[17] When Moses sent them out to scout Canaan, he told them: "Go up through the Negev, then push into the hill country. [18] See what the land is like. See the people who live there — strong or weak? Few or many? [19] What kind of country do they live in — good or bad? Their towns — open camps or walled fortresses? [20] What's the soil like — rich or starved? Is there timber? Show some courage. And bring back samples of the fruit." (It was the season of the first ripe grapes.)

[21] So up they went. They scouted the land from the Wilderness of Zin all the way to Rehob, near Lebo-hamath. [22] They came up through the Negev and reached Hebron, where Ahiman, Sheshai, and Talmai lived — descendants of the giant Anak. (Hebron had been built seven years before Zoan in Egypt.) [23] They reached the Eshcol Valley. There they cut down a single cluster of grapes — so heavy it took two men to carry it on a pole between them — along with pomegranates and figs. [24] The place was named the Valley of Eshcol — "Cluster" — because of the cluster the Israelites cut there.

[25] Forty days later, they came back from scouting the land. [26] They returned to Moses and Aaron and the whole community of Israel in the Wilderness of Paran, at Kadesh. They gave their report to the whole assembly and showed them the fruit. [27] Here's what they said:

"We went into the land you sent us to. It really does flow with milk and honey — look at this fruit. [28] But — the people who live there are strong. The cities are massive, fortified. And we saw the descendants of Anak there. [29] Amalek holds the Negev. The Hittites, Jebusites, and Amorites hold the hill country. The Canaanites are along the sea and the Jordan."

[30] Caleb silenced the people in front of Moses. "Let's go up — right now. Let's take it. We can absolutely do this."

[31] But the men who had gone up with him said: "We can't attack those people. They're stronger than we are." [32] And they started spreading a poisoned report among the Israelites about the land they'd scouted:

"That land we walked through? It eats its own inhabitants. Every person we saw was a giant. [33] We saw the Nephilim there — the sons of Anak come from the Nephilim. Next to them we felt like grasshoppers. And that's how we looked to them too."

[1] That night the whole community erupted. They lifted their voices and wept, and the wailing didn't stop.

[2] Every Israelite turned on Moses and Aaron. The whole assembly threw it in their faces: "We should have died in Egypt! Or right here in this wilderness — anywhere but this!

[3] Why is the LORD dragging us into that land just to cut us down by the sword? Our wives and children will become someone's plunder. We'd be better off heading back to Egypt."

[4] And they said to each other, "Let's pick a leader and go back."

[5] Moses and Aaron hit the ground, face-down, in front of the entire assembly of Israel.

[6] But Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh — two of the men who had scouted the land — tore their clothes in grief

[7] and pleaded with the whole community: "The land we walked through, the land we explored — it is stunning. Astonishingly good.

[8] If the LORD delights in us, he'll bring us into that land and hand it over — a country flowing with milk and honey.

[9] Just don't rebel against the LORD. And don't be afraid of the people in that land — we'll devour them. Whatever protection they had is gone. The LORD is with us. Don't fear them."

[10] The whole congregation talked about stoning them on the spot. Then the glory of the LORD blazed out over the Tent of Meeting in full view of every Israelite.

[11] The LORD said to Moses: "How long will these people treat me with contempt? How long will they refuse to trust me, after every sign I've performed right in front of them?

[12] I'll strike them with plague. I'll disinherit them. And I'll make you into a nation bigger and stronger than they are."

[13] But Moses said to the LORD: "Egypt will hear about this. You brought these people out from among them by sheer power —

[14] and they'll tell the people who live in this land. They've already heard, LORD, that you are among this people, that you appear to them face to face, that your cloud stands over them, that you go in front of them in a pillar of cloud by day and a pillar of fire by night.

[15] If you kill this whole people in a single stroke, the nations who've heard about your reputation will say,

[16] 'The LORD couldn't bring this people into the land he swore to give them — so he slaughtered them in the wilderness.'

[17] No. Let your power show itself now, LORD, exactly as you said:

[18] 'The LORD is slow to anger, overflowing with loyal love, forgiving sin and rebellion — though he doesn't acquit the guilty, and he visits the consequences of fathers' sins on children to the third and fourth generation.'

[19] Forgive the sin of this people, in the immensity of your loyal love — the way you've forgiven them from Egypt until now."

[20] The LORD said: "I forgive them, as you've asked.

[21] But as surely as I live, and as surely as the glory of the LORD will fill the whole earth —

[22] not one of these people who saw my glory and the signs I performed in Egypt and in the wilderness, and who tested me now ten times and refused to listen —

[23] not one of them will see the land I promised their ancestors. None of those who treated me with contempt will see it.

[24] But my servant Caleb — there's a different spirit in him. He followed me wholeheartedly. I will bring him into the land he explored, and his descendants will possess it.

[25] (The Amalekites and Canaanites are settled in the valleys.) Tomorrow, turn around. Head back into the wilderness, toward the Red Sea."

[26] The LORD said to Moses and Aaron:

[27] "How much longer will I put up with this wicked assembly grumbling against me? I have heard every complaint the Israelites are leveling at me.

[28] Tell them: As I live — declares the LORD — what I heard you say, I will do to you.

[29] Your corpses will fall in this wilderness. Every one of you counted in the census, twenty years old and up — every one who grumbled against me —

[30] not one of you will enter the land I swore by raised hand to settle you in. Except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

[31] As for your children — the ones you said would become plunder — I'll bring them in. They will know the land you rejected.

[32] But you? Your bodies will fall here in this wilderness.

[33] Your children will be shepherds out here for forty years, bearing the weight of your faithlessness until the last of your bodies rots in the wilderness.

[34] Forty days you scouted the land. Forty years you'll pay for it — a year for each day. Then you'll know what it means to have me against you.

[35] I, the LORD, have spoken. This is exactly what I will do to this whole wicked assembly that has banded together against me. In this wilderness they will be finished. Here they will die."

[36] The men Moses had sent to scout the land — the ones who came back and stirred the whole community against him with their poisonous report —

[37] those men, the ones who spread the lies about the land, were struck dead before the LORD by plague.

[38] Of the scouting party, only Joshua son of Nun and Caleb son of Jephunneh survived.

[39] When Moses delivered this message to all the Israelites, the people mourned bitterly.

[40] Early the next morning they pushed up into the hill country. "We're ready," they said. "We'll go up to the place the LORD promised. We sinned."

[41] But Moses said: "Why are you violating the LORD's command? It won't work.

[42] Don't go up — the LORD isn't with you. You'll be cut down by your enemies.

[43] The Amalekites and Canaanites are there waiting for you, and you'll fall by the sword. Because you turned away from the LORD, the LORD won't be with you."

[44] They went up anyway, recklessly, into the high country — though the ark of the LORD's covenant and Moses didn't move from camp.

[45] The Amalekites and Canaanites who lived in those hills came down, struck them, and crushed them all the way to Hormah.

[1] The LORD said to Moses: [2] "Speak to Israel. Tell them: When you enter the land I'm giving you to settle, [3] and you bring an offering by fire to the LORD — a burnt offering, a sacrifice for a vow, a freewill gift, or one tied to your appointed feasts — to send up an aroma that pleases the LORD, whether from the herd or the flock, [4] the one bringing the offering shall present to the LORD a grain offering: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a quarter of a hin of oil. [5] Pour out a quarter of a hin of wine as a drink offering alongside the burnt offering or sacrifice — with each lamb.

[6] If it's a ram, prepare a grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with a third of a hin of oil, [7] and a third of a hin of wine for the drink offering — an aroma to please the LORD.

[8] When you bring a young bull as a burnt offering, a sacrifice for a vow, or a peace offering to the LORD, [9] present with the bull a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with half a hin of oil, [10] and half a hin of wine for the drink offering — an offering by fire, an aroma that pleases the LORD.

[11] This is the protocol for every bull, every ram, every lamb or young goat. [12] However many you prepare, do it this way for each one. [13] Every native-born Israelite shall handle these offerings exactly like this when presenting an offering by fire, an aroma that pleases the LORD.

[14] And if a foreigner living among you — whether passing through or settled for generations — wants to bring an offering by fire, an aroma that pleases the LORD, they shall do it the same way you do. [15] One statute for the assembly, for you and the foreigner residing with you — a permanent statute through all your generations. Before the LORD, you and the foreigner stand equal. [16] One law, one ruling — for you and for the foreigner living among you."

[17] The LORD said to Moses: [18] "Speak to Israel. Tell them: When you enter the land where I'm bringing you, [19] and you eat the bread of that land, you shall lift up a contribution to the LORD. [20] From the first of your dough, lift up a loaf as a contribution — like the contribution from the threshing floor, lift it up. [21] From the first of your dough, give the LORD a contribution — throughout all your generations.

[22] When you slip up and fail to keep any of these commands the LORD spoke to Moses — [23] anything the LORD commanded you through Moses, from the day the LORD gave the command and on through your generations — [24] if it was done unintentionally, out of sight of the community, then the whole assembly shall offer one young bull as a burnt offering, an aroma that pleases the LORD, with its grain offering and drink offering as prescribed, and one male goat as a sin offering. [25] The priest shall make atonement for the entire community of Israel, and they will be forgiven, because it was unintentional. They've brought their offering, an offering by fire to the LORD, and their sin offering before the LORD for the wrong they did. [26] The whole community of Israel shall be forgiven — and the foreigner living among them — because the whole population was caught up in an unintentional wrong.

[27] If a single person sins unintentionally, that person shall bring a year-old female goat as a sin offering. [28] The priest shall make atonement before the LORD for the one who strayed by accident — make atonement, and they will be forgiven. [29] One law for the one who acts unintentionally — whether native-born Israelite or foreigner living among you.

[30] But the person who acts defiantly — native-born or foreigner — that one is blaspheming the LORD. That person shall be cut off from among the people. [31] They despised the word of the LORD, broke his command. They shall surely be cut off. Their guilt is on them."

[32] While Israel was in the wilderness, they caught a man gathering wood on the Sabbath day. [33] The ones who found him gathering wood brought him to Moses, Aaron, and the whole assembly. [34] They held him in custody, because it hadn't been spelled out what should be done to him.

[35] Then the LORD told Moses: "The man must die. The whole assembly shall stone him outside the camp." [36] So the whole assembly took him outside the camp and stoned him to death — exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.

[37] The LORD said to Moses: [38] "Tell Israel — for all generations — to make tassels on the corners of their garments, and bind a blue cord on each tassel. [39] These will be your tassels. When you see them, you'll remember every command of the LORD. You'll do them. You won't go chasing after your own heart and your own eyes, the way you keep prostituting yourselves. [40] You'll remember, and do my commands, and stand holy before your God. [41] I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of Egypt to be your God. I am the LORD your God."

[1] Korah son of Izhar, grandson of Kohath, great-grandson of Levi, made his move. With him stood Dathan and Abiram, sons of Eliab, and On son of Peleth — all Reubenites. [2] They rose against Moses, backed by two hundred and fifty Israelites — chieftains of the community, men called up to the council, men of reputation. [3] They massed against Moses and Aaron and threw down the challenge: "You've taken too much on yourselves. Every single person in this assembly is holy — every one — and the LORD is among them. So who made you the ones who tower over the LORD's congregation?"

[4] Moses heard them and fell on his face. [5] Then he spoke to Korah and his whole faction: "Tomorrow morning the LORD will reveal who belongs to him, who is holy, and who he lets approach him. The one he chooses — that one comes near. [6] Here's what you do: take censers, Korah, you and your whole crew. [7] Tomorrow put fire in them and lay incense on top before the LORD. The man the LORD chooses — that's the holy one. You sons of Levi have taken too much on yourselves."

[8] Moses kept going: "Listen, sons of Levi. [9] Is it a small thing to you that the God of Israel pulled you out of the rest of Israel to bring you close to himself — to do the work of the LORD's tabernacle, to stand before the congregation as their servants? [10] He brought you near — you and all your Levite brothers with you — and now you're grasping for the priesthood too? [11] You and your whole faction have ganged up against the LORD himself. And Aaron — what is Aaron that you should grumble against him?"

[12] Moses sent for Dathan and Abiram, the sons of Eliab. They answered, "We're not coming. [13] Wasn't it enough that you dragged us out of a land flowing with milk and honey to kill us in this wilderness — and now you want to lord it over us too? [14] You haven't brought us into any land flowing with milk and honey. You haven't given us any inheritance of fields and vineyards. Do you think you can gouge out the eyes of these men? We're not coming."

[15] Moses burned with rage. He said to the LORD, "Don't accept their offering. I haven't taken a single donkey from them. I haven't wronged a single one of them."

[16] Moses said to Korah: "You and your whole faction — present yourselves before the LORD tomorrow. You, them, and Aaron. [17] Each man takes his censer, puts incense on it, and brings it before the LORD — two hundred fifty censers. You too. And Aaron, with his." [18] So each man took his censer, laid fire and incense on it, and stood at the entrance to the tent of meeting with Moses and Aaron. [19] Korah had rallied the whole congregation against them at the entrance to the tent of meeting. Then the glory of the LORD blazed out over the whole assembly.

[20] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: [21] "Separate yourselves from this congregation. I'm going to consume them in an instant."

[22] They fell on their faces. "God — God of the spirits of all flesh — one man sins, and you rage against the entire congregation?"

[23] The LORD answered Moses: [24] "Tell the congregation: pull back from around the dwellings of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram."

[25] Moses got up and went to Dathan and Abiram, the elders of Israel following him. [26] He told the congregation: "Get away from the tents of these wicked men. Don't touch anything that belongs to them, or you'll be swept away in all their sins." [27] So they pulled back from the dwellings of Korah, Dathan, and Abiram on every side. Dathan and Abiram came out and stood at the entrance of their tents — wives, sons, little children, all of them.

[28] Moses said: "Here is how you'll know the LORD sent me to do all this — that none of it came out of my own head. [29] If these men die a natural death, the way every human dies — if what happens to everyone happens to them — then the LORD did not send me. [30] But if the LORD does something unprecedented — if the ground opens its mouth and swallows them and everything they have, and they go down alive into Sheol — then you'll know these men have despised the LORD."

[31] The instant he finished speaking, the ground beneath them split open. [32] The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them — them, their households, every person who belonged to Korah, every last possession. [33] Down they went, alive, into Sheol — they and everything they owned. The earth closed over them. They were gone from the assembly. [34] All Israel around them scattered at their screams, shouting, "The earth's going to swallow us too!"

[35] Then fire roared out from the LORD and devoured the two hundred fifty men offering incense.

[36] The LORD spoke to Moses: [37] "Tell Eleazar son of Aaron the priest to lift the censers out of the burning — the fire scatter the coals away from there — because the censers are holy. [38] The censers of these men who paid for their sin with their lives — hammer them into plating for the altar. They were brought before the LORD; they became holy. Let them stand as a sign to the Israelites."

[39] So Eleazar the priest took the bronze censers the burned men had brought, and they were hammered out to plate the altar — [40] a reminder to the Israelites that no outsider, no one not descended from Aaron, should ever come forward to burn incense before the LORD, or he'd end up like Korah and his faction. Just as the LORD had said through Moses.

[41] The very next day, the whole community of Israel turned on Moses and Aaron: "You killed the LORD's people."

[42] But as the congregation gathered against Moses and Aaron, they turned toward the tent of meeting — and there was the cloud covering it, and the glory of the LORD appeared. [43] Moses and Aaron went to the front of the tent of meeting. [44] The LORD said to Moses: [45] "Get away from this congregation. I'm going to consume them in an instant."

They fell on their faces. [46] Moses said to Aaron, "Take the censer, put fire from the altar in it, lay incense on it, and run to the congregation — make atonement for them. The wrath has gone out from the LORD. The plague has begun."

[47] Aaron took it, just as Moses said, and ran into the middle of the assembly. The plague was already tearing through them. He put on the incense and made atonement for the people. [48] He stood between the dead and the living — and the plague stopped. [49] Fourteen thousand seven hundred died in the plague — on top of those who died over Korah. [50] Then Aaron came back to Moses at the entrance of the tent of meeting. The plague was over.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: [2] "Tell the Israelites to bring you twelve staffs—one from each tribal leader, one per ancestral house. Write each man's name on his own staff. [3] Write Aaron's name on the staff of Levi, because there will be one staff for the head of each ancestral house. [4] Place them in the Tent of Meeting in front of the covenant, where I meet with you. [5] The staff belonging to the man I choose will sprout. This is how I will silence, once and for all, the grumbling the Israelites keep throwing at you."

[6] Moses spoke to the Israelites, and every leader handed him a staff—one for each tribal chief, twelve in all—and Aaron's staff was among them. [7] Moses laid the staffs before the LORD inside the Tent of the Covenant.

[8] The next day Moses walked into the Tent of the Covenant—and there it was: Aaron's staff, the one belonging to the house of Levi, had budded. It had sprouted buds, broken into blossoms, and produced ripe almonds. [9] Moses carried all the staffs out from before the LORD to the Israelites. They looked. Each man took back his own.

[10] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Put Aaron's staff back in front of the covenant. Keep it there as a warning sign for these rebels—so their complaining stops and they don't die." [11] Moses did exactly as the LORD commanded.

[12] The Israelites said to Moses, "We're finished. We're all going to die. Every one of us is doomed. [13] Anyone who even comes near the LORD's dwelling dies. Are we all going to be wiped out?"

[1] Then the LORD spoke to Aaron: You, your sons, and your father's house with you — you will bear the weight of any offense against the sanctuary. And you and your sons with you will bear the weight of any offense against your priesthood.

[2] Bring your brothers too — the tribe of Levi, your father's tribe — bring them alongside you. Let them attach themselves to you and serve you while you and your sons stand before the Tent of the Covenant.

[3] They will handle your duties and the duties of the entire Tent. But they must not come near the holy vessels or the altar — if they do, both they and you will die.

[4] They join you. They guard the Tent of Meeting. They handle all its service. But no outsider comes near you.

[5] You will guard the sanctuary. You will guard the altar. And wrath will not fall on Israel again.

[6] Look — I myself have taken your brothers the Levites from among the Israelites. They are a gift to you, given to the LORD, to perform the service of the Tent of Meeting.

[7] But you and your sons with you — you will guard your priesthood in everything that concerns the altar and what lies behind the veil. That is your work. I give the priesthood to you as a gift of service. Any outsider who steps in dies.

[8] The LORD spoke to Aaron: Listen — I am putting you in charge of the offerings lifted up to me. Every sacred gift the Israelites bring, I give to you as your portion and to your sons as a perpetual share.

[9] This is yours from the most holy gifts — what is kept from the fire: every grain offering, every sin offering, every guilt offering they bring back to me. It is most holy, belonging to you and your sons.

[10] Eat it in a most holy place. Every male may eat it. Treat it as holy.

[11] And this also is yours: the elevated portion of their gifts, every wave offering of the Israelites — I give them to you, to your sons, and to your daughters with you as a perpetual share. Anyone in your household who is ceremonially clean may eat them.

[12] All the best of the olive oil, all the best of the new wine and grain — the firstfruits they give to the LORD — I give them to you.

[13] The firstfruits of everything in their land that they bring to the LORD are yours. Anyone in your household who is clean may eat them.

[14] Everything in Israel placed under the ban — yours.

[15] Every firstborn that opens the womb, human or animal, offered to the LORD — yours. But you must redeem the firstborn of humans, and you must redeem the firstborn of unclean animals.

[16] As for the redemption price: from one month old, redeem them at five shekels of silver by the sanctuary weight — twenty gerahs per shekel.

[17] But the firstborn of a cow, a sheep, or a goat — these you do not redeem. They are holy. Splash their blood on the altar and burn their fat as a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

[18] Their meat is yours, like the breast of the wave offering and the right thigh — yours.

[19] Every sacred offering the Israelites lift up to the LORD, I give to you, to your sons, and to your daughters with you as a perpetual share. It is a covenant of salt before the LORD — eternal — for you and your descendants with you.

[20] The LORD said to Aaron: You will have no inheritance in their land. You will own no portion among them. I am your portion. I am your inheritance among the Israelites.

[21] As for the Levites — I am giving them every tithe in Israel as their inheritance, in exchange for the work they do, the service of the Tent of Meeting.

[22] From now on the Israelites must not come near the Tent of Meeting, or they will bear the guilt and die.

[23] The Levites will do the work of the Tent of Meeting, and they will bear any guilt connected with it. This is a perpetual statute through all your generations. They will receive no inheritance among the Israelites.

[24] Because I have given the Levites the tithe the Israelites offer up to the LORD — that is their inheritance. That is why I told them they would have no inheritance among the Israelites.

[25] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[26] Tell the Levites this: When you receive from the Israelites the tithe I have assigned you as your inheritance, you must lift up from it a tithe of the tithe as an offering to the LORD.

[27] This will count as your own offering, as if it were grain from the threshing floor or the fullness of the winepress.

[28] So you too will lift up an offering to the LORD from every tithe you receive from the Israelites. Give the LORD's portion from it to Aaron the priest.

[29] From every gift you receive, lift up the LORD's portion — the best of it, the consecrated part.

[30] Tell them this: When you lift up the best of it, the rest is credited to the Levites like the yield of the threshing floor and the winepress.

[31] You and your households may eat it anywhere, because it is your wages in exchange for your service at the Tent of Meeting.

[32] You will bear no guilt for it once you have lifted up the best of it. Do not desecrate the sacred gifts of the Israelites — or you will die.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron:

[2] Here is the statute of the Torah that the LORD commands: Tell the Israelites to bring you a red heifer — flawless, without blemish, one that has never borne a yoke.

[3] Hand her over to Eleazar the priest. She is to be led outside the camp and slaughtered in his presence.

[4] Eleazar will dip his finger in her blood and sprinkle it seven times toward the front of the Tent of Meeting.

[5] Then the heifer is to be burned in his sight — hide, flesh, blood, dung, all of it consumed by fire.

[6] The priest takes cedar wood, hyssop, and crimson thread, and tosses them into the blaze where the heifer burns.

[7] Then the priest washes his clothes, bathes his body in water, and afterward he may enter the camp — but he stays unclean until evening.

[8] The one who burned her also washes his clothes and bathes — unclean until evening.

[9] Someone ceremonially clean gathers up the ashes of the heifer and places them outside the camp in a clean spot. They are kept there for the Israelite community, to be mixed into the water of cleansing. It is a purification for sin.

[10] The one who gathered the ashes washes his clothes — unclean until evening. This is a lasting statute for the Israelites and for the foreigner living among them.

[11] Anyone who touches a human corpse is unclean for seven days.

[12] He must purify himself with the water on the third day and the seventh day, and then he is clean. Skip the third day, skip the seventh — he stays unclean.

[13] Whoever touches a corpse and does not purify himself defiles the LORD's dwelling. That person is cut off from Israel. The water of cleansing was not thrown on him — his uncleanness clings to him.

[14] Here is the rule when someone dies in a tent: everyone who enters the tent and everyone already inside it is unclean for seven days.

[15] Every open container without a sealed lid is unclean.

[16] Anyone out in the open who touches someone killed by the sword, or a corpse, or a human bone, or a grave — unclean for seven days.

[17] For the unclean person, take some ash from the burned purification offering and pour fresh running water over it in a vessel.

[18] A clean person dips hyssop in the water and sprinkles the tent, all the vessels, and everyone who was there — and whoever touched the bone, the slain, the corpse, the grave.

[19] On the third day and the seventh day the clean person sprinkles the unclean. On the seventh day he purifies him. Then the unclean person washes his clothes, bathes in water, and by evening he is clean.

[20] But anyone who is unclean and refuses to purify himself — that person is cut off from the assembly. He has defiled the LORD's sanctuary. The water of cleansing was not thrown on him. He is unclean.

[21] This is a lasting statute for them. The one who sprinkles the water of cleansing must wash his clothes, and anyone who touches the water is unclean until evening.

[22] Whatever the unclean person touches becomes unclean, and whoever touches him is unclean until evening.

[1] In the first month, the whole community of Israel arrived at the Wilderness of Zin and camped at Kadesh. There Miriam died. There she was buried.

[2] There was no water for the community. So they massed against Moses and Aaron.

[3] The people picked a fight with Moses. "If only we'd dropped dead when our brothers dropped dead before the LORD!

[4] Why did you drag the LORD's assembly out into this wilderness — so we could die here with our livestock?

[5] Why did you haul us up out of Egypt and bring us to this miserable place? No grain. No figs, no grapes, no pomegranates. And no water to drink."

[6] Moses and Aaron walked away from the crowd, went to the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, and fell on their faces. The glory of the LORD blazed out in front of them.

[7] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[8] "Take the staff. Gather the community — you and your brother Aaron. Then, with them watching, speak to that rock. It will give up its water. You'll bring water out of the rock for them — water for the community, water for their livestock."

[9] Moses took the staff from before the LORD, just as he was commanded.

[10] Then he and Aaron assembled the crowd in front of the rock. Moses said to them, "Listen now, you rebels — must we bring water for you out of this rock?"

[11] Moses raised his hand and struck the rock with his staff. Twice. Water came gushing out — torrents of it. The community drank. Their animals drank.

[12] But the LORD said to Moses and Aaron, "Because you didn't trust me — because you didn't show me as holy in the eyes of Israel — you will not lead this assembly into the land I'm giving them."

[13] These are the waters of Meribah — Quarrel — where Israel quarreled with the LORD, and where he showed himself holy among them.

[14] Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom: "Your brother Israel says this. You know all the hardship we've endured —

[15] how our ancestors went down to Egypt, how we lived there a long time, how the Egyptians brutalized us and our fathers.

[16] We cried out to the LORD, and he heard us. He sent a messenger and brought us out of Egypt. Now we're at Kadesh, a town on the edge of your territory.

[17] Let us pass through your land. We won't cut across fields or vineyards. We won't drink from your wells. We'll stay on the King's Highway — no swerving right or left — until we're through your borders."

[18] Edom answered, "You will not pass through. Try it, and I'll come out against you with the sword."

[19] The Israelites said, "We'll keep to the main road. If we or our animals drink your water, we'll pay for it. Just let us cross on foot — that's all we ask."

[20] Edom said, "You will not pass through." And he marched out against them with a heavy force, armed and ready.

[21] Edom refused to let Israel cross his territory. So Israel turned away.

[22] The whole Israelite community set out from Kadesh and came to Mount Hor.

[23] At Mount Hor, on the border of Edom, the LORD said to Moses and Aaron,

[24] "Aaron is about to be gathered to his people. He will not enter the land I'm giving Israel — because the two of you defied my word at the waters of Meribah.

[25] Take Aaron and his son Eleazar. Bring them up Mount Hor.

[26] Strip Aaron of his priestly garments and put them on Eleazar his son. Aaron will be gathered there. He will die there."

[27] Moses did as the LORD commanded. They climbed Mount Hor with the whole community watching.

[28] Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the summit. Then Moses and Eleazar came down the mountain.

[29] When the community saw that Aaron was gone, all Israel wept for him — thirty days of mourning, the whole house of Israel.

[1] When the Canaanite king of Arad, who ruled the Negev, caught wind that Israel was coming up the road to Atharim, he attacked and took some of them captive.

[2] Israel made a vow to the LORD: "If you hand this people over to us, we will devote their cities to destruction."

[3] The LORD heard Israel and gave the Canaanites over. Israel wiped them out — them and their towns — and named the place Hormah, "Devoted to Destruction."

[4] They broke camp at Mount Hor and headed toward the Red Sea, swinging around to bypass Edom. The people's patience snapped along the way.

[5] They turned on God and on Moses: "Why did you drag us out of Egypt to die in this wasteland? No bread. No water. And we're sick to death of this miserable food."

[6] So the LORD sent venomous snakes among them. They bit the people, and many Israelites died.

[7] The people came to Moses and confessed: "We sinned. We spoke against the LORD and against you. Pray. Get the snakes away from us." So Moses prayed for them.

[8] The LORD told Moses, "Make a snake — mount it on a pole. Anyone who's been bitten can look at it and live."

[9] Moses fashioned a bronze serpent and lifted it on a pole. When a snake bit someone, they looked at the bronze serpent — and they lived.

[10] Israel moved on and camped at Oboth.

[11] From Oboth they marched to Iye-abarim, in the wilderness east of Moab, facing the sunrise.

[12] From there they moved on and camped in the Zered Valley.

[13] Next they camped across the Arnon, in the wilderness that spills out from Amorite territory. The Arnon is the boundary between Moab and the Amorites.

[14] That's why the Book of the Wars of the LORD says: "Waheb in Suphah, and the wadis — the Arnon

[15] and the slopes of the wadis that bend toward the settlement of Ar and lean against the border of Moab."

[16] From there they pressed on to Beer — the well where the LORD said to Moses, "Gather the people and I will give them water."

[17] Then Israel sang this song: "Spring up, well! Sing to it! —

[18] the well princes dug, that the nobles of the people sank with scepter, with staff." From the wilderness they went to Mattanah,

[19] from Mattanah to Nahaliel, from Nahaliel to Bamoth,

[20] from Bamoth to the valley in the country of Moab, near the summit of Pisgah, which looks out over the wasteland.

[21] Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, with this word:

[22] "Let us pass through your country. We won't cut into your fields or your vineyards. We won't even drink from your wells. We'll keep to the King's Highway until we're through your territory."

[23] But Sihon would not let Israel through. He mustered his entire army, marched into the wilderness against Israel, and met them at Jahaz. He attacked.

[24] Israel cut him down with the sword and seized his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok — as far as Ammonite country, where the border was fortified.

[25] Israel took every one of those Amorite cities and settled in them, including Heshbon and all its outlying towns.

[26] Heshbon was Sihon's capital — the Amorite king who had fought the previous king of Moab and stripped him of his land all the way to the Arnon.

[27] That's why the poets say: "Come to Heshbon — let it be rebuilt. Let Sihon's city be restored.

[28] A fire blazed out of Heshbon, a flame from Sihon's stronghold. It consumed Ar of Moab, devoured the lords of the Arnon's heights.

[29] Woe to you, Moab! You're finished, people of Chemosh! He handed over his sons as refugees, his daughters as captives to Sihon, king of the Amorites.

[30] We shot them down. Heshbon is destroyed all the way to Dibon. We laid them waste as far as Nophah, which reaches to Medeba."

[31] So Israel settled in Amorite territory.

[32] Moses sent scouts to Jazer. Israel took its surrounding villages and drove out the Amorites living there.

[33] Then they turned and headed up the road to Bashan. Og, king of Bashan, marched out against them with his whole army to meet them in battle at Edrei.

[34] The LORD said to Moses, "Don't be afraid of him. I've handed him over to you — him, his entire army, and his land. Do to him what you did to Sihon, king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon."

[35] So they struck him down — him, his sons, his entire army — left no survivor. And they took possession of his land.

[1] Israel pulled up stakes and camped on the plains of Moab, across the Jordan from Jericho.

[2] Balak son of Zippor had seen everything Israel did to the Amorites.

[3] Moab was terrified. The people were a swarm, and the dread of them sat heavy on every Moabite heart.

[4] Moab said to the elders of Midian, "This horde is about to lick up everything around us — like an ox stripping a field bare." Balak son of Zippor was king of Moab at the time,

[5] and he sent messengers to Balaam son of Beor at Pethor, by the River, in his native land. "Listen," he said, "a people has come up out of Egypt. They cover the face of the earth. They are camped right next to me.

[6] Come — curse them for me. They are too strong for me. Maybe then I can strike them and drive them out. I know how it works: whoever you bless is blessed, whoever you curse is cursed."

[7] The elders of Moab and Midian set out with the fee for divination in hand. They reached Balaam and laid Balak's words before him.

[8] "Stay the night," Balaam said. "I will bring you whatever word the LORD speaks to me." So the princes of Moab stayed with Balaam.

[9] God came to Balaam and asked, "Who are these men with you?"

[10] Balaam answered God, "Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, has sent word to me:

[11] 'A people has come out of Egypt and covers the face of the earth. Come, curse them for me. Maybe then I can fight them and drive them out.'"

[12] God said to Balaam, "Do not go with them. Do not curse that people. They are blessed."

[13] In the morning Balaam rose and said to Balak's princes, "Go home. The LORD refuses to let me go with you."

[14] The princes of Moab went back to Balak: "Balaam would not come with us."

[15] So Balak sent again — more princes, more distinguished than the first.

[16] They arrived at Balaam and said, "This is what Balak son of Zippor says: 'Do not let anything stop you from coming to me.

[17] I will honor you lavishly. Whatever you ask, I will do. Just come — curse this people for me.'"

[18] Balaam answered Balak's servants, "If Balak gave me his palace stuffed with silver and gold, I could not cross the word of the LORD my God — not in anything, small or great.

[19] But stay the night here too, and I will find out what more the LORD has to say to me."

[20] God came to Balaam that night and said, "If these men have come to summon you, go with them. But do only what I tell you."

[21] Balaam got up in the morning, saddled his donkey, and went with the princes of Moab.

[22] But God's anger flared because he was going, and the angel of the LORD planted himself in the road to block him. Balaam was riding his donkey; his two servants were with him.

[23] The donkey saw the angel of the LORD standing in the road, sword drawn in his hand. She bolted off the road into the field, and Balaam beat her to turn her back.

[24] Then the angel of the LORD took up his stand in a narrow lane between vineyards, walls on either side.

[25] The donkey saw the angel of the LORD and pressed against the wall, crushing Balaam's foot against the stones. He beat her again.

[26] The angel of the LORD moved ahead and stood in a tight place, nowhere to turn — right or left.

[27] The donkey saw the angel of the LORD and sank down beneath Balaam. Balaam's fury exploded, and he beat her with his staff.

[28] Then the LORD opened the donkey's mouth, and she said to Balaam, "What have I done to you, that you have beaten me three times now?"

[29] Balaam said to the donkey, "You have made a fool of me. If I had a sword in my hand, I would kill you right now."

[30] The donkey said, "Am I not your own donkey? Have you ever ridden anything else your whole life? Have I ever done this to you before?" And he said, "No."

[31] Then the LORD uncovered Balaam's eyes, and he saw the angel of the LORD standing in the road, sword drawn. He bowed low and fell on his face.

[32] The angel of the LORD said to him, "Why did you beat your donkey three times? I came out to block you, because your way is reckless before me.

[33] The donkey saw me and turned aside three times. If she had not turned aside, by now I would have killed you and let her live."

[34] Balaam said to the angel of the LORD, "I have sinned. I did not know you were standing in the road against me. If this displeases you, I will turn back."

[35] The angel of the LORD said to Balaam, "Go with the men. But speak only the word I give you." So Balaam went on with Balak's princes.

[36] When Balak heard Balaam was coming, he went out to meet him at the Moabite town on the Arnon border, at the far edge of his territory.

[37] Balak said to Balaam, "Did I not send for you? Urgently? Why did you not come? Am I really unable to honor you?"

[38] Balaam answered, "Look — I have come. But I have no power to say anything on my own. The word God puts in my mouth — that is what I will speak."

[39] Balaam went with Balak, and they came to Kiriath-huzoth.

[40] Balak sacrificed cattle and sheep, and sent portions to Balaam and the princes with him.

[41] In the morning Balak took Balaam up to Bamoth-baal, and from there he could see the edge of the people.

[1] Balaam said to Balak, "Build me seven altars here, and bring me seven bulls and seven rams."

[2] Balak did exactly what Balaam asked, and together they offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

[3] Then Balaam said, "Stand here by your offering. I'll go off alone. Maybe the LORD will come and meet me. Whatever he shows me, I'll tell you." And he walked off to a barren ridge.

[4] God met him there. Balaam said, "I've arranged seven altars, and on each one I've offered a bull and a ram."

[5] The LORD put words in Balaam's mouth and said, "Go back to Balak. Speak what I've given you."

[6] So Balaam returned and found Balak still standing beside his offering, all the chiefs of Moab with him.

[7] Then Balaam opened his mouth and spoke his oracle:

"Balak hauled me down from Aram,
the king of Moab from the eastern hills:
'Come,' he said, 'curse Jacob for me.
Come, damn Israel.'

[8] But how can I curse what God has not cursed?
How can I damn what the LORD has not damned?

[9] From the crag I see them.
From the heights I watch them —
a people who camp alone,
who refuse to be counted with the nations.

[10] Who could number the dust of Jacob?
Who could count even a quarter of Israel?
Let me die the death of the upright.
Let my end be like theirs."

[11] Balak exploded. "What have you done to me? I brought you here to curse my enemies, and look — you've blessed them. Blessed them!"

[12] Balaam answered, "Should I not be careful to say exactly what the LORD puts in my mouth?"

[13] Balak said, "Come with me to another place where you can see them — not the whole camp, just the edge. Curse them from there for me."

[14] So he took him to the Field of the Watchmen, on top of Pisgah. He built seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each.

[15] Balaam said, "Stand here beside your offering while I go meet him over there."

[16] The LORD met Balaam, put words in his mouth, and said, "Go back to Balak. Speak."

[17] He came back and found Balak standing beside his offering, the chiefs of Moab with him. Balak demanded, "What did the LORD say?"

[18] Then Balaam lifted his oracle:

"Get up, Balak, and listen.
Lean in, son of Zippor.

[19] God is not a man, that he should lie,
not a son of man, that he should change his mind.
Does he speak and not act?
Does he promise and not deliver?

[20] I was commanded to bless.
He has blessed — I cannot reverse it.

[21] He sees no trouble in Jacob,
no misery in Israel.
The LORD their God is with them,
and the shout of a king rings out among them.

[22] God brought them out of Egypt
with the strength of a wild ox.

[23] No sorcery works against Jacob,
no divination against Israel.
The time will come when it is said of Jacob and Israel:
'Look what God has done!'

[24] These are a people who rise like a lioness,
who rouse themselves like a lion —
they don't lie down until they've devoured the prey
and drunk the blood of the slain."

[25] Balak said to Balaam, "If you won't curse them, then at least don't bless them!"

[26] Balaam answered, "Didn't I warn you? Whatever the LORD says — that's what I do."

[27] Balak tried again: "Come, let me take you somewhere else. Maybe God will be pleased to let you curse them from there."

[28] So he took Balaam to the summit of Peor, looking down over the wasteland.

[29] Balaam said, "Build me seven altars here, and bring me seven bulls and seven rams."

[30] Balak did what Balaam said, and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

[1] By now Balaam knew the truth: Yahweh meant to bless Israel. So he didn't go hunting for omens like before. He turned his face toward the wilderness.

[2] And when he looked up and saw Israel camped tribe by tribe across the plain, the Spirit of God came over him.

[3] Then he spoke his oracle: "This is the word of Balaam son of Beor, the word of the man whose eye sees clearly,

[4] the word of one who hears what God says, who sees what the Almighty shows him — falling into trance, eyes wide open:

[5] How beautiful your tents are, Jacob — your dwellings, Israel!

[6] They stretch out like river valleys, like gardens beside a stream, like aloe trees Yahweh planted, like cedars beside the water.

[7] Water spills from his buckets; his seed drinks from rivers in flood. His king will rise higher than Agag; his kingdom will be lifted up.

[8] God brought him out of Egypt — he has the strength of a wild ox. He'll devour the nations that hunt him, crush their bones, drive his arrows through them.

[9] He crouches, he lies down like a lion — like a lioness — who dares wake him? Blessed is anyone who blesses you. Cursed is anyone who curses you."

[10] Balak exploded. He slammed his hands together and shouted at Balaam, "I called you here to curse my enemies — and you've blessed them three times now!

[11] Get out. Go home. I was going to make you a wealthy man, but Yahweh has cheated you out of your reward."

[12] Balaam answered, "Didn't I tell your messengers from the start —

[13] even if Balak gave me his palace stuffed with silver and gold, I couldn't go past what Yahweh says, not one step, good or bad, on my own? Whatever Yahweh speaks — that's what I speak.

[14] Now I'm going back to my people. But before I leave, let me tell you what this people will do to your people in the days ahead."

[15] Then he spoke his oracle: "This is the word of Balaam son of Beor, the word of the man whose eye sees clearly,

[16] the word of one who hears what God says, who knows what the Most High knows, who sees what the Almighty shows him — falling into trance, eyes wide open:

[17] I see him — but not now. I behold him — but not near. A star will rise out of Jacob. A scepter will rise out of Israel. It will crush the skull of Moab, the heads of all the sons of tumult.

[18] Edom will be conquered. Seir, his enemy, will be conquered. But Israel will grow stronger.

[19] One from Jacob will rule — he'll destroy whatever survives in the city.

[20] Then Balaam looked at Amalek and spoke: "Amalek was first among the nations — but his end is ruin."

[21] He looked at the Kenites and spoke: "Your home is solid, your nest set in the rock —

[22] yet Kain will be burned away. How long before Asshur drags you off?"

[23] Then he spoke once more: "Who can survive when God does this?

[24] Ships will come from the coast of Kittim. They'll humble Asshur, they'll humble Eber — and they too will end in ruin."

[25] Then Balaam got up and went home. And Balak went his own way.

[1] Israel camped at Shittim, and the men started sleeping with Moabite women. [2] The women drew them in — invitations to their sacrifices, feasts for their gods. Israel ate at those tables and bowed to those gods. [3] Israel yoked itself to Baal of Peor. The LORD's anger ignited against Israel. [4] The LORD told Moses: "Take every ringleader and execute them in broad daylight, in full view of the LORD, so the heat of his anger turns away from Israel." [5] Moses commanded Israel's judges: "Each of you — kill the men under you who yoked themselves to Baal of Peor."

[6] Then — right there, in front of Moses, in front of the entire assembly weeping at the Tent's entrance — an Israelite man strolled up and brought a Midianite woman to his brothers. [7] Phinehas, son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron the priest, saw it. He rose from the assembly, grabbed a spear, [8] followed the man into the tent, and drove the spear through them both — through the man and into the woman's belly. The plague stopped. [9] But not before twenty-four thousand were dead.

[10] The LORD said to Moses: [11] "Phinehas, son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron the priest, turned my fury away from Israel. He burned with my own jealousy among them, and because of that I did not consume Israel in my jealousy. [12] So tell him: I am cutting a covenant of peace with him. [13] For him and his descendants after him — a covenant of priesthood that does not end. Because he was zealous for his God. Because he made atonement for the people of Israel."

[14] The Israelite man who was killed alongside the Midianite woman was Zimri son of Salu, a chieftain of a Simeonite family. [15] The Midianite woman was Cozbi daughter of Zur — and Zur was a tribal head, a chief's daughter, in Midian.

[16] The LORD said to Moses: [17] "Treat the Midianites as enemies. Strike them down. [18] They treated you as enemies first — with their schemes, with Peor, with Cozbi the daughter of a Midianite chief, their sister, who was struck down on the day the plague came because of Peor."

[1] After the plague burned itself out, the LORD said to Moses and to Eleazar son of Aaron the priest:

[2] "Take a fresh count of the whole community of Israel — every male twenty years old and up, by ancestral house — everyone fit to march out for war."

[3] So Moses and Eleazar the priest spoke with them on the plains of Moab by the Jordan, across from Jericho:

[4] "Count the men from twenty years old and up, just as the LORD commanded Moses." These are the Israelites who came out of Egypt:

[8] Pallu's son was Eliab,

[41] These were Benjamin's clans — 45,600 mustered.
