[52] The LORD said to Moses:

[53] "Divide the land among these as their inheritance — measured out by the count of names.

[54] Give a larger inheritance to a larger group and a smaller one to a smaller group. Each tribe's inheritance is to match the number of those enrolled.

[55] But the actual allotment will be by lot — they will inherit according to the names of their ancestral tribes.

[56] By lot the inheritance is divided between the larger groups and the smaller ones."

[57] These were the Levites mustered, by clan: Gershon and the Gershonite clan, Kohath and the Kohathite clan, Merari and the Merarite clan.

[58] These were the Levite clans: Libnite, Hebronite, Mahlite, Mushite, Korahite. Kohath fathered Amram.

[59] Amram's wife was Jochebed, daughter of Levi — born to Levi in Egypt. To Amram she bore Aaron, Moses, and their sister Miriam.

[60] Aaron's sons were Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.

[61] Nadab and Abihu died when they brought unauthorized fire before the LORD.

[62] The total Levite muster was 23,000 — every male a month old and up. They were not counted with the rest of Israel, because they were given no inheritance among the Israelites.

[63] These were the men Moses and Eleazar the priest enrolled when they counted the Israelites on the plains of Moab by the Jordan across from Jericho.

[64] Among them not a single man was left from those Moses and Aaron had first counted in the wilderness of Sinai —

[65] for the LORD had told them: they would die in the wilderness. Not one survived — except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.

[1] Then the daughters of Zelophehad came forward. Their father was Zelophehad son of Hepher, son of Gilead, son of Machir, son of Manasseh — from the clans of Manasseh son of Joseph. Their names: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

[2] They stood at the entrance of the tent of meeting — facing Moses, facing Eleazar the priest, facing the chiefs and the whole assembly — and they said:

[3] "Our father died in the wilderness. He was not part of Korah's faction, the ones who banded together against the LORD. He died for his own sin. And he had no sons.

[4] Why should our father's name be erased from his clan just because he had no son? Give us a holding among our father's brothers."

[5] Moses brought their case before the LORD.

[6] And the LORD said to Moses:

[7] "The daughters of Zelophehad are right. Give them, without question, a piece of land alongside their father's brothers. Transfer their father's inheritance to them.

[8] And tell Israel this: When a man dies leaving no son, his inheritance passes to his daughter.

[9] If he has no daughter, it goes to his brothers.

[10] If he has no brothers, it goes to his father's brothers.

[11] And if his father had no brothers, then it goes to the nearest relative in his clan — he becomes the heir. This is the binding ruling for Israel, just as the LORD commanded Moses."

[12] Then the LORD said to Moses, "Climb this mountain — the Abarim range — and look out over the land I am giving to the people of Israel.

[13] When you have seen it, you too will be gathered to your people, just as your brother Aaron was gathered.

[14] Because back in the wilderness of Zin, when the community broke into rebellion, the two of you defied my command to display my holiness before their eyes at the waters." (These are the waters of Meribah at Kadesh, in the wilderness of Zin.)

[15] Moses said to the LORD:

[16] "LORD — God of the breath of all flesh — appoint someone over this community,

[17] someone to go out before them and come in before them, to lead them out and bring them home, so the LORD's people are not like sheep with no shepherd."

[18] The LORD said to Moses, "Take Joshua son of Nun — a man with the Spirit in him — and lay your hand on him.

[19] Set him before Eleazar the priest and the whole assembly, and commission him in their sight.

[20] Transfer some of your authority to him, so the entire community of Israel will obey him.

[21] He will stand before Eleazar the priest, who will seek decisions for him through the Urim before the LORD. At his word they will go out, at his word they will come in — he and all the people of Israel with him, the whole community."

[22] Moses did what the LORD commanded. He took Joshua, set him before Eleazar the priest and the whole assembly,

[23] laid his hands on him, and commissioned him — exactly as the LORD had spoken through Moses.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Give this command to Israel: Make sure you bring me my offerings — the food for my fires, the aroma that pleases me — at the appointed times.

[3] Tell them: This is the fire-offering you bring to the LORD. Every day, without fail: two year-old lambs, flawless, as a continual burnt offering.

[4] One lamb at dawn. The other at twilight.

[5] With each lamb, a grain offering — two quarts of fine flour mixed with a quart of pressed olive oil.

[6] This is the continual burnt offering, the one established at Sinai — a fire-aroma rising up, pleasing to the LORD.

[7] With it, the drink offering: a quart of strong drink, poured out at the sanctuary for each lamb — a libation to the LORD.

[8] The second lamb at twilight — same grain offering, same drink offering as the morning. A fire-aroma, pleasing to the LORD.

[9] On the Sabbath: two flawless year-old lambs, plus four quarts of fine flour mixed with oil, plus the drink offering.

[10] This is the Sabbath burnt offering — every Sabbath — in addition to the continual offering and its drink offering.

[11] At the start of every month: present a burnt offering to the LORD — two young bulls, one ram, seven flawless year-old lambs.

[12] With each bull, six quarts of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering. With the ram, four quarts.

[13] With each lamb, two quarts. This is the burnt offering — a fire-aroma, pleasing to the LORD.

[14] And the drink offerings: two quarts of wine for each bull, a quart and a third for the ram, a quart for each lamb. This is the monthly burnt offering — every new moon, all year long.

[15] Also one male goat as a sin offering to the LORD — in addition to the continual burnt offering and its drink offering.

[16] On the fourteenth day of the first month: Passover to the LORD.

[17] The fifteenth begins the festival — seven days of unleavened bread.

[18] Day one: a sacred assembly. No regular work.

[19] Bring a fire-offering, a burnt offering to the LORD: two young bulls, one ram, seven flawless year-old lambs.

[20] Their grain offerings — fine flour mixed with oil: six quarts for each bull, four quarts for the ram,

[21] two quarts for each of the seven lambs.

[22] Plus one male goat — a sin offering, to make atonement for you.

[23] All this is in addition to the morning burnt offering — the continual one.

[24] You bring this — the food for the LORD's fire, the pleasing aroma — every single day of the seven. Add it to the continual offering and its drink offering.

[25] On day seven: a sacred assembly. No regular work.

[26] On the day of firstfruits — when you bring the new grain offering at the Festival of Weeks — hold a sacred assembly. No regular work.

[27] Bring a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma to the LORD: two young bulls, one ram, seven year-old lambs.

[28] Their grain offering — fine flour mixed with oil: six quarts for each bull, four quarts for the ram,

[29] two quarts for each of the seven lambs.

[30] Plus one male goat to make atonement for you.

[31] All this is in addition to the continual burnt offering and its grain offering — and make sure they're flawless — along with their drink offerings.

[1] On the first day of the seventh month, gather as a sacred assembly. Do no ordinary work. This is your day to sound the trumpets.

[2] Bring a burnt offering — a fragrance that pleases the LORD: one young bull, one ram, seven year-old lambs without flaw.

[3] With them, the grain offering: fine flour mixed with oil — three-tenths of an ephah for the bull, two-tenths for the ram,

[4] one-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

[5] Add a male goat as a sin offering, to make atonement for you.

[6] All of this stands alongside the monthly burnt offering with its grain, the daily burnt offering with its grain, and their drink offerings — each in the prescribed measure. A fragrance that pleases the LORD, a fire-offering to him.

[8] Bring a burnt offering — a fragrance that pleases the LORD: one young bull, one ram, seven year-old lambs without flaw. They must be unblemished.

[9] With them, the grain offering: fine flour mixed with oil — three-tenths of an ephah for the bull, two-tenths for the ram,

[10] one-tenth for each of the seven lambs.

[11] Add a male goat as a sin offering — beyond the atonement sin offering, the daily burnt offering with its grain, and the drink offerings.

[37] with grain and drink offerings for the bull, ram, and lambs — measured by their number, by the rule.

[38] And a male goat as a sin offering — beyond the daily burnt offering with its grain and drink offering.

[39] These are the offerings you present to the LORD at your appointed festivals — over and above whatever you bring as vows, freewill gifts, burnt offerings, grain offerings, drink offerings, or peace offerings.

[40] Moses told the Israelites everything the LORD had commanded him.

[1] Moses spoke to the heads of the tribes of Israel: This is what the LORD commands.

[2] When a man makes a vow to the LORD or swears an oath binding himself to something, he cannot break his word. Every syllable that left his mouth — he carries it out.

[3] When a young woman still living in her father's house makes a vow to the LORD or binds herself by some pledge,

[4] and her father hears the vow or pledge and says nothing — every vow stands, every pledge holds.

[5] But if her father, the day he hears it, refuses her — none of her vows or pledges stand. The LORD releases her, because her father said no.

[6] If she marries while bound by vows or by some rash word that slipped from her lips,

[7] and her husband hears and says nothing on the day he hears — her vows stand, her pledges hold.

[8] But if her husband, the day he hears it, refuses her — he annuls the vow that binds her, the rash word that escaped her lips. And the LORD releases her.

[9] As for a widow or a divorced woman — whatever she binds herself to, stands.

[10] If she made the vow inside her husband's house, bound herself by oath,

[11] and her husband heard and said nothing, did not refuse her — every vow stands, every pledge holds.

[12] But if her husband, the day he heard, voided them — nothing she promised stands. Her husband canceled them; the LORD releases her.

[13] Every vow, every binding oath to humble herself — her husband can confirm it or void it.

[14] If he says nothing, day after day, he confirms every vow and every pledge she carries. He confirmed them by his silence the day he heard.

[15] But if he voids them later, after hearing — he bears her guilt.

[16] These are the statutes the LORD gave Moses regarding a man and his wife, a father and his daughter still young, still under his roof.

[1] The LORD spoke to Moses: [2] "Settle the score with the Midianites for what they did to Israel. After that, you'll be gathered to your people."

[3] So Moses told the people: "Arm some of your men for war. They're going against Midian to execute the LORD's vengeance. [4] Send a thousand from each tribe — every tribe in Israel — into battle."

[5] So they mustered from Israel's clans: a thousand per tribe, twelve thousand armed for war. [6] Moses dispatched them — a thousand from each tribe — with Phinehas son of Eleazar the priest, who carried the sacred vessels and the trumpets for the war cry.

[7] They attacked Midian exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses, and they killed every male. [8] Among the slain were the five kings of Midian — Evi, Rekem, Zur, Hur, and Reba — and they put Balaam son of Beor to the sword as well.

[9] The Israelites took the Midianite women and children captive, seized all their livestock, flocks, and wealth as plunder. [10] They torched every Midianite town and every settlement camp. [11] They hauled off all the spoil — people, animals, everything — [12] and brought the captives, plunder, and loot to Moses, Eleazar the priest, and the Israelite assembly at the camp on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho.

[13] Moses, Eleazar the priest, and the leaders of the community went out to meet them beyond the camp. [14] Moses was furious with the commanders of the army — the officers over thousands and hundreds returning from the campaign.

[15] "You let the women live?" Moses demanded. [16] "These are the very ones who — on Balaam's advice — seduced Israel into betraying the LORD at Peor. That's what brought the plague on the LORD's people. [17] So now: kill every male child. Kill every woman who has slept with a man. [18] But spare the young girls who have never been with a man — keep them alive for yourselves.

[19] "Now everyone who killed anyone or touched a corpse — stay outside the camp seven days. Purify yourselves and your captives on the third day and the seventh. [20] Purify every garment, every leather item, every piece of goat hair, every wooden vessel."

[21] Then Eleazar the priest said to the soldiers returning from battle: "This is the ruling of the law that the LORD gave Moses: [22] Gold, silver, bronze, iron, tin, lead — [23] anything that can withstand fire — pass it through fire, and it's clean. Still, it must also be purified with the water of cleansing. Whatever can't go through fire — run it through the water. [24] On the seventh day, wash your clothes. Then you're clean. After that, you can come back into camp."

[25] The LORD said to Moses: [26] "You, Eleazar the priest, and the heads of the community's clans — take inventory of the plunder, both people and animals. [27] Split it in two: half for the soldiers who went out to fight, half for the rest of the community. [28] From the soldiers' share, levy a tribute for the LORD: one out of every five hundred — people, cattle, donkeys, sheep. [29] Take it from their half and give it to Eleazar the priest as the LORD's offering. [30] From the Israelites' half, take one out of every fifty — people, cattle, donkeys, sheep, every kind of animal — and give them to the Levites who tend the LORD's tabernacle."

[31] Moses and Eleazar the priest did exactly as the LORD had commanded.

[32] The plunder — what was left after the soldiers had taken theirs — came to: 675,000 sheep, [33] 72,000 cattle, [34] 61,000 donkeys, [35] and 32,000 young women who had never slept with a man.

[36] The half belonging to those who fought came to: 337,500 sheep, [37] of which the LORD's tribute was 675. [38] Cattle: 36,000, of which the LORD's tribute was 72. [39] Donkeys: 30,500, of which the LORD's tribute was 61. [40] People: 16,000, of which the LORD's tribute was 32.

[41] Moses handed the tribute — the LORD's offering — to Eleazar the priest, exactly as the LORD had commanded.

[42] The Israelites' half, which Moses separated from what the soldiers received, [43] came to: 337,500 sheep, [44] 36,000 cattle, [45] 30,500 donkeys, [46] and 16,000 people. [47] From this half, Moses took one out of every fifty — people and animals — and gave them to the Levites who tended the LORD's tabernacle, just as the LORD had commanded him.

[48] Then the officers over the army's units — the commanders of thousands and hundreds — came to Moses [49] and said: "Your servants have counted the warriors under our command. Not one man is missing. [50] So we've brought an offering to the LORD: every man what gold he found — armlets, bracelets, signet rings, earrings, pendants — to make atonement for our lives before the LORD."

[51] Moses and Eleazar the priest accepted the gold from them — every kind of crafted ornament. [52] All the gold offered up to the LORD from the commanders of thousands and hundreds weighed 16,750 shekels. [53] (The rank-and-file soldiers had taken plunder for themselves.) [54] Moses and Eleazar received the gold from the commanders and brought it into the tent of meeting — a memorial for the Israelites before the LORD.

[1] The tribes of Reuben and Gad owned massive herds—enormous, staggering numbers of livestock. When they got a look at the territory of Jazer and Gilead, they saw it instantly: this was cattle country.

[2] So they came to Moses, to Eleazar the priest, and to the leaders of the community, and laid it out:

[3] "Ataroth, Dibon, Jazer, Nimrah, Heshbon, Elealeh, Sebam, Nebo, Beon—

[4] the whole region the LORD broke open before Israel—it's grazing land. Perfect for livestock. And your servants have livestock.

[5] If we've found favor with you," they said, "let this land be our inheritance. Don't make us cross the Jordan."

[6] Moses turned on them: "So your brothers march off to war while you sit here?

[7] Why would you drain the courage out of Israel, make them lose heart about crossing into the land the LORD has given them?

[8] This is exactly what your fathers did when I sent them from Kadesh-barnea to scout the land.

[9] They went up to the Valley of Eshcol, took one look, came back, and gutted the morale of the whole community—talked Israel out of entering the land the LORD had given.

[10] The LORD's anger blazed that day, and he swore:

[11] 'Not one of the men who came up out of Egypt, twenty years old and up, will see the land I promised to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—because they didn't follow me with their whole heart.

[12] Not one—except Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite and Joshua son of Nun. They followed the LORD all the way.'

[13] The LORD's anger burned against Israel, and he made them wander the wilderness for forty years until the entire generation that had done evil in his sight was finished.

[14] And here you are—a new crop sprouted from the same rotten stock—doing the same thing, stoking the LORD's fury against Israel all over again.

[15] If you turn away from following him, he'll abandon them again in the wilderness, and you'll be the ones who destroyed this whole people."

[16] They came closer and said, "We'll build sheep pens here for our livestock and towns for our little ones.

[17] But we ourselves will arm up and march at the front of Israel until we've brought them to their land. Meanwhile our families will stay in the fortified towns, safe from the people of the land.

[18] We won't go home until every Israelite has taken hold of his inheritance.

[19] We're not asking for any inheritance with them across the Jordan—our inheritance is right here, east of the river."

[20] Moses answered: "If you do this—if you arm yourselves for battle before the LORD,

[21] if every fighting man among you crosses the Jordan armed, before the LORD, until he has driven out his enemies,

[22] and the land is subdued before the LORD—then you can come back. You'll have done your duty to the LORD and to Israel, and this land will be yours, held before the LORD.

[23] But if you don't—understand this: you'll have sinned against the LORD, and your sin will track you down.

[24] So build your towns for your children, your pens for your flocks. But do what your mouth has promised."

[25] The Gadites and the Reubenites answered Moses: "Your servants will do exactly what you've ordered.

[26] Our children, our wives, our flocks, our livestock—all of them will stay here in the towns of Gilead.

[27] But your servants—every man armed for war—will cross over before the LORD into battle, just as you say."

[28] So Moses gave the orders to Eleazar the priest, to Joshua son of Nun, and to the heads of the tribal families of Israel.

[29] He told them: "If the Gadites and Reubenites cross the Jordan with you, every man armed for battle before the LORD, and the land is subdued before you—give them Gilead as their property.

[30] But if they don't cross with you armed, they get their property among you in Canaan."

[31] The Gadites and Reubenites replied: "What the LORD has spoken to your servants, that's what we'll do.

[32] We'll cross over armed, before the LORD, into the land of Canaan—and our inheritance will stay with us here, east of the Jordan."

[33] So Moses gave them—the Gadites, the Reubenites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh son of Joseph—the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan, the whole territory: the land with all its towns and the surrounding country.

[34] The Gadites rebuilt Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer,

[35] Atroth-shophan, Jazer, Jogbehah,

[36] Beth-nimrah, and Beth-haran—fortified towns with sheep pens.

[37] The Reubenites rebuilt Heshbon, Elealeh, Kiriathaim,

[38] Nebo, Baal-meon (the names changed), and Sibmah. They renamed the towns they rebuilt.

[39] The descendants of Machir son of Manasseh marched into Gilead, captured it, and drove out the Amorites who lived there.

[40] So Moses gave Gilead to Machir son of Manasseh, and he settled there.

[41] Jair son of Manasseh went and seized their villages and named them Havvoth-jair—Jair's Villages.

[42] And Nobah went and captured Kenath and its surrounding settlements and renamed it Nobah, after himself.

[1] Here is the itinerary of Israel's march out of Egypt, regiment by regiment, under the command of Moses and Aaron.

[2] Moses kept the record of each leg of the journey, stage by stage, at the LORD's order. These are the stages:

[3] They pulled out of Rameses in the first month, on the fifteenth day — the morning after Passover. Israel marched out triumphant, in full view of every Egyptian still standing,

[4] while Egypt buried its firstborn, every last one struck down by the LORD. Their gods? The LORD had passed judgment on them too.

[5] From Rameses they camped at Succoth.

[6] From Succoth, to Etham on the desert's edge.

[7] From Etham they doubled back to Pi-hahiroth, east of Baal-zephon, and camped near Migdol.

[8] From Pi-hahiroth they walked straight through the sea into the wilderness, traveled three days into the Etham desert, and pitched camp at Marah.

[9] From Marah to Elim — twelve springs and seventy palm trees — and they camped there.

[10] From Elim to the Red Sea.

[11] From the Red Sea into the wilderness of Sin.

[12] From Sin to Dophkah.

[13] From Dophkah to Alush.

[14] From Alush to Rephidim — where there was no water for the people to drink.

[15] From Rephidim into the wilderness of Sinai.

[16] From Sinai to Kibroth-hattaavah.

[17] From Kibroth-hattaavah to Hazeroth.

[18] From Hazeroth to Rithmah.

[19] From Rithmah to Rimmon-perez.

[20] From Rimmon-perez to Libnah.

[21] From Libnah to Rissah.

[22] From Rissah to Kehelathah.

[23] From Kehelathah to Mount Shepher.

[24] From Mount Shepher to Haradah.

[25] From Haradah to Makheloth.

[26] From Makheloth to Tahath.

[27] From Tahath to Terah.

[28] From Terah to Mithkah.

[29] From Mithkah to Hashmonah.

[30] From Hashmonah to Moseroth.

[31] From Moseroth to Bene-jaakan.

[32] From Bene-jaakan to Hor-haggidgad.

[33] From Hor-haggidgad to Jotbathah.

[34] From Jotbathah to Abronah.

[35] From Abronah to Ezion-geber.

[36] From Ezion-geber to the wilderness of Zin — that is, Kadesh.

[37] From Kadesh to Mount Hor, on the border of Edom.

[38] There at Mount Hor, Aaron the priest climbed up at the LORD's command and died — the first day of the fifth month, the fortieth year after Israel left Egypt.

[39] He was a hundred twenty-three years old when he died on that mountain.

[40] The Canaanite king of Arad, who held the Negev in southern Canaan, heard Israel was coming.

[41] They moved on from Mount Hor and camped at Zalmonah.

[42] From Zalmonah to Punon.

[43] From Punon to Oboth.

[44] From Oboth to Iye-abarim, in Moabite territory.

[45] From Iyim to Dibon-gad.

[46] From Dibon-gad to Almon-diblathaim.

[47] From Almon-diblathaim into the Abarim range, facing Nebo.

[48] From the Abarim mountains down to the plains of Moab, along the Jordan across from Jericho.

[49] They spread out along the Jordan in Moab, camp after camp, from Beth-jeshimoth all the way to Abel-shittim.

[50] There on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan opposite Jericho, the LORD spoke to Moses:

[51] "Tell the Israelites: when you cross the Jordan into Canaan,

[52] drive out every person living there. Smash their carved stones. Shatter their cast idols. Tear down every high place they worship at.

[53] Take the land. Settle it. I have handed it over to you — it's yours.

[54] Divide it by lot among your clans. Larger clans get more, smaller clans get less. Wherever the lot falls, that's their portion. Allot it by ancestral tribe.

[55] But if you fail to drive the inhabitants out, the ones you let stay will become splinters in your eyes and thorns in your sides. They will torment you in the very land you live in.

[56] And then — what I planned to do to them, I will do to you."

[1] Out on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho, the LORD spoke to Moses:

[2] "Tell the Israelites to give the Levites cities to live in from the inheritance the others hold. Give them the cities themselves and the pastureland around each one.

[3] The cities will be their homes; the pastureland will support their cattle, their flocks, and every animal they own.

[4] The pastureland you give the Levites extends out from the city wall a thousand cubits in every direction.

[5] Measure two thousand cubits east of the city, two thousand south, two thousand west, two thousand north — with the city at the center. That is their pastureland.

[6] Among the cities you give the Levites, designate six as cities of refuge — places a killer can flee to. Add forty-two more to these.

[7] In all, give the Levites forty-eight cities, each with its surrounding pastureland.

[8] The cities you give from the Israelites' holdings — take more from the larger tribes and fewer from the smaller ones. Each tribe gives in proportion to what it has received."

[9] The LORD said to Moses:

[10] "Tell the Israelites: When you cross the Jordan into Canaan,

[11] choose cities to serve as places of refuge — so that someone who kills another person by accident can run to safety.

[12] These cities will shield the killer from the avenger of blood, so that he isn't cut down before he stands trial before the assembly.

[13] Six cities, then, will function as places of refuge.

[14] Three on the east side of the Jordan, three in the land of Canaan. These six cities of refuge

[15] are for the Israelites, for the foreigner, and for anyone living among you — so that anyone who kills another by accident can flee there.

[16] But if a man strikes another with an iron object and the man dies — that is murder. The murderer must be put to death.

[17] If he uses a stone in his hand large enough to kill, and the man dies — that is murder. The murderer must be put to death.

[18] If he uses a wooden weapon capable of killing, and the man dies — that is murder. The murderer must be put to death.

[19] The avenger of blood is the one who carries out the execution. When he meets the murderer, he puts him to death.

[20] If a man shoves another out of hatred, or hurls something at him with intent to kill, and the man dies —

[21] or strikes him with his fist in hostility, and he dies — the assailant must be put to death. He is a murderer. The avenger of blood, on meeting him, will execute him.

[22] But if a man shoves another without hostility, or accidentally throws something that strikes him,

[23] or drops a stone heavy enough to kill on someone he didn't see — without enmity, without meaning him harm —

[24] then the assembly must judge between the killer and the avenger of blood, following these rules.

[25] The assembly must rescue the killer from the avenger's hand and return him to the city of refuge where he had fled. He must live there until the high priest, anointed with holy oil, dies.

[26] But if the killer ever steps outside the boundary of the city of refuge where he fled,

[27] and the avenger of blood finds him beyond it and kills him — the avenger is not guilty of bloodshed.

[28] The killer must stay inside the city of refuge until the high priest dies. After the high priest's death, he is free to return to his own land.

[29] These are the legal standards binding on you, generation after generation, wherever you live.

[30] Whoever kills another must be executed only on the testimony of witnesses. The word of a single witness is not enough to put a man to death.

[31] Do not accept a ransom to spare the life of a murderer who deserves to die. He must be put to death.

[32] And do not accept a ransom from someone who has fled to a city of refuge, letting him come home before the high priest dies.

[33] Do not defile the land where you live. Bloodshed defiles the land. And no atonement can be made for the land when blood is spilled on it, except by the blood of the one who shed it.

[34] Do not pollute the land you live in — the land where I myself dwell. For I, the LORD, dwell among the Israelites."

[1] The clan leaders from Gilead's line — Gilead son of Machir son of Manasseh, from Joseph's clans — came forward and laid out their case before Moses and the chieftains, the heads of Israel's families.

[2] They said: "The LORD ordered our lord to parcel out the land by lot to Israel as their inheritance. And the LORD ordered our lord to assign the inheritance of our brother Zelophehad to his daughters.

[3] But here's the problem. If they marry men from other Israelite tribes, their inheritance gets pulled out of our ancestral share and folded into the tribe they marry into. Our allotment shrinks.

[4] And when Israel's Jubilee comes around, their inheritance stays absorbed into that other tribe — gone from our father's tribe for good."

[5] So Moses, on the LORD's orders, gave Israel this ruling: "Joseph's tribe has spoken rightly.

[6] This is what the LORD commands concerning Zelophehad's daughters: They may marry whomever they choose — only it must be within a clan of their father's tribe.

[7] No inheritance in Israel shall pass from tribe to tribe. Every Israelite stays anchored to the ancestral land of his father's tribe.

[8] Any daughter who inherits land in any tribe of Israel must marry within a clan of her father's tribe, so that every Israelite holds onto his fathers' inheritance.

[9] No inheritance shall drift from one tribe to another. Each tribe of Israel keeps what is its own."

[10] Zelophehad's daughters did exactly as the LORD ordered Moses.

[11] Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah — all five married their cousins,

[12] men from the clans of Manasseh son of Joseph. Their inheritance stayed in the tribe of their father's clan.

[13] These are the commands and rulings the LORD issued through Moses to the Israelites on the plains of Moab, by the Jordan across from Jericho.

[1] The vision Obadiah saw.

This is what the Lord God says about Edom—

We've heard a report from the LORD. A messenger has been dispatched among the nations: "On your feet! Let's march against her. Let's go to war."

[2] Listen, Edom—I'm making you small among the nations. You will be despised, utterly.

[3] The arrogance of your heart has fooled you—you who nest in the cliff's clefts, who make your home in the heights, who say in your heart, "Who can drag me down?"

[4] Soar like the eagle. Build your nest among the stars. Even from there I will haul you down. The LORD has spoken.

[5] If thieves came for you, if raiders came in the night—how completely you'd be ruined!—wouldn't they steal only what they wanted? If grape-pickers came through your vineyard, wouldn't they at least leave the gleanings?

[6] But Esau—he will be ransacked. His hidden treasures, hunted out.

[7] Your allies will drive you to the border. Your friends will deceive you and overpower you. Those who eat at your table will set the trap beneath you. Edom will not see it coming.

[8] On that day, says the LORD, won't I wipe out the wise from Edom, the discerning from Esau's mountain?

[9] Your warriors, Teman, will lose their nerve. Every last man on Esau's mountain will be cut down in the slaughter.

[10] Because of the violence you did to your brother Jacob, shame will bury you. You will be erased—forever.

[11] On the day you stood by, doing nothing—the day strangers carried off his wealth, foreigners poured through his gates and cast lots over Jerusalem—you were one of them.

[12] You should not have gloated over your brother on the day of his disaster. You should not have celebrated over Judah's children on the day of their ruin. You should not have run your mouth on the day of their distress.

[13] You should not have marched through the gate of my people on the day of their catastrophe. You—of all people—gloating over their misery on the day of their catastrophe, helping yourself to their wealth on the day of their catastrophe.

[14] You should not have stood at the crossroads to cut down the ones who escaped. You should not have handed over the survivors on the day of their distress.

[15] Because the Day of the LORD is closing in on every nation. What you did will be done back to you. Your dealings will return upon your own head.

[16] Just as you drank on my holy mountain, all the nations will drink—drink and drink—until they swallow themselves into oblivion, as if they had never been.

[17] But on Mount Zion there will be a remnant who escape, and the mountain will be holy. The house of Jacob will reclaim what is theirs.

[18] The house of Jacob will be fire. The house of Joseph, flame. The house of Esau—stubble. They will set it ablaze and consume it. Not one survivor of Esau's house. The LORD has spoken.

[19] The people of the Negev will take Esau's mountain. The people of the foothills will take Philistia. They will possess the fields of Ephraim and Samaria, and Benjamin will take Gilead.

[20] The exiles of Israel—this army—will claim Canaan all the way to Zarephath. The exiles of Jerusalem in Sepharad will possess the cities of the Negev.

[21] Deliverers will climb Mount Zion to judge the mountain of Esau. And the kingdom will belong to the LORD.

[1] Paul and Timothy, slaves of the Messiah Jesus — to every saint in the Messiah Jesus living in Philippi, together with the overseers and deacons. [2] Grace to you, and peace, from God our Father and the Lord Jesus, the Messiah.

[3] Every time you cross my mind, I thank God. [4] Every prayer, every time, for all of you — I pray with joy. [5] Because from day one until now, you have been my partners in this gospel. [6] And I am dead certain of this: the One who started the good work in you will see it through to completion on the day of the Messiah Jesus.

[7] I am right to feel this way about you. You are lodged in my heart. Whether I am in chains or standing before a court defending and confirming the gospel, you share this grace with me — every one of you. [8] God himself can vouch for how I ache for you with the very affection of the Messiah Jesus.

[9] And this is what I pray: that your love would overflow more and more, with real knowledge and clear-eyed discernment, [10] so you can sort out what truly matters and arrive at the day of the Messiah pure and blameless, [11] full to the brim with the fruit of righteousness that grows through Jesus the Messiah — to the glory and praise of God.

[12] I want you to know, family: everything that has happened to me has actually pushed the gospel forward. [13] The whole imperial guard — and everyone else — knows by now that my chains are for the Messiah. [14] And most of the brothers and sisters, emboldened by my imprisonment, have found their nerve and are speaking the word without fear.

[15] True — some preach the Messiah out of envy and rivalry. Others do it with good motives. [16] These last act out of love, knowing I am stationed here to defend the gospel. [17] The others proclaim the Messiah out of self-interest, not sincerity, hoping to make my chains chafe a little more.

[18] So what? Either way — pretense or truth — the Messiah is being preached. And in that I rejoice. I will keep on rejoicing. [19] Because I know this whole situation will land me in deliverance, through your prayers and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus the Messiah. [20] My deepest expectation, my hope, is this: that I will be ashamed of nothing, but with full courage — now, as always — the Messiah will be magnified in my body, whether I live or die.

[21] For me, living is the Messiah. And dying is gain. [22] If I keep living in this body, that means fruitful work — and I do not know which I would choose. [23] I am torn between the two. I long to pull up stakes and be with the Messiah — that is far and away the better thing. [24] But staying in this body is more necessary for you. [25] So I am convinced of this: I will stay. I will remain with all of you for your progress and joy in the faith, [26] so that when I come back to you, you will have even more reason to boast in the Messiah Jesus because of me.

[27] Only — live as citizens worthy of the gospel of the Messiah. Whether I come and see you or only hear about you from a distance, I want to know that you are standing firm in one Spirit, fighting shoulder to shoulder with one soul for the faith of the gospel, [28] never flinching at your opponents. That fearlessness is a sign — to them, of ruin; to you, of salvation. And this is from God.

[29] Because you have been given — as a gift — not only to believe in the Messiah, but to suffer for him. [30] You are in the same struggle you once saw me in, and now hear that I am in still.

[1] So if there's any encouragement in Christ, any comfort from love, any partnership in the Spirit, any tenderness or compassion at all— [2] then complete my joy. Think the same way. Love the same way. Be one in soul, one in purpose. [3] Nothing out of rivalry. Nothing out of vanity. Instead, with humility, count others as more important than yourselves. [4] Don't fixate on your own interests. Watch out for the interests of others.

[5] Carry the mindset Christ Jesus carried: [6] He existed in the very form of God, but didn't treat equality with God as something to clutch. [7] He emptied himself. Took the form of a slave. Came in human likeness. [8] And being found in human shape, he humbled himself further— obedient all the way to death. Death on a cross. [9] So God lifted him to the highest place and gave him the name above every name. [10] At the name of Jesus, every knee will bow— in heaven, on earth, beneath the earth— [11] every tongue confessing: Jesus Christ is Lord. To the glory of God the Father.

[12] So then, my loved ones— you've always obeyed, not just when I was there but even more now in my absence— work out your salvation with fear and trembling. [13] Because God is the one at work in you, igniting both the desire and the doing of what pleases him.

[14] Do everything without grumbling, without arguing. [15] So you'll be blameless and pure, children of God without a stain in the middle of a crooked, twisted generation— shining like stars in a dark sky, [16] holding out the word of life. Then I can boast on the day of Christ that I didn't run for nothing or wear myself out for nothing. [17] And even if my life is being poured out as a drink offering on the sacrifice and service of your faith, I'm glad. I celebrate with all of you. [18] You should celebrate too. Celebrate with me.

[19] I'm hoping in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, so I can be encouraged when I get news of how you're doing. [20] I have no one else like him— no one who genuinely cares about your welfare. [21] Everyone else chases their own interests, not the interests of Jesus Christ. [22] But you know Timothy's character— how he served alongside me in the gospel like a son with his father. [23] So I'm hoping to send him the moment I see how things go for me. [24] And I trust in the Lord that I'll come soon myself.

[25] Meanwhile, I thought it necessary to send Epaphroditus back to you— my brother, my coworker, my fellow soldier, the one you sent to look after my needs. [26] He's been longing for all of you and deeply troubled because you heard he was sick. [27] And he was sick— sick to the point of death. But God had mercy on him. And not just on him— on me too, so I wouldn't be drowning in grief on top of grief. [28] So I'm sending him back to you as quickly as I can, so you can see him again and be glad, and my own anxiety can ease. [29] Welcome him in the Lord with overflowing joy. Honor people like him— [30] because he came close to dying for the work of Christ, risking his life to fill in what you couldn't do for me yourselves.

[1] Finally, my brothers and sisters — rejoice in the Lord. Writing the same things to you again costs me nothing, and it keeps you safe.

[2] Watch out for the dogs. Watch out for the workers of evil. Watch out for the mutilators who keep cutting away at flesh.

[3] We are the circumcision — we who worship by the Spirit of God, who glory in Christ Jesus, who place zero confidence in the flesh.

[4] Though if anyone has grounds to trust the flesh, I do — and more than most.

[5] Circumcised on the eighth day. Israelite by blood. Tribe of Benjamin. A Hebrew of Hebrews. Pharisee when it came to the Law.

[6] Zealous to the point of hunting down the church. As for righteousness measured by the Law — flawless.

[7] But everything I once counted as gain — I now count as loss because of Christ.

[8] More than that — I count everything as loss compared to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord. For him I have lost it all, and I consider it garbage — so that I might gain Christ

[9] and be found in him, not clutching a righteousness of my own carved out by Law-keeping, but the righteousness that comes through the faithfulness of Christ — the righteousness from God that rests on faith.

[10] I want to know him. To know the power of his resurrection. To share in his sufferings, becoming like him in his death —

[11] somehow, by any means, arriving at the resurrection from the dead.

[12] Not that I have already grabbed hold of it. Not that I am already finished. But I press on — to seize that for which Christ Jesus seized me.

[13] Brothers and sisters, I do not consider myself to have taken hold of it yet. But one thing I do: forgetting what is behind, straining toward what is ahead,

[14] I drive hard toward the finish line — toward the prize of God's upward call in Christ Jesus.

[15] So all of us who are mature — let us think this way. And if on some point you think differently, God will reveal that to you too.

[16] Only — let us hold fast to what we have already attained.

[17] Brothers and sisters, imitate me, together. Watch closely those who walk according to the pattern you see in us.

[18] Because many walk — I have told you about them often, and now I say it again in tears — as enemies of the cross of Christ.

[19] Their end is destruction. Their god is their appetite. Their glory is their shame. Their minds are fixed on earthly things.

[20] But our citizenship is in heaven — and from there we are waiting eagerly for a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ,

[21] who will take these humiliated bodies of ours and transfigure them to match his own glorious body, by the power that allows him to bring everything under his rule.

[1] So then, my brothers and sisters — the ones I love, the ones I ache to see, my joy, my crown — stand firm in the Lord. Stand firm, dear friends.

[2] Euodia, I'm pleading with you. Syntyche, I'm pleading with you. Find your way back to the same mind in the Lord.

[3] And you too, my faithful partner — help these women. They fought side by side with me for the gospel, alongside Clement and the rest of my co-workers, whose names are written in the book of life.

[4] Rejoice in the Lord — always. I'll say it again: rejoice.

[5] Let your gentleness be unmistakable to everyone. The Lord is near.

[6] Don't be strangled by anxiety about anything. Instead, in every situation — through prayer, through pleading, with thanks woven through it all — bring your requests before God.

[7] And the peace of God, the kind that runs deeper than any mind can map, will stand guard over your heart and your thoughts in Christ Jesus.

[8] Finally, brothers and sisters: whatever is true, whatever commands respect, whatever is right, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is worth admiring — if anything is excellent, if anything deserves praise — train your mind on these things.

[9] Whatever you learned from me, received from me, heard from me, saw in me — put it into practice. And the God of peace will be with you.

[10] I rejoiced — deeply, in the Lord — when at last your concern for me bloomed again. Not that you'd stopped caring; you just hadn't had the chance to show it.

[11] I'm not saying this because I'm in need. I've learned how to be content no matter the circumstances.

[12] I know what it is to be brought low, and I know what it is to have more than enough. I've been initiated into the secret of it all — full stomach or empty, abundance or want.

[13] I can do all of it through the One who pours strength into me.

[14] Still — it was beautiful of you to share in my hardship.

[15] You Philippians know how this works. Back in the early days of the gospel, when I left Macedonia, no church partnered with me in the ledger of giving and receiving — none but you.

[16] Even when I was in Thessalonica, you sent help for my needs, again and again.

[17] It's not the gift itself I'm chasing. What I'm after is the interest piling up in your account.

[18] I have everything I need, and more. I'm overflowing — fully supplied now that I've received from Epaphroditus what you sent. A fragrant offering, a sacrifice that pleases God, that delights him.

[19] And my God will fill up everything you lack, drawing from his riches — riches measured in glory — in Christ Jesus.

[20] To our God and Father be the glory, forever and ever. Amen.

[21] Greet every one of God's people in Christ Jesus. The brothers and sisters with me send their greetings.

[22] All God's people here send their greetings — especially the ones in Caesar's household.

[23] The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.

[1] Paul — prisoner for Christ Jesus — and Timothy our brother, to Philemon, our dear friend and partner in this work,

[2] to Apphia our sister, to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the church that gathers in your house:

[3] Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

[4] I thank my God every time I bring you to mind in my prayers —

[5] because I keep hearing about your love and the faith you have in the Lord Jesus and toward all his people.

[6] My prayer is that the generosity flowing from your faith becomes contagious, as you come to know in full every good thing we have in Christ.

[7] Your love has given me deep joy and real encouragement, brother — the hearts of God's people have been refreshed because of you.

[8] So here it is. In Christ I have every right to order you to do what needs doing.

[9] But I would rather ask. As an appeal — love to love. This is Paul speaking, an old man now, and these days a prisoner of Christ Jesus as well —

[10] and I am asking you on behalf of my child. I became his father here in chains. His name is Onesimus.

[11] Yes — that Onesimus. Once useless to you. Now genuinely useful to both of us.

[12] I am sending him back to you. And I am sending my own heart with him.

[13] I would have kept him here with me, to serve in your place while I sit in chains for the gospel.

[14] But I would not do it without your consent. I want your kindness in this to be freely given, not squeezed out of you.

[15] Maybe this is why he was torn from you for a while — so you could have him back for good.

[16] No longer as a slave. Better than a slave. As a beloved brother — beloved to me, and how much more to you, both as a man and as a brother in the Lord.

[17] So if you count me a partner, welcome him the way you would welcome me.

[18] If he wronged you, if he owes you anything — put it on my account.

[19] I, Paul, am writing this with my own hand: I will pay it back. And I will not even mention that you owe me your very self.

[20] Yes, brother — let me have this favor from you in the Lord. Refresh my heart in Christ.

[21] I am writing with full confidence that you will say yes — and knowing you, you will go even further than I am asking.

[22] One more thing — get a guest room ready for me. I am hoping that, through your prayers, I will be given back to you.

[23] Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, sends greetings,

[24] as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, and Luke — my fellow workers.

[25] The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.

[1] The proverbs of Solomon, son of David, king of Israel —

[2] written so you can grasp wisdom and discipline, decode words that carry weight,

[3] absorb the kind of instruction that sharpens you: doing what's right, fair, and straight,

[4] giving street smarts to the naive, knowledge and clear thinking to the young.

[5] Let the wise listen and grow stronger. Let the discerning learn to navigate.

[6] These are proverbs, parables, the sayings of sages — riddles you'll need to crack.

[7] Fearing the LORD — that's where knowledge starts. Fools sneer at wisdom and discipline.

[8] Listen, my son, to your father's instruction. Don't shrug off your mother's teaching.

[9] They are a crown of grace on your head, a chain of honor around your neck.

[10] My son, if sinners try to seduce you — don't go.

[11] When they say, "Come with us. Let's lie in wait for blood. Let's ambush some innocent soul just for the thrill —

[12] swallow them alive like the grave, whole, like bodies dropped into a pit.

[13] We'll score everything valuable, stuff our houses with the spoils.

[14] Throw in your lot with us. One purse, all of us together" —

[15] my son, do not walk that road with them. Pull your foot back from their path.

[16] Their feet sprint toward evil. They race to spill blood.

[17] No bird is dumb enough to fly into a net it watched being set —

[18] but these men? They're setting the trap for themselves. They're ambushing their own lives.

[19] That's how it ends for everyone hungry for stolen gain. It strips the life right out of them.

[20] Wisdom is out there shouting in the streets, raising her voice in the public squares.

[21] She calls from the noisiest corners, at the city gates she delivers her speech:

[22] "How long, you clueless ones, will you love being clueless? How long will mockers savor their mockery and fools despise knowledge?

[23] Turn when I correct you — and watch. I'll pour out my spirit on you. I'll make my words known to you.

[24] But I called and you refused. I reached out my hand and nobody looked up.

[25] You brushed off every piece of counsel I offered. You wanted nothing to do with my correction.

[26] So I'll laugh when disaster hits you. I'll mock when terror crashes down —

[27] when terror rolls in like a storm front and disaster sweeps over you like a hurricane, when anguish and distress pin you down.

[28] Then they'll call me and I won't answer. They'll search hard for me and won't find me.

[29] Because they hated knowledge. They refused to fear the LORD.

[30] They wouldn't take my counsel. They despised every correction I gave.

[31] So they'll eat the fruit of the road they chose. They'll gorge on their own schemes.

[32] The naive get killed by their own drifting. Fools get destroyed by their own ease.

[33] But whoever listens to me will live in safety, settled and unafraid of harm."

[1] My son, if you take my words in,
if you tuck my commands away inside you,

[2] tuning your ear to wisdom,
bending your heart toward understanding —

[3] yes, if you call out for insight,
if you lift your voice for discernment,

[4] if you hunt for it like silver,
dig for it like buried treasure —

[5] then you will grasp what it means to fear the Lord.
You will find the knowledge of God.

[6] Because the Lord is the one who gives wisdom.
From his mouth: knowledge and understanding.

[7] He stockpiles success for the upright,
shields those who walk with integrity,

[8] guarding the paths of justice,
watching over the road of his faithful ones.

[9] Then you will understand righteousness, justice, fairness —
every good road there is.

[10] Wisdom will move into your heart.
Knowledge will become a delight to your soul.

[11] Discretion will stand guard over you.
Understanding will keep watch —

[12] rescuing you from the way of evil,
from men whose words twist,

[13] who abandon straight paths
to walk in darkness,

[14] who get a thrill from doing wrong,
who celebrate the worst kind of corruption,

[15] whose roads are crooked,
whose tracks lead nowhere good.

[16] It will rescue you, too, from the seductress,
the smooth-talking stranger,

[17] who walked out on the partner of her youth
and forgot the covenant she made before God.

[18] Her house tilts down toward death.
Her tracks lead to the shades.

[19] No one who goes to her comes back.
No one finds the paths of life again.

[20] So walk the road of the good.
Stay on the paths of the righteous.

[21] The upright will live in the land.
People of integrity will remain in it.

[22] But the wicked will be cut off from the land,
the treacherous torn out of it by the roots.

[1] My son, lock in on what wisdom is telling you. Lean toward understanding. [2] Hold onto discernment. Let your lips guard what you know.

[3] Because the lips of the seductress drip honey, and her mouth slides smoother than oil — [4] but the aftertaste is wormwood, and what felt like silk becomes a blade with two edges. [5] Her feet are walking straight down to death. Every step she takes is sinking toward the grave. [6] She refuses to consider the road of life. Her paths wander and she has no idea — and that is exactly how she wants it.

[7] So listen to me, sons. Do not turn from what I am saying. [8] Keep your distance from her. Do not even come near the door of her house. [11] And at the end — when your flesh and your body are spent — you will groan. [12] You will say: How did I despise correction? How did my heart sneer at every warning? [13] I would not listen to my teachers. I refused to lean in when they spoke. [14] I was a breath away from total ruin, right there in front of the whole assembly.

[15] Drink from your own cistern. Drink the running water of your own well. [16] Should your springs spill into the street, your channels of water into the public square? [17] They belong to you. Let her body satisfy you always. Let her love intoxicate you, every time. [20] Why, my son, would you be drunk on a stranger? Why pull a foreign woman to your chest?

[21] Because every road a man walks is laid bare before the LORD. He examines every path. [22] The wicked man's own crimes will hunt him down. The ropes of his sin will hold him fast. [23] He dies for lack of discipline. He staggers, lost, drowning in his own folly.

[1] My son, if you've cosigned for your neighbor, shaken hands on a stranger's debt — [2] you're snared. Your own words have trapped you. Your own mouth has tied the knot. [5] Tear free like a gazelle from the hunter's grip, like a bird breaking the trapper's net.

[16] Six things the LORD hates. Seven that disgust him: [17] arrogant eyes, a lying tongue, hands that spill innocent blood, [18] a heart that drafts evil schemes, feet that race to do harm, [19] a false witness breathing out lies, and anyone who sets brothers against each other.

[20] My son, hold tight to your father's command. Don't throw away your mother's teaching. [21] Tie them over your heart. Wear them around your neck. [22] When you walk, they'll guide you. [1] Son, hold tight to what I say. Store my commands deep inside you. [2] Guard them and live. Treat my teaching like the pupil of your eye. [3] Tie them around your fingers. Carve them onto the walls of your heart.

[6] One day I stood at my window, peering through the lattice, [7] and I watched the street. Among the clueless, the easily-led, I spotted a young man with no sense at all. [8] He took the corner near her house — strolling, casual — and headed straight down her street. [9] Twilight was falling. Evening dimmed into night, and the dark closed in.

[10] And there she came to meet him — dressed like a prostitute, calculating behind her eyes. [11] Loud. Restless. The kind of woman whose feet refuse to stay home — [12] always out in the street, in the squares, lurking on every corner. [13] She grabbed him. Kissed him. Looked him dead in the face and said, without a flicker of shame:

[14] "I owed a peace offering today, and I paid my vows. [15] So I came looking for you — wanted you specifically — and here you are. [16] I've made the bed up with colored linens, fine Egyptian fabric. [17] I've drenched the sheets in myrrh, aloes, cinnamon. [18] Come on. Let's drink love till morning. Let's lose ourselves in each other. [19] My husband isn't home. He's gone — far away. [20] Took the money bag with him. Won't be back till the full moon."

[21] She wore him down with her words. Her smooth lips reeled him in. [22] He followed her like an ox walking to slaughter — like a deer stepping into the snare, [23] until an arrow goes through its liver. Like a bird flying straight into the net, never seeing it was about to die.

[24] So listen to me, sons. Pay attention. Hear what I'm telling you. [25] Don't let your heart wander her direction. Don't drift onto her streets. [26] She has dropped countless men. [1] Listen — Wisdom is calling. Insight is raising her voice.

[23] More from the wise:

Playing favorites in court is wrong. [24] Tell the guilty, "You're innocent," and nations will curse you, peoples will damn your name. [25] But those who convict the guilty — they'll be blessed. A rich blessing will fall on them.

[26] An honest answer is like a kiss on the lips.

[30] I walked past the field of a sluggard, past the vineyard of a man without sense. [31] Thorns everywhere. Weeds had swallowed it whole. The stone wall lay in ruins. [32] I looked. I took it in. I saw it and learned: [33] A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest — [34] and poverty arrives like a prowler, want like a man with a weapon.
