[24] If you were cut from a wild olive tree, against nature, and grafted into a cultivated one — how much more easily will the natural branches be grafted back into their own tree?

[25] I don't want you missing this, brothers and sisters — I don't want you thinking you've got it all figured out: a hardening has come over part of Israel until the full number of the nations comes in. [26] And then — all Israel will be saved. As Scripture says:

The Deliverer will come from Zion;
he will sweep godlessness away from Jacob.
[27] And this is my covenant with them
when I take away their sins.

[28] In terms of the gospel, they are enemies — for your sake. In terms of God's choosing, they are loved — for the sake of their ancestors. [29] Because God's gifts and his call are never taken back.

[30] Once, you were the disobedient ones. Now, through their disobedience, you've received mercy. [31] In the same way, they've been disobedient now — so that, through the mercy shown to you, they too may receive mercy. [32] God locked everyone up in disobedience so he could pour mercy out on everyone.

[33] Oh, the depth — the riches, the wisdom, the knowledge of God!
How unsearchable his judgments,
how untraceable his ways.

[34] Who has ever known the mind of the Lord?
Who has ever advised him?
[35] Who has ever handed him anything
and put God in their debt?

[36] Because everything comes from him.
Everything moves through him.
Everything bends back toward him.

To him be the glory forever. Amen.

[1] So here's what I'm asking you, brothers and sisters—and I'm asking it on the strength of everything God's mercy has done for you: take your body, your actual physical life, and place it on the altar. A living sacrifice. Holy. The kind of offering God actually wants. This is what real worship looks like—worship with your mind switched on.

[2] Stop letting this age press you into its mold. Be transformed instead—from the inside out, by a mind made new. Then you'll be able to discern what God actually wants: what's good, what pleases him, what hits the mark perfectly.

[3] By the grace given to me, I'm telling every single one of you: don't think of yourself as more than you are. Think with sober judgment. Measure yourself by the faith God has handed you—nothing more, nothing less.

[4] Think of it like a body. One body, many parts, and each part has its own job to do.

[5] That's us. Many of us, but one body in Christ—and each of us belongs to all the others.

[6] We've been given different gifts, shaped by the grace poured into us. If your gift is prophecy, prophesy in proportion to your faith.

[7] If it's service, serve. If it's teaching, teach.

[8] If it's encouragement, encourage. If you give, give generously. If you lead, lead with intensity. If you show mercy, do it with joy.

[9] Let love be the real thing—no masks, no performance. Hate what is evil. Cling to what is good with both hands.

[10] Love each other like family. Outdo one another in showing honor.

[11] Don't drift into spiritual laziness. Keep the fire burning. Serve the Lord with everything you've got.

[12] Let hope make you celebrate. Let suffering make you steady. Let prayer be constant—a current running through everything.

[13] When God's people are in need, step in. Make hospitality your reflex.

[14] Bless the people who persecute you. Bless them—do not curse them.

[15] When others are celebrating, celebrate with them. When they're grieving, grieve with them.

[16] Live in harmony with each other. Don't get above yourself—stay close to the people the world overlooks. Don't be impressed with your own wisdom.

[17] Don't repay evil with evil. Aim for what everyone can recognize as good.

[18] As much as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone.

[19] Don't take revenge, friends. Leave room for God's wrath. Scripture says, "Vengeance is mine. I'll handle it," says the Lord.

[20] Try this instead: if your enemy is hungry, feed him. If he's thirsty, give him a drink. Doing that piles burning coals on his head.

[21] Don't let evil defeat you. Defeat evil with good.

[1] Submit to the governing authorities. Every authority that exists exists because God placed it there. The structures of power did not arise on their own — God arranged them.

[2] So when you defy authority, you are defying what God set in place, and those who do will face the consequences they have earned.

[3] Rulers are not a threat to those doing good — they are a threat to those doing evil. You want to live without fear of the authorities? Do what is right. You will get their approval, not their wrath.

[4] The one in power is God's servant working for your good. But if you cross the line into evil, then fear — because the sword in their hand is not decorative. They are God's servant, an agent of justice executing wrath on the wrongdoer.

[5] So submit — not just because you are afraid of getting punished, but because your conscience demands it.

[6] This is also why you pay taxes. The officials are God's servants, devoting themselves to exactly this work.

[7] Give everyone what you owe them: taxes to the tax collector, tariffs to the tariff officer, respect to those who deserve respect, honor to those who deserve honor.

[8] Owe nothing to anyone — except love. That debt never gets paid off. And whoever loves another has already fulfilled the law.

[9] Every command — do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not covet, and all the rest — gets summed up in one line: love your neighbor as yourself.

[10] Love does no harm to a neighbor. That is why love is the law fulfilled.

[11] And you know what time it is. The hour has come to wake up. Our rescue is closer now than when we first believed.

[12] The night is almost gone. Daybreak is here. So strip off the works of darkness and put on the armor of light.

[13] Walk like people who live in the daylight — not in wild parties and drunken stupors, not in beds of indulgence and shameless lust, not in fights and jealous rages.

[14] Instead, clothe yourself in the Lord Jesus Christ. And stop scheming ways to feed the cravings of the flesh.

[1] Welcome the one whose faith is fragile — but not to argue them into the ground over what they believe. [2] One person is convinced they can eat anything; another, whose faith is shaky, sticks to vegetables. [3] The eater must not look down on the abstainer, and the abstainer must not pass judgment on the eater — because God has welcomed them both. [4] Who do you think you are, judging someone else's servant? Whether they stand or fall is their Master's business. And they will stand — because the Lord has the power to hold them up.

[5] One person treats certain days as sacred; another treats every day the same. Let each be fully convinced in their own mind. [6] The one who honors the day does it for the Lord. The one who eats does it for the Lord — they thank God. The one who abstains does it for the Lord too — and gives thanks just the same. [7] None of us lives for ourselves alone, and none of us dies for ourselves alone. [8] If we live, we live for the Lord. If we die, we die for the Lord. Living or dying — we belong to him. [9] This is exactly why Christ died and rose again: so he could be Lord of both the dead and the living.

[10] So why are you sitting in judgment over your brother? Why do you sneer at your sister? Every one of us will stand before the judgment seat of God. [11] As Scripture says: *As surely as I live, says the Lord, every knee will bend before me, and every tongue will confess to God.* [12] Each of us, then, will give an account of ourselves to God.

[13] So let's stop putting each other on trial. Instead, make this your verdict: never lay down a stumbling block in your brother's path. [14] I know — I'm convinced in the Lord Jesus — that nothing is unclean in itself. But if someone believes it's unclean, then for them, it is. [15] If your eating wounds your brother, you are no longer walking in love. Do not let your food destroy someone Christ died for. [16] Do not let what you consider good be slandered as evil. [17] The kingdom of God is not about food and drink. It's righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. [18] Serve Christ this way, and you please God — and people see it.

[19] So chase after what makes for peace, and what builds each other up. [20] Do not tear down God's work over a plate of food. Everything is clean — but it becomes wrong when what you eat makes someone else stumble. [21] Better to skip the meat, skip the wine, skip anything that trips your brother up. [22] Whatever you believe about these things, keep it between you and God. Blessed is the one who doesn't condemn themselves over what they've decided is fine. [23] But the one who has doubts and eats anyway is already condemned, because they're not acting from faith. And anything that doesn't come from faith is sin.

[1] Those of us who are strong have an obligation — to carry the weaknesses of those who aren't, and not just please ourselves. [2] Each of us should aim to please our neighbor, for their good, to build them up. [3] Even the Messiah didn't please himself. As it stands written: "The insults of those who insulted you fell on me." [4] Everything written in earlier times was written to teach us — so that through endurance and the encouragement of the Scriptures we might hold onto hope. [5] May the God who supplies endurance and encouragement give you the same mindset toward one another that flows from Christ Jesus, [6] so that together, with one voice, you would glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus the Messiah.

[7] So welcome one another, then — just as Christ welcomed you, to the glory of God. [8] What I'm saying is this: Christ became a servant to the circumcised on behalf of God's truthfulness, to confirm the promises made to the patriarchs, [9] and so that the nations would glorify God for his mercy. As it stands written: "Therefore I will praise you among the nations, and sing to your name." [10] And again: "Rejoice, you nations, with his people." [11] And again: "Praise the Lord, all you nations; let all the peoples sing his praise." [12] And again, Isaiah says: "The root of Jesse will rise — he who rises to rule the nations. In him the nations will set their hope." [13] May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace as you trust him, so that you overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit.

[14] I'm convinced about you, my brothers and sisters — convinced that you yourselves are full of goodness, packed with knowledge, fully capable of instructing one another. [15] Still, I've written to you boldly on certain points, as a reminder, because of the grace God gave me [16] to be a minister of Christ Jesus to the nations — serving as a priest of God's good news, so that the offering of the nations might be acceptable, made holy by the Holy Spirit.

[17] So in Christ Jesus I have something to boast about in my work for God. [18] I won't presume to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through me to bring the nations to obedience — by word and deed, [19] by the power of signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God. The result: from Jerusalem all the way around to Illyricum, I have fully delivered the gospel of Christ. [20] My ambition has always been to preach where Christ's name had never been spoken — I wouldn't build on someone else's foundation. [21] As it stands written: "Those who were never told of him will see, and those who never heard will understand."

[22] That's why I've been kept from coming to you so many times. [23] But now I'm out of room in these regions, and for years I've longed to see you — [24] so I plan to do it on my way to Spain. I hope to see you as I pass through, and to be sent on my way by you after I've enjoyed your company for a while. [25] Right now, though, I'm headed to Jerusalem to serve the saints there. [26] Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a contribution for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem. [27] Pleased to do it — and they owed it. If the nations have shared in the spiritual blessings of the Jews, they owe it to serve them in material things. [28] So once I've finished this task and sealed this offering safely to them, I'll set out for Spain by way of you. [29] And I know that when I come to you, I'll come in the full blessing of Christ.

[30] I'm begging you, brothers and sisters — by our Lord Jesus Christ and by the love of the Spirit — join me in the struggle, by praying to God for me. [31] Pray that I'll be rescued from the unbelievers in Judea, and that my service in Jerusalem will be welcomed by the saints there, [32] so that by God's will I can come to you with joy and find rest among you. [33] May the God of peace be with all of you. Amen.

[1] Let me introduce you to Phoebe, our sister, who serves the church at Cenchreae. [2] Welcome her in the Lord the way God's people should — and give her whatever she needs from you. She has been a patron to many, including me.

[3] Greet Prisca and Aquila — my partners in the work of Christ Jesus. [4] They put their own necks on the line to save my life. I'm not the only one grateful — every Gentile church owes them. [5] Greet the church that meets in their house too.

Greet my dear friend Epaenetus — the first convert in Asia for Christ.

[6] Greet Mary, who has worn herself out for you.

[7] Greet Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen, my fellow prisoners — standouts among the apostles, and in Christ before I was.

[8] Greet Ampliatus, beloved to me in the Lord.

[9] Greet Urbanus, our co-worker in Christ, and my dear Stachys.

[10] Greet Apelles, tested and proven in Christ.

Greet the household of Aristobulus.

[11] Greet my kinsman Herodion. Greet those in the household of Narcissus who belong to the Lord.

[12] Greet Tryphaena and Tryphosa — women who labor in the Lord. Greet beloved Persis, who has labored hard in the Lord.

[13] Greet Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and his mother — who has been a mother to me as well.

[14] Greet Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermes, Patrobas, Hermas, and the brothers and sisters with them.

[15] Greet Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the saints with them.

[16] Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the churches of Christ send their greetings.

[17] One more thing, brothers and sisters — and listen carefully. Keep your eyes open for the ones who create division and lay traps that contradict the teaching you received. Walk away from them. [18] People like this aren't serving Christ our Lord — they're serving their own appetites. With smooth talk and pious flattery they reel in the unsuspecting. [19] Your obedience is famous everywhere — that thrills me — but I want you wise about what is good and untouched by what is evil. [20] The God of peace will crush Satan under your feet, and soon.

The grace of our Lord Jesus be with you.

[21] Timothy, my co-worker, sends greetings, along with Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater — my kinsmen.

[22] I, Tertius, who wrote this letter down, greet you in the Lord.

[23] Gaius — host to me and to the whole church — sends greetings. So does Erastus, the city treasurer, and Quartus, our brother.

[25] To the One who has the power to make you stand firm — according to my gospel, according to the proclamation of Jesus Christ, according to the unveiling of the mystery kept under wraps through long ages of silence [26] but now thrown open, made known through the prophetic writings by command of the eternal God, to bring all the nations to the obedience of faith — [27] to the only wise God, through Jesus Christ, be the glory forever. Amen.

[1] Back in the days when the judges ruled, a famine struck the land. A man from Bethlehem in Judah packed up his wife and two sons and went to live as a foreigner in the country of Moab.

[2] His name was Elimelech, his wife was Naomi, and their sons were Mahlon and Kilion — Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah. They reached Moab and settled in.

[3] Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died. She was left with her two sons.

[4] The sons married Moabite women — one named Orpah, the other Ruth. They lived there about ten years.

[5] Then both Mahlon and Kilion died too. Naomi was left — no husband, no sons. Nothing.

[6] Word reached her in Moab that the Lord had finally visited his people and given them bread. So she got up to go home, her daughters-in-law with her.

[7] She walked out of the place where she had buried her life, the two young women at her side, and they started down the road back to Judah.

[8] Then Naomi turned to them. "Go. Both of you. Go back to your mothers' homes. May the Lord show you the same loyal love you showed our dead — and me.

[9] May he grant each of you rest in a new husband's house." She kissed them. They wept, loud and broken.

[10] "No," they said. "We're going with you. Back to your people."

[11] "Turn around, my daughters," Naomi said. "Why come with me? Do I have more sons inside me who could grow up and be your husbands?

[12] Turn back, my daughters. Go. I'm too old for another man. Even if I said tonight there's still hope — even if I had a husband tonight and bore sons —

[13] would you wait for them to grow up? Would you stay unmarried for them? No, my daughters. It's worse for me than for you. The Lord's hand has come down against me."

[14] They wept again. Then Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye. But Ruth clung to her.

[15] "Look," Naomi said, "your sister-in-law is going home to her people and her gods. Go with her."

[16] But Ruth said: "Stop pushing me away. Stop telling me to leave you. Wherever you go, I go. Wherever you stay, I stay. Your people, my people. Your God, my God.

[17] Where you die, I'll die — and there I'll be buried. May the Lord do terrible things to me if anything but death separates us."

[18] When Naomi saw she was set on it — fully, immovably — she stopped arguing.

[19] So the two of them walked on together until they came to Bethlehem. When they arrived, the whole town stirred. "Is this Naomi?" the women said.

[20] "Don't call me Naomi," she told them. "Call me Mara — bitter. The Almighty has made my life bitter.

[21] I went out full. The Lord brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi? The Lord has testified against me. The Almighty has broken me."

[22] So Naomi came back from Moab, and with her came Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law. They arrived in Bethlehem just as the barley harvest was beginning.

[1] Naomi had a relative on her husband's side — a man of standing, from Elimelech's clan. His name was Boaz.

[2] Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, "Let me go out to the fields and glean behind whoever shows me kindness." Naomi said, "Go ahead, daughter."

[3] So she went. She came to a field and gleaned behind the harvesters. As it turned out — though "turned out" is the wrong word for it — the field belonged to Boaz, from Elimelech's clan.

[4] Right then, Boaz arrived from Bethlehem. He called to his harvesters, "The LORD be with you." They called back, "The LORD bless you."

[5] Boaz said to the foreman over his harvesters, "That young woman — whose is she?"

[6] The foreman answered, "She's the Moabite. The one who came back with Naomi from the country of Moab.

[7] She asked if she could glean — gather among the sheaves behind the harvesters. She came, and she's been on her feet since morning. Hasn't rested but a moment in the shelter."

[8] Boaz went to Ruth. "Listen, daughter. Don't glean in any other field. Don't leave here. Stay close to my young women.

[9] Keep your eyes on the field they're harvesting, and follow them. I've ordered the men not to lay a hand on you. When you're thirsty, go to the jars and drink what the men have drawn."

[10] She dropped face-down to the ground. "Why this? Why have I found favor in your eyes — that you would notice me, a foreigner?"

[11] Boaz answered, "I've heard everything. Everything you did for your mother-in-law after your husband died. How you left your father and mother and your homeland and came to a people you'd never known.

[12] May the LORD repay what you've done. May your wages be full from the LORD, the God of Israel — under whose wings you've come to take shelter."

[13] She said, "May I keep finding favor in your eyes, my lord. You've comforted me. You've spoken to the heart of your servant — though I'm not even one of your servant girls."

[14] At mealtime Boaz said to her, "Come over. Eat some bread. Dip your piece in the wine vinegar." She sat beside the harvesters. He passed her roasted grain. She ate her fill, and there was some left over.

[15] When she got up to glean again, Boaz gave his men orders: "Let her glean even among the sheaves. Don't shame her.

[16] In fact — pull some stalks out of the bundles on purpose. Leave them for her. Don't rebuke her."

[17] So she gleaned in the field until evening. Then she beat out what she'd gathered — about thirty pounds of barley.

[18] She hoisted it and went back to town. Her mother-in-law saw what she'd gleaned. Ruth also pulled out what she'd saved from her meal and gave it to her.

[19] Naomi said, "Where did you glean today? Where did you work? Blessed be the man who took notice of you." So Ruth told her mother-in-law whose field she'd worked in. "The man's name," she said, "is Boaz."

[20] Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, "Blessed be he by the LORD, who has not abandoned his kindness — not to the living, not to the dead." Then Naomi added, "The man is a close relative. One of our redeemers."

[21] Ruth the Moabite said, "He also told me, 'Stay close to my workers until they've finished all my harvest.'"

[22] Naomi said, "Good, daughter. Go out with his young women. Better that than being harassed in some other field."

[23] So Ruth stayed close to Boaz's young women, gleaning until the barley harvest and the wheat harvest were both done. And she lived with her mother-in-law.

[1] One day Naomi said to her, "My daughter, shouldn't I be looking for a place where you can finally settle down — somewhere good for you?

[2] Listen — Boaz, our kinsman, the one whose women you've been working alongside, he's winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.

[3] So: wash up. Put on perfume. Dress in your best. Then go down to the threshing floor — but don't let him see you until he's finished eating and drinking.

[4] When he lies down, mark the spot. Then go over, uncover his feet, and lie down there. He'll tell you what to do next."

[5] Ruth said, "Everything you've told me — I'll do."

[6] So she went down to the threshing floor and did exactly what her mother-in-law had told her.

[7] Boaz ate, drank, and his heart was light. He went off to lie down at the edge of the grain pile. She came in quietly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.

[8] In the middle of the night something startled him. He turned over — and there was a woman lying at his feet.

[9] "Who are you?" he said. "I am Ruth, your servant," she answered. "Spread the corner of your cloak over me — you are a redeemer."

[10] "May the LORD bless you, my daughter," he said. "This loyalty you're showing now is greater than what came before — you haven't chased after younger men, rich or poor.

[11] So don't be afraid. Whatever you ask, I'll do. Everyone in town knows what kind of woman you are — a woman of strength.

[12] Yes, it's true I'm a redeemer. But there's another man closer in line than I am.

[13] Stay here tonight. In the morning, if he wants to redeem you — fine, let him. But if he won't, then as the LORD lives, I will. Lie down until morning."

[14] So she lay at his feet until dawn, then rose before anyone could recognize a face. He said, "Don't let it be known a woman came to the threshing floor."

[15] Then he said, "Bring me the shawl you're wearing — hold it open." She did, and he measured out six portions of barley into it, lifted it onto her, and headed back to town.

[16] When she came to her mother-in-law, Naomi asked, "How did it go, my daughter?" And Ruth told her everything the man had done for her.

[17] "He gave me these six measures of barley," she said. "He told me, 'Don't go back to your mother-in-law empty-handed.'"

[18] Naomi said, "Sit tight, my daughter, until you see how this falls out. That man won't rest until he's settled it — today."

[1] Boaz went up to the city gate and sat down there. And the very kinsman-redeemer he'd mentioned walked by. Boaz called out, "Friend, come over here. Sit." The man turned aside and sat down.

[2] Then Boaz gathered ten of the city elders and said, "Sit with us." They sat.

[3] Boaz turned to the redeemer. "Naomi — back from Moab — is selling the parcel of land that belonged to our relative Elimelech.

[4] I figured you should know. So here it is: buy it, in front of these men sitting here, in front of the elders of my people. If you'll redeem it, redeem it. If not, tell me straight, because I'm next in line after you — there's no one else." The man said, "I'll redeem it."

[5] Then Boaz said, "The day you buy the field from Naomi's hand, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the widow of the dead man — to raise up the dead man's name on his inheritance."

[6] The redeemer said, "Then I can't redeem it. I'd ruin my own inheritance. You take it. Take my right of redemption. I can't."

[7] (Now this was the old custom in Israel for sealing any matter of redemption or exchange: a man would pull off his sandal and hand it to the other. That was how Israel notarized a deal.)

[8] So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it yourself." And he pulled off his sandal.

[9] Boaz turned to the elders and all the people. "You are witnesses today. I have acquired from Naomi's hand everything that belonged to Elimelech, and everything that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon.

[10] And Ruth the Moabite, Mahlon's widow — I have acquired her as my wife, to raise up the dead man's name on his inheritance, so his name won't be cut off from his brothers or from the gate of his hometown. You are witnesses today."

[11] All the people at the gate, and the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May the LORD make this woman coming into your house like Rachel and Leah — the two who built the house of Israel. May you prosper in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem.

[12] May your house become like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring the LORD gives you by this young woman."

[13] So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. He went to her, and the LORD gave her conception, and she bore a son.

[14] The women said to Naomi, "Blessed be the LORD, who did not leave you today without a redeemer. May his name ring out in Israel!

[15] He will restore your life and sustain you in your old age — because your daughter-in-law who loves you, who is worth more to you than seven sons, has given birth to him."

[16] Naomi took the child, laid him on her lap, and became his nurse.

[17] The neighborhood women named him. "A son has been born to Naomi!" they said. They called him Obed. He became the father of Jesse, the father of David.

[20] Amminadab fathered Nahshon, Nahshon fathered Salmon,

[21] Salmon fathered Boaz, Boaz fathered Obed,

[22] Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.

[1] Gather yourselves. Gather, you nation with no shame —

[2] before the decree drops, before the day blows past like chaff in the wind, before the heat of the LORD's anger crashes down on you, before the day of his fury arrives.

[3] Seek the LORD, all you humble of the land, you who actually do what he commands. Seek righteousness. Seek humility. Maybe — just maybe — you'll be hidden on the day the LORD's anger burns.

[4] Gaza will be abandoned. Ashkelon left in ruins. Ashdod driven out at noon. Ekron uprooted.

[5] Disaster on you who live along the sea, you Cherethite nation. The word of the LORD is against you, Canaan, land of the Philistines: I will destroy you. No one left.

[6] The coast where you live will become pastureland — shepherds' meadows, sheep pens.

[7] It will belong to the remnant of the house of Judah. There they'll graze their flocks. At evening they'll lie down in the houses of Ashkelon. The LORD their God will visit them and restore their fortunes.

[8] I've heard Moab's insults and the Ammonites' taunts — how they mocked my people and bragged against their borders.

[9] So as I live — declares the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel — Moab will become another Sodom, the Ammonites another Gomorrah: a wasteland of weeds and salt pits, a desolation forever. What's left of my people will plunder them. The survivors of my nation will inherit their land.

[10] This is what their arrogance buys them — payment in full for insulting and strutting against the people of the LORD of Hosts.

[11] The LORD will terrify them. He will starve every god on earth into nothing. And the nations on every distant shore will bow down to him — each from their own place.

[12] You too, Cushites — run through by my sword.

[13] He will stretch out his hand against the north and shatter Assyria. He will turn Nineveh into a wasteland, dry as desert.

[14] Flocks will bed down in the middle of her — every kind of wild creature. The desert owl and the screech owl will roost on her columns. Their calls will echo through the windows. Rubble in the doorways. The cedar paneling stripped bare.

[15] This was the carefree city, sprawling in her own security, telling herself, *I am, and there is no one else.* Look at her now — a ruin, a den for animals. Everyone who passes by hisses and waves them off in disgust.

[1] Disaster waits for the rebel city — filthy, brutal, drunk on its own oppression.

[2] She refuses to listen. She won't accept correction. She doesn't trust the LORD. She won't come near her God.

[3] Her officials are lions mid-roar. Her judges are wolves at dusk — bones gnawed clean before morning.

[4] Her prophets are reckless, treacherous men. Her priests defile what is holy and twist the Law into something it was never meant to be.

[5] But the LORD is there in her streets — righteous, never wrong. Morning by morning he hands down his verdict, never failing, never absent. Yet the corrupt feel no shame.

[6] I have wiped out nations. I have leveled their fortresses. I emptied their streets — no one walking, no one left. Their cities lie in ruins. Not a soul, not a survivor.

[7] I thought, "Surely now she'll respect me. Surely now she'll accept correction." Then her dwelling wouldn't be erased, despite all the punishment I had prepared. But no — they rose up early and went right back to their corruption.

[8] So wait for me — this is the LORD speaking — wait for the day I rise to seize the prey. I have already decided: I will gather the nations, assemble the kingdoms, and pour my fury out on them — every burning ounce of my anger. The whole earth will be consumed in the fire of my jealousy.

[9] Then — then — I will give the peoples clean lips, so they can all call on the name of the LORD and serve him shoulder to shoulder.

[10] From beyond the rivers of Cush, my scattered worshipers, my exiled daughter, will bring me their offering.

[11] On that day, you won't have to carry the shame of all you did against me. I will remove from your midst the arrogant celebrators — and you will never again strut on my holy mountain.

[12] What I will leave with you is a people humble and lowly, who shelter in the name of the LORD.

[13] The remnant of Israel will do no wrong, speak no lies. No deceitful tongue will be found in their mouths. They will graze and lie down, and no one will make them afraid.

[14] Sing, daughter Zion! Shout it out, Israel! Let your whole heart break open with joy, daughter Jerusalem!

[15] The LORD has thrown out your sentence. He has turned back your enemy. The King of Israel — the LORD himself — is right here with you. You will never fear disaster again.

[16] On that day they will say to Jerusalem: Don't be afraid. Zion — let your hands hang strong, not slack.

[17] The LORD your God is in your midst — a warrior who saves. He will delight over you with joy. He will quiet you with his love. He will spin over you with singing.

[18] Those grieving the lost festivals — I will gather them in. They are yours. The shame they carried becomes a burden lifted.

[19] Watch me — at that time I will deal with everyone who crushed you. I will rescue the lame. I will gather the scattered. I will turn their shame into praise and rename their disgrace as renown in every land where they were humiliated.

[20] At that time I will bring you home — at that time I will gather you in. I will make your name known and your praise loud among all the peoples of the earth, when I restore your fortunes right before your eyes. The LORD has spoken.
